question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current average waiting time for children to access Occupational Therapy services, in each Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) any interim support that is available to children with identified developmental needs while awaiting assessment or treatment; and (iii) any steps he is taking to reduce waiting times and ensure timely early intervention, given the impact of delays on child development and family wellbeing.

To ask the Minister of Education To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 43957/22-27, to detail the timeline for (i) the publication of the Report on Demand for Integrated Education and associated sectoral profile; (ii) the launch of the Call for Transformation; and (iii) when a communication and engagement plan will be finalised and implemented.

To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 43957/22-27, to detail (i) any assessment that has made of the impact of the delays on the expansion of integrated school places, particularly in areas of oversubscription; and (ii) any steps he is taking to prevent further slippage in delivering the Integrated Education Strategy.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any steps he is taking to expand access to peer mentoring and restorative practice programmes in schools, given evidence from the Education Authority that such approaches have reduced suspensions, alongside a broader upward trend in pupil suspensions; and (ii) whether he will roll out these programmes more widely, particularly in schools with higher levels of deprivation.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how the Protection from Harassment (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 is being applied in practice in cases involving persistent intimidation, including (i) the number of reports recorded in the last three years; and (ii) the number that resulted in arrest, charge, or protective measures.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps being taken by her Department to improve protections for victims of stalking and harassment, who do not meet the threshold for immediate enforcement action, including (i) access to civil protective orders; and (ii) measures to ensure victims are not left at risk or feeling compelled to relocate for their safety.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether any assessment has been made of the impact of the lack of entry-level posts for newly qualified physiotherapists and other healthcare graduates; and (ii) to detail any immediate, funded plans that are in place to create graduate positions, to prevent the loss of these professionals.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has carried out any assessment of the impact on disabled people being required, or effectively required, to accept electric vehicles through disability mobility provision, including in cases where no viable home charging infrastructure is available.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment that has been made of the road-safety, active-travel and environmental benefits of reducing speed limits to 20mph in urban areas and on certain rural roads, including any findings.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any urgent steps being taken, in conjunction with the Education Authority, to support coach and bus operators delivering mainstream home-to-school transport services, particularly those operating large-capacity vehicles on long-standing fixed-price contracts, in light of recent diesel price increases of up to 30–40 pence per litre.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions being taken to address teacher burnout arising from pressures associated with supporting pupils with special educational needs, as reported by 46 per cent of teachers, including any changes to resourcing, staffing or specialist provision.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any reduction in teacher workload that has been measured or independently verified as a result of the ongoing curriculum and assessment reforms, in light of research indicating that 95 per cent of teachers cite workload as a primary driver of burnout.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment that has made of the consistency of the Police Service of Northern Ireland's response to repeated reports of harassment and intimidation, including (i) how patterns of behaviour are identified and escalated; and (ii) whether current operational thresholds are sufficient, to ensure early intervention to protect women and children.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail, (i) on a cross-government basis, all instances of use, trial, or deployment of services, software, or data analytics platforms provided by Palantir Technologies within Northern Ireland public services, including any use arising via UK-wide procurement frameworks, departmental systems, or arm’s-length bodies; and (ii) the Department responsible for each.

To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 43826/22-27, whether his Department has evaluated the effectiveness of pastoral support provided to pupils following bereavement or significant life events, in cases where consent has been given for information to be shared.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 43445/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will review the non-domestic rating system, including the operation of temporary allowances by Land and Property Services, to consider introducing specific relief for businesses experiencing significant loss of trade due to public infrastructure works, including cases where access is restricted or visibility is significantly reduced by scaffolding, hoardings, roadworks or similar.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any measures in place to ensure the safety of victims, defendants, witnesses, and their families, while inside and outside court buildings; and (ii) whether any additional safeguards will be introduced, following recent incidents involving violence within court premises.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 43070/22-27.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when she will answer AQW 42287/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will commission an urgent, independent review of dog control and animal welfare services, in light of concerns regarding limited staffing, fragmented delivery across councils, and a lack of clear accountability, including the proposed timeline for a review.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when she will answer AQW 42286/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of occasions in the past five years that the Education Authority has been made aware of a pupil experiencing bereavement, or a similarly significant life event; (ii) the number of such cases that he Education Authority provided direct support to the school; (iii) the nature of that support, including any counselling, educational psychology, or pastoral interventions; and (iv) how his Department monitors and ensures consistent application of this guidance across all schools so that access to support for young people is not dependent on t
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 43309/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 41090/22-27, whether any assessment has been made by his Department of the role of online appointment booking systems in managing GP appointment demand in circumstances where demand exceeds available capacity.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 43310/22-27.
To ask the Minister for the Economy when she will answer AQW 41187/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of schools which have reported difficulties in facilitating in-school counselling services due to a lack of appropriate space; (ii) whether any assessment has been made by his Department regarding the impact of (a) limited physical capacity within schools; and (b) restricted provider availability, including services offered only on specific days, on schools’ ability to provide counselling support for pupils.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 42399/22-27.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) when she first became aware of Ulster University’s proposed staff redundancy scheme; (ii) any engagement her Department has had with Ulster University since she became aware of the scheme; and (iii) any alternative options, including funding, restructuring, or other mitigation measures, that were considered with the University, to avoid or reduce job losses.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail to what extent the TransformED curriculum reforms will explicitly promote, support and assess pupils’ creativity, innovation and collaboration across all key stages.
answer_text: The ongoing curriculum reforms are explicitly designed to promote pupils’ creativity, innovation and collaboration across all key stages, not as standalone or generic skills, but as outcomes that arise from highquality curriculum design.Alongside a new statutory curriculum framework, the strategic review of the curriculum recommended that a capabilities framework should be developed that sets out what young people should be able to do and be by the end of compulsory education.   Creativity, innovation and collaboration will be articulated as capabilities that young people are enabled to develop through sustained engagement with strong subject teaching, clear progression and purposeful learning.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has undertaken any assessment of the administrative and financial costs associated with requiring individuals with profound, lifelong, and irreversible medical conditions to undergo repeated reassessments for Personal Independence Payment.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any assessment her Department has made of Ulster University's expenditure on its campuses and operations outside Northern Ireland, including its campus in Qatar, London and Birmingham; and (ii) whether any assessment has been made of whether such expenditure has had any impact on the financial position that has led to the proposed staff redundancy scheme.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 42399/22-27.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how their Department will ensure payments under the proposed redress scheme established under the Inquiry (Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses) and Redress Scheme Bill (NIA Bill 15/22-27) will not impact benefits and pensions.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any discussions she has had with the Police Service of Northern Ireland, regarding its response to reports of anti-social behaviour, including loud music complaints; and (ii) whether any guidance or protocols are in place for officers attending such incidents.
answer_text: PSNI have advised that while statutory noise nuisance enforcement is primarily a matter for local district councils, PSNI may attend incidents reported as loud music/noise where there is a policing purpose required, and will seek to resolve matters, safeguard those affected, and refer or signpost to the relevant council where appropriate. They have also advised that the following guidance documents are available to all officers on the internal PSNI intranet: PO!NT; Powers and Tactical Options to deal with persistent ASB – Awareness building and interventions; Northern Ireland Housing Executive Statement of Policy and Procedures on Anti-Social Behaviour; Anti-Social Behaviour Orders and Acceptable Behaviour Contract and Anti-Social Behavioural Forums Guidance; Proposal for Acceptable Behaviour Contract; Confiscation of Alcohol (Young Persons) Act 1997; On street drinking SOE proforma; Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.The operational response to an investigation of ASB, including how resources are deployed and how officers are guided to respond are operational matters for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the NI Policing Board. Therefore, you may wish to contact the Chief Constable directly regarding PSNI response to ASB incidents, and the guidance provided to officers.Responses to a joint DoJ and Department for Communities (DfC) public consultation on ASB legislation, including issues of street drinking, were published in April 2025 and suggested a need for legislative reform. My officials are working with partners to develop proposals on ASB and drinking in public, with a view to bringing forward revised legislation in the next Assembly mandate, subject to Executive and Assembly approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of job losses proposed by Ulster University under its staff redundancy scheme, broken down by (i) campus; (ii) staff category; and (ii) staff grade.
answer_text: I am aware that Ulster University has announced its intention to commence a consultation process regarding a proposed staff redundancy scheme. I understand this is the start of a process, the details of which have yet to be determined.  I, along with my officials, will continue to engage with the university.		This will be a worrying time for staff and I am working to secure a funding settlement that can deliver a sustainable Further and Higher Education sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of evictions due to anti-social behaviour carried out by (i) the Housing Executive; and (ii) registered housing associations, broken down by Housing Executive area, in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 43068/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether his Department has undertaken an assessment of progress against commitments within the Integrated Education strategy; (ii) any actions being taken to address delays in (a) engagement with schools and local communities in areas of oversubscription or limited provision; (b) the publication of key reports including the Demand for Integrated Education and sectoral profile; and (c) the development of a communication and engagement plan, including updated timelines for delivery.
answer_text: The Department has undertaken ongoing assessment of progress against the commitments set out in the Integrated Education Strategy and Action Plan as part of routine programme management and governance arrangements. Progress is kept under regular review by senior officials and is reported to the Minister.The Department has taken, and continues to take, a number of actions to maintain delivery momentum.(a) Engagement with schools and local communitiesThe Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education (NICIE) leads engagement with schools and local communities, supported by the Education Authority and other partners, particularly in areas of oversubscription or limited integrated provision. Several Integrated schools have recently submitted development proposals for increased enrolment or admissions numbers, which have been approved and officials are aware of a further oversubscribed school that is working on a case for change for increased admission numbers.The Department continues to work with NICIE to prioritise activity and to align engagement with emerging evidence on demand. Launch of a Call for Transformation has been briefly paused to allow for a support programme for those schools who express an interest in Transformation to be developed and for the the Report on Demand for Integrated Education to be finalised. Engagement activity will be phased and targeted as resources allow. (b) Publication of key reportsA report on demand for Integrated Education has taken longer than anticipated, as it represents a new and complex area of analysis. Additional time was required to ensure the robustness of the methodology and to facilitate appropriate consultation and engagement with statutory consultees. This has had a consequential impact on the timing of the sectoral profiling work and a planned call for Transformation, given their interdependency. The Department is working to progress these publications as soon as practicable in the coming weeks, with a view to supporting evidencebased decisionmaking.(c) Communication and engagement planNICIE has been tasked with the development of communication and engagement plans in partnership with the Department; however, the availability of the resources required to facilitate delivery remains constrained in the current financial environment. Progress is also linked to the commissioning of qualitative and quantitative research on the extent of public understanding and knowledge about Integrated Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail Northern Ireland’s participation in the OECD Programme for International Student Assessment 2025 cycle, including (i) whether Northern Ireland schools will take part in the creative thinking assessment component; (ii) the basis on which schools are selected for participation; and (iii) how any findings from the creative thinking domain will be used to inform curriculum and assessment policy.
answer_text: The Programme for International Student Assessment (PISA) measures pupils’ skills and knowledge in mathematics, science and reading.  Each cycle has a major subject domain and two minor domains.  Science was the major domain in the 2025 cycle. In addition, each cycle of PISA has an innovative domain.  In PISA 2022, the innovative domain was creative thinking, however in PISA 2025 the innovative domain was Learning in a Digital World.  In autumn 2025, 104 schools and 3,464 pupils in Northern Ireland participated in PISA.  The sample of students selected is representative of the full population of 15-year-old students in each participating country (or education system). Each country submits a sampling frame to the Organisation for Economic Co-Operation and Development’s (OECD's) international sampling contractor who then validates it. Once a sampling frame is validated, the international contractor draws a scientific random sample of a minimum of 150 schools from each frame with two replacement schools for each original school, unless there are fewer than 150 schools, in which case all schools are sampled. The list of selected schools is delivered to each country’s PISA National Research Coordinator (NRC) (Pearson in the case of Northern Ireland).  The sample chosen reflects a mixture of schools by size, type and socio-economic composition.  This ensures that participants accurately reflect the whole of the country and not just a specific area/type of school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 30950/22-27, to detail whether (i) he has issued any direction to the Curriculum Taskforce regarding the inclusion of evidence-based harm reduction approaches within the development of the statutory curriculum framework; and (ii) he expects the Taskforce to undertake specific engagement with (a) young people; and (b) public health experts, during this process.
answer_text: The Curriculum Taskforce has been commissioned to advise on the content and structure of the new statutory curriculum framework. The Taskforce is supported by subject-specific working groups, which have drawn on disciplinary research evidence to inform the drafting process. As part of curriculum reform, a broader, knowledge-rich approach to health and wellbeing education is being developed.Engagement with young people, alongside other stakeholders, has informed the development of a draft Capabilities Framework, particularly in shaping priorities related to emotional understanding and wellbeing.I expect to receive a draft curriculum framework in the coming weeks and intend to launch a full public consultation before the summer, providing opportunities for a wide range of stakeholders to engage fully with the proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of SENDIST appeals awaiting hearing dates for over 26 weeks; and (ii) the number of appeals in excess of one year, subsequent to an appeal being lodged.
answer_text: This question was transferred to my office as the Special Educational Needs and Disability Tribunal (SENDIST) operates under the Department of Justice. I am advised that, as of 20 April 2026:There are no appeals awaiting a hearing date over 26 weeks.There are 35 appeals which have been ongoing for more than one year since being lodged. 23 appeals relate to Special Education Needs (SEN) and 12 are Disability Discrimination (DIS) appeals. These are live cases and resolution is dependent upon the timely cooperation and information flows from the appellant but also partner organisations such as the Education Authority and the Department of Education.For context, in 2025/26 SENDIST received 1,056 appeals, a 49% increase on the previous year, and 79.5% of appeals were disposed of within 15 weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any current statutory and departmental guidance in place for schools to ensure that teaching and pastoral staff are informed when a pupil has experienced bereavement or a similarly significant life event; and (ii) how this guidance ensures consistent pastoral support across all schools.
answer_text: There is no requirement for a family / caregiver to notify school staff when a pupil has experienced bereavement or other significant life event.Should a school become aware of a bereavement or any other significant life event that is affecting a child, the Education Authority provides guidance to schools on supporting pupils, including advice on sharing relevant information with teaching and pastoral staff (with family consent) and putting appropriate support arrangements in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any enforcement actions taken by the Charity Commission for Northern Ireland where charities fail to submit annual accounts and returns within the required timeframe, including (i) the average timescale for the initiation of such enforcement action; (ii) the number of charities currently recorded as overdue on the public register, with a breakdown of those overdue by more than (a) 6 months; (b) 12 months; and (c) 24 months; (ii) any steps being taken by the Charity Commission for Northern Ireland to progress cases of prolonged non-compliance with a

To ask the Minister of Education to detail the stakeholders referred to in his Oral Statement to the Assembly on 3 March 2026, regarding the need for general qualifications offered in Northern Ireland to be valued by key stakeholders, including (i) any criteria used to identify those stakeholders; and (ii) any engagement undertaken with them, as part of the TransformEd programme.
answer_text: Whilst I did not use the phrase “the need for general qualifications offered in Northern Ireland to be valued by key stakeholders” in my oral statement, there is no doubt as to the importance of this principle. Ensuring that qualifications offered in Northern Ireland are understood, valued and respected by all stakeholders underpins my Department’s work in this area, although it is undoubtedly the case that the input of teachers, school leaders, pupils and parents are key to the process of qualification reform. This is why my Department conducted significant, wide-ranging consultation throughout 2025, with further events held in January 2026. The consultation was promoted extensively in order to encourage engagement from as broad a range of stakeholders as possible. Further details of the engagement undertaken with stakeholders, including teachers, school leaders, pupils and parents is available in the Consultation Report- Future of CCEA GCSE, AS level and A level Qualifications:https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/consultations/consultation-future-ccea-qualificationsFurthermore, my Policy Framework for General Qualifications in Northern Ireland (3 March) will form the basis for CCEA to develop a new suite of high-quality, up-to-date qualifications at GCSE and A level that will serve future generations of pupils and young people. This development work will be informed by significant subject-specific engagement with stakeholders across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of students who sat each subject at (i) GCSE; and (ii) A-Level, for each of the last ten years, broken down by (a) subject; (b) year; and (c) school type.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the rationale for maintaining a half fare concessionary travel scheme for people with disabilities, while free travel is provided for in the Republic of Ireland and other UK regions; (ii) whether her Department has undertaken any recent assessment of the economic, social and employment benefits of introducing free public transport for people with disabilities; and (iii) whether she will review and bring forward proposals to extend free travel to all people with disabilities, including a timeframe for any changes.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the disparity between the travel concessions available to disabled people in the North and that provided across these islands. While the budgetary outlook has not changed, I had planned to reconsider the option to extend half fare travel to free travel again as part of my 2025/26 budget allocation decisions but due to a major ticketing systems upgrade in Translink, this was not possible. Officials are actively working in close collaboration with Translink to consider how this policy could be implemented.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether (i) his Department is considering, as part of a new curriculum as being developed through TransformEd, the delivery of a preventative education-based approach to tackling ending violence against women and girls; and (ii) any assessment has been made of whether current education policy is sufficiently focused on prevention, rather than responding to incidents after they occur.
answer_text: The current curriculum, from Foundation Stage to Key Stage 4, provides opportunities for children and young people to develop the knowledge, skills and attitudes needed to form healthy and respectful relationships. While a specific preventative educationbased programme focused explicitly on ending violence against women and girls is not a discrete focus of the TransformED curriculum review, the new curriculum framework will continue to provide opportunities to support learning in this area.Officials keep education policy under ongoing review. The curriculum, alongside schools’ wider pastoral and safeguarding arrangements, supports prevention and early intervention by promoting positive relationships, respectful behaviour and wellbeing.As officials take forward the Healthy Relationships Action Plan within the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Delivery Plan 2026/28, they will consider the role of educationbased, evidenceinformed approaches, including how existing resources can best support schools and education settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to his statement on 3 March 2026 that Northern Ireland intends to keep the qualifications market open, allowing awarding organisations from England and Wales to offer their GCSEs and A-levels where they meet the required criteria, to detail (i) whether all such qualifications currently meet the required criteria; and (ii) the full criteria used to determine whether qualifications are approved for use in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Yes, the GCSEs and A levels from England and Wales that are currently approved for use in Northern Ireland schools meet the required criteria and will therefore continue to be approved for use.The full criteria are set out in my Policy Framework for General Qualifications in Northern Ireland of 3 March 2026:https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/policy-framework-general-qualifications-northern-ireland

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any steps that his Department and Health and Social Care Trusts are taking to ensure that patients with severe and high-risk mental health conditions receive timely access to psychiatric review, continuity of care, and appropriate follow-up; (ii) the number of complaints or reports have been received regarding delays in medication management, lack of assigned key workers, or discharge from services due to missed appointments arising from administrative or communication failures, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Tr

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any discussions with the Minister of Education in relation to the provision of nursing cover for special school summer schemes; (ii) the date on which the issue of nursing provision was first raised with his Department; (iii) any requests that were made by the Minister of Education or the Education Authority to secure nursing support, including any actions that were taken by his Department in response to those requests;  (iv) whether any workforce planning, including consideration of Registered Nurses in Learning Disability or the use of agency or ba
answer_text: Appropriate nursing support has been provided to support summer schemes in Special Schools for many years, with a number of different models of delivery operating depending on the identified need. Not all children attending these schemes require nursing support, but around 20% of children attending SEN schools with complex health care needs require a higher level of support with healthcare interventions.Concerns over the delivery of SEN summer schemes were first brought to my attention at a joint meeting with the National Association of Head Teachers (NAHT) on 3 February 2026, which I attended along with the Minister of Education.  Among other issues discussed at that meeting, concerns were raised about the impact on Principals of ensuring safe delivery of the summer schemes, this did not however include any suggestion that a nurse would be required on each site.On 2 March 2026, my officials met with the Education Authority (EA) and it was at that point that EA first shared its view that a nurse was required on each summer scheme site to provide oversight and healthcare interventions for children with complex needs in those settings. This level of nursing provision has not been in place in previous years and indeed is currently not the case during term time for many of the children who will be attending summer schemes.At that meeting on 2 March 2026, my Department’s Chief Nursing Officer, confirmed that in her clinical professional opinion, onsite nursing support for all SEN Summer Schemes is not considered necessary, a view supported by senior nurse leaders within HSCTs.  To allow the continuation of the SEN Summer Schemes my officials engaged with the Department of Education (DE) and EA to explore appropriate nursing cover options. Those options would have provided an enhanced level of support including additional onsite training for classroom assistants and also direct telephone access to a registered Community Children’s Nurse (CCN) familiar with that cohort of children during the school/scheme day as required.  My officials began the process of identifying the best means of delivering the Summer Schemes safely, and in line with the clinical assessment of need, this involved planning for training of classroom assistants to deliver the necessary healthcare under the Delegation Framework, with appropriate support from the relevant HSC Trust’s CCN Service.EA did not accept this offer, and in the absence of their desired resolution, they proceeded to make an announcement of the cancellation of the schemes for summer 2026 on the grounds of safety on 25 March 2026.Recognising the importance of these Summer Schemes to the children attending them and their families, I have since tasked the Chief Nursing Officer to work with the five regional HSC Trust nursing leaders to develop plans to meet the request of a registered nurse on each site to ensure the summer schemes can progress this year. Whilst extremely challenging, my Department is committed to m

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the current level of nursing provision in all special schools during the standard school term; (ii) any measures in place to ensure the medical needs of pupils with complex care requirements are met safely in schools, without on site registered nurses; and (iii) any justification for requiring on site nursing cover for special school summer schemes, while many special schools operate during the term without full-time on-site registered nurses; and (iv) any steps his Department is taking to ensure the safety of pupils in those schools.
answer_text: EA has provided the following information;The current level of nursing provision in all special schools during the standard school termDuring the normal school term seven schools have a nurse on site with the remaining schools relying on other provisions provided by the Health and Social Care (HSC).Any measures in place to ensure the medical needs of pupils with complex care requirements are met safely in schools, without on site registered nurses; Children and young people who require health and social care interventions will receive appropriate care by the HSC. The HSC can, if appropriate, delegate tasks to education staff, mostly classroom assistants. Any justification for requiring on site nursing cover for special school summer schemes, while many special schools operate during the term without full-time on-site registered nurses;During the summer schemes the normal staff are not available. This means other staff need trained in healthcare interventions. Due to limited time available a nurse on site is required for 2026 to ensure the safety of children and young people. any steps his Department is taking to ensure the safety of pupils in those schools.Officials from my Department and Department of Health, along with representatives from the Education Authority and the Public Health Agency (PHA), have commenced work to develop a new model of care in special schools that best meets the healthcare needs of these children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any discussions with the Minister of Health regarding the provision of nursing cover for special school summer schemes, including (i) the date on which the issue of nursing provision was first raised with his Department; (ii) any requests made by his Department or the Education Authority to the Minister of Health to secure nursing support; (iii) any actions taken by his Department or the Education Authority to support or facilitate those requests; (iv) any steps taken to communicate with parents, carers, or scheme providers regarding potential impacts
answer_text: (i) EA officials engaged with my department and DoH for many months to find solutions to healthcare in special schools.  Officials advised me on 3 February 2026 that challenges around, inter alia, on-site nursing provision created a risk to the safe running of summer schemes in 2026.  On Tuesday 24 March 2026, DoH informed my department in writing that the required nursing cover would not be available. If it is not safe to deliver the schemes, the EA had no choice but to cancel them. Since the original decision DoH has confirmed nursing cover, which reduces the healthcare risks making the schemes safe to run.(ii) EA, DoH and my department engaged extensively through meetings and written communications, discussing healthcare staffing, oversight, and training needs. Senior EA officials have engaged directly with senior DoH officials, however, no resolution was found and DoH confirmed on Tuesday 24 March that they were unable to provide solutions as required. As advised above, since the original decision DoH has confirmed nursing cover, which reduces the healthcare risks making the schemes safe to run.(iii) EA officials engaged with my department and DoH for many months to find solutions to healthcare in special schools. Considering the timing the only safe option for summer 2026 was nurse on site.(iv) On 27 March 2026 the EA’s Chief Transformation Officer wrote to Special Schools to update them on the joint working between Education and Health and highlighted that appropriate nursing provision will be in place to support pupils who require healthcare support during Special School Summer Schemes in 2026.  A copy of the Minister’s announcement is on the department's website at Ministers welcome solution enabling Special School Summer Schemes to proceed | Department of Education.(v) EA has confirmed that the primary reasons for the schemes not to be able to proceed was a lack of nursing cover and appropriate staffing levels. EA and DoH continue working jointly on sustainable nurse-led healthcare models in special schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any provisions in place to support pupils for whom English is an additional language, including all dedicated programmes and support services.
answer_text: The Department uses the term ‘Newcomer’ to refer to a pupil who has enrolled in a school, but who does not have satisfactory language skills to participate fully in the school curriculum and does not have a language in common with the teacher.  The Department works alongside the Education Authority (EA), particularly the Intercultural Education Service, in providing support to schools and families of Newcomer children for whom English is an additional language.The Department provides additional funding to schools with Newcomer children enrolled at census date.  Schools receive additional funding through the Department’s Common Funding Formula (CFF) into their Local Management of Schools (LMS) budget.  An additional funding premium of ½ Average Weighed Pupil Unit (AWPU) is provided per Newcomer pupil, per annum. This funding allows schools to support the additional needs of this cohort of children and young people, and many schools utilise this funding to employ an English as an Additional Language teacher, or to ‘buy in’ accredited English for Speakers of Other Languages (ESOL) courses in Post Primary schools.The EA delivers support for pupils for whom English is an Additional Language (EAL) consistently across all Education Authority districts through a needsled, inclusive model. Under the Local IMPACT Teams (LITs) approach, support is provided to all children based on identified need rather than diagnosis or background. For the month of March 2026, the EA’s Local Impact Team has received 1203 requests for involvement, and of these 75 (6%) indicated the child’s first language is not English.In terms of more targeted supports, the EA’s Intercultural Education Service (IES) provides advice, guidance and training to schools with Newcomer pupils.  Teachers can access this support service via the IES Help Desk telephone and email enquiry pathways at any time; all requests are answered within 48 hours and school visits or family support meeting can be arranged, including the provision of an interpreter, if required.IES has a small team of teachers who provide teaching, learning and curriculum advice to schools with Newcomer pupils across Northern Ireland. IES also provides initial support around how to record initial assessments using the CEFR (Common European Framework of Reference for Languages), as well managing the regional translation and interpreting service for schools.  The team also deliver on-line toolkits for diversity in pre-school, primary and post-primary, as well as an OCN Level 1 certificate in Intercultural Education for teachers and classroom assistants.Within the overall EA budget, IES supports for Newcomers account for approximately £600k per annum in direct teacher time support to schools, with translation and interpreting accounting for a further £120k per annum.  This is a regional support service which is not broken down into localities or districts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether, in light of the use of assessed oral components within the International Baccalaureate, he considers that issues relating to (a) reliability; (b) workload; and (c) scalability, remain barriers to their inclusion in statutory assessment; and (ii) any evidence underpinning that assessment.
answer_text: Statutory Assessment in Northern Ireland is currently undertaken at the end of Key Stages 1 - 3, when pupils are typically aged 8, 11 and 14.  The International Baccalaureate (IB) uses assessed oral components in a formal manner in the Diploma Programme, for students aged 16 - 19.  By comparison, the IB Primary Years Programme (Ages 3–12) does not use formal or externally moderated oral assessments. Instead, oral assessment is part of ongoing informal classroom-based assessment in the same way that teachers in Northern Ireland will reflect on pupil learning based on oral contributions.The published report from the Independent Panel Supporting Progress for Every Child - Transforming Statutory Assessment: An Independent Review for Northern Ireland sets out the evidence underpinning their recommendation that oracy should not be included in statutory assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how programmes and support services to support pupils for whom English is an additional language are delivered across each Education Authority district.
answer_text: The Department uses the term ‘Newcomer’ to refer to a pupil who has enrolled in a school, but who does not have satisfactory language skills to participate fully in the school curriculum and does not have a language in common with the teacher.  The Department works alongside the Education Authority (EA), particularly the Intercultural Education Service, in providing support to schools and families of Newcomer children for whom English is an additional language.The Department provides additional funding to schools with Newcomer children enrolled at census date.  Schools receive additional funding through the Department’s Common Funding Formula (CFF) into their Local Management of Schools (LMS) budget.  An additional funding premium of ½ Average Weighed Pupil Unit (AWPU) is provided per Newcomer pupil, per annum. This funding allows schools to support the additional needs of this cohort of children and young people, and many schools utilise this funding to employ an English as an Additional Language teacher, or to ‘buy in’ accredited English for Speakers of Other Languages (ESOL) courses in Post Primary schools.The EA delivers support for pupils for whom English is an Additional Language (EAL) consistently across all Education Authority districts through a needsled, inclusive model. Under the Local IMPACT Teams (LITs) approach, support is provided to all children based on identified need rather than diagnosis or background. For the month of March 2026, the EA’s Local Impact Team has received 1203 requests for involvement, and of these 75 (6%) indicated the child’s first language is not English.In terms of more targeted supports, the EA’s Intercultural Education Service (IES) provides advice, guidance and training to schools with Newcomer pupils.  Teachers can access this support service via the IES Help Desk telephone and email enquiry pathways at any time; all requests are answered within 48 hours and school visits or family support meeting can be arranged, including the provision of an interpreter, if required.IES has a small team of teachers who provide teaching, learning and curriculum advice to schools with Newcomer pupils across Northern Ireland. IES also provides initial support around how to record initial assessments using the CEFR (Common European Framework of Reference for Languages), as well managing the regional translation and interpreting service for schools.  The team also deliver on-line toolkits for diversity in pre-school, primary and post-primary, as well as an OCN Level 1 certificate in Intercultural Education for teachers and classroom assistants.Within the overall EA budget, IES supports for Newcomers account for approximately £600k per annum in direct teacher time support to schools, with translation and interpreting accounting for a further £120k per annum.  This is a regional support service which is not broken down into localities or districts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of (i) ring-fenced; and (ii) non-ring-fenced, funding that has been allocated to programmes and support services that are delivered to support pupils for whom English is an additional language in each of the last five years, broken down by Education Authority district.
answer_text: The Department uses the term ‘Newcomer’ to refer to a pupil who has enrolled in a school, but who does not have satisfactory language skills to participate fully in the school curriculum and does not have a language in common with the teacher. Teachers can access this support service via the IES Help Desk telephone and email enquiry pathways at any time; all requests are answered within 48 hours and school visits or family support meeting can be arranged, including the provision of an interpreter, if required.IES has a small team of teachers who provide teaching, learning and curriculum advice to schools with Newcomer pupils across Northern Ireland.
