question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail whether (i) their Department has considered, or is currently considering, the introduction of a structured integration programme for newly arrived migrants, including refugees, incorporating English language provision and civic orientation on living and working in Northern Ireland, including awareness of rights and responsibilities, equality legislation, and principles such as gender equality; and (ii) will they consider any comparable schemes in other countries, such as Norway, with a view to supporting social integration, particip
answer_text: The Executive’s Refugee Integration Strategy (RIS) sets out the importance of, and our approach to, supporting successful integration for those who arrive here seeking protection.  Additionally, we launched a public consultation on a draft Framework for Race Relations on 11th March 2026 and recognise there are opportunities to align delivery to address shared needs and maximise outcomes. Both of these incorporate actions around English for Speakers of Other Languages (ESOL) and in the first year of delivery of the RIS we have prioritised the establishment of Community Hubs in each Council area to provide support to migrants.  The Hubs act as a focal point for integration and, as a minimum, include signposting to key service provision, ESOL and orientation.  Alongside this, and to support delivery, The Executive Office led, multi-agency Community Cohesion Group (CCG) is developing scalable interventions to help tackle the root causes of hate and build cohesion in our local communities.  This includes consideration of a structured orientation programme.We will of course continue to learn from other jurisdictions to inform delivery of this important work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 42579/22-27, to detail (i) how he can demonstrate that proposals to close small rural schools deliver a net financial benefit once all associated costs are included; and (ii) whether he will publish a system-wide cost-benefit analysis covering transport, capital investment, staffing, and community impacts.
answer_text: (i) Proposals for significant changes, including closures or amalgamations of rural schools, are brought forward through the statutory Development Proposal (DP) process and each DP is considered based on its own individual circumstances.  It is for the proposer of a DP to make the case for change by providing an evidence-based assessment of the school or schools involved against the Sustainable Schools Policy criteria, including detailing the potential costs and benefits of the proposal.  My Department does not routinely calculate savings that accrue from the implementation of area solutions.(ii) The costs and benefits of individual proposals are considered in the context of the Sustainable Schools Policy criteria.  I have no plans currently to commission a system-wide cost-benefit analysis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how the Education Authority’s SEN reform proposal to reduce reliance on one-to-one classroom assistant support aligns with its assurance that pupils who require such support will continue to receive it; and (ii) whether any criteria used to determine a child's level of need for one-to-one support will change.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:Every pupil who needs individual classroom support will continue to receive it.The Enhanced Support Model does not change the legislative criteria relating to statutory assessments, and all legal protections including the right to appeal to Tribunal will remain. Any decisions on how support is provided in the new model, will only be determined after the consultation has closed and the EA has fully analysed stakeholder feedback.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of instances of administration of rapid tranquillisation or other acute behavioural management sedative medication without a patient’s consent that have been recorded in psychiatric hospitals, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of incidents involving the discovery or seizure of controlled or non-prescribed drugs that have been recorded within psychiatric hospitals, in each of the last five years, broken down by hospital.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any reason why approximately £25 million per year continues to be spent on permanent bedroom tax mitigation, rather than being redirected toward the construction of one-bedroom social housing, given departmental estimates that such funding could deliver around 300 units annually and reduce structural reliance on houses in multiple occupation.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Assembly passed legislation to extend the Social Sector Size Criteria mitigation scheme indefinitely on 8 February 2022.The Executive agreed this should continue following the Welfare Mitigation Schemes Statutory Report that was laid in the Assembly in December 2024.It would be a decision for the Executive to cease mitigation payments in respect of the Social Sector Size Criteria and for the Assembly to pass any required legislation to this effect.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration she has given to introducing measures to prevent the misuse of parking spaces designated for parents with young children, including (i) improved enforcement mechanisms; and (ii) the potential introduction of a permit or badge scheme to demonstrate eligibility.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my response to AQW 40391/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the rationale as to why patients receiving inpatient psychiatric care, who are identified by clinicians as potentially having conditions such as ADHD, are not able to access formal diagnostic assessment within that setting; and (ii) any assessment that has been made on whether this delays appropriate treatment and undermines patient outcomes.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment that has been made of the effectiveness of trauma-informed safeguarding practices within abortion and reproductive healthcare services, particularly in identifying and supporting individuals experiencing sexual abuse, coercion, or incest; and (ii) whether any data is available on how often such concerns are detected and acted upon.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of times patients receiving inpatient psychiatric care have been transferred between hospitals, in each of the last five years; and (ii) in how many of those cases the transfer occurred without the patient’s consent.
answer_text: The transfer of patients between hospitals is governed by the Regional Clinical Resource Efficiency Support Team (CREST) Protocol for Inter-Hospital Transfer of Patients and their Records, as well as the Regional Bed Management Protocol for Acute Psychiatric Beds. These regional protocols ensure that transfers are conducted safely, consistently, and in accordance with best practice across all Trusts.Under the Regional Bed Management Protocol, admission to an acute psychiatric bed outside a patient’s home Trust should occur only when absolutely necessary, in circumstances where; there is no available local bed at the time of assessment; and the clinical assessment clearly indicates that enhanced bedbased care is required.The way in which transfer data has historically been recorded means that it is not easily extractable and reported in the specific form requested, without substantial manual review.Furthermore, since the implementation of the new electronic patient record system, Encompass, Trusts have been working through a number of data validation issues. As a result, producing accurate figures for all five years would require extensive manual extraction and verification, involving considerable staff time and resource.Although the specific data requested is not easily reportable in aggregate form, the relevant information is recorded within individual patient records. In addition, the Acute Mental Health Bed Dashboard captures and monitors daily data regarding the number of patients admitted from other Trusts and the number of local patients placed in other Trusts’ beds. However, the dashboard does not track or identify individual patient transfers over the fiveyear period in the way required to answer the question directly.Every effort is made to communicate with patients and their carers, to involve them in decisionmaking, and to obtain their consent for transfer wherever possible. However, for patients who are detained under mental health legislation, consent is not always required. Decisions in these circumstances are made on the basis of clinical need, assessment of risk, and the legal framework in place to safeguard the patient and others. Even in such cases, staff will continue to seek to engage, to explain, and to involve patients to the fullest extent appropriate and practicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) lessons that have been identified from recent meningitis clusters elsewhere in the UK; and (ii) actions that have been taken to mitigate the risk of similar outbreaks locally.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) led the operational response to meningitis, working closely with the Department of Health.  The PHA have been an invited member of the UKHSA Incident Management Team (IMT) established in response to the recent meningococcal cluster in Kent and is keeping the situation in Northern Ireland under close review.  To date there is no evidence of spread beyond the southeast of England.As this IMT remains ongoing, a full debrief and formal lessons learned report is expected in due course.  However early emerging themes have included the importance of timely statutory notification of suspected meningococcal disease. Technical aspects of the incident, including pathogen characteristics, transmission dynamics, and findings from epidemiological investigations, etc will be set out in the UKHSA outbreak report and related research outputs. The PHA has taken a proactive approach to strengthening local preparedness and reducing the risk of delayed recognition or response including issuing formal communications to clinicians reminding them of their legal obligations under the Public Health Act (Northern Ireland) 1967 to notify suspected cases of specified infectious diseases, including meningococcal disease.  PHA are also carrying out training with primary care and have also engaged with education leaders to ensure they have clear information.   Ahead of the Kent incident, PHA worked with Encompass to introduce onscreen reminders for clinicians placing clinical orders relevant to bacterial meningitis. This helps support timely notification and early public health action.PHA and HSC Trusts have been reviewing local capability for rapid mobilisation of responses as part of wider pandemic and incident preparedness planning.The PHA Immunisation Team, along with other UK devolved administrations, have been linking with UKHSA via the MenB Vaccine Response Meetings to keep up to date with vaccination activity as part of the broader outbreak response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of classroom assistant posts that have been advertised, but not filled, in each of the last three years; and (ii) whether his Department has carried out any assessment of the reasons for posts remaining unfilled.
answer_text: The table below provides details of the number of classroom assistant posts that have been advertised by the Education Authority (EA), but not filled, in each of the last three years.202320242025Total number of openings advertised.1,8812,2292,246Total number of candidates hired.1,110 1,281 1,448Total number not filled 771948798% of openings filled59%57%64%Assessment and analysis of vacancies is continually carried out by the EA Resourcing Service and the main challenges in filling the posts have been identified as follows:Capacity - increasing Special Educational Needs numbers has significantly increased the demand for Classroom Assistants.Competition - highly competitive job market for posts, with candidates being attracted to many other large employers including health and retail.Contract types - Contract terms are not considered to be attractive, narrowing the candidate pool, due to a high proportion of temporary and part-time posts.Job Role - perception of the job in the current model as a ‘community-based’ role with candidates focused on working in their local schools or nearby schools.  This limits the pool of candidates.The EA is currently progressing a strategic review of the Classroom Assistant employment model, in line with the SEN Transformation Programme.  The scope of this review will include all Classroom Assistant roles and will include job descriptions, contracts and a range of other employment-related matters including qualifications, professional standards and professional development for Classroom Assistants. The outcome of a such a review will better support workforce stability, enhance recruitment and retention, and ultimately deliver improved outcomes for staff and children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 37503/22-27, for an update on the Violence at Work policy he has referenced, including (i) whether the policy has now been formally launched; (ii) to detail a summary of the key measures and guidance it contains to protect teachers and classroom assistants from violence; (iii) any specific support and interventions that have been implemented in schools experiencing repeat or high-volume incidents since his last update; and (iv) whether there is a timeline for evaluating the effectiveness of the policy in reducing assaults on school staff.
answer_text: As highlighted in the referenced AQW 37503, my Department is not the employers of teachers or classroom assistants. Work related accidents or assaults on school staff are not reported to my Department but instead are reported to the Education Authority (EA).We have again asked the EA for a response to this question, and they have provided response to each part of your question.The issuing of the Preventing Violence at Work Framework is at a very advanced stage.  The EA is currently working with Trade Union side to reach agreement before it can be formally launched.This framework and guidance has been collaboratively developed across EA to ensure, that so far as is reasonably practicable, that EA staff, do not become subject to violence from other adults  (members of public, parents, other staff) or pupils whilst in the workplace or on any premises or vehicle for which the EA has responsibility.  The objective of this framework is to provide advice and guidance and to reduce the risk of workplace violence to as low a level as is reasonably practicable. The EA provides a range of staff training offers to support those schools experiencing repeat or high-volume incidents.The EA’s Local Impact Teams provide tailored support based on the school’s evidence and assessment of need. This may include:		Team around the school providing localised support and training.		Short-term intensive 1:1 or small group interventions.			Targeted small group support or training for school staff to model interventions.			Partnered support – modelling, training, CA development, co-teaching.		A Regional Advice and Guidance telephone helpline 028 9031 7777 (option 2) – no longer required as a prerequisite to an RFI (Request for Involvement).		Social, Behavioural, Emotional and Wellbeing (SBEW) training via the SDS Portal – A suite of SBEW training modules currently available to schools. EA Learning Portal : EA Bookings		Training delivered by Pupil Wellbeing Teams – Targeted professional learning to support schools in managing complex behaviours and promoting whole-school wellbeing approaches.•     Bespoke Training – schools may request tailored training packages aligned to their specific needs, including support around high-risk behaviour and deescalation strategies.The EA has developed a bespoke training programme for accredited safe handling training for staff working within Specialist Provisions in Mainstream Schools (SPiMS), called Connecting and Caring Across the Whole School Community – a wholes school framework to prevent, relate, support and respond to behaviours that require intervention. This programme was delivered as a pilot to over 60 SPiMS Term 3 of 2023/2024, with the revised programme delivered to a number of specialist provisions in February 2025. This training will be available to all settings in 2026.SEND and Youth Directorate launched the Protocol for Special Schools in October 2025.  This was coproduced with Special Schools and allows these schools to se

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any targeted public health measures that are in place to protect students and young people from meningitis, including (i) awareness campaigns; (ii) on-campus vaccination access; and (iii) guidance for further and higher education institutions.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) is leading the operational response to meningitis, working closely with the Department of Health.  The PHA is closely linked into the UKHSA response to the outbreak in Kent, attending Incident Management Team meetings, and is keeping the situation in Northern Ireland under close review.  To date there is no evidence of spread beyond the south east of England.The PHA worked closely with a school in Belfast that had a suspected case which proved to be negative, providing advice to the school, parents and staff.  The Agency also met with the Departments of Education and Economy, Education Authority and the two NI universities to provide advice and public health information.  Public health information and advice is available on the PHA website at: PHA statement - meningitis | HSC Public Health Agency and on NI Direct at: Meningitis | nidirect and the public have been urged to remain vigilant for the signs and symptoms of meningitis and to act fast if they suspect they may be symptomatic.  Vaccination of eligible students returning from the University of Kent will be undertaken by GP practices, not on campus.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, as part of ongoing assessment reform under TransformED, to detail the extent to which he is considering the use of oral presentations, structured discussion, and debate as methods of assessing pupil learning across the curriculum.
answer_text: On 5 March I welcomed the publication of the Independent Review of Assessment, commissioned in June 2025 as part of my TransformED Strategy. The report provides the evidence needed to redesign statutory assessment in Northern Ireland, setting out a clear pathway to the end of Key Stage 3.  My Department will publish its response and implementation proposals in the coming weeks.The Panel recommends statutory assessments that are highvalue, lowworkload and technically robust and new assessment formats will be developed in line with these principles.  Although recognising the importance of oracy, the Panel advises that it should not form a standalone statutory assessment, as this could increase workload burdens, present challenges for reliable measurement at scale and risk narrowing practice.However, teachers will continue to use oracybased assessment in everyday practice to support and monitor pupils’ reasoning and communication abilities. Oracy will also be reflected in the aims and content of the new curriculum, with supporting materials and professional development for teachers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of complaints received regarding failure to implement special educational provision, in the last year.
answer_text: The Department can advise that there were no complaints received through the formal complaints procedure; however the Department receives a very high volume of correspondence on the issue of special educational needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has made any assessment of the consistency of effectiveness of Boards of Governors across schools.
answer_text: Once appointed, each Board of Governors is a body corporate and is responsible for its decisions and as a result there is no overall assessment of consistency across schools.In order to assist Boards, the Department has published a Governors Guide which is available online and from each school.  In addition, the Education Authority provides a suite of governor training courses, including a number which are sector specific.  Courses are mainly available online and through webinars and can be accessed outside of ‘normal’ working hours. The Northern Ireland Audit Office has published advice online for Board effectiveness, ‘School Governance - A Good Practice Guide’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department plans to take to ensure that Emergency Departments implement systems to prevent patients from missing their call due to not hearing their name being announced, including (i) consideration of visual display boards showing patient names or queue status; and (ii) other inclusive communication methods for those with hearing difficulties.
answer_text: The Department of Health is committed to ensuring that Emergency Departments (EDs) have effective and inclusive systems in place so that patients, particularly those who are deaf or hard of hearing, do not miss their call for assessment.Across all Health and Social Care Trusts, patient communication needs are recorded on systems such as EPIC, where hearing difficulties can be flagged for the attention of clinical and reception staff. This enables staff to take additional steps, such as entering the waiting area to locate the patient directly, where there is a risk that an audible call may not be heard.Health and Social Care Trusts have advised of a range of measures currently in place or under development:Belfast Health and Social Care Trust – Adult EDsThe Trust has established a Task and Finish Group to review patient-calling systems. The group has identified gaps for patients who may not hear audible announcements and is actively considering more inclusive options, including the potential introduction of visual display systems and clearer presentation of realtime waiting information.ED staff are also undertaking British Sign Language (BSL) training to strengthen communication with patients who are deaf or hard of hearing.Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick ChildrenAudible calling remains the primary system; however, the ED is undergoing major redevelopment that will provide opportunities to modernise calling systems and explore visual or other inclusive solutions. Staff BSL training is also being considered as part of wider accessibility improvements.Northern Health and Social Care TrustWhere communication issues are known, these are flagged on EPIC and patients are called twice before being departed. Staff also cover all waiting areas to ensure calls are not missed. The Trust is progressing work to enhance patient information screens, but due to the complexity and size of the Antrim and Causeway hospital sites, it has advised that implementing a single standardised calling system within each of the two sites is not currently feasible.South Eastern Health and Social Care TrustThe Emergency Department has implemented several inclusive communications supports, including:A tannoy system in the waiting room and outside the ED entrance;A LOOP system for hearing aid users;Processes for reception staff to highlight declared hearing impairments to triage nurses, who will then enter the waiting area to visually call the patient; andAccess to sign language interpreter services.The Trust has advised that visual display boards are not currently in place due to footprint constraints and the rapidly changing nature of clinical prioritisation, which would limit the reliability of static displays. The Trust continues to explore further accessibility improvements.Southern Health and Social Care TrustEmergency Departments call patients three times, followed by a phone call if there is no response. EPIC includes a prominent alert banner for hearing difficulties, prom

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the increase in pupil suspensions since 2019-2020, to detail whether any assessment has been made of the underlying causes of this rise, including whether it reflects a deterioration in pupil behaviour, or the effectiveness of current disciplinary approaches.
answer_text: My department has undertaken a review of the existing suspension and expulsion arrangements.Work carried out as part of this Review focused on the broader legislative and administrative framework governing suspensions. While it did not include a detailed examination of the underlying behavioural causes of recent trends, stakeholders did highlight broader issues relating to behaviour, early intervention, and the challenges faced by schools in supporting pupils with complex needs.A more detailed assessment of the root causes of changing suspension patterns, including whether this reflects shifts in pupil behaviour or the effectiveness of current disciplinary approaches, would form part of future work on behaviour and supporting systems, subject to my consideration and available resources.I am currently considering the Review, including the recommendations for change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether his Department has carried out any assessment of whether short-term, uncertain funding for youth services undermines the community approach of the RAISE programme; and (ii) any steps he will take to ensure those services are not lost.
answer_text: Funding for Locality Led Projects within the RAISE Programme is provided by the Shared Island Fund outside of the normal Executive budgeting process and is available until August 2027.  Delivery organisations have secure funding during this period.  No assessment has been made to ascertain whether the approach to funding for youth services has any effect on the community approach of the RAISE Programme.  The Education Authority (EA) took a decision to award successful applicants, to the EA Funding Scheme for Regional and Local Voluntary Youth Organisations, 3/12ths of their requested allocation to cover the first quarter’s work commencing on 1 April 2026.  There are no sustainability or viability tests included in the registration, eligibility or application processes. Community and voluntary sector organisations are advised on sustainability through support from the Department for Communities, with many of these organisations receiving funding from other sources.As you are aware, Executive departments’ budgets for 2026-27 and beyond have not yet been agreed by the Northern Ireland Executive and therefore my Department is unable to provide a commitment in respect of funding beyond March 2026, either for existing activities or for any proposed additional actions, until the Executive has formally confirmed the final opening Budget for 2026–27.  Current indications are that the Education sector is facing a projected funding gap of c£0.6 billion in 2026-27, increasing to c£0.9 billion in 2028-29.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any measures in place to ensure that all schools have adequate library provision, including dedicated library spaces, trained librarians, and up-to-date reading resources; (ii) any steps his Department takes to monitor and evaluate the impact of library provision on pupils’ literacy and learning outcomes; and (iii) any plans in place to address gaps where schools currently lack these facilities or support.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) maintains the Education Library Service (ELS) to fulfil its statutory duty to make library services available to grant-aided schools and other grant-aided educational establishments (other than institutions of further education). The ELS provides support to schools that request advice and guidance in the development and maintenance of their library provision.  In the 2025-2026 school year to date, advice and support has been provided to 442 schools.  Some schools also work in partnership with Libraries NI where a local public library is close to their school.The School Building Handbook sets out the requirements for dedicated library facilities in all new schools to ensure that adequate library provision is allocated in accordance with the school's enrolment.Schools use a range of self-evaluation frameworks to review their provision. Where a need is identified, the ELS is available to provide: advice to support the development of the library in the school; training and support to staff; and advice on what books and resources to make available in a library. The ELS is now offering a new digital library management system (ELMS) to all schools, and a large-scale training programme is currently under way. This system is available free of charge at the point of delivery to all schools including EOTAS Centres and statutory nursery settings in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any steps he is taking to ensure that nursery schools are recognised as providing statutory early education, rather than simply childcare, in the Executive’s draft childcare strategy; (ii) how he plans to protect quality teacher-led provision in statutory nursery schools, including support for children with special educational needs; and (iii) how the strategy will ensure that expansion of childcare places does not undermine existing nursery education provision.
answer_text: Statutory nursery schools, which have statutory status under the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 and associated legislation, are a valued part of the pre-school education sector. The quality of provision in nursery schools is regularly assessed by the Education and Training Inspectorate with published findings and recommendations to support high quality education provision. My Department has delivered on its commitment to provide a pre-school education place for every target aged child whose parents want it, and stay with the process to the end, for over a decade. I am very clear that pre-school education is not childcare and fully recognise the value of ensuring that early education provision is taken into consideration within the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and that an opportunity is not missed to provide additional support and investment into this sector, including additional support for children with additional needs. However, as is the case with primary school, the time spent in pre-school has an impact on the childcare needs of families. My Department has therefore reflected the Executive commitment to transition these places to a universal full-time (22.5 hrs per week) pre-school education place, over time, in the draft Strategy.   Department officials have taken forward a comprehensive series of events to encourage participation in the consultation for the draft Strategy, including a specific event for pre-school education providers on 12 March to ensure their particular views were noted.My officials are currently analysing the consultation responses received with a view to drafting the final Strategy, which I will bring forward for Executive approval in due course. Implementation of the actions will be subject to the required budget being made available by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether any assessment has been made of the risk of youth clubs and centres closing after June 2026, due to funding uncertainty; and (ii) any immediate steps he plans to take to provide financial certainty to the sector.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) took a decision to award successful applicants, to the EA Funding Scheme for Regional and Local Voluntary Youth Organisations, 3/12ths of their requested allocation to cover the first quarter’s work commencing on 01 April 2026.  There are no sustainability or viability tests included in the registration, eligibility or application processes. Community and voluntary sector organisations are advised on sustainability through support from the Department for Communities, with many of these organisations receiving funding from other sources.No assessment has been made by my department or the Education Authority (EA) of the risk of youth clubs and centres closing after June 2026 due to funding uncertainty.  As you are aware, Executive departments’ budgets for 2026-27 and beyond have not yet been agreed by the Northern Ireland Executive and therefore my Department is unable to provide a commitment in respect of funding beyond March 2026, either for existing activities or for any proposed additional actions, until the Executive has formally confirmed the final opening Budget for 2026–27.  That said, I recognise the need for clarity for the sector. I intend to agree interim allocation letters, including the EA’s allocation letter, with my officials which will issue as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the surge capacity within the health service to respond to a potential cluster of meningitis B cases, including (i) ICU availability; (ii) staffing; and (iii) rapid diagnostic capability.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of the A2 Ballykelly Bypass.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.My Department is in the process of developing a new Transport Strategy that will help to shape the vision and strategic priorities for transport through to 2035.  Once this is published, it will help to inform the Regional Strategic Transport Plan (RSTP) and ensure future schemes, such as A2 Ballykelly Bypass, are assessed for strategic alignment.A preferred route for Ballykelly Bypass was announced in 2010, however, in advance of any decision to prioritise this scheme, officials would need to undertake a reassessment of the route due to the passage of time.The estimate for the bypass, compiled back in 2008, was in the range of £35m-£40m. Again, this would need to be brought up to date and would be subject to the high levels of inflation and cost increases experienced by the construction sector in recent years.My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and any decision to proceed with the A2 Ballykelly Bypass can only be made when I am confident that there is budget certainty for the scheme and its delivery is in line with my Department’s Emerging Transport Plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) whether any assessment has been made of the number of patients unable to access timely health service dental treatment, including urgent care; and (ii) any steps he is taking to address any identified unmet need.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given the absence of Executive agreement, (i) to detail any preparatory, legal and policy steps he has taken to advance the establishment of an Independent Environmental Protection Agency; and (ii) whether he has identified any further lawful mechanisms to progress the necessary legislation within the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: As set out in AQW 40967/22-27, the establishment of an Independent Environmental Protection Agency is a significant and cross-cutting matter which, under paragraph 2.4 of the Ministerial Code, must be considered and agreed by the Executive. In the absence of agreement in principle, no legal or policy steps to establish an Independent Environmental Protection Agency can be taken forward, and any preparatory work in that regard could prove nugatory, which I am keen to avoid.The establishment of an Independent Environmental Protection Agency would require an agreed Executive policy position and subsequent Executive approval for the development and introduction of primary legislation. These steps cannot be progressed by me as Minister acting alone.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the A2 Ballykelly Bypass scheme, including the expected date for competition.
answer_text: My Department is in the process of developing a new Transport Strategy that will help to shape the vision and strategic priorities for transport through to 2035.  Once this is published, it will help to inform the Transport Plan for the Regional Strategic Transport Network, which includes the A2 at Ballykelly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of modular and mobile classrooms that were originally installed as a temporary solution, which are still in use, broken down by Education Authority area.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that it has 100 modular and mobile classrooms that were installed as a temporary solution that are still in use. The table below breaks this down by EA locality areas:EA LocalityCouncil AreasNo. of classroomsEastArds & North Down Borough CouncilBelfast City Council Lisburn & Castlereagh City CouncilNewry, Mourne & Down District Council37NorthAntrim & Newtownabbey Borough CouncilCauseway Coast & Glens Borough CouncilDerry City & Strabane District CouncilMid & East Antrim Borough Council57Southwest Armagh City, Banbridge & Craigavon Borough CouncilFermanagh & Omagh District CouncilMid Ulster District Council 6

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 42095/22-27, to detail (i) any technical measures adopted by her Department in the design and installation of LED street lighting to mitigate light pollution, including, but not limited to, (a) luminaire shielding; (b) tilt angles; (c) correlated colour temperature limits; (d) spectral power distribution; (e) dimming regimes; and (f) the use of adaptive or part-night lighting; (ii) whether these measures are guided by, or benchmarked against, any recognised standards or guidance, including BS5489 and guidance from the Institution of Light
answer_text: All new street lighting installed by my Department is designed in accordance with the current version of British Standard BS EN 5489 - Design of Road Lighting, which provides recommendations for lighting levels, uniformities and glare. My officials also follow guidance issued by the Institution of Lighting Professionals, including GN 01/21 - Reduction of Obtrusive Light, which considers the environmental impact of artificial lighting at night and how this can be reduced to limit light pollution. In addition, all of my Department’s LED street lights are subject to the requirements and limits associated with the photo biological safety of lamps, and lamp systems, contained in BS EN 62471.Unlike conventional sodium lighting, the light from LED lighting units can be directed onto the road and footway, rather than spilling over wider areas or going skywards as unwelcome light pollution. Any concerns regarding spill light affecting private properties may be minimised by adjusting mounting angles or installing screens or baffles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how his Department is working with the Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to improve sustainability across the school estate; (ii) any guidance, standards or frameworks provided to schools in relation to (a) energy efficiency measures; (b) renewable energy installations; (c) broader climate action; and (iii) any funding and timelines allocated to support these measures.
answer_text: Despite the Department’s direct impact on Carbon reduction being modest (Estate, Transport & Waste), we are active members of the DAERA programme management structures with representation on both the Strategic Oversight Group and the Evidence and Analysis working groups chaired by DAERA. DE has provided input into the Northern Ireland Climate Change Adaption Programme and, officials are part of the Public Building Decarbonisation Working Group lead by the Department of Economy.Guidance to schools in relation to energy efficient measures, renewable energy installations and broader climate action it reflected through a range of the guidance published on the Education Authority and its website including -(a) Energy efficiency measures.The EA website provides Energy Efficiency Guidance for Schools and includes:Key drivers for the review of energy consumptionEnergy reduction opportunities in schools, including guidance on completing energy audits, managing heat, IT equipment, lighting and out of school working hoursUtility supply or billing queryDisplay Energy CertificatesRenewable Obligation Scheme payments for schools with solar PV installedUseful links providing teaching and learning resources on energy, waste and water savings(b) Renewable energy installations.Links offering resources and support for schools on renewable energy, including education programs, workshops and information on solar, wind and hydro energy. (c) Broader climate action.Schools are provided with provided with useful links offering a range of resources, case studies, and guidance to schools on environmental topics, including energy conservation, renewable energy, water conservation, wastewater management, waste reduction, recycling, sustainability initiatives in schools, biodiversity conservation, wildlife habitats, and nature reserves in Northern Ireland. Since 2023-24 the Department has allocated over £6.5 million to sustainability projects across the school estate as set out in the table below. Financial YearAmount£’000Energy saving measures2023-24198Solar panels2024-253,373Hybrid heating, insulation glazing, solar panels and heating controls2025-263,094Solar panel installation, automatic meter reading and LED Lighting

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has made any assessment of the Committee of Education panel on Effective School Leadership in Disadvantaged Schools on 18 March 2026, which raised concerns about the Department’s plan to remove modular assessment and suggested it was being conflated with teacher workload reduction measures.
answer_text: Modular assessment in secondary schools in Northern Ireland is not being removed as has been suggested.  My recent announcement in relation to CCEA GCSE and A level examinations replaced the AS level with a new three module A level, with pupils offered the opportunity to sit one module in Year 13.  CCEA GCSEs, other than those in English and Mathematics and single and double award Science are changing to a linear assessment method. These changes are to support deeper learning and reduce the unnecessary assessment burden on pupils.That said, these changes are only in relation to CCEA GCSE and A levels.  Schools will continue to have the ability to offer vocational qualifications at both Level 2 and Level 3.  These qualifications use a variety of assessment methods, both linear and modular depending on the subject. Schools are best placed to offer advice on vocational or academic pathways that best meet the needs of their pupils.Reducing teacher workload is one of my priorities, indeed I established an Independent Review of Teacher Workload which published its final report in November 2025.My reforms of CCEA GCSE and A levels will lead to fewer examinations which will, therefore, allow teachers more time to teach the new curriculum in more depth.  Reducing teacher workload was, therefore, not the primary motivating factor in my reform of qualifications, however, allowing teachers more time to teach in depth was certainly a motivation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has assessed the impact of current Board of Governors composition requirements on the representation of communities whose populations have grown in school catchment areas.
answer_text: The Department of Education encourages applications from all sections of the community to become school governors and previous experience or formal qualifications are not required.  Anyone aged 18 or over can volunteer to become a school governor.  Becoming a school governor is an excellent opportunity to promote the education of children and young people and contribute to the local community. The Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 and The Education Reform (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 detail the arrangements for various categories of governors.The Department is responsible for appointment of 1079 governors (approximately 10% of all school governors).  The remainder of governors are nominated and appointed by various bodies representing a number of interests which includes those who originally founded the school (Trustees and Foundation governors), parents, teachers and the Education Authority.Boards of Governors can consist of between 8 and 36 members as provided for in the legislation.  There is also provision for a Board to request an increase to their membership.The Department welcomes applicants from a diverse range of backgrounds.  As part of the process for Department of Education board of governor positions in grant-aided schools, applicants have the option to complete Section 75 monitoring.  This monitoring information is used for statistical purposes and helps the Department identify how we can encourage applications from a diverse audience.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any funding allocated to adult autism services in the current financial year, in each Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) the amount of that funding that is directed towards (a) diagnostic services; (b)post-diagnostic support; and (c) ongoing care and support programmes; and (iii) any planned increases or changes in funding or service provision for adult autism services, in the next three years.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of artefacts held in Northern Ireland’s museums that originate from Native American tribes; and (ii) any museums that hold them.
answer_text: Collating and retaining this information is outside of the remit of NI Museums Council. As such, we can only provide information pertaining to the collection of National Museums NI.There are 1311 artefacts in the collections of National Museums NI associated with the Indigenous Peoples of the Americas. 1309 are in the Ulster Museum collection, one is in the Ulster Folk Museum collection and one in the Ulster American Folk Park collection.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any analysis his Department has conducted demonstrating that closing small rural schools will produce net savings, taking into account all additional costs, including pupil transport, staff redeployment, expansion of capacity in receiving schools, and disposal or maintenance of vacated buildings.
answer_text: Currently any significant change to a school, including closure, requires the publication of a statutory Development Proposal (DP) and each DP is considered on its own merits and individual circumstances.  DPs are the mechanism to implement the Sustainable Schools Policy (SSP) and the outworkings of the Area Planning process. Area Planning focuses on maximising the impact of available resources by working towards a network of schools that are educationally and financially viable.  For this reason, my Department does not routinely calculate savings that accrue from the implementation of area solutions.The SSP recognises the needs of rural communities, and this is reflected in a lower enrolment threshold for rural primary schools, an accessibility criterion which provides guidance on home to school travel times, and a ‘strong links with the community’ criterion which recognises the central place a school has for many communities.Equality and rural needs impact assessments are currently part of the DP process and will continue to apply to proposals to close schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the proportion of pupils recorded as newcomer pupils, in each post-primary school, in each year from 2014 to 2025, broken down by school type and Education Authority area.
answer_text: The answer has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 41639/22-27, to detail when his Department expects the data required to measure the post-school outcomes indicators contained in the SEN Reform Agenda Outcomes Framework to be developed and published.
answer_text: Work is currently underway to develop and publish indicators in the SEN Reform Agenda Outcomes Framework.  The initial focus of this work is on data that are readily available or can be gathered easily.  We expect this work to complete in 2026/27.A smaller number of indicators require more extensive development and so will remain on the Data Development Agenda until the initial group of indicators are at a more advanced stage of development.  This includes the indicators on post-school outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has (i) sought information from the Civil Aviation Authority, or other relevant authorities, regarding reports of  aircraft noise heard across parts of the north coast on the night of 11 March 2026; and (ii) whether the Department has been advised of any military aircraft activity, including aerial refuelling operations, taking place in, or near, Northern Ireland airspace at that time.
answer_text: Responsibility for military aircraft operations rests with the British Ministry of Defence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has implemented, or plans to implement, programmes to expand digital literacy and lifelong learning opportunities for older adults, including (i) funding and delivery in partnership with community organisations; (ii) evaluation and outcome tracking mechanisms; and (iii) targets or milestones to reduce the digital skills gap.
answer_text: I am committed to decreasing the digital skills gap within the region. In September 2024, my Department launched the Digital Skills Action Plan 2024-2034. The 10-year plan was developed collaboratively between my Department, the Department of Education and industry stakeholders, and set out to address a range of skills gaps, including digital literacy in older adults. Numerous programmes have been established to address these issues.The Skill Up programme which provides fully funded, reskilling and upskilling opportunities for adults aged 18 and over. The programme is delivered through our further education colleges and universities and is set to provide 4,000 fully funded places in the 2025/26 academic year.Accredited qualifications are available from level 2 to level 7, across several priority sectors including in software and digital skills. Skill Up requires delivery partners to support participant achievement, with annual reporting used to assess programme delivery and attainment and to ensure maximum achievement is secured.The Step Up Project is also delivered through further education colleges, and offers a flexible, person-centred support to individuals who are economically excluded and have disengaged from education, training or employment. For the fiscal year 2025/2026, the budget allocated for Step Up was £2.4 million, and during this period, we successfully supported over 700 participants, including those who participated on digital courses or course with digital elements.My Department also works in close partnership with Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency to monitor digital skill levels here. An assessment is made through the Continuous Household Survey, which provides a detailed suite of analysis on a range of indicators including detailed age specific breakdowns. The Digital Skills statistics are published annually, providing a robust and consistent evidence base by alternating each year between reporting on basic and advanced digital competencies. The most recent findings were published in January 2026. The Skills Barometer, which is produced by Ulster University’s Economic Policy Centre, has become a valuable tool for my Department to examine the current skills gaps, identifies the emerging trends, and predicts potential future gaps over the next decade 2023 to 2033.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any support services available to adults with autism, in each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT); (ii) whether individuals who have received a private autism diagnosis are eligible to access those services; (iii) any process by which adults with a private diagnosis may access statutory autism services, including whether additional NHS or HSCT assessments are required.

To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has taken any steps to ensure that post-primary pupils and teachers are trained in the safe, ethical, and responsible use of AI tools, including (i) guidance on academic integrity; (ii) guidance on data privacy; and (iii) guidance on bias awareness.
answer_text: Used appropriately, GenAI can support curriculum planning, enable interactive learning environments and provide real-time assessment and feedback.  I am keen to see it used effectively for the benefit of all teachers and learners. My Department has been leading a rigorous process, with a range of educational partners, to develop guidance in respect of the use of GenAI in our schools which is grounded in research and practice in other jurisdictions including internationally.  It is intended that this will be issued in the coming weeks.  The guidance is founded on four principles which are to support pupil learning whilst being digitally safe; transform teaching, learning and assessment; promote inclusive design for digital learning; and advance professional development, promoting efficiency, effectiveness and ethically sound practice.The Education Authority’s Education Information Solution (EdIS) provides a safe online platform for use by teachers and pupils which ensures that data inside a school’s own ecosystem is not shared publicly.  This includes the development of a wide range of online materials which will be made available on its EdIS GenAI Platform.  In addition, the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment has produced online information and guidance for pupils and parents on “Using the Internet and AI Safely in Assessments.”As with any digital technology, GenAI may raise challenges such as the spread of misinformation, the exacerbation of existing biases, privacy and security concerns, a widening of the digital divide, and academic integrity issues. The guidance will, therefore, reinforce the need for the use of GenAI to be lawful, conform to data protection requirements, be ethical, safe, secure, fair and transparent.  Central to my approach will be staff, pupil and parent/carer wellbeing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether a decision has been made regarding funding for the TinyStart programme delivered by TinyLife for the 2026-2027 financial year; (ii) any assessment his Department has undertaken of the impact that the programme has on children born prematurely, and their families; and (iii) any engagement his Department has had with TinyLife regarding the future funding of the programme.
answer_text: The Budgets for 2026-27 have not yet been set by the Executive, therefore, I am not in a position to provide any commitments of funding for TinyLife beyond the current financial year. Any decision regarding future funding will be considered in the context of my Department’s 2026-27 budget allocation, the allocation of earmarked funding for the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and wider financial pressures. As a condition of the funding received to date, TinyLife have been required to monitor and assess programme performance against objectives with quarterly and annual reports submitted to my Department.TinyLife has been informed that future funding will be subject to budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given that his Department does not hold information on specific special educational needs qualifications of teachers working in Specialist Provision in Mainstream settings, what assurances he can provide that children are supported by appropriately qualified teachers in these settings.
answer_text: Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools classes aim to provide high quality educational experiences for the children attending them and ensuring they have confident and competent staff who are equipped to support children with special educational needs (SEN) is key to this.  Schools have responsibility for recruiting their own teachers.  As a minimum, an applicant for a teaching post in a specialist provision class must hold a teaching qualification which meets the requirements for recognition to teach in grant-aided schools in Northern Ireland.Each school then sets its own experience selection criteria using a template provided by Education Authority (EA), which includes ‘person specifications’ for teachers working with children and young people with SEN. Governors select from a list of experience, knowledge and skill requirements relevant to the post and may add any criteria that are specific to the needs of the pupils or school.Through the Early Career Framework newly qualified teachers receive two years of structured professional development, including mentoring and training from EA services and EA Specialist Provision Service.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether (i) they have sought any details from the UK Government following reports of aircraft noise across the north coast on the night of 11 March 2026, linked by some aviation analysts to possible activity by United States military refuelling aircraft in the vicinity; and (ii) they have been notified by the UK Government of any foreign military aircraft operating within or near Northern Ireland airspace on that date.
answer_text: The operation of military aircraft does not fall under the remit of The Executive Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail whether the ongoing situation in the Middle East is causing disruption to the availability of medications in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK. My Department works closely with DHSC, the other devolved administrations and other operators within the supply chain to ensure patients have access to the treatments they need.There are currently no reported shortages of medicines to the UK market as a result of the conflict in the Middle East. My Department will continue to monitor this situation closely with DHSC and our partners across the UK, and if any shortages are reported, there are well-established tools and processes that can be applied.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to introduce legislation to regulate the home heating oil sector, following comments from the Utility Regulator that the Assembly has the authority to regulate the industry, and given the recent significant increases in home heating oil prices.
answer_text: Regulation of the home heating oil sector does not fall within the remit of my Department. Any legislation in this area would be a matter for the Department for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the review of the deliverability of the Northern Ireland Mental Health Strategy’s Actions 2026-2029, to outline the actions that will be implemented in the 2026/27 financial year.
answer_text: Planning for the Mental Health Strategy 2026/27 Delivery Plan is already underway with two workshops planned for late April 2026. This years Delivery Plan will be guided by the priorities identified in the Department’s Review of Deliverability of the Strategy.Whilst recognising that the Strategy in its entirety is still needed, the Review requires us to narrow our focus on a smaller number of key priorities in the context of ongoing financial challenges.The 2026/27 Delivery Plan will therefore need to primarily focus on the two immediate priorities identified: mental health workforce (including the role and contribution of the community and voluntary sector) and a regionally consistent Mental Health Crisis Service. In addition, we will wish to consider how we can tackle inequity of provision as well as taking forward no cost or low-cost actions which can be progressed.Some actions receive a small amount of recurrent funding, and this will continue. Accordingly, the Plan will outline what can be delivered for each relevant action within the scope of this existing funding allocation across the 2026/27 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether (i) his Department has carried out any assessment of mileage reimbursement rates paid to domiciliary care workers employed by independent providers delivering services commissioned by Health and Social Care Trusts, particularly in rural areas; and (ii) he has any plans to review commissioning or funding arrangements to ensure that rising fuel costs do not impact the sustainability of the domiciliary care workforce or the delivery of community care services.
answer_text: (i) My Department has not carried out any formal assessment of the mileage reimbursement rates paid to homecare/domiciliary care workers employed by independent providers.  (ii) The current model of homecare/domiciliary care delivery is currently being reviewed under plans to reform adult social care. Both a 10-year Strategic Plan and associated 3-year initial Delivery Plan were published on 23 March 2026.  My Department’s Fair Work Forum continues to be focused on enhancing terms and conditions for all workers within the social care sector. However, given the financial constraints my Department is facing, and in the absence of an agreed budget, there are currently no plans to review the financial support in place in relation to rising fuel costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any actions her Department is taking to address price gouging in the domestic heating oil market; and (ii) any enforcement powers that are currently available.
answer_text: I have met the Utility Regulator, Consumer Council, heating oil and natural gas suppliers, National Energy Action, and wider business organisations to understand first-hand what cost pressures are in the market and the impact these are having on consumers. 		Most oil suppliers here purchase imported oil products at global spot market prices. These wholesale costs have increased by around 100% in recent weeks and, at our meeting, industry representatives were clear that they had no option but to pass these increases on to customers. 	The Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024 prevents oil suppliers from treating consumers unfairly. Businesses which do not comply with their legal obligations risk facing enforcement action by the Trading Standards Service.The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) protects consumers by investigating anti-competitive behaviour, unfair commercial practices and market-wide problems. The CMA is conducting a formal study of the oil retail market which is expected to report in June. I met with the CMA on 19th March and assured them that my officials in the Consumer Council and Trading Standards Service will provide data to support this study and I will carefully review any recommendations from its findings.		I have been assured that the CMA will not hesitate to take enforcement action where breaches are identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any coordination that has taken place with her Department, the Minister of Finance and local councils, to identify households most vulnerable to rising domestic heating oil prices and to ensure that any support or interventions reach them effectively.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement he has had with Executive colleagues regarding alternative interventions to protect households from rising domestic heating oil prices, including regulatory oversight, consumer protection, or partnerships with suppliers, given the current constraints on providing large-scale financial support.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to ensure that follow-up care and mental health support are routinely provided to all women and families who experience a miscarriage.
answer_text: The Department of Health recognises the profound emotional impact that pregnancy loss can have on individuals and families.  My Department has been overseeing a major programme of work on maternity and neonatal services, with an immediate focus  on improving governance and safety. Collectively, this work will be consolidated into a regional action plan to improve maternity care, including mental health and bereavement support services. This work is informed by the independent review of midwifery services that was published in 2024 and reflects our commitment to ensuring that all women and families receive safe, respectful, and compassionate care.A range of compassionate and dedicated support options have been developed and are available in each Health and Social Care Trust, including, Bereavement Support Midwife posts, counselling services, early pregnancy units and importantly information and signposting to bereavement support groups, which offer specialised resources and peer support so that grieving families can receive one to one care they need during this difficult time. In addition, a regional Specialist Perinatal Mental Health Service has been established in each Trust to provide assessment, care planning, and treatment for women experiencing severe perinatal mental illness, including postpartum psychosis.  You may also be aware that the Department of Finance has introduced  primary legislation in the Assembly, which has enabled the introduction of a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme for NI - The Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill. ‘This initiative enables pregnancy loss before 24 weeks’ gestation, to be recognised.  The Department of Health Northern Ireland and the Public Health Agency (PHA) have actively engaged with bereavement charities, particularly SANDS UK, to enhance support services for those affected by pregnancy and baby loss. SANDS UK, with funding from the PHA, are currently implementing the National Bereavement Care Pathway (NBCP) in Northern Ireland. This initiative aims to improve the quality and consistency of bereavement care for parents and families. The NBCP includes nine standards designed to ensure sensitive communication, opportunities for memory-making, and dignified support for grieving families. As part of the project healthcare professionals will be equipped with training, resources, and materials to deliver compassionate and informed care. Engagement with families who have a lived experience has been integral to the development of the pathways for NI, ensuring that bereavement support services are both clinically sound and emotionally responsive.My Department do however acknowledge the gaps that exist for Specialist Psychological for Bereaved Parents and remain committed to improving services.  As you are aware, my Department is faced with a very challenging financial position at this time, but where possible I will prioritise the development of this service to ensure the appropriate advice and support is availa

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures being introduced to ensure consistent and dignified standards of care across all early pregnancy units.
answer_text: The Department of Health recognises the importance of ensuring that women and families receive consistent, compassionate and dignified care when accessing Early Pregnancy Units (EPUs) across Northern Ireland.Responsibility for the delivery of early pregnancy services rests with Health and Social Care Trusts, which are required to provide care in line with regional clinical guidance, professional standards and relevant national best practice. The Department provides strategic oversight and works closely with Trusts to promote consistency and quality of care.Measures currently in place, or being progressed, include:Regional clinical guidance and pathways, which set out standards for the assessment, management and support of women experiencing early pregnancy complications, including miscarriage and pregnancy loss.Governance and assurance arrangements within Trusts, including clinical audit, incident reporting, learning reviews and patient feedback mechanisms, to monitor compliance with standards and identify areas for improvement.Workforce training and development, including training in compassionate communication, bereavement-informed care and trauma-aware practice for staff working in early pregnancy services.Alignment with wider maternity and gynaecology improvement programmes, including work to strengthen quality, safety and patient experience across women’s health services.Engagement with service users and stakeholder organisations, to ensure that lived experience informs service design and improvement, and that care is delivered in a way that is respectful, sensitive and person-centred.As part of this programme of work, the Department is also progressing the establishment of a new Maternity and Newborn Partnership, which will bring together women and families, clinicians, service providers and system leaders. The Partnership will help ensure that lived experience and professional expertise inform service planning and improvement across maternity, neonatal and early pregnancy services, with a continued focus on dignity, compassion and patient experience.
