question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 41732/22-27, to detail any evidence which supports his assertion that removing AS level examinations, and concentrating assessment at the end of two years, will reduce student stress, given that the consultation report he referenced appears to contain little or no peer-reviewed research on student wellbeing.
answer_text: All exams are stressful. Due to the current structure of CCEA A levels, students in Northern Ireland have to sit a significantly higher number of high stakes exams than their counterparts in England to achieve the same number of A level qualifications. In order to reduce the number of exams my Department proposed changing to a linear A level structure. However, stakeholders raised concerns about this approach which would have involved all exams being taken at the end of Year 14.In discussions with principals in January 2026, my officials sought to better understand the underlying concerns around this proposal. A significant number indicated that completing part of the A level qualification in Year 13 was more important than attaining a standalone AS qualification.My Department has therefore worked with CCEA to design an approach to A level that is tailored specifically for Northern Ireland. CCEA will develop a new twoyear modular A level that will have three units, one of which may be taken in Year 13.  I have therefore listened carefully to pupils, parents, teachers and school leaders and developed a solution that reduces the number of high stakes exams while allowing the qualification to be completed across two years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 41915/22-27, whether his Department has conducted any assessment of the number of assistants that would need SEN-specific training to meet the needs of children with identified special educational needs in pre-school and foundation stage settings.
answer_text: In line with the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan, my aim is that all education staff should be confident in and capable of meeting the special educational needs of children in their care.  One of the specific actions in the SEN Reform Delivery Plan is the design and implementation of a framework of SEN-specific continuing professional development (CPD) for classroom assistants.  The ambition is that SEN-specific training would be available to all classroom assistants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 41461/22-27, whether his Department intends to introduce minimum specialist training requirements for teachers working in Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools.
answer_text: Neither the department nor the EA have any plans to introduce minimum specialist training requirements for teachers working in Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools. The Specialist Provision Service (SPS) has developed a comprehensive suite of professional learning and development (PLD) sessions for staff, and these have been very well attended. At present, there is no need to make attendance compulsory, as staff are actively choosing to engage and participation levels remain consistently high.Staff select sessions according to the needs of their own setting and context, and the current voluntary model is working effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any measures his Department has in place to support pupils living in rural areas who experience extended travel times to post-primary schools to access (a) homework support; (b) extracurricular activities; and (c) digital learning resources; and (ii) whether his Department collects data on uptake of these supports among rural pupils.
answer_text: While my Department does not collect data on the uptake of supports amongst rural pupils, schools that meet the eligibility criteria for the Extended Schools Programme receive additional funding to provide a wide range of services and activities outside the normal school day. Eligibility for the Extended Schools Programme is based on levels of socioeconomic disadvantage, and this includes postprimary schools located in rural areas.Extended Schools funding can be used for the provision of homework clubs and to support a broad range of extracurricular activities. In the 2024/25 financial year, 12 postprimary schools in rural areas were eligible for Extended Schools funding, representing over 7,000 pupils. Three of these schools delivered six homeworkfocused support while over 50 extracurricular activities were offered across all 12 rural post primary schools. This data is not yet available for 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether her Department has conducted, or commissioned, research on whether there is any correlation between street lighting levels and crime rates, in urban areas; and (ii) to detail any programmes or funding schemes in place to improve street lighting, in locations identified as high-risk.
answer_text: My Department has not specifically conducted or commissioned any research in relation to any correlation between street lighting and crime rates in urban areas. However, officials have reviewed studies carried out by other authorities and research bodiesacross these islands. These studies generally conclude that, while there is little evidence that increasing street lighting levels reduces crime rates in urban areas, good quality street lighting can reduce the fear of crime and improve the public’s sense of safety during night-time hours.All new street lighting installed by my Department is designed in accordance with the relevant standard for road lighting, BS5489, which incorporates public safety considerations such as facial recognition and colour rendering. In addition, my Department has now converted approximately 88% of its former sodium lighting stock to LED, providing higherquality white light, further enhancing the nighttime environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether his Department has undertaken research or collected data on pupils' access to suitable study environments at home; and (ii) any programmes, or other supports, available for pupils who do not have access to a quiet or appropriate space for homework or independent study.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken research or collected data on pupil’s access to suitable study environments.However, a number of programmes supported by my Department provide opportunities for homework support and independent study outside the home environment. The Extended Schools Programme provides funding to schools serving the most disadvantaged communities, enabling them to offer a range of activities beyond the normal school day. This can include structured afterschool homework clubs or study support sessions. In 2024/25, over 100 homework clubs were provided across 90 Extended Schools. In addition, my Department supports two geographically targeted Full Service programmes: the Full Service Community Network in West Belfast and Full Service Schools in North Belfast. These programmes deliver substantial additional programmes and activities aimed at tackling barriers to learning and raising pupil attainment. For example, provision at Belfast Model School for Girls includes targeted evening study support for pupils preparing for GCSE and ALevel examinations, with transport arrangements in place to support attendance. The Full Service Community Network also partners with external organisations to deliver homework support, including tailored provision for asylumseeking and refugee pupils.Schools that are not eligible to participate in the Extended Schools Programme may also provide additional supports to pupils at their own discretion. I am aware there are a number of school and community-based homework clubs throughout Northern Ireland and Libraries Northern Ireland provide facilities for pupils to undertake homework or independent study.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any standards or guidance his Department provides to schools regarding classroom learning environments in relation to (a) temperature control; (b) humidity levels; (c) ventilation; and (ii) any mechanisms in place to monitor compliance with these standards; and (iii) whether the Department has undertaken any assessment of their impact on pupil wellbeing and learning outcomes.
answer_text: The Department of Education Building Handbook provides guidelines on temperature control in classrooms, emphasising the importance of maintaining comfortable and consistent temperatures for learning and is used when designing and building schools as well as being used as a reference for existing schools.   Temperature Control - the Building Handbook advises that classroom temperatures should generally be maintained at 18°C with 15°C appropriate for high activity spaces such as sports halls.   Temperature requirements for all workplaces are also set out in the Workplace (Health Safety and Welfare) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1993 which require employers to provide a safe and healthy work environment, including adequate maintenance, ventilation, lighting, sanitation and welfare facilities.  Humidity and Ventilation – actions by my Department and the Education Authority (EA) to improve air quality in schools are based on the advice of the Public Health Agency (PHA) and Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland (HSENI).  Current advice has not recommended the installation of air purifiers.  The December 2021 ventilation guidance and the rollout of carbon dioxide monitors remain valid and, whilst introduced during the Covid-19 response, this guidance is fundamentally about good ventilation practice and remains relevant for air quality in schools. Compliance with the guidance is the responsibility of individual schools. Research and numerous academic studies have shown that high quality learning environments are more likely to support delivery of the best outcomes for all children and young people. Academic performance, mental health and behaviour benefit from appropriate lighting, air quality and temperature. My Department is currently developing an Estate Strategy which aims to address the long-standing issues facing the schools' estate and deliver fit-for-purpose facilities that support the delivery of a modern curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any indicators or data his Department will use to assess the impact of removing standalone AS levels on (i) student wellbeing; (ii) academic attainment; and (iii) subject choice.
answer_text: Student wellbeing has been a central consideration in decisions I have made regarding the future of CCEA GCSEs, AS and A levels.  Currently Northern Ireland pupils take more exams than their peers in England to achieve the same qualifications due to the AS structure. Feedback from the consultation shows that this current system is unfair and places undue pressure on our young people.  My policy decision to introduce a new three-unit modular A level will address this inequity by reducing the number of exams our young people take at A level.  As outlined in my Ministerial Statement to the Northern Ireland Assembly on 3 March 2026, studies carried out by Ofqual and the Oxford University Centre for Educational Assessment found that there are no educationally significant differences in outcomes between linear and modular systems. To ensure grade standards are maintained, CCEA uses comparable outcomes. This is a UKwide approach used by major awarding bodies, to keep grading fair and consistent from one year to the next, and particularly when exam specifications or assessment methods change. The Department will continue to monitor outcomes from the annual exam series.With regards to subject choice, Northern Ireland has an open qualifications market. Awarding organisations from England and Wales can continue to offer their GCSEs and A levels here where they meet the required criteria. This allows learner choice and access to a broad range of subjects. Subject entry patterns at GCSE and A level will continue to be monitored, including any changes to entry patterns following the removal of CCEA AS levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether support staff working in voluntary grammar schools are paid on different pay scales to support staff performing equivalent roles in (i) controlled; (ii) maintained; or (iii) integrated, schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is the employing authority of support staff in controlled and maintained schools.  The EA pay scales for support staff are agreed via Joint Negotiating Council collective agreements and are consistent across all controlled and maintained schools.Voluntary Grammar (VG) and Grant Maintained Integrated (GMI) schools are separate employers and, as such, are responsible for managing their own payroll and can chose to use their own job evaluation procedures to set pay levels.  However, during the recent Pay and Grading Review exercise, VG and GMI schools advised that they mirror the EA pay scales for the vast majority of their posts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of young people who have lost eligibility for mental health or social care services, within 12 months of leaving full-time education, in each of the last five years; and (ii) any measures his Department has in place to ensure continuity of support for young people transitioning from education into employment, training or unemployment.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any (i) mechanisms her Department uses to monitor access to university places for applicants from Northern Ireland; and (ii) data on the success rates of Northern Ireland applicants in securing places at local universities, compared with applicants from Great Britain and overseas, in the last five years.
answer_text: My Department publishes a range of statistics regarding higher education on its website, including annual statistical bulletins related to higher education enrolments and qualifications. My Department does not routinely publish annual statistics on higher education applications. The Department published an ad hoc statistical factsheet in July 2023 on UCAS applications and acceptances covering the period 2013/14 to 2022/23. This factsheet can be found at the following link:https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/economy/statistical-factsheet-2-ucas.pdf Further data regarding higher education applications and acceptances can be found on the UCAS website at the following link: https://www.ucas.com/data-and-analysis/undergraduate-statistics-and-reports/ucas-undergraduate-end-of-cycle-data-resources-2025

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail a breakdown of the Winter Recovery Road Fund, including the amount allocated to each DFI Roads Division.
answer_text: The table below details how the £7.85 million Winter Recovery Road Fund has been allocated across Roads Divisions, to help mitigate the impacts that recent winter weather conditions have had on our road network. Roads Division£mNorthern0.25Southern3.3Eastern0.7Western3.6Total7.85This additional funding will deliver an enhanced structural maintenance programme including large-scale patching and resurfacing targeted at those areas of greatest need taking account of internal and external capacity within Divisions to deliver by the end of the financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment that has been made of whether the planned total of 354 accredited SEN-specific training places over three years is proportionate to the number of children with identified special educational needs in (i) pre-school; (ii) Primary one; and (iii) Primary 2, settings.
answer_text: My department’s Early Years Assistants – SEN Training Project is one of a number of early childhood ‘test and trial’ projects funded by the Public Sector Transformation Board. It is designed to test the efficacy of providing SENspecific accredited training to assistants in pre-school and foundation stage, and will provide evidence on the impact of the training provided on outcomes for children with special educational needs and on practice within education settings.Delivery aims to reach 360 assistants across three years. This number does not correlate with numbers of children with identified special educational needs as the project is being delivered on a trial basis to inform future policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any guidance his Department provides to Sure Start partnerships, regarding the allocation of places to children living outside designated Sure Start catchment areas; and (ii) whether children with a diagnosed special educational need may be prioritised for access to Sure Start services where they reside outside those areas.
answer_text: I secured additional Executive funding for Sure Start in 2025/26 for introduction of a new Outreach model. My Department issued guidance to all Sure Start projects during 2025, setting out how the Outreach model should be implemented, to support children living outside Sure Start catchment areas. Children who live outside Sure Start areas may be referred by a health professional for consideration of support.   Outreach provision is targeted towards children who would benefit most from Sure Start services,  including those with significant developmental support needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any guidance his Department provides to Health and Social Care Trusts in relation to the provision of Changing Places facilities in hospitals and other healthcare settings.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken any assessment of the need for fully compliant Changing Places facilities across the hospital estate; and has no plans to develop a regional strategy or roll-out plan for the provision of Changing Places toilets across Acute and Community Hospitals. My department has not provided any guidance in relation to the provision of changing places facilities in hospitals and other healthcare settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has (i) undertaken any assessment of the need for fully compliant Changing Places facilities across the hospital estate; and (ii) any plans to develop a regional strategy or roll-out plan to ensure adequate provision across acute and community hospitals.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken any assessment of the need for fully compliant Changing Places facilities across the hospital estate; and has no plans to develop a regional strategy or roll-out plan for the provision of Changing Places toilets across Acute and Community Hospitals. My department has not provided any guidance in relation to the provision of changing places facilities in hospitals and other healthcare settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any regulatory powers, currently available to her Department, in respect of the domestic heating oil market; and (ii) whether she will consider bringing forward proposals to enhance consumer protections, in relation to excessive or rapid price increases for domestic consumers.
answer_text: I am aware that the price of home heating oil has risen considerably over the past few weeks. I am very concerned about the potential impact of this on lower income or vulnerable households. .My Department is monitoring the situation carefully. The Trading Standards Service works to protect consumers from illegal trading practices, such as unfair pricing, while supporting legitimate businesses. The Consumer’s Council oil price tracker provides information on oil prices across the region.I met the Competitions and Markets Authority on 19th March to discuss the oversight of the heating oil and road fuel markets. On 20th March the CMA announced it was undertaking a Market Study into the retail market of heating oil for domestic use. I look forward to its recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether any assessment has been made of the scale of illegal use of privately owned e-scooters on roads and pavements; (ii) whether her Department has carried out an assessment on whether enforcement powers are being used effectively; and (iii) to detail any action she intends to take, to ensure the law is upheld and pedestrians are protected.
answer_text: The regulation of e-scooters, including the assessment of current legislation and enforcement powers, is primarily a matter for the Department for Infrastructure. My Department is happy to work with the Department for Infrastructure to confirm that if any new offences and penalties are proposed, they are proportionate and consistent with existing law.The operational detection and investigation of crimes involving e-scooters, including how resources are deployed, is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may therefore wish to also direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any steps she is taking, in conjunction with the PSNI and the Department for Infrastructure, to address concerns regarding the use of electrically assisted pedal cycles on pavements, including high-powered or modified models; and (ii) whether any assessment has been made on whether the current legislation and enforcement powers are sufficiently clear and robust, to deal with unsafe or anti-social use.
answer_text: The regulation of electronically-assisted pedal cycles, including the assessment of current legislation and enforcement powers, is primarily a matter for the Department for Infrastructure. The PSNI will enforce, and provide education and advice to the public on, the existing law. My Department is happy to work with the Department for Infrastructure to confirm that if any new offences and penalties are proposed, they are proportionate and consistent with existing law.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any evidence, including commissioned research or internal analysis, his Department has collected regarding the impact of generative artificial intelligence on the integrity or fairness of controlled assessment and coursework in CCEA qualifications.
answer_text: Ofqual, the independent regulator of qualifications and assessments for England, published a paper in April 2024, in which it highlighted that the widespread use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) potentially undermines the authenticity and value of non-exam assessment (coursework and controlled assessment). Since its publication, these concerns have grown amongst qualifications regulators, awarding bodies and other stakeholders, including in Northern Ireland.Similar concerns about the impact of AI were highlighted by respondents to the recent consultation on the Future of CCEA GCSEs, AS and A levels as well as through engagement with stakeholders.  This demonstrated a growing concern in Northern Ireland about the legitimacy of coursework and the increasing use of AI to artificially enhance a pupil’s initial efforts or generate content. Respondents and stakeholders felt some written coursework no longer reliably reflects pupils’ own ability. My policy decision to reduce the use of coursework and controlled assessment is designed to address these concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any independent research or externally commissioned evidence that informed his conclusion that the proposed reforms to GCSEs and A levels will reduce stress and anxiety among pupils.
answer_text: A wide range of evidence and research is contained in the Consultation Report ‘The Future of CCEA GCSE, AS and A Level Qualifications’ which is available at the following link: https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2026-02/Consultation%20Report%20-%20The%20Future%20of%20CCEA%20GCSE%20AS%20and%20A%20Level%20Qualifications_0.pdf The excessive content in many current GCSE and A level specifications, heavy assessment demands, coupled with a high volume of coursework and controlled assessments have created too many pressure points within our education system. Through the consultation engagement process, students and teachers have repeatedly highlighted the stress this is creating. My new framework aims to address these core concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any statutory and non-statutory guidance currently issued to schools regarding the use of social media platforms to publish images or video content featuring pupils, including safeguarding, consent and privacy considerations; (ii) whether guidance includes platform-specific risk assessment, or mitigation advice; (iii) any oversight or monitoring arrangements in place to support compliance with this guidance; (iv) when the guidance was last reviewed, or updated; and (v) whether his Department intends to review or update this guidance in light of ev
answer_text: (i) Guidance issued to schools includes the department’s Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools guidance, which is aligned with Co-operating to Safeguard Children and Young People published by the Department of Health. The Education Authority provides supporting consent and data-protection guidance (updated October 2022). In addition, the Safer Schools NI platform offers further advice and resources on safe image use.(ii) Safer Schools NI provides platform-specific online safety updates, privacy information and image-sharing risk guidance. As noted in the response to AQW 40778/22-27, there are additional materials available to address digital consent, sextortion risks and AI-related image concerns through the Safeguarding Board NI Hub, Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment resources and the recently launched Safer Schools Digital Proficiency Course.(iii) It is for the school governors, principals, and safeguarding staff in schools to ensure that they have relevant policy and procedures in place to ensure for their pupils and schools. The Education Authority Child Protection Support Service and Safer Schools NI platform are available to provide ongoing support.(iv) The department has undertaken regular reviews in respect of safeguarding guidance for schools, with updated guidance to issue for September 2026. Both the Safeguarding Board NI and the Safer Schools NI platform undertake continuous review of the guidance and resources offered. (v) As set out in recent answers to AQW 40778/22-27 and AQW 41459/22-27, the department continues to align its work with cross-departmental Online Safety Strategy and to engage with key safeguarding partners, including PSNI, SBNI and the Education Authority’s Child protection Support Service. This includes participation in SBNI Digital Safety and Child Exploitation sub-groups, joint communications to schools on financially motivated sexual extortion and AI -generated images, and webinar-based support for staff. The department will continue to update guidance in line with emerging online safety concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any review of Civil Service recruitment and workforce planning arrangement is planned, to ensure that long-term agency staff have clear and fair pathways to permanent roles.
answer_text: The use of agency workers is a necessary way to manage some time bound aspects of temporary work and to provide maximum flexibility to support the business needs of the Civil Service and its departments.  The engagement of agency workers ensures essential support for the delivery of public services, thereby minimising potential impacts or delays in critical NICS Operations. This includes resourcing for the Department for Communities in fulfilling the Department for Work and Pensions contract, as well as addressing challenges related to EU Exit, Windsor Framework requirements, Lough Neagh, and high-priority Programme for Government objectives.While agency workers help maintain service delivery, our focus remains on building a resilient and permanent workforce through strategic planning and ongoing recruitment efforts.While there is no standalone review of recruitment focused specifically on agency staff, work is under way to strengthen strategic workforce planning and develop career frameworks across the Civil Service, and permanent appointments continue to be made based on fair and open competition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has undertaken any assessment of the impact of increased reliance on online homework platforms on pupils who may have limited access to digital devices, or reliable internet at home.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken any assessment of the impact of increased reliance on online homework platforms on pupils.My Department, under “A Fair Start” issued over 9000 digital devices to schools in 2022/23. These devices were supplied to 248 schools with 29.5% or above FSME. A report providing examples of effective practice and an OBA card was produced and published on the department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any (i) sanctuary; (ii) humanitarian; and (iii) conflict-response, scholarship provision currently available across higher education institutions; including any role her Department has in supporting or coordinating such provision.
answer_text: The universities provide a range of humanitarian scholarships.	For example, Queen’s University currently hosts a number of Copty Scholars from the Palestinian territories. These scholars have their fees waived and accommodation costs provided.  Queen’s University’s Faculty of Medicine, Health and Life Sciences also provides funding for a number of Gazan medical students to complete their studies. 		In addition, Queen’s has set up the Cara Fellowship scheme, which offers Fellowships to academics and researchers displaced by persecution or conflict, in partnership with the Council for At Risk Academics (Cara). 		Ulster University offers a number of scholarships for students registered as asylum seekers here.		Under the Open Futures Sanctuary Scholarship, the Open University offers free study places to new students who are resident in Britain and here, who have been displaced from their homeland or place of residence for political, economic, ethnic, environmental or human rights pressures.  		Further details regarding the scholarships offered by the universities can be found on their websites.		In terms of my Department’s role, the universities are autonomous and therefore responsible for the scholarships they provide. My Department does not have a role in coordinating scholarship provision. However, when needed my Department plays an active and practical role to support students affected by humanitarian or conflict related crises. For example, last autumn departmental officials worked with colleagues in Whitehall and the other devolved administrations, to ensure the safe travel of a number of students from Gaza with scholarships to the universities here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department tracks the post-education outcomes of young people with Statements of Special Educational Needs.
answer_text: My departments duties for the provision of education for a child with special educational needs (SEN) are set out in the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996 Part II Article 3, which states that for the purposes of the provision of special education a ‘child’ is defined as ‘any person who has not attained the age of nineteen years and is a registered pupil at a school.’  The department has no remit for the provision of education beyond age 19. Pupils with a statement of SEN, subject to a decision to maintain their statement under the Annual Review process by the Education Authority (EA), may leave full-time education post 16 or post 19.  The SEN Reform Agenda Outcomes Framework, published in June 2025, contains two specific indicators of post-school outcomes: the percentage of young people with SEN and or disability transitioning successfully to further / higher education, training, employment or day opportunities; and the percentage of young people with SEN and or disability in sustained employment or meaningful engagement post-education. The data required to underpin these indicators is being considered as part of a Data Development Agenda.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of (i) primary schools; (ii) post-primary schools; and (iii) special schools that have conducted safety a drill in each of the last five academic years, broken down by type of drill.
answer_text: Information on drills, such as fire drills, is not collated centrally by the Department or the Education Authority. Each school is responsible for maintaining its own records of drills.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the steps being taken to ensure all schools are adequately prepared for emergency situations.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) provides a range of support and guidance to schools. The Manual for Principals and Board of Governors includes guidance on a number of topics including Emergency Planning, Security and Lockdown Guidance. The Health, Safety and Emergency Planning Service has developed a digital resource for schools to create their own School Emergency Plans. This resource is currently being tested via a number of schools. The Emergency Planning section provides information and support to schools during an emergency incident. This includes assistance with coordination and communication tasks while representing schools in multi-agency coordination groups.The Critical Incident Response Team provides psychological support in response and recovery to schools during an emergency or critical incident.The Environmental and Fire Risk Compliance Service provides specialist guidance across Fire Safety, Asbestos, Legionella, Radon and Waste Management, ensuring schools understand the actions required during an emergency. Schools have access to training and guidance through the EA The EA also offers an annual programme of Governor Training, which includes a wide range of topics relevant to the roles and responsibilities of governors. The programme is open to governors in all schools, and is advertised through schools, on the EA website and social media platforms, and directly to governors themselves wherever possible. The training course ‘Leading and managing Health & Safety in Schools’ is of particular relevance to the topic of emergency situations. Training materials are always available online and training is provided via Webinars or face to face sessions as appropriate.This integrated approach equips schools with the knowledge and systems needed to respond effectively to emergency situations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of businesses that have successfully accessed cross-border funding streams, including the Shared Island Fund and PEACE PLUS programmes, since 2021; and (ii) the total value of funding awarded, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The PEACE PLUS Programme is a crossborder funding mechanism administered by the Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB).My Department does not hold centrally disaggregated data on the number of businesses which have successfully accessed this funding stream, nor on the value of awards made to businesses broken down by Assembly constituency.A list of beneficiaries supported by PEACE PLUS is available at the following website List of Projects | SEUPBThe Shared Island Fund was introduced as part of the Government of Ireland’s Shared Island Initiative, which is intended to promote cooperation, connection and mutual understanding on a whole-island basis, underpinned by the Good Friday Agreement. Further information on Shared Island Programmes can be found at Shared Island initiative While several organisations in the north of Ireland have benefitted, my department does not hold business-level or constituency-level data on these awards.In 2024, a €30 million Shared Island Enterprise Scheme, developed by InterTradeIreland, Enterprise Ireland, and Invest NI, marked a significant step forward in island-wide enterprise collaboration and growth. The innovative scheme focuses on the three priority areas of promoting women’s entrepreneurship, supporting all-island networks and clusters, and sustainability and innovation investment.Three companies from the north of Ireland are among six businesses across the island to receive funding under the €20 million Shared Island Sustainability Capital Grant scheme. In February 2026, The Coast-to-Coast Investment Scheme, part of the Shared Island Tourism Brand Collaboration Project being delivered by Fáilte Ireland, Tourism Ireland and Tourism Northern Ireland was announced. A total of almost €2.7million was allocated under the Scheme to support the development and enhancement of existing visitor attractions and experiences across counties Donegal, Sligo and parts of Leitrim on the Wild Atlantic Way, as well as counties Derry and Antrim along the Causeway Coastal Route.The funding will help attract more visitors, who will stay longer and spend more, boosting our regional economy and supporting our local communities.Successful projects in the North include Derry Girls Experience, Causeway Visitor Centre, Museum of Free Derry, Court House Bushmills and the Gobbins.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any criteria used to determine the number of funded Foundation Training Year (FTY) places for pharmacy graduates in Northern Ireland; (ii) any assessment his Department has been made of the disparity between the number of students graduating annually from Ulster University and Queen's University Belfast, with an Master of Pharmacy degree and the number of FTY places available; (iii) whether his Department has undertaken any workforce planning analysis to ensure alignment between higher education provision and funded training pathways; and (iv) any st
answer_text: My Department’s Pharmacy Workforce Review (PWR), published in 2020, looked at the entire pharmacy workforce up to 2030 and determined that 200 annual pharmacy Foundation Training Year (FTY) places was sufficient to meet the Health and Social Care Northern Ireland (HSCNI) workforce needs.  It is recognised that there are now more pharmacy students graduating and applying for FTY than there are funded places available, due to the universities increasing their undergraduate intake.  This will unfortunately lead to some students not being able to undertake their FTY training in NI and having to train in Great Britain. The Department of Health does not commission pharmacy undergraduate places and has no influence on the universities’ student intake. The two universities are asked to work with the Northern Ireland Centre for Pharmacy Learning and Development (NICPLD) who manage the FTY selection and admission process, to discuss and manage numbers.  NICPLD regularly engages with both universities to ensure that they have sufficient information about the FTY selection process and information is also available on their website at the following link: https://www.nicpld.org/courses/fty/2627.asp The schools of pharmacy at both NI universities make their students aware that gaining an FTY placement in NI is now a competitive process because undergraduate numbers have risen despite being advised that 200 FTY places are available in NI. Students are advised to apply for FTY places in Great Britain as well as NI, to secure a place. Consideration to increase capacity in the FTY programme is currently constrained by both funding and the capacity within the HSC to provide the independent prescribing element of the training. This will remain under review.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any assessment his Department has made of the potential impact on freedom of religion and equality rights where individuals recently granted refugee status are issued eviction notices from asylum accommodation, during the month of Ramadan; and (ii) whether he intends to implement a temporary pause on such evictions for the duration of Ramadan 2026.
answer_text: Immigration, including the Move On policy, is not devolved and is a matter for the Home Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any plans his Department has to strengthen specialist training for teachers working in Specialist Provision in Mainstream (SPiMs) settings; (ii) whether he intends to introduce minimum specialist training requirements for such posts; and (iii) any funding allocated, or planned to be allocated, in the 2026–2027 budget to support additional SEN-specific professional development for the SPiMs workforce.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) provides extensive professional development for specialist provisions. The Specialist Provision Service (SPS), is a multi-service team, providing integrated support for Specialist Provisions in Mainstream Schools. SPS works to ensure that staff in specialist provisions are effectively supported in meeting the challenge of delivering high-quality provision and educational experiences for our children and young people.A comprehensive, professional learning and development programme is available, delivered both online and in person for senior leadership, teachers, classroom assistants and parents. Professional Learning and Development opportunities focus on the capacity building of all staff. The EA has advised that it has a range of programmes across all phases (early years, Primary and Post primary) designed to support settings with: Initial set up, quality of provision and teaching and learning, supporting learning and curriculum, baselining and assessment, inclusion and wellbeing, social skills, attention and engagement, supporting Autistic children and young people with Severe Learning Difficulties and supporting Social, Behavioural, Emotional and Wellbeing needs. Further to this, it provides accredited TEAM Teach Level 2 training to all staff working in Specialist Provisions Special.  This is renewed annually. For pupils in other types of Specialist Provisions, the EA’s support services have developed an enhanced professional learning whole-school framework, called “Connecting & Caring Across the Whole School Community”. This training can also be accessed via the SDS online platform.   Whilst this framework is to support the embedding of a trauma informed and nurturing whole school community ethos and culture for responding to behaviours that require intervention, there is recognition that some of the more complex pupil profiles require a training package that includes an aspect of risk reduction involving physical intervention in the form of prompts, guides and restrictive physical interventions (RPI). Therefore the Specialist Provisions Service can offer Team Teach Level 1 and 2 to settings that meet the required indicators of need.Please see the link below for SPS Professional Learning and Development opportunities:25-26 SPS PL&DWithin the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan, there is an action to “design and implement a professional learning programme for teaching staff” as well as to “design and implement a universal SEN training programme targeted at all frontline staff.” These actions are still in the early stages of development and will be progressed in conjunction with the TransformED changes.As part of the enhanced package of support being made available to schools who develop specialist provisions for September 2026 the EA is offering SEN training for up to 20% of teaching staff in the mainstream school and up to 5 classroom assistants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of teachers currently working in Specialist Provision in Mainstream (SPiMs) settings; (ii) a breakdown of those teachers by Education Authority area; and (iii) the number of teachers who hold additional specialist qualifications in special educational needs or inclusion, over and above their initial teacher education qualification, including the nature of those qualifications.
answer_text: At present the Department of Education (DE) does not hold centralised data on the settings in which individual teachers are employed, other than the schools at which they are based.If their role qualifies for a special needs allowance, DE will be informed so that it can be paid. However, schools would never specify the reason for the allowance, and as the paying authority, DE would not be required to know this detail either.In terms of qualifications these are all checked now by the General Teaching Council (Northern Ireland) since 2002 and they would only check the initial teaching qualifications to ensure a teacher is qualified to teach.  It is not possible to confirm the existence of any other specialist qualifications mentioned, except in circumstances where those qualifications have been duly furnished by the teachers themselves.Subsequent to the initial response being issued yesterday, the department has received updated information from the Education Authority as follows:Education Authority AreaNo. of operating classes 25/26No. of core teachers 25/26Armagh195194Ballymena159159Belfast112113Dundonald 159159Omagh 9494Total719719

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given the serious and evolving nature of sextortion and online sexual exploitation affecting children and young people, (i) to detail any engagement his Department has had with the PSNI to develop a coordinated approach to prevention, early intervention and victim support; and (ii) whether any formal joint guidance or response protocols are in place to assist schools managing these incidents.
answer_text: Officials engage with PSNI officers as part of the ongoing work to take forward the cross-departmental Online Safety Strategy. At an operational level, the Education Authority’s Child Protection Support Service (CPSS) has advised that it engages with the PSNI via several Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland (SBNI) sub-groups, including the SBNI Digital Safety committee and Child Exploitation committee. In addition to this, CPSS co-ordinates with the PSNI via the 5 local SBNI area safeguarding panels. CPSS has also distributed bespoke communication to schools in partnership with the PSNI/SBNI/National Crime Agency on Financially Motivated Sexual Extortion and AI generated images. Previously, the CPSS has worked alongside the PSNI and developed a webinar for teachers on Child Sexual Exploitation.The department issues safeguarding guidance to schools, which is informed by other key safeguarding stakeholders. The EA operates a helpline where advice and support is provided to school safeguarding teams, including in the areas of online sexual exploitation by experienced social work staff within CPSS. Where schools have identified an immediate risk of harm, they should inform the PSNI and/ or their local Health Trust Gateway Team.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in light of the Holywell Trust survey showing support in Donegal, west Tyrone, and west Derry for official recognition of the area as a single economic sub-region and greater cross-border cooperation in health, transport and environmental management, to detail any steps she will take with her Dublin counterpart to advance this initiative.
answer_text: I welcome the findings of the Holywell Trust survey which showed that more than two thirds of respondents agree that the region of Donegal, West Tyrone and West Derry should be officially recognised by the British and Irish Governments as one economic sub-region, and recognised that there should be an integrated cross-border approach in the areas of health services, transport infrastructure and environmental management.The all-island economy has tremendous, unrealised potential.  By pooling resources and expertise, we can build stronger, more competitive economies and deliver long-term benefits for businesses and citizens.A Memorandum of Understanding signed by this Department and the Department for Enterprise, Tourism and Employment in November 2025, seeks to build on the positive working relationship between the two Departments around areas of mutual interest in advancement of the all-Ireland economy. This partnership is already driving progress through initiatives such as Shared Island Funding and Peace Plus and allowing us to develop common policy approaches to all-Ireland trade issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any specific plans and mechanisms in place to ensure that children in Irish medium schools, in full language immersion settings, receive support that is linguistically and pedagogically appropriate.
answer_text: My Department recognises the unique requirements of Irishmedium immersion education and is actively taking forward a programme of work to provide appropriate and effective support for children and teachers in the sector.As part of the ongoing development of a dedicated IrishMedium Education (IME) Strategy, officials are working closely with Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG), the Education Authority (EA), Altram and other stakeholders to ensure that the specific linguistic and pedagogical needs of the sector are fully addressed. This includes work relating to resources, assessment, teacher supply, SEN, and broader school sustainability.Teachers in the IME sector typically qualify through specialist Bachelor of Education or PGCE pathways delivered through Irish, which include immersion specific pedagogy and language development.  IME schools can access professional learning and curriculum support through the Education Authority, CCEA and An tÁisaonad.As set out within TransformED, the Department is committed to investing in the further development of CCEA Computer Adaptive Assessment (CAA) as a formative assessment tool at pupil level and to inform school self-evaluation. An Irish-medium CAA Numeracy assessment and a trans adaptation of the CAA English Numeracy test is currently in development. It is anticipated that this assessment will be operational across Key Stage 2 and 3 by autumn 2028. My Department works closely with CnaG to identify sector needs and to inform the targeting of support across the system, and has appointed a Lead Advisor for IME as part of the ongoing curriculum review.The above examples are not exhaustive but show some of the work that takes place to ensure that pupils receive support tailored to the linguistic and pedagogical requirements of immersion education. My Department is committed to fulfilling its statutory duty under Article 89 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 to encourage and facilitate the development of Irishmedium education. This duty applies across all areas of policy and delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment he has made of the availability of linguistically appropriate assessment tools and evidence-based interventions for pupils in Irish-medium schools.
answer_text: As set out within TransformED, the Department is committed to investing in the further development of CCEA Computer Adaptive Assessment (CAA) as a formative assessment tool at pupil level and to inform school self-evaluation. An Irish-medium CAA Numeracy assessment and a trans adaptation of the CAA English Numeracy test is currently in development. It is anticipated that this assessment will be operational across Key Stage 2 and 3 by autumn 2028. The Irish-medium adaptive Numeracy assessment was piloted with a sample of 158 pupils in 9 schools in January/February 2024 and again in May/June 2025 with 491 pupils across 14 schools. This assessment has been translated and adapted, including accommodations in line with international best practice thus ensuring that it does not disadvantage second-language pupils.Literacy assessments in Irish require a bespoke test. CCEA has worked in collaboration with schools and stakeholders to draw up an assessment framework for an Irish Literacy test which will now be integrated within the assessment tool infrastructure and test development, and piloting can then begin.From 2025-26 school year, interim assessments arrangements are being put in place to identify end of Key Stage pupil performance in literacy and numeracy via paper-based tests. All pupils in all Irish-medium schools in Years 4, 7 and 10 will participate in new Key Stage assessments during each year of the interim arrangements. Pupils in Irish-medium schools will complete two tests at Key Stage 1 (Irish; and Numeracy) and three tests at Key Stage 2 and 3 (Irish, Literacy (English) and Numeracy) to reflect the introduction of English as a formal area of learning in Year 4. The tests will be provided by and marked by CCEA. Pupils will sit the written assessments in early March 2026. The Department will meet the costs of substitute cover for the invigilation of the assessment. CCEA will ensure robust development and quality assurance processes are in place. Irish Medium practitioners are currently drafting the tests. These teams will tailor the language within the tests to both the standard of the Levels of Progression, as well as the age-appropriate Irish vocabulary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to bring forward amendments to the Chronically Sick and Disabled Persons (Northern Ireland) Act 1978 to reflect modern service models and the needs of young people with complex disabilities transitioning from special schools into adult services.
answer_text: I am committed to reviewing the Chronically Sick and Disabled Persons (NI) Act 1978 and any related legislation during the first year of implementing the three-year Service Delivery Plan (SDP) for the Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM), to determine whether legislative change is required to better reflect changing demographics and modern models of support for people with complex disabilities. While I recognise the case for exploring legislative reform in the area of transitions, progressing new primary legislation within the remainder of the current mandate presents practical challenges. Any decision to bring forward amendments will be informed by the outcome of the LDSM consultation and the subsequent legislative review.My officials are currently analysing responses to the recent public consultation on the LDSM and the SDP. An initial high level summary report was published on my Department’s website in January 2026, with a full analysis report expected to follow in Spring 2026. The findings from this will be used to further refine and finalise the LDSM and SDP. Once finalised, they will inform the scope and sequencing of the legislative review. I will also wish to take cognisance of any relevant recommendations emanating from the Muckamore Abbey Hospital Inquiry.Any future legislative reform must be evidence-based, properly sequenced and aligned with wider system reform already underway across Health and Social Care. It will require cross-Governmental involvement (spanning Health, Education, Economy and Communities) given the cross-cutting nature of transition arrangements and services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any equality impact assessment that was undertaken, including a summary of any findings, in relation to the reduction in Local Growth Fund support for services assisting people with learning disabilities and autism.
answer_text: The Ministry for Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) is the Accountable Department for the Local Growth Fund, and as such responsibility to undertake any impact assessments sits with them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement that has taken place between his Department and the Department for the Economy in relation to the impact of Local Growth Fund reductions on specialist employment support for people with learning disabilities and autism.
answer_text: Engagement in relation to the Local Growth Fund has taken place through cross-Departmental structures led by the Department of Finance, through the Funding Policy Inter-Departmental Working Group.Through this mechanism, officials from my Department and other Executive Departments, including the Department for the Economy, have been involved in discussions regarding the transition from the UK Shared Prosperity Fund to the Local Growth Fund and the potential implications that changes to the funding profile may have for specialist employment support for people with learning disabilities and autism.The impact of the Local Growth Fund funding profile was discussed at Executive level in January 2026, following which the Executive collectively wrote to the Secretary of State for Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government, raising concerns regarding the resource allocation of the Local Growth Fund.The Department of Finance continues to lead engagement on the Local Growth Fund on behalf of the Executive and officials from my Department will continue to participate in cross-Departmental discussions as further detail on the operation and delivery of the fund becomes available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has considered extending its planned legislative provision for young people, to include those with complex needs leaving special schools.
answer_text: The statutory assessment and support arrangements that I announced in June, as part of my overall reform proposals, are intended to apply to all eligible young adults in Further Education.  Many Special School leavers already attend our Further Education Colleges and my reforms are intended to lead to improved access and participation for school leavers with Special Educational Needs.      A large proportion of leavers transition onto Department of Health funded pathways as they consider that these better meet their needs.  I can only make legislative changes for provision and supports that are within the remit of the Department for the Economy.  My officials will work with the Department of Health to maximise the coherence across the whole system if they decide to make changes as well.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any guidance his Department has issued to schools, in the last three years, specifically addressing the underachievement of boys; and (ii) any measurable targets in place to reduce the attainment gap between boys and girls.
answer_text: The Department of Education has not issued guidance to schools specifically addressing the underachievement of boys in the last three years. However, I am committed to addressing all forms of educational underachievement through the TransformED strategy, which places a strong emphasis on improving outcomes for all children. TransformED embeds a culture of monitoring, evaluation and quality assurance across all stages of implementation. Progress will be reported through key milestones, including an Annual Progress Report, the first of which will be published in May 2026. The Department recognises that an attainment gap persists between boys and girls. In 2023/24, 75.3% of girls and 68.1% of boys achieved at least five GCSEs at grades A*–C including English and maths. This represents a gender gap of just over seven percentage points in favour of girls, a pattern that has remained broadly consistent over recent years. Factors that impact upon boys’ educational underachievement are complex and addressing this longstanding issue requires collaboration across the wider educational system. In delivering against “A Fair Start” Key Area 5(i), the framework for a “Maximising Potential Hub” has been developed. This will be an interactive framework that will be continuously populated. The Hub will feature regional and international research around identifying and removing those factors which impact upon pupil engagement and resources for teacher professional learning.I also welcome the establishment of Ulster University’s Northern Ireland Boys’ Impact Hub, which builds on the longrunning Taking Boys Seriously research. The Hub will work to embed the evidence-based approach developed through the Taking Boys Seriously research across educational policy, pedagogy, and practice in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any requirements set by his Department for Initial Teacher Education providers in relation to training to address boys' underachievement and male educational disengagement.
answer_text: Initial Teacher Education (ITE) programmes in Northern Ireland are delivered by the four local Higher Education Institutions (HEIs). Each institution undertakes annual reviews to ensure programmes remain current, taking account of developments across the education sector, helping to ensure that student teachers receive comprehensive preparation in all key areas of professional practice.ITE programmes are aligned with the Northern Ireland Curriculum and are currently structured to ensure that new teachers meet the range of professional competences set out by the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland (GTCNI). Every element of ITE can be mapped to one or more of these competences and although they do not explicitly address boys’ underachievement or male educational disengagement, they provide a robust professional framework that equips teachers to respond effectively to any pupil at risk of underachievement or disengagement.It is also important to recognise that ITE marks only the starting point of a teacher’s professional learning journey. Teachers are expected to continue developing their knowledge, skills and practice throughout their careers, supported by ongoing professional development and reflective practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment he has made of the disparity in suicide rates between men and women; (ii) the amount of funding allocated to male-specific suicide prevention initiatives; and (iii) any plans to develop a dedicated men’s health strategy.
answer_text: (i)The 2024 Suicide statistics, published in November 2025, indicated that 230 (79.3 per cent) of the 290 suicide deaths in 2024 were males and 60 (20.7 per cent) were females. The age-standardised mortality rate for males was 28.0 per 100,000 in 2024, while for females, the equivalent rate was 7.1 per 100,000.(ii) Around £12m is allocated annually to suicide prevention. As services are generally provided on a universal basis it is difficult to provide a breakdown of how this budget is broken down in terms of spend on males. However, over £66k was provided to Rainbow Project, Bangor YMCA and Rural Support. Under the PHA small grants programme 30 men’s health projects were funded totaling more than £103k during 2025/2026 with over 1200 beneficiaries.(iii)The position regarding the development of a Northern Ireland Men’s Health Strategy or Action Plan remains unchanged from that given in my answers provided for AQW 18437/22-27 and AQW 35532/22-27. The financial position of the Department remains extremely challenging.I remain supportive of work to improve men’s health and have engaged with a variety of men’s groups since my appointment as Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when she will answer AQW 39563/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 39563/22-27 on 24 February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) whether any assessment has been undertaken of the impact on students of the requirement to sit the UCAT for entry to medical schools in Northern Ireland and the HPAT for entry to medical schools in the Republic of Ireland; and (ii) whether discussions have taken place regarding reciprocal recognition.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of additional Special Educational Needs Coordinator (SENCO) posts, or equivalent full-time release hours, that will be required to deliver the Enhanced Support Model effectively; (ii) any workforce modelling that has been undertaken to determine that figure; and (iii) any specific, ring-fenced funding allocated to ensure that SENCOs are not expected to absorb expanded leadership, data, and multidisciplinary coordination responsibilities, within existing teaching commitments.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised:The Enhanced Support Model is subject to a co-production and engagement process and will be consulted upon in Spring 2026. Given that the final Enhanced Support Model will only be confirmed following completion of this process, it is not possible to provide the detailed information that has been requested at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has made any assessment of whether online appointment booking improves GP practices effectiveness, such as shorter waits and better access.
answer_text: Evidence suggests that offering online appointment booking can support patients and GP practices by offering increased convenience.  Benefits can include fewer visits or telephone calls to make, cancel or reschedule appointments, 24/7 access to the appointment system for patients, increased patient choice and fewer missed appointments.  However, this must be balanced against individual practice capacity, as demand is likely to outweigh available appointments.At this point in time it is not mandatory for GP practices to make online appointments available to their patients.  While all practices have the technology available to enable this, it remains their choice in how they manage and deliver their services within their individual practice.  To support GPs in improving service delivery, my Department has issued a best practice guide, ‘Making All Contacts Count’, which includes recommendations on how practices could maximise their telephony and technology to help meet patient demand.  My Department has commenced negotiations with the NIGPC on the GMS contract for 2026/27. Developing robust measures to improve access to GP services for implementation in 2026/27 will be a core requirement in seeking agreement on the Contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the date on which he will meet with the Minister for Health, regarding supporting children of alcoholics.
answer_text: I received an invitation from the Minister of Health on 24 February 2026 inviting me to meet to discuss support for the health and wellbeing of children of alcoholics, and a meeting will be arranged in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any mental health support available for children who have been impacted by seclusion and restraint.
answer_text: Support services such as EA Being Well, Doing Well; Primary Children Looked After Advisory Service and the Nurturing Approaches in Schools Servicepromote whole school responses that are nurturing and trauma informed. In post primary mainstream, Special and EOTAS educational settings, pupils have access to the Hope Counselling & Therapeutic Service. Pupils can access this service for a range of needs and presenting issues, however specialist mental health support would be available through a range of Health & Social Care Trust services or voluntary and community specialist services.Recognising the importance of supporting children’s emotional wellbeing following any incident involving the use of seclusion or restraint is essential. A key element of this support is the use of restorative conversations, which provide an opportunity for children to reflect on what has happened in a safe, structured and compassionate way.  With this in mind, restorative conversations form the final stage within the Team Teach ‘Stages of Distress’ and Support model, following crisis, recovery, and depression. This stage plays an essential therapeutic role in helping children process their emotions, repair relationships, and move forward positively. When delivered effectively, it ensures that feelings are acknowledged and validated, reducing the likelihood of incidents reoccurring.These conversations can involve the child and a trusted professional, or where appropriate, peers or colleagues. They are held only when the child has fully recovered from the incident and is ready to engage. Sessions take place in a calm, neutral environment with sufficient time and privacy to support open and honest communication.To ensure the process is accessible to every child, staff may use adapted communication approaches such as visual supports, roleplay, social stories, sensory activities, or creative methods like art and music. This allows children to express their feelings and experiences in a way that feels safe and familiar to them.Where appropriate, learning from the conversation may also be used to update the child’s Individual Support Plan, helping to identify triggers and introduce new strategies to support their future wellbeing.  Through this restorative process, children impacted by seclusion or restraint can receive meaningful emotional support that strengthens relationships, promotes healing, and contributes positively to their overall mental health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in respect of Classroom Assistants employed by the Education Authority (EA) to detail, (i) the number engaged on Temporary Engagement Forms, in each of the last five years, broken down by EA region; (ii) of those staff, the number who subsequently became permanent under the Fixed Term Workers (Prevention of Less Favourable Treatment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2002; (iii) the number (a) of Classroom Assistants engaged on Temporary Engagement Forms; and (b) subsequently made permanent under the Fixed Term Workers Regulations, who were offered the option to
answer_text: Temporary Engagement Forms are intended for shortterm, emergency use, allowing schools and services to secure immediate cover for unexpected absences or to fill vacancies that arise at very short notice. The table below provides the number of Classroom Assistants engaged on Temporary Engagement Forms the last five year.Region2025-26*2024-252023-242022-232021-22Belfast1,2961,5191,6391,4821,348North Eastern1,5061,8441,6781,6611,966South Eastern1,7061,8631,6271,6501,689Southern2,5132,6632,1462,0392,447Western1,6691,7231,6051,6551,857Total 8,6909,6128,6958,4879,307Note: * 2025/26 figures relate to the year up to the date of the report: 25/02/26The Education Authority (EA) does not hold this information.(iii)a	When Classroom Assistants are appointed, they are offered contract options in line with a collective agreement, known as the ‘Term Time Agreement’. These options are: (1) 52 weeks, (2) 52 weeks with unpaid leave during school closures or (3) term time. EA applies this circular routinely as part of the appointment process. In relation to those engaged on a temporary engagement form within the last 5 years, 9,855 (22%) had a 52-week contract and 34,936 (78%) had a term time contract. 	(iii)b 	The EA does not hold this information.  	As noted in (iii)a above, when Classroom Assistants are appointed, they are offered contract options in line with the ‘Term Time’ collective agreement. The EA has recently been undertaking a streamlined temporary to permanent process in order to stabilise the classroom-based workforce.  This exercise has been carried out in line with the Fixed Term Workers Regulations which requires employers to convey permanent employment status to temporary staff with more than 4 years’ service (unless particular conditions are met). The rights for employees under this legislation is to permanency in the role being undertaken at the effective date and the terms and conditions associated with that role remain unchanged. The £10 million cost estimate of extending Term Time Agreement options to affected staff is based on the assumption of an increase of 14 weeks per year (i.e. moving from term time 38 weeks to 52 weeks) for each grade of Classroom Assistant (CA).  The calculation also assumes that the CAs are part-time, working 18 hours per week.  Details of the estimated costs are included in the table below: GradeNumber of staff with 4 yrs service in relevant groupCost per person of moving from 38 to 52 weeks**Total CostCA775£4,837£3,748,985SEN  CA1,044£5,328£5,562,265Additional SEN CA98£5,880£576,232Total Increase£9,887,482Note: ** Figures include employers’ costs of 34%The figure of c£10m quoted in the table above is based on an assumption of average contractual hours of 18 per week.  The actual total cost would be higher if the individual contracted hours are higher.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has carried out to evaluate the accessibility of public transport for people living with disabilities, including how any assessments are used to inform future transport strategy and service improvements.
answer_text: I have identified “Including People” as one of the key strands of my “Foundations for a Better Future” programme and this work is focussed primarily on identifying and addressing the transport challenges experienced by older, Deaf, and disabled users. The first phase of this work identified a range of issues of concern to these groups and culminated in an inclusion conference at Stormont Hotel and a training initiative which saw the entire senior team of the Department undertaking disability equality training. My officials have commenced work on the second phase of this foundation to continue to make progress on those issues which remain.My Department provides funding (£90,000 in 2025/26) to the Inclusive Mobility Transport Advisory Committee (IMTAC). IMTAC represents the views of older, Deaf, and disabled transport users and provides ongoing advice and guidance to the Department and other stakeholders (such as Translink) on transport and travel matters as they relate to these groups of users.IMTAC engages with a range of officials right across my Department on an ongoing basis to identify, discuss, and resolve issues relating to travel and transport which affect those people they represent. Additionally, I meet directly with IMTAC regularly to discuss progress against issues which they have identified as being of priority concern.In addition to engagement with my Department, I also require Translink to engage directly with IMTAC representatives and this takes place through a variety of mechanisms. This engagement has resulted in more accessible public transport infrastructure across a range of areas through direct input to: new stations and station redevelopment; procurement of more widely accessible vehicles (including the recent introduction of dual wheelchair buses on Metro); and ongoing operational improvements to services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether her Department has undertaken any assessment of the Police Service of Northern Ireland’s compliance with its statutory language and equality duties, including in respect of the use of the Irish language, in relation to its branding, logos and public signage; and (ii) to detail any engagement her Department has had with the Chief Constable or the Northern Ireland Policing Board on this matter.
answer_text: The Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) is a designated public authority and so is required to comply with statutory language and equality duties and responsibilities accordingly. Any assessments of these duties would be undertaken by the Northern Ireland Policing Board (NIPB).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given that almost two-thirds of respondents to his Department’s consultation opposed the proposal to scrap AS-Levels and move to a fully linear A-Level, whether he will now withdraw that proposal.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that qualifications in Northern Ireland remain relevant and aligned to the needs of our students, as they progress into further study, employment or training.In relation to my proposals to change CCEA GCSEs and A levels to a two-year structure, there are a broad range of views. I will reflect carefully on the views of stakeholders and will consider all the evidence before reaching a final decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that statutory nursery schools are treated on an equal footing with statutory primary and post-primary schools, including in workforce planning and wider policy development initiatives, where appropriate.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department ensures all statutory schools, including nursery schools, are treated on an equal footing in relation to policy development. All statutory schools are invited to inform policy through surveys, focus groups, engagement events and formal responses to public consultations as appropriate.  The draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy is currently out to public consultation and a series of in-person and online consultation events, including specific events for pre-school stakeholders, are being delivered to engage and encourage responses from all key stakeholders.  Initial dates have already been communicated to all pre-school settings and dates for further events will be communicated when arrangements for them have been finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 39880/22-27, to detail (i) any steps he will take to introduce a structured, region-wide framework for life skills and independent living skills development for autistic pupils, in both mainstream and special schools; (ii) whether he will publish minimum standards and statutory guidance to ensure consistency of provision across all Education Authority areas; and (iii) how he will measure and report on outcomes for autistic young people in relation to successful transition to further education, employment and independent living, including the ev
answer_text: (i) Individual schools are responsible for ensuring, through the graduated response framework, that autistic children and young people have opportunities to develop life and independence skills.  Each individual school will provide its own curriculum programmes around life skills depending on the needs and pathway for the young person.  (ii) I have no plans to publish minimum standards or statutory guidance in this area at this time.  (iii) The Local Impact Teams became operational in September 2025, alongside the introduction of the new Information System designed to record outcomes for autistic children and young people. As this system is in its early stages of implementation, it is not yet possible to produce reliable or meaningful data to support evaluation of evidence-based programmes.  Outcome reporting will be developed as the system matures and sufficient data becomes available.Evaluation by Middletown Centre for Autism demonstrates strong impact across participants engaging in bespoke support programmes offering life skills training.
