The Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda Outcomes Framework, published June 2025 contains two specific indicators of post-school outcomes: the percentage of young people with SEN and or disability transitioning successfully to further / higher education, training, employment or day opportunities; and the percentage of young people with SEN and or disability in sustained employment or meaningful engagement post-education.  The data required to underpin these indicators is being considered as part of a Data Development Agenda.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to prepare the health and social care system for the projected rise in dementia prevalence and associated costs, as highlighted by the Commissioner for Older People for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department acknowledges that dementia is one of the biggest challenges to our health and social care system in Northern Ireland, with the region projected to have the largest increase in the number of people living with dementia across the UK by 2040 and a rise in cost from £800 million in 2020 to around £2.3 billion in 2040. My department’s HSC Reset Plan sets structural foundations relevant to current challenges in dementia care. Key commitments include shifting more care into primary and community settings, which is essential for the current waits for dementia diagnosis in some areas. The plan includes initiatives to improve productivity, workforce stabilisation, and patient safety, helping prepare the system for rising demand from dementia and other age-related conditions.The implementation of a neighbourhood model will focus on shifting services closer to home and enabling earlier support which is critical for dementia care. There is also an emphasis on investment in the community care and social care sector where significant levels of care and support are provided to people living with dementia.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he will take to increase the availability of (i) nursing beds; and (ii) domiciliary care packages, for older people, given the reported decline in provision relative to population growth.
answer_text: Across the region HSC Trusts advise that there are currently no plans to increase nursing bed provision within the Statutory Sector. They have however advised as and when independent sector providers develop proposals relating to the increase of care home bed capacity, they engage with them and consider in line with contractual requirements and tariff.  Trusts have been supported to access additional beds with the availability of growth funding.HSC Trusts report that they frequently publish market engagement notices to explore increasing of bed stock.  Market engagement notices are issued to encourage providers to make additional care home beds available by signalling current demand, outlining projected need, and inviting expressions of interest from the sector.I acknowledge the need to increase bed availability and make better use of existing capacity. The Social Care Collaborative Reform Board is progressing a regional digital solution for care home bed availability. The solution will improve communication regarding the location of available beds and provide an improved approach to data gathering and resource identification.My Department is committed to the review of homecare through the work of the Social Care Collaborative Reform Board.  We need to grow the homecare workforce in line with the growth in the older persons population.  I continue to prioritise the need to roll out the Real Living Wage to the independent sector as I have promised, as I know this will make a tangible difference and will enable the HSCTs and the independent sector to recruit and retain social care staff.  I appreciate that currently the homecare sector is losing staff to other sectors such as hospitality and retail. The roll out of the Early Review Teams (ERTs) across all five Trusts has ensured that an early and timely review of an individual’s needs takes place.  This ensures that individuals have the correct support in place to enable them to remain as independent as possible.  Any surplus hours identified through the review process are recycled back into the system to take others off the waiting list.Care Line Live is a digital system that is used to manage and ensure tight governance of the five HSCTs in- house home care system. It has been implemented at scale within the Trusts, delivering measurable gains in capacity, efficiency, and quality of care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 39043/22-27, to detail (i) whether his Department has undertaken any independent evaluation of the impact of widespread classroom use of generative AI and digital technologies on pupils’ reading fluency, extended writing and critical thinking outcomes; (ii) any safeguards that will be put in place to prevent over-reliance on AI tools in assessment and homework, including in qualifications regulated by the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment; (iii) whether the forthcoming AI guidance will include clear limits on AI use in ear
answer_text: My Department has not, to date, commissioned an independent evaluation on the use and impact of the use of Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) in the classroom.  However, one of the Cross Cutting Initiative’s under the RAISE Programme is the Reading with AI Research Project.  This is a research study that will provide an opportunity to see how AI tools can support literacy. The study is led by Oxford Brookes University, using Amira Learning, a reading support tool, aligned with research-informed approaches to early literacy and powered by AI.  A research report will be provided to my Department by Oxford Brookes University at the close of the project.As with all curricular materials and tools, it is a matter for schools to decide how they are used and for how long.  The guidance being developed will encourage the use of GenAI in a safe, secure and supportive manner. GenAI presents opportunities to improve people’s lives, including by making our public services better. Whilst it has impact and value, there are also risks that need to be proactively managed in all phases of school and the guidance will support schools to do this.It is also important that all education settings take time to review, revise and align all related policies in keeping with this guidance not just digital/ICT policies. Education settings’ policies may include, for example, strategies and practices in the areas of teaching, learning, assessment and reporting, administration and management, self-evaluation and improvement, and teacher professional learning.  School policy reviews should be specific for the age/stage of learners and the phase/type of school.Schools may wish to identify someone to take the lead on GenAI to advise school leaders, staff and governors. Their role should be to monitor and evaluate the impact of school policies on practice and review regularly as required.The Education Authority (EA) is bringing forward plans to run a large scale Proof of Concept for the safe use of GenAI with students in Key Stage 4 and 5.  In conjunction with AI providers, EA is working to develop GenAI in the form of guided learning i.e. the software will establish a student’s knowledge of a particular subject area and offer learning opportunities based on that knowledge.  My “Strong Foundations” literacy framework makes clear that children require sustained, explicit teaching to develop reading fluency, extended writing and critical engagement with texts, and that digital tools, including emerging GenAI, are intended to complement, rather than replace these core literacy experiences. The Framework highlights the need for welltaught digital, media and information literacies so that pupils can evaluate and use technologies responsibly and avoid overreliance on them.  This is consistent with TransformED NI, which places strong literacy and numeracy at the centre of educational excellence.  The Department’s approach to GenAI will continue to align with the principle that tec

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 39989/22-27, to detail (i) whether his Department has reviewed any research evidence on the impact of structured philosophical enquiry, including Philosophy for Children, on pupils’ reasoning, literacy and critical-thinking outcomes; (ii) any specific elements of philosophy, ethics or moral reasoning being considered for inclusion within Learning for Life and Work, as part of the curriculum reform process; and (iii) whether the Northern Ireland Curriculum Taskforce Advisory Committee has been asked to consider the role of explicit critical-thin
answer_text: My Department’s Strong Foundations: A Literacy Framework for Primary Schools published in December 2025 highlights the benefits of Philosophy for Children and Socratic seminars in the development of oracy skills within the curriculum to promote equitable classroom participation, enhance purposeful dialogue and foster inclusive, talk-rich classrooms. Amongst other articles, it draws schools’ attention to the following important research:R. Miles (2025), Socratic Seminar: Bringing Ancient Wisdom to Modern Elementary Classrooms Through Engaging Discussion. Edu.com.Midwest Teachers’ Institute (2025), Teaching Students to Think, Not Just Answer: Socratic Seminars in Action.K. J. Topping, S. Trickey & P. Cleghorn (2019), A Teacher’s Guide to Philosophy for Children. London: Routledge.The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum drew on a range of research papers and curriculum theory.  The Review also included significant stakeholder engagement including school visits, 70 focus groups, 149 submissions, and a teacher survey with over 1,400 responses. As noted previously in AQW 39989/2227, Subject Working Groups (SWGs), operating under the Taskforce Advisory Committee, are developing updated curriculum content across subjects. While no specific philosophical programmes have been prescribed, key aspects of philosophy, ethics and moral reasoning will inform elements of the revised curriculum.The Northern Ireland Curriculum Taskforce Advisory Committee (TAC) has been asked to consider how “critical thinking” is developed within the knowledge-rich model, asset out by the Strategic Review.  Critical thinking is treated as a “cognitive capability” that arises from secure disciplinary knowledge, rather than a transferable, generic skill to be taught and measured separately across subjects.  The Advisory Committee will ensure that subject frameworks and shared pedagogical principles make clear how disciplinary ways of knowing (e.g., historical, scientific, geographical, literary) give rise to pupils’ capacity to evaluate claims and reason well.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any actions her Department is taking to help households manage electricity and gas costs; (ii) any measures in place to support households installing renewable energy systems, including solar panels; (iii) how her Department's current policies compare with other parts of the UK; and (iv) whether her Department has any changes planned, to align Northern Ireland more closely with best practice in other jurisdictions.
answer_text: (i) Regulated electricity and natural gas tariffs are subject to regular review and approval by the Utility Regulator, in consultation with the Department for the Economy and the Consumer Council.The UR’s role is to protect customers by ensuring domestic consumers pay fair costs along with a capped profit margin for electricity or natural gas from the regulated supplier. (ii) There is currently no Departmental support available for households installing renewable energy systems, including solar panels. However, under the Windsor Framework, VAT relief for energy saving materials previously available only in Britain has been extended to the North. As a result, a zero rate of VAT now applies to the installation of energy saving materials such as solar panels. (iii) As energy policy is a devolved matter in the North, it operates under a separate market and regulatory framework, and as a result, support arrangements may differ from those in Britain.(iv) My officials are in regular contact with colleagues in the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) and other government departments, to share best practice and explore opportunities to inform renewable energy policy development in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how Health and Social Care Trusts assess and respond to requests for respite and emergency care for children with severe disabilities when primary carers are temporarily incapacitated; (ii) any steps that are being taken to ensure that families in these circumstances can access timely care to prevent breakdowns in care or avoidable hospital admissions; (iii) the number of children who require such emergency or temporary respite support; (iv) the average waiting times for such care, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (v) whether his Depa
answer_text: When primary carers are temporarily incapacitated and Trusts receive requests for respite and emergency care for children with severe learning disabilities the Trust Social Workers will engage in completing an updated assessment of family circumstances to consider the needs of the child or young person, and how best to support the family, taking account of the individual circumstances of the family. This process will involve engaging with children and young people as appropriate, parents, wider family networks, and relevant professionals and services to develop packages of support which are responsive to the needs of families.  The five Trusts have procedures in place to respond in a timely way to ensure access to support that prevents family breakdown, in the best interests of families and the young people open to the service. Trusts’ actions are underpinned by the premise that children/young people are best cared for by parents/carers and their wider family networks in times of crisis, to avoid unnecessary breakdowns of family units, and admissions to care and/or hospital. All five Trusts seek to provide timely access to support, notwithstanding pressures on services to ensure minimal disruption and pressure on families.  An exact figure is unable to be provided at this time - considering that these situations are responded to on a case by case basis, and also arise within a broad range of contexts.  Considering that the response to such care and respite requests often depends on the family’s circumstances and involves a range of services, supports and actions by services, the Trusts are unable to provide an average wait time for service provision for these situations, as this will be dependent on a variety of factors and the individual circumstances of the case.  However, all Trusts do attempt to respond promptly to emergency respite requests as and when they would arise.My Department’s strategic response to the increasing level of need for Children with Disability across Northern Ireland has been set out through a regional Framework for Children with Disability. This has been partly progressed through the Regional Children’s Services Strategic Reform Programme, which my Department is leading on, and which was established in response to Professor Ray Jones’ Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services. A number of the recommendations from Professor Jones’ Review relate to the expansion of services for children with disabilities (CWD), and the Ministerial Investment in the CWD service that was announced in October 2024 has supported Trusts with this. For example, there have been various initiatives developed across all five Trusts to improve family and community support services for children with disability, enhance short break provision and expand targeted care programs. The Trusts have also increased contracts with a range of voluntary and community services to provide additional family support, or breaks from caring through youth cl

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any ways he is using the duties and powers under the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 to work with other Departments and public bodies, to improve the availability of school based counselling and mental health support for children and young people; (ii) any progress made in achieving shared outcomes across Departments for children’s mental health and well being, including the pooling of resources where appropriate, to fund dedicated counsellors in schools; (iii) any data his Department holds on the current gap between d
answer_text: The duties and the powers under the Children’s Services and Co-operation Act should be used where this is identified as an effective way to improve services, reduce duplication and ensure effective use of public funds, however, this does not negate the challenging financial context across both the education and health systems. My officials liaise regularly with health officials, including as part of the Mental Health Strategy - Early Intervention/ Prevention work and the joint DE / DoH Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.There is no proposal within my Department (DE) to fund dedicated counsellors in schools, however the HOPE Service (post-primary school counselling and therapy) is funded, administered and managed by the Education Authority.  This is a longstanding provision is available to all pupils in post-primary mainstream, special schools and EOTAS provisions.My department does not hold information on demand and provision of counselling services for children and young people across other government, voluntary and community sectors.The Education Authority advised there were 321 pupils on the waiting list on 31 December 2025 for the HOPE Service (following an initial assessment).While I recognise that education has a part to play in supporting children and young people in respect of their emotional health and wellbeing, the provision of universal counselling support would primarily be a matter for the Department of Health (DoH) as part of wider mental health services and therapeutic provisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) steps his Department is taking to ensure addiction treatment services, including centres such as Northlands in Derry, are adequately funded to meet current and rising demand; (ii) ways his Department is supporting the development of addiction centres of excellence to provide integrated, high-quality residential and non-residential care; (iii) assessment that has been made of the long-term impact of investing in community-based addiction services on the health service, justice system, and social care; and (iv) timescale that is envisaged for progr
answer_text: Ensuring addiction services are adequately funded remains a central priority under the Executive’s 10year Substance Use Strategy Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery (2021–31), which commits to improving access to highquality treatment and reducing substancerelated harm. The Substance Use Strategic Commissioning and Implementation Plan 2024–2028, also provides the framework for recommissioning services, strengthening community detoxification provision, and establishing clear pathways of care to meet rising and more complex demand. The Department is taking forward the recommendations of the Independent Review of Tier 4 Addiction Services, which sets out a model for a regionally consistent network of specialist assessment, treatment, and recovery services. This includes enhancing community detoxification, improving integration between mental health and addiction services, strengthening crisis response, and ensuring specialist residential and nonresidential services operate cohesively as part of a centres of excellence approach.Evidence underpinning both the Strategy and Implementation Plan shows that investment in effective communitybased services reduces pressure on hospitals, the justice system, and social care. For example, alcohol misuse alone is estimated to cost Northern Ireland £900 million annually, rising to £1.5 billion when drugrelated harms are included; community interventions are essential to reducing these pressures. The Tier 4 Review further highlights that unplanned detoxification in acute hospitals places significant demand on services, and that improved pathways can reduce repeat presentations and improve outcomes.Finally, with regard to Northlands, any funding decisions for this service must be considered within the recommendations of the Independent Review of Tier 4 services and available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any steps his Department is taking to educate pupils, particularly teenage boys, about the risks of sextortion and online sexual exploitation; (ii) any steps being taken to support and train teachers and parents, to recognise and respond to these risks; (iii) whether age-appropriate guidance on online safety, sextortion, and digital consent is included in the current curriculum; and (iv) any data his Department holds on incidents of sextortion among school-aged children, including how this informs policy and preventive measures.
answer_text: The focus is on promoting online safety through the curriculum and guidance for schools. My department provides a range of supports to educational settings including through issuing safeguarding guidance and the provision of resources on the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) hub which are available and accessible to pupils.Guidance and support is available from CCEA through its Online Safety section, which covers areas such as sextortion, child sexual exploitation and online grooming. There is very useful support available from Safer Schools Northern Ireland platform, the Safeguarding Board’s Online Safety Hub, and schools can also contact the from the Education Authority Child Protection Support Service.The digital literacy skills embedded across the curriculum help prepare pupils for a changing digital world. Supporting this, the CCEA hub provides a range of age-appropriate resources providing guidance and materials for schools in exploring safe internet use, and which cover topics including social media, sharing of and altering images, staying safe online, cyberbullying and sexting.I also recently launched the Safer Schools Northern Ireland Digital Proficiency Course across Northern Ireland schools and education settings, which promotes a whole school, whole family approach to understanding online harms.The department does not hold this data however, officials draw on a variety of sources and published information in order to strengthen education safeguarding guidance and policy development work in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any current (i) guidelines; and (ii) procedures, governing the (a) regulation; (b) monitoring; and (c) processing, of letter correspondence sent to and from prisoners in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: (i) The Northern Ireland Prison Service manage letter correspondence under the Prison and Young Offender Centres Rule (Northern Ireland) 1995.(ii) All mail entering or leaving the Prison will be recorded.(a) The regulation can be found in; Prison Rule 68 paragraphs 1-9 Personal letters and Visits and Prison Rule 72 Correspondence in connection with Legal matters.(b) Monitoring is applied when deemed appropriate and necessary to do so under Prison Rule 68A paragraph 1-6 Interceptions of communications.(c) All mail entering and leaving the Prison will be opened and the contents checked for the safety and security of the Prison, with the exception of legal correspondence, unless it is deemed necessary and proportionate to apply Prison Rule 68A to a legal correspondence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has undertaken any modelling, or impact assessment, of the cumulative effect of the caps on Housing Executive Primary and Other Social Needs Factors, particularly in cases involving large families with multiple dependants with complex needs.
answer_text: As part of the ongoing Fundamental Review of Allocations, the Housing Executive is currently progressing the review of Primary Social Needs criteria and points. This includes modelling and impact assessment to inform the Housing Executive’s recommendations for my consideration in due course.I refer the Member to AQW 31612/22-27 for the position on Other Social Needs Factors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether additional funding, or administrative support, has been provided to voluntary youth organisations to meet the new compliance requirements; and (ii) any assessment made of the impact of these requirements on frontline youth service delivery.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that the compliance requirements to be eligible for youth service grant aid assistance has not changed since the “Funding Scheme for Regional and Local Voluntary Youth Organisations” was introduced in 2020.  It is incumbent on organisations to demonstrate, as part of their application for funding, they have the following requirements in place:appropriate governance arrangements.child protection and safeguarding arrangements which include adults at risk of harm, if the youthwork is being delivered to 18-25 year olds. appropriate tenure for the delivery of the youth services.appropriate insurance and health and safety arrangements.Local voluntary youth organisations are supported to meet with compliance requirements by regional voluntary youth organisations through funding allocated by the EA.Assessment of the compliance requirements is completed by the EA with signposting and support provided to organisations through the conditional offer stage of their application.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of whether the current multi-agency approach to bonfires provides clear and effective environmental leadership; and (ii) whether he will consider designating his Department as the lead coordinating authority where environmental contamination risks arise.
answer_text: The management of annual bonfires is recognised as a challenging and complex issue which requires a range of agreed multiagency preventative and, where appropriate, enforcement activity.While the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) has a valuable role to play in terms of the enforcement of controlled waste and, by extension, any serious environmental risks surrounding bonfires, it does not have the lead role for bonfires, nor is it ‘responsible’ for bonfires.  The NIEA also does not authorise or permit controlled waste to be burned on bonfires.  It is the legal responsibility of the landowner to secure their land to prevent illegal depositing of waste, and the depositing and burning of controlled waste at a bonfire site has the potential to be in breach of The Waste and Contaminated Land (NI) Order 1997. Both NIEA and the councils possess enforcement powers under this Order. Currently there is no consideration being given to designating my Department as a lead authority in relation to bonfires or the environmental contamination risks that potentially occur as a result.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any steps his Department will take to reduce the length of time it takes to fill vacancies within the Northern Ireland Civil Service.

To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 38826/22-27, to detail (i) the number of additional appointments anticipated; (ii) any criteria used to identify gaps in expertise; (iii) any process by which additional members are selected; (iv) whether there was an open call for further nominations or expressions of interest; and (v) whether he intends to publish the finalised membership of each working group once appointments are complete.
answer_text: Further appointments to the Subject Working Groups are not anticipated at this stage.  Permission is being sought from members for release of names. Where consent is granted, a list will be published on the Department of Education website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any Northern Ireland-specific modelling, or analysis, that has been undertaken to assess the likely impact of a knowledge-rich curriculum on pupils entitled to free school meals, and lower-attaining learners; (ii) whether any pilot studies have been conducted within Northern Ireland schools; and (iii) how any success will be measured in terms of closing the attainment gap.
answer_text: The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum (Crehan, 2025) drew upon a broad and robust evidence base, including systemlevel performance data, international research, cognitive science, and extensive stakeholder engagement. The Review identified several ways in which the current curriculum framework disadvantages pupils entitled to free school meals and lowerattaining learners.The Review found that the existing curriculum does not sufficiently take account of variations in pupils’ prior knowledge. This shortcoming disproportionately affects those who begin school already behind their peers, including many pupils from socioeconomically deprived backgrounds. It also points to marked inequality and inconsistency between schools, arising from curricular guidance that is overly broad and open to wide interpretation, with the consequence that disadvantaged and lowerattaining learners are most at risk of experiencing a less coherent and less rigorous curriculum offer.Additionally, evidence from other highperforming education systems, as referenced in AQW 36915/2227, demonstrates that a carefully sequenced, knowledgerich curriculum can be particularly beneficial for disadvantaged pupils, ensuring that all learners gain access to the foundational knowledge required for sustained academic progression.My department will establish clear arrangements for the implementation and evaluation of the new curriculum. This will include evaluating how effectively it raises attainment for all pupils and contributes to narrowing the gaps in achievement between different learner groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether his Department holds data on the value of outstanding essential maintenance requests in Voluntary Grammar and Grant Maintained Integrated schools; and (ii) the current estimated maintenance backlog in those sectors; (iii) any capital support available to them; and (iii) whether any assessment has been made of the current funding model creates inequity across the schools’ estate; (iii) whether any assessment has been made of funding inequity across the schools estate.
answer_text: As Voluntary Grammar (VG) and Grant Maintained Integrated (GMI) schools are responsible for their own maintenance, my Department does not hold data on the value of outstanding essential maintenance requests in these schools. VG and GMI schools receive an additional amount per square meter for landlord maintenance under the Common Funding Formula. This funding is fully delegated to the Boards of Governors, who are responsible for the planning and monitoring of expenditure.The Education Authority is currently in year three of a five-year condition survey cycle across the schools’ estate. To date, 40 VG schools have been surveyed and the total backlog for these schools is estimated at £68m. No GMI schools have yet been surveyed.If VG and GMI schools consider that a scheme is beyond routine maintenance, they can submit an Unavoidable Minor Works Application to the Department. These applications will be considered against competing priorities and progressed in line with available budget.Due to budget constraints, capital minor works are currently prioritised on the basis of health and safety, statutory compliance and the need to address urgent and emergency repairs, which could close a school or present a significant health and safety risk.		As part of the Common Funding Scheme, the Landlord Maintenance Factor, which is allocated only to VG and GMI schools, is assessed against the actual amounts spent by the EA on landlord maintenance for controlled and maintained schools.  This assessment is uprated for diseconomies of scale (please refer to paragraphs 4.13 and 5.11 of the Common Funding Scheme)				The purpose of this annual exercise is to assess the relative maintenance funding allocated to VG and GMI schools as compared to controlled and maintained schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any reports, relating to the suitability of education, or safeguarding of children being electively home educated, received by the Education Authority, in each of the last five years; (ii) the number of times the Education Authority has intervened as a result; and (iii) the form those interventions took.
answer_text: (i) The Education Authority (EA) has provided the information below:YearChildren Known to the EA as Electively Home EducatedConcerns Received Relating to Suitability of Education2024/20251220242023/20241060132022/2023907222021/2022808162020/202171016(ii) (iii) The EA responds to safeguarding concerns about a home educated child in the same way it would for any child attending a school, in accordance with its Child Protection Policy. Health and Social Care Trusts are responsible for investigating and responding to Safeguarding concerns in respect of electively home educated children. If concerns are raised about the suitability of the education a home educated child is receiving, the EA will request information from the family and undertake an assessment of the education plan, based on the information voluntarily provided. If the parent or carer demonstrates that the education provided is suitable, the EA records the outcome and arranges to review provision on a 12-month cycle. Where families choose not to engage with the EA, or the information provided is insufficient to make an assessment, a referral made to the Education Welfare Service (EWS). Further details on these processes may be accessed on the EA’s website: Guidelines for EHE 23112020.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 30452/22-27, to detail (i) the number of harm-reduction actions, within the Substance Use Strategy, that fall within her Department’s remit; (ii) the number of those actions that have been fully implemented, to date; (iii) any actions that remain outstanding; (iv) any reasons for any delays; and (v) the expected timescales for their completion.
answer_text: The actions set out in the Substance Use Strategy, by their very nature, require a coordinated, crossdepartmental and multiagency response.Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery is a Healthled, crossExecutive strategy that depends on effective collaboration across a wide range of departments and statutory partners, including my own.Within the Strategy, my department contributes to a series of harmreduction actions aimed at preventing drugrelated harm, supporting individuals in contact with the justice system, and tackling the illicit supply of drugs. Many of these actions are already underway and continue to be taken forward in partnership with the Department of Health, the Public Health Agency, the PSNI and other key stakeholders.This work includes:further developing and evaluating ProblemSolving Justice initiatives and examining opportunities for wider implementation.Ongoing participation in the Organised Crime Task Force Drugs Subgroup to coordinate enforcement efforts and target those involved in the illicit supply and distribution of drugs; andcollaborative efforts to enhance the availability of data and evidence on substance use trends through the development of the Drug and Alcohol Monitoring and Information System.We are also working closely with health partners to improve continuity of care and access to appropriate services for people who interact with the justice system, including those transitioning from custody. In addition, we are contributing to wider crossgovernment work aimed at reducing violence, or the threat of violence, towards people who use drugs.As this is a 10year strategy: delivery is phased and ongoing. Progress is overseen by the Substance Use Strategy Programme Board, which provides crossgovernment governance and ensures momentum against the Strategy’s agreed outcomes.My department remains fully committed to working collaboratively with Executive colleagues and partners to progress these actions and support the successful delivery of the wider Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) his assessment of the increasing number of children being electively home educated, and attending independent schools; (ii) how educational progress and suitability of provision for such children is monitored; (iii) whether he is satisfied that current arrangements are sufficient to ensure educational standards and safeguarding; and (iv) any consideration his Department has given to the recent rise in pupils being deregistered from mainstream schools.
answer_text: The legislative position in Northern Ireland is that the responsibility for a child’s education rests with the child’s parents. Parents may therefore choose to educate their child at home or at an independent school rather than at a grant aided school. If parents choose to educate their child at home, they take on full responsibility for ensuring their child receives an efficient full-time education suitable to their needs. Where a concern is raised about the suitability of the education a home educated child is receiving, the EA will request information from the family and will undertake an assessment of the education plan, based on the information voluntarily provided. If the information shared by the parent/carer indicates that the education being provided is appropriate, the EA records the outcome and arranges to review the provision on a 12-month cycle.  Where families choose not to engage with the EA, or it is unable to assess the educational provision due to lack of information, the matter is escalated and a referral is made to the Education Welfare Service (EWS).The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) is responsible for monitoring, inspecting and reporting on the standard of education being provided across all phases of education in Northern Ireland, which includes independent schools. ETI inspection reports help inform my Department’s decision as to whether an independent school should be registered or continue to be registered to provide education.With regards to safeguarding, the EA responds to safeguarding concerns about a home educated child or a pupil at an independent school in the same way it would for any child attending a grant aided school, in line with the EA’s Child Protection Policy. It is the responsibility of the Health and Social Care Trusts to investigate and respond to safeguarding concerns in respect of a child who is home educated.Currently, there is no legislation in Northern Ireland requiring home educating parents to register their child with the EA, or to explain their reasons for choosing to home educate.  This reflects the current position in other UK jurisdictions. The EA holds records of children who have been registered at a school and subsequently deregistered to be home educated, however information is not held on the reasons why parents have chosen to home educate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 39883/22-27, to detail (i) whether his Department has carried out a formal risk assessment on the impact of generative artificial intelligence on pupils’ critical thinking, independent learning and academic integrity; (ii) any specific research, evaluations or international evidence that informed that work; (iii) whether any risks were identified, including how the forthcoming guidance to schools will mitigate any risks identified in practice.
answer_text: The Department has not carried out a formal risk assessment on the impact of generative artificial intelligence on pupils’ critical thinking, independent learning or academic integrity. Generative AI (GenAI) presents both opportunities and risks. The Department is finalising its high-level guidance for schools on the use of AI / GenAI which highlights the potential risks and recommends that schools develop and / or  review their existing policies, avail of teacher training in respect of GenAI, make adjustments to curriculum and assessment policies as needed and do so in safe, ethical and educationally sound manner.The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) has produced guidance for teachers and pupils on "Using the Internet and AI Safely in Assessments" and this together with the Education Authority's GenAI resources and use cases developed by practitioners will ensure that the risks associated with GenAI are minimised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to carers’ legislation, to detail (i) the number of carer’s assessments requested, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency; (ii) the average waiting time from request to assessment being conducted in each of those years; (iii) the number of requests for a carer’s assessment that were declined, including the principal reasons for those refusals; (iv) any steps his Department is taking to ensure timely access to assessments for both adult and young carers; and (v) following a carers' assessment (a) to detail any data collected on out
answer_text: The publicly available data relating to Adult Carers is held on the Department of Health website at Carers’ assessments and reassessments | Department of Health. The DoH have included a note with regard to the latest data publication indicating that the rollout of the Encompass system has impacted on the reporting of this data.  The SPPG does have access to data on the number of requests for a carer’s assessment that were declined, including the principal reasons for those refusals; this has been included in the table below (note data is not available at constituency level but has been provided at Trust level).Main reason adult carer declined carer’s assessment 2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25The carer sees their caring duties as a private matter which they prefer not to discuss.448557517466558The carer does not see themselves as a carer and therefore does not see assessment as relevant.827770871865808The carer felt that time/ place/ environment offered was unsuitable but would like the opportunity to consider an assessment at a later date.1,2621,7011,8391,4591,071The carer feels that they do not need any support/ additional support.2,1801,8721,9032,3131,840The carer feels that an assessment would not result in additional support/ service.283283287439383The carer was concerned about the impact on their benefits/ income.2463354889The carer feels that assessment would be too complicated or time consuming.7713421014393The carer would not give a reason/No reason recorded.1,1029671,4041,7091,251Any steps his Department is taking to ensure timely access to assessments for both adult and young carers; and (v) following a carers' assessment (a) to detail any data collected on outcomes; and (b) whether any subsequent support is offered. Adult and young carers have a statutory right to a carer’s assessment from their local Health & Social Care (HSC) under the Children (NI) Order 1995 and the Carers and Direct Payments Act (NI) 2002. In respect to adults each trust will offer an identified carer a carers assessment at the point of the cared for person initial assessment. Carers can request reassessment at any time if their needs change, supporting ongoing and timely access. For children under 8 year of age the HSC Trusts conduct assessments. For children aged between 8–18 years old assessments are commissioned through Regional Young Carers Services, often delivered by Action for Children or Barnardo’s NI via the UNOCINI framework. Trusts must ensure young carers and their families are informed of their right to be assessed, including transition to adult carer assessments at age 18. Regional Young Carers services provide needs assessments, support planning, and signposting, helping ensure timely access to carers support Published Carer data focus mainly on activity data rather than detailed personal outcomes. The carers assessments capture the impact of the caring role on all aspects of a person’s life. Assessments may lead to a variety of supports in

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the public reporting on Peter Mandelson’s representations in support of the Dalradian gold mine, to detail whether any review of safeguards within her Department, to protect the integrity and independence of environmental planning decisions from undue political influence, have been undertaken.
answer_text: My Planning officials have advised me that Peter Mandelson has not written  to my Department regarding to the Dalradian gold mine planning application proposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant of AQW 39375/22-27, to detail (i) whether he considers the current non-statutory provision for teaching about the Holocaust, and other genocides, to be sufficient to ensure consistent coverage across all schools; (ii) any assessment his Department has made of the risk posed by historical misinformation, denial or distortion in relation to the Holocaust; and (iii) whether he is considering making teaching about genocides a statutory element of the reformed curriculum.
answer_text: (i)Teaching about the Holocaust and other genocides is not currently a statutory element of the Northern Ireland Curriculum. However, as outlined in AQW 39375/22-27, there are existing opportunities for pupils to study these topics across several Areas of Learning at Key Stages 2 and 3. As the provision is embedded within broader themes, the extent and depth of coverage can vary between schools. While many schools make substantive provision, particularly within History, Learning for Life and Work, and The World Around Us Areas of Learning, my Department recognises that non-statutory arrangements do not guarantee uniformity of practice. It is a matter for each school to ensure that the minimum content is delivered as statutorily required. (ii)My Department is aware of the wider international concerns regarding contemporary misinformation, denial, or distortion relating to The Holocaust and other genocides. While my Department has not conducted a formal standalone risk assessment, issues such as these will be considered as part of the ongoing programme of curriculum reform as previously stated in my answer to AQW 39375/22-27. My Department also recognises that robust, well-supported teaching is an important safeguard against misinformation and distortion.(iii)As stated previously in my answer to AQW 39375/22-27, subject-specific working groups for History and Learning for Life and Work are considering how these areas will be developed within the new statutory curriculum, including how sensitive and important historical topics can be effectively supported.I expect to receive the draft curriculum framework by the end of March 2026; a public consultation will follow in due course providing an opportunity for all stakeholders, including Members, to provide their views.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any oversight and enforcement arrangements his Department has in place to ensure compliance with the Registration and Attendance of Pupils Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1974, and Article 48 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986, including the duty on school proprietors to keep accurate registers; (ii) the number of cases of non-compliance identified; (iii) any sanctions, or formal interventions, which have been applied; and (iv) any steps being taken to prevent inaccurate attendance recording from disproportionately impacti
answer_text: Officials receive management system data on a monthly basis from the Education Authority. This data is at school level, across all sectors and allows officials to identify schools who have an unusually high level of missing marks, that is to say the attendance register is not being kept up to date. These schools will then be contacted and asked to explain why these circumstances exist, they will be reminded that the school register is a legal record and that missing marks must be accounted for before the next set of monthly figures are received.The overall numbers of missing marks are shared in a monthly attendance report with colleagues within the Education Authority and the Education Welfare Service, who will also be in receipt of the files which identify schools with missing marks.  In the event the Department has a concern over repeated inaccuracy in a school register it will raise the issue with the EA.  School Improvement (EA) will be notified, as well as Senior Management in EWS, in order to address with School Leadership.The Department has no record of schools being formally sanctioned in relation to failure to keep a school register.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 39043/22-27, to detail (i) any research, including any international evidence, his Department has considered regarding the impact of widespread use of digital technologies, including AI and generative language models, on pupils’ foundational literacy, critical thinking and core skills; (ii) whether he has taken account of recent policy developments in countries such as Sweden, where government reports and international assessments have noted digital screen use and over-reliance on classroom technology as correlated with classroom distractions,
answer_text: As outlined in AQW 39043/22-27, my Department has been leading a rigorous process, in partnership with a range of educational partners, to develop guidance for schools in respect of the use of GenAI.  This is grounded in research and emerging practice in other jurisdictions including international evidence on the impact of digital technologies on pupils’ foundational literacy, critical thinking and core skills.As part of the curriculum review, I have established the Northern Ireland Curriculum Taskforce Advisory Committee (TAC) to design a new statutory curriculum framework. This work draws on effective practice nationally and internationally and will consider how best to deliver literacy and numeracy in the classroom ensuring an appropriate balance between technology-enabled approaches and traditional pedagogies.  Public consultation will take place in due course.The Strategic Review of the Curriculum also recommended introducing Digital Technology as a new curriculum strand. This reflects its importance for future learning, employability, and societal engagement. DE has accepted this recommendation and is working to embed digital skills more explicitly within the curriculum and integrate digital technology across all key stages and subjects.Literacy is also fundamental to pupils’ access to the curriculum and long term education success.  Through the TransformED NI reform agenda, I have committed to bringing forward a new literacy strategy informed by international best practice and designed to strengthen equity and raise attainment. This approach recognises the importance of securing strong foundations in reading and writing before the use of digital or AIenabled tools and of ensuring that technology is used to support, rather than displace, sustained literacy development.Through the RAISE Programme, my Department has introduced a Reading with AI Research Project that will help schools explore how AI tools can assist literacy development while maintaining a strong focus on foundational reading skills. The study is led by Oxford Brookes University, using Amira Learning, a reading support tool, aligned with research-informed approaches to early literacy and powered by AI.The draft guidance for schools on the use of AI / Generative AI recommends that schools monitor and evaluate the impact of school policies on teaching and learning,  review these regularly as required and ensure digital safety while supporting pupil learning.  It also promotes the development of technological skills, critical / analytical thinking, computational thinking / problem-solving and creative capacity across the curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any information her Department holds, regarding the number of confirmed cases of Artificial Intelligence-related academic misconduct at (a) Queen’s University Belfast; and (b) Ulster University, in each of the last three academic years; and (ii) any discussions she has had with those institutions, regarding the issue.
answer_text: Both Queen’s University Belfast and Ulster University are autonomous institutions responsible for their own policies and procedures including those in relation to academic misconduct and the use of artificial intelligence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether any consideration has been given, as part of the current consultation on establishing a new organisation to support controlled schools, to how teacher employment, accountability and lines of responsibility will operate where teachers are deployed across more than one school; and (ii) any safeguards in place to ensure clarity of governance for boards of governors, the Education Authority and the new support body.
answer_text: Feedback to the public consultation on my proposal to set up a new organisation to support controlled schools highlighted that, ninety-one percent of respondents agreed that support for controlled schools must improve, while eighty-four percent indicated agreement with the proposal for establishment of a new organisation to support controlled schools.  Considering these unequivocal consultation findings, I have presented my proposal again to the Executive for agreement with the intention to begin work on the necessary primary legislation. While planning work continues as to what the functions of this organisation may be, it is understood that when Executive agreement is given work will progress at pace to shape and clarify the details of the form and functions of the organisation. This will include engagement with key stakeholders.  In the event of teachers being required to work across a number of schools, protocols and guidance will be developed to ensure that appropriate governance arrangements are in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether (i) his Department has assessed the extent to which undergraduate degree requirements are used as eligibility criteria in Civil Service recruitment, particularly for entry-level grades, and (ii) consideration has been given to recognising equivalent professional, or life, experience as an alternative pathway into public service roles.
answer_text: The Department of Finance has responsibility for recruitment policy across the Civil Service, which is based on the principle of appointment on merit through fair and open competition.There is no general requirement for an undergraduate degree as an eligibility criterion in Civil Service recruitment, including at entrylevel grades. Qualification requirements are applied only where they are essential to the effective performance of a role, such as in certain professional or technical posts. For the majority of entrylevel and general service competitions, eligibility is based on the assessment of relevant skills, competencies and experience rather than formal academic qualifications.The Department supports the recognition of equivalent professional and relevant experience as an alternative pathway into public service roles. Recruitment processes are designed to assess candidates on their ability to demonstrate the skills required for the role, regardless of how those skills have been acquired. Ongoing work to develop clearer job families and career pathways will further support a balanced and proportionate approach to qualifications and experience, while maintaining the principle of appointment on merit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any targets and indicators that will be used to measure whether TransformED is reducing the attainment gap for pupils entitled to free school meals; and (ii) any interim milestones that have been set, ahead of the first Annual Progress Report in May 2026; and (iii) how progress will be reported to the Assembly.
answer_text: Embedding a culture of monitoring, evaluation and quality assurance is woven through all aspects of implementation of the TransformED Strategy. Integral to success will be consistent improvement in pupil attainment, including the attainment of pupils in Free School Meals.The TransformED Programme Board is considering further appropriate system measures and targets, as it takes forward work on curriculum and qualifications review, Key Stage assessment and literacy and numeracy. Inspection evidence will also inform the development of measures and targetsI will provide a statement to the Assembly on publication of the first annual progress report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the amount of the recently announced £7.85 million Winter Recovery Road Fund, that will be allocated to the completion of the roadworks in Ballykelly; and (ii) any additional steps her Department will take to minimise further disruption to local residents and road users.
answer_text: I have recently allocated an additional £7.85 million funding to help mitigate the impacts that recent winter weather conditions have had on our road network. This will be used to deliver an enhanced structural maintenance programme including large scale patching and resurfacing targeted at those areas of greatest need across the north. This work will commence without delay.An allocation of £250,000 has been made to my Department’s Northern division from this fund. Officials will identify areas of greatest need in which to utilise this targeted funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any assessment that her Department has undertaken, regarding the risks posed by off-road scrambler bikes when used in public places; (ii) the number of scramblers seized by the PSNI, in each of the last five years; and (iii) whether she intends to introduce measures to prohibit or further restrict the use of scramblers.
answer_text: (i) I recognise that the unlawful use of off-road scrambler bikes in public places poses risks to public safety and can also contribute to antisocial behaviour.  While my Department has not undertaken an assessment specific to scramblers, the risks associated with their misuse are well known and have been publicly highlighted by the PSNI through safety awareness messaging and targeted policing operations such as Operation Chicquer.(ii) My Department neither collates nor holds the information requested on the number of scramblers seized by the PSNI, in each of the last five years.  I would suggest that you contact the PSNI directly for a more detailed response.(iii) Responsibility for the legislation governing the use of scramblers rests with the Department for Infrastructure, and therefore I have no plans to introduce further measures in this area. However, my Department will continue to work with DfI and the PSNI to support any proportionate and effective approaches to tackling the risks posed by illegal use of these vehicles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any consideration his Department has given to introducing philosophy as a discrete subject, or formally embedding philosophical enquiry, within the school curriculum; (ii) any assessment made of its potential contribution to pupils’ critical-thinking skills and engagement with controversial or ethical issues; and (iii) whether he intends to bring forward proposals in this regard.
answer_text: My Department has no plans to introduce philosophy as a discrete subject within the school curriculum.Work is ongoing to reform the Northern Ireland Curriculum to ensure it is knowledge-rich, evidence-based, well sequenced, and designed to support progression for all learners. Whilst it important that the curriculum does not become overloaded, aspects of philosophy, ethics or moral reasoning may influence the revised framework for Learning for Life and Work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 36913/22-27, whether any teachers from (i) Irish-medium schools; or (ii) special schools, were appointed to the Curriculum Taskforce working groups.
answer_text: Teachers from Irish-medium schools and special schools were appointed to the Curriculum Taskforce subject working groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has considered establishing a scheme to provide bicycles or bicycle loans for school pupils from low-income families, similar to the free bike pilots in Scotland.
answer_text: My Department does not have the vires to provide funding for equipment to individuals, however I recognise the many benefits active travel can provide and would welcome the opportunity to engage with other government departments or councils should they wish to bring proposals forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) his assessment of whether the current budget for free school meals is sufficient to ensure that all pupils receiving them are provided with meals that meet recommended nutritional standards; (ii) any assessment that has been made of the quality and nutritional content of meals currently served under the scheme; and (iii) any steps in place to address any shortfalls to ensure pupils receive adequately nutritious meals.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that the rising operational costs associated with the provision of free school meals has not impacted the quality of the food served nor the portion size or nutritional standards of its school meals. The EA has advised that its school meals service offers a varied range of menu items that take account of feedback from children and their families to ensure that the EA offers a nutritious and appealing school lunch each day to all pupils, including those entitled to free school meals. School meal menus are developed and reviewed on a regular basis by the Regional Food in Schools Coordinator and the menu development team within the EA to ensure quality, choice and nutritional content remain core to menu development.When operational issues arise that impact the availability of a dish or food item, the EA will endeavour to find an appropriate substitute to mitigate any impact on pupil choice and nutritional quality.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any criteria and data that will be used to identify smaller schools for potential closure; (ii) any assessment that has been made of the likely impact on class sizes and educational outcomes, particularly in rural areas; and (iii) any safeguards that will be put in place to mitigate effects on local communities, including increased travel times and loss of local provision.
answer_text: My Department has set out its proposals for a Five-Year Education Budget aStrategy.  Consultation on the strategy closes on 3 April 2026.  The strategy includes proposals to restructure the school estate and within that context a Commission may be appointed to lead on this work.  If a Commission is to be established, it will have a mandate to carry out a comprehensive viability audit of all schools to assess sustainability against agreed criteria and will be tasked to make evidence-based recommendations for restructuring the school estate, ensuring resources are focused where they deliver the greatest benefit for pupils.  The Commission will also be asked to produce a detailed five-year implementation plan to guide change in a phased, manageable way.  Equality and rural needs impact assessments will continue to apply to proposals to close schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any assessment that has been made of the potential risks posed by the increasing use of generative artificial intelligence in classrooms to pupils’ critical thinking and independent learning; (ii) any evidence that underpins that assessment; and (iii) whether guidance to be issued to schools will include safeguards to address those risks.
answer_text: Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) has the potential to significantly enhance the delivery of the curriculum in schools and many schools are already using GenAI in their day-to-day work. I am keen to see it used effectively for the benefit of all learners. Used appropriately, GenAI can support curriculum planning, enable interactive learning environments and provide real-time assessment and feedback, helping pupils understand their progress and areas for improvement. My Department has been leading a rigorous process, with a range of educational partners, to develop guidance for schools in respect of the use of GenAI in our schools which is grounded in research and practice in other jurisdictions including internationally. The Education Authority (EA) and the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Curriculum (CCEA) have played a significant role in the development of this guidance. The EA’s Education Information Solution (EdIS) provides a safe online platform for use by teachers and pupils and none of the data inside a school’s own ecosystem is shared publicly. This is a significant feature which is not available in other jurisdictions. The EA is also developing a wide range of online materials which will be available on its EdIS GenAI Platform when the guidance is issued to schools. In addition to this, CCEA has produced online information and guidance for pupils and parents on “Using the Internet and AI Safely in Assessments”.As with any digital technology, GenAI may raise challenges such as the potential for over-reliance, the spread of misinformation, the exacerbation of existing biases, privacy and security concerns, a widening of the digital divide, and academic integrity issues. The guidance will, therefore set out the need for the use of GenAI to be lawful, conform to data protection requirements, be ethical, safe, secure, fair and transparent. Central to all of this will be staff, pupil and parent / carer wellbeing. I anticipate that guidance will be available to issue to schools soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any assessment his Department has made of the current provision for life skills and independent living skills training for pupils on the autism spectrum in schools; (ii) any evidence or examples from international programmes, such as those used in the United States, Europe and Singapore, that have been considered; and (iii) any steps being taken to ensure that pupils with autism receive structured support in life skills development, to improve transitions to adult life.
answer_text: (i) Schools are responsible for ensuring, through the graduated response framework, that autistic children and young people have opportunities to develop life and independence skills. For pupils requiring an additional level of support, Local Impact Teams can provide advice, guidance and direct input to assist schools in developing these skills in autistic children and young people.The ongoing curriculum review is founded on an inclusive and flexible approach designed to accommodate diverse learner needs while remaining ambitious for all. Clearer guidance on essential learning is expected to support all pupils, including those with SEN, in accessing core knowledge. Alongside the main framework, consideration is being given to a bespoke strand for special schools that supports broader life skills development, adapted in pace and context as needed.(ii) Autism specialists within the Local Impact Teams draw on a wide range of evidence-based approaches and utilise programmes recommended by the Middletown Centre for Autism. (iii) To strengthen structured support transitions, the Local Impact Teams have developed the Stepping On Up programme, designed to support schools to assist autistic young people as they move into college or further education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether animal welfare convictions secured in Great Britain are recognised or taken into account by his Department when assessing an individual’s suitability to work with animals or operate as a licensed breeder.
answer_text: The question does not specify a particular type of breeding; the answer is therefore based on the assumption that it is in relation to dogs. The Welfare of Animals (Dog Breeding Establishments and Miscellaneous Amendments) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2013 contains provision that a person should not apply for a dog breeding establishment licence if they are subject to a disqualification relating to specified animalrelated activities. This may also cover a person’s ability to work with animals. This provision encompasses disqualifications imposed in other UK jurisdictions. Responsibility for inspecting and licensing dog breeding establishments rests solely with local councils.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether applicants for dog breeding licences are required to declare animal welfare convictions held by themselves or immediate family members, particularly where breeding establishments operate as family businesses or where multiple licences are held within the same family.
answer_text: The Welfare of Animals (Dog Breeding Establishments and Miscellaneous Amendments) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2013 contains a provision that a person should not apply for a dog breeding establishment licence if they are subject to a disqualification relating to specified animalrelated activities.Local councils are responsible for the licensing of dog breeding establishments and related enforcement. The regulations provide councils with the power to amend, suspend or revoke a licence where they are satisfied such action is necessary to protect the welfare of an animal.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps he is taking to improve cross-jurisdictional information sharing and strengthen licensing and enforcement arrangements to prevent repeat animal welfare breaches within the breeding sector.
answer_text: My Department is represented on the UK Pet Trade Task Force, alongside delegates from all UK governments, enforcement agencies, and organisations within the animal welfare charitable sector. This group routinely exchanges cross-jurisdictional information regarding illegal dog breeding and related animal welfare concerns. Additionally, my officials participate in a Cross Border Taskforce, which includes representatives from key statutory bodies to share intelligence on unlawful activities involving animals. Additionally, in 2020, my Department established the Paws for Thought Group, bringing together representatives from various enforcement agencies to work together in identifying, stopping, and preventing illegal puppy breeding.An expert advisory group has been undertaking a review of the dog breeding regulations and is currently preparing a report with recommendations, which I expect to receive soon. I will carefully evaluate the findings and determine appropriate next steps.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any impact that an animal welfare conviction in another UK jurisdiction has on an individual’s eligibility to apply for or retain a dog breeding licence in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Welfare of Animals (Dog Breeding Establishments and Miscellaneous Amendments) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2013 contains provision that a person should not apply for a dog breeding establishment licence if they are subject to a disqualification relating to specified animalrelated activities; this may include the ownership or keeping of animals. This provision encompasses disqualifications imposed in other UK jurisdictions. Any licence issued to a person so disqualified is invalid. Furthermore, where a person becomes disqualified while holding a licence, that licence may be revoked.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment on whether current enforcement powers are sufficient to prevent individuals with a known history of serious animal welfare concerns from engaging in unlicensed or repeat breeding activity.
answer_text: The question does not specify a particular type of breeding; the answer provided is therefore based on the assumption that it is in relation to dogs. Regulation 6 of The Welfare of Animals (Dog Breeding Establishments and Miscellaneous Amendments) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2013 states that any person who is disqualified from keeping animals under the respective animal welfare acts in Scotland, England and Wales or Northern Ireland cannot obtain a dog breeding licence. Where persons have information relating to the operation of unlicenced breeding establishments or breaches of disqualification orders, this should be passed on to the relevant enforcement agency, so action can be taken where necessary.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any meetings she has held, since 2020, relating to the protection of asylum seekers and minority communities from intimidation or protest activity, including (i) the dates of those meetings; (ii) the organisations or agencies represented; (iii) the issues discussed; and (iv) any actions or follow-up agreed.
answer_text: Since 2020, I have had meetings with a wide range of organisations, agencies and individuals which has included discussion on the protection of asylum seekers and minority communities from intimidation or protest activity. The detail of specific topics of discussions is not recorded.  However, the table below sets out details of meetings where it has been possible to determine from the title of the meeting that the subject matter is relevant to the question.Date of meetingOrganisation / Agency representedIssues discussedActions / follow-up agreed11/08/2020Patrick Yu and Anna LoRacism and anti-racism training22/06/2021Steve Aiken, MLA and the All Party Group (APG) on Ethnic Minority CommunitiesHate Crime Legislation ReviewDoJ officials to attend APG meetings to provide updates on the Department’s work.  The APG agreed to would work collaboratively with the DoJ and Justice Committee to progress the Hate Crime legislation.01/11/2021Dame Sara Thornton, Anti - Slavery CommissionerModern Slavery and Human TraffickingThe Minister would write to the Executive regarding the preparation and publication of a Modern Slavery Statement by the Executive.06/10/2022Fiona Donnelly, Children’s Order PanelModern Slavery and Human Trafficking StrategyFurther work to be done on scoping a post to support the work of the panel.24/04/2024Eleanor Lyons, Independent Anti-Slavery CommissionerModern SlaveryA copy of the draft strategy to be shared with Eleanor .Follow up with Dame Sara Thornton regarding the possibility of working after a period of time, if an asylum application has not been processed.26/06/2024Representatives from The Rainbow Project, Cara-Friend and HERe NIHate Crime legislationA copy of the report ‘We are getting hurt’ to be shared with Kate Nicholl MLA.27/06/2024Kate Nicholl MLA, Paula Bradshaw MLA and representatives from the African and Caribbean Support Organisation NI (ACSONI)Racist Hate CrimePotential for a further meeting to discuss racial harassment of PSNI officers.05/08/2024Secretary of State for Northern IrelandViolence and disorder in BelfastMinister and Secretary of State agreed to maintain contact and speak again as necessary.05/08/2024Helen McEntee TD, Irish Justice MinisterViolence and disorder in BelfastMinisters agreed to maintain contact and to speak again as necessary.07/08/2024Dr. Wasif Naeem and other representatives from Belfast Islamic CentreViolence and disorder in Belfast and threats and intimidation against the ethnic minority community02/10/2024Chris Quinn, Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People (NICCY)A range of issues involving young people including, the involvement of children and young people in the summer disorder and DoJ legislative updateNICCY to be advised about potential meeting of Barnahus stakeholders.  Update to be provided on settlements for minors, including any legislative change. Update to be provided on anti-social behaviour legislation and ASBOs.05/06/2025Eleanor Lyons - Independent Anti-

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any contractual provisions in place to prevent taxi operators, providing home-to-school transport for pupils with special educational needs, from withdrawing from individual routes at short notice, while continuing to operate others; (ii) any assessment made of the impact of such withdrawals on families who must make alternative childcare and travel arrangements; and (iii) any steps being taken to reduce delays in retendering disrupted routes, and to ensure sufficient provider capacity.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport and this includes full responsibility for the procurement and management of contracts for the provision of school taxi services. The EA has advised that the terms and conditions for school transport taxi services include provisions for dealing with withdrawal of services by contractors. The EA may view such an event as a breach of contract and it reserves the right to take whatever action it deems appropriate. This may include suspension from re-tendering for the affected service for a period of between three to 12 months.I understand the frustration and inconvenience that disruption to home to school arrangements can cause for families and the EA assures me that it makes every effort to ensure that arrangements remain consistent where possible. Private operators do on occasion withdraw from their contracts for operational reasons and at times this is with limited notice. In such instances the EA endeavours to minimise the disruption to children and families and will retender the route immediately; it must, however, ensure that all necessary compliance and Access NI checks are completed prior to awarding a school transport contract to a new operator. The EA encourages private taxi operators who are interested in school transport contracts to register on its Dynamic Purchasing System (DPS).
