All school transport tenders are announced, and every operator who has registered on the EA's DPS receives a notification about the tender opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the rising number of suspensions, the disproportionate representation of pupils in receipt of free school meals, and the concentration of suspensions in non-selective schools, to detail any steps he will take to review current suspension practices to ensure they effectively promote positive behaviour, prevent disengagement, and support his Department’s aim of tackling educational disadvantage.
answer_text: Article 3 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, as amended by Article 19 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 2003, requires a school to have a discipline policy which outlines the behaviour it expects from registered pupils and the sanctions it will impose for breaches of discipline. The Order does not dictate any fixed format for the discipline policy; the content and implementation of that policy, including the use of suspension (and other sanctions) is a matter for the school Principal and Board of Governors to determine. The Department has produced guidance on this issue entitled Pastoral Care in Schools: Promoting Positive Behaviour, which sets out how a school should develop a discipline/positive behaviour policy using a whole-school approach and in consultation with pupils and parents. My department has undertaken a review of the existing suspension and expulsion arrangements. I am currently considering that review, including the recommendations for change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how the costs for the Education Authority’s procurement and contract-management arrangements for school taxi services rose to almost £40 million a year; and (ii) any action being taken to (a) ensure effective oversight; and (b) prevent further escalation.
answer_text: (i) The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of school transport. This includes determining the most appropriate mode of transport for each pupil and full responsibility for procurement, management and oversight of school transport contracts with private taxi operators. The EA has advised that the increased cost of school taxi transport has been driven by an increased demand for transport for pupils with a statement of special educational need (SEN) combined with increasing costs across the transport industry. The shortfall in specialist school placements for pupils with a statement of SEN has made the provision of transport more complex as more pupils require transport to schools outside their local communities to which there are no existing school bus networks. (ii)(a) I am advised that the EA is recruiting additional contract managers to provide further oversight and monitoring of contract management processes. (ii)(b) The EA is currently engaging with private hire operators, having sought their cooperation to reduce costs. It is also exploring potential amendments to the tendering process for private hire services that would help to limit contracted costs and ensure that the transport service is more sustainable in the longer term. Work is ongoing within the EA, exploring options in respect of the SEN transport referral process and my officials will support the EA in exploring any policy issues raised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of off-road scrambler bikes and e-scooters, seized by the PSNI, in each of the last five years; (ii) the number of those seizures involving riders, under the age of 16, operating such vehicles in public places; (iii) any assessment that has been carried out of the impact this has on (a) public safety; (b) antisocial behaviour; and (c) noise pollution; and (iv) whether she will introduce proposals to restrict or regulate the sale or use of these vehicles, particularly in light of recent fatal incidents elsewhere involving scrambler
answer_text: Firstly, I would like to acknowledge and extend my sincere condolences to the families of those killed on our roads. Road safety is a key priority for me, and I consider very carefully how to take forward work that will have the greatest impact on saving lives.The information that you have requested in points (i) and (ii) is not held by my Department. It is therefore not possible for my officials to carry out such an assessment as described in point (iii). The information requested may be held by the PSNI and I would direct you to contact them in order to obtain this information.In relation to point (iv), specifically scramblers - these are designed primarily for off road use only. If they are to be used on a road then they must comply with the same rules as for all other motorbikes i.e. the correct driving licence category for the size of engine, insurance, MOT and must be taxed and registered.It remains illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces here. Anyone using an e-scooter illegally may be subject to enforcement action by the PSNI. The Department is exploring measures to raise awareness around the dangers associated with e-scooters. The Department is also monitoring the progress of a pilot scheme for rental e-scooters in Britain, as well as the impact of the legislative changes in the south which made the use of e-scooters legal and regulated their use on public roads from May 2024. This evidence base can help inform the department of what steps to take in relation to the registration and regulation of e-scooters.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any public shelter and civil defence provision, including (i) the (a) number; and (b) location, of shelters, or bunkers, suitable for use during a military or airborne attack; (ii) their estimated capacity, and operational condition; (iii) the proportion that are fully accessible to disabled people, including wheelchair users, and those dependent on medical equipment; (iv) any former Cold War–era shelters that have been sold, or decommissioned, in the past 15 years; (v) any plans to refurbish existing facilities ,or construct new sh
answer_text: Defence and national security are reserved matters; we are therefore unable to provide information relating to any civil defence provision or facilities which may fall within the reserved sphere.The Executive Office however, has responsibility for a Cold War legacy civil defence building (the Regional Government Headquarters in Ballymena also referred to as ‘the Bunker’) which is a scheduled historic monument. The Regional Government Headquarters is unsuitable as a functional asset in its current state; and there are no plans for it to be converted into a public shelter.  The Department for Communities (DfC) is responsible for the Historic Environment Record of Northern Ireland and the Defence Heritage Record. Their records identify Cold War structures in government or public ownership, including the Stormont Civil Defence Sub Control Centre. DfC is unaware of any which are suitable for use as a public shelter or of plans to refurbish any of these structures into modern day public shelters.  Responsibility for devolved civil contingencies arrangements is shared among all Northern Ireland Departments. The Executive Office Civil Contingencies Division is responsible for the management of the Northern Ireland Civil Contingencies Framework, which outlines how government organisations will prepare for, respond to and recover from emergencies. Community Assistance Centres, Community Rest Centres and Evacuation Centres are organised at the local level by the NI Emergency Preparedness Group, in conjunction with their partner organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any preparations the PSNI make when demonstrations or rallies are advertised by groups linked to hate-based rhetoric or individuals with violent criminal convictions; (ii) how risk levels are assessed; and (iii) any existing powers to restrict or condition such events.
answer_text: In answer to parts (i) and (ii), as the operational policing of demonstrations is a matter for PSNI, I would recommend that the member write to the Chief Constable to request this information.  In answer to part (iii), Part II of The Public Order (Northern Ireland) Order 1987 creates powers for the PSNI to impose conditions upon the holding of any open-air public meeting – not falling within the meaning of the Public Processions (Northern Ireland) Act 1998 – where a senior officer believes that it may result in serious public disorder, serious damage to property, serious disruption to the life of the community, or for the purposes of the intimidation of others.  Failure to comply with those restrictions is an offence, attracting on conviction a term of imprisonment not exceeding 6 months, and/or a fine of up to Level 5 on the Standard Scale.If these restrictions are deemed insufficient to prevent the harms identified, the matter may be referred to the Department of Justice.Part III of the Public Order (Northern Ireland) Order 1987 provides that a person who uses threatening, abusive or insulting words or behaviour, or displays or publishes any threatening, abusive or insulting written material against a protected group (race, religion, disability, sexual orientation), is guilty of an offence if they intend to stir up hatred or arouse fear; or hatred is likely to be stirred up or fear is likely to be aroused.A person guilty of such an offence is liable on summary conviction to a term of imprisonment of up to 6 months, and/or the statutory maximum fine. On conviction on indictment, the offence carries a potential maximum 7 year term of imprisonment, and/or a fine of up to the Statutory Maximum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will answer AQW 38074/22-27.
answer_text: A response to AQW 38074/22-27 issued on 6 February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any immediate steps his Department is taking to (i) ensure that the youth services in Limavady, currently supporting over 400 children and young people, continues to receive funding after their application for the 2026-2027 financial year was deemed ineligible due to a clerical error of £0.01; and (ii) prevent the complete withdrawal of funding on 1 April 2026, given that this service has been successfully funded for the past five years.
answer_text: On 5 February my Department contacted the Education Authority Youth Service (EAYS) to request that it re-release the funding opportunities. This included the area for which an application was deemed ineligible by EAYS due to a clerical error of £0.01.The EA has now confirmed it will provide a limited opportunity for the previously unsuccessful applicants to reconsider and re-submit their applications.    Correspondence has been issued to the sector informing them of this decision and reminding them that EA will not be in the position to provide grant aid assistance on 01 April 2026 until the budget has been confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce a health and social care youth advisory board, comparable to the NHS Youth Forum and associated youth advisory structures operating within NHS England, in the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement with (i) SEAG; and (ii) relevant stakeholders, to ensure that access arrangements, including extra time for pupils with dyslexia and other specific learning difficulties, are fair, evidence-based and sufficient to provide an equitable assessment for those pupils.
answer_text: I have not engaged with either the Schools’s Entrance Assessment Group (SEAG) nor relevant stakeholders regarding access arrangements for the SEAG entrance assessment. My Department is not responsible for the administration of the post-primary transfer tests but the Department is aware that the SEAG entrance assessment facilitates applications for access arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to his recent written statement on the Armed Forces Bill, to detail any steps his Department will take to support children living with parents who have post-traumatic stress disorder.
answer_text: I recognise the impact that challenging home circumstances can have on a child’s development and lifetime achievement. Parental mental health can have a major bearing on children’s wellbeing, attendance and educational outcomes. Therefore, my Department seeks to support children who have difficult family situations, including those who have parents who experience posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD).While the Education Authority does not have a specific service focused on supporting the needs of children whose parents have PTSD, all schools across Northern Ireland have access to a range of training and support services which are relevant to this issue and can assist schools in responding to the needs of their pupils, including:Being Well Doing Well; A Whole School Approach to Emotional Health and WellbeingNurture Advisory Support ServiceHOPE Counselling and Therapy ServiceAll of these Services are underpinned by trauma and attachment informed approaches which are responsive to the needs of all children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether specific provision exists within the Northern Ireland Curriculum for teaching about the Holocaust and other genocides; and (ii) his assessment of whether this provision is sufficient to ensure pupils understand their historical causes, scale and contemporary relevance.
answer_text: While teaching about The Holocaust and other genocides is not a statutory requirement, there are currently opportunities for pupils to learn about them across the Curriculum.At Key Stage 2, The Holocaust, other genocides and related themes can be explored within ‘The World Around Us’ and ‘Personal Development and Mutual Understanding’ areas of learning.At Key Stage 3, pupils can study The Holocaust and other genocides through History, where there is a statutory requirement to examine significant events of the 20th century, as well as through ‘Learning for Life and Work’ and ‘Environment and Society’ areas of learning.As part of the wider work on curriculum reform, I have established the Northern Ireland Curriculum Taskforce to design a new statutory curriculum framework. Subject-specific working groups for History and Learning for Life and Work are considering how these areas will be developed within the new statutory curriculum, including how sensitive and important historical topics can be effectively supported. This work is progressing, and there will be public consultation in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will answer AQW 38070/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 38070/22-27 was answered on 19 March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of PSNI officers and staff, who have been charged with a domestic violence offence, in the last five years, including (i) the number of those who had access to a firearm; and (ii) the number of those who retained access to a firearm, after being found guilty.
answer_text: The Department does not hold the information requested in the question.  The recording of information on numbers of PSNI officers and staff who have been charged with a domestic violence offence, and whether or not any retained access to a firearm after conviction, is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to these matters to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when the funding for the NI Boardroom Apprentice Programme will be announced in 2026.
answer_text: The Executive Office funding for the NI Boardroom Apprentice Programme ceased from the beginning of 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will undertake any equality screening or equality impact assessment of suspension and expulsion patterns since 2019, including analysis by school sector, special educational needs status and socioeconomic deprivation.
answer_text: The Department of Education annually publishes data (official statistics in development) on pupil suspensions and expulsions. The data includes breakdowns by key stage, school type, special educational needs status and free school meal entitlement, as well as a range of other factors. There is no requirement to conduct Equality Impact Assessments in relation to these statistics, and no current plans to do so.Should any policy proposals emerge in relation to suspension and expulsion, these will be screened for equality impacts in accordance with our statutory obligations, and a full EQIA will be undertaken if screening indicates this is necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures in place to ensure that publicly funded enterprise support initiatives, such as Enterprise Causeway, do not operate in direct competition with existing small businesses.
answer_text: Local Enterprise Agencies (LEAs) such as Enterprise Causeway are independent of my Department. My Department does not have the remit to intervene in how they operate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any evaluation his Department has undertaken of the effectiveness of current curriculum provision, and CCEA digital wellbeing resources, in equipping pupils to identify online misinformation, including deepfakes.
answer_text: Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) provides a range of age-appropriate resources on the CCEA RSE Hub. These resources provide guidance and materials for schools in exploring safe internet use, and cover topics including social media, sharing of and altering images, staying safe online, cyberbullying, sexting, and pornography.Most existing CCEA curriculum resources and qualifications predate the emergence of generative AI and therefore do not yet cover issues such as AIgenerated misinformation, deepfakes or algorithmic bias. However, the digital literacy skills embedded across the Curriculum help prepare pupils for an evolving digital world. The CCEA RSE Hub was launched in 2023 and continues to expand in response to emerging needs, with  usage and effectiveness monitored through resource downloads and engagement with educators. CCEA has advised that no formal consultations on these resources are planned for this financial year, as the sector is already involved in wider TransformED NI consultations.CCEA also signposts uptodate guidance from trusted organisations through its Online Safety section, directing educators to credible resources on issues such as cyberbullying, imagebased abuse, and online exploitation.For Safer Internet Day 2026 (10 February), the UK Safer Internet Centre has produced resources themed around artificial intelligence, focusing on safe, responsible use and the ethics of generative tools, including preventing misuse for harmful image manipulation. Agespecific materials support pupils aged 11–14 to understand and use AI tools responsibly, while resources for 14–18 year olds explore broader ethical and societal responsibilities.Childnet provides a learning resource on AI misuse aimed at 13–17 year olds alongside guidance for parents and carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether modern languages will be made compulsory, to the age of 16, as part of the reformed curriculum.
answer_text: Currently at Key Stage 3 (age 11-14), pupils must study an official language of the European Union other than English (and, in the case of Irish-medium schools, other than Irish), while at Key Stage 4 (age 14-16), Modern Languages are a qualification choice.As part of the broader curriculum reform programme, the Curriculum Taskforce is developing a new statutory curriculum framework for Northern Ireland and  will consider the role of Modern Languages within that framework, including their position in the curriculum at different phases.A draft of the new statutory curriculum framework will be brought forward for public consultation and wider stakeholder engagement in Spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) research commissioned; (ii) pilot programmes initiated; or (iii) guidance issued, by his Department regarding the educational impact of ChatGPT and other large language models on (a) teaching; (b) assessment; and (c) curriculum, delivery.
answer_text: It is generally acknowledged that Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) has the potential to significantly enhance the delivery of the curriculum in schools and many schools are already using GenAI in their day-to-day work. I am keen to see it used effectively for the benefit of all learners.  Used appropriately, GenAI can support curriculum planning, enable interactive learning environments and provide real-time assessment and feedback, helping pupils understand their progress and areas for improvement.GenAI-powered tools, particularly in language learning, can assist in teaching reading, writing, and in new languages by adapting to each learner’s pace and ability.  They also offer opportunities to reduce administrative burdens in schools, freeing up time for principals and teachers to focus on teaching and learning. My Department has been leading a rigorous process, with a range of educational partners, to develop guidance for schools in respect of the use of GenAI in our schools which is grounded in research and practice in other jurisdictions including internationally.  The Education Authority (EA) and the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Curriculum (CCEA) have played a significant role in the development of this guidance.The EA’s Education Information Solution (EdIS) provides a safe online platform for use by teachers and pupils and none of the data inside a school’s own ecosystem is shared publicly.  This is a significant feature which is not available in other jurisdictions.  The EA is also developing a wide range of online materials which will be available on its EdIS GenAI Platform when the guidance is issued to schools.  In addition to this, CCEA has produced online information and guidance for pupils and parents on “Using the Internet and AI Safely in Assessments”.Of course, as with any digital technology, GenAI may raise challenges such as the potential for over-reliance, the spread of misinformation, the exacerbation of existing biases, privacy and security concerns, a widening of the digital divide, and academic integrity issues.  The guidance will, therefore set out the need for the use of GenAI to be lawful, conform to data protection requirements, be ethical, safe, secure, fair and transparent.  Central to all of this will be staff, pupil and parent / carer wellbeing.I anticipate that guidance will be available to issue to schools soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant of AQW 37678/22-27, to detail any steps her Department plans to take to strengthen local public engagement for future active travel schemes, including the (i) consultation timelines; (ii) methods for gathering community input; and (iii) mechanisms to ensure stakeholder feedback is incorporated into final design decisions.
answer_text: I recognise that meaningful engagement is critical to the delivery of pedestrian and cycling infrastructure that is welcomed by users and the local community. The updated active travel design guide currently being developed by my department will embed meaningful stakeholder and community engagement throughout the design process.  In addition to targeted engagement with key stakeholders and user groups throughout the design process, there will typically be two public engagement events for each scheme. The first at preliminary design stage to help inform detailed design, and the second as part of the statutory consultation associated with traffic regulation orders for the new infrastructure towards the end of the detailed design process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether (i) patients discharged from the Causeway Hospital are receiving equitable care compared with those in urban Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCT), given that (a) the Northern HSCT does not track domiciliary care by individual service user; (b) HSCT-funded care packages have remained static over the last five years; and (c) families are increasingly offered Direct Payments that may be impractical for rural households; (ii) this constitutes postcode-based disparity.
answer_text: My Department have reached out to the NHSCT to assist with your queries, they have provided the following information:(i|) 	The Northern Trust offers equity in terms of discharge pathways for individuals leaving all its acute hospital sites, this includes Antrim Area Hospital, Causeway Hospital and Holywell Hospital. (a)	The Northern Trust has a full record of all services users currently receiving Homecare and those awaiting to have a Home Care service sourced.(b)	Since 2022 the number of individuals receiving Home Care in the Northern Trust has increased by 16.1%, with projected growth of hours delivered in 2025/26 sitting at 12.3%.(c)	The option of Direct Payments would routinely be explored with all individuals and/or families alongside the option of a Trust arranged Home Care service. This approach is undertaken to promote choice and flexibility.(ii)     Access to a care package is not dependent on a person’s postcode.  Entitlement to a care package is based on assessed need, under the Health and Personal Social Services framework.  All individuals, regardless of where they live have the same statutory right to request an assessment.  The Trust carries out the assessment and operates under regionally agreed eligibility criteria and the Care Management Circular, 2010.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has held discussions with CCEA regarding any risks posed, and opportunities offered, by ChatGPT, and similar artificial intelligence tools.
answer_text: It is generally acknowledged that Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) has the potential to significantly enhance the delivery of the curriculum in schools and many schools are already using GenAI in their day-to-day work. I am keen to see it used effectively for the benefit of all learners.  Used appropriately, GenAI can support curriculum planning, enable interactive learning environments and provide real-time assessment and feedback, helping pupils understand their progress and areas for improvement.GenAI-powered tools, particularly in language learning, can assist in teaching reading, writing, and in new languages by adapting to each learner’s pace and ability.  They also offer opportunities to reduce administrative burdens in schools, freeing up time for principals and teachers to focus on teaching and learning. My Department has been leading a rigorous process, with a range of educational partners, to develop guidance for schools in respect of the use of GenAI in our schools which is grounded in research and practice in other jurisdictions including internationally.  The Education Authority (EA) and the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Curriculum (CCEA) have played a significant role in the development of this guidance.The EA’s Education Information Solution (EdIS) provides a safe online platform for use by teachers and pupils and none of the data inside a school’s own ecosystem is shared publicly.  This is a significant feature which is not available in other jurisdictions.  The EA is also developing a wide range of online materials which will be available on its EdIS GenAI Platform when the guidance is issued to schools.  In addition to this, CCEA has produced online information and guidance for pupils and parents on “Using the Internet and AI Safely in Assessments”.Of course, as with any digital technology, GenAI may raise challenges such as the potential for over-reliance, the spread of misinformation, the exacerbation of existing biases, privacy and security concerns, a widening of the digital divide, and academic integrity issues.  The guidance will, therefore set out the need for the use of GenAI to be lawful, conform to data protection requirements, be ethical, safe, secure, fair and transparent.  Central to all of this will be staff, pupil and parent / carer wellbeing.I anticipate that guidance will be available to issue to schools soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any measures he is taking to ensure the long-term future of non-teaching staff positions at North Coast Integrated College, Dunluce School and Coleraine College, including Human Resources, Finance, Payroll, canteen and catering staff, and classroom assistants; (ii) whether any changes to staff hours are anticipated as a result of shared-campus arrangements; and (iii) whether any changes to travel arrangements, insurance, health and safety duties, safeguarding responsibilities, or contractual terms are anticipated as a result of timetable or schedu
answer_text: The Education Authority, Principal Designate and Interim Board of Governors is planning for the establishment of Causeway Academy to meet the needs of both pupils and staff.  The non-teaching staff for the three schools will transfer to Causeway Academy on 1 September 2026 operating over three campuses:   Bushmills Campus, Carthall Campus and Cloyfin Campus.   A virtual information session has taken place with non-teaching staff to provide an overview and advise that a non-teaching staffing structure would be developed during the 2026/27 academic year - full consultation with affected staff and trade unions will take place in line with statutory requirements and agreed procedures.The employment protections afforded within the Collective Agreement for the Protection of Employment for Non-Teaching Staff (JNC 224), which applies in the event of school amalgamations, and those afforded under TUPE (Transfer of Undertakings (Protection of Employment)) Regulations will be complied with as appropriate.  Causeway Academy’s non-teaching staff will have the protections and support afforded to all Education Authority staff and subject to the appropriate protections applicable to their current employment status.  As Causeway Academy will operate over three campuses from 1 September 2026, staff and student schedules are currently in planning.   In this planning it is the intention to keep staff travel between campuses to a minimum.  However, any changes impacting staff will be subject to appropriate consultation with staff and recognised trade unions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment he has made of the impact of the new non-domestic valuation list on (i) town-centre regeneration; (ii) employment in local communities; and (iii) community wellbeing.
answer_text: The Department of Finance published the most recent ‘Reval 2026’ nondomestic valuation list on 22 January 2026, however the DoF Minister has since paused the outworkings of that process and will be considering next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment his Department has made of the impact of nitrous oxide use on public health.
answer_text: The misuse or illicit use of Nitrous Oxide can have impacts on the physical and mental health of individuals.My Department provided input into the Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs’ report “Research and Analysis Nitrous Oxide: Updated harms assessment” which sets out a robust assessment of the impact of the use and misuse of this substance. The report can be found at: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/nitrous-oxide-updated-harms-assessment/nitrous-oxide-updated-harms-assessment-accessible#conclusions-and-recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the (i) average; and (ii) median, time taken by the Education Authority to complete the statutory process for issuing Statements of Special Educational Needs; and (iii) number of current cases exceeding 22 weeks; and (a) the number of weeks by which those cases exceed that timeframe, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that:The answers for parts (i) and (ii) have been calculated using data from completed Statements within the last 12 months. Over this period, the mean time taken to complete a Statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN) was 25 weeks, and the median time was 23 weeks.iii) In relation to the open cases referenced, it should be noted that the initiation of a statutory assessment does not automatically result in the issue of a Statement. While a 22week timeframe has been referred to, the current statutory timeframe for completing a Statement of SEN is 26 weeks, as set out in the Special Educational Needs Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005.There are currently 124 open cases which have exceeded 22 weeks, of which 52 cases have progressed beyond the 26week statutory timeframe.iii a) Of the current 124 open cases exceeding the 22-week point, the total number of weeks beyond this timeframe amounts to 750 weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any evaluation his Department has conducted of the effectiveness of Local IMPACT Teams in ensuring children with suspected autism receive appropriate support; and, (ii) any data on the number of children with suspected autism (a) who received needs-led support, broken down by type of intervention; (b) who received support within four weeks of first concern; (c) who achieved targets set in their individual education plans; (d) who had reduced behavioural incidents or exclusions; and (e) for whom parents, or schools, reported that support met the ch
answer_text: No evaluation has been conducted to date. The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:Due to limitations in legacy Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) Services data systems, the EA is unable to provide the requested data for the past three academic years. Since September 2025, a new Management Information System is now in place for the Local IMPACT Teams, this will provide improved and more consistent data and information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider expanding the definition of educational disadvantage under point 8 of the TransformED strategy, to include (i) children of parents with addiction issues; (ii) looked-after children; and (iii) children with caring responsibilities.
answer_text: TransformED sets out a number of specific actions focused on tackling educational disadvantage. These are complementary to my Department’s existing suite of interventions designed to tackle educational disadvantage.    My Department recognises that educational disadvantage takes many forms and programmes such as RAISE seek to support children and young people facing a wide range of barriers. Last month, I announced an investment of over £3.7 million in 61 Locality Led Projects. This funding will support initiatives that promote educational excellence, raise standards, improve attendance, and strengthen pathways for learners at risk of underachievement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how research on adverse childhood experiences has informed education policy, during the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: I recognise the impact that adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) can have on children and their life chances, and also the importance of timely interventions to address these negative impacts. Evidence-based policy making is key to ensuring that policies and programmes deliver the best possible outcomes.One research-driven Departmental programme which is helps to address adverse childhood experiences is the Nurture Programme. Nurture is an earlyintervention measure for pupils experiencing social, emotional, or behavioural difficulties that may otherwise limit their learning and overall academic progress. As Nurture is a globally recognised early intervention approach, the development of the Programme has been informed by wider research into Nurture. To supplement this and to better inform policy and practice during the current Assembly mandate, the Department commissioned an external evaluation report which was published in January 2026.The report shows that Nurture Provision is already closely aligned with efforts to address the impact of ACEs. TraumaInformed Practice (TIP) is embedded within nurture approaches, helping children build trusting relationships, develop resilience and regulate emotions; key protective factors for those affected by early adversity. This alignment is significant given the scale of ACErelated harm in Northern Ireland, where individuals with four or more ACEs are far more likely to experience school exclusion, SEN needs and mental health difficulties.The findings and recommendations of the report will inform the Department’s decisions on the future approach to supporting Nurture. Furthermore, supports provided by the Education Authority's Education Welfare Service are underpinned by a Trauma Informed Approach, and Trauma Informed Practice training informed by the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland is available to all education sector staff by the Education Authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps he intends to take to ensure suicide prevention education is provided to every post-primary student, by the end of the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: The matter of suicide prevention is already covered within the statutory Northern Ireland Curriculum for post-primary. It is a matter for individual schools and teachers to judge when and where it is discussed. Schools should consider age and maturity of their pupils, ensure staff are suitably trained, and take account of pupils’ personal exposure/experiences before covering suicide directly.Pupils are provided with opportunities to develop an understanding of how to maximise and sustain their mental health, self-esteem and wellbeing, including managing emotions and reactions to ongoing life experiences and enhancing self-awareness and developing coping strategies.   As part of the Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum, Lucy Crehan examined the content and delivery of Learning for Life and Work (LLW), identifying significant weaknesses and recommending reform. Her recommendations include the rebranding and redesign of the delivery of LLW.As part of my response to the Strategic Review, I established the Northern Ireland Curriculum Taskforce to take forward work on designing and developing a new curriculum framework for Northern Ireland. As part of its work, the Taskforce will consider how each current component of Learning for Life and Work including Citizenship, Employability, Personal Development and Home Economics are best developed within the new statutory Curriculum.This work is currently progressing and there will be an opportunity for public consultation in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the reason no requirement was included to ensure representation from all school sectors in the selection of Curriculum Taskforce working group members; and (ii) any steps he will take to assure parents, teachers and governing bodies that the reformed curriculum will reflect the needs of pupils across controlled, maintained, integrated, Irish-medium and special schools.
answer_text: As confirmed in my response to AQW 37966/22-27, selection for the Subject Working Groups (SWG) was on based on merit, assessed against the published essential criteria in the Expression of Interest. All school sectors are represented in the membership of the SWGs.  The needs of all pupils are central to the programme of curriculum reform.   Further, the new curriculum will include bespoke strands for Irish-medium education and for pupils with Special Educational Needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how widely restorative practice is implemented across schools, and (ii) any funding that supports its delivery.
answer_text: Restorative practice (RP) has been progressively introduced across schools since 2022, following the publication of A Fair Start.  The Education Authority (EA) has worked to embed restorative and relational practices within a wider traumainformed and relational framework across schools and my Department has provided in the region of £500k to the EA to take forward this work.At present, 83 schools have engaged directly with EA restorative practice programmes, with 56 participating in wholeschool development, 176 accessing restorative conferencing or responsive referrals, and 66 staff trained to Facilitation Level through school-based CPD. With the appointment of six specialist practitioners, the EA intend to increase school engagement over the next two years, prioritising areas with higher levels of disadvantage, persistent absence, and exclusions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how the proposed Dalradian gold mine and associated waste facility aligns with (i) the protection of Areas of Outstanding Natural Beauty; and (ii) the Executive’s commitments to environmental sustainability and climate responsibility.
answer_text: The Planning and Water Appeals Commission are holding a public local inquiry on the proposed Dalradian mining proposal, re-commencing in April 2026. It is envisaged that issues such as the potential impact upon the Sperrin Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty, environmental and climate impacts will be considered at the Inquiry, and will be covered in the Commission’s report and recommendations to my Department on the proposal. It would therefore not be appropriate for me to provide comment, at this stage, as to do so may undermine the purpose of the Inquiry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether an equality impact assessment has been conducted on suspension patterns by school sector, particularly in relation to special educational needs pupils, and those from socioeconomically deprived backgrounds.
answer_text: My Department has not completed an equality impact assessment focused on suspension patterns by school sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any funding allocated to in-school alternatives to suspension and exclusion, including nurture units and behaviour support provision; and (ii) how this funding is distributed across school sectors; and (iii) whether current provision is sufficient to meet demand.
answer_text: The Department does not fund in-school alternatives to suspension or expulsion. Suspension and expulsion are formal disciplinary sanctions. Nurture provision and the Education Authority’s (EA) Behaviour Support Services are not alternatives to such sanctions, but are types of preventative and early-intervention supports designed to address underlying social, emotional and behavioural needs.As the Department does not fund specific in-school alternatives to suspension or expulsion, it is not possible to answer parts (ii) and (iii) of the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps being taken (i) to ensure that rural communities in the Sperrins are not disproportionately exposed to environmental and health risks arising from large-scale extractive industries; and (ii) by his Department to protect local residents, schools, and community facilities from potential pollution.
answer_text: The Planning (Environmental Impact Assessment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017 require certain extractive industry developments, which would include large scale extractive proposals, to undertake an Environmental Impact Assessment. This must address the likely significant effects on the environment. The findings must be reported in an Environmental Statement and accompany a planning application.My Department is a statutory consultee in the planning process and, when consulted along with other relevant consultees, will provide advice to planning authorities to inform their decisions.In addition, my Department operates a range of consenting, licensing and permitting regimes that regulate particular environmental aspects of certain operational project activity.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) oversight and guidance that exists to ensure that pupil suspension is used proportionately and appropriately; and (ii) any measures in place to promote restorative and supportive approaches as alternatives to exclusion from school.
answer_text: (i) The procedures governing the suspension and expulsion of pupils are underpinned by a statutory framework made up of departmental regulations and circulars, which together support the proportionate and appropriate use of suspension across all grant-aided schools.A key element of this statutory framework is the Schools (Suspension and Expulsion of Pupils) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1995, as amended, which set out the legal requirements governing the suspension and expulsion process and define the responsibilities placed on schools and Boards of Governors. This is supported by a series of departmental circulars, including:Circular 2015/19, which provides guidance on notifying the Education Authority of suspensions and sets out the reporting and record-keeping duties placed on principals.Circular 1995/09 and Circular 1998/25, which summarise the 1995 Regulations and outline the matters that must be included in each school’s suspension and expulsion scheme.Circular 2011/25, which gives effect to Article 34 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 2006 and places a duty on Boards of Governors to ensure that pupils receive suitable education while suspended.DE Circular 2021/04, which sets out the legal requirement for all sectors to operate a formal suspension and expulsion scheme. Under this arrangement, the Education Authority prepares the scheme for controlled schools, CCMS for Catholic maintained schools, and Boards of Governors for voluntary, grant-maintained integrated and Irish-medium schools.Under the existing legislation a pupil can be suspended only by the Principal, and they must immediately give written notification of a suspension to the parent of the pupil, the Chair of the Board of Governors, the Education Authority Region in which the school is located and (in the case of a pupil at a Catholic maintained school) to CCMS. The initial suspension must not exceed 5 days and a pupil can only be suspended for a maximum of 45 school days in a school year. In addition, the Principal cannot extend a period of suspension without the prior approval of the Chair of the Board of Governors. It is the duty of the Board of Governors of a grant-aided school to make arrangements for the provision of suitable education to a suspended pupil.Schools must operate within the discipline policies approved by their Boards of Governors. The combined effect of the statutory framework, sectoral suspension schemes, and school-level discipline policies provides the oversight and guidance necessary to ensure that suspension is used proportionately and appropriately.(ii) Suspension is a formal disciplinary measure, applied in accordance with schools’ behaviour and discipline policies and the statutory framework governingits use. Restorative and supportive practices do not operate as direct alternatives to suspension, but form part of a wider whole-school, trauma-informed and relational approach intended to support positive behaviour, address underlying need, an

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has reviewed and approved the Outline Business Case for the Dungiven Regeneration project.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) guidance; and (ii) resources, available to schools to address issues related to misogyny, gender-based attitudes and so-called toxic masculinity, through preventative education and staff training, rather than through disciplinary action alone.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Curriculum places a strong emphasis on healthy relationships, and through Relationship and Sexuality Education (RSE), schools can play a key role in addressing emerging issues such as violence against women and girls, misogyny, gender-based attitudes, coercive control, and sexual harassment.  Council for Curriculum Examination and Assessment (CCEA) has  developed an RSE progression framework and online RSE Hub which signposts schools to organisations that offer training and support for teachers and other educators. There is also a Training and Professional Development section, which details the types of RSE training that different organisations offer.CCEA has also developed extensive age-appropriate resources, which includes lesson plans, PowerPoints, case studies, activities and short films to support the delivery of RSE. These include the context of a preventative curriculum and materials relevant to misogyny related matters.  It is a matter for individual schools to decide which resources they will use to support their delivery of the Curriculum.My Department is working closely with The Executive Office, and other key stakeholders, in respect of delivering the Healthy Relationships Forum and an agreed action plan that will coordinate interventions targeted at children and young people and focussed on developing healthy relationships.  Since 2022, Education Authority has worked to embed restorative and relational practices within a wider trauma informed and relational framework across schools.  In 2025, a business case was approved to transform the Education Welfare Service, which included the recruitment of six specialist practitioners in restorative practice who took up post in October 2025.Regarding staff training, as part of my TransformED NI agenda, the Teacher Professional Learning Fund provides a £31m investment in teacher professional learning (TPL) over the next three academic years. Teaching and learning - including pedagogy, subject specific learning and behaviour management was identified as one of seven strategic priorities for the 2025/26 academic year. This will enable schools to use the TPL Fund to address specific issues they have identified as professional learning priorities within their school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps he will take to address the £250 million in-year funding gap for education, without reducing frontline services in schools
answer_text: Unfortunately, the steps which my Department can take to address the Education Authority’s inyear funding gap of £250 million, without impacting frontline services in the current financial year, are extremely limited.  The vast majority of my Department’s budget addresses statutory and demand-led obligations, leaving very little scope to reduce expenditure in the short term.A key limiting factor is the large proportion of the Education budget which relates to staff costs.  Only c£550m of this year’s total resource budget relates to non-staff costs whilst the Education Authority’s (Education Authority) forecast overcommitment exceeds £250m.The scale of the EA’s budget shortfall is such that the short-term savings required could only be delivered through an extensive redundancy programme which would devastate educational outcomes and undermine the very fabric of our system, as well as requiring dedicated in-year redundancy funding of a similar level to the necessary savings.In recent years, and again this year, many difficult decisions have been taken to operate within budget.  These include cessation of the Holiday Hunger, Engage, Healthy Happy Minds, and Home-to-School Link programmes; cuts to the EA Block Grant; non-funding of pressures such as school maintenance; the EA’s recent decision to increase the price of school meals to address inflationary cost increases; non-funding of NDPB pressures; and ongoing vacancy controls.  In addition, my Department has taken a number of other actions which, whilst they may not address immediate budget pressures, they aim to reform education delivery in Northern Ireland and support long-term efficiencies.  These include: TransformED, SEN Transformation and the recent Review of Schools’ Baseline Funding.  My Department has also been engaging with the EA to bring forward further savings measures including:implementing savings in home-to-school transport;a 50p increase in the price of a school meal for paying pupils from 1 January 2026;savings impacting the Education Authority's Music Service; andhalting referrals to external, contracted providers of Education Other Than At School (EOTAS) services.My Department continues to work closely with the EA to pursue all feasible measures to reduce pressures and slow the rate of expenditure. However, even with maximum effort, it is clear that the Department will still face an overspend significantly in excess of £200m.Looking ahead, the current draft multi-year budget will have grave consequences for our education system. Under the proposed allocations, my Department would be required to make savings of approximately £0.9 billion in 2026-27, £1.01 billion in 2027-28, and £1.15 billion in 2028-29. These levels of reduction are simply not achievable.Recognising the gravity of the financial situation, my Department has established an Education Sustainability Taskforce comprised of Departmental policy leads, senior EA officials and Department of Finance officials.  This grou

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that data on inspections, enforcement outcomes, and repeat offenders is not held centrally, to detail (i) any steps he is taking to ensure that existing enforcement against illegal vape sales to children is effective; and (ii) by what date he will mandate standardised, local council-wide reporting of inspections, prosecutions, and repeat offences.
answer_text: There are currently 12 Tobacco Control Officers (TCOs) operating across the 11 councils in Northern Ireland. Funded by the Public Health Agency (PHA) under the Department’s Tobacco Control Strategy, TCOs enforce tobacco and nicotinerelated legislation and support wider public health objectives.Smoke free inspections, tobacco retailer advisory visits / compliance checks and age of sale test purchases form the key focus areas of tobacco legislation enforcement. Councils record and submit this detailed information on a quarterly basis to the Public Health Agency (PHA) who, in turn, share it with the Department of Health.  NI Council Tobacco Control Officers (TCOs) are represented by Belfast City Council (BCC) on the Tobacco Strategy Implementation Steering Group (TSISG), which is chaired by the Public Health Agency (PHA). BCC also sits on the Policy, Legislation and Enforcement Subgroup, which meets on a quarterly basis and includes a report on Council enforcement activity.The Tobacco and Vapes Bill includes provision to strengthen enforcement, including extending the Tobacco Retailer Register to cover vapes (including nonnicotine) and nicotine product retailers, and enabling the future introduction of a retail licensing scheme. My Department continues to work closely with the PHA and Councils to prepare for implementation of the provisions of the Bill following its enactment.  Given the range of relevant legislation in this area, my officials would be pleased to facilitate a meeting alongside the PHA, to provide an update on the Bill and to address any specific queries you may have in relation to the enforcement work undertaken by the TCOs.  If this would be helpful, please contact tobaccocontrolteam@health-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on any progress made by the Cross-Departmental Working Group on the Irish Language Strategy, including (i) the departments involved; (ii) any work completed, to date; (iii) the anticipated timeline for publication of a draft strategy; and (iv) how inclusivity, non-partisanship, and accessibility will be reflected in the final proposals.
answer_text: All Executive Departments are currently engaged through the work programme of the Cross-Departmental Working Group (CDWG). To date, the CDWG will aim to ensure that each strategy is coherent, costed and accompanied by a series of achievable actions within departmental remits.		The timetable for publication of the draft strategies is largely outside my control given the range of material to be reviewed by each Executive Department within the work programme of the CDWG.Equality Impact Assessments and Rural Needs Impact Assessments shall be conducted for both.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given that his Department does not collect data on dog breeding licences, inspections or enforcement by councils, to detail (i) any steps he takes to ensure consistent animal welfare standards; and (ii) whether he will introduce a requirement for local councils to submit annual, standardised returns on licensing and enforcement activity.
answer_text: Councils, as autonomous organisations, have scope to discharge their statutory responsibilities for licensing of dog breeding establishments, in accordance with their own enforcement policies, governance frameworks and reporting requirements. For information, my department has established an Expert Advisory Group to conduct a comprehensive review of the operation of dog breeding regulations. The Group has engaged with a wide range of stakeholders, including councils and will publish a report and recommendations later this year. I look forward to considering the Group’s recommendations once received.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) terms of reference; (ii) reporting arrangements; and (iii) key outputs, of all panels and task forces established under TransformED, including (iv) recommendations that have been (a) accepted; (b) rejected; or (c) are pending consideration
answer_text: Three panels have been specifically established to support implementation of the overall TransformED strategy as follows:Academics’ PanelPrimary Principals’ Panel Post Primary Principals’ Panel The groups do not make formal recommendations to the Department but rather provide practitioner and academic perspectives on emerging policy.  All discussions and documents relating to the panels are treated as confidential as they relate to policy under development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how the TransformED strategy will specifically reduce educational disadvantage, particularly for pupils from low socioeconomic backgrounds; (ii) how this approach differs from previous reforms in England; and (iii) how his Department will (a) measure; and (b) report on, TransformED’s impact on educational disadvantage.
answer_text: As in many systems internationally, there remains a long-standing and strong correlation between socio-economic disadvantage and educational outcomes in Northern Ireland. There are also significant disparities in outcomes between schools with pupils experiencing similar levels of disadvantage. TransformED, therefore, places tackling educational disadvantage at the heart of education reform in Northern Ireland. It recognises that genuine excellence is impossible without equity, and that pupils from lowincome and deprived backgrounds require not only targeted support but a fundamentally stronger, fairer education system. For this reason, TransformED delivers both major systemwide reforms and specific programmelevel interventions designed to raise attainment, improve life chances and remove the structural barriers that persist for disadvantaged children and young people.At system level, TransformED represents a comprehensive reform of curriculum, assessment, qualifications and school improvement, all underpinned by significant investment in Teacher Professional Learning (TPL). It seeks to address longstanding inequalities by improving curriculum design and resources, tackling teacher shortages in key subjects, and ensuring that teachers are supported to deliver a coherent, knowledgerich curriculum. The most fundamental feature of an education system which promotes social mobility is one in which every child is able to attend a good school. There is a need to secure educational excellence everywhere - so that a pupil, in whatever circumstances, wherever they live, and in any school, receives the highest possible standard of education. This is a key focus of our commitments in regard to school improvement and inspection within the TransformED strategy. High-quality teaching is the most important lever schools have to improve outcomes for their pupils. Yet it has long been recognised that schools serving disadvantaged communities are more likely to be staffed by teachers with fewer years of experience, lower qualifications and by non-specialist science and maths teachers.Inequality in access to suitably qualified, high-quality teachers is likely to be an important contributor to the attainment gap that exists between pupils who come from disadvantaged families and those who do not. TransformED’s detailed plans to address teacher shortages in key subjects, support high-quality teaching, improve curriculum resources and invest in professional learning are, therefore, critical to raising attainment for disadvantaged pupils. Disadvantaged children are more likely to rely upon the school curriculum to provide the intellectual foundation they need to grow into confident, articulate young adults able to advance to an apprenticeship, university or a rewarding career.As E.D. Hirsch has powerfully argued, and cognitive psychologists such as Daniel Willingham have proved, a ‘skills-based’ curriculum, light on knowledge provides scant support to disadvantaged chil

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any support being provided to schools to deal with drug-related incidents involving synthetic substances, and laced vapes; and (ii) whether teachers are receiving specific training on these emerging risks.
answer_text: Supporting schools to respond to these challenging situations is a key priority for my department. CCEA issued the ‘Drugs Guidance for Schools in Northern Ireland’, to provide an outline in respect of the statutory requirements for all schools in Northern Ireland. These requirements include that schools must have a drugs policy in place which is published in their school prospectus, alongside providing drugs education and also to inform the PSNI if they believe or suspect a pupil to be in possession of a controlled substance. The department has also set out its expectations that every school should appoint a designated teacher with responsibility for drugs and should have agreed, identified procedures in place for handling cases of suspected drug misuse on the premises. The Public Health Agency continues to commission a range of drug and alcohol intervention and prevention services across Northern Ireland, that schools can access within their local trust areas, to provide information and signposting to services for young people. Accessing support via these services and the local Health Trust Drugs and Alcohol Coordination Teams will ensure that schools and pupils have access to the most up to date and relevant information, from trusted sources in this field.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of (i) whether the CCEA drugs guidance, last updated in 2019, adequately addresses the rise in (a) synthetic cannabinoids; (b) vaping of illegal substances; and (c) social media-based drug supply; and (ii) whether he has any plans to update the guidance.
answer_text: Council for the Curriculum, Examination and Assessment (CCEA), as an arm-length body of my Department, provides a range of resources and guidance on drugs education through its website to support both the Northern Ireland Curriculum and CCEA qualifications.CCEA has advised my Department that it is actively monitoring this area of the curriculum as behaviours are constantly evolving. Guidance and resources are reviewed in line with CCEA’s overall review of curriculum materials policy and will be updated over time, and in line with the incoming Northern Ireland Curriculum under the TransformED strategy. In 2015, on behalf of my Department, CCEA published Revised Guidance on Drugs Education for schools which is available on its website at https://ccea.org.uk/learning-resources/drugs-education-revised-guidanceThis guidance includes explicit reference to new psychoactive substances (NPS), which include synthetic cannabinoids, and electronic cigarettes or vapes. The guidance states that ‘it is a statutory requirement for all schools in Northern Ireland to:have a drugs policy and publish details in relation to the policy in their prospectus (Education (School Information and Prospectuses) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003); anddeliver drugs education to include legal and illegal substances (The Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007).’The definition of drugs within the guidance includes ‘over-the-counter medicines such as paracetamol and cough medicine; prescribed drugs, such as antibiotics, painkillers, antidepressants, antipsychotics, inhalers and stimulants such as Ritalin. Each school’s policy should include reference to ‘prescription drugs, NPS, tobacco, tobacco-related products, electronic cigarettes (vapes) and alcohol’.In November 2024, CCEA also published a series of lessons on Alcohol and substance misuse. These comprehensive, adaptable lessons for Key Stage 3 provide up-to-date information on the risks associated with alcohol and substance misuse. They aim to equip pupils with the knowledge and skills to make healthier choices and empower them to make informed decisions.The CCEA website also provides educators with a link to You and Me N.I. Vape Free. This educational, evidence-based programme was produced by Northern Ireland Chest Heart and Stroke, in collaboration with Stanford University REACH Lab, to address the growing issue of youth vaping across Northern Ireland. Topics include:Brain development and addictionThe effects of e-cigarettes on the bodyThe environmental impact of vapingMarketing tacticsStress and mental health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail a breakdown of the £5,422,503 spent to date under the TransformED Delivery Plan, including expenditure for each of the 30 actions, including (i) the specific projects or initiatives funded; (ii) the organisations, or individuals, in receipt of funding; (iii) the amount allocated to each; (iv) whether the funding was awarded through (a) open competition; (b) direct award; or (c) existing frameworks; and (v) any assessment that has been made of (a) value for money; and (b) impact.

The vast majority of this expenditure has been allocated directly to school budgets to support teacher professional development. All to provide resources and professional learning directly to schools.Commercial contracts and grant.  This includes one Direct Award Contract for the Science of Learning CPD Academy for post-primary schools, which was awarded in full compliance with public sector requirements. Research £140,000Research FunctionGeneral expenditureRevision of CCEA Qualifications£65,000Technical work undertaken in preparation for the revision of CCEA qualificationsEar marked budget allocation to CCEA for 2025/26Each project is subject to specific evaluation methodology, including post project evaluation, and in some cases ETI evaluation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of rural premises that currently do not have access to gigabit-capable, full-fibre broadband, and (ii) the timeline for extending access to those premises.
answer_text: Information published by Ofcom in November indicates that in July 2025, 26,678 (10%) of the 270,351 rural premises here could not access a gigabit-capable broadband service. Ofcom estimate that 94% of all premises here can access a gigabit-capable broadband service and 90% of rural premises can access a gigabit-capable broadband service. Availability here is higher than in neighbouring regions. Ofcom also estimate that 87% of all premises in England (60% of rural premises), 80% of all premises in Scotland (49% of rural premises) and 79% of all premises in Wales (58% of rural premises) can access gigabit-capable broadband. The Irish Commission for Communications Regulation (ComReg) estimate that 89% of all premises in the South have access to gigabit-capable broadband. ComReg’s information is not broken down by rurality. In December 2025, a contract to deliver Project Gigabit was awarded to Fibrus Networks. This contract will see gigabit-capable services delivered to 9,333 premises, most of which are rural, over the next two years. It is anticipated that ongoing commercial rollout will address some of the other premises currently without access to a gigabit-capable broadband service, however it is recognised that not all premises will be able to access such services; due to current funding available, some premises will not be covered. Those premises are very remote, and a solution currently does not represent value for money and is not affordable. My officials are exploring viable and affordable alternatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any formal role the Department of Health has in (i) setting; (ii) reviewing; or (iii) monitoring, nutritional standards for school meals.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) and the Department of Health (DoH) are jointly responsible for the food in schools policy Healthy Food For Healthy Outcomes. A key part of this policy is the use of nutritional standards for all food consumed in schools. DoH and the Public Health Agency (PHA) (previously the Health Promotion Agency (HPA)) continue to play an important role in the use of nutritional standards in schools as they have since nutritional standards were first introduced into schools over 20 years ago. The current Nutritional Standards for School Lunches and Other Food and Drinks in Schools were developed in close collaboration with DoH and the HPA. The implementation of which is supported through the publication of Nutritional Standards for School Lunches - A Guide for Implementation under the School food: Top Marks Programme, which was a joint venture by DE, DoH and the HPA. To support and oversee the implementation of the joint policy, a cross-organisational Food in Schools Forum meets regularly, chaired by DE with both DoH and PHA represented on the Forum. It was instrumental in the most recent review of the nutritional standards that was completed in 2021. Unfortunately, severe budgetary pressures mean that the revised standards resulting from this review have not been able to be implemented to date. A group of food experts with educational and health representatives led the detailed work to review and make recommendations on the nutritional standards. This group was chaired by the Regional Food in Schools Co-ordinator, a post jointly funded by DoH and DE. The Co-ordinator is able to advise and support the Education Authority and schools in all aspect of school food including the implementation of nutritional standards as well as providing policy advice to both Departments.Both the practical implementation of the current nutritional standards and ongoing work seeking to enable the implementation of the revised nutritional standards is discussed at each Food in Schools Forum meeting. A sub-group of the Forum is currently working on the latter and DoH and PHA are closely involved in this ongoing work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any formal assessment his Department has made of the longer-term impact on (i) schools; (ii) pupils; and (iii) educational provision, arising from the current financial pressures and overspending within his Department.
answer_text: Our Education system continues to face unavoidable cost pressures and rising service demands associated with delivering statutory and policy obligations that the current 2025-26 budget does not fully address. My Department’s allocation for 2025-26 post-December monitoring remains significantly less than what is required and leaves a substantial shortfall across the Education sector. The Education Authority currently faces a Resource DEL overspend in excess of £250m in respect of inescapable and pre-committed pressures only and therefore only the most limited funding is available for Special Educational Needs (SEN), schools, transport and schools’ maintenance.  This has had an extremely damaging impact on the education system, with our children with SEN potentially facing the most significant harm. The multi-year budget allocations proposed by the Minister of Finance, which have not yet been agreed by the Executive, are out for public consultation until 3 March 2026.  The draft budget would require my Department to make savings of c£826m in 2026-27, £1.01bn in 2027-28 and £1.15bn in 2028-29. Given that c82% of the education budget meets staffing costs, there is no reasonable prospect of my Department being able to live within the proposed budget in the short-term, without mass compulsory redundancies, which would require dedicated in-year funding of a similar level to the required savings.  Such redundancy funding does not currently exist.  Clearly, this would have a devastating impact on educational outcomes and undermine the very fabric of our system. In the absence of redundancies, I would be required to cut major schemes on which families and communities rely, including home-to-school transport, free school meals, school uniform grant, youth services and extended schools. The Northern Ireland Childcare Scheme would also become unsustainable.  Even after cuts of this magnitude my Department would still fall far short of the savings required. As we look ahead to 2026-27, it is worth noting that Finance Minister’s budget consultation fails to highlight the stark reality, being that the proposed draft budget is simply undeliverable.  In particular, the draft three-year resource budget issued for public consultation is silent on the quantum of financial pressures facing departments.   My Department has faced historic underfunding for many years and an inadequate baseline that has only been sustained through in-year allocations.  This structural weakness is only set to worsen over the next three-year spending period. This cannot continue to be ignored and nor can we continue to neglect education through three more years of underfunding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 36913/22-27, to detail (i) any steps taken to ensure balanced representation across (a) school sectors; (b) subject specialisms; and (c) community backgrounds, in the final composition of the Curriculum Taskforce working groups; (ii) who assessed the expressions of interest for membership of the working groups; and (iii) any moderation or quality assurance processes applied to the selection decisions.
answer_text: An open call for membership of the Subject Working Groups issued to all teachers and academics across Northern Ireland.Selection for the Groups was based on the information provided by applicants assessed against the published essential criteria.School sector was not a criterion, reflecting both the merit principle and that the curriculum is delivered in all publicly funded schools. I am pleased to note, however, that the Working Groups include representation from all school sectors. Applicants were asked to indicate their subject specialisms and preferences.   A group was then established for each subject within the curriculum with groups for English (including literacy and drama), Mathematics, Science, Art and Design, Digital Technology / Information Technology, History, Geography, Modern Languages, Music, Physical Education, Technology and Design, Home Economics, Personal Social Civic and Careers Education (PSCC), thus ensuring that subject specialists were placed within the relevant groups. Community background was not considered as part of the process, as selection was based on merit from information provided. Expressions of interest were assessed by officials in my department, as well as the Chair and Deputy Chair of the Taskforce allowing appropriate quality assurance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to the TransformED strategy, to detail any actions his Department is taking to ensure that the history and cultural significance of the Irish language will be considered, in the development of the new statutory curriculum framework, including any (i) timelines; (ii) stakeholder engagement processes; and (iii) expected outputs, from the relevant curriculum taskforces.
answer_text: The Curriculum Taskforce is developing a new statutory curriculum framework for Northern Ireland, to be supported by high-quality learning resources and a clear plan for implementation and evaluation.  As part of this work, a new framework will be developed for Modern Languages.  The work will include proposals that reflect not only linguistic competence but also the cultural significance of languages, other than English,  within the new curriculum framework.  There may also be the need for bespoke curriculum strands for the Irish-medium sector.  A draft of the new statutory curriculum framework will be presented in Spring 2026, for public consultation and wider stakeholder engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any requirements placed on schools to ensure that key policy documents are kept up to date, and accessible on their websites, for parents and guardians.
answer_text: Responsibility for setting and maintaining policies generally rests with the Board of Governors of the school.The Freedom of Information Act 2000 requires public bodies, including schools, to establish and maintain a Publication Scheme. A Publication Scheme sets out a school’s commitment to make certain classes of information, which can include its policies, routinely available.
