question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) his Department’s assessment of the introduction of an online system for the delivery of GCSE results in Northern Ireland, comparable to the education record app being introduced in England from 2026; and (ii) any engagement he has had with CCEA on this matter.
answer_text: CCEA Regulation advised that consideration had previously been given to a similar approach to GCSE results publication here. However, concerns were raised about the appropriateness of students aged 15 or 16, receiving their GCSE results potentially on their own, without the opportunity to receive on-site pastoral, academic and careers support from school staff.   As with all aspects of examinations service delivery, the Department and CCEA will keep the matter under review.  It should be noted that AS level and A level students in Northern Ireland, are able to access their results digitally through an online system. It was judged to be appropriate given the age profile of pupils who would be availing of this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the minimum standards for toilet provision in Emergency Departments; (ii) how compliance is monitored; and (iii) whether patient flow and overcrowding are considered in facilities planning.
answer_text: The standards for toilet provision in Emergency Departments, in line with other workplace environments, are provided within British Standard (BS) 6465-1, Code of Practice for the design of sanitary facilities and scales of provision of sanitary and associated appliances. Additional information on the scale of provision and ergonometric data is provided in relevant Health Building Notes (HBN) including HBN15-01 Planning and Design Guidance for Accident and Emergency Departments. The number of toilets provided will be determined by design calculations using relevant tables contained within BS 6465-1, based on the anticipated number of persons using the facility.Compliance with these standards relating to the provision of WC facilities is monitored at initial building design and construction stages by Building Control.Patient flow is considered during the design, planning and commissioning of all health and social care facilities. Where applicable, arrangements may be made for the provision of additional spatial accommodation to account for demand variations and minimise overcrowding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of public houses that have closed, in each of the last ten years, broken down by constituency; and (ii) any steps her Department is taking to support the sustainability of the hospitality sector.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the information you have requested. The closure of pubs and nightclubs is disappointing not only in economic terms but also from a cultural and community perspective. My Department, alongside Tourism NI and the three Belfast Business Improvement Districts, has commissioned Ipsos to conduct research to better understand the issues affecting the night-time economy.While the final report is anticipated soon, the interim findings suggest reliable late-night transport, safety, tourism and licensing are key factors affecting the success of our night-time economy. I was pleased to work with Minister Kimmins, Executive colleagues and Belfast City Council to secure funding for a late-night public transport pilot scheme in Belfast. Initial feedback on uptake is positive and my hope is that this pilot will lead to advancements in late-night transport provision across our region, thereby supporting our night-time economy.Collaboration is crucial if we are to support and sustain our hospitality and cultural venues. I recently met with Hospitality Ulster and NI Food to Go Association to discuss the challenges facing their sector, particularly around VAT. I remain committed to engaging with key stakeholders as we seek to develop a safe, thriving and diverse night-time economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of nightclubs that have closed, in each of the last ten years, broken down by constituency; and (ii) any assessment her Department has made of the impact such closures have had on local night-time economies.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the information you have requested. The closure of pubs and nightclubs is disappointing not only in economic terms but also from a cultural and community perspective. My Department, alongside Tourism NI and the three Belfast Business Improvement Districts, has commissioned Ipsos to conduct research to better understand the issues affecting the night-time economy.While the final report is anticipated soon, the interim findings suggest reliable late-night transport, safety, tourism and licensing are key factors affecting the success of our night-time economy. I was pleased to work with Minister Kimmins, Executive colleagues and Belfast City Council to secure funding for a late-night public transport pilot scheme in Belfast. Initial feedback on uptake is positive and my hope is that this pilot will lead to advancements in late-night transport provision across our region, thereby supporting our night-time economy.Collaboration is crucial if we are to support and sustain our hospitality and cultural venues. I recently met with Hospitality Ulster and NI Food to Go Association to discuss the challenges facing their sector, particularly around VAT. I remain committed to engaging with key stakeholders as we seek to develop a safe, thriving and diverse night-time economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any (i) feasibility studies; (ii) options appraisals; and (iii) community consultations, undertaken by her Department, prior to the approval of the Greysteel to Ballykelly greenway, including the (a) dates; (b) methods; and (c) outcomes, of any public engagement.
answer_text: The local council report first lists the scheme in Spring 2021. During that time, the process for public engagement on active travel schemes was not as embedded within the scheme development procedures as it will be going forward, however local engagement with those affected would also have been carried out under Minister Mallon in 2021 and 2022.Throughout design development the Department consulted with key stakeholders including adjacent landowners, residents, statutory undertakers, Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council, PSNI Traffic Section, Translink and various DfI internal offices. Each identified stakeholder was notified and asked for their views for consideration by the project team.On completion, the scheme will provide significantly enhanced cycling and pedestrian facilities between Ballykelly and Greysteel and is part of a long-term plan outlined in my department’s Strategic Plan for Greenways that was published in 2016.Further details can be found on the Department’s website: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/dfi2025-0560-details-a2-clooney-road-active-travel-scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 36634/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 36634/22-27 was answered on 9 January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any measures her Department is taking to increase the proportion of officers from a Catholic background, within the Police Service of Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Recruitment decisions are operational matters for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  There is legislative provision for targeting advertising to reach under-represented groups and I am encouraged by the efforts of the PSNI to ensure advertising is as wide-reaching as possible.As Minister I have been proactive in calling for a fully representative police service and encouraging applicants from under-represented groups. I would emphasise once again that it is incumbent upon all of us, as elected representatives, to encourage all members of our community to consider joining the PSNI and to help to create the societal conditions where that is most likely to occur.Threats and intimidation by dissident and other paramilitary actors is a significant barrier to the recruitment of those from Catholic or Nationalist backgrounds, so the work of the justice system to tackle those groups also has an important role to play.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has considered securing grit or salt boxes to the ground, in order to prevent theft.
answer_text: Salt bins are assessed for suitability following requests from the public or elected representatives. Bins are replaced as necessary when they reach the end of their serviceable life. My Department is not aware of a significant issue concerning bins being stolen and currently has no plans to secure grit bins to the ground.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) criteria; and (ii) decision-making processes, used by his Department when considering whether a teacher holding a Postgraduate Certificate in Further Education may be permitted to teach at post-primary level, including any role played by the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has no input into the criteria and decision-making processes determining whether a teacher holding a Postgraduate Certificate in Further Education may be permitted to teach at post-primary level. The General Council Teaching for Northern Ireland (GTCNI) maintains the register for teachers and as the professional body for teaching in Northern Ireland, it sets its own qualification rules to be admitted to its register, as per Article 36(2)(a) of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, as amended by Article 35 of The Education (Northern Ireland) Order 2006, to enable a teacher to teach in grant aided schools in Northern Ireland. These were approved by the previous Council in 2017 before it was stood down in 2021.The consistent application of GTCNI’s Qualification Rules is seen as an important safeguard for the quality of our teaching workforce and the Department has no authority to intervene in these.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he is aware that patients with idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) are being refused access to mounjaro (tirzepatide) on funding grounds; (ii) to detail any clinical and commissioning criteria Health and Social Care Trusts currently use to approve or reject access to this medication; (iii) for his assessment of whether this disadvantages patients with IIH compared to patients with diabetes; and (iv) whether he will review current funding arrangements in light of emerging clinical evidence that weight-loss medications can preserve sight and induc
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.NICE published TA1026 which is recommends tirzepatide as an option for managing overweight and obesity, and TA924 tirzepatide for treating for treating type 2 diabetes. NICE has not yet published any recommendations for the use of tirzepatide for the treatment of idiopathic intracranial hypertension. In these circumstances the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) does not routinely commission that therapy for use in Northern Ireland.However in the absence of a published NICE determination or the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) has the discretion to reflect another UK Health Technology Appraisal body (such as the Scottish Medicines Consortium (SMC) or All Wales Medicines Strategy Group (AWMSG)), pending any recommendation from NICE to inform longer-term commissioning decisions. This is commonly referred to as the Process for the Managed Entry of New Medicines or ‘Managed Entry’. To date, neither SMC or AWMSG have published a recommendation in support of the use of tirzepatide for individuals with IIH.In these circumstances, the individual funding request (IFR) process provides a mechanism to consider requests from clinical consultants for treatments for individual patients that fall outside the range of services routinely commissioned. Any request must meet the criteria as determined by the Department’s IFR policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department plans to take to improve Emergency Department waiting areas for (i) women experiencing miscarriages; (ii) patients with sensory needs; and (iii) patients experiencing drug withdrawal.
answer_text: All spaces within the waiting areas and treatment areas of an Emergency Department (ED) are designed to be used as flexibly as possible to accommodate a wide range of presenting conditions including but not limited to the conditions mentioned in the question.My Department does not currently have any plans to mandate improvements to Emergency Department waiting areas for (i) women experiencing miscarriages; (ii) patients with sensory needs; and (iii) patients experiencing drug withdrawal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any actions he is taking to prevent increased Education Authority Music Service fees from widening educational disadvantage, given that pupils just above the Free School Meals threshold receive no remission; and (ii) how this policy is being kept under review in the context of the RAISE Programme.
answer_text: (i) To mitigate the risk of widening educational disadvantage, the EA has retained the 70% remission rate for pupils entitled to Free School Meals (FSME). While pupils just above the FSM threshold do not currently receive remission, the EA has undertaken an Equality Screening exercise. The screening concluded that maintaining a significant remission rate for FSME pupils mitigates the most acute risk of exclusion. (ii) The policy is being kept under review in the context of the RAISE Programme, which seeks to address educational disadvantage. EA will monitor the effect of fee changes on participation rates and income generation, with particular attention to pupils from low-income households.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration his Department has given to piloting accredited arts programmes in primary schools as a means of supporting pupil engagement, confidence, and wider educational outcomes, particularly in areas of social and economic disadvantage.
answer_text: My Department funds the Extended Schools (ES) programme, which supports schools with high levels of social and economic disadvantage to help pupils reach their full potential. Schools may use ES funding, as well as their own delegated budgets, to deliver arts activities as part of their provision though a register of such arts programmes, accredited or otherwise is not maintained. In February 2025, I launched the pilot Creative Connections programme, inviting primary, post primary and special schools the opportunity for their teachers and pupils, through the RAISE programme, to collaborate with professional creative practitioners. The programme is not accredited and there is currently no consideration being given to piloting an accredited arts programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of early years arts and cultural education initiatives, that help build skills, confidence and engagement from a young age; and (ii) whether any funding streams could align with a pilot of this nature.
answer_text: It is my assessment that early years arts and cultural education initiatives build skills, confidence and engagement from a young age.  The work the Arts Council does helps to support this by funding arts organisations which provide a wide range of quality arts engagement in schools throughout Northern Ireland.  The Arts Council may be contacted via info@artscouncil-ni.org for further information in relation to any funding streams which may be available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any funding administered by his Department, including through the Arts Council of Northern Ireland or community-based arts programmes, could support a pilot delivering accredited arts education within disadvantaged primary schools.
answer_text: The Arts Council, through its funding to arts organisations, already supports a wide range of quality arts engagement in schools throughout Northern Ireland, some of which is accredited.  The Arts Council may be contacted via info@artscouncil-ni.org for further information in relation to any funding streams which may be available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 37397/22-27, to detail (i) any actions he is taking to address the approximately 185 per cent increase in recorded assaults on teachers and classroom assistants between 2021 and 2024; and (ii) whether he is prioritising schools experiencing repeat, or high-volume incidents for support.
answer_text: As highlighted in the referenced AQW 37397, my Department is not the employers of teachers or classroom assistants. Work related accidents or assaults on school staff are not reported to my Department but instead are reported to the Education Authority (EA).We have again asked the EA for a response to this question, and they have provided their response on behalf of EA and CCMS.(i) The Education Authority Health and Safety Service works continuously with schools to reduce all work-related accidents and incidents in accordance with the Health and Safety at Work Order 1978. The Health and Safety Service manages the Accident Reporting System which schools use to report accidents and incidents as required under the Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrences Regulations 1997.  The Accident Reporting System is also used to identify trends and inform the development of appropriate mitigations. In the context of assaults on teachers and classroom assistants, a significant mitigation has been the development of a Violence at Work Policy and supporting guidance documents.   This Policy and guidance has been collaboratively developed with the CYPS, HR and Education Directorates to ensure, that so far as is reasonably practicable, that EA staff for whom they hold a duty of care, do not become subject to violence from other adults (members of public, parents, other staff) or pupils whilst in the workplace or on any premises or vehicle for which the EA has responsibility.  The objective of this Policy is to reduce the risk of workplace violence to as low a level as is reasonably practicable.  The EA Education Directorate has led the working group that is responsible for engagement with the Northern Ireland Teaching Council (NITC), CCMS and the sectoral support bodies (NICIE, CnaG, GBA) and will oversee its launch in January 2026. (ii) The health and safety team continues to provide ongoing support and guidance to principals and service managers e.g. Behaviour Support and other specialists directly supporting those schools experiencing repeat or high-volume incidents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of (a) teachers; and (b) teaching assistants, who have been the victim of a work-related accident or assault; and (ii) the number of teachers who have had to (a) take time off work; and (b) retire, as a consequence of these incidents in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department is not the employers of teachers or classroom assistants. Work related accidents or assaults on school staff are not reported to my Department but instead are reported to the Education Authority (EA).We have asked the EA for a response to this question, and they have provided their response on behalf of EA and CCMS.The EA has indicated that the figures provided in the table are over the past 5 years and outlines the number of teachers and classroom assistants who have recorded a workplace accident on the health and safety database that is determined to be an assault. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of (i) men; and (ii) women that have been the victim of a (a) violent; and (b) sexual assault in a public space, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on victims of sexual assault offences reported to police.  The recording of information on victims of crimes reported to police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to victims of sexual assaults reported to police, as detailed in your question, to the PSNI.  The Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, jointly led by my Department and the Department for Health, reflects that while women are disproportionately affected by both domestic and sexual abuse, anyone can be a victim, and therefore a fully inclusive approach to addressing these crimes, through strengthened prevention, protection, and support measures, has been adopted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 35949/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 35949/22-27 was answered on 6 January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment that has been made of the decline in GCSE and A-level art entries in the last 15 years.
answer_text: CCEA has examined the patterns of entries for both its GCSE and A level Art and Design qualifications between 2008 and 2023. CCEA has identified that over that 15-year period, entries for A level Art and Design declined from 4% of all CCEA A level entries in 2008, to 2.5% in 2023.  There is a similar pattern for CCEA’s GCSE Art and Design with entries declining from 3.1% of all entries in 2008 to 1.9% in 2023. CCEA advises that teachers have indicated that a range of factors may have contributed to this decline including: the perceived difficulty of these qualifications relative to other specifications; the workload and associated time commitment involved in completing the qualifications; parental/peer influence; and timetabling issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment made of (i) the impact of increased musical instrument fees on the wellbeing and educational outcomes of children from lower-income families; and (ii) how this accords with the objectives of the RAISE Programme.
answer_text: (i) The Education Authority (EA) has considered the implications of the approved savings measure for the EA Music Service, which includes increases to musical instrument hire fees and related charges.To mitigate any adverse effects on lower-income families, the EA will retain the current remission rate of 70% on all Music Service charges which is provided to pupils entitled to Free School Meals. A draft equality screening has been completed to assess the impact of the wider changes, and the EA will continue to monitor uptake and income to ensure that increases do not disproportionately disadvantage pupils from lower-income households.(ii) The RAISE Programme objectives are to raise achievement and address educational disadvantage and raise aspirations through a whole community approach to education. The EA recognises that music education contributes significantly to pupil wellbeing, confidence, and attainment, particularly for those from disadvantaged backgrounds.While the Music Service is non-statutory, the EA has sought to balance financial sustainability with accessibility by maintaining the 70% remission policy for FSME pupils and avoiding a blanket approach to fee increases.  The proposed mitigations, including retention of remission, ongoing monitoring, and clear communication, are intended to ensure that the changes do not undermine the availability of access to extra-curricular music activities for vulnerable learners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant of AQW 36317/22-27, and given that almost 45 per cent of all medical special dietary requirements relate to children with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD), yet his Department’s figures rely on a single Education Authority survey with an 18 per cent non-response rate, and which acknowledges that the figures under-represent reality, to detail (i) any assessment he has made of the true scale of ASD-related dietary need; and (ii) any immediate steps he will take to establish a reliable, comprehensive system for recording and tracking these needs across all
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised:(i) no further assessment has been made by the EA Catering Service of Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)-related dietary need, and(ii) the EA Catering Service is currently examining options to digitise this process, which would enable recording and tracking across schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will consider measures to incentivise the conversion of upper floors of commercial premises into residential accommodation, given the potential to alleviate housing pressures and revitalise urban centres.
answer_text: The Housing Supply Strategy, published in December 2024, includes several commitments and actions which will include the consideration of housing led regeneration and the reuse of existing buildings to generate additional supply. However, there are currently no plans to consider measures to incentivise the conversion of upper floors of commercial premises into residential accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rational as to why Education Authority transport contracts were awarded to taxi drivers at an hourly rate above the standard rate, if that rate was not sustainable, especially given that the proposed reductions will adversely affect drivers who relied on the previously agreed terms.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport, which includes the procurement and management of contracts held with private taxi operators. The EA has advised me that taxi contracts are awarded through competitive tender in compliance with procurement legislation and the fare for each journey is set in advance.  School transport does not have standard rates or fixed tariffs because the services offered are customised and differ from regular taxi services, which require the use of taximeters and have legally set maximum fare rates per mile. In determining the fare for a school run, taxi operators need to account for factors such as time to allow children to board safely and be secured in the vehicle, multiple stops, rural routes, non-continuous journeys and significant repositioning “dead miles” where there are no passengers on board. An in-depth analysis of EA taxi contracts revealed considerable disparities in fare rates among operators. Many providers charged rates substantially higher than the maximum taximeter rates, which while not directly applicable to EA contracts, offered a valuable reference point for comparison. The EA has asked all taxi operators to voluntarily agree to modify their contracts and reduce invoiced rates by 10%, seeking their cooperation to manage costs to protect the transport service and ensure that pupils can access transport to school. In addition, the EA will review all taxi runs for the 2026/27 school year for value for money and sustainability. The EA assures me it is taking a balanced and considered approach in reviewing the responses received from taxi operators, however, where operators decline and rates are well above benchmarks, the EA may consider alternatives or terminate and re-tender the contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any new measures his Department is taking to improve treatment and crisis support for individuals with psychosis and schizophrenia, and their families; and (ii) any new medications currently being considered for use.
answer_text: (i) Implementing the new regional crisis service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031. The components of the proposed regional mental health crisis service are: accessing mental health crisis services; primary care and interagency partnership; community crisis service; mental health liaison service; and crisis response home treatment. A draft lifespan pathway has been presented to all stakeholders including those with lived experience and carers. This draft pathway remains under review.A Mental Health Liaison and Crisis Response Home Treatment Care Network has been set up. The primary aim is for consistent evidence-based practice, improving the quality, safety and experience of care received by those in mental health crisis to ensure equitable access to care across the region. Regionally agreed information materials will become available to ensure families providing care or those in need of treatment will know how to access it, including those experiencing psychosis and schizophrenia.The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service is also conducting a pilot with a mental health practitioner based in their control room. Patients and carers will have the opportunity to speak with a fully qualified mental health professional who can assess the clinical needs and determine the appropriate course of action for the individual. A full evaluation of the project is just about to begin; however, the initial results are encouraging.When a person in mental health crisis requires the legal authority of the Mental Health (NI) Order 1986 to be used to ensure they receive the care and treatment appropriate for their needs, an Approved Social Worker will provide advice and support to the family and nearest relative to ensure the appropriate care pathway is achieved.Departmental officials are working collaboratively with PSNI and other key stakeholders across health and social care to plan for the implementation of Right Care Right Person (RCRP) in NI to ensure individuals receive the response and care they require from the most appropriate professional, in the most appropriate setting. (ii) My Department is not aware of any new medications currently being evaluated by NICE under its Technology Appraisal programme for the treatment of psychosis and schizophrenia. However, should NICE publish recommendations for the use of any new medications in the treatment of this condition, the Department will consider the recommendations under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of inspections of vape retail premises carried out, in each council area, in each of the last five years; (ii) any enforcement outcomes of those inspections; (iii) the reasons why this information is not held centrally; (iv) whether he has any plans to require councils to collect and report standardised data on inspections and enforcement; (v) any assessment he has made of enforcement capacity within councils; (vi) any actions he will take in response to the disproportionately high rate of illegal vape sales to children in Belfast; (vii) t
answer_text: Vapes may be sold in various premises.  There is no legal requirement and consequent enforcement framework for premises to be registered or licensed for this purpose.(i and ii) The Nicotine Inhaling Products (Age of Sale and Proxy Purchasing) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2021 provides a minimum age of sale of 18 years for nicotine inhaling products, also known as electronic cigarettes or nicotine vapes. The Regulations came into operation in February 2022.  Councils use ‘test purchasing’ as one of the primary enforcement mechanisms for the Nicotine Inhaling Products Regulations and this data was provided in the previous response (AQW 36125/22-27).(iii) Performance data in respect of the work undertaken by Tobacco Control Officers is provided by local councils to the Public Health Agency (PHA) on a quarterly basis. This data includes inspections and enforcement actions (test purchasing) regarding tobacco and nicotine vapes age of sale compliance, and in respect of smoke-free premises and vehicles.    (iv) My Department is working with the PHA and councils to review and agree requirements for standardised reporting as part of our preparations for the implementation of the provisions in the Tobacco and Vapes Bill.   The Bill, when enacted, will in the long term introduce a new licensing system in Northern Ireland for the retail sale of tobacco, vapes (both nicotine and non-nicotine), and nicotine products. As an interim measure, the Bill will provide a power to extend the existing Tobacco Retail Register to include businesses that sell vapes (both nicotine and non-nicotine) and other nicotine products. It is my intention to exercise this power to extend the Register as soon as possible following the Bill being made, subject to the necessary IT systems being in place and appropriate communication with retailers.(v) My Department meets regularly with Belfast City Council (BCC) to discuss enforcement challenges and receives feedback from the Tobacco Control Officers Group, which is facilitated by BCC. BCC also represents councils as a member on the Tobacco Strategy Policy, Legislation and Enforcement SubGroup which meets quarterly and reports to the overarching Tobacco Strategy Implementation Steering Group, which is chaired by the PHA.  There are currently 12 PHA-commissioned TCOs operating across the 11 councils in Northern Ireland.  At present, the PHA is content that this is sufficient resource to gather relevant information and carry out core duties.  However, this will be kept under review, particularly once the Tobacco and Vapes Bill is made. (vi) The high rate of test purchasing visits in Belfast compared to other councils is largely due to the higher number of retail premises within its city centre. We must also consider the greater local population and numbers of those who travel through the city centre, particularly when commuting to schools.(vii) Data on the number of any repeat offenders identified and sanctioned, in each of the last thr

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 34990/22-27.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any engagement his Department has had with the Housing Executive regarding concerns raised about workplace culture and bullying; and (ii) any actions his Department is taking to ensure that the Housing Executive has appropriate measures in place to protect staff, and address such concerns.
answer_text: These issues are operational matters for the Housing Executive. Through her quarterly Assurance Statement to the Department, the Chief Executive provides confirmation that she is satisfied that the organisation has robust HR policies in place, and these are being complied with.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment made of the impact of (i) the Housing Executive’s absence management policy on staff experiencing workplace bullying; and (ii) whether the current system may discourage employees from reporting bullying due to fear of triggering absence reviews.
answer_text: The Housing Executive’s policies and procedures for its staff in relation to absence management and Dignity at Work, which includes addressing allegations of bullying or harassment, are operational matters for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive as an employer.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:   “The Housing Executive is committed to promoting a good and harmonious working environment where individuals are treated with dignity and respect. Harassment, bullying and victimisation in the workplace are unacceptable and will not be tolerated.  We have a specific Dignity at Work Policy which sets out how concerns about bullying and harassment can be raised, and how these are dealt with. All such concerns are treated seriously and are investigated and addressed in line with policy. We also provide dignity at work training for our staff and dignity at work masterclasses for managers, to ensure that everyone is aware of the policy and the importance of treating everyone with dignity and respect.Employees are encouraged to raise concerns about potential harassment, bullying or victimisation as quickly as possible and our policy provides several options to facilitate this. For example, Dignity at Work Advisors are based throughout the organisation and can provide information to employees on a confidential basis on issues relating to harassment, bullying or victimisation.The Attendance Management Policy is designed to maintain service delivery while supporting employee health and wellbeing. Its primary aim is to ensure that absences are managed fairly, consistently, and in a way that helps staff return to work as soon as they are able. The policy operates alongside our Dignity at Work and other people policies, to provide a holistic approach to employee well-being.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will publish the full selection criteria and scoring framework used to assess applications from teachers for membership of the Curriculum Taskforce working groups, including (i) how applicants are ranked; and (ii) how final selections are made.
answer_text: A series of Subject Working Groups have been established to support the drafting of a reformed curriculum framework in advance of public consultation.  As part of this, expressions of interest were sought from local practitioners to join these groups.  In total 366 expressions of interest were received and 75 individuals selected.  Whilst not a formal recruitment process, applications were assessed and shortlisted for selection against four criteria:Subject expertiseCurriculum design and pedagogical understandingCommunication skills – clarity and effectiveness in written responsesCurriculum VisionFeedback is available to applicants on request.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether his Department has carried out any formal engagement with teaching unions and representative bodies on the recruitment, release and workload implications for classroom teachers being asked to join Curriculum Taskforce working groups.
answer_text: My department informed teaching unions and representative bodies in advance of issuing the expression of interest for teachers to participate in the Curriculum Taskforce Subject Working Groups. This was not a recruitment process; teachers are not directly remunerated, but substitute cover is provided.Participation by classroom teachers is entirely voluntary. Where teachers opted to apply, prior approval was required from their school management.   Involvement in the working groups will not exceed 20 days in total.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any evidence base underpinning his view that proposals for a new knowledge-rich curriculum would improve outcomes for pupils from socioeconomically disadvantaged backgrounds, given that an academic from Queen’s University Belfast, who has observed similar reforms in England and reported that they left many behind, especially lower-attaining pupils and those struggling to meet rigid academic standards.
answer_text: The view expressed has been critiqued by a number of leading academics. The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum found that the current curriculum framework is too high-level, leading to inconsistency in delivery across schools, with teachers often left to make disproportionate decisions about content. Additionally, the current framework lacks coherence, making it difficult for learning to build logically over time, as there is not clear, well-sequenced content for each subject supported by high-quality resources.The aim of introducing a ‘knowledge rich curriculum’ is to ensure the curriculum framework is clearer, more coherent and better connected across key stages.  This approach is grounded in cognitive science that demonstrates that learning depends on building long-term memory and structured schemas because working memory is limited. Sequenced, coherent knowledge enables higher-order thinking and problem-solving. Internationally there is a growing trend towards this approach to curriculum design, often described as ‘knowledge rich’.  Singapore consistently leads global rankings by combining rigorous content with cognitive principles such as mastery and spaced practice, resulting in the highest proportion of top performers and one of the lowest proportions of low achievers. Portugal also offers a clear example of impact, experiencing a dramatic improvement during the 2011–2015 knowledge-rich phase which was then reversed when the country shifted to a competency-based model. England’s 2014 curriculum reform coincided with a sharp rise in PISA rankings, moving from 28th in mathematics and 25th in reading in 2009 to 14th and 13th respectively by 2022. Estonia, which emphasises core knowledge alongside teacher autonomy, ranks first in Europe for mathematics, science, and reading, with strong equity outcomes. Similarly, Canadian provinces such as Quebec, which maintain structured syllabi and prioritize foundational knowledge, outperform most OECD peers, particularly in mathematics and reading.The knowledge-rich curriculum – what is the evidence? (Muijs et al., 2025) reviewed empirical evidence on the impact knowledge rich curricula has on pupil outcomes.  The paper provides examples of where such curricula approaches have supported improved reading comprehension as well as broader attainment.  These benefits are often strongest for pupils at risk of educational disadvantage providing the approaches are implemented early and systematically, accompanied by structured support.  The Curriculum Policy Report (Centre for Education Systems 2025) explains that curriculum specificity is important for equity as this provides clear, detailed curriculum expectations that help ensure consistency and progression. The report explains that low-specificity curricula often lead to confusion, variability between schools, and gaps in foundational knowledge, which disproportionately harm disadvantaged pupils who cannot compensate at home.  The evidence

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any measures his Department is currently considering to ensure that children experiencing Emotional-Based School Non-Attendance have access to appropriate, and consistent, educational provision, including tailored support, alternative learning arrangements, and resources to safeguard their academic progress and well-being.
answer_text: Work is being undertaken in schools under the auspices of the DE – DoH Emotional Health & Wellbeing Framework in Education.  The framework has the stated aims noted below:•	To ensure that children and young people are empowered and assisted to understand and take care or manage their emotional health and wellbeing. •	That their needs are identified early and addressed effectively when required. •	To establish an integrated model that supports an early help, support and intervention focus on children’s emotional health and wellbeing needs. •	That fewer numbers of children and young people will require specialist intervention from Mental Health Services.The associated framework projects are invaluable in prevention and early intervention in respect of the emotional health and wellbeing of children and young people.The Education Authority’s Educational Psychology Service provides training in relation to emotional based school non-attendance.  To date 245 school staff across primary and post primary have received training on Emotional Based School Non-Attendance.  15 staff have also availed of additional Motivational Interviewing (MI) training.  A further 271 EA corporate staff have been trained with 103 attending additional MI training. This training is not mandatory.Support is also available to young people who by virtue of illness are unable to attend school, this is often but not solely facilitated by Exceptional Teaching Arrangements (ETA).  ETA supports those children and young people who by way of referral for medical need (physical and mental health) and with medical consultant oversight, may be suitable for ETA support. Referrals for ETA are made by schools and are subject to criteria as noted in the link below:https://send.eani.org.uk/support-services/exceptional-teaching/information-schools/criteria-and-timeline

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) any guidance Land and Property Services applies in cases where a domestic property becomes uninhabitable due to storm damage, including whether such properties remain liable for full domestic rates; and (ii) whether ratepayers in these circumstances are eligible for any (a) rebate; (b) exclusion; or (c) revaluation.
answer_text: Domestic rates apply to all domestic properties with a Capital Value of £20,000 or more, including those that are vacant. The rating liability for a vacant property remains the same as if the property were occupied.If there is a change in circumstances that has caused major structural issues rendering a property uninhabitable due to storm damage, this may impact the property’s Capital Value and, in turn, its rates liability. The ratepayer should inform Land & Property Services (LPS) as soon as possible and retain evidence of the damage. Each case will be considered on its own facts. Guidance on the Valuation of domestic properties for rates can be found here:https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/valuation-domestic-properties-rates In addition to the question of the Capital Value, there are a number of Rating of Empty Homes exclusions. If an application for an exclusion is successful, the property will attract zero rates liability for the relevant period. An exclusion must be applied for, and supporting evidence may be required.One such exclusion relates to properties that are vacant due to flooding. A ratepayer will qualify for this exclusion if:The property qualified for compensation from the local council;No one has lived continuously in the property for at least four weeks; andThe property was the ratepayer’s main residence before the flooding occurred.This exclusion from rates liability lasts for up to six months. A property may be eligible even if the flooding has not reduced its rateable value.Alternatively, a domestic property can be lawfully prohibited from occupation when a competent authority issues a formal notice or order under housing or building legislation. Typical examples include hazard or prohibition notices addressing health and safety risks, building regulation contravention notices, demolition orders for dangerous structures, and closure notices for derelict or unsafe premises. These notices, generally served by district councils, make the property incapable of lawful occupation until required remedial action is completed. Where storm damage results in such a notice being issued, the ratepayer may be eligible to apply for a Rating of Empty Homes exclusion.Guidance on the Rating of Empty Homes, including details of exclusions and the application process, can be found on the NI Direct website:https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/rating-empty-homes

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given the ongoing issues with roof safety at Nazareth House Primary School, to detail any action his Department is taking (i) to protect pupils; and (ii) to address the maintenance and repair backlog across the school estate.
answer_text: The temporary closure of Nazareth House Primary School was necessary to ensure the health and safety of the pupils, staff and parents. Years of underinvestment in our ageing school estate have left us facing difficult decisions and, in this case, the only option was to temporarily close the school.The Education Authority (EA) has commenced planning for urgent remedial works to make the roof of the main school building safe, with the aim of allowing the whole Nazareth House school community to return to learning on their own site by half-term break in February 2026.Following intensive work with the school leadership, the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS) and the EA, it has been agreed that from 7 January, pupils and staff of Nazareth House Primary School, including the nursery, will share a site with St Anne’s Primary School. In parallel with these temporary contingency arrangements and the remedial works, my Department has commenced planning for the more extensive roofing works, which will be prioritised for delivery.The experience of the Nazareth House school community brings into sharp focus the critical and long-standing issues of school infrastructure and funding. I remain committed to improving the condition of our school estate and have called for urgent, cross-party support to secure the investment needed to ensure that every child in Northern Ireland is educated in a safe, modern and inclusive environment. The current situation cannot continue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration her Department has given to introducing a public awareness campaign to improve passenger understanding of how to correctly use the ticket scanners when boarding and alighting from trains, in light of ongoing difficulties experienced by rail users.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink is not aware of any major difficulties experienced by customers using its new Gatelines.  In fact, last week over 161,000 customers passed through its Gatelines at the 5 key stations below and at Christmas last year (maximum peak period) over 120,000 customers in one week passed through the Gateline at Belfast Grand Central Station alone. To support the introduction of electronic Gatelines at 5 main NI Railways Stations (Belfast Grand Central, North West Transport Hub, Portadown, York Street and Lanyon Place) Translink have provided the following Marketing materials to inform and educate customers:Video assets to clearly demo and explain to customers how to use the ticket gates (long & short versions). Posted across Translink's social media channels and on YouTube. Static social assets to inform customers where the gates are located, advising to have tickets ready for quick passage through the gate, and that staff assistance is available if needed. Station Posters were produced and displayed at the stations with Gatelines.  The posters were posted when gates were first introduced, the video / social assets are long running going out frequently on Facebook, Instagram and Twitter.  Gateline staff are present in each location to assist and support customers on an ongoing basis and this has been the case since the gates went live.Teams of additional Translink staff were present at the Gateline when Belfast Grand Central Station opened in October 2024. Dedicated staff were also deployed at the Gateline at NWTH in July 2025 in the lead up to the 153rd Open to familiarise passengers with the gates and the ticket types which could be used.  Translink will continue to push out these informative and helpful assets to assist our customers using the Gatelines.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any consideration his Department has given to supporting the development of modular, off-site constructed homes paired with small-scale anaerobic digestion systems, in order to deliver fast, affordable, energy-self-sufficient housing solutions; and (ii) any plans and timelines he has to explore this model for rural areas, eco-villages, or community-led regeneration projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will review its approach to allocating emergency and temporary accommodation for vulnerable adults, particularly those with serious mental-health conditions, to ensure they are prioritised for placements as close as possible to their home area, recognising the detrimental impact that being housed far from family and support networks can have on their health and wellbeing.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“When providing temporary accommodation placements, the Housing Executive will always consider the individual circumstances of the applicant including any relevant support needs or vulnerabilities. This includes proximity to facilities including hospitals, day centres or other specialised services on which applicants may be reliant, and proximity to family and support networks.It should, however, be noted that while the Housing Executive will make every effort to identify temporary accommodation that aligns to the needs of the household, there may be occasions due to the challenges in supply and demand for temporary accommodation where some aspects of suitability may not be entirely satisfactory to applicants.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether his Department will examine the potential introduction of a four-day flexible teaching week, as recently proposed in Scotland, in light of evidence that such an approach may support teacher retention, reduce burnout, and enhance opportunities for professional learning.
answer_text: There is no proposal to consider the introduction of a four-day flexible teaching week in Northern Ireland. As part of the teachers’ pay settlement for 2024/25, (TNC 2025/1) I announced the establishment of an independent panel to undertake a review of teachers’ and school leaders’ workload. The independent panel recently submitted their report to me making recommendations on how to resolve the issue of workload faced by teachers and school leaders and I will be considering these recommendations.In addition to the work of the independent panel the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) is progressing 26 agreed measures detailed in the TNC 2025/1 Heads of Agreement to address workload issues for both teachers and school leaders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will review the level of funding allocated to HIV awareness and prevention programmes, given the recent increase in HIV diagnoses.
answer_text: Early HIV surveillance figures for 2024 indicate that there were 101 new diagnoses, the same number as reported in 2023. Finalised statistics for 2024 are expected to be available in early 2026.While the overall number of diagnoses remains stable, I recognise that there is more to do. The Sexual Health Action Plan, launched by my department in 2023, includes key actions for raising awareness of sexual health and HIV. I recently spoke at the 30th Regional Annual Sexual Health Conference to reiterate my commitment to tackling HIV, through testing, awareness and preventative measures including PrEP and Cabotegravir injections. I will continue to monitor the data closely and will be guided by the evidence as to how best to proceed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of individuals who have been handcuffed while being detained or transported under the Mental Health (Northern Ireland) Order 1986, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any immediate actions he will take to address the generational homelessness crisis highlighted by the Simon Community; and (ii) any steps he will take to ensure transparent delivery and coordination across the eight homelessness strategies, referenced at the Public Accounts Committee on 27 November 2025.
answer_text: Increased housing supply is fundamental to addressing the challenge of generational homelessness. The Executive’s Housing Supply Strategy sets out the long-term framework for delivering the supply we need across Northern Ireland. My Department is currently leading on work, in collaboration with other Departments and the NIHE, to produce the first action plan detailing a range of actions to be delivered in implementing the Strategy. The NIHE has statutory responsibility for responding to homelessness, including the delivery of the Homelessness Strategy. It publishes an annual progress report on the delivery of all actions within the Homelessness Strategy and associated action plans. The most recent Report can be found at https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/79626162-cec6-479b-8ba7-d22dcffea566/Year-3-Annual-Progress-Report-2024-25.pdf.Similarly, the Department publishes an annual report on the delivery of the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan. The most recent report can be found at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/communities/dfc-inter-departmental-homelessness-action-plan-year4-report.pdf.   		My Department has already accepted the recommendations from the Northern Ireland Audit Office’s Report on Homelessness and will consider how coordination can be improved as part of the review of oversight groups.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Strategic Framework to End Violence against Women and Girls.
answer_text: As an Executive, we are determined to do all that we can to end violence against women and girls.We have said from the outset that this was an issue we are committed to tackling, and we have made significant progress since we launched the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework and Delivery Plan in September last year.We are driving meaningful change across communities and organisations, with visible impact in schools, sports clubs, and the community and voluntary sectors.We are getting money directly to organisations with the experience to make a difference. Through the Change Fund, over £3.2 million has been invested in community-based action to support prevention and cultural change.We have also invested an additional £2.2 million to build capacity in the community and voluntary sector, amplify key messages, and scale delivery.A few weeks ago, Junior Minister Reilly and the deputy First Minister attended the launch of the Fair Game Initiative which we are supporting through the Change Fund. This innovative programme, which is a collaboration with the Irish Football Association, Women’s Aid and the PSNI, uses sport to challenge harmful attitudes and create safe environments for young people both on and off the pitch. It was such a positive event and highlighted what can be done when we all work together to address these issues.The 16 days of activism against gender-based violence has also been an opportunity to reinforce our messaging and to engage with our partners. Last week, we issued an Executive statement reaffirming our determination to tackle all forms of gender-based violence, abuse, and harm.We have also supported events both here in Parliament Buildings and on our social media platforms including an event with Women’s Aid and the Mothers’ Union, to amplify our collective approach to dealing with this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment been made of the sustainability of ADHD assessment provision in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, given that the number of children and young people on the waiting list has increased 650 per cent in less than four years; (ii) any assessment of the reasons for this increase; (iii) any actions being taken to stabilise and reduce the waiting list; and (iii) when his Department expects waiting times to reduce.
answer_text: There is currently no specifically commissioned service for ADHD in Northern Ireland. While there is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system which has evolved in response to population demand and expertise within Health & Social Care teams, it is inconsistent and there are lengthy waiting lists across Trusts. The Northern Health and Social are Trust is not an outlier in this regard. There has been a significant increase in the number of children referred for an ADHD assessment across the region and regrettably, ADHD services do not currently have the capacity to meet this need. Funding in respect of ADHD has failed to keep pace with this emerging area of clinical need. I am committed to addressing this through investment, subject to funding being available.My Department published the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework for public consultation. The Framework endorses an inclusive, neurodiverse, needs-based approach. It is designed to help reduce stigma and the perceived need to obtain a diagnosis in order to access support through health and education services. Response to the consultation has been very positive and officials are currently considering the feedback which has been provided. Department of Health officials meet monthly with HSC Trusts Heads of ADHD Services. These meetings provide a forum where professionals can participate in regional discussion to explore demand-capacity challenges and consider opportunities for standardised pathways. Services are observed to be adapting to increasing complexity in referrals characterised by more children presenting with comorbid ASD and ADHD and requiring more intensive assessment accordingly. Plans are progressing with PHA and Child Health colleagues to develop regionally agreed neurodevelopmental pathways which will allow earlier assessment of need and opportunities to intervene accordingly. Health and Social Care Trusts are also reviewing and refining assessment pathways to reduce clinical time per case while maintaining quality. The assessments that are carried out are aligned with NICE guidelines to ensure consistency and clinical robustness. My Department has commissioned a regional needs assessment on ADHD demand across all Trusts including the Northern Trust. This initiative represents an important point of departure in establishing the basis upon which a future commissioning model in support of ADHD services across Trusts might be based. In approaching this exercise, the primary objectives are to gauge the overall demand for an ADHD service based upon a model of provision across the lifespan, and to provide detailed recommendations to inform discussion. It is envisaged that these recommendations will offer a view upon the proposed structure of a future clinical model, the necessary steps that might be required to ensure regional consistency and equity of access, an exploration of operational feasibility and proposals associated

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Personal Independence Payment applicants who, after completing an assessment interview, were subsequently asked to attend a further interview without being provided with a reason for the additional assessment, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This information is not held.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of children recorded as having special dietary requirements; (ii) the percentage of children with an Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) diagnosis, recorded as having special dietary requirements; (iii) any protocols currently in place to meet the dietary needs of children with an ASD diagnosis; (iv) any data his Department holds on the number of children diagnosed with Avoidant Restrictive Food Intake Disorder; and (v) the number of complaints his Department has received relating to schoolchildren not being provided with food that adequatel
answer_text: (i) Education Authority (EA) Catering conducted a survey of Catering Managers in April 2025 (18% of Catering Managers did not participate in the survey (844 unique responses received out of 1034)), but in the absence of a digital application process it is the best information available. Not all customers requiring special diets follow the special diet application process, so the survey results are an under-representation of reality. The survey gives an indication of the number of children with special dietary requirements.This survey informed EA 13,308 meals per day are provided for a special dietary requirement. This equates to 9.5% of the daily volume of meals served. Of these 13,308 special diets it is reported: •	3,604 are for medical diets;•	6,266 are for allergen diets; and •	3,438 are for religious/cultural/lifestyle diets.(ii) EA Catering advised 1,611 customers are reported to have Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD). This equates to 1.2% of total daily customers in school meals, 12.1% of special diet customers, or 44.7% of customers with medical special dietary requirements. Again, these figures are almost certainly an under-representation of reality. The 1,611 ASD customers may have other special dietary needs in addition to ASD including, an allergy or multiple allergies.(iii) The protocol for the provision of special diets for all children is set out in EA Guidance For Provision of Special Diets In Schools 0.4[96].docx. Food sensitivities and avoidant eating linked to neurodivergence are highly individual, therefore support is provided on a case-by-case basis. (iv) My department does not record the number of children having special dietary requirements or a diagnoses of Avoidant Restrictive Food Intake Disorder. Local Impact Teams offer tailored guidance to parents, caregivers, and schools through Autism Specialists, accessed via the Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) Central advice line or a Request for Involvement. This advice helps staff respond to sensory differences around food and may include signposting to occupational therapy where appropriate. EA do not have information relating to the prevalence of Avoidant Restrictive Food Intake Disorder.(v) There have been no formal complaints made to my department in relation to the provision of food that does not adequately meet the needs of children.From 2 December 2019 to 2 December 2025 the EA have received a total of 80 complaints from members of the public, they were processed in line with the Education Authority’s Complaints Handling Policy and Procedure. It is important to note that although a child may have a special diet such as coeliacs, it does not necessarily mean they will have a statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment his Department has made of the disparity in financial support between Northern Ireland medical students funded through the his Department's bursary and English medical students funded through the NHS bursary, including (a) the difference between the non–means tested elements of each bursary; and (b) the absence of equivalent additional allowances for Northern Ireland students undertaking mandatory placements abroad, such as accommodation costs for clinical placements; and (iii) any steps his Department will take to ensure that Northern
answer_text: Health and Student Finance are devolved matters, and the support arrangements vary markedly across the UK, depending upon where the individual is ordinarily resident. Policy reflects the resources available to each administration and their assessment of workforce supply priorities. From year 5 onwards my Department offers a bursary package for eligible medical students, this includes a non-payable tuition fee, and a means tested bursary. This is in addition to the standard student finance available in years 1- 4.I am aware of the concerns raised relating to the funding disparity between Northern Ireland-domiciled medical students and their peers from other parts of the UK. Work is underway to develop a public consultation on potential reforms to student support arrangements for those undertaking health-related pre-registration degree courses. Through the forthcoming consultation process, my Department will aim to gather views on how best to structure support to meet workforce needs while ensuring fairness. While any changes could have implications for the support provided to medical students, the precise scope and nature of potential reforms have not yet been determined. As the consultation scope is developed, my officials will consider whether additional measures such as support for compulsory overseas placements should be explored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether, before concluding that significant capital expenditure would be required for universal Free School Meals, his Department assessed options such as staggered lunch sittings, which allow full cohorts to be served without additional dining hall construction, as is common in other jurisdictions.
answer_text: The Education Authority has confirmed that staggered school lunch services are already in operation in the vast majority of its schools. This maximises the number of children and young people able to access their lunch in the school dining area in the time allocated within each school’s timetable.  When considering the overall capital spend required for the delivery of universal free school meals, the capacity of both school kitchens and dining halls would need to be significantly increased to deliver a large increase in meal numbers. This additional capacity would be necessary to enable the appropriate storage of foodstuffs, the preparation and serving of the meals and the provision of suitable facilities for pupils to consume their meals. A substantial amount of capital investment would be necessary to take this forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether centralised guidance will be issued to ensure all public sector managers apply (i) carer's leave; and (ii) flexible working, policies consistently across Departments and arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: Civil servants may apply for time off for caring responsibilities in accordance with the provisions of the Civil Service Special Leave policy, which applies to all Departments. Requests for paid time off for care of dependants are considered by line managers on a case-by-case basis, with additional time off available to those with particularly demanding caring responsibilities. Managers will also consider applications made under the statutory right for unpaid time off for dependants.Further guidance for line managers on how to support civil servants with caring responsibilities is available in the Civil Service Guide to Supporting Carers. Applications for flexible working made under the Civil Service Alternative Working Patterns policy, including applications made under the Employment (Northern Ireland) Order 2002, are also considered on a case-by-case basis and in line with business needs. Arm’s-length bodies of Civil Service departments are not bound by civil service policies or guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, under the provisions of the Health (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 which prohibits the sale of nicotine inhaling products to persons under 18, to detail (i) the number of inspections of vape retail premises carried out by Environmental Health Officers in local councils to enforce this ban; and (ii) the outcomes of these inspections, including (a) the number of breaches identified; and (b) the enforcement actions taken, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The enforcement of age of sale restrictions for tobacco and nicotine inhaling products in Northern Ireland is led by local district councils. Tobacco Control Officers conduct test purchases and support retailer compliance in the prohibition of the sale of nicotine inhaling products to persons under the age of 18. Inspections and enforcement actions have been recorded by each council area since the Nicotine Inhaling Products (Age of Sale and Proxy Purchasing) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2021 came into effect in February 2022. This information is not held by constituency.The Public Health Agency does not receive a breakdown of the number of Council Inspections. The outcome of inspections, such as the number of breaches identified and enforcement actions taken in respect of premises that sell Nicotine Inhaling Products are incorporated within the Councils’ Tobacco control responsibilities and are reported as an overall figure.However, data from the last three years relating to 'Test Purchasing' exercises conducted by Councils in respect of premises that specifically sell Nicotine Inhaling Products, and the subsequent enforcement actions of Councils, is available to the PHA at Council level and is provided below:Test Purchasing - question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether the Education Authority provides mandatory training for managers regarding reasonable adjustments for employees with caring responsibilities for disabled dependants.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) provides mandatory “Introduction to Equality,” training for all EA Staff. which covers content on Equality of Opportunity, Dependants, Disability, and Reasonable Adjustments. This training references associative discrimination and consideration of reasonable adjustments for employees with caring responsibilities for disabled dependants. EA also provides a range of training that is not mandatory, but audiences are strongly encouraged to attend. For example, “Understanding Diversity and Inclusion” training is available to new Principals and Boards of Governors and includes content on Equality of Opportunity, Dependants, Disability, and Reasonable Adjustments.  In addition, “Disability Awareness in the Workplace” training is available to both EA staff and Governors.  This course includes content surrounding the concept of associative discrimination, including examples of employees with primary caring responsibilities for disabled dependants.
