question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given that the Air Quality Standards Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2010 and the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 place a statutory duty on his Department to identify, assess and reduce all significant sources of harmful pollutants, including PM2.5 and PM10, to detail (I) any assessment his Department has undertaken of emissions from bonfires; (ii) any measures implemented to mitigate their impact on air quality and public health; and (iii) how his Department coordinates with local councils and the Public Health Agenc
answer_text: As ‘Competent Authority’ under the Air Quality Standards Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2010 the Department has an obligation to assess ambient air quality.  DAERA meets this statutory duty through routine monitoring of air quality at 23 sites across Northern Ireland and liaises closely with the district councils via the Local Air Quality Management regime. Further information on monitoring is available in the annual air pollution report air-pollution-in-northern-ireland-2024-issue1-print-optimised-6915a4eaa1e5b061262632.pdf.  Detailed assessments of bonfire emissions are not a requirement of either the Air Quality Standards Regulations (NI) 2010 or the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department will monitor compliance with its equality obligations, under Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, in relation to transgender and non-binary pupils’ access to physical education and school sport, when his Department does not collect any participation data.
answer_text: Physical Education is a part of the Northern Ireland Curriculum and applies to all pupils of compulsory school age. The delivery of the Curriculum is the responsibility of each school's Board of Governors, and accordingly, it is a matter for them to ensure that a suitable curricular programme is delivered which is accessible to all registered pupils and that the statutory minimum content is covered.Day-to-day management of how the Curriculum programme is delivered, including how it is monitored, is a matter for individual schools and they should work together with pupils and parents/carers to agree on support required to facilitate delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider reinstating late-night rail services, departing from Portrush, given that the lack of services is having an adverse impact on local businesses.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:The last rail service departing Portrush is currently 23:02 hours (Monday to Saturday, and 22:03 on a Sunday).  These departure times have not altered in recent years.  Outside of the summer months, the current last trains carry low passenger numbers (averaging fewer than 7 persons per train).  Translink keep all its passenger loadings under review but it is required to operate efficiently and within its public service obligations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the UK Supreme Court judgement in the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant), to detail (i) any steps his Department will take to (a) bring the Religious Education curriculum and associated guidance into compliance with human-rights standards; and (b) ensure pupils receive balanced teaching on a range of faiths and beliefs; and (ii) whether his Department intends to review the statutory provisions on collective worship in schools.
answer_text: The Supreme Court’s decision in the matter of JR87 has significant implications for our schools. I will consider the judgment in full before determining any essential remedies that both respect the law and safeguard the role of religion in education. This is a complex ruling and I have requested further legal advice to understand the full implications of the judgment.However, it is vital to underline that the Court did not strike down the existing legislation.  Both Religious Education and collective worship can continue in schools.  Indeed, there is a legal obligation that they must continue. Article 21(2) Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 makes this clear: controlled schools must provide undenominational Religious Education based on the Holy Scriptures and collective worship that is not distinctive of any denomination. These statutory duties remain unchanged. My department is already reviewing the overall school curriculum for the first time in almost 20 years; it is therefore timely to reform the RE syllabus. Under the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 2006, the Department of Education is responsible for the RE Core Syllabus.  The order states that the syllabus should be prepared by “a group of persons appearing to the Department to be people who have an interest in the teaching of religious education in grant-aided schools.”  I will set out the process for reforming the RE syllabus in due course.  The area of collective worship is particularly complex and I am awaiting further legal advice. While the right to withdraw already exists, the Court found that current arrangements in a particular school did not meet legal requirements.  I will explore options to ensure the right to withdrawal is effective, standardised and legally compliant.  I have written to schools and advised that updated guidance will be provided in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action his Department plans to take on the University of Stirling’s August 2024 report Independent Review of the Liquor Licensing System in Northern Ireland, including the Surrender Principle which recommends the replacement of the current alcohol licensing system.
answer_text: My Department’s response to the Report of the Independent Review of the Liquor Licensing System in Northern Ireland was published on 18 November.A copy of the response and a corresponding Executive Summary can be found at: www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/department-communities-response-report-independent-review-liquor-licensing-system-northern-ireland-including-surrender-principle

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has to ensure that schools systematically identify and record pupils with rare diseases, given current gaps in data and the evidence that improved awareness and whole-school understanding, significantly enhances outcomes for these children.
answer_text: All schools have a Medical Register within the School Information Management System. Pupils with a medical diagnosis/es should be recorded on the Register as per guidance in DE Circular 2019/03. This includes the ability to capture rare diseases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether fee-paying or voluntary-grammar schools are permitted to use the payment or non-payment of (a) school fees; (b) voluntary contributions; or (c) consolidated charges, as criteria for determining pupil eligibility for over-subscribed school trips; and (ii) the protections in place, to ensure that pupils are not disadvantaged or excluded from trips or activities, on the basis of their family’s ability to pay voluntary contributions.
answer_text: For clarity, this answer only relates to grant-aided schools funded via the Common Funding Scheme.  That is, the term “fee-paying” could be interpreted to mean independent schools in Northern Ireland which are fully funded by parental contributions.  In preparing this answer, my officials have assumed that such schools are not within the scope of this question.  (i) (a) School Fees:  Under legislation any sum payable by parents or guardians in respect of statutory fees and which are not covered by remission arrangements may be recovered as a civil debt.  The conditions under which a school imposes or remits a charge must be clearly outlined in the school's charges and remissions policy.(i) (b) Voluntary Contributions:  Under legislation Boards of Governors are permitted to seek voluntary contributions from parents / guardians.  Any request must make it clear that:  there is no obligation to contribute; and pupils at the school will not be treated differently according to whether or not their parents / guardians have made a contribution. (i) (c) Consolidated Charges:  The term consolidated charge is not used or defined in legislation.  Similarly, it is not a term which my Department or the Education Authority uses.  As set out in Chapter 18 of my Department’s Governor Guide, schools should be mindful of the sprit as well as the letter of the law and should ensure that the voluntary nature of contributions is highlighted in all correspondence that requests them.  Indeed, the design of stationery used for billing or invoicing should clearly distinguish between any compulsory charges being levied and any voluntary component.   (ii) Legislation protects pupils from being disadvantaged if parents do not make a voluntary contribution.On 2 September 2025 my Department issued Circular 2025-10 Charges in grant aided schools to schools. This replaced circular 1991/21 and is consistent with the relevant legislation.  The circular draws attention to Chapter 18 of my Department’s Governor Guide which sets out schools’ legal responsibilities with regards school fees, charges, remissions and voluntary contributions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any protections available to consumers from the Republic of Ireland, purchasing vehicles in Northern Ireland, that subsequently fail National Car Testing due to defects that should have been identified at MOT; and (ii) whether her Department plans to enhance cross-jurisdictional cooperation on such cases.
answer_text: My Department has no remit in recognising or agreeing standards for vehicle testing in the South. Protections to consumers are provided by The Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024 (DMCCA) which prohibits traders from engaging in unfair commercial practices by providing false or misleading information to consumers or failing to disclose material information regarding the goods or services they offer. This includes, for used car sales, information relating to the history, condition or any defects of the vehicle. The DMCCA is enforced by my Department’s Trading Standards Service (TSS). Under the Consumer Rights Act 2015, vehicles purchased from a trader must meet standards of satisfactory quality, accurate description and fitness for purpose. Vehicles must not be sold in an unroadworthy condition and must comply with Road Traffic legislation, enforced by the Police and the Driver and Vehicle Agency.The National Car Test (NCT) is a compulsory vehicle inspection programme in the south of Ireland and its results may be used by a consumer as supporting evidence of defects with a vehicle. The European Consumer Centre (ECC) Ireland is part of the EU-wide European Consumer Centre Network, providing free and independent advice to consumers experiencing issues with traders based in other European countries. ECC Ireland assists residents of the south of Ireland with disputes involving traders across all EU Member States.Similarly, the UK International Consumer Centre (UKICC) provides free legal and practical guidance to UK-based consumers with disputes involving businesses located outside the UK. Both organisations frequently liaise on cross-border cases, sharing information to support consumers.Although neither the ECCs nor the UKICC have enforcement powers, they play a crucial role in intelligence gathering, regularly sharing information with enforcement bodies, including the TSS. Intelligence provided by ECC Ireland and the UKICC has supported civil intervention activities, investigations and enforcement action by the TSS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 32502/22-27.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether her Department has considered establishing formal engagement with the Road Safety Authority in the Republic of Ireland, to explore greater alignment and cooperation between the MOT and National Car Testing regimes, with a view to improving cross-border consistency, consumer confidence, and overall road-safety outcomes; and (ii) any plans to develop a shared framework or working arrangement in this area.
answer_text: A Consultation on the future of MOT testing here launched earlier this year and closed on 16 April 2025. The consultation primarily sought views on potentially changing the frequency of MOT testing for private cars, motorcycles and light goods vehicles after their initial test at 4 years and 3 years respectively. The consultation presented a number of options for consideration. There was a focus on road safety, economic and environmental impact, increased insurance premiums and the impact on our local automotive industry. All of these issues will be factored into future deliberations on potential changes to the vehicle testing regime. My officials are currently preparing a detailed paper for my consideration on the future of MOT testing here.   Through the North-South Ministerial Council I meet with my counterpart in the South to discuss key road safety issues.  My officials already meet regularly to maintain strong links with neighbouring jurisdictions, for continual learning and sharing of best practice and developments in road safety. Once I have considered the outcome of the consultation there may be opportunity to include MOT testing at future council meetings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the amount of (a) funding; and (b) resources available to assist victims of romance scams; (ii) any (a) services; or (b) support mechanisms that exist for victims and for family members seeking to help someone escape such a scam; and (iii) any (a) awareness campaigns; or (b) preventative initiatives her Department is currently running or planning, to raise public understanding of romance scams and how to respond to them.
answer_text: My Department provides around £2 million in core grant funding to Victim Support NI to provide a range of support services to all victims and witnesses of crime, including victims of romance scams. These services include the provision of emotional support, advocacy, information, and advice to victims; assistance with claiming Criminal Injuries Compensation; and court support to adult prosecution witnesses called to give evidence at court. Together, these services help those affected by crime, including those affected by romance scams, to deal with the impact, claim compensation and give their best evidence at court.Romance scams are particularly cruel, often preying on someone’s loneliness, vulnerability, fear, or kindness to gain access to their personal information and ultimately their money.  My Department helps to address harm and vulnerability caused by fraud and supports partnership working to tackle the threat of scams through its membership of the ScamwiseNI Partnership, which is chaired by the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI).  The Partnership was established in 2016 and now has over 45 partners who work collaboratively to provide valuable information and crucial advice to keep the public informed, aware and vigilant. The ScamwiseNI Partnership has an effective online presence Scamwiseni | nidirect and my Department also uses its own social media channels (X, Bluesky, YouTube & Instagram) to highlight current scams and the steps people can take to protect themselves. On 29 September 2025 the PSNI launched a Romance Fraud social media campaign sharing advice on how to spot and stop these scams. A ScamwiseNI conference, led and facilitated by the PSNI, on scam and fraud prevention was held on 6 November 2025 where Dr Elisabeth Carter, a criminologist and forensic linguist, was the key speaker. She educated partners about how romance scams work, explained the psychological manipulation involved and provided insight as to why victims stay engaged with the scammer. Also at the conference, Ofcom introduced partners to their recently launched ‘Serious Game’, an online interactive simulator to help users identify romance scam red flags. More information can be found atHarnessing the power of serious games to protect people against romance fraud.In addition, my Department in partnership with the Northern Ireland Policing Board, provides annual operational funding of £4.25m to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), across each of the 11 council areas, who lead on my Department’s response to community safety issues at a local level. Many PCSPs have a dedicated Community Safety Warden Scheme, which provides sessions on crime prevention and raises awareness of a wide range of scams and fraud, including romance scams.An example of this is the Derry City & Strabane District 2025/26 PCSP action plan, where £500 has been allocated towards cybercrime including romance scams and child sexual exploitation.  This year, the PCSP

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (ii) the number of quality assurance checks, carried out on individual MOT testers, in each Driver and Vehicle Agency centre, over the last three years; and (ii) the number of cases resulted in (a) retraining; (b) disciplinary action; and (c) internal review.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) carries out MOT inspections to provide an impartial and independent assessment of a vehicle’s roadworthiness. All DVA vehicle examiners are fully qualified mechanics who have completed a full apprenticeship, obtained a Level 3 qualification, and gained at least one year of post-apprenticeship experience in the motor vehicle industry before joining the Agency. Upon employment, they receive intensive training in vehicle testing standards and regularly undergo quality assurance checks to ensure these standards are consistently applied during the testing process.During a quality assurance check, an examiner is observed conducting a live MOT test. Each examiner undergoes four quality assurance checks annually across the DVA’s network of test centres: two conducted by their immediate line management and two independent checks carried out by the DVA’s Quality Unit.Staff movements such as transfers, resignations and promotions, and periods of unavailability due to for example, long-term sick leave can affect the number of quality assurance checks completed for each examiner.If any non-conformance with testing processes is identified during a quality assurance check, the test centre management team addresses the issue through informal training to reinforce correct procedures. A record of the intervention is also noted on the examiner’s file.The table below sets out the total number of quality assurance checks conducted over the past three financial years by test centre, the number of staff at each location, and the total non-conformances identified in the testing process.CentreFinancial year2022-20232023-20242024-2025Number of checksNumber of staffNumber of checksNumber of staffNumber of checksNumber of  Checks161616981636Total non -conformances6312676Multiple non-conformances can be identified during a check. The figures in the table represent the total number of non-conformances recorded in each financial year.These non-conformances generally relate to failures to follow the correct operating procedures set out in the tester’s manual and/or health and safety risk assessments. They are considered minor and are addressed at a local level.No vehicle examiners have been subject to disciplinary action or internal review as a result of these quality assurance checks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of online child sexual abuse offences that have been recorded by the PSNI, in each of the last three years; (ii) the number of these cases which are currently under investigation; (iii) the data the PSNI hold on those cases, broken down by (a) Trust or locality; and (b) victim and offender profiles; and (iv) within that data, the number of offenders who were (a) adults; and (b) minors and child-on-child cases.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on the instances of online child sexual abuse offences reported to police, or for the number of cases currently under investigation, as part of its datasets.  The recording of information on offences reported to police, including on any subsequent investigation and on any information relating to victims or potential offenders associated with those cases, is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to instances of instances of online child sexual abuse reported to police, as detailed in your question, to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the recent Police Ombudsman report into the handling of the Alexander McCartney case, to detail (i) any actions her Department is supporting or requiring the PSNI to take, to address the systemic failings identified; (ii) whether her Department has (a) received; or (b) commissioned an action plan from the PSNI for implementing the Ombudsman’s recommendations, including on resourcing, capacity, and risk management; and (iii) how her Department will measure the effectiveness of these reforms through (a) performance indicators; (b) reporting; or (c) ove
answer_text: I am fully aware of the concerns raised in relation to the PSNI’s handling of this matter.  The Policing Board has already indicated that it will discuss the report and its findings with the Chief Constable and with the Director of the National Crime Agency at the December Board meeting.  Given the Board’s statutory duty to hold the Chief Constable to account, that is the appropriate forum for detailed scrutiny.My Department will, of course, provide support where appropriate. In terms of the capacity and resourcing challenges that exist within the PSNI today, the Member will be aware that I have worked closely with the Finance Minister and Chief Constable to progress the PSNI’s Recovery Plan. I am confident that funding for Year 1 of the Plan will be made available soon via the December Monitoring Round, and also welcome the Finance Minister’s recommendation that the subsequent years of the Plan are met in full in the Executive’s pending multi-year budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will give consideration to working with his colleagues in Dublin to explore the creation of an all-Ireland health system, given the Chairman of the British Medical Association NI stated that such an arrangement may lead to better health outcomes for service users in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has no plans to consider an all-Ireland heath system. I have been clear that I, and my Department, remain fully committed to the principle that health should be free at the point of service.  This includes the service provided by GPs. We have no plans to introduce a hybrid system in primary care, which would mean some patients having to pay to see their GP,

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider collecting data on the participation of (i) non-binary; and (ii) transgender, pupils in school sport and physical education.
answer_text: I have no plans to collect this data.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the amount of funding allocated for specialist recovery services, such as therapeutic and counselling, for victims of online child sexual abuse; (ii) which Health and Social Care Trusts or localities, currently host such recovery services; and (iii) the number of children referred to social services or otherwise identified as victims of online child sexual abuse, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The provision of specialist recovery and therapeutic services for victims of online sexual abuse takes place in a variety of settings across the Region, however Online Child Sexual Abuse is not a standalone category recorded within referral information. Funding and resources are allocated directly by My Department to Health and Social Care Trusts, and a proportion of children and young people who have been exposed to online sexual abuse will receive support directly from the Health and Social Care Trust where they reside.The Health and Social Care Trusts also refer children and young people to external services from independent providers. These providers engage with children and young people across a broad continuum of presenting need including those children and young people who have encountered sexual harm in an on-line setting. In formulating this response, my officials have asked the Health and Social Care Trusts to assign a quantum to the allocation of funding and internal expenditure in this specific domain, however it is not possible to disaggregate service delivery between those children and young people who have experienced sexual abuse and those who have specifically experienced online sexual abuse. All children, including children and young people who have experienced online child sexual abuse who are referred to Social Services for assessment and/or intervention are assessed in line with the Understanding the Needs of Children in Northern Ireland (UNOCINI) framework, following which a pathway plan is agreed based on the individual needs of the child. Regionally the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services which are situated within the Health and Social Care Trusts do work therapeutically with children and young people who have experienced mental health issues secondary to having experienced child sexual abuse. My Department currently procures specific Therapeutic Support for Children and Young People who are potentially vulnerable to Child Sexual Exploitation through Barnardo’s who operate regionally. My Department also supports the National Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children (NSPCC) through the Department of Health’s Core Grant. Although this Grant does not specifically procure a Therapeutic Service, it does support the NSPCC to deliver their services which are inclusive of Therapeutic Support.BHSCTHosts the Child Care Centre which is available regionally. The Family Trauma Centre provides a regional trauma service for children and young people. NHSCTNo specific recovery services.SEHSCTNo specific recovery services.SHSCTSAFE4UWHSCTMoving On and Recovery ServiceMy Department also supports Nexus NI to provide counselling services to adult victims and survivors of sexual abuse and violence. These adult victims and survivors may have experienced online sexual abuse as children or young people.   Online Child Sexual Abuse is not a standalone category recorded within referrals and therefore neither my Department nor the H

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any plans to outline a national men's health policy, similar to that in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: The position regarding the development of a Northern Ireland Men’s Health Strategy or Action Plan remains unchanged from that given in my answer provided for AQW 18437/22-27. The financial position of the Department remains extremely challenging.The Public Health Agency (PHA) supports a wide range of men’s health initiatives which focus on providing services to those experiencing limited or restricted access to services. These reduce social isolation and promote opportunities for social connection and health enhancing behaviours. Initiatives supported by PHA include: the Farm Families Health Check Programme; funding for Cancer Focus NI to coordinate the implementation of the Skin Cancer Prevention Strategy Action Plan; funding for  Groundwork NI to provide a coordination and support service to Men’s Sheds across the region; support and funding for HSC Trusts and community and voluntary networks and groups to plan, promote and facilitate events during Men’s Health Week each year; funding for a range of locally based physical activity programmes under the banner Older and Active; Community Gardening and Growing initiatives, and funding for programmes across the region which provide opportunities for men to engage with and participate in community gardens, allotments, growing initiatives and outdoor Green Gym sessions.I remain supportive of work to improve Men’s Health and have engaged with a variety of men’s groups since my appointment as Minister. Both myself and the Chief Medical Officer have recently supported the Belfast Men’s Health Group event held on 19 November to mark International Men’s Day.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 34470/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 34470/22-27 was answered on 27 November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to evidence heard by the Education Committee on 5 November, which indicated that over 90 per cent of parents believe that the transfer test is damaging to their children’s mental health, to detail any steps his Department taking to (i) abolish academic selection; and (ii) develop alternative approaches to post-primary transfer that support pupils’ wellbeing.
answer_text: I have no such plans. My Department is not responsible for the administration or operation of post-primary transfer tests.  Participation in the assessments is entirely a matter of parental choice. Transfer tests have never been compulsory for any school nor pupil, but they provide an opportunity for pupils to gain a place at a selective post-primary school of their choosing based on the school’s admissions criteria.	The Education Authority operates the process that facilitates the transfer from primary to post-primary school for all pupils, regardless of whether or not they sit the transfer test. There has been significant public debate on the use of academic selection by grammar schools with no public consensus. Rather than focus on divisive structural reforms, the Departments priority will be for a sustained evidence-based approach to improvement of the education system focused on the core areas of curriculum, assessment, qualifications, school improvement, and tackling educational disadvantage. Reform in each of these areas will support pupils’ wellbeing and achievement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 34723/22-27, to detail (i) any mechanisms his Department uses to assess the adequacy of library provision across schools; and (ii) how it ensures that all pupils have equitable access to reading resources.
answer_text: The Education Library Service (ELS) is a statutory service within the Education Authority (EA) that provides a comprehensive library service to Nursery, Primary, Post Primary and Special schools. Schools use a range of self-evaluation frameworks to review their provision. Where a need is identified the ELS is available to provide: advice to support the development of the library in the school; training and support to staff; and advice on what books and resources to make available in a library. All schools are able to access the services of the ELS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any consideration she has given to introducing a scheme to provide victims with an appropriate adult, when making a statement to the PSNI.
answer_text: The Appropriate Adult Scheme provides support for vulnerable people and juveniles who are detained in police custody by safeguarding their rights, entitlements and welfare.  My department does not have any plans to extend this scheme to victims. However, my department has put in place a range of other support services for victims that are available during their engagement with the justice system.  These include services such as the Registered Intermediaries Scheme, the Young Witness Scheme, Assist NI’s domestic and sexual abuse advocacy services for both adult and child victims, Men’s Advisory Project, Victim Support NI and the Sexual Offence Legal Advice schemes which are available for both adult and child complainants in serious sexual offence cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 32502/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Communities To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 31612/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 31612/22-27 was answered on 13 January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 32083/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when she will answer AQW 32731/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 32942/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail why Concerta XL has become less accessible.
answer_text: My Department has been aware of ongoing supply disruptions affecting various strengths and preparations of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) treatments since September 2023 due to a combination of factors including manufacturing issues and an increase in global demand for these products. Whilst most of the supply disruptions have now been resolved, there were a range of methylphenidate prolonged-release tablet brands affected at various times including Concerta XL tablets. These supply disruptions may have impacted on the accessibility of Concerta XL tablets for patients; however, all strengths of Concerta XL tablets are currently available from selected local wholesalers. In addition, my Department issued guidance in the form of a National Patient Safety Alert and updated Medicines Supply Notifications to healthcare professionals providing them with advice and actions to take if patients were unable to access their prescribed ADHD medicines. A list of currently available and unavailable ADHD products has also been published to assist healthcare professionals prescribing these medications on the NHS Specialist Pharmacy Service’s (SPS) website, and specific guidance on prescribing and switching between modified release methylphenidate products - https://www.sps.nhs.uk/articles/prescribing-available-medicines-to-treat-adhd/.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of (a) schools; and (b) Education Authority staff, who have received training from Education Authority Educational Psychology service on Emotional Based School Non-Attendance; and (ii) whether this training is mandatory.
answer_text: To date 245 school staff across primary and post primary have received training on Emotional Based School Non-Attendance. 15 staff have also availed of additional Motivational Interviewing (MI) training.A further 271 EA corporate staff have been trained with 103 attending additional MI training. This training is not mandatory.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail under what criteria Health and Social Care Trusts determine which brand of methylphenidate is prescribed to patients.
answer_text: There is no regional set of criteria for determining which brand of methylphenidate is prescribed; there is no set brand within the Northern Ireland Formulary. There is a regional recommendation to prescribe by using a brand name, for patient safety reasons, but what that brand actually is will be determined by the particular clinical presentation.  Health and Social Care Trust local formulary decisions may influence prescribing pattern however the choice of brand is a clinical decision made by the initiating specialist on an individual patient basis, considering factors such as formulation requirements and patient-specific needs.The choice of brand may be influenced by factors such as the ability to swallow tablets or open capsules, licensed indications for adults or children, clinical response, supply availability, and cost-effectiveness where appropriate. In line with best practice and the Northern Ireland Formulary, modified-release methylphenidate should be prescribed by brand name. This is because different brands contain varying proportions of immediate- and modified-release components, which are not clinically interchangeable. Prescribing by brand ensures therapeutic consistency and reduces the risk of confusion. As prescribing is often by shared care arrangement between Trust specialist and GP this consistency is important.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether patients are provided with an explanation or review prior to being switched from one brand of medication to another.
answer_text: Medications may be switched from one brand of medication to another for a number of reasons.  Most commonly these changes are made to provide an alternative to a medication which is in short supply, to follow nationally recognised guidelines and Northern Ireland formulary choices, or to support cost-effective prescribing.  Like other regions of the UK, my Department provides a list of cost-effective branded medicines choices that it asks prescribers in NI to adopt. It is recommended that prescribers communicate with patients if there have been any changes to their medication.  The level of information and how this is communicated is at the discretion of the prescriber and will usually depend on the clinical implications of the medication change. A change of manufacturer or brand of the same drug (with the same clinical effectiveness) may be communicated with a simple notification, for example, via a letter or annotated note on the right-hand side of the patients’ repeat prescription.  A change in medication from one drug to another may require more detailed information either in written format, discussed over a phone call, or in person during a face to face consultation.  It is for the prescriber to review any medication changes and to communicate with the patient using the most appropriate method for the individual patient.For any medication change it is recommended that the patient should be provided with the details of the change, the rationale, and a mechanism to gain further information or speak to a clinician if they have any queries.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has conducted any assessment of the impact of (i) social media use; and (ii) screen time, on pupils’ (a) mental health; (b) self-image; and (c) concentration.
answer_text: The department has not conducted any assessment of the impact of social media use or screentime on pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his Department is taking to ensure that (i) body confidence; and (ii) self-esteem, education are embedded within the Learning for Life and Work module in secondary schools.
answer_text: ‘A Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum’ made a number of recommendations including around Learning for Life and Work (LLW). A newly appointed  Curriculum Taskforce will consider how best to develop the core components of the LLW curriculum, including citizenship, employability and personal development, to ensure clear progression throughout each key stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether his Department is working with the (a) Department of Health; or (b) Department for the Economy, to address the growing influence of digital marketing and body-focused advertising targeted at children and young people; and (ii) any safeguards that are being explored to educate pupils on harmful content.
answer_text: My officials are working closely with the Departments of Health and Economy, other government departments and key stakeholders in respect of delivery of the objectives as set out in the Northern Ireland Executive Cross Departmental Online Safety Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to support teachers and classroom assistants in delivering age-appropriate lessons on (i) body image; (ii) self-worth; and (iii) digital literacy, particularly in relation to online appearance pressures and influencer culture.
answer_text: The department supports teachers and classroom assistants in delivering age-appropriate lessons on body image, self-worth and digital literacy through the Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) curriculum framework. Schools can access resources to support the delivery of the curriculum topics through the CCEA RSE Hub and by accessing professional development through the Education Authority. Schools are also encouraged to engage with external agencies for specialist subject support. Safer Schools NI is also available to all education settings with online safety resources and information for pupils, school staff and parents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has to introduce, or expand, confidence-building and resilience programmes in schools, to counteract the negative effects of (i) screen use; (ii) social comparison; and (iii) unrealistic beauty standards on young people’s wellbeing.
answer_text: There are no plans to introduce or expand specific confidence-building and resilience programmes in schools.  Through the curriculum, schools already provide pupils with opportunities to develop their confidence, understanding of self-esteem and wellbeing.  They have flexibility to tailor delivery to address topical or emergent issues and may choose to adopt specific programmes to support this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether local councils are required to submit annual figures on (i) dog breeding licences; (ii) inspection outcomes; and (iii) enforcement actions, to his Department.
answer_text: Local councils have statutory responsibility for the licensing of dog breeding establishments which they exercise in accordance with their own enforcement policies and governance structures. My Department does not collect information in relation as to how councils discharge these responsibilities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the timeline for the planned closure of the Ross Thomson Unit at Causeway Hospital, Coleraine; and (ii) any assessment made of the impact the closure of the Ross Thomson Unit will have on (a) patient health outcomes; (b) service accessibility; and (c) the regional provision of care.
answer_text: In relation to details surrounding the Department’s engagement with the Northern Health and Social Care Trust regarding the decision to close the Ross Thomson Unit at Causeway Hospital, Coleraine; no decision has been taken.With regards to a timeline for the planned closure of the Ross Thomson Unit at Causeway Hospital, Coleraine, the Northern Health and Social Care Trust has advised that the inpatient mental health ward at the Ross Thomson Unit at Causeway Hospital is planned to transfer to the proposed new Birch Hill Centre for Mental Health. While it is hoped that the Birch Hill Centre project can progress, this will depend on the capital funding allocation to the Department of the Health (DoH) as part of the ongoing Northern Ireland Executive Budget process.  The funding profile for the project will then be confirmed as part of the development of the DoH ten-year Capital Investment Plan.All other mental health services on the Causeway Hospital site (such as Mental Health Liaison Service and Crisis Resolution Home Treatment Service) would remain in situ, following a transfer of the beds.In relation to assessments made of the impact the closure of the Ross Thomson Unit will have on (a) patient health outcomes; (b) service accessibility; and (c) the regional provision of care, the Northern Health and Social Care Trust have explained that currently, all inpatient mental health services for the Trust area, with the exception of Ross Thomson Unit, are provided at Holywell. The Northern Health and Social Care Trust recognise that centralisation of services would have an impact on some inpatients and their relatives/carers from Causeway as they will have further to travel for some services. This was balanced against the increasing challenges presented in providing care safely and effectively in a small stand-alone mental health unit.  Ross Thomson Unit at Causeway Hospital does not have access to the other specialist wards (such as psychiatric intensive care) and clinical teams which will be available in a larger central service. A single unit will give the Trust the opportunity to better meet patient needs and improve recovery through a high-quality integrated service, with integrated team working and a single cohesive staff culture. The Northern Health and Social Care Trust also advised they will also be able to introduce new service models which will enhance patient experience and recovery in an improved therapeutic environment.Furthermore, the Northern Health and Social Care Trust have proposed that the Birch Hill Centre for mental health will be built to modern national and regional standards; bedrooms will be single rooms and accessible for people with a physical or sensory disability. Both the internal and external surroundings will maximise therapeutic benefits for patients. Dedicated car parking will be available for service users and carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any shared care arrangements for ADHD, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: As clinical and legal liability associated with a prescribed medication falls to the doctor who issues and signs the script, a GP must be confident in the diagnosis, support and guidance given to them and the patient, by the recommending specialist.  The decision to enter into a shared care agreement, or not, is therefore a clinical decision for individual GPs to make and as such, is not monitored, nor numbers of shared care agreements for adult ADHD in operation collected.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 33295/22-27, in light of the 30 per cent uplift in health service dental treatment fees in 2025-26, to detail any monitoring or evaluation mechanisms in place to ensure that this additional investment results in increased availability of NHS dental appointments for patients, rather than solely supplementing existing practice income.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) are self-employed, independent contractors who are free to choose the amount of Health Service (HS) care they provide, and are paid for this work through a fee-per item system, known as Item of Service. After a patient’s treatment is complete, the dental practice submits a claim for payment through the Business Services Organisation (BSO).  To ensure the accuracy of any HS payments for treatment carried out, regular verification checks of claims submitted by dentists are carried out by the Business Services Organisation (BSO) Counter Fraud and Probity Services Unit. These checks ensure that claims submitted by dentists reflect the treatment actually provided to HS patients. The 30% uplift was specifically targeted at fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for HS patients.  While this measure  cannot guarantee increased availability of HS dental appointments, it is intended to encourage dentists to carry out more HS work.My Department will continue to monitor HS dental activity through BSO data to assess the impact of this investment, and inform future policy decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department’s engagement with the Northern Health and Social Care Trust regarding the decision to close the Ross Thomson Unit at Causeway Hospital, Coleraine.
answer_text: In relation to details surrounding the Department’s engagement with the Northern Health and Social Care Trust regarding the decision to close the Ross Thomson Unit at Causeway Hospital, Coleraine; no decision has been taken.With regards to a timeline for the planned closure of the Ross Thomson Unit at Causeway Hospital, Coleraine, the Northern Health and Social Care Trust has advised that the inpatient mental health ward at the Ross Thomson Unit at Causeway Hospital is planned to transfer to the proposed new Birch Hill Centre for Mental Health. While it is hoped that the Birch Hill Centre project can progress, this will depend on the capital funding allocation to the Department of the Health (DoH) as part of the ongoing Northern Ireland Executive Budget process.  The funding profile for the project will then be confirmed as part of the development of the DoH ten-year Capital Investment Plan.All other mental health services on the Causeway Hospital site (such as Mental Health Liaison Service and Crisis Resolution Home Treatment Service) would remain in situ, following a transfer of the beds.In relation to assessments made of the impact the closure of the Ross Thomson Unit will have on (a) patient health outcomes; (b) service accessibility; and (c) the regional provision of care, the Northern Health and Social Care Trust have explained that currently, all inpatient mental health services for the Trust area, with the exception of Ross Thomson Unit, are provided at Holywell. The Northern Health and Social Care Trust recognise that centralisation of services would have an impact on some inpatients and their relatives/carers from Causeway as they will have further to travel for some services. This was balanced against the increasing challenges presented in providing care safely and effectively in a small stand-alone mental health unit.  Ross Thomson Unit at Causeway Hospital does not have access to the other specialist wards (such as psychiatric intensive care) and clinical teams which will be available in a larger central service. A single unit will give the Trust the opportunity to better meet patient needs and improve recovery through a high-quality integrated service, with integrated team working and a single cohesive staff culture. The Northern Health and Social Care Trust also advised they will also be able to introduce new service models which will enhance patient experience and recovery in an improved therapeutic environment.Furthermore, the Northern Health and Social Care Trust have proposed that the Birch Hill Centre for mental health will be built to modern national and regional standards; bedrooms will be single rooms and accessible for people with a physical or sensory disability. Both the internal and external surroundings will maximise therapeutic benefits for patients. Dedicated car parking will be available for service users and carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment that has been made of the impact that increasing the price of school meals from January 2026 will have on (i) children’s health; (ii) nutrition; and (iii) learning outcomes, particularly for struggling families who do not qualify for Free School Meals.
answer_text: In taking its decision to raise the price of a school meal for those not entitled to free school meals, the Education Authority (EA) recognised that children and young people having access to a nutritious lunch positively impacted both their educational and health outcomes.The EA acknowledged that if a child is no longer be able to access a healthy lunch, it could result in a negative impact on these outcomes for any such children. However, a family not entitled to free school meals retains the choice of whether to purchase a school meal or to make their own arrangements, such as providing a packed lunch. To support parents in this, the Public Health Agency (PHA) publication “Are you packing a healthy lunch?” provides advice and guidance to parents to ensure their child is able to access an affordable, healthy and nutritionally balanced packed lunch.Consequently, if a family decides not to pay for a school meal, the availability of this guidance on preparing a healthy packed lunch means that all parents should be able to ensure that children are able to access an affordable, healthy lunch, either through the canteen or a packed lunch, without it having an adverse impact on their educational and health outcomes.The PHA publication is available at: https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/publications/are-you-packing-healthy-lunch-english-and-irish-translation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant of AQW 33407/22-27, to detail (i) any actions his Department has taken to ensure that all pupils, including those in schools that do not offer Irish as a subject, are guaranteed curriculum-based learning on the history and cultural significance of the Irish language; (ii) examples or evidence of Shared Education programmes that specifically address the cultural significance of the Irish language; and (iii) the number of schools that have been supported in implementing these programmes.
answer_text: (i) My department has a duty under Article 89 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, to encourage and facilitate the development of Irish-medium education, not to mandate teaching the history or cultural significance of the Irish language.  Through immersion pedagogy, pupils engage with the language in a way that naturally includes its historical and cultural context.    Outside Irish-medium schools, modern languages are compulsory at Key Stage 3, with schools choosing the language(s) to offer.  Opportunities to explore Irish culture exist through cross-curricular learning, supported by Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) resources such as Primary Irish and Aspects of Shared Cultural Heritage,  Place-Names in Northern Ireland and  Cultural Traditions.  Additionally, the delivery of Shared Education in schools also provides opportunities to explore matters relating to the history and cultural significance of the Irish language.(ii) Shared Education partnerships set their own action plans bespoke to their specific needs and objectives of their school development plan.  Education Authority (EA) guidance encourages inclusion of Irish-medium settings and a provides case study example that outlines how pupils from different language backgrounds can engage meaningfully while promoting mutual respect and collaboration.  Shared Education Partnerships often explore cultural identity and diversity, creating opportunities to appreciate Irish language. My department and EA also work with Gael Linn on resources like Scoil Spreagtha for non-Irish-medium schools(iii) There are currently two Irish-medium settings and three primary schools with Irish-medium units, engaged in experienced partnerships, supported with Mainstreamed Shared Education resources. As part of the Mainstreaming Shared Education Strategy, a focus on enhancing participation was pinpointed, and as a result, there are currently a further 12 Irish-medium settings engaged in the PEACEPLUS ASPIRE Project which commenced operation last year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any assessment that has been made of the impact that second homes and holiday lets are having on housing availability and affordability, particularly in coastal areas.
answer_text: Officials from my Department are engaging with colleagues in DfE and DfI to scope and commission research on the nature, scale and potential impacts of short-term holiday lets on local housing markets.  There have been numerous discussions on this issue in this chamber, I was sorry not to be able to attend the recent debate.  I am keen to see what can be done. Regarding second homes, or holiday homes, there is very limited data available. This makes it difficult to establish their number, location and any impact they may have. My Department is currently carrying out research on second home ownership to improve our understanding of the issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why capital funding was not secured for a new, purpose-built site as part of the development plan for the new Causeway Academy.
To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 33408/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 33408/22-27 was answered on 8 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) his assessment of the impact of the growth in holiday homes and short-term lets on access to affordable housing in rural and coastal communities; and (ii) whether he has any plans to regulate, or limit such properties in areas where they are having a negative effect on local housing availability and community sustainability.
answer_text: There is very limited data available on second homes and short-term holiday lets which makes it difficult to establish their extent, location and any impact they may have. My Department is currently carrying out research on second home ownership to improve our understanding of the issues. In addition, officials from my Department are engaging with colleagues in DfE and DfI to scope out and commission research on the nature, scale and potential impacts of short-term holiday lets on local housing markets.My Department does not have the relevant powers to regulate or limit the number of second homes or short-term holiday lets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of schools that have a dedicated library; (ii) the number of these schools that have a full-time, qualified librarian; and (iii) the annual funding allocated to school libraries, broken down by type of school.
answer_text: The Education Library Service (ELS) is a statutory service within the Education Authority (EA) that provides a comprehensive library service to Nursery, Primary, Post Primary and Special schools. The ELS does not maintain a register of schools with dedicated library space.The ELS also does not maintain a register of schools with qualified, full-time librarians.Dedicated annual funding is not allocated to school libraries.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any formal process, and required approvals, for a Northern Ireland Minister to travel abroad on a visit funded, in whole or in part, by a foreign government, including any use of Departmental staff time or resources; and (ii) whether such visits must be cleared in advance by the Executive Office.
answer_text: The decision to undertake a foreign visit on official business is a matter for each Minister to consider. Approval by The Executive Office is not required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will review the current rates exemption criteria for domestic properties, given that houses with no access to electricity or running water are deemed liable for rates, despite being uninhabitable.
answer_text: The legislative framework for domestic rating is governed by the Rates (Northern Ireland) Order 1977. Under this Order, liability for rates is determined by reference to the existence of a hereditament, that is, a unit of property capable of being separately assessed for rating purposes.The valuation is subject to statutory assumptions, including the assumption that the property is in an average state of internal repair. This means that poor condition or lack of services does not automatically remove a property from the valuation list or negate its liability for rates.The threshold for removing a property from the valuation list is high. The hereditament test, as considered in numerous decisions of the Valuation Tribunal and the Lands Tribunal, asks whether the property is physically capable of beneficial occupation when the assumption of average internal repair is applied. If the answer is yes, the property will remain rateable, even if it lacks utilities or is not currently occupied.Any ratepayer who believes that a property may not meet the criteria for rating, is encouraged to contact Land & Property Services (LPS) to request a review. Each case is considered on its individual merits, and LPS will assess whether the property should remain on the valuation listWhere a property is retained on the valuation list, and is vacant, there are a number of exclusions from rating liability under the Rating of Empty Homes provisions. These apply only in specific circumstances, for example, where occupation is legally prohibited or where a public authority has taken formal action.Any change to these provisions would require legislative amendment and would need to be carefully considered to ensure the integrity and fairness of the rating system is maintained.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 32734/22-27, to detail how his Department reconciles its lack of routine monitoring of bonfire emissions with its obligations under the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 and Air Quality Standards Regulations (NI) 2010 to monitor and reduce emissions of particulate matter and other pollutants.
answer_text: The Environment Order (Northern Ireland) 2002 requires District Councils to review and assess air quality within their districts. This can take the form of active or passive monitoring or modelling. The Air Quality Standards Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2010 place a duty on DAERA and other Northern Ireland government departments to, amongst other things, monitor levels of certain air pollutants. Neither piece of legislation requires point source emission monitoring of bonfires. The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 does not require any monitoring of bonfires in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any engagement her Department has had with Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council, regarding the restoration of Runkerry Bridge, Portballintrae; and (ii) a timeline for when the bridge will be reinstated.
answer_text: The Runkerry Footbridge is not adopted or maintained by my Department. Officials are aware that the bridge was removed by Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council in December 2021 due to storm damage.  My Department has had no engagement with Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council as to its restoration and is not aware of any timeline for its reinstatement.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 30813/22-27, to detail (i) any specific community, and environmental benefit obligations included in the sale agreement; (ii) any progress made, to date, in meeting each requirement; (iii) how the Executive is monitoring and evaluating delivery of the full range of commitments attached to the sale of the site; (iv) whether the Executive will publish reports, or information, arising from this monitoring to ensure transparency regarding the benefits delivered to the local community; and (v) any jobs which have been created at t
answer_text: The Shackleton agreement for sale required the inclusion of a Community Benefits plan and an Environmental Benefits plan detailing how community and environmental benefits would be delivered. To date, MJM Renewables have demonstrated compliance with all conditions of the agreement for sale.The Executive Office no longer owns the Shackleton site, The Executive Office has no plans to publish reports, or information, arising from our engagement with MJM.  From September 2020 to September 2025, MJM Renewables has advised that 200 jobs have been created at the Shackleton site. These jobs include a range of manufacturing, medical and operational roles.
