question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 32943/22-27, to detail (i) how his Department intends to monitor, analyse and report, on the data recorded under the Emotionally Based School Non-Attendance absence code, to ensure pupils are receiving appropriate educational provision; and (ii) what minimum standards, or statutory guidance exist to guarantee that pupils recorded under this code are provided with suitable education during their absence.
answer_text: The Department receives monthly attendance data from C2K, lifted from the Schools Information Management System* (SIMS) which is updated by schools and counts as a ‘legal document’. A monthly report is produced based on this data which is then circulated for information to officials within both DE and the EA, including the head of Education Welfare Service.As this is a new code, officials within the DE Tackling Educational Disadvantage Team will be monitoring the usage closely and highlighting any concerns within the monthly report. The Department is also recommending that school settings advise their EWO in cases where this code is applied, this is to ensure that every avenue is explored to reengage the child with education as early as possible.EA Education Psychology provide training for schools and EA staff on Emotional Based School Non-Attendance.In conjunction with the pupil and parent, the school and Education Welfare Service should consider the most appropriate educational option on an individual basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will issue a letter to major internet service providers, requesting voluntary domain name system delisting of Tattle.Life, as an interim public-interest safety measure.
answer_text: I am aware that Tattle Life, an online forum, has recently attracted significant media attention, largely due to a civil case which was heard in Belfast’s High Court.  I recently met the Member in relation to this matter.  My Department has sought legal advice from the Departmental Solicitor’s Office on this issue and my officials are currently considering the matter further. I have always been very clear that any form of abuse, harassment or any other associated behaviours, whether conducted online or offline, are completely unacceptable and I would urge victims to report such matters to the police.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when the Dungiven regeneration plan will commence.
answer_text: My officials have been working with Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council (CCGBC) to prepare the Outline Business Case (OBC) for the Dungiven Regeneration project.  This was ratified by Council at a meeting on 4 November and is expected to be received by my Department in the coming weeks for review and approval.		CCGBC are planning Growth Deal signing for June 2026 which will secure the funding package. Council anticipate the construction phase of the Dungiven Regeneration project will commence in June 2028 and complete in June 2029.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department will support pupils living with epilepsy.
answer_text: The current Department of Education guidance for schools for children and young people with medication needs can be viewed at this link https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/articles/support-pupils-medication-needs. Schools have a responsibility to follow this guidance and make reasonable adjustments to ensure that that the needs of pupils with epilepsy are met. The guidance is currently under review.The healthcare professional involved in supporting the medical needs of a child or young person will provide a detailed plan, which is agreed with the parents and school (Epilepsy Care Plan or Individual Healthcare Plan (IHP)), this can be used as all or part of a pupil’s Medication Plan and will detail the actions to be taken in the event of an emergency. Healthcare professionals are responsible for updating a child’s IHP. IHPs are vital to helping school staff understand the pupil’s epilepsy, ensure their safety and inclusion in school activities.Once this information is provided schools have a duty to ensure the relevant staff are aware of each individual child’s Care Plan. The school will ensure that relevant Individual Medication Forms are completed (if required), agreed with the young person (if appropriate), parents/carers, healthcare professionals and the relevant staff. Schools have a duty to ensure the relevant staff are aware of each individual child’s Care Plan.It is recommended that schools also put in place a Risk Management Plan (RMP). The RMP tailored by school staff to meet a child’s needs is central to the management of the child’s health and safety within the school. This document is also informed by the child’s Care Plan. Risk Assessment training is available for schools at: Risk Assessment Training | EA Staff HubTo ensure that school staff have a basic understanding, can recognise symptoms and seek appropriate support, awareness training should be completed. The Education Authority’s website (SEND Plan of Arrangements) provides a link to Epilepsy Action training, a free module for schools: Available courses | Epilepsy Action Learning

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement his Department has had with pupils with epilepsy, to discuss how to best support them, in the last 10 years.
answer_text: Schools have a responsibility to follow the “Supporting Pupils with Medication Needs” guidance and make reasonable adjustments to ensure that that the needs of pupils with epilepsy are met. My department is reviewing the 2008 joint guidance in conjunction with colleagues from the Department of Health (DoH), the Education Authority (EA) and the Public Health Agency (PHA).Officials from my department met with a member of Young Epilepsy Youth Voice Network, who represented children and young people (CYP) in Northern Ireland with Epilepsy, in June. They discussed the challenges and lived experiences of these CYP and agreed to feed them into the ongoing work around the guidance “Supporting Pupils with Medication Needs”.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) how often the Sexual Violence Reduction Group provides updates to her; and (ii) whether these updates are made public.
answer_text: The Sexual Violence Reduction Group was replaced in 2020 by the Strategic Justice Group on Sexual Harm (SJGSH) which had an extended remit to include oversight of the criminal justice system’s response to domestic abuse, as well as sexual violence.In 2022, given the need for a dedicated focus on delivery of the Gillen Review, the SJGSH was discontinued and was replaced by a new Gillen Programme Board.Separately, the cross-Departmental Domestic and Sexual Abuse (DSA) Strategic Oversight Board provides strategic leadership in relation to tackling domestic and sexual abuse whilst also ensuring alignment and join-up with The Executive Office led EVAWG Strategic Framework.I receive regular updates in relation to both the Gillen Implementation Programme and cross-Departmental work under the joint DSA Strategy and Action Plan to tackle domestic and sexual abuse.In terms of the provision of information to the public, along with the Minister of Health, I intend to publish an annual report in relation to the DSA Strategy and Action Plan, the first of which will be published early next year.Separately, information about the Gillen Review Implementation Programme, including a newsletter setting out progress, is publicly available on my Department’s website at this link - Gillen Review Report into the law and procedures in serious sexual offences in NI | Department of Justice

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the current Northern Ireland laws regarding online catfishing.
answer_text: The term “catfishing” refers to the creation of a fake identity and where a person communicates with another, typically online, often with the intention to deceive or mislead the individual into a romantic or sexual relationship; or to extort money or commit fraud.  The creation of a fake identity in and of itself is not a criminal offence.  However, where such an identity has been created with particular intentions, then the behaviour may be captured by existing offences and the relevant sanctions which apply.  Where the act of catfishing involves sexual motivation, there is already a comprehensive and robust framework of law on sexual offences in place in Northern Ireland which, for the main part, is included in the Sexual Offences (Northern Ireland) Order 2008. The offences apply equally to both online as well as offline behaviours. I strengthened the offences of the 2008 Order via the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 to provide a further range of protections from sexual harm.  Amongst others, this included four new offences of an adult pretending to be a child for the purposes of child grooming, which are unique offences to this jurisdiction.  In the Protection from Stalking Act (NI) 2022, although catfishing behaviour is not specifically mentioned, if the intention is proven of causing a person to suffer fear, alarm or substantial distress (in the section 1 stalking offence); or there was an intention by the behaviour to cause fear or alarm, or recklessness as to whether the behaviour causes fear or alarm (in the section 2 threatening or abusive behaviour offence), then these offences could be made out in court.  As above, both offences apply equally to both online as well as offline behaviours.   On the wider matter of malicious communications, telecommunications and internet legislation are reserved matters and therefore the responsibility of the UK Government.The Online Safety Act 2023 introduced new UK-wide laws that protect both children and adults from online harm. It created new communication offences including a false communications offence and a threatening communications offence.  In addition, the cross Executive Online Safety Strategy has been put in place to assist children and young people to participate in the online world in a positive, safe and responsible way.  This includes a particular focus on prevention and education to help tackle online abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any training given to NHS staff on booking British Sign Language Interpreters for deaf patients.
answer_text: There are various approaches taken across the region’s Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts and via my Department to ensure appropriate training for all staff in relation to booking British Sign Language (BSL) and Irish Sign Language (ISL) interpreters for d/Deaf and d/Deafblind individuals. My Department commissions the contract for the Regional Communication Support Service (RCSS) which is provided by Sign Language Interactions (SLI) by Sorenson.  The RCSS is for people who are d/Deaf, Deafblind, or hard of hearing and require an interpreter when accessing health and social care services.  The booking of this service can be undertaken via a regional generic email address or telephone number.Since the commencement of this contract, my department and SLI have been proactively working with the HSC Trusts to promote ongoing awareness of this service including how to book an interpreter.  The following resources and services are provided by HSC Trusts and my Department and are available for HSC staff and provide guidance on how to book a sign language interpreter;Staff poster outlining how to book a BSL/ISL interpreter.  This is to be displayed by Trusts in all staff areas and housed within Trust staff information intranet sites.  QR codes on posters that take staff directly to the remote sign video option for accessing an interpreter.Trusts share guidance and reminders to all staff on how to book an interpreter through various forums such as global staff emails, staff newsletters and desktop computer screensavers.Posters relating to the service to be displayed in all public facing Trust facilities/service areas to increase service user awareness.SLI have appointed community liaison officers (CLOs) who provide service awareness training to both the d/Deaf community and HSC staff on booking and accessing interpreters.   The British Deaf Association (BDA) are contracted by my Department to provide Deaf Awareness training, in person for HSC staff and it covers the content of how to book a BSL/ISL interpreter.  Over 400 HSC staff have availed of this training in the last 3 years.The development and dissemination of an online “accessible and inclusive communication toolkit” for staff, which incorporates guidance on how to book a sign language interpreter.The dissemination and promotion of videos raising awareness of the importance of the need to book sign language interpreters.There are a number of different training initiatives across Trusts, within which the content covers guidance on how to book a BSL/ISL interpreter; Staff Disability Awareness TrainingMandatory Deaf Awareness training and induction for emergency department staff. Sight Loss and Deaf Awareness training. Business Services Organisation (BSO) foreign language interpreting service provide “Working Well with Interpreters” training. Making Communication Accessible for All training.Mandatory Equality, Good Relations and Human Rights E-Learning Programme “Making a Difference”.There is currentl

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the data her Department keeps regarding the number of referrals made by the Police Service of Northern Ireland to the Sexual Offences Legal Advisory team, over the last five years.
answer_text: The Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) is a key partner in the Sexual Offences Legal Adviser (SOLA) pilot and has committed to ensuring that all officers working in the specialist units investigating sexual offences are aware of the SOLA pilot and are trained appropriately in the referral process. Victims of serious sexual offences are offered a referral to the SOLA service either directly through a PSNI specialist investigation team or via the daily crime record that is shared with Victim Support NI (VSNI), which is funded to deliver the SOLA service. Referrals to the SOLA service may be made by any organisation and victims may also self-refer even without or before making a report to police.Since the launch of the SOLA pilot in April 2021 and up to 30 June 2025, PSNI has made a total of 1,337 referrals to the Adult Sexual Offences Legal Adviser service. In February 2025, the Children’s SOLA (CSOLA) service was launched, and from that date until 30 June 2025, PSNI has made a further 278 referrals to the CSOLA service. Since August 2024, the Department has implemented a new system for recording PSNI referral data. This allows for referrals to be identified by the originating PSNI department, known as ‘direct referrals,’ as well as those arising from the daily crime record provided to Victim Support NI. The table below shows the numbers of referrals to the SOLA service.PSNI referrals to the SOLA service: Adult SOLA ServiceChildren’s SOLA ServiceReferrals before August 20241080N/ADirect Referrals from August 2024 – June 20258131Daily Crime Report from August 2024 – June 2025176247Total Referrals by PSNI for both SOLA and CSOLA service 1615In total, the number of referrals to both SOLA and CSOLA services, from all sources, up to 30 June 2025, including self-referrals is 2914.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the data her Department holds on the timeliness of referrals from the Police Service of Northern Ireland to the Advocacy team, for victims of sexual violence, over the last five years.
answer_text: Following competitive procurement processes, publicly funded advocacy services for victims of domestic and/or sexual abuse have been in place since September 2021, delivered by ASSIST NI. The Department does not hold information on the timeliness of referrals from the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) to these advocacy services.This information may be available directly from the PSNI, and any request for such data should be directed to them.However, ASSIST NI receives a daily referral list from the PSNI containing the details of victims who meet the eligibility criteria for the service.  This process ensures that referrals are made promptly and consistently, allowing ASSIST NI to make timely contact with victims and offer appropriate support.Under the terms of its contract, ASSIST NI is required to report quarterly on its performance, including the number of referrals that are contacted, or where contact is attempted, within 24 and 48 hours of receipt.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any public messaging campaigns his Department currently have regarding male fertility.
answer_text: Whilst there are no specific public messaging campaigns currently running in relation to male fertility, information on fertility, including actions that individuals could take that may improve male fertility, is available on the NI Direct and Public Health Agency websites. This includes public health advice on issues that can affect fertility, such as alcohol consumption, weight reduction, smoking, stress reduction and environmental factors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the Lady Chief Justice on implementing changes to legislation, to allow all victims the right to read out their victim statement in court in their own voice.
answer_text: I am supportive of measures which can help victims on their journey to recovery, such as allowing victims to read a Victim Personal Statement (VPS) in court, if they wish to do so.Section 33 of the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 sets out the provision for making a Victim Personal Statement (VPS), including that the statement is made in writing and provided to the court. The legislation is not prescriptive regarding the manner in which a VPS may be read in court or by whom.  Legislative change is not, therefore, required to enable victims to read their VPS in court.A working group led by my officials is taking forward changes to the current procedures and guidance for VPSs including clarifying that victims may request to read their VPS in court, and that such requests will be subject to judicial consideration on a case-by-case basis. These matters have been discussed with the Office of the Lady Chief Justice, as well as with the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the guidelines his Department has issued, in relation to the correct terminology when describing individuals with autism.
answer_text: Neuro-affirming identity first terminology i.e. autistic children and young people is used throughout guidance and communications with and about autistic people. This terminology aligns with the preferences expressed by many within the autistic community. The terminology preferences of the individual are acknowledged and respected when supporting a child or young person.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any discussions she has had in relation to traffic issues in Ballykelly.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any strategies he has to improve testing for male fertility.
answer_text: There is no specific strategy for male fertility testing; however, testing is a routine part of the fertility service available to those who have difficulty conceiving. If a woman is referred for investigations, the male partner involved will also undergo testing to help determine an appropriate treatment pathway. I recognise the difficulty faced by those experiencing fertility issues and remain supportive of New Decade New Approach commitment to increase fertility treatment. However, in order to deliver on this commitment, funding will be essential to increase both the workforce and the physical space required to expand the service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the action he can take to improve support for women with polycystic ovarian syndrome.
answer_text: Improvements in women’s health, including conditions such as Polycystic Ovary Syndrome, remain a priority for my Department. Significant work is underway to enhance gynaecology pathways across Northern Ireland’s health and care system, ensuring early diagnosis and comprehensive treatment plans are available across primary, secondary, and tertiary care.To support this, a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum has been established, with representation from Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and GPs. This clinically led forum oversees service improvements and is implementing recommendations from the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report.For women with Polycystic Ovary Syndrome, improvements include better access to multidisciplinary care—combining gynaecology, endocrinology, dietetics, and mental health support—to address the complex nature of the condition. While multidisciplinary team management is the preferred approach for many gynaecological conditions, funding and staffing constraints do not always allow this to be implemented fully. Nonetheless, it remains my aspiration.Enhanced referral pathways from primary care ensure timely access to appropriate services, including the Gynaecology Enhanced Primary Care Service (GPES).Red flag and time-critical gynaecology patients on the waiting list have already benefited from Elective Access Funding. As of 30 September 2025, the GPES delivered 1,567 face-to-face appointments and 587 remote consultations, at a cost of £1.07m. Trusts have also been provided with an additional £3.9m in quarters 1 to 3 to support further in-house and independent sector activity.My Department recognises the need for improved public awareness, education, and information on Polycystic Ovary Syndrome and broader women’s health across the life course. My Officials and Trusts continue to explore options to strengthen pathways and improve care, with some recommendations requiring investment and others achievable through reform.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any guidelines from his Department on correct terminology used when describing autistic individuals.
answer_text: Guidelines on terminology used when describing autistic individuals are set out at paragraph 1.2 of the Autism Strategy 2023-2028. Following consultation and agreement with stakeholders, the term which is used throughout the strategy and in Department of Health publications is ‘autistic person’ or ‘person who is autistic’.  This also aligns with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines which were revised in 2021 to use the term ‘autistic child or young person’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the rationale as to why St. Colmcille’s Primary School and Nursery Unit, Claudy, does not yet have full-time nursery; and (ii) any plans to (a) review; or (b) expand, provision in the area.
answer_text: My Department, in conjunction with the Education Authority (EA), has been progressing the Pre-School Standardisation Programme (PSP) at pace. In Spring 2025, the EA undertook a further survey of all pre-school settings to refresh the information held on the individual state of readiness of settings. The EA is responsible for using this data to engage with individual pre-school settings and select those that will be invited to progress at each stage of the programme.  It is my intention to transition a further 2,200 pre-school places or circa 100 settings to the full-time session length from September 2026.  Consultation on the first two tranches of settings identified as potential candidates to transition to full-time provision from September 2026 was launched on 25 September and 9 October. It is anticipated that consultation on a further tranche will be launched in the coming weeks.   It will take a number of years for all pre-schools to transition to the standardised session length and it is not possible to give a timeline for individual pre-school settings to complete the change. The speed of transition will largely be dependent on the resources available from the Executive at each stage, as well as the circumstances of individual pre-school settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current funding for the Independent Autism Reviewer; and (ii) whether he plans to increase this in 2026-27.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to delivering its commitments under the Autism Act (NI) 2011 (as amended).  Annual funding of £300k has been provided for the delivery of the Autism Strategy 2023 to 2028.  A proportion of this budget has been utilised to support the initial running costs for the Autism Reviewer’s office.  In January 2025, my Department allocated a dedicated staffing resource to provide full time Head of Office support; this resource is being funded from within my Departmental administrative budget and is not taken from the programme delivery allocation.  We are also committed to providing additional in-year resources where funding allows and my officials continue to discuss this with the Reviewer’s Office. Given the level of financial pressures currently facing my Department it will be challenging to increase funding for 2026/27.  However, I will not be able to confirm allocations for the next financial year until the outcome of the 2026/27 budget is known. I recently met with the Autism Reviewer and one of the issues we discussed was the resource requirements to allow the Reviewer to deliver the full remit of her role appropriately. My officials continue to consider these arrangements and there is ongoing engagement with the Autism Reviewer on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any steps his Department is taking to reduce waiting times for gynaecological treatments, given that over 37,000 women are currently on hospital waiting lists; and (ii) when his Department expects these waiting lists will be reduced
answer_text: There is currently significant work underway across the health and care system to reduce waiting times and develop pathways for gynaecology-specific conditions within each Trust across Northern Ireland.To support this, the Department has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, which includes representation from Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and General Practitioners. This forum is overseeing ongoing service improvements and the development of gynaecology service pathways across primary, intermediate, secondary, and tertiary care levels which are clinically led. It is also responsible for implementing the recommendations of the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report.Most referral pathways begin in primary care, and the gynaecology pathways being developed reflect this. If a GP is unable to assess or treat a patient locally, the patient may be referred to the Gynaecology Enhanced Primary Care Service—if they meet the criteria—or to secondary care gynaecology services.Primary and Secondary Care teams are working collaboratively to ensure patients are seen in the right place, at the right time, by the right professional. In addition to accepting referrals from primary care, a pilot initiative is underway to identify patients on secondary care waiting lists who could be managed within the Enhanced Primary Care Service. Eligible patients will be assessed and treated by one of the 16 specialist GPs with enhanced skills in gynaecology. This service currently manages referrals for pessary fitting, HRT management, initial assessment and treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding, and contraceptive management including coil fitting.  As of 30 September 2025, the Enhanced Primary Care Service has delivered 1,567 face-to-face appointments and 587 remote consultations at a cost in-year of £1.07m. When fully operational, it is expected to manage approximately 6,072 patient contacts annually (including both face-to-face and remote appointments).Secondary Care services are also developing action plans to address waiting times and implement relevant GIRFT recommendations within each Trust. Between April and December 2025, additional non-recurrent funding has been approved of £3.9m to provide waiting list initiatives—both in-house and via the independent sector to address red flag or other time-critical assessments and treatments. As well as Trusts carrying out administrative validation exercises, to review current outpatient waiting lists, clinicians are also carrying out enhanced clinical validation of outpatient lists. Additional outpatients are also in place to provide one stop clinics for colposcopy, hysteroscopy, smears and pessaries that cannot be provided in primary care.   Trusts have established a Gynaecology Provider Collaborative - February 2025. As part of this process, two Collaborative sub-groups have been established, namely:   -A Clinical Standardisation Group – with Clinical and Director/Assistant Director represent

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any steps they are taking to coordinate a cross-departmental strategy to promote understanding of the Irish language as part of Northern Ireland’s shared cultural heritage; and (ii) how this strategy will ensure that initiatives are inclusive, non-partisan, and accessible to all communities.
answer_text: With my Department leading, all Executive Departments are currently engaged in the development of the Irish Language Strategy and the Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture Strategy through the work programme of the Cross-Departmental Working Group (CDWG). Once the CDWG work has completed, I shall consider both draft strategies accordingly. Each strategy will go through the Equality Impact Assessment process and public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure the history and cultural significance of the Irish language is incorporated into the curriculum, in a way that is accessible and relevant to pupils of all communities.
answer_text: The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) provides resources in support of the current curriculum that support teaching and learning about Irish culture, tradition and language. These resources are available to all schools through the CCEA website at: www.ccea.org.ukAdditionally, the delivery of Shared Education in schools also provides opportunities to explore matters relating to the historical and cultural significance of the Irish language.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any progress made against the clawback clause in the Shackleton site agreement in Ballykelly, which requires the creation of 100 jobs within five years of the first planning approval in September 2020.
answer_text: As of September 2025, MJM Renewables has advised TEO that 200 jobs have been created to date, demonstrating compliance with the terms of the Shackleton agreement for sale.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of drones, recorded as having been flown in the vicinity of prisons, in each of the last five years, broken down by institution.
answer_text: There have been six reported sightings of drones in the vicinity of a Northern Ireland prison in the last five years; one in 2022, four in 2023 and one in 2024.  All sightings occurred in the vicinity of Maghaberry Prison and had no impact upon the normal operation or the safety of the prison.

answer_text: AQW 31614/22-27 was answered on 19 November 2025.AQW 32381/22-27 was answered on 1 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 31610/22-27, to outline the timeline and key milestones for implementing the Animal Welfare Pathway, including when stakeholders and the public can expect delivery of the first phase of reforms.
answer_text: I have already completed a public consultation on my proposals to regulate the sale and supply of pups and kittens which also outlined my aspiration to ban third party sellers.  over 480 responses were received, and I intend to publish a formal consultation response soon which will set out my final way forward.An expert advisory group has been established to review the operation of the current dog breeding regulations and examine the provision of canine fertility services. The group has been gathering evidence and has met with a wide range of stakeholders. A consultation response and way forward document was recently published on my Department’s website, outlining my plans to make the use of CCTV mandatory in slaughterhouses. The legislation required to implement the proposal is currently being drafted.Finally, my officials have commenced initial policy proposals on the remaining reforms in the pathway and have been examining the case for mandatory microchipping of cats, enhancements to microchipping regulations, review of dog licence fees, regulation of rescue and rehoming organisations and exploring potential changes to the operation of the current animal boarding regulations. While this work is in an early phase, I hope to be in a position to initiate a public consultation on all these topics around the end of this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what urgent measures are being taken to address the shortage of NHS dental provision in East Derry, given that constituents report being unable to register with a dentist despite repeated attempts.
answer_text: Access to General Dental Services is challenging across Northern Ireland at present and I am aware that some patients, particularly those not currently registered with a dentist are finding it more difficult to obtain an appointment with a Health Service dentist. General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) are self-employed, independent contractors who are free to choose the amount of HS care they provide, and under the current General Dental Service arrangements, the Department does not have a role in determining the location of dental practices. The closest comparative local government district for East Londonderry is Causeway Coast and Glens. The most recent General Dental Statistics show that this district has the third highest ratio of General Dental Practitioners, registered on the dental list to carry out health service treatment, to head of population in Northern Ireland, 65.7 dentists per 100,000 head of population. However, I acknowledge that this is of little solace if they are not committing sufficient time to Health Service work and patients are unable to obtain access.In an effort to improve access for all patients, an additional £7m is being invested in General Dental Services in 2025/26. The investment is funding the continuation of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme and 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for Health Service fillings, extractions, and root canals, alongside a new £1.6m allocation to provide additional support to dental practitioners who continue to provide Health Service dental care for their patients.In parallel the Dental Access Scheme (DAS) continues to provide access for unregistered patients with an urgent or pressing oral health need.  East Londonderry falls into the Western Local Commissioning Group (LCG) area. The most recent data shows that 4,298 patients have been seen under DAS in this area up to 30 September 2025 and 9722 in Northern LCG for same period, which is the neighbouring area and may be accessed by patients from east Londonderry.Longer term, my Department’s Health and Social Care NI three-year plan includes a commitment to develop an action plan and programme of work for the reform of General Dental Services by April 2027.While that work is progressed consideration will be given to other actions which could be taken to improve access to HS dental care for both registered and unregistered patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: My Department carried out a public consultation last year, seeking views on potential updates to employment law. In April this year, I announced proposals for an ambitious package of upgrades to our employment law framework. This includes secondary legislation, codes of practice and guidance, as well as primary legislation in the form of an Employment Rights Bill.Since then, work on the Bill has been taken forward, with continuing focused engagement with key stakeholders on the finer detail of a small number of matters. My Department has been working closely with the Office of the Legislative Counsel on drafting, with a view to bringing a draft Bill to my Executive colleagues for agreement, before introduction to the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have considered creating a ministerial role for Ending Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: As First Minister and deputy First Minister, we jointly hold responsibility for the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Strategic Framework. Junior Minister Reilly and Junior Minister Bunting also take an active role, including chairing the EVAWG Oversight Board.In recognition of the significance of this cross-Executive work, we are pleased that EVAWG has been identified as a priority within the Programme for Government, reflecting our commitment to this important work.

answer_text: AQW 31614/22-27 was answered on 19 November 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 31266/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 31266/22-27 was answered on 14 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have considered establishing a ministerial role for mental health in the last five years.
answer_text: The Minister of Health has lead responsibility for co-ordinating the Executive’s strategy on mental health and there are no plans to appoint an additional Minister for this purpose.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 30813/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 30813/22-27 was answered on 14 October 2025

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will bring forward the start date of the Initial Teacher Education Bursary Scheme to include PGCE students enrolled in 2025, given the current teacher shortages in post-primary schools.
answer_text: The purpose of the Initial Teacher Education (ITE) bursary scheme, and the basis of both business case approval and the proposed evaluation of the pilot, is to increase the number of both applications and enrolments in ITE in subjects which are experiencing teacher shortages.Extending the scheme to include current students would not increase either applications or enrolments and would not, therefore, be deemed a Value for Money option that would fulfil the objectives of the scheme. It would also incur significant additional costs, which cannot be met from within the Department’s existing 2025/26 budget. The Education Sector is already facing an overcommitment of c£279m in 2025/26 for inescapable pressures only.I understand this is disappointing for those already enrolled.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 31138/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31517/22-27, to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure consistency and accountability in educational provision for pupils recorded as absent due to Emotionally Based School Non-Attendance, given that no central data currently exists on the provision or outcomes for these pupils.
answer_text: Attendance codes as required by DE Policy are now available in the existing and future School Management Systems (SIMS and Bromcom), this includes the introduction of additional codes for Emotionally Based School Non-Attendance and Temporary Exceptions to the Curriculum.  From the start of this academic year, schools can record using these absence codes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the support available for individuals with pituitary conditions, including (i) medications; (ii) therapies; and (iii) public awareness or information initiatives relating to pituitary conditions.
answer_text: Support available for Pituitary conditions is delivered as detailed in the following range of specialities including: Endocrinology, for hormone assessment, diagnosis, and long-term management; Neurosurgery, for surgical removal of pituitary tumours (e.g. transsphenoidal surgery); ENT, often related to supporting endoscopic transsphenoidal approaches; Radiology and Neuroradiology for MRI and CT imaging for diagnosis and surgical planning; Ophthalmology for visual field testing and management of optic nerve compression and Oncology / Radiotherapy for the treatment of residual or recurrent tumours.  Rarer conditions associated with the pituitary gland include rare conditions such as: Craniopharyngioma, Nelson’s Syndrome, Cushing’s disease,  Apoplexy, Autoimmune Hypophysis, Arginine Vasopressin Deficiency (AVP-D) previously called cranial diabetes insipidus which involves a severe water balance disorder due to lack of vasopressin and pregnancy related disorders such as Sheehan’s 	Less rare conditions of pituitary origin include: Prolactinoma, Hypopituitarism, Cushing’s Disease and Acromegaly, Medicines and therapies for pituitary conditions which have been recommended through the NICE Technical Appraisal process are routinely commissioned by the HSC within Northern Ireland.  These will include a range of medications for both specialist and general conditions linked to the treatment or management of pituitary conditions delivered in the specialties and services highlighted above. These can be reviewed on the NICE website via the following link About technology appraisal guidance | NICE.  	With regards to public awareness, there are a number of organisations such as Pituitary Foundation and Pituitary Ireland CLG, which operate an annual UK-wide campaign, supported locally in Northern Ireland to raise awareness of pituitary conditions. Activities include webinars, social media campaigns, and local support events aimed at improving early diagnosis and patient education and awareness days.Locally, the Northern Ireland Pituitary Support Group (linked to Belfast Trust meets every 2–3 months (in-person and online) to provide peer support and educational talks and is supported endocrinology trainees and volunteers.Patients with rare conditions can also engage via the NI Rare Disease Partnership (NIRDP).  NIRDP are members of the Department of Health NI Rare Disease Implementation Group representing the views of the rare disease community.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the protocol for the management of polycystic ovary syndrome, including (i) available medications; and (ii) therapies offered.
answer_text: There is no single protocol for management of polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS).  This is because treatment is individualised and tailored to a woman’s associated symptoms or problems.(ii)	Many women with PCOS have no symptoms apart from a slightly irregular menstrual cycle, while others may require hormone treatment for infrequent periods or excessive hair growth. While there is no cure for PCOS, the symptoms can be treated.  Patients should speak to their GP initially if they have symptoms as medicines are available to treat symptoms such as excessive hair growth, irregular periods and fertility problems.Women who struggle with weight management are offered referral to the dietetics service.  For women who are overweight, losing weight often results in a very significant improvement in their symptoms.  While many women with PCOS have no difficulty conceiving, those women who have problems conceiving, because of very infrequent periods, may require treatment with ovulation induction.  These women can be referred to the Regional Fertility Clinic for a full assessment to include their male partner if they have one.With regards to medications and therapies, those are generally managed and prescribed by colleagues in Primary Care until the patient is trying to conceive.  If there are ongoing fertility challenges, ovulation induction is considered using medication like clomiphene, or letrozole, or follicle stimulating hormone (FSH).  If ovulation induction does not succeed, then referral for in vitro fertilisation (IVF) is progressed in consultation with the patient and based on relevant criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any evidenced benefits of the Accelerated Reader programmes; and (ii) the cost of the programme being implemented.
answer_text: Accelerated Reader is one of a range of products that can be purchased from Renaissance, a company that specialises in education technology and is used by a number of schools across Northern Ireland.  It is not a resource provided by the Department, CCEA or EA and the Department therefore does not hold the information sought on the cost of the programme to individual schools. Literacy is a fundamental foundation of education and pupils must develop strong literacy skills to succeed in school and thrive in life and work.  As part of the TransformED NI Strategy, I have committed to developing a new literacy strategy for Northern Ireland. This strategy will be informed by international best practice, shaped by the needs of pupils in Northern Ireland and built on examples of effective literacy teaching already taking place in our schools.In coming weeks, I will publish new literacy guidance for schools which will provide advice to schools on developing a whole-school strategic approach to reading for pleasure.  This includes the thoughtful use of incentives and an awareness that the points and rewards systems associated with some reading schemes can shift focus from intrinsic to extrinsic motivation, where pupils read for prizes rather than enjoyment. They may also inadvertently lead some pupils to choose shorter or easier books to earn rewards quickly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools with access to Accelerated Reader programmes, broken down by District Electoral Area.
answer_text: Accelerated Reader is one of a range of products that can be purchased from Renaissance, a company that specialises in education technology and is used by a number of schools across Northern Ireland.  It is not a resource provided by the Department, CCEA or EA and the Department therefore does not hold the information sought on the cost of the programme to individual schools. Literacy is a fundamental foundation of education and pupils must develop strong literacy skills to succeed in school and thrive in life and work.  As part of the TransformED NI Strategy, I have committed to developing a new literacy strategy for Northern Ireland. This strategy will be informed by international best practice, shaped by the needs of pupils in Northern Ireland and built on examples of effective literacy teaching already taking place in our schools.In coming weeks, I will publish new literacy guidance for schools which will provide advice to schools on developing a whole-school strategic approach to reading for pleasure.  This includes the thoughtful use of incentives and an awareness that the points and rewards systems associated with some reading schemes can shift focus from intrinsic to extrinsic motivation, where pupils read for prizes rather than enjoyment. They may also inadvertently lead some pupils to choose shorter or easier books to earn rewards quickly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 31834/22-27, to outline why the Northern Ireland Environment Agency does not conduct routine monitoring of pollution at bonfire sites, given these being large-scale public events which generate significant emissions.
answer_text: While NIEA has a valuable role to play in terms of the enforcement of controlled waste and, by extension, any serious environmental risks surrounding bonfires, it does not have the lead role for bonfires, nor is it ‘responsible’ for bonfires.  In line with the DAERA enforcement policy, NIEA assesses each incident on its merits before deciding on the most appropriate action.The Northern Ireland Environment Agency does not routinely monitor bonfire sites primarily due to resource limitations and the scale of bonfire activities across the region. With hundreds of bonfires, it would be logistically and financially challenging for the NIEA to carry out regular, site-specific monitoring.It is the legal responsibility of the landowner to secure their land to prevent illegal depositing of waste.  Where sufficient evidence is available NIEA may conduct criminal investigations in pursuit of offenders responsible for the depositing of illegal waste at bonfire sites.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 29743/22-27, to outline (i) any evidence his Department holds to show that children with special educational needs are receiving equal access to physical education and sport, in practice; and (ii) any enforcement or intervention measures available, should Boards of Governors fail to meet their statutory duties on inclusion.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) carried out an evaluation of Physical Education (PE) in primary schools in 2022.  The ETI found that in the majority of primary schools children are provided with relevant learning experiences to develop their PE skills. This includes those children with special educational needs (SEN). The ETI report highlighted best practice examples, where the school consults with skilled and experienced staff from the local special school to investigate and explore ways of adapting practice to ensure children with specific additional needs can integrate into and participate more fully in PE lessons. ETI have been monitoring the progress in addressing the seven ‘next steps’ identified in the report. A report summarising ETI’s evaluation of progress will be published later in the autumn 2025 academic term.(ii)   All schools have a duty to deliver the statutory curriculum and to identify and meet the needs of pupils with SEN, as set out in the ‘Northern Ireland Code of Practice on the Identification and Assessment of Special Educational Needs’.  Inclusion is a core principle of this and schools are expected to ensure the participation and progress of all pupils, including those with SEN. If an inspection identifies practice which requires support, the Department can apply the interventions set out in its School Improvement policy, Every School a Good School, which includes the Formal Intervention Process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to ensure Translink maintains full public transport provision on the Ulsterbus 115 service between Kilrea, Rasharkin and Ballymena during the closure of the Bann Bridge, given the disruption faced by passengers.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 31834/22-27, what discussions his Department has had with local councils and the Public Health Agency to ensure that any public health risks arising from bonfire emissions are being properly monitored and addressed.
answer_text: Managing annual bonfires is acknowledged as a complex and challenging issue that requires a coordinated, multi-agency preventative approach involving Councils, the Public Health Agency, and, where necessary, enforcement measures.While NIEA has a valuable role to play in terms of the enforcement of controlled waste and, by extension, any serious environmental risks surrounding bonfires, it does not have the lead role for bonfires, nor is it ‘responsible’ for bonfires.  The NI Environment Agency (NIEA) monitors air quality where a statutory responsibility exists.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 30213/22-27, to detail any mechanisms his Department has in place to demonstrate compliance with Article 86 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, for children unable to attend school due to emotionally based school avoidance.
answer_text: The Department does not use the terminology “emotional school avoidance”. It must be recognised that some children do wish to be in school, but their mental health does not permit this. Therefore, the term ‘Emotionally Based School Non Attendance’ (EBSNA) is preferred.The EA’s approach to EBSNA is built on a graduated response. This means support starts with universal strategies that benefit all pupils, such as high-quality teaching and a positive school environment. To help schools manage EBSNA, the Educational Psychology Service (EPS) has developed comprehensive training, which has been well received by schools. This training includes a series of modules and provides advisory support for both staff and parents/carers. The aim is to equip schools and families with the knowledge and tools to identify and address EBSNA early. Where universal strategies are not enough, the approach moves to more targeted interventions tailored to the individual needs of the child. The overall goal is to address issues as soon as they arise, preventing them from becoming entrenched and reducing the risk of long-term absence and negative outcomes for pupils. If a pupil’s needs cannot be met through school-based support alone, the school can make a direct request for involvement to Locality Impact Teams (LITs) and/or the Educational Psychology Service. This is done once the school has gathered evidence of interventions and reviewed progress, and with parental consent demonstrating how the school have applied the graduated response.  Once a request for involvement is agreed and progressed (i.e., accepted by the relevant EA service), the timeline for direct consultation begins. From the point of contact (when the request for involvement is accepted), the EA aims to provide direct consultation (e.g., Educational Psychologist involvement) within 16 weeks.  During this period, schools should continue to implement and review support plans. The approach within the EPS is based on a consultation model, which emphasises collaboration between Educational Psychologists, school staff, parents, and other professionals. This model focuses on jointly understanding the child’s needs and co-producing effective strategies and interventions, ensuring that support is tailored, holistic, and sustainable. Should it become necessary, schools can make application for Exceptional Teaching Arrangements (ETA). ETA is a service that assists schools to deliver education to young people who for medical reasons are temporarily unable to attend school for a period lasting more than 20 days. ETA is a curriculum-based service which offers teaching for 4.5hrs per week from KS1 to GCSE in Maths and English only. It is short-term in nature, and the focus is always on a return to some form of full-time education. It must be emphasised that while ETA may appear a neat solution to the problems associated with EBSA, relevant research clearly indicates that the education of a pupil outside their registered sc

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her decision to reclassify road resurfacing, footpath maintenance, street lighting and staff costs, as active travel expenditure, was subject to (i) consultation with local councils and external stakeholders, such as active travel organisations; and (ii) approval or consideration by the Executive, prior to implementation.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 does not define what constitutes overall transport budgets or spend on active travel, although understanding of the term active travel is generally accepted to mean walking, wheeling or cycling. My department has had to quantify spend on all activities that are carried out for the primary benefit of pedestrians and cyclists in order to measure progress towards compliance. This exercise was not subject to separate formal consultation, nor was further Executive approval required. It reflects my department’s legal obligation under the Act to undertake the exercise. My department does not typically classify road resurfacing as active travel spend. In some cases, it is necessary to alter the width or profile of a road to deliver improvements to active travel infrastructure. In these cases, resurfacing of the carriageway is a necessary consequence of the active travel improvement works and therefore funded from the active travel budget. I am committed to ensuring that our streets are accessible to as many people as possible so that they feel confident to walk, wheel or cycle for many of their shorter journeys at all times of the day. Maintenance of existing active travel infrastructure is an essential element of that, including the staff associated with delivering those activities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 31410/22-27, whether his Department will take steps to begin collating data on the number of people on waiting lists for psychological services that have accessed primary care and secondary care prior to their initial appointment, in order to better understand patterns of demand and inform the planning of mental health provision.
answer_text: The Department of Health in Northern Ireland has not yet committed to collating data on the number of individuals on waiting lists for psychological services who have accessed primary or secondary care prior to their initial appointment. The rollout of the Encompass system, a new electronic patient record system, aims to unify health and social care data across Northern Ireland. This could eventually enable tracking of patient journeys across primary, secondary, and psychological services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail, in light of evidence given to the Education Committee by the Independent Autism Reviewer, (i) any actions his Department is taking to address concerns that hundreds of children are currently on reduced timetables, or are out of school; and (ii) how he plans to ensure information on these pupils is fully and transparently shared with the Education Authority.
answer_text: Schools may place pupils on a reduced timetable for a range of reasons, including welfare concerns, long-term health conditions, or Emotionally Based School Non-Attendance. These decisions should be based on individual circumstances and aim to support the child’s wellbeing and engagement with education.To support schools in managing reduced timetables appropriately, the Department has issued guidance through Circular 2025/09 . This sets out that flexible timetables should only be used in exceptional circumstances, for a limited period, and with clear plans to help pupils return to full-time education. Decisions must be agreed with parents, reviewed regularly, and recorded clearly to support safeguarding and data monitoring.Alongside this guidance, my Department is working with the Education Authority (EA) to improve how pupil attendance data is recorded. A detailed implementation plan for the EdIS programme is being finalised, with the aim of transitioning schools to the Bromcom system by the 2026/27 academic year. While schools are advised to record ‘Flexible Timetable’ in School Management System notes, this data is not captured in the Department’s monthly C2K reports. A separate data collection exercise would be needed to gather this information centrally.Schools are not required to inform the EA when placing a pupil on a flexible timetable. Progress dates should be agreed with parents or carers, and many children’s needs can be met within their current educational setting without EA involvement. However, where there are concerns about attendance, schools should refer the pupil to the Education Welfare Service (EWS), which works with families to support a return to full-time education. In recognition of the challenges surrounding attendance, the EWS is recruiting 16 specialist posts to strengthen support for children and young people and help ensure they can participate fully in education.Where a pupil’s needs cannot be met within their school, a referral can be made to the EA’s Exceptional Teaching Arrangements service. This short-term, curriculum-based provision offers 4.5 hours of weekly Maths and English teaching for pupils from Key Stage 1 to GCSE who are medically unable to attend school for more than 20 days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that all schools adopt a whole-school approach to emotional wellbeing.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s Being Well Doing Well programme, funded under the joint Department of Education / Department of Health Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework, aims to support all schools to develop a Whole School Approach to Emotional Health and Wellbeing.  Further information can be found on the Education Authority website (Being Well Doing Well Programme | Education Authority Northern Ireland).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the reason why the £100 million allocation for new social housing in 2025-26, presented in January as additional to his Department’s capital budget, is now recorded within the Department’s existing allocation; and (ii) the number of new social homes will be delivered as a result.
answer_text: The Executive agreed to access an additional £100m of borrowing under the Reform and Reinvestment Initiative. This was allocated to DfC as part of its opening budget and was earmarked by DoF for Social Housing.		The £100M has gone directly towards the £105.5m opening commitment required to continue construction for homes already on site.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any assessment he has made of the effectiveness of the Arts Council’s mechanisms to withhold or withdraw funding through the Musical Instruments Scheme if an applicant organisation does not meet its equality of opportunity and good relations duty.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer AQW 30680/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 30680/22-27 on 17 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any evaluation his Department has carried out of international models of digital access to GPs, including video consultations.
answer_text: My Department has not commissioned an evaluation of international models of digital access to GPs, including video consultations. All GP practices within Northern Ireland are equipped to provide video consultations but it is up to individual GPs to decide the most appropriate method of consulting with their registered patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how his Department will measure the effectiveness of the new requirement for GP practices to establish patient participation groups; and (ii) whether patients will be able to directly report persistent access difficulties to his Department.
answer_text: Access is an integral part of the overall GMS contact for 2025/2026. As part of their core GMS contract GP practices in Northern Ireland are required to provide assurance by 30 April 2026 that they have formed a Patient Participation Group (PPG) which has met at least biannually. Adherence to this and other measures is monitored by my Department through the usual contract management processes.Patient participation groups is not a vehicle for individuals to voice personal interests nor resolve personal issues. Rather, it aims to bring about positive change to the benefit of all patients and practice staff. In line with legislative requirements, any complaints/issues regarding a GP Practice should be reported in the first instance to the GP Practice directly to ensure they are aware of the problem. In addition, patients currently highlight persistent access difficulties through my Private Office and these are directed to appropriate Departmental officials. This channel still remains open.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail a timeline for the introduction of statutory guidance on children missing education.
answer_text: My Department is currently reviewing whether there are any gaps in legislation and is working to develop clear and comprehensive guidance on how to support Children Missing in Education (CME).Tackling CME effectively requires collaboration between different organisations.  Officials are working closely with colleagues in the Department of Health and the Education Authority to improve how information is shared and how services coordinate their efforts.Work is underway to examine how other parts of the UK approach CME, to learn from best practice and inform our local response. At the same time, officials are considering what data is needed to better understand and address the issue. Given the complexity of this work and the need for multi-agency collaboration, it is not yet possible to provide a clear timeline for the introduction of statutory guidance.  However, this remains a priority for my Department, and work is progressing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assessment he has made of the effectiveness of the new access improvement measures in the 2025-26 general medical services contract, particularly in reducing the number of patients being asked to call back multiple times before securing a GP appointment.
answer_text: Access is an integral part of the overall GMS contract for 2025/26.The access measures do not seek to increase the number of consultations delivered, or make GPs work to unsafe levels. Rather, they are aimed to create small but significant improvements to the patient experience of access, to remove some of the friction points for patients that can make the experience worse for patients and practices alike.  The Northern Ireland Assurance Framework (NICAF) introduced a range of new requirements aimed at improving the patient experience of accessing services. To allow time for these measures to bed in, under the terms of the NICAF, practices are required to provide evidence or assurance of compliance with the requirements stipulated under Access by 30 April 2026. On that basis, it is not possible at this time to provide a comprehensive record of performance to date against these measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has engaged in any formal cooperation or dialogue with the Irish Government or the European Environment Agency in relation to the September 2025 report Europe's Environment 2025; and (ii) whether there are plans to coordinate environmental responses at an all-island level.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the Europe's Environment 2025 report and is considering its contents.  I and my officials will continue co-operation on environment issues via established arrangements, such as NSMC and BIC.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of emotional support dogs housed within Northern Ireland prisons in each of the last ten years; (ii) the number of incidents of abuse or harm towards these dogs reported in each of the last ten years; (iii) the number of emotional support dogs that have been euthanised due to poor health or trauma linked to their treatment in prison, and (iv) the steps her Department is taking to address these issues.
answer_text: One emotional support dog has been used in Maghaberry from 2025; two have been used in Hydebank Wood College each year from 2015; and one dog has been deployed in Magilligan from 2021 to 2025, together with an additional dog that was deployed for a short period in 2025.There have been no reported incidents of abuse or harm towards these dogs in each of the last ten years.  One near miss incident occurred at Magilligan Prison when, whilst being walked by a member of staff and a prisoner, a raw egg was thrown in the direction of the emotional support dog and staff.  The egg did not make contact with either.No emotional support dogs have been euthanised due to poor health or trauma linked to their treatment in prison.The use of emotional support dogs by the Northern Ireland Prison Service is an operational matter for the Service.  As there are no concerns around their deployment or care, my Department does not propose to take any action.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 31614/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 31614/22-27 was answered on 19 November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps he plans to take in Northern Ireland, or with the Department for Work and Pensions, to address the issue whereby Universal Credit claimants paid every four weeks receive two payments within a single monthly assessment period, leading to reduced or nil awards and financial hardship.
answer_text: Universal Credit assessment periods and payment structures in Northern Ireland are maintained in parity with the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) in Great Britian.DWP has committed to reviewing Universal Credit, to make sure it is doing the job the UK Government wants it to, to make work pay and tackle poverty. DWP is aware that Universal Credit’s monthly assessment period doesn’t suit household circumstances in all cases and has advised that it is meeting with expert stakeholders to explore this as part of the review.  My Department will continue to engage with DWP as this work progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider ring-fencing contingency funds for the Affordable Warmth Scheme.
answer_text: As a Minister, I am unable to ring fence normal Capital Departmental Expenditure Limits. It is the power of the Northern Ireland Executive to ringfence funding for a specific purpose.I provided an additional allocation of £2.5m from the Department’s opening budget to bring the total Affordable Warmth Scheme allocation to £7.5m in this financial year and will continue to press for additional funding in this financial year for this important scheme through the monitoring round process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31047/22-27, whether he will urgently intervene to address the ongoing uncertainty regarding the permanent site and capital funding for Causeway Academy, in light of concerns that the lack of clarity is already impacting staff retention, enrolment projections, and the ability to issue permanent contracts.
answer_text: Causeway Academy will open in September 2026 across three existing sites. The Education Authority is currently working with the Principal Designate on the configuration of the three sites. When this has been confirmed, the Principal Designate will issue relevant communications, by newsletter and social media, to relevant stakeholders.Whilst a new school formed and operating on a split site following an amalgamation would be well placed to receive funding for a new build in a future major capital call, there can be no guarantees that this would be the case. Given the constrained capital budget position, I cannot confirm when I will be in a position to make a new call for major works projects. I will continue to make the case for increased and sustained investment in education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to clarify the process for initiating a special educational needs statutory assessment, including (i) whether a referral for statutory assessment must be made by a community paediatrician, or whether parents, schools, educational psychologists, or other professionals are equally entitled to make such a request; (ii) whether the Education Authority is legally required to complete the statutory assessment process within the 26 week timeline, except in cases where the formal exceptions set out in The Education (Special Educational Needs) Regulations (Northern Irel
answer_text: A Special Educational Needs (SEN) Statutory Assessment can be made via an online portal on the Education Authority website.(i)The Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996 sets out the legislation in relation to statutory assessments.  Article 14 places a duty on Health and Social Services boards to bring any children under statutory school age to the attention of the Education Authority (EA). Parents and schools can request statutory assessment (Articles 20 and 20A).  Educational Psychologists can also bring children to the attention of the statutory assessment and review service for consideration of a statutory assessment (Article 15).(ii) The Education (SEN) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005 set out the legislative timeframes and exceptions in detail.  The totality of decision points is 26 weeks unless exceptions are applied.(iii) The data for statutory assessment is held by EA and is monitored by the department on a monthly basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on the performance of Northern Ireland’s tourism sector over the summer of 2025, including (a) visitor numbers; and (b) hotel occupancy rates; (ii) for a breakdown of those visitors from (a) Northern Ireland; (b) the Republic of Ireland; (c) Great Britain; and (d) overseas; and (iii) to provide comparable figures, for each of the previous five years.
answer_text: NISRA does not release visitor numbers or hotel occupancy rates specifically for the summer period. The most up to date 2025 visitor figures published by NISRA relate to January-March 2025.  NISRA anticipate that tourism performance statistics, which would cover the summer months, will be published for Quarter 2 (April-June) on 6 November 2025 with Quarter 3 (July-September) available early 2026.    Hotel room and bed occupancy rates released by NISRA for the months of June, July, August and September are presented in Tables 1 and 2 below.Table 1: Hotel Room Occupancy Rates  202020212022202320242025JuneN/A57%77%81%75%78%July30%62%75%76%75%65%August57%78%77%78%78%71%September41%66%76%69%75%N/ASource: NISRATable 2: Hotel Bed Occupancy Rates  202020212022202320242025JuneN/A49%58%64%60%59%July25%60%64%61%64%58%August52%74%64%66%65%66%September33%58%55%59%59%N/ASource: NISRANISRA is unable to provide  a breakdown of where those staying in hotels over the summer period are from due to small sample sizes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs pursuant to AQW 30560/22-27, (i) to detail any assessment his Department has made of the approximately 70 per cent decline in farmer participation in forestry grant schemes over the last three years; and (ii) to detail any steps being taken to address this trend.
answer_text: Despite a reduction in applicants in the last three years the area of new woodland planted with forestry grant scheme support during this time has remained generally consistent in each year 451, 433 and 427 hectares respectively. This winter, planting of new woodland is being supported by the Forest Expansion Scheme, the Small Woodland Grant Scheme and also the tree planting option under the Farming with Nature scheme. However, it is widely recognised that the rate of tree planting and woodland creation needs to increase substantially to meet afforestation policy objectives and deliver Climate Action Plan tree planting targets in future years. The Department is engaging with stakeholders and exploring options for future support schemes to encourage greater interest from land owners to incorporate new woodlands within their business activities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount of funding from the Shared Island Enterprise Scheme or related cross-border economic programmes, allocated specifically to the North Coast region.
answer_text: Under the Shared Island Enterprise scheme, Invest NI delivered the Shared Island Sustainability Capital Grant which was launched in February 2025 and remained open until May 2025. The grant targeted sustainable practices in industrial and manufacturing sectors and was promoted to all Invest NI clients. Invest NI received no expressions of interest or applications from the North Coast Region, so no funding was allocated to businesses in that region.  Invest NI has approved three projects west of the Bann, with funding announcements to follow once legal agreements are finalised.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will AQW 29046/22-27.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 30813/22-27.
answer_text: AQW30813/22-27 was answered on 14 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 30560/22-27, (i) how the current rate of new woodland creation compares with the targets set under the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022; and (ii) whether his Department is on track to meet these targets.
answer_text: he target, for woodland creation modelled within the draft Climate Action Plan (CAP) is 2431 hectares (net) of new woodland in the 2023 – 2027 period. To date, 1386 hectares (57%) has been planted in the budget period. The remaining 1045 hectares needs to be achieved by the end of the budget period to meet the target. My Department is committed to delivering the targets set in the draft CAP and is actively working with stakeholders to consider options to further increase the rate of afforestation over subsequent carbon budget periods.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 30563/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 30563/22-27 was answered on 30 September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any plans in place to address the key capital and staffing pressures identified in the scoping study on the standardisation of pre-school provision, including the need for additional (a) dining space, (b) administration time, and (c) staff numbers; and (ii) how the Department will ensure that non-statutory providers are sufficiently supported to meet these requirements and transition to full-time provision within the proposed timescales.
answer_text: I published the final report of the Standardisation of the Pre-School Education Programme Scoping Study and wrote to all MLAs on 13 June 2024 about its contents.  In addition to the core objective of providing all children with 22.5 hours per week of pre-school education, the Scoping Study report identifies and costs a range of potential additional actions, including in relation to pre-school staffing. The scale of what I can do in implementing options contained in the report will be determined by the budget allocated by the Executive.
