My Ministerial Statement of 16 May 2025 on Early Learning and Childcare measures provided details of the package of measures within the £55m budget allocated in the current financial year, of which £12m was assigned for pre-school standardisation. This will allow me to continue the process of transition, and I intend to have approximately 2,200 additional 22.5-hour places available for September 2026. Consultation on the first tranche of potential settings to transition by that date began on 25 September.   This tranche comprises 23 non-statutory and 10 statutory settings, which, if approved, will be supported by the Education Authority to meet the requirements to transition to full-time provision by September 2026. I have also approved a new funding model, effective from 1st September 2025, which includes increased funding rates for non-statutory providers participating in the Pre-School Education Programme.  The model is intended to facilitate the approved session length for each setting, plus paid release time for activities such as planning, administration and training as well as overheads such as rent, utilities and consumables.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline (i) what the average response time is for the NI Environment Agency to investigate reported pollution incidents; and (ii) whether additional resources have been allocated to improve enforcement and monitoring capacity.
answer_text: The NIEA targets response within 2 hours (in office hours) and 4 hours (out of hours) for high and medium severity water pollution incidents, and within 14 days for low severity incidents. The NIEA met its 90% response target in 2024 and 2025 to date.The NIEA has recruited 17 additional staff to its Water Management Unit to improve the capacity for water quality enforcement and monitoring.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the 63 fines issued between 2020 and 2024 for water pollution, (i) for a breakdown of the severity classification of the incident as (a) high; (b) medium; and (c) low; and (ii) to detail what enforcement action was taken in the remaining 4,139 cases of confirmed water pollution.
answer_text: High: 16; Medium: 47; Low: 0. These 63 fines reflect court outcome dates and may relate to pollution incidents before 2020.Department records verify 4,204 confirmed water pollution incidents between 2020 and 2024, with 383 meeting the criteria for formal enforcement action under the department enforcement policy. Actions included prosecutions, cautions, warning letters, enforcement notices, cross-compliance breaches, and advisory letters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) what mandatory medical or first aid training is required for staff working in hostels accommodating homeless people and asylum seekers; (ii) the number of reported medical incidents in such hostels in the past three years; and (iii) what procedures are in place for recording and responding to these incidents.
answer_text: AnswerThis issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Any accommodation provided to asylum seekers is the responsibility of the Home Office.Independent organisations providing accommodation-based services for people experiencing homelessness via the Supporting People (SP) programme are responsible for their own staff training. First aid training is available for all Housing Executive staff, including those that manage Housing Executive owned hostels. Attendance is determined by completion of a first aid needs assessment by the Premises Officer / Line Manager, to establish the number of trained staff required and courses to be attended.A Major Adverse Incident (MAI) Framework is in place to outline information management arrangements regarding MAIs that must be reported to SP by providers. The table below shows the MAIs reported by SP providers operating homeless hostels in the last 3 years.2022/23 - 2024/25MAI NatureNumber of reported MAIs Assault - Physical25Assault - Serious5Assault - Sexual13Assault - Staff10Attempted Suicide16Damage to Property6Death - Drugs9Death - Suicide7Death - Unexpected26Drugs - Non-Fatal Overdose17Drugs Related6Financial Abuse1Fire5Ill health20Injury - Damage/loss of function3Injury - Permanent2Missing Person7Reputational Damage4Security Breach3Self Harm5Staff14Grand Total204The Housing Executive’s Incident and Reporting Policy includes mandatory incident reporting and investigation training for all People Managers, with relevant forms available on the staff internet. Three incidents were reported within Housing Executive owned hostels between 22/23 and 23/24.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action is being taken to improve early diagnosis, support, and long-term care pathways for children affected by fetal alcohol spectrum disorder.
answer_text: Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorder (FASD) is an umbrella term describing a range of physical, behavioural, and cognitive conditions that can occur in individuals exposed to alcohol before birth.This is a disorder that can present across a number of disciplines within paediatrics.  It could be noted at the time of birth right through to an assessment by a health visitor or midwife who is attending the child in the first few weeks after birth.This is a broad disorder that could require a wide range of input from a varied group of clinical professionals.  For example, a child with Foetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorder (FASD), depending on the individual symptoms may be cared for by community paediatrics, acute paediatrics or a range of other professionals.  There is no specific test for FASD so diagnosis can only be made after a number of observations have been made by the clinicians involved.  Once a definitive diagnosis is made, again depending on the specific symptoms, a longer-term treatment and care plan can be agreed.  Clinicians are aware of the signs and symptoms of this disorder and ensure that it is part of their early clinical considerations when they assess a child with potential FASD.FASD is completely preventable and I would urge any individual who is pregnant, trying to become pregnant or could become pregnant to avoid alcohol completely as even small amounts of alcohol can pose a risk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps being taken to ensure that recipients of teacher training bursaries remain in the teaching profession in Northern Ireland beyond the minimum two-year post-qualification period.
answer_text: The vision outlined in TransformED is of teaching in Northern Ireland as an attractive, high-status profession where every teacher receives world-class professional learning and development. In realising this, my Department is constantly striving to improve the terms and conditions for teachers alongside the development and implementation of an enhanced professional learning offer. On the terms and conditions, the starting salary for teachers has increased to £31,650 (with a pay award for 2025 under consideration) and I have commissioned a review of teacher workload in response to the specific concerns of teachers and school leaders. Professional learning support for all teachers, at every stage of their career is being reviewed and expanded. Good quality professional learning empowers teachers with relevant skills, confidence, and support, helping them feel valued and professionally fulfilled. This sense of growth and belonging significantly reduces burnout and increases retention in the profession.This investment to date includes an updated and expanded Teacher Induction Programme for newly qualified teachers; broader access to education research through the Making Best Practice, Common Practice programme and a partnership with the Chartered College of Teaching for access to their programmes; £31 million in additional funding for schools to purchase high-quality professional learning through the Teacher Professional Learning Fund; and access to specialist professional learning provision including the Science of Learning CPD Academy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline (i) how toxic blue-green algae was able to reach red alert levels at Portstewart Strand; and (ii)  the immediate measures being taken to address the level of existing nutrient management and pollution control strategies in designated bathing waters.
answer_text: High rainfall increases the movement of blue green algae from Lough Neagh to the River Bann and subsequently the North Coast.  Occurrences on the North Coast beaches, including Portstewart Strand, depend on the mass of blue green algae flowing from the Bann, tidal conditions and the prevalent direction of the wind. 		Immediate measures being taken by my Department aim to protect public health by continued implementation of the Interagency Blue Green Algae Monitoring Protocol outside of the official bathing seasons at sites susceptible to blue green algae.  This ensures coordinated action and communication between public bodies to ensure affected sites display “advice against bathing” notices. Site updates are available online via the NI Bathing Water Quality Dashboard.	My Department continues to work with other government bodies and stakeholders to reduce nutrient inputs and pollutants through Initiatives like the Lough Neagh Action Plan, Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) and catchment-based strategies under River Basin Management Plans. Further information on water quality improvement at Bathing Waters is in the Better Beaches Forum Action Plan available via the DAERA website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 30684/22-27, to detail the urgent steps she is taking with the PSNI to address the scale of the arrest warrant backlog and to restore confidence in the enforcement of justice.
answer_text: The enforcement of arrest warrants is an operational matter for the PSNI which is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board for such matters. As I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the PSNI and the oversight role of Policing Board, it would not be appropriate for me to comment.  You may, therefore, wish to raise this matter directly with the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the steps she is taking, in light of the findings of the McCullough Review, to (i) strengthen legal oversight of covert surveillance authorisations involving journalists; (ii) support the referral of PSNI actions to the Information Commissioner; and (iii) establish an independent public inquiry into breaches of press freedom.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of the issues raised in the McCullough Review particularly in relation to the protection of press freedom and the oversight of covert surveillance authorisations involving journalists.   (i) While the legislation governing surveillance authorisation is largely reserved and/or excepted, I note that the Chief Constable has acknowledged that the Review identified concerns in relation to specific authorisations and has committed to improving processes and addressing inconsistencies.I also note that the NI Policing Board reviewed Covert Surveillance as a key area in its 17th Human Rights Annual Report 2024-25. The Policing Board has recommended that PSNI should provide a report to the Board every year on its use of covert surveillance powersPSNI has already enhanced the central record of surveillance authorisations to provide greater transparency to Investigatory Powers Commissioner’s Office inspections.(ii) On the referral to the Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO), this is a matter for the Chief Constable.  However, I understand that he has already informed the ICO.  My Department will support the process where appropriate.(iii) While the McCullough Review found no evidence of systemic surveillance of journalists, it did not amount to a clean bill of health, with several findings of poor or unlawful practice.  This is not solely a matter of operational policing, for which the Chief Constable is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. The Policing Board has stated that it views the Report as part of fulfilling its duties under Section 59 of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 2000.  The Board has made clear it wishes to carefully review the findings of the McCullough Review, and to receive a formal response from the Chief Constable on remedial action and plans for implementing the recommendations, before deciding on next steps. I believe it is important that the Board is given the time and space to fully consider the report and its preferred way forward. As I have said previously, I share the serious concerns which have been expressed by the Board in relation to this issue and stand ready to support the Board in whatever actions it considers necessary.  In addition, the Investigatory Powers Tribunal has yet to report, after which a further update to McCullough will be made. Ahead of the Tribunal findings and the Board’s considerations, it would be premature to comment on what actions may be required on my part.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how the Executive Office can present a coherent and effective approach to international diplomacy and economic engagement when its joint officeholders adopt publicly divergent positions on high-profile events such as the state banquet for President Trump.
answer_text: Our clear priority for international engagement is to promote NI as a place where people want to live, to visit, to study and invest.Our track record in international engagements shows that we have provided a platform through our joint engagements to showcase what we have to offer to the representatives of the largest economies in the world.  We will continue to engage with international partners to showcase what we have to offer in respect of our economy, tourist attractions and people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant of AQW 30214/22-27, in light of recent reports that fewer than 10 per cent of businesses in Northern Ireland currently offer apprenticeship placements, to detail what assessment she has made of the effectiveness of current financial incentives for employers to take on apprentices.
answer_text: Under the ApprenticeshipsNI programme, businesses are eligible for an employer incentive payment upon the successful achievement of Level 2 and Level 3 apprenticeship frameworks, ranging from £670 to £2,008 depending on the occupational area.  This is a 20% increase under ApprenticeshipsNI 2025 compared to the previous ApprenticeshipsNI 2021 programme.  In addition, the Construction Industry Training Board (CITB NI), offers an Employer Apprentice Grant to further support employers in the construction sector.		My Department’s ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher level Apprenticeship programmes are witnessing increasing numbers with participation on both programmes at an all time high.  For example, the most recently published statistics show that total starts on the ApprenticeshipsNI programme increased by 70% between 2019/20 and 2023/24 and by 48% between 2022/23 and 2023/24.  		This suggests that many employers are embracing apprenticeships, however I want more employers to recognise that investing in an apprentice is of longer term beneft to the business.  The Apprenticeship Action Plan recogises the need to encourage more employers to consider recruiting apprentices and includes deliverables such as piloting Apprenticeship Navigators to support SMEs employing their first apprentices and commissioning research to quantify the financial impact of the apprenticeship system on individual businesses and the economy.		In line with all public expenditure, apprenticeship programmes are subject to post project evaluations in order to help inform future policy design.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 30810/22-27, whether his Department classifies all eleventh of July bonfires as illegal waste activities.
answer_text: The deposit and burning of controlled waste at a bonfire site has the potential to be in breach of The Waste and Contaminated Land (NI) Order 1997.The Department, through its Executive Agency NIEA, is an enforcing authority under the 1997 Order. The Agency exercises discretion on how it applies its powers under the 1997 Order, in line with DAERA’s enforcement policy. Local Councils are also an enforcing authority under the 1997 Order and have equivalent powers to NIEA. In line with the DAERA enforcement policy, NIEA assesses each incident on its merits before deciding on the most appropriate action.It is the legal responsibility of the landowner to secure their land to prevent illegal depositing of waste.  Where sufficient evidence is available NIEA may conduct criminal investigations in pursuit of offenders responsible for the depositing of illegal waste at bonfire sites.  If the offenders responsible cannot be identified, it is the legal responsibility of the landowner to remove any waste deposited.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant of AQW 30810/22-27, in the absence of routine monitoring, how any risks to public health can be assessed and mitigated.
answer_text: Whilst my Department does not carry out specific monitoring in the vicinity of bonfire sites, ambient air quality for a variety of pollutants is monitored at 23 sites across Northern Ireland. The monitoring network is also supplemented by air quality modelling through the national atmospheric emissions inventory, which calculates and reports on the quantity of pollutants that are emitted to air. This provides a key tool for officials to identify public health impacts and informs policymaking.To further protect health, the public can be informed about high air pollution episodes via the Northern Ireland Air app and the DAERA/Department of Health Air Aware SMS service.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking (i) to monitor and respond to medical disinformation, including recent claims linking paracetamol use during pregnancy to autism; and (ii) to safeguard public confidence in evidence-based healthcare advice.
answer_text: In response to recent claims linking paracetamol use during pregnancy to autism, the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) has confirmed that there is no evidence that taking paracetamol during pregnancy causes autism in children.  MHRA continuously monitors the safety of medicines, including those used during pregnancy, through robust monitoring and surveillance. All regulatory advice on medicines is based on rigorous assessment of the best available scientific evidence. In my address to the Assembly on Tuesday 23 September, I rebutted the alleged link between paracetamol and autism. I noted that the independent autism reviewer supported this statement. My address was shared on the Department’s social media, as well as the video address by the MHRA’s Chief Safety Officer, Dr Alison Cave, where she noted there is no evidence that taking paracetamol during pregnancy causes autism in children.As a measure to safeguard public confidence in evidence-based healthcare advice, my Department cascades Drug Safety Alerts and Drug Safety Updates issued by the MHRA, to communicate safety messages to healthcare professionals to support the safer use of medicines and healthcare products by highlighting new risks, providing guidance for action, and ensuring patient safety. My Department circulated the MHRA’s Drug Safety Update Paracetamol and pregnancy – reminder that taking paracetamol during pregnancy remains safe, to the HSC by way of a HSS MD 33 2025 letter available  via the Department’s letters and urgent communications webpage at the following link https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/letters-and-urgent-communications-2025 on 24 September, advising that taking paracetamol during pregnancy remains safe. Healthcare professionals are asked to note and action the advice for healthcare professionals, advice for healthcare professionals to provide to patients, and recommendations contained within the Drug Safety Update. The MHRA has comprehensive systems including the Yellow Card Scheme for reporting suspected side effects, analysis of healthcare databases, and review of international evidence. The Yellow Card scheme safeguards medical products quality and efficacy in the UK. Anyone including patients, healthcare professionals and members of the public can report an issue with a medicine, vaccine, medical device, blood product or e-cigarette to the Yellow Card scheme. Reports are vital for identifying new safety issues and making healthcare products safer for everyone. Paracetamol remains the recommended pain relief option for people who are pregnant when used at the lowest dose and for the shortest duration. Untreated pain and fever can pose risks to the unborn baby, so it is important to manage these symptoms with the recommended treatment.People who are pregnant are advised that they should continue to follow existing HSC guidance and speak to their healthcare professional if they have questions about any medication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 30452/22-27, in relation to her Department’s responsibilities under the Substance Use Strategy, to detail (i) the number of harm-reduction actions that have been fully implemented to date; and (ii) the measures being taken to address delays in the implementation of any outstanding actions.
answer_text: Within the Substance Use Strategy, my Department has responsibility for the consideration of the further roll out of Problem-Solving Justice initiatives across Northern Ireland. Under this action, as at June 2025, the Probation Board (PBNI) has expanded the availability of Enhanced Combination Orders to courts in the North Antrim area and is currently working on an evaluation of the Substance Misuse Court (SMC) which will inform any decisions regarding wider access to SMCs. Community Resolution Notices for drug possession and drug-related offences are an operational matter for the Chief Constable.A number of other harm reduction actions are currently also being taken forward and considered to be ongoing under the Substance Use Strategy Action Plan, in partnership with other statutory partners, including DoH, the Public Health Agency and the PSNI. This includes ensuring fewer people are at risk of harm through the facilitation of the Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF) Drugs Subgroup, which is chaired by the PSNI, and which brings together agencies on a strategic basis to ensure they are collectively, as well as individually, alive to emerging threats. The Drugs Subgroup meets on a quarterly basis to co-ordinate enforcement activity and ensure that those involved in the illicit supply and distribution of drugs are targeted. Drug related deaths is a standing agenda item at each of the quarterly meetings.My Department is committed to working in collaboration with other partners across government and beyond in delivery of these actions and the broader strategy. This commitment is delivered through the Substance Use Strategy Programme Board, chaired by DoH, which supports the oversight and implementation of the 10-year strategy and associated Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline (i) how his Department defines early intervention in relation to Emotionally Based School Avoidance; and (ii) the typical timeline from first referral to support being provided in cases involving neurodiverse pupils.
answer_text: To help schools manage Emotionally Based School Non-Attendance (EBSNA), the Education Authority’s Educational Psychology Service (EPS) has developed comprehensive training, which has been well received by schools.The Education Authority (EA) and associated guidance define early intervention for EBSNA as the process of identifying and addressing the emotional, social, and environmental factors that contribute to a pupil’s difficulty in attending school as soon as concerns arise. Early intervention is recognised as the most effective approach to preventing escalation and supporting positive outcomes. For neurodiverse children with SEN (including those with Autism, ADHD, and other neurodevelopmental differences), early intervention involves:Prompt identification of risk factors and signs of EBSNA, such as increased anxiety, changes in behaviour, or patterns of absence.Collaboration between school staff, parents/carers, and the young person to gather information and understand the underlying causes.Assessment of both risk and resilience factors, considering the unique needs of neurodiverse pupils.Implementation of tailored support—this may include reasonable adjustments to the school environment, differentiated learning approaches, and targeted interventions to build emotional literacy, resilience, and self-regulation skills.If a pupil’s needs cannot be met through school-based support alone, the school can make a direct request for involvement to Locality Impact Teams (LITs) and/or the EPS. This is done once the school has gathered evidence of interventions and reviewed progress, and with parental consent demonstrating how the school have applied the graduated response.  Once a request for involvement is agreed and progressed (i.e., accepted by the relevant EA service), the timeline for direct consultation begins. From the point of contact (when the request for involvement is accepted), the EA aims to provide direct consultation (e.g., Educational Psychologist involvement) within 16 weeks.  During this period, schools should continue to implement and review support plans. The approach within the EPS is based on a consultation model, which emphasises collaboration between Educational Psychologists, school staff, parents, and other professionals. This model focuses on jointly understanding the child’s needs and co-producing effective strategies and interventions, ensuring that support is tailored, holistic, and sustainable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of outstanding fine warrants; (ii) the data held on individuals with multiple outstanding warrants; and (ii) how these cases are managed.
answer_text: At 1 September 2025 there were 781 live warrants in respect of outstanding fines.  (ii) 	The warrants were issued in respect individuals and companies, 114 of whom have multiple outstanding warrants. The data held by the Northern Ireland Courts & Tribunals Service (NICTS) is based on the information captured by the prosecuting authority and brought before the court.  This information is obtained from the live Integrated Court Operations System (ICOS) and should be treated as management information rather than official statistics.(iii) 	Warrants are passed to the Police Service of Northern Ireland for execution. Warrants must be executed within one year of their issue, following which, the reasons for non-execution and a recommendation regarding re-issue or remittance is forwarded to the relevant court to be considered by a Judge.Following a Public Accounts Committee recommendation in 2015, the Department of Justice and NICTS introduced the Fine Collection and Enforcement Service (FCS) on 1 June 2018. Fines which are unpaid by the due date set by the court are passed to FCS for recovery and collection. FCS have a number of enforcement powers such as granting time to pay, making deductions from benefits or earnings, making interim bank account orders and vehicle seizure orders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant of AQW 29741/22-27, to provide a breakdown of (i) the number of applications (a) received; and (b) approved; (ii) the total area of land reforested; (iii) the total funding allocated under (a) the Forest Expansion Scheme; (b) the Small Woodland Grant Scheme; and (c) the Woodland Investment Grant, in each of the last three years, broken down by constituency; and (iv) what assessment has been made of the effectiveness of these schemes in increasing tree cover on private land.
answer_text: Details of grant applications submitted, approved and associated funding allocation is detailed by individual grant scheme below over the three year period.Forest Expansion SchemeApplications submittedSuccessful applicationsFunding allocated (£k)2022/2334331,7652023/2429252,5672024/2524181,055Small Woodland Grant SchemeApplications submittedSuccessful applicationsFunding allocated(£k)2022/232091721,3122023/241731005552024/259743739Woodland Investment GrantApplications submittedSuccessful applicationsFunding allocated(£k)2022/231716822023/241211292024/258871The areas of new woodland established over the last three years are 451, 433 and 502 hectares respectively against a target of 600 hectares annually. A breakdown by Assembly Area is provided in the table below. It is widely recognised that annual tree planting targets need to increase significantly in future years. and the Department is currently considering options with stakeholders to increase the rate of afforestation and deliver significant increased afforestation in carbon budget period 2028-32.Assembly AreaForestry grant funded(hectares)Forest Service (hectares)East Antrim174East Belfast0East L/Derry11423Foyle99Lagan Valley74Mid Ulster48Newry & Mourne307North Antrim101North Belfast6North Down3South Antrim66South Down155South Tyrone & Fermanagh22745Strangford71Upper Bann17West Belfast15West Tyrone111
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the expected timelines for the (i) temporary; and (ii) permanent, remedial works to the Kilrea Bridge, Derry, including what measures are being taken to minimise disruption for local residents and road users during the closure.
answer_text: The Kilrea Bridge was closed on 20 September in the interest of public safety due to extensive cracking on the retaining wall located at the south-west end of the Bridge. My Department is currently working at pace to develop a scheme to repair the wall and is assessing if a temporary solution can be implemented in the interim to allow the road to be reopened as quickly as possible, while a permanent solution is developed. Regrettably, I am currently not able to provide a timeframe at this stage. A signed diversionary route is currently in place. However, I fully appreciate the impact of the closure and the understandable frustration of the local community, including businesses in the area and I apologise for the inconvenience caused.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any measures he is taking to increase girls’ participation in sport.
answer_text: My department recommends that schools should provide all children and young people with at least two hours of good-quality curricular Physical Education each week. This is designed to develop physically literate individuals who have the knowledge, skills and confidence to enjoy a lifetime of healthful physical activity.In collaboration with Education Authority and Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment, my department has also recently produced resources for teachers, and PE co-ordinators which focus on effective practice and enhancement of PE delivery.Schools have the autonomy to engage with a range of external organisations and initiatives that promote girls’ participation in sport and physical activity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department will take to fill the information gap left by the removal of the 2017 LGBT pupils’ research, recognising the importance of such data in understanding and addressing bullying and discrimination in schools.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, requires a Board of Governors of the school to determine the measures pursued at the school to prevent, respond to and record all incidents of alleged and/or confirmed bullying type behaviour involving registered pupils. Officials continue to work closely with colleagues within the Education Authority (EA) Child Protection Support Service and the Addressing Bullying in Schools Teams, to ensure that relevant system level data is captured as the EA move forward with the implementation of the Education Information Solutions (EdIS) digital change programme. Alongside this, the continued roll out of inspections using the Empowering Improvement Framework will also play an important role in monitoring schools’ arrangements for safeguarding, pastoral support and promoting a positive approach to pupil wellbeing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will take steps to establish an independent animal welfare commission and commissioner, similar to the Scottish model, to provide expert guidance, improve coordination between public bodies and strengthen efforts to prevent and respond to animal abuse.
answer_text: While I recognise the merit of this proposal, I do not intend to introduce an independent animal welfare commission and commissioner at this time. I am satisfied that my Department has adequate resources through which it can commission animal welfare advice. My priority remains delivering the reforms set out in the Animal Welfare Pathway, which were developed through comprehensive stakeholder consultation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 20176/17-22, to detail (i) whether the job creation targets linked to the clause have been met within the required timeframe; (ii) the assessment made of the purchaser’s compliance with the agreement; and (iii) the actions they will take, should the commitments not been fulfilled, to ensure that the site delivers the intended economic benefits to the local community.
answer_text: MJM Renewables was selected as the preferred purchaser of the Shackleton site following an assessment process in 2016. The Shackleton agreement for sale included a Clawback/Penalty clause in relation to the requirement to deliver 100 jobs on site within five years of the owner receiving an initial planning approval for a Shackleton project. The initial planning application for the Shackleton site was approved by the local council in September 2020. As of September 2025, MJM Renewables has advised TEO that 200 jobs have been created to date, demonstrating compliance with the terms of the agreement.The Executive Office no longer owns the Shackleton site but continues to engage with MJM Renewables on the development of the site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what measurable outcomes have resulted from his Department’s collaboration with local councils and animal welfare organisations to address illegal puppy farming, including the (i) number of investigations; (ii) enforcement actions; and (iii) prosecutions supported or initiated through these partnerships, in the last three years.
answer_text: My Department regularly engages with Councils, which enforce dog breeding legislation, through various forums such as meetings with the Northern Ireland Dog Advisory Group. Any intelligence on illegal dog breeding, including material from animal welfare organisations, is promptly shared with local councils. My Department does not hold statistics on council enforcement activities; please contact councils directly for this data.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline his Department's plans to review and reform the Northern Ireland Housing Executive's Housing points system for complex and social needs, particularly the caps that limit points for applicants with serious, multiple, and wide-ranging challenges, to better reflect the scale of their need.
answer_text: The Fundamental Review of Allocations is currently ongoing, and officials have commenced work on Proposal 20, which is that specialised properties should be allocated by a separate process. The Department proposed that a time bound review should be led by social landlords to determine how specialist properties should be allocated. A report detailing the outcome of this review is scheduled to be with Department by spring 2026.I have also sought input from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive, who has advised as follows.“The Housing Selection Scheme was designed to provide a balance between a range of housing need factors and to give each an appropriate level of weighting, so any one factor or type of need may not disproportionately affect allocations.The current cap in relation to Social Needs Factors reflect this aim to strike the appropriate balance between the full range of housing need factors. There are seven Primary Social Needs (PSN) factors within the Scheme. An Applicant will be awarded 20 points for each PSN Factor that applies. However, this is capped at to a maximum of 2 factors (i.e., maximum 40 points). It may be useful to note that the first 3 PSN Factors deal with violence or the fear of violence and are mutually exclusive (that is it is not possible to qualify for more than one of these). The review of PSN criteria under Rule 43 of the Scheme is currently underway, as part of the ongoing Fundamental Review of Allocations (FRA) Implementation Project - https://www.nihe.gov.uk/housing-help/apply-for-a-home/fundamental-review-of-allocations, which is being delivered by the Housing Executive. The scope and purpose of this work is to review the Housing Selection Scheme and the recognition of the housing needs of victims of violence, abuse and trauma, and those at risk of violence and abuse. The review will consider any proposed changes alongside the recognition already in place in relation to the award of points associated with statutory homelessness, including those who are victims of violence or who are at fear of violence, and the prioritisation of housing need factors in a cumulative points-based scheme. The changes agreed under the PSN review are scheduled to be introduced with the final group of changes which are due to be delivered in Stage 4 of the FRA Project, currently estimated to be Autumn 2026.  There are 16 Other Social Needs Factors (OSN), and each attract and award of 10 points, with a maximum points cap of 40 points. It is felt that the cap on OSN Factors was and continues to be a reasonable and proportionate response, so as not to give a disproportionate weighting to social factors when set against other elements such as homelessness, functionality and housing conditions.For applicants with particularly high/intense support and care needs the option of supported housing exists and is often the most appropriate housing solution. Supported housing is allocated without reference to points from a separate administ

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 30215/22-27, to outline what alternative measures his Department is currently using or developing to tackle long-term vacancy and dereliction, particularly where there is an impact on (i) public realm; or (ii) housing supply.
answer_text: i) Impact on Public RealmMy department delivers a number of programmes, these include the Urban Development Grant Scheme, Comprehensive Development Schemes, the Village Catalyst Scheme and the Historic Environment Fund.The Department has also supported local councils, who have developed grant schemes in response to problems with vacant and derelict properties, through the Department’s Revitalisation Programme.In addition to these measures, DfC launched its Shaping Sustainable Places (SSP) consultation in June 2025 which proposes a long-term, collaborative regeneration programme with the Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) and the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) for Northern Ireland’s towns, villages and city centres.	The draft Business Case for SSP contains a specific objective to ‘Create more productive local economies by bringing forward a projected level of up to 100 vacant and derelict buildings/sites back into use - positively impacting non-domestic occupancy rates where interventions are delivered.’	ii) Impact on Housing Supply	The Executive Housing Supply Strategy published in December 2024, recognises the opportunity to consider actions around the re-use of existing buildings that can support housing supply. The Department is currently examining ‘empty homes’ approaches in other jurisdictions with a view to determining what possible policy interventions could be utilised in NI to bring vacant properties back into use.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps he will take to support the establishment of a dedicated, student-led and education-focused representative body for second-level pupils similar to the Irish Second-Level Students’ Union, recognising that the Youth Assembly do not provide a formal mechanism for ongoing student representation within education policy development.
answer_text: I do not have any plans to establish a student led and education focused representative body for post primary students.  In June of this year, following agreement by my Executive colleagues, I issued a set of Participation Principles for adoption and use by all departments. The principles are designed to support a structured and consistent approach to engaging with children and young people as part of policy and decision making. The principles have been issued to all staff in my department and published on the department’s website. They can be accessed here: https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/principles-participation-children-and-young-people-june-2025

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline (i) the evidence he used to take his decision on the removal of AS levels; and (ii) an assessment of how this will impact university admissions, particularly for students who rely on AS performance to guide A level subject choices or applications.
answer_text: My Department’s consultation on proposals for the Future of CCEA GCSEs, AS Levels and A Levels is an opportunity for students, parents, educators, employers and wider stakeholders to contribute by sharing their views on how we can improve the structure, content and assessment of CCEA GCSE, AS level and A level qualifications.  The proposals, developed following ongoing engagement with stakeholders, are intended to facilitate high-quality teaching and learning, reduce unnecessary pressure on students and teachers whilst ensuring the quality of these qualifications is maintained. The consultation is open until 13 November, and I have not yet made decisions on the proposals, including the proposal to reduce pressure on students and teachers by removing AS levels.It is not necessary for students to rely on external exams in Year 13 in order to determine their progress in a subject. Feedback can be provided by teachers, through in-year formative assessment which can be tailored to suit the learning needs of students.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 30213/22-27, to outline (i) how his Department monitors the implementation of Article 86 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, with respect to children who cannot attend school due to emotional school avoidance; and (ii) what data exists on (a) provision; and (b) outcomes for these children.
answer_text: The Department does not use the terminology “emotional school avoidance”. It must be recognised that some children do wish to be in school, but their mental health does not permit this. Therefore, the term ‘Emotionally Based School Non Attendance’ (EBSNA) is preferred.This is the first academic year in which a pupil can be recorded as absent with a code specifically reflecting EBSNA. The monthly school level data which the department receives does not identify individual pupils as absent due to EBSNA, rather the data provides information on the number of ½ days recorded as absent due to EBSNA.There is therefore no data available on outcomes for pupils with EBSNA and provision for pupils will be dependent on individual circumstances of the pupil, school phase and resources available.  Every school should work in conjunction with the Education Welfare Service and parents/carers to ensure that pupils with attendance issues are able to return to school as soon as is possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people currently on waiting lists for psychological care have presented to (i) primary; and (ii) secondary care services, prior to their initial appointment.
answer_text: Please be advised that my Department does not collate the requested information in relation to how many people currently on waiting lists for psychological care have presented to (i) primary; and (ii) secondary care services, prior to their initial appointment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether a referral from a paediatrician is required for a statutory assessment for a statement of special educational needs, even in scenarios where a GP has provided a clinical opinion indicating autism.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that under the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996 if a Health and Social Services Authority, in the course of exercising its functions in relation to a child who has not attained the lower limit of compulsory school age, forms the opinion that the child has or probably has special educational needs (SEN), they should bring this child to the attention of the EA.  If a statutory assessment is required, this will be actioned.  It is not dependent on a diagnosis.   For children who are school age, schools will follow the graduated response framework and if they consider support beyond Stage 2 is required, they can submit a request for statutory assessment attaching any relevant evidence which may include any medical reports, such as an autism spectrum diagnostic report.Not all children with a medical diagnosis such as autism spectrum disorder will require a statutory assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps are being taken by their Department to ensure that unpaid carers are recognised and supported within cross-departmental poverty alleviation measures.
answer_text: This question has been directed to the Department for Communities for response as my Department is leading on the development of the Executive’s Anti-Poverty and Disability Strategies.Unpaid carers fulfil an essential role, and I want to ensure that my Department and the cross-Executive work that my Department leads reflects and supports this role, where possible. Carer’s Allowance, for example, is the main benefit support targeted towards certain unpaid carers, aiming to provide a measure of financial support for people who give up the opportunity of full-time employment in order to provide regular and substantial care.In June this year, the Executive agreed to consult on a draft Anti-Poverty Strategy which is both sustainable and deliverable and which prioritises the issues that make a tangible difference to people’s lives throughout NI, including the needs of unpaid carers. In addition, in the coming weeks I will bring a draft Disability Strategy to the Executive for consideration, which will aim to improve the lives of people who are Deaf and disabled, as well as their families and carers, so that they can play a full and active role in all aspects of our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will carry out a full review of the impact of school closures in during the COVID-19 pandemic, noting recent comments from the World Health Organisation that school closures had a hugely detrimental effect on children.
answer_text: The Queen’s University of Belfast conducted a review of the evidence related to the impact of the Covid-19 pandemic in Northern Ireland.  Their Report, Pupil Attainment, Wellbeing, and Teacher Practices during the Pandemic: Findings from an Evidence and Gap Map, was published in May 2022.  There is also a wide body of international evidence which indicates the significant impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on children’s attainment and wellbeing. For example, Northern Ireland’s average scores for both mathematics and reading in PISA 2022 had declined significantly since 2018. This pattern was mirrored across OECD countries following the pandemic. As highlighted in my Department’s TransformED Strategy, the COVID-19 impact in schools presents governments internationally with a public policy problem unique in its depth, distribution and persistence. All children were affected, some profoundly. Disadvantaged groups were disproportionately affected and different age groups were affected in different ways.  It is likely that a COVID impact will be present in education for the next decade. From the 2025–26 academic year, system-level sample assessments will be introduced in literacy and numeracy. These assessments, which will be designed and administered by CCEA, will be taken by a representative sample of pupils in Years 4,7 and 10. They are designed to measure the overall performance of the education system and will help identify areas for concern and improvement across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the Minister of Education’s statement of 16 September 2025 where he said that the rise in special educational needs is not a temporary spike, it is a sustained and accelerating trend, (i) whether his Department will undertake investigation of the reasons for this trend; and (ii) whether his Department will cooperate with the Department of Education to formulate a plan to meet the whole needs of our special educational needs population.
answer_text: My Department fully recognises the inseparable relationship between Health and Education in the delivery of Special Educational Needs. Improved data sharing between early years services and education is a necessary component to anticipating population changes and ensuring that a sufficient level of services is commissioned. This is key to ensure we give all children the best start in life, in particular our most vulnerable. Equally, the use of data is critical in planning from SEN transitions into adult services, such as further education, supported employment and social care. My Officials are working closely with the Department for Education (DE) who are leading in terms of early years data to project future demand. This will not only benefit Education forecasting but, in the presence of rising demand and increased complexity, it will equally benefit Health in relation to workforce planning to ensure the therapeutic supports and interventions are delivered to enhance the child’s learning and school experience, enabling them to reach their potential and fully access the educational curriculum. My Department is fully committed to continued collaboration, accountability and responsiveness to the diverse needs to children and young people with Special Educational Needs, their families and those who support them. Earlier this year, DE published the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan and subsequently an accompanying governance framework in which Health is strongly engaged and will be represented across the governance structures, from the Central Strategic Committee, to the groups that will support it such as its advisory forums and data and monitoring groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the percentage of unsuccessful Personal Independence Payment assessments that were appealed to a tribunal were subsequently overturned, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold appeals information in the format requested.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will engage with the UK Government to encourage support for international legal accountability mechanisms, including the International Criminal Court, in response to the United Nations' finding of genocide in Gaza.
answer_text: There is no agreed approach to this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 30449/22-27, for a timeline of when his Department intends to begin measuring this information and make it publicly available.
answer_text: Some qualification data is already collected from schools for special educational needs and disabilities, but the information is currently not validated, collated or included in Official Statistics publications, primarily due to concerns about its coverage, comparability and suitability.With the objective of having schools on the new Bromcom information management system for the 2026/27 academic year, the expectation is that full coverage will be achieved. At that stage further work could be undertaken to investigate the comparability and suitability of the collected data.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 27428/22-27, whether his Department has any interim measures in place to ensure the accurate monitoring of all children who are placed on reduced timetables.
answer_text: Within the current Circular 2025/09 Attendance Guidance and Absence Recording by Schools , settings are advised that where it is felt  appropriate to introduce a flexible timetable that:Such occasions should be exceptional, short term and fully justified since there may be significant safeguarding concerns associated with such decisions.Flexible timetables must be for an agreed limited period of time with target progress dates established with the intention of seeing pupils returning to full time education in school.In agreement with parent/carers, progress dates should be established as part of a multidisciplinary approach with a robust review process in place.School Principals should keep Board of Governors advised where flexible timetables are applied in exceptional circumstances.Pupils should have the words ‘Flexible timetable’ recorded in SMS notes to allow for ease of data retrieval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether his Department will (i) increase the school uniform grant allowance; and (ii) consider expanding uniform grant for all those in receipt of Universal Credit for the next academic year.
answer_text: Following a review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals (FSM) and uniform grants (UG), I announced in July that for families applying for these supports, the net household earnings threshold (income threshold) for those in receipt of Universal Credit would rise to £15,390 for the 2025/26 academic year. This was the maximum level of income threshold that was affordable in light of the current budgetary constraints facing both my Department and the wider Executive. I have also committed to linking future increases to the rate of consumer price inflation, so that these supports keep pace with the cost of living and continue to reach those most in need.These funding pressures also meant that no increase in the rates of UG for the 2025/26 academic year was possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any discussions she has had with the UK Home Office, regarding the unlawful surveillance of a former BBC Northern Ireland home affairs correspondent by MI5.
answer_text: While I share the concerns raised around the surveillance of journalists, UK Security Services and the oversight of such surveillance are excepted matters and fall outside the purview of my role as Minister of Justice. Since the recent disclosure via the IPT, I have had no engagement with the Home Office on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment on whether there should be a review of protections for journalists in Northern Ireland, in light of the Investigatory Powers Tribunal ruling regarding the alleged police surveillance of a former BBC Northern Ireland home affairs correspondent.
answer_text: While much of what has been disclosed falls outside the Devolved sphere, I share the concerns raised around any routine surveillance of journalists, which are a recognised sensitive profession. In its 17th Human Rights Annual Report 2024-25 published on 4 September 2025 the NI Policing Board reviewed key areas of policing in Northern Ireland – including Covert Surveillance.That document recommended that PSNI should provide a report to the Policing Board every year on its use of covert surveillance powers including: the numbers of each type of surveillance; the numbers of journalists and members of other sensitive professions subject to covert surveillance; the numbers of its cases pending before the Investigatory Powers Tribunal, as well as providing a summary of each of those cases to the Policing Board’s Human Rights Advisor.PSNI have already enhanced the central record of surveillance authorisations to provide greater transparency to Investigatory Powers Commissioner’s Office inspections.I also welcome the publication of the findings of Angus McCullough KC into any PSNI use of surveillance in relation to specific, sensitive groups. It is important now to give the Policing Board and the PSNI the opportunity to reflect on its contents and respond. I also welcome acceptance of the report by the Chief Constable, and his acknowledgment of the need for improvement of processes and commitment towards realising that improvement.I stand ready to assist the Board further once its deliberations on the report findings are concluded.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any investment made in increasing the availability of (i) foster care; and (ii) residential placements since 2020, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The following response is provided in the context of children and young people in care, including those children with disabilities, and any investment from the Department of Health to Health and Social Care Trusts, specifically focused on increasing the availability of foster care and residential placements. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assessment has been made of the impact on the (i) mental health; (ii) safety; and (iii) educational continuity, of children in care who are placed in temporary or geographically distant accommodation.
answer_text: All children and families referred to social services and allocated a social worker will have a UNOCINI (Understanding the Needs of Children in Northern Ireland) assessment completed.  This includes children and young people who progress to being accepted into the care of a Trust care (Children Looked After).  This is the agreed regional assessment framework for all Children’s Social Services in Northern Ireland.  This a comprehensive tool that assesses child’s developmental needs, parenting capacity, family and environmental factors leading to a holistic view of the child’s circumstances and identification of support needs to promote the child’s well-being and development. Therefore the impact on any particular young person is child specific and regularly assessed and reviewed via their pathway.Mental health Looked After Children with mental health needs identified during assessment are able to access dedicated mental health support via CAMHS Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) or therapeutic support via Therapeutic Children Looked After Services.  The new Northern Ireland Framework for Integrated Therapeutic Care (FITC) is currently being implemented across Trusts and is designed to offer children in care and care experienced children and young people with trauma informed and attachment focussed interventions. Where children are placed outside of the region/ jurisdiction then the Trust must establish contact with the local authority or other Trust area to establish what access to mental health supports can be accessed in the area of placement. Children Looked After will continue to have access to their local Trust area therapeutic team. Safety Risk assessment and safety planning are at the core of social work care planning for Children Looked After.  A range of tools are used to assess risks that are incorporated into the UNOCINI framework.  The Trust will continuously maintain and revise its safety plan based upon information gleaned from direct social work visits, information from others involved in their care or other information the Trust is made aware of from the ‘team around the child’.  The Care Plan agreed by the Children Looked After Review, will include [as standard] a section detailing the consideration given to the protection needs of the child who is the subject of the plan and any child protection actions agreed. (Protecting Looked After Children Guidance DoH 2024)Education and Educational continuityA further key component of the aforementioned Looked After care plan is educational need which is completed via a Personal Education Plans (PEPS) and developed for all looked after children.  These plans are designed to support the educational needs of the child outlining strengths, needs and goals.  As Children Looked After often require additional educational support due to complex needs and trauma this support can include individualised learning plans and strategies to support young people to remain regulated within

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will hold discussions with the Education Authority as a matter of priority in relation to the new Causeway Academy being housed over three campuses as opposed to one single site.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA), as proposer of the Development Proposal to establish the new Causeway Academy, has a statutory obligation to proceed with its implementation. The EA is working with the school’s Principal Designate on the configuration of sites for the opening of the new school on 1 September 2026.   The EA has advised that the Principal Designate will issue communications to staff, parents and other stakeholders when the configuration has been agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will explore awarding funding from RAISE to the new Causeway Academy, recognising that the non-selective sector is in critical need for support in the bid to tackle educational underachievement.
answer_text: The RAISE Programme provides an opportunity to raise achievement and tackle educational disadvantage through a whole community, place-based approach. Bespoke Strategic Area Plans have been developed for each RAISE locality, including Coleraine, identifying needs and prioritised objectives to improve the educational outcomes for children and young people, and to facilitate delivery against the strategic objectives of the programme.All Strategic Area Plans have now been published on the DE website. Funding for Locality Led Projects will be allocated though a call process.  The first call for the projects to deliver the against objectives set out in the 18 Strategic Area Plans was launched on 5 September 2025. Guidance on how to apply for Locality Led Projects has been published on the DE website. This outlines the process for bringing forward projects to deliver against the objectives in each Strategic Area Plan.  Causeway Academy, when opened, will be part of the Coleraine RAISE locality. Two of the three amalgamating schools, Coleraine College and Dunluce School are represented on the Coleraine Locality Reference Group and will be aware of the process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQO 941/22-27, for an update on the review of current skills and training provision for school leavers with special educational needs, and on the legislative protections.
answer_text: On 16 June 2025, I provided an update on the findings of the review and set out my proposed programme of reform to improve and expand the Department for the Economy funded pathways and support for learners with special educational needs. It is a significant programme, impacting on many aspects of our provision and supports, and it aligns with the Programme for Government commitment to better support young people with special educational needs. The detailed information can be accessed on my Department’s website – www.economy-ni.gov.uk/articles/review-post-school-provision-special-educational-needs-learnersInvestment will be crucial to enable us to develop and deliver our reform programme. We have submitted a collaborative bid to the Transformation Fund, as well as preparing Departmental bids for the multi-year Spending Review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) the criteria used in the assessment and selection process for stadia funding under the Northern Ireland Football Fund; (ii) the reasons for the rejection of Coleraine FC’s application for Tier 3 funding; and (iii) what steps will be taken to ensure full transparency and regional balance in the allocation of the £36.2 million funding,
answer_text: The criteria to be met for each application is set out in the guidance notes on the Northern Ireland Football Fund website - The Northern Ireland Football Fund Performance Programme documents | Department for Communities.I cannot discuss individual applications; however, my officials have now reached out to applicants not taken forward in the prioritised cohort to arrange to meet and discuss their application and score.This was a fair and transparent process. All applicants had an equal opportunity to make their case for why their project should be taken forward first.Well in advance of the call for applications a Rural Needs Impact Assessment) (RNIA) was completed on The Northern Ireland Football Fund (TNIFF) Performance Programme. This assessment noted that TNIFF was not intended to disburse funding in such a way as to secure a regional spread; rather all applications would be subject to the same scoring matrix with no advantage or disadvantage given to rural clubs. Decisions were therefore taken on an assessment of need and merit rather than to achieve geographical spread.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 30107/22-27, to detail (i) whether the Curriculum Taskforce will consider the inclusion of evidence-based harm reduction approaches within the statutory curriculum framework; and (ii) any engagement his Department plans to undertake with (a) young people; and (b) public health experts during this process.
answer_text: The work of the Taskforce is still at an early stage. A key objective is the development of a new statutory curriculum framework, which will specify subject content that is carefully sequenced to support progression across all curriculum areas.The Taskforce will advise on the specific content and concepts within each subject area. It will consider a range of issues as part of its commitment to designing a curriculum that is both academically ambitious and practically deliverable.  Of course, there are also opportunities to deliver learning experiences out with the statutory curriculum framework.  The Taskforce and its working groups will determine engagement processes for stakeholders to inform the drafting process.  The new statutory curriculum framework will be subject to public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any cross-border co-operation that has taken place in relation to the 9 per cent of Lough Neagh's catchment located in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: My Department continues to collaborate with the Department of Housing, Local Government and Heritage and the Environment Protection Agency on the development of a ‘Shared Waters’ document.  This document plays an important role in outlining the environmental landscape that will steer the implementation of the Third Cycle River Basin Management Plans for Ireland and Northern Ireland in our shared waters.  Both Administrations remain committed to the ongoing exchange of knowledge and expertise on water quality matters through the North South Water Quality Coordination Group.In addition, the Northern Ireland Environment Agency actively participates in the Borders Region Operational Committee, which consists of operational staff from various local authorities and 23 public bodies.  Meeting on a bi-annual basis, the Committee facilitates knowledge-sharing and collaboration on improvement measures for water quality, at both regional and local levels, benefitting all from shared expertise and resources.The River Blackwater Catchment Trust is a cross-border organisation dedicated to conserving, protecting, rehabilitating, and improving water bodies within the Blackwater catchment, which is part of the larger Lough Neagh catchment.  My Department has supported the Trust through the Environment Fund Strategic Strand, enabling the Trust to work with cross-border stakeholders on various projects.My Department remains committed to identifying further opportunities for cross-border cooperation to enhance knowledge and collaboration.  These efforts are crucial for addressing challenges within the Lough Neagh catchment to achieve sustainable improvements in water quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) a breakdown of the uptake of each A-level subject by gender over the last five years; and (ii) any assessment his Department has made of any consistent gender disparities in the uptake of certain subjects.
answer_text: The answer to (i) has been placed in the Assembly Library.In relation to part (ii) of the question, my Department has not conducted research on gender disparities in the uptake of certain subjects.  CCEA design qualifications to be accessible for all students and aim to remove any gender bias in the content. However, certain areas of study appeal to different groups of students and there are some qualifications that are taken by more females than males or vice versa.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail her Department's engagement in active cross-border economic initiatives with the Irish Government or Ireland's Foreign Direct Agency, to stimulate investment and job creation in the North West.
answer_text: My department, along with its economic agencies – InterTradeIreland and Invest NI – is actively engaged in a range of cross-border economic initiatives that directly support investment and job creation in the North West.My officials continue to work closely with their counterparts in the Department of Enterprise, Tourism and Employment to strengthen cooperation on areas of shared economic priorities, including delivery of the €30m Shared Island Enterprise Scheme. They are also engaging regularly around future Shared Island proposals. This collaboration aims to unlock further strategic investment, promote inclusive growth, and improve connectivity across the island and in border communities like the North West.InterTradeIreland plays a vital role in supporting businesses through programmes focused on innovation, entrepreneurship, and trade. Recent initiatives include trade promotion events in Derry’s Guildhall and a major conference at the Ebrington Hotel, delivered in partnership with the Department for Business and Trade and Invest NI. InterTradeIreland also facilitates knowledge exchange through webinars and academic collaboration events such as the Business Explorer initiative with North West Regional College and Ulster University. Invest NI continues to work in partnership with Enterprise Ireland and IDA Ireland. Notably, in November 2024, they jointly delivered an all-island trade mission to the Singapore Fintech Festival, the world’s largest fintech event. This mission included 15 companies from across the island,  including those with operations in the North West. The event showcased the region’s fintech capabilities, including the North West’s RegTech Supercluster, and created valuable business opportunities in a global market.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of jobs creation at Shackleton Barracks, in light of the late deputy First Minister’s comments in 2015 stating he had no doubt that we are going to create thousands of new jobs
answer_text: MJM Renewables was selected as the preferred purchaser of the Shackleton site following an assessment process in 2016. The Executive Office no longer owns the Shackleton site. The conditions of the sale, regarding employment, was to create 100 new jobs and we are advised this has been fulfilled.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 28415/22-27, to detail whether any (i) assessment has been carried out on the appropriateness of continuing trade in goods and services between Northern Ireland and Israel, in light of the ongoing proceedings at the International Court of Justice, concerning alleged violations by Israel of its obligations under the Genocide Convention in relation to Palestinians in the Gaza Strip; and (ii) consideration has been given to suspending trade or imposing restrictions on exports, particularly in sectors involving potentially dual-use or sensitive
answer_text: I have set out my position on these matters in my statement of 16 October.Minister's statement.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration which has been given to (i) exploring cooperation with the Department of Education in the Republic of Ireland to share Irish-medium teaching resources; and (ii) employing Irish-speaking educational psychologists to support pupils in Irish-medium schools.
answer_text: No specific engagement has taken place with the Department of Education the Republic of Ireland on this matter. This will be explored in the near future. The Education Authority (EA) has advised that Educational Psychology Service (EPS) support, including planning, consultation, assessment, intervention and training, to the Irish-Medium sector is calculated based on the needs across Northern Ireland schools, via an equitable allocation formula.  Efforts are made during school allocation to match Education Psychologists interested in Irish/bilingualism with Irish-Medium settings.  An internal EA EPS Working Group continues to meet regularly and is focused on identifying ways of supporting Irish-Medium schools.Further consideration will be given to this issue in the ongoing development of a DE-led IME Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any (i) monitoring of air and soil pollution carried out by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency in the vicinity of bonfire sites during July 2025; and (ii) pollutants detected.
answer_text: No routine monitoring of air or soil pollution is carried out by the Agency in or near any bonfire site given bonfires are an illegal waste activity and I can confirm that no such routine testing was conducted by the Agency during July 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: (i) The number of outstanding arrest warrants, as of 9 September 2025, broken down by (a) warrant type; and (b) the length of time each has been outstanding, is presented in the table below.Time PeriodBench WarrantsArrest WarrantsTotal0-1 Years253503751-2 Years171761932-3 Years81972053-4 Years41091134-5 Years393965-10 Years2536038510-15 Years628829415-20 Years2042244220-25 Years12655667More than 25 Years14495509Source: Integrated Court Operations System (ICOS) Arrest/Bench Warrants Report.  The information is lifted from the live system and should be treated as management information rather than official statistics.An individual may have more than one warrant issued for their arrest.Fine Warrants are not included.(ii) 	The enforcement of Arrest Warrants is a matter for the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI).  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable.  You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the current (i) prison population; and (ii) number of unoccupied prison cells, broken down by institution, as of 9 September 2025.
answer_text: On 9 September 2025 1,478 prisoners were being held in Maghaberry Prison, 499 in Magilligan Prison and 172 at Hydebank Wood.  On this date there were 105 unoccupied cells available at Maghaberry, four at Magilligan and 102 at Hydebank Wood.  Whilst these figures are for unoccupied cells at a point in time, this number can vary dependent upon a range of factors including what residential accommodation is open and in use, the size and configuration of cohorts within the population, the appropriate use of cell sharing, and maintenance work etc.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment his Department has made of the implications for the delivery of Relationships and Sexuality Education following his direction to the Education Authority to withdraw its guidance on supporting transgender pupils, particularly in relation to how issues of (i) gender identity, (ii) inclusion; and (iii) the support needs of transgender pupils, will now be addressed in schools.
answer_text: On 12 May 2025 I articulated my clear position in respect of the UK Supreme Court judgment in the Northern Ireland Assembly.  Having sought legal advice and having received advice from officials on these matters, it is my view that the Education Authority’s guidance document, ‘Guidance for Schools, EOTAS Centres and Youth Service on Supporting Transgender Young People’, is unlawful and is inconsistent with the policy of the Department.  The guidance has now been withdrawn.    The delivery of Relationships and Sexuality Education is a part of the Northern Ireland Curriculum for all pupils of compulsory school age. It is the responsibility of each school's Board of Governors to ensure that a comprehensive, age-appropriate Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) programme is delivered, that the statutory minimum content is covered, and that the programme including teaching on gender identity reflects both the law and the school's own RSE policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer AQW 29043/22-27, in reference to question AQW 25434/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 29043/22-27 on 10 October 2025.
