question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) what assurances she has received from the PSNI that individuals on bail with an imposed internet ban are being appropriately monitored; and (ii) what measures are in place to ensure compliance with such conditions, recognising that internet bans have been shown to be ineffective in recent publicly reported cases.
answer_text: The monitoring of individuals on bail is an operational matter for the PSNI who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. As I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the PSNI and the oversight role of Policing Board, it would not be appropriate for me to comment.  You may, therefore, wish to raise this matter directly with the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the average waiting time for applicants to the Affordable Warmth Scheme to receive financial support; (ii) what percentage of applicants to the Affordable Warmth Scheme have received financial support; and (iii) when will more funds be delivered to the Housing Executive so as to ensure all applicants who have passed all stages of the Affordable Warmth Scheme will receive the support required.
answer_text: The issues at (i) and (ii) are operational matters for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:(i) “Cases are currently waiting an average of 67 days for the grant to be released.” (ii) “In Q1 of 25/26, we received 649 applications and 120 approvals were issued. Of these cases approved, 27.5% of cases have completed measures and received payment. Information for Q1 of the current financial year (2025-26) has been provided only.  Information for Q2 (1 July  – 30 September 2025) forms part of the quarterly and annual compendium publication of housing statistics.” (iii) I committed £7.5m to the scheme this year. This covered £5m of committed approvals from last year and £2.5m for new approvals this year meaning approximately 570 new approvals can be issued by NIHE to those in greatest need. I requested additional funding for the Affordable Warmth Scheme in the June Monitoring Round, however that bid was not met. I will continue to press for additional funding for this important scheme through the monitoring round process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in light of the proposed ending of the UK Shared Prosperity Fund, what immediate measures her Department is taking to ensure the continuity of support for economically inactive cohorts, particularly carers, disabled people, and those transitioning from the justice system.
answer_text: My Department offers a suite of skills and education measures, despite the challenges caused by loss of EU funding and it not being fully replaced through the Shared Prosperity Fund.  This includes Skills for Life and Work, Traineeships, Apprenticeships and a range of short-term skills courses.  Embedded within these programmes are a range of support measures designed to assist those who face barriers into learning. This includes childcare support and financial assistance.I have also introduced a Skills Fund which provides a responsive and agile system to meet the learning needs of citizens.  The fund includes interventions such as Inclusive Futures, and Step Up which will target 545 participants, delivered via the FE Sector to support those with special educational needs, women with a focus on dependents, and young people not in employment, education or training.  For those transitioning from the justice system a Service Level Agreement exists between NI Prison Service, Belfast Metropolitan College and North West Regional College for the provision of learning and skills services delivery to learners in prison.  This initiative aims to support future transition back into society.On 28 April 2025 I announced my intention to include a new statutory entitlement to carers leave and enhanced rights to flexible working, including form the first day of employment, for eligible working parents in the North of Ireland in my forthcoming Employment Rights Bill. These important legislative developments will help those with caring responsibilities and disabilities to remain within or enter the workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) an update on the scheme that will replace the Affordable Warmth Scheme; and (ii) a timeline of when he expects this to be implemented.
answer_text: (i) My Department is currently developing a new fuel poverty energy efficiency scheme that will replace the Affordable Warmth Scheme when it ends. The new Warm Healthy Homes Scheme will provide continued support to low-income households experiencing fuel poverty. It will offer a range of measures including insulation, low carbon heating solutions and renewable technologies. Officials have completed their analysis of responses from the Fuel Poverty Strategy Consultation which closed on 6 March 2025 and this along with lessons learned from a series of stakeholder workshops will help inform the proposed scheme design and secondary legislation, which will be consulted on early next year.		(ii) I am aiming to introduce the Warm Healthy Homes Scheme as early as possible in 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of (a) landowners; (b) farmers; and (c) community organisations, that have accessed support through the forestry grant schemes in each of the last three years, and (ii) the total area of land reforested through these schemes to date.
answer_text: Forestry grant schemes are available for land owners, including farmers, charitable organisations and local councils. Forestry grants were paid to 393 businesses for the creation of 1,386 hectares of new woodland over the last three years. In the period, 195, 123 and 57 farmers plus 10, 4 and 4 non farmers received forestry grant payments respectively in each of the last three years. No community organisations applied for or received forestry grant support.In addition, a further 2,386 farming businesses received annual premium payments under their agreements for woodlands planted in previous years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) what percentage of children attending schools for special educational needs and disabilities leave school with GCSEs; and (ii) what percentage of children who attend those schools at age 16 are entered into GCSE examinations.
answer_text: Data on GCSE entries and attainment for pupils attending schools for special educational needs and disabilities are not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will work with her Executive colleagues to develop a coordinated, interdepartmental plan, focused on harm-reduction to prevent further drug-related deaths, particularly in light of the recent tragedies associated with events such as the Emerge Festival.
answer_text: May I first extend my deepest condolences to the families and friends of the two young people who tragically lost their lives recently, my thoughts are with all those affected at this heartbreaking time. The Department of Health (DoH) lead on the development of a public health intervention to address problematic substance use but, given the complexities of addressing the social determinants underlying this issue, input from key partners is critical to success, with actions required from those in education, safeguarding, criminal justice, housing and employment. This joint responsibility is manifested via the 10 year DoH-led Substance Use Strategy Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery, co-produced across government departments, including my Department, agencies, health professionals, the community and voluntary sector and service users. This strategy has a specific outcome focussed on harm reduction, including drug deaths.My Department is also a member of the Health-led Substance Use Strategy Programme Board, together with the Department of Education, Department for Communities, Department for Infrastructure, Society of Local Authority Chief Executives amongst others, helping to drive forward and oversee the implementation of the above strategy to tackle the harms caused by substance use. There are several actions within the strategy that my Department is either directly or indirectly involved in delivering and my officials work alongside other departmental officials on the implementation of these

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the tragic deaths at the Emerge Festival in August 2025, whether he will give consideration to making drugs testing facilities available at future music festivals and similar public events as part of a broader harm-reduction strategy.
answer_text: I was deeply saddened to learn of the events over the Bank Holiday weekend. My thoughts are first and foremost with the family and friends of all those impacted and with the loved ones of those who tragically lost their lives. I share your concerns about the circumstances surrounding these issues, and my Department and the Public Health Agency have been in ongoing conversations with the PSNI. However, it is important that we do not mislead the public or speculate on connections between current cases or suggest this was a result of specific batches of drugs, until the facts are clearly established.In terms of the general position on drug testing here, there are resource, licensing, and legislative implications to drug testing that would need to be fully considered and agreed before any on site testing such as this could take place. It is important to note, however, that the Drug & Alcohol Monitoring & Information System (DAMIS), which is operated by PHA but overseen by a cross-departmental steering group, continues to provide up-to-date information on substances of concern to service users and those likely to come into contact with potentially harmful substances. Under DAMIS currently we have the ability to pro-actively seek testing of substances which are seen to be causing harm through partnership working with the PSNI and Forensic Science Northern Ireland. This information is used to inform related advice and alerts to service users, vulnerable groups, and those in key services.A review of DAMIS is pending, the intention is that enhanced testing possibilities will be fully considered as part of this work. However, any outcomes will be subject to resource and legislative implications.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any discussions to date about the need for a transition plan to support school leavers with special educational needs post-19; and (ii) a timeline for when an interdepartmental plan to address this will be established.
answer_text: The Department of Education is responsible for the transition arrangements for young people with Special Educational Needs, including the development of a transition plan.  Other Departments, including the Department of Health and my Department’s Careers Service are also involved in the process as required. In June 2025, I outlined the findings of my Department’s review and set out my proposed programme of reform, subject to the necessary investment, to improve and expand the Department for the Economy funded pathways and support for learners with special educational needs.  It is a significant programme, involving many aspects of our provision and supports. Officials are currently scoping the timeline and resources required to bring forward our proposed reforms. We have submitted a collaborative bid to the Transformation Fund, as well as preparing Departmental bids for the multi-year Spending Review.  As I highlighted in my June Statement, many school leavers with learning disabilities will transition onto the pathways funded by the Department of Health.  The Programme for Government reflects our shared commitment to ensure better support for young people with Special Educational Needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) what discussions he has had with (a) the Education Authority; and (b) Departmental officials, regarding ongoing problems with the provision of Special Educational Needs transport; and (ii) his assessment of current arrangements, particularly in light of repeated annual reports of transport provision failing in the first week of term for children with special educational needs.
answer_text: I have raised the issue of transport provision at the start of term for pupils with Special Educational Needs (SEN) with the Education Authority (EA). I am assured that the Transport service has been working at pace to arrange transport for those who have experienced disruptions or delays. I am advised that transport was provided for nearly 15,000 children with SEN on the first week of term, however the EA apologises to those children and families impacted by delays. Any additional transport requirements for a child with a statement of SEN are determined by the EA during the statutory assessment and review process. A referral is only made to the EA transport service after a school place has been allocated. It takes time to put transport arrangements in place, particularly for children who require bespoke arrangements and where further input is needed from parents or health professionals. Where a new taxi service is required, the EA must follow a procurement process and carry out driver and vehicle compliance checks. Enhanced Access NI checks must be carried out on drivers and escorts and any necessary specialist training must be delivered.  Securing taxi services has become more complex in recent years due to the decline in the number of licensed taxi operators in Northern Ireland and the rise in demand for school transport.My officials are in regular contact with EA Transport colleagues and I have asked them to work closely together to identify any improvements that can be made in SEN transport provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what training and support is provided to Emergency Department staff on recognising and appropriately managing pain in patients with complex conditions such as cystic fibrosis and pancreatitis.
answer_text: Pain management is part of core training for all clinical staff (nursing and medical).   Pain is one of the most common reasons for people to attend the Emergency Department (ED).  Recognition and alleviation of pain should be a priority when treating the ill and injured. This process should start at triage, be monitored during the patient’s time in the ED and continue through to admission or discharge ensuring adequate analgesia is offered at all times, including beyond discharge where appropriate.The experience of pain is different for different patients, with the exact same situation, and changes depending on what they have presented with.As part of the initial assessment, pain evaluation is an essential component. Multiple assessment tools are in use and include the visual analogue scale (VAS), the numeric ratings scale (NRS) and the four point verbal categorical rating scale (VRS).An algorithm for treatment of Acute Pain (eg. WHO pain ladder) is often used in the ED to manage analgesic strengths appropriate to their assessed pain severity. There is no single best ‘one size fits all’ solution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what procedures are in place to escalate care where a patient’s condition worsens while waiting in Emergency Departments.
answer_text: All HSC Trusts have confirmed they adhere to the nationally recognised Manchester Triage System upon each patient’s arrival at an Emergency Department.  This system allocates the patients to one of five categories ranging from Immediate (Category 1) to Non-urgent (Category 5).  This will allow the patient’s clinical needs to be appropriately escalated at the time of their initial attendance.  Also, at triage, investigations such as blood sample analysis and Electrocardiograms (ECGs) are taken and responded to as appropriate.  Following Triage, all Trusts’ Emergency Department staff complete clinical observations and adhere to the National Early Warning Score (2017) guidance and follow appropriate escalation as per this guidance.Staff are rostered to ensure all areas of Emergency Departments are adequately staffed and if any concerns requiring escalation are advised this will be escalated to senior nursing or medical staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the measures his Department plans to implement this year to ensure that children to who are unable to attend school due to (i) emotional school based avoidance; or (ii) any other reason, are able to receive access to the curriculum.
answer_text: While a child is absent from school with illness there is no expectation to complete work remotely.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action he will take to fulfil his legal duty under the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, in light of the High Court’s ruling that his Department’s strategy breached that legislation, ignoring both parental demand and official advice.
answer_text: The recent High Court ruling was regarding the initial Integrated Education Strategy for Northern Ireland published on 26 April 2023.The Department acknowledges that this first strategy, published before I was Education Minister, did not fully meet the detailed requirements as set out within the Integrated Education Act. A revised strategy: Vision 2030 – A Strategy for Integrated Education was published on 11 June 2025 and has been positively received by stakeholders and is not subject to any legal challenge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 25337/22-27, whether she will bring proposals to ban the smacking of children to the Executive during the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: ANSWER As you are aware I am fully supportive of removing the defence of reasonable chastisement from the law here.  However, any such amendment will require Executive agreement and to this end I have already circulated a draft Executive paper in October 2024 seeking support for proposals to do so.Despite my efforts the position has not changed in that I have not been able to secure Executive agreement to deliver a change in the law in the current Justice Bill.  However, I will continue to work with my ministerial colleagues to seek their support to repeal the defence of reasonable chastisement here and move towards a position of equal protection for children in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans he has to introduce a derelict property tax, to incentivise the redevelopment of long-term vacant or neglected buildings, particularly those impacting the public realm.
answer_text: I have no powers to introduce a derelict property tax.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions she has had with further education colleges in Northern Ireland to address the difficulties that many young people encounter when trying to secure a trades placement.
answer_text: Work-based learning is a key component of my Department's vocational training programmes. It provides learners with on the job experience in a business, allows them to further develop their vocational skills and to inform their future career path. My Department recognises the challenges faced by some learners in securing work placements and in response to this, has worked closely with the further education sector to develop alternative modes of delivery, such as simulated work environments, to enable students to further develop a range of skills. In collaboration with the further education sector, more consideration is being given to what more can be done to increase the number of work placements available to learners. My Department also recognises the critical importance of aligning apprenticeship provision with employer demand across a broad spectrum of occupational areas.  As apprenticeships are employer-led programmes, their success is dependent on businesses creating job opportunities and having the capacity to support apprentices throughout their training journey.  To support this, the Department funds the off-the-job training element and provides incentive payments to employers upon the successful achievement of the full apprenticeship framework.  In addition, the Construction Industry Training Board (CITB NI), an Arm’s Length Body of my Department, offers a cumulative Employer Apprentice Grant to further support employers in the construction sector. Furthermore, my Department’s recently published Apprenticeship Action Plan sets out the strategic direction for apprenticeships in the North. It highlights the economic value of apprenticeships and builds on our well-established system, with the aim of expanding the talent pipeline for employers and increasing access to apprenticeship opportunities for individuals.My Department remains committed to working in partnership with the further education colleges, as well as those other training providers delivering our programmes and employers to ensure that vocational education and training pathways are accessible, responsive to labour market needs, and supportive of young people’s aspirations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the tragic deaths of two young people following the Emerge Festival in Belfast, will his Department consider (i) reviewing education around the misuse of drugs; and (ii) the implementation of new harm reduction measures in the curriculum.
answer_text: The health and safety of all children and young people is of the utmost importance. My Department remains firmly committed to addressing the issue of drug and substance misuse. All grant-aided schools in Northern Ireland are required by law to have a drugs education policy in place, and drugs education is a statutory component of the curriculum — delivered through Personal Development and Mutual Understanding (PDMU) at primary level and Learning for Life and Work (LLW) at post-primary level.To support schools in delivering this vital area of learning, the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) provides a wide range of guidance and teaching resources. These materials help schools contextualise drugs education, respond to emerging issues, and tailor their approach to the needs of their pupils. Schools can also address specific topics when they are particularly relevant or the subject of public concern.My Department recently concluded a Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum. A Curriculum Taskforce has now been established to take forward the findings of that review, including the development of a new statutory curriculum framework. As part of this work, the Taskforce will give careful consideration to how health and wellbeing education is delivered within the revised curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what steps have been taken to ensure that Educational Psychology Services can be delivered in Irish to support those studying in the Irish medium sector this academic year.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that Educational Psychology Service (EPS) support, including planning, consultation, assessment, intervention and training, to the Irish Medium sector is calculated based on the needs across Northern Ireland schools, via an equitable allocation formula.  Efforts are made during school allocation to match Education Psychologists interested in Irish/bilingualism with Irish Medium settings.   An internal EA EPS Working Group continues to meet regularly and is focused on identifying ways of supporting Irish Medium schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has plans to provide those on disability allowance access to free public transport, as is available in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: The NI Concessionary Fares Consultation ran from 1st June 2023 to 24th August 2023 and included an option which would extend free travel for those currently receiving a half fare concession due to a qualifying disability.Although public support for this option was strong (with over 80% of respondents in favour of the change), unfortunately, due to the severe financial pressures facing the Department, changes which would increase the cost of the Scheme could not be implemented at that time.While the budgetary outlook has not changed, I had planned to reconsider the option to extend half fare travel to free travel again as part of my budget allocations decisions in this financial year but due to a major ticketing systems upgrade in Translink, this was not possible. My officials are actively working in close collaboration with Translink to consider how this policy could be implemented should budget be available in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what steps he has taken to ensure that the Arts Council is in compliance with the Letter of Expectations sent in February, specifically that any activity disrespectful of any tradition, in locations or by groups receiving Council funding, results in specific and substantive action as regards funding.
answer_text: The monitoring arrangements are set out in my Letter of Expectations which can be accessed on my Department’s website (https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/ministerial-letter-expectations-2025-26-arts-council-ni#:~:text=The%20letter%20of%20expectations%20sets%20out%20Ministerial%20priorities,the%20work%20of%20the%20Arts%20Council%20for%202025-26).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 27250/22-27, (i) to provide a timeline of when an Irish language option will be operational on all Translink ticket machines; and (ii) whether there are plans to ensure that all place names will be announced and presented on all Translink services in both Irish and English.
answer_text: I remain committed to the visibility and promotion of Irish Language across public services including the public transport network. The Irish language is a living and thriving language used daily by many people across the north.   As per my previous response, given the on-going legal proceedings in relation to my decision, it would not be appropriate to comment further.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce legislation within the 2022-27 Assembly mandate to comprehensively ban the smacking of children, recognising that this has long been urged by the United Nations Human Rights Committee.
answer_text: I have accepted transfer of this question from the Minister of Health as, whilst joint working between our departments will be required to introduce this successfully, to date the work on legislation has been led by the Department of Justice.I am fully supportive of changing the law here in Northern Ireland; however, any change to the law requires Executive agreement.Despite my efforts to do so, I have not been able to secure the necessary cross-party agreement to deliver a change in the law, but I will continue to work with my ministerial colleagues and with community and voluntary partners to obtain the required support to repeal the defence of reasonable chastisement here and move towards a position of equal protection for children in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant of AQW 28689/22-27 and in light of his Department's responsibilities for promoting good relations, tackling social exclusion, and delivering the Racial Equality Strategy, to detail (i) any actions he will take to address racism and xenophobia among young people; and (ii) whether he will engage with the Minister of Education to ensure these issues are addressed both within schools and in the wider community.
answer_text: The Executive Office has lead responsibility for promoting good relations, tackling social exclusion, and delivering the Racial Equality Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will answer (i) AQW 28573/22-27; (ii) AQW 28574/22-27; (iii) AQW 28575/22-27; (iv) AQW 28827/22-27; (v) AQW 28903/22-27; (vi) AQW 28972/22-27; and (vii) AQW 28973/22-27.
answer_text: All of these questions have been answered. AQW 28573/22-27, AQW 28574/22-27, AQW 28575/22-27, AQW 28827/22-27 and AQW 28973/22-27 were answered on 7 July 20025; AQW 28972/22-27 on the 9 July 2025; and AQW 28903/22-27 on 22 July 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department intends to enhance the role of independent ambulance services in handling low acuity emergency calls, in order to free up the NI Ambulance Service's frontline crews for high acuity emergencies.
answer_text: The independent ambulance sector supports the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) emergency responses by providing relief at emergency departments (EDs), conveyance of lower acuity emergency patients to hospital and planned transfers of patients from one location to another.In addition to the emergency service, NIAS utilises the independent sector to support the delivery of routine, non-emergency patients through the Patient Care Service (PCS).NIAS is in the process of conducting a value for money analysis comparing the use of the private ambulance sector with in-house capacity, as set out in the Northern Ireland Audit Office (NIAO) report on ambulance handovers.My Department is working with NIAS and Trusts to implement a regional ambulance handover protocol to improve handover delays to increase NIAS’s capacity to respond to emergency calls.Ambulance handover delays at EDs impact on NIAS’s ability to respond to emergency calls in the community.  Earlier this year, Departmental officials, along with clinicians from Trusts and the ambulance service visited a hospital in London to discuss a protocol that has been put in place to improve ambulance handover times and therefore release ambulances in a timelier manner to respond to calls in the community. Trusts have been asked to implement changes which will move us towards the London protocol. These changes are to be fully implemented by the end of October 2025, with an incremental improvement in ambulance handover delays thereafter. The first stage is to see the eradication of ambulance delays over 2 hours. Once the 2 hour target has been achieved, Trusts will then have to deliver improvements against a 90 minute maximum, followed by a 60 minute maximum, moving towards the 15 minute handover standard.In addition, my Department is working with NIAS to ensure sufficient ambulance and staffing capacity to meet Category 1 and Category 2 response time standards through the development of a 10-year workforce and financial plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the amount of money allocated to support (i) children's summer schemes; and (ii) summer schemes specifically for children with special educational needs, in each of the last five years
answer_text: In June 2021, the department announced funding of £4.5 million for summer schemes in Primary, Post Primary, Education other than at School Centres and Special Schools.  The amount of money specific to Special Schools was £154K.The Education Authority (EA) funds summer schemes in 21 Special Schools.  Table below shows funding allocated for the last 5 years.YEARFUNDING ALLOCATED (£)2020/21312,3872021/22796,9692022/23974,9312023/241,247,5022024/251,349,549In recent years the department has also allocated funding to all special schools for summer schemes.
