question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking (i) to recognise Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) as a serious metabolic and hormonal condition; and (ii) to improve PCOS treatment options available through the NHS.
answer_text: Women’s health, including services for those suffering from conditions such as Polycystic Ovary Syndrome and Endometriosis, is a priority for my Department.Following a Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) review, my Department has established a regional forum, with Trust, GP and PHA representation, to oversee ongoing service improvement and implementation at a local and regional level in Gynaecology services.The aim of the review was to identify areas where improvements could be made in the extensive waiting lists for gynaecology services, and to ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes, this includes those women suffering from conditions such as Polycystic Ovary Syndrome.Work to develop standardised clinical pathways for common gynaecology conditions is underway, with mapping from a patient’s initial presentation at GP services into enhanced GP Service Hubs, secondary, regional and tertiary services. These new pathways will benefit both patients and clinicians ensuring that patients are treated by the right person, at the right time, in the right place. These changes will of course require additional funding to achieve.As outlined in my statement to the Assembly on 6 May 2025, £215m has been ringfenced for Health in this year’s budget to be invested across three areas for waiting list activities: to expand core capacity to reduce the red flag/time critical gap (£85m); increase core routine capacity (£80m); and, non-recurrent funding (£50m) to start tackling the backlog of patients waiting. Gynaecology Services is an area which has been identified for additional funding need and Trusts are working to address the long waiting times through validation, additional clinics, daycases and inpatients activity.In addition, my Department recognises that there is a clear need for improved public awareness, information and education on women’s health matters from an early age and across the life course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in light of EirGrid’s Generation Capacity Statement, warning of capacity deficits up to 2031, to detail any steps her Department is taking to address (i) energy security; and (ii) market stability on an all-island basis.
answer_text: Security of Energy Supply is a key priority for the Department for the Economy. We work collaboratively with the System Operator for Northern Ireland (SONI), and the Utility Regulator to review and monitor the security and resilience of the changing energy system.SONI also work in close collaboration with their counterparts in the South, Eirgrid, to ensure that demand is met.Due to the nature of the all-island Single Electricity Market, my Department maintains a strong working relationship with Government colleagues at the Department of Climate Energy and the Environment (DCEE) in the South, on all aspects of Energy Security.  My Department will continue to work diligently with all stakeholders to ensure that our integrated electricity market remains resilient, efficient and capable of delivering a secure and stable supply for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given EirGrid’s contestation of the Utility Regulator’s findings and its warning about the adverse impacts of a demerger on electricity consumers and the single electricity market, for her Department’s assessment of the stated need for SONI’s demerger.
answer_text: My Department has engaged with all relevant stakeholders, including the Utility Regulator and SONI, in respect of the overall management and governance of SONI.  The Utility Regulator is independent and has advised us that following a rigorous process they made no decisions which mandate or require the full demerger of SONI from EirGrid.  However, officials continue to monitor the position, and I understand that SONI and EirGrid continue to engage on a new operating model. This will need to be fit for purpose for the energy transition and ensure that SONI continues to operate in a manner which safeguards the interests of consumers here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps her Department is taking to ensure the continued delivery of Irish-medium educational resources and services at third level institutions, including the An tÁisaonad project.
answer_text: I am fully committed to initiatives which support and promote the Irish language and recognise the importance of the An tÁisaonad project in creating high quality Irish-medium teaching and learning resources for schools.  Whilst the delivery of such services is a matter for the universities, which are autonomous institutions, I will be happy to engage with them to support their work in this area.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any economic activity that has taken place on the Shackleton site since its acquisition by MJM Group in 2016, including any monitoring that has been conducted to ensure it delivers on the promised job creation.
answer_text: MJM Renewables was selected as the preferred purchaser of the Shackleton site following an assessment process in 2016. The Executive Office no longer owns the Shackleton site but, continues to engage with MJM Renewables on the development of the site.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how many Paralympic athletes have received funding; and (ii) the total funding awarded to each Paralympic athlete, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for Sport NI, I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has provided me with a response.Due to the volume of information to be provided I have arranged for the detail contained in two tables to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of funding that has been allocated to specifically support and promote the involvement of children with special educational needs in sport and athletics within (i) primary schools; and (ii) post-primary schools, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: My department is committed to ensuring that all children, including those with special educational needs (SEN), have equal access to all areas of the curriculum, including physical education and sport.It is not possible to isolate the specific funding related to promoting the involvement of children with SEN in sport and athletics.     Schools receive delegated budgets through the Common Funding Scheme to enable Boards of Governors and Principals to use resources in accordance with individual schools’ needs and priorities, including effective delivery of the curriculum. Each school’s delegated budget share is expected to meet a range of items of expenditure, which includes physical education as a statutory element of the curriculum in primary and post-primary schools.The Board of Governors and Principal of every grant-aided school have a statutory duty to ensure that ‘the minimum content for each area of learning is taught.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how his Department is working with (i) landowners; (ii) farmers; and (iii) communities to incentivise and support reforestation on private land.
answer_text: My Department supports landowners, including farmers, charitable organisations and local councils through forestry grant schemes for planting new woodlands, and the reestablishment of woodlands after tree harvesting.The Forest Expansion Scheme is available for establishing woodlands of 3 hectares and above. The Small Woodland Grant Scheme supports smaller woodlands, down to 0.20 hectares, and is dedicated to creating new native woodland. My Department also offers the Woodland Investment Grant scheme to assist woodland owners to reestablish woodlands after tree harvesting.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant of AQW 26848/22-27, for an update on Project Gigabit, including (i) the procurement process; (ii) the proposed timeline for roll-out; and (iii) the specific areas expected to benefit from full fibre broadband.
answer_text: The procurement for Project Gigabit commenced on 11 November 2024, with an invitation to interested suppliers to complete a selection questionnaire to demonstrate their capacity and capability to deliver such a contract. The procurement is seeking a supplier to address an initial scope of approximately 11,000 premises. The deadline for the submission of bids for Project Gigabit closed in May, and the technical and financial evaluation of bids is ongoing. My Department expects to award a contract for Project Gigabit in August, subject to compliant bids being made, bids meeting the published criteria, and all necessary approvals being agreed. Details of the proposed timeline for roll out and the specific areas to benefit are expected to be made available shortly after contract award.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to review and expand NHS prescribing guidelines to include medications such as Mounjaro (Tirzepatide) for the treatment of polycystic ovary syndrome, particularly for patients for whom first-line treatments such as hormonal contraceptives, have proven ineffective or harmful.
answer_text: As previously advised, like all medicines authorised in Northern Ireland and across the UK, glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs) such as Mounjaro® (tirzepatide), are authorised by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA). Marketing authorisations are granted by MHRA if they meet the MHRA’s high standards on safety, quality and efficacy.In respect of Mounjaro® (tirzepatide), this is a GLP-1 RA product which is currently licensed by the MHRA for the treatment of type 2 diabetes and for weight loss purposes. However, it is not specifically licensed to treat patients experiencing symptoms associated with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and so use for this purpose is considered to be ‘off-label’.Additionally, my Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals and Clinical Guidelines are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and were found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.There are currently no NICE recommendations for the use of Mounjaro® (tirzepatide) or any other GLP-1 RA medicinal product for management of PCOS. In the absence of a marketing authorisation for this indication or any NICE recommendations, GLP-1 RAs including Mounjaro® (tirzepatide) are not routinely commissioned for this purpose within the HSC.Should NICE publish new recommendations for Mounjaro® (tirzepatide) or any other GLP-1 RA product, for its use in the treatment of PCOS after a marketing authorisation is granted, the Department will consider the recommendations under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22.  Available at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will answer AQW 28573/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 28573/22-27 was answered on 7 July 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how his Department ensures that children with special educational needs have equality of access to physical education and sport in school; and (ii) how compliance with inclusion and equality obligations is monitored and evaluated across schools.
answer_text: My department is committed to ensuring that all children, including those with special educational needs (SEN), have equal access to all areas of the curriculum, including physical education and sport.  Board of Governors and the Principal of every grant-aided school have a statutory duty to ensure that ‘the minimum content for each area of learning is taught’.The Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, which is not yet fully commenced, places a renewed emphasis on the continuum of provision across all stages of the Code of Practice, reinforcing the need for schools to be confident and capable in recognising and responding to the diverse abilities and interests of pupils in their setting. This approach aims to ensure that all children with disabilities receive appropriate support tailored to their individual needs, promoting inclusive and equitable access to the curriculum. Chapter 2 of the Department of Education Guide to Governor Roles and Responsibilities highlights that: “The role of the Board of Governors is to promote equality, diversity, good relations and to comply with education, employment legislation, anti-discrimination, human rights and equality legislation that affect its statutory duties.”  The Education Authority (EA) has a range of tools to assist schools in monitoring and evaluating inclusion and equality available on the EA website. This includes a Model Equality and Diversity Policy template, accompanying guidance and an Equality and Inclusion Self-Audit tool. The implementation of these tools is monitored by the Board of Governors at individual school level. The EA also provides further support through training and capacity building of governors and staff on equality, diversity, inclusion and disability duties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to increase the number of native speaker language assistants in the school system, in light of the 67 per cent decrease since 2012.
answer_text: My Department provides funding to the British Council to deliver the Language Assistants Programme as part of the International Education Programme. The number of incoming modern language assistants is dependent on the number of applications received for the Programme. While numbers coming into Northern Ireland schools have dropped from previous highs, the number of applications has increased by 56% since a low in 2021/22 at which time the Programme was significantly impacted by travel restrictions due to the covid pandemic.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of funding that has been allocated to specifically support and promote the involvement of children with special educational needs in sport and athletics, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for Sport NI, I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has provided me with a response.Due to the volume of information to be provided I have arranged for the detail to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will answer AQW 28973/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 28973/22-27 was answered on 7 July 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the PSNI’s (i) obligations; and (ii) protocols, regarding the referral and transportation of victims of sexual offences to the Rowan Sexual Assault Referral Centre; during (a) weekdays; and (b) weekends.
answer_text: PSNI’s protocols and obligations relating to the referral and transportation of victims of sexual offences to the Rowan Sexual Assault Referral Centre are operational matters for the PSNI who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. As I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the PSNI and the Policing Board, it would not be appropriate for me to comment.  You may, therefore, wish to raise this matter directly with the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 28415/22-27, what reassurances she can provide that no goods exported from Northern Ireland are entering the West Bank.
answer_text: Responsibility for export controls lies with the British Government’s ‘Export Control Joint Unit’, which sits within the Department for Business and Trade.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the steps her Department is taking to address the gap in AccessNI procedures, whereby self-employed individuals cannot apply for enhanced checks, resulting in those individuals' ability to lawfully undertake roles involving regulated activity with children or vulnerable adults.
answer_text: The Department is currently liaising with the Home Office and Ministry of Justice to explore what needs to be done to extend eligibility of Enhanced checks to those self-employed.The AccessNI disclosure process is primarily arranged to help employers and voluntary organisations to make safer recruitment decisions.  The long-standing position is that self-employed people cannot obtain an Enhanced check as they have no-one to which they are responsible for any information that might be disclosed on their certificate as they are, in effect, the employer.  The legislation requires that an employer or regulator must be able to ask an exempted question (in accordance with provisions within the Rehabilitation of Offenders (Exceptions) Order (NI) 1979) and is required to view the disclosure certificate and to make an assessment on the appropriateness of the individual to undertake the specific role.  For the self-employed there is currently no such employer / decision maker.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following changes to Causeway Hospital general surgery, will additional resources will be given to Altnagelvin Area Hospital.
answer_text: I am aware that the Western Trust has raised concerns that there could be an impact on Altnagelvin Hospital of the preferred option for general surgery in the Northern Trust. The Trust staff are confident that they have mitigations in place which will avoid any large-scale transfer of unscheduled surgical demand from Causeway to Altnagelvin.  Apart from a small number of exceptions there will be no change to the profile of patients who will be managed through Causeway ED as they will still be providing assessment and treatment for patients with surgical presentations, and for most trauma cases. It is estimated that the volume of patients bypassing Causeway by ambulance to be no more than 2 patients per week, with the majority going to Antrim. NIAS may bring a small proportion of these patients to Altnagelvin depending on pick-up location. Other than these bypass patients, NIAS will continue to bring the current profile of patients to Causeway ED, including those with possible surgical pathology.Initial modelling on the preferred option, based on catchment population suggest that an additional 300 walk-in patients per year to go to Altnagelvin Emergency Department. This modelling has been shared with the Western Trust.The Project Board for the development of the General Surgery Consultation included a representative from the Western Trust, and the Trust are committed to monitoring any impact post-implementation should I approve the proposed reconfiguration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether she, or her Department, has been informed of any allegations that junior female officers in the PSNI are being pressured into making official complaints against other officers; and (ii) to detail any communications she has had with the Police Federation for Northern Ireland in relation to any such allegations.
answer_text: As a political member of the Policing Board, you will be aware that the recruitment of both full-time and reserve police officers is an operational matter for the Chief Constable. You may therefore wish to contact the Chief Constable directly for further detail.However, it is important to note that in the previous mandate when I was in office, police numbers were increased to 7,100, and it is deeply regrettable that the subsequent suspension of the political institutions contributed to a situation where that fell so significantly. I remain firmly committed to addressing the situation and supporting the PSNI’s Workforce Recovery business case. With the recruitment and training process already underway, it is reassuring that my Department will be given first call to access up to the £7m required in future monitoring rounds in the current financial year to support the first year of the business case. Furthermore, the Finance Minister has given a commitment to pursue full funding for the business case through the multi-year Budget process, commencing after 2025-26.  I have been clear in my discussions with the Finance Minister and Executive colleagues that the stabilisation plan requires the full £200m to be made available, without which the value of year one commitments is extremely limited. It is therefore vital that subsequent years of the business case are supported in the 3-year budget process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 27193/22-27, to detail the number of occasions that funding has been (i) withheld; and (ii) withdrawn from organisations under the Musical Instruments Scheme, due to breaches involving sectarian behaviour, in the last ten years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Arts Council.	I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:	‘Funding has never been withheld or withdrawn in the past 10 years; however, one application was made ineligible in part due to online content which was deemed to be in breach of good relations.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider creating weekend cervical smear clinics to accommodate women who cannot attend during the working week.
answer_text: My Department officials have advised there have been no requests to hold weekend cervical smear clinics from providers and therefore at present there are no plans to create weekend cervical smear clinics. Coverage data for 2023/24 shows an increase in age-appropriate coverage across all Trusts compared to 2022/23 and I would encourage all women to take up the offer of cervical screening when invited to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, further to AQW 27194/22-27, to detail when (i) the independent review of the licensing system; and (ii) the review of provisions within the Licensing and Registration of Clubs (Amendment) Act (NI) 2021, are expected to conclude.
answer_text: The report on the independent review of the licensing system was published on 20 February 2025.The review of the provisions within the Licensing and Registration of Clubs (Amendment) Act (NI) 2021, is anticipated to conclude in December 2026 with the publication of the report on the third review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what steps he is taking to ensure that all children with special educational needs (SEN) are provided with an appropriate place in a special school this September, recognising that the Independent Autism Reviewer has said that any attempt to enforce enrolment of SEN children into mainstream settings would be wholly unacceptable.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that;The EA provide a range of support to our special schools as they provide for growing numbers of children with complex needs. This includes:Staffing resources to meet needsEquipment for individual children in line with SENAccommodationLink Officer support from both Special Schools and Special Provisions Division and SEN Statutory Services DivisionArea Planning for all 40 schools in the short (25-26), medium (26-28) and long term (28-35)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on at-home cervical smear provision; and (ii) to detail how many kits have been provided to women, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The UK National Screening Committee (UKNSC) announced on 24 June 2025 that they were recommending that cervical screening providers should be allowed to offer a self-sampling test option to women who never or rarely attend their routine cervical screening appointments.  This recent UKNSC recommendation will now be considered by the Northern Ireland Screening Committee before seeking my approval for any policy decision.   To date, self-sampling kits have not been offered as part of the Northern Ireland cervical screening programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what immediate actions are being taken to strengthen regulation and oversight of private ambulance providers, particularly with respect to staff training and contract compliance, as recommended in the NI Audit Office report on Ambulance Handovers published in March 2025.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) has developed an extensive internal process of monitoring, reporting, governance and assurance in relation to the use of private ambulances. In addition, NIAS plan to recruit 48 new Ambulance Care Attendants within this financial year.  These are key roles within the service with staff undergoing a robust period of in-house training upon commencement of post.  These additional staff will enable NIAS to continue to progress with its commitment to ongoing service developments and ongoing patient care delivery. They will also allow for a review of independent ambulance provision in line with recommendation 3 from the NI Audit Office report on Ambulance Handovers published in March 2025.  While NIAS has enhanced its oversight role following the recommendation by NIAO, it will also be important to explore stronger regulation of this sector. This is indeed also an emerging theme from early work on the Review of HSC’s Non-emergency Transport Strategy, which I initiated earlier this year.  While private ambulance regulation is not directly related to the Review, I anticipate that this will be progressed  by the policy team as soon as possible subject to available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people have been diagnosed with sexually transmitted infections (STI) in each of the last five years, broken down by type of STI along with their respective rates.
answer_text: A detailed breakdown of the number of people diagnosed with sexually transmitted infections (STI) from 2014 to 2024 in Northern Ireland (NI), by type of infection, along with their respective rates, is available from the PHA website at the following links:Number of new episodes for selected STIs 201-2024.pdf Rates of episodes for selected STIs 2014-2024.pdf For ease of reference, information relating to the last five available years is provided in the table below.20202021202220232024NumberRateNumberRateNumberRateNumberRateNumberRateChlamydia77540.875039.4118161.8124264.7111458.0Gonorrhoea45523.965234.2160684.1156181.3120862.9Syphilis1397.31316.919710.323012.024112.5Herpes29315.434818.344023.047724.847924.9Warts92948.991948.388946.579541.467435.1Diagnostic rates are estimated by dividing the annual number of diagnoses by estimated mid-year resident population. Rate is per 100,000 of population.The data provided is produced from the Genitourinary Medicine Clinic Activity Dataset (GUMCAD). The data provided does not include those diagnosed via the online service SH:24, however those who test positive for syphilis and gonorrhoea via SH:24 are advised to attend sexual health clinics and should be captured in GUMCAD surveillance data.The most recent report, which provides details on sexually transmitted infections within NI, from both sexual health clinics and SH:24 testing is available online via: STI Report 2023.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what specific measures his Department has implemented to support children fleeing conflict who arrive in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Children fleeing conflict arriving in Northern Ireland, where nobody in the UK holds parental responsibility, are described as separated or unaccompanied asylum-seeking children (S/UASC).  S/UASC children are subject to the same care planning arrangements as children born in Northern Ireland. In terms of specific measures implemented:All HSC Trusts have developed S/UASC specific reception and assessment placements. An additional focus is placed upon trafficked and potentially trafficked children who are referred to the Independent Guardian Service for support. A Multi Agency Risk Assessment Meeting (MARAM) is held within 48 hours of all arrivals to develop an immediate safety plan.S/UASC are assisted to get access to an immigration solicitor who will advise and oversee asylum applications, and the relevant Trust will support the young person to access ESOL (English classes), appropriate education or training places, develop independence skills and engage in voluntary work. Health, appropriate next accommodation, cultural and religious needs, emotional welfare and support are assessed and supported on an ongoing basis.  Once a young person gets a positive asylum decision, they are supported to access work, social housing, etc., and support as per leaving care obligations, remaining in place until at least 21 years of age and, if engaged in education, until 24.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what assessment has been made of the feasibility to introduce free public transport for those aged under 25, or at a minimum a further reduced fare, to encourage a transition to a greener and more socially equitable transport system.
answer_text: As part of the review of the NI Concessionary Fares Scheme, officials explored a number of options prior to the launching of the public consultation in June 2023, including the introduction of free travel for young people under the age of 24.While it was acknowledged that such a scheme could help reduce the financial burden of travel for young people, combat social isolation, and promote long-term sustainable travel habits, the proposal was not progressed further due to existing budgetary pressures on the concessionary fares programme.However, a number of discounted fares are already available.  Translink currently offer half fare to those age 5-16.  From age 16-23 Translink offer a yLink card which is a commercial product to encourage young people to use public transport. The scheme entitles them to 50% discount on bus and rail fares in the North and on Enterprise cross border services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department plans to introduce Adverse Childhood Experiences testing within the prison system, in light of the research report, 'The Prevalence and Impact of Adverse Childhood Experiences in Northern Ireland, February 2025', which highlighted the significant impact of childhood trauma on offending and life outcomes in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: While the link between adverse childhood experiences and negative outcomes for individuals, including engagement in offending behaviour, has previously been established through research, the Prevalence and Impact of Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACEs) Report which I launched in February, provided evidence for the first time of the impact of childhood trauma on the people of Northern Ireland.  Whilst the focus of the report was not on offending, it found that compared to those with no ACEs, adults with four or more were 8.3 times more likely to be arrested.   In light of this, my Department is keen to explore further the relationship between ACEs and offending behaviour and is actively considering options for progressing this area of research.Recognising that many of those who come into contact with the criminal justice system will have experienced some level of trauma, my Department has established a justice-wide Trauma Informed Practice Subgroup.  This group aims to embed a trauma informed approach right across the justice system by recognising the impact of trauma and introducing measures to mitigate across re-traumatisation.   The Northern Ireland Prison Service is a member of this group and continues to engage with the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland in order to support the development of a range of measures aimed at ensuring a trauma informed approach is applied.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what steps are being taken to conduct a cost-benefit analysis comparing the private ambulance sector with NI Ambulance Service patient care services and frontline emergency crews, in line with Recommendation 3 of the NI Audit Office report on Ambulance Handovers, published in March 2025.
answer_text: In response to Recommendation 3 of the Northern Ireland Audit Office (NIAO) report on Ambulance Handovers, the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) is in the process of conducting a cost-benefit analysis comparing the private ambulance sector with in-house capacity.The NI Audit office report was specific to the use of the Independent Sector with regard to supporting the emergency component of the service. The independent sector supports NIAS emergency response in three ways:Relief at Emergency DepartmentsConveyance of lower acuity emergency patients to hospital, and Planned transfer of patients from one location to another.In addition to the emergency service, NIAS utilise the independent sector to support the delivery of routine, non-emergency patients through the Patient Care Service (PCS).  Governance of Independent Ambulance Sector (IAS) providers is managed by NIAS through a structured assurance framework. This includes twice-yearly unannounced inspections of provider vehicles and quarterly governance meetings with all IAS contractors. These meetings include both plenary learning sessions and individual provider reviews. There are currently 15 IAS contractors on the NIAS Framework, accessible to NIAS and other Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts.In response to Recommendation 3 of the NIAO report, I have instructed officials to undertake a comprehensive review of the current non-emergency transport policy across HSC, with a view to completion in early 2026, following a public consultation exercise. The review will focus on identifying and addressing any issues with the current policy, including enhancing the eligibility criteria, as appropriate. It is important to note, however, that the Department’s policy relates specifically to transport provision for those with a medical need, as defined by a clinician.This analysis will help inform future policy decisions to ensure that patient transport services are safe, effective, and provide value for money.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has any plans to review how family courts assess and respond to allegations of domestic abuse and child distress, particularly in cases involving children with additional needs.
answer_text: The legislation that applies in private law proceedings concerning children (The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995) is the responsibility of the Department of Finance. In applying that legislation, the court’s paramount consideration should be the child’s welfare and there are a number of factors to which the court should have particular regard. These include the child’s physical, emotional and educational needs; the child’s age, sex, background and any characteristics the court considers relevant; and any harm the child has suffered or is at risk of suffering. The definition of harm covers non-physical harm and cases where a child has been affected by domestic abuse.To assist consideration of the child’s welfare, a court may ask for a social work report and, in more complex cases, may direct other expert evidence. Training and guidance for Social Services would come within the remit of the Department of Health.   Family courts deal with some of the most vulnerable people in society and it is important that the family justice system protects them. Therefore, any report of domestic abuse or child distress should be taken seriously. However, the principle of judicial independence means that in individual cases, the application of the legislative framework and consideration of the evidence before the court is a judicial matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his considerations of funding weekend cervical smear clinics to improve accessibility for women.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, the Cervical Screening Programme is managed by the Public Health Agency (PHA). PHA is not currently funded for the delivery and provision of weekend cervical smear clinics and advises that the responsibility for the delivery and provision of weekend cervical smear clinics sits with primary care. My officials have confirmed that no requests for weekend cervical clinics have been received from any organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what steps her Department is taking to ensure that expressions of trauma by protective parents in family court proceedings are not misinterpreted as evidence of instability or emotional harm.
answer_text: Under the legislation (The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995) that applies in proceedings between parents relating to arrangements for children - for which the Department of Finance has policy responsibility - the court’s paramount consideration should be the child’s welfare. The Member may therefore wish to contact the Department of Finance for more information on this matter.When considering the welfare of the child, family courts should take account of evidence given by parents, together with the other evidence before the court. It is important that reports of trauma are taken seriously. However, assessment of the evidence in an individual case, having regard to the legislative framework, is a judicial matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether the Mentoring Circles Programme available in Magilligan Prison, is operating in all other custodial institutions; and (ii) whether she will take steps to remove the requirement that a prisoner must be found in possession of drugs, in order to access the Programme, recognising that it may be beneficial to all who wish to participate.
answer_text: The Circle Programme is currently delivered within the Prisoner Development Unit at Magilligan Prison.The Programme has been developed to support those in custody who are struggling with addictions issues, evidenced through failing a drug test, not those found in possession of illicit substances.The Circle directly follows on from the START 360 AD:EPT “Getting Smart” programme and forms part of a suite of interventions, which have proven successful as part of a whole prison approach in stabilising behaviour and reducing the number of prisoners taking illicit substances whilst in custody.The Northern Ireland Prison Service continually assesses the priority needs of the population to best utilise programmes to maximise positive outcomes for prisoners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many hospitality venues have received training as part of the Ask for Angela initiative.
answer_text: Ask for Angela is a joint venture between the PSNI and Hospitality Ulster and as such my Department does not hold this information. A list of local venues which have received training and registered as part of the Northern Ireland Ask for Angela scheme is helpfully provided by PSNI via their website. For any further information you may wish to direct your query towards the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail their Department's engagement with the United States of America's Government, regarding the challenges faced by people from Northern Ireland in obtaining a J1 visa.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of students from Northern Ireland who have attended university in the United States, over the last ten years for (i) undergraduate; and (ii) postgraduate studies.
answer_text: Study USA is my Department’s flagship international mobility programme. The number of participants in the programme in each of the last ten academic years was as follows:Academic Year Study USA Participants   2014/15682015/16542016/17472017/18522018/19482019/20482020/21*02021/22552022/23442023/2450* Cancelled due to Covid-19Statistics are not held on students attending universities in the United States by other routes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant of AQW 27994/22-27, in the absence of departmental research, whether he will commission an independent assessment into the comparative welfare outcomes of racing and companion greyhounds, including injury rates, rehoming data, and life expectancy.
answer_text: My focus currently is on delivering the priorities as outlined in my Animal Welfare Pathway. The welfare of racing greyhounds was not raised as an issue in the stakeholder engagement undertaken when identifying the priorities to be included in the Animal Welfare Pathway.	 However, I am cognisant of the recent developments regarding greyhound racing in both New Zealand and Wales, and my department will continue to monitor their progress, along with any evidence gathered, in relation to welfare, to inform decisions around commissioning of any additional research.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer AQW 25434/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 25434/22-27 on 6 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of meeting the target of 80 per cent electricity consumption from renewable sources by 2030.
answer_text: One of my four key objectives for the Economy is to reduce carbon emissions; generating sufficient renewable electricity is key to facilitate this change.We must build on our previous success in renewables development and the Executive needs to take a collaborative, whole system approach to facilitate delivery of the 80% by 2030 target. Whilst my Department has legislative responsibility for the target, collaboration across departments and with wider stakeholders, including industry, will be essential to the successful delivery of this target.Whilst the Department has legislative responsibility for the target, collaboration across departments and with wider stakeholders, including industry, will be essential to the successful delivery of this target.Progress is already being made, and we have recently appointed a Project Director from the Strategic Investment Board to drive this work forward. The Project Director will provide significant practical and strategic leadership in developing and delivering plans and work closely with senior leaders in DfE’s Energy Group and develop and maintain effective relationships across departments, delivery partners and industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what assessment his Department has made of the presence of per- and poly fluoroalkyl substances in rivers, lakes, and drinking water supplies; and (ii) what steps are being taken to monitor, report, and reduce contamination in line with the EU REACH Regulation.
answer_text: The only legislative driver currently in place for the monitoring of Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) in inland surface waters is The Water Framework Directive (Classification, Priority Substances and Shellfish Waters) Regulations (NI) 2015 which transposes Annex 10 of the Water Framework Directive and which lists PFOS (Perfluorooctane sulfonate) as a Priority Hazardous Substance. The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) is currently developing a method of analysis for PFOS, and no data is currently available. There is currently no statutory requirement to monitor PFAS substances in drinking water in Northern Ireland. However, the Drinking Water Inspectorate has carried out preliminary testing for PFAS in private water supplies. In addition, Northern Ireland Water has undertaken PFAS surveys on its raw water sources and treated drinking water supply.The EU REACH Regulation (EC 1907/2006) applies in Northern Ireland as it comes under the Windsor Framework and is enforced in Northern Ireland through The REACH Enforcement Regulations 2008. The NIEA inspects industrial businesses using chemicals that are registered or restricted under REACH and has enforcement powers that inspectors can apply where the regulations have not been complied with.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any steps he will take to address the impact of second home ownership on housing affordability, especially along the North Coast.
To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 27193/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 27193/22-27 was answered on 26 June 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any discussions they have held in the last six months with the R&A regarding the future of The Open Championship at Royal Portrush.
answer_text: We are incredibly proud to be hosting The 153rd Open Championship at Royal Portrush this year.  Growing a globally competitive and sustainable economy is a key priority in our Programme for Government. The Open is an opportunity to showcase not only our golfing talent, but also our ability to successfully host major events and our tourism potential.   Following on from the The 148th Open in 2019, the R&A has continued its commitment by holding the second of three planned Championships here.  We met with the Chief Executive of the R&A on the 27th May 2025 to discuss the upcoming Championship and we look forward to discussing future events, following what we are positive will be an extremely successful 153rd Championship, later this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail  (i) the timeline his Department has established to close the data gaps on non-household municipal waste reporting; and (ii) how the published data will be independently verified to ensure transparency and accountability on progress towards statutory recycling targets.
answer_text: My department currently obtain data from WasteDataFlow (WDF), the web-based system for municipal waste data reporting by all UK local authorities (councils) to government. However, this information relates exclusively to council collected municipal waste and there are no plans to go beyond its current remit at present.My officials have also been working collaboratively with the other nations across the UK on the development of a digital waste tracking (DWT) system to report waste data and provide the Department with a powerful tool to track non-household municipal waste. While timescales for implementation are currently unclear, a UK-wide DWT system could potentially take over from WDF in the future. In addition, work is currently ongoing to address the data gap for municipal recycling rates in Northern Ireland. This work is due to complete in March 2027 and will provide DAERA with current data in respect of municipal waste, commercial and industrial wastes (C&I) and construction, demolition and excavation wastes (C, D&E). Currently, local authority collected (LAC) municipal waste data and reports are published as accredited Official Statistics by the Statistical and Analytical Services Branch (SASB) within my department. Quarterly and annual validation on the data supplied by local councils is carried out by the Control & Data Management Branch of NIEA as well as SASB. These official statistics are independently reviewed by the Office for Statistics Regulation (OSR) and are confirmed to comply with the standards of trustworthiness, quality and value in the Code of Practice for Statistics.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether her Department has conducted a recent assessment of (a) the impact of drug availability within prisons; and (b) the lack of systematic recording of suicide attempts on the rehabilitation of repeat offenders; and (ii) what plans are in place to address these issues to improve prisoner outcomes and reduce reoffending rates.
answer_text: (i) (a) The Northern Ireland Prison Service continuously monitors the impact of drug availability within prisons, both on individuals and on the prison regime. Many people entering our prisons have long-standing issues regarding addictions which lead to them continuing their drug seeking behaviour whilst in prison. Prison Officers work closely with colleagues from other agencies, including the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust who deliver healthcare services in prisons, to help address individual issues and also to reduce the supply of unauthorised drugs. (i) (b) All incidents of self-harm by those in prison are recorded. Under the joint Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) and South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust’s (the Trust) Supporting People at Risk Evolution (SPAR Evo) procedures, self-harm is detailed as:•	Taking overdose of medication, illicit substances or poison.•	Cutting self.•	Biting, picking, scratching skin.•	Burning skin.•	Inserting objects into own body/swallowing.•	Banging/hitting oneself off walls, tables, etc.•	Exercising excessively.•	Pulling out hair.•	Over-eating or under-eating.•	Getting into fight where person knows they will get hurt.(ii) As recommended by the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority in 2021, NIPS and the Trust commissioned an external review of SPAR Evo which reported in May 2024, the recommendations of which are being implemented. NIPS takes its duty of care seriously in supporting all those in prison who may be experiencing a period of distress or crisis on a multidisciplinary basis.  Rehabilitation work is prioritised to support and challenge prisoners to change and address their offending behaviour to reduce their risk of reoffending on release. Voluntary and Community Sector partners deliver a suite of interventions ranging from support with dealing with addictions, to mental health challenges, and past trauma.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer AQW 27250/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 27250/22-27 on 10 July 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will answer AQW 27994/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 27994/22-27 was answered on 19 June 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant of AQW 28154/22-27, to outline what specific, structured educational provision his Department will make available this year for children experiencing prolonged Emotionally Based School Avoidance.
answer_text: At present there are a number of services that support schools with a range of emotional health and wellbeing issues. Funded under the DE/DoH Emotional Health & Wellbeing Framework in Education Settings, services such as Being Well Doing Well: A Whole School Approach to Emotional Health and Wellbeing, works at a systemic level in schools to empower and enable teachers to support the emotional health and wellbeing needs of their pupils.https://www.eani.org.uk/services/pupil-health-and-wellbeing/being-well-doing-well-programmeIn addition, the HOPE Counselling & Therapy Service is available in post primary schools. https://www.eani.org.uk/services/pupil-health-and-wellbeing/school-counselling-and-therapy-hope-serviceExceptional Teaching Arrangements are available where a mental health condition has been diagnosed, and a need identified by a medical consultant.https://send.eani.org.uk/support-services/exceptional-teachingFurthermore, the Education Welfare Service is currently recruiting 16 specialist posts, five of which will have a specific focus on Anxiety Based School Non Attendance.The EA Educational Psychology Service is also developing training for schools on the management of Emotionally Based School Non-Attendance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant of AQW 27327/22-27, given that neither his Department nor the Education Authority holds data on Key Stage 2 swimming proficiency outcomes, to detail (i) any plans to collect and publish this information; and (ii) any specific actions his Department will take to ensure all pupils receive consistent and equitable access to quality swimming instruction, regardless of school resources or location.
answer_text: Swimming is a statutory component of the Northern Ireland Curriculum for all schools as part of the Physical Education area of learning at Key Stage 2. Schools are best placed to make informed decisions about delivery that take account of their local context, facilities and available resources.I have no plans to ask schools to provide data on swimming proficiency.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether her Department has conducted any independent evaluation of the current use and economic potential of the former Shackleton barracks site, Ballykelly.
answer_text: MJM Renewables was selected as the preferred purchaser of the Shackleton site following an assessment process in 2016. 	The Executive Office no longer owns the Shackleton site.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a breakdown of spend on road repairs for each district electoral area, over the last three years.
answer_text: Details of total capital and resource structural maintenance expenditure on the roads infrastructure by District Council for each of the last three financial years available is set out in the Table below.
