question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on when the Glenariff Forest Park playpark is due to reopen.
answer_text: The playpark is currently closed pending necessary repairs for safety reasons. This type of facility requires the sourcing of bespoke components to replace unserviceable equipment. Forest Service expects that these will be available in the near future to allow works to be completed and the playpark to reopen, in the next few weeks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail why no woman has been appointed to the Independent Review of Teacher Workload panel.
answer_text: As part of the Teachers’ Pay Agreement 2024-25, it was agreed that the panel that would take forward the Independent Review of Teacher Workload would consist of three members.  This would comprise of a chairperson appointed by the Minister, a nominee from the NITC trade unions and a nominee from Management Side of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee.   I selected Paul Sweeney for the role of chairperson due the significant experience he has in senior roles and his specific knowledge of the education sector from his time as Permanent Secretary of the Department of Education.  The other two members, who also have extensive experience of the education sector and workload issues, were nominated separately in line with the agreement noted above.  Both Management Side and NITC trade unions had complete autonomy in relation to their nominations. It wasn’t until the separate nominations had been agreed and submitted that the full composition of the panel was known.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of Lord Alderdice's comments in relation to loyalist paramilitary transitioning when he said that there comes a point when you have to say no this hasn't been delivered, it's not going to be delivered and actually by continuing we are making it worse.
answer_text: I understand and share many of the concerns expressed by Lord Alderdice.  We must be honest about where progress has been made in the fight against paramilitarism and where it has not.  Whilst some former paramilitaries have disengaged from their previous offending, other paramilitary groups have already transitioned — not into peaceful civic actors, but into organised criminal gangs.  The idea that paramilitary groups can transition and become coherent, law-abiding entities has, in my view, long since passed the point of credibility.  Continuing to treat them as though they are on a path to reform risks legitimising their influence and undermining public confidence in the rule of law.  I have previously expressed my concerns about the intention of the UK and Irish Governments to appoint an independent interlocutor to engage with paramilitary groups on transition.  In my view, this process may give these groups a platform and a credibility which they do not deserve and which is not afforded, correctly, to any other organised crime gang.I have consistently said that there is no place in our society for coercive control, criminality, or the threat of violence masquerading as community leadership.  While I support efforts to encourage individuals leave behind paramilitarism and crime, public resources should not be used to support those who are claiming to be part of a transition process while continuing to exert control and engage in harm.  Our focus must now be on supporting individuals who have genuinely left behind both paramilitarism and criminality, and on building wider community resilience through education, opportunity, and respect for the rule of law.    Executive departments continue to work closely with other statutory organisations, as well as community and voluntary sector partners, to deliver change on the ground.  I am lead Minister for the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime which is active across all of Northern Ireland.  During 2025/26, the Programme will invest £16m in a range of projects which provide support for people and communities who are vulnerable to paramilitary influence and harm.  The Programme works to reduce harm in the here and now and prevent harm occurring in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail the number of businesses supported by (a) Invest NI; and (b) local council-led funding schemes that have, in the last five years, progressed from micro or small enterprises into medium or large businesses; and (ii) what assessment her Department has made of the overall effectiveness of these schemes in enabling substantial business growth, particularly in terms of turnover, job creation, and long-term sustainability.
answer_text: In the last 5 years Invest NI has supported 4,120 individual businesses of which 85% are micro or small businesses. Invest NI’s primary focus is on boosting business investment across the North to maximise business growth and help our businesses become more productive, competitive, and sustainable. This includes supporting micro and small businesses to scale to become medium and large businesses. To demonstrate the impact of Invest NI support, a sample of 612 micro and small businesses it supported were surveyed in 2019 and by 2024, 65 (or 11%) had increased their employment to become Medium or Large enterprises with more than 50 employees.  Collectively these 65 businesses have created 2,124 new jobs and increased sales by £520m over the five years, providing additional high quality, sustainable employment and boosting economic growth.Invest NI, as the intermediary for the Local Economic Development (LED) Programme, supported 41 projects through the North’s 11 councils under the EU’s Investment in Growth Jobs 2014–2020 programme, funded by the European Regional Development Fund (ERDF). Councils spent £20.32m on these projects supported by assistance of 60% from ERDF, via Invest NI, and 20% from Invest NI’s baseline budget. Analysis commissioned by Invest NI on the effectiveness of the LED programme demonstrated that it supported over 34,400 businesses which created 15,666 jobs. LED activity ceased in September 2023 and Invest NI does not currently provide any financial assistance towards council’s business growth activity and therefore cannot comment on the effectiveness of their current schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any specific steps which are being taken to strengthen media literacy within the education system, in order to equip pupils with the skills to critically assess information online.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Curriculum outlines that pupils should be provided with opportunities to develop knowledge and understanding of e-safety and acceptable online behaviour and Council for the Curriculum, Examinations Assessment (CCEA) has developed a number of Digital Well Being Resources which are available on the website. These cover all Key Stages. To ensure that emerging risks, including misinformation and deep fakes, are appropriately reflected within digital literacy education, my officials work with the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) and the Safer Schools Northern Ireland app. Officials are also working with a range of education partners to develop guidance for schools on Artificial Intelligence. The guidance, which I anticipate will be made available to schools in the 2025/26 academic year, will include online safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department will now commit to suspending the processing of the seven mineral prospecting applications, namely (i) CA3/25; (ii) CA1/25; (iii) DGA4/25; (iv) DGA3/25; (v) KDRA4/25; (vi) KDRA1/25; and (vii) OMA4/25), pending the completion of a Strategic Environmental Assessment and an Equality Impact Assessment, in light of the documented failures in consultation, environmental screening, and cumulative impact assessment.
answer_text: The processing of the seven Mineral Prospecting Licence applications has already been completed. The seven Mineral Prospecting Licences were granted by my Department on 08 May 2025. It would not be legal to suspend the licences.The process for providing a Mineral Prospecting Licence is set out in law and cannot lawfully be contravened by a Minister.  A detailed explanation for the decisions, based on the existing legislative framework, has been published on the DfE website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions his Department is taking to support, promote and protect Irish language learners and community classes, in light of the recent hate incident in Belfast where threatening anti-Irish language leaflets were placed on students’ cars and given that sectarian intimidation led to the cancellation of an Irish class last year in Castlerock.
answer_text: My Department provides around £3.5m in annual funding to Foras na Gaeilge, who have legislative responsibility to promote the Irish language in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the PSNI’s admission that a five-night St Patrick’s Day trip to Washington DC cost almost double the initially stated amount, what oversight mechanisms will be put in place to ensure future transparency and best value for money.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  The Member may wish to note that the role of Internal Audit is to provide independent assurance that the PSNI’s risk management, governance and internal control processes are operating effectively.  Internal Audit submits regular reports on the management of key business risks, and the effectiveness of the system of internal control, making recommendations for improvement where appropriate. This is formally reported to the PSNI Audit Risk and Assurance Committee at each meeting. For further information, I would suggest that you contact the PSNI and the Policing Board directly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any new measures he will take to ensure that education around the issues of racism and xenophobia is provided in schools.
answer_text: Education on issues of racism and xenophobia in schools falls outside the remit of my Department and is the responsibility of the Department of Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of existing electric vehicle charge points currently operational across Northern Ireland; and (ii) what measures are in place to repair those that are not operational.
answer_text: The provision and operation of both Electric Vehicles and publicly accessible Electric Vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure is a commercially led activity in the north.  Responsibility for the maintenance, development and utilisation of charge points rests solely with the Charge Point Operators (CPOs). Relevant CPOs are best placed to advise you on the position of their current installations.There has been significant investment from CPOs in EV infrastructure over the last two years. The commercial publicly accessible charge point network now almost 700 chargers in place here, a growth in EV charging provision of 75% since April 2023. The Rapid/Ultra Rapid charging public network, which offers the fastest, most convenient method of charging has grown by over 400% here in the same period.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether he has engaged with Facebook and other social media platforms regarding the need for the urgent removal of posts or pages that share the addresses and other identifying details of migrants and other vulnerable members of our community, who are at risk of racist attacks.
answer_text: No contact has been made with Facebook or any other social media platforms regarding this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to develop a railway station serving Belfast International Airport.
answer_text: I can confirm that Translink received funding, through the Department of Transport’s Union Connectivity Fund, to undertake a feasibility study regarding the re-opening of the railway line between Lisburn and Antrim. As well as considering engineering requirements, demand forecasting and cost estimates, the study is considering reopening the three stations previously in operation on this line at Ballinderry, Glenavy and Crumlin. In addition, three locations for a new station at Belfast International Airport are also being appraised to identify the preferred option. The feasibility studies for the projects funded by the Union Connectivity are at an advanced stage and are due to be submitted to my Department shortly for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any specific steps his Department will take to ensure that migrant communities and new arrivals are made to feel safe and welcome.
answer_text: My Department has a network of 35 Jobs & Benefit offices located across Northern Ireland where staff are committed to supporting people who have recently joined our communities. Individuals are allocated a dedicated Work Coach who will provide ongoing tailored support and signpost them to specialist organisations in local areas.The Discretionary Support scheme assists with a range of short-term financial support needs including, for example, living expenses and essential household items. The scheme is open to all including migrant communities subject to meeting the eligibility criteria.My officials work with Belfast City Council’s Wider University and Lower Ormeau Interagency Group to improve the well-being of residents in the area, address and improve community cohesion and integration of newcomer communities.The NI Housing Executive, an arm’s length body sponsored by my Department provides a range of schemes and assistance.  The Refugee Integration Team assists households through the transition and exit from the asylum process. Where an individual requires assistance, the Team will link in with support providers and triage the household to the relevant Housing Solutions Team within the Housing Executive.Through the Community Involvement & Cohesion Strategy it has created opportunities to improve community relations, address difficult and contentious issues, and help develop greater bonding, bridging and linking opportunities within its estates.	 Their Race Relations Officers work with Good Relations Officers to create more stable and safer neighbourhoods by collaborating with local community and voluntary organisations to foster a community led approach to promoting positive race relations within and between diverse ethnic communities and the wider community.Last year the Housing Executive funded 32 projects across Northern Ireland. These projects are designed to foster integration, raise awareness, and greater understanding of cultural diversity.Libraries NI, another arm’s length body sponsored by my Department, provides a safe and welcoming environment to all people living, working or studying here. Resources include welcome messages in multiple languages, book stock in community languages, newspapers and magazines in a range of languages together with translation software. There is an accredited Library of Sanctuary located at Ormeau Road Library and staff work with organisations including City of Sanctuary and the Red Cross throughout the year to deliver multi-cultural events including during Good Relations Week and Refugee Week, which this year had over 25 events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any assessment his Department has made of the Barnett consequentials expected from the rollout of expanded Free School Meals in England; and (ii) whether this funding would be sufficient to cover the estimated £22 million annual cost of extending the same entitlement to pupils in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The rollout of expanded free school meals in England is scheduled to commence in September 2026.As this change will not take effect until the 2026-27 period, there will be no Barnett consequentials for Northern Ireland in 2025-26.  Further, given that the policy is still at an early stage of development it is not possible for me to comment on the quantum of the future Barnett consequentials for Northern Ireland or to confirm whether such funding would be sufficient to cover the estimated cost of extending the same entitlement to pupils in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 27993/22-27, what specific monitoring or enforcement mechanisms are in place to ensure compliance with the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 at greyhound racing tracks and training facilities.
answer_text: The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 does not provide specific monitoring or enforcement mechanisms for individual breeds or species. Rather, it empowers councils to enforce welfare requirements for all companion animals, including greyhounds, to ensure they are safeguarded from unnecessary suffering.  This responsibility applies in all settings where companion animals are kept.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what discussions she has had with the Chief Constable regarding the resourcing and operational capacity of the PSNI, recognising that the recent unrest in Ballymena resulted in only six arrests on 10 June 2025.
answer_text: I meet the Chief Constable on a regular basis to discuss a wide range of policing matters.  I can confirm that our most recent meeting took place on 18 June, during which we discussed the impact of the recent disorder and violence on the PSNI.  I assured the Chief Constable that policing has my full support, and my thoughts are with the officers who were injured as a result of the unrest.You may wish to note that, as of 17 June, the PSNI has made 31 arrests in connection with the recent disorder.  Of those arrested, 23 individuals have been charged in relation to these incidents.You may wish to contact the PSNI directly for further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his Department’s assessment of the environmental impact of disposable vapes in the last five years; and (ii) for an estimate of how many have been disposed of through the most environmentally sound methods during that period.
answer_text: Improper disposal of single-use vapes create significant environmental impacts, including pollution, an increase in landfill waste, and increased greenhouse gas emissions. These devices contain batteries and other critical raw materials that can and should be recycled, but often end up in landfill or incineration, releasing harmful substances into the environment and endangering wildlife. Fire risks are also associated with their unsafe disposal or inappropriate mixing with the recycling stream due to the lithium batteries they contain. Vapes that are littered cause visual pollution and can lead to dangerous chemicals entering the environment. A UK wide impact assessment was carried out in 2024 and can viewed at https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/65eb1dac62ff489bab87b371/disposable-vapes-impact-assessment.pdf.My department does not hold specific single-use vape disposal data for the relevant 5 year period. However, a study commissioned by Defra to enhance the evidence base on the single-use vape market and its environmental impacts within the UK, estimated that less than 1% of all single-use vapes sold in the UK are recycled, due to limited recycling capacity.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 27722/22-27, how his Department ensures that hazardous waste consignment data is verified and reported in compliance with Article 35 of the EU Waste Framework Directive, particularly regarding traceability from origin to disposal.
answer_text: The requirements of Article 35 of the Waste Framework Directive are transposed to Northern Ireland under The Hazardous Waste Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005. Under these regulations authorised sites receiving Hazardous Waste must keep records of waste movements and submit related documentation to the Department. The NIEA records and checks hazardous waste movement data on an internal database and actively carries out site inspections as part of a monitoring and inspection programme to ensure compliance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail what discussions she is having with the PSNI to ensure the safety and wellbeing of new arrivals and migrant communities; and (ii) what discussions she is having with her Executive colleagues to tackle online misinformation and hate speech.
answer_text: The safety and wellbeing of new arrivals and migrant communities is an issue that is largely driven by the Executive's broader work implementing the recently published Refugee Integration Strategy and through the work of the inter-Departmental Strategic Planning Group on Race Relations and Integration, convened by The Executive Office.As recently as Wednesday 11th June, I joined the First Minister and deputy First Minister in a meeting with Assistant Chief Constable Ryan Henderson to discuss the then developing public disorder and attacks on members of our minority ethnic and migrant communities in Northern Ireland. Civil contingency arrangements were stood up immediately following the outbreak of the trouble and continue on a multi-agency basis in order to: protect people and property; bring the perpetrators of violence and hate crimes to justice; and provide practical support and assistance to those affected. As that work moves from initial response to recovery, I have raised with Executive colleagues the need to revisit the drivers for such disorder in discussion with the police and have communicated my view that there is an urgent need for an agreed cross-Executive approach to addressing the multiple issues that inform racist attitudes, inter-community tension and fuel hate in Northern Ireland.The recent violent disorder and racist attacks exposed the dangerous consequences of the role of social media in spreading misinformation, creating fear and igniting violent unrest. I am committed to protecting those most vulnerable in our society from online abuse and hate speech.Judge Marrinan’s review of hate crime legislation made several recommendations in relation to online harms noting the threat the Internet poses.  You will be aware that the Online Safety Act (2023) places greater obligations on social media companies to keep people safe online and to provide greater protection for victims of hate speech. Officials are continuing to monitor progress on the implementation of the legislation. The Department is broadly satisfied that that the Act delivers Judge Marrinan’s recommendations and will strengthen provisions on stirring up hatred. False communication offences and threatening communications offences in Northern Ireland to address online harms have already been upgraded as a result of the Online Safety Act.Protecting and educating children and young people from online harms is a key priority. The Online Safety Strategy, which is co-ordinated by the Department of Health and which involves my Department and the Department of Education amongst others – aims to educate, support and involve a wide range of key stakeholders, including, parents, carers and the wider family as well as schools, colleagues and youth organisations and practitioners working with children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the total (i) value; and (ii) volume of goods and services, (a) exported from Northern Ireland to Israel; and (b) imported from Israel to Northern Ireland, since 7 October 2023, broken down by sector, including the monetary value involved in each sector.
answer_text: According to HMRC’s Regional Trade Statistics, the North’s goods exports to Israel from Q4 2023 to Q1 2025 were £45 million. This represented 0.27% of the North’s total goods exports for that period. The net mass of goods exports was 4,045 tonnes. The top export category was Medicinal and pharmaceutical products totalling £14 million, representing 31% of total goods exports to Israel for the period. This was followed by Machinery specialized for particular industries at £6.1 million (14%) and Power generating machinery & equipment totalling £5.5 million (12%).  Table 1 provides the value and weight of the North’s exports to Israel using 2 digit Standard International Trade Classification codes (SITC 2). Goods imports from Israel for the same period were £22.2 million. This represented 0.15% of the North’s total good imports. The net mass of goods imports totalled 1,778 tonnes.The top import category was Non-metallic mineral manufactures totalling £5.3 million (24%), followed by Electrical machinery, apparatus and appliances at £4.1 million (19%) and Professional, scientific and controlling instruments and appliances at £3.9 million (17%). Table 2 reports the value and weight of the North’s imports from Israel.No data is available on the North’s services trade with Israel. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps he is taking to ensure that upcoming WEEE reforms will significantly expand public access to convenient and consistent collection points for electrical waste, with particular emphasis on promoting reuse in line with the waste hierarchy.
answer_text: My department, working in conjunction with UK and the other devolved governments, wishes to make it easier for householders to access WEEE collection points. Following on from the 2024 Call for Evidence, we are currently collecting additional evidence and views to assist further policy development in some key areas. These include assessing the viability of introducing a household collection system, free collection of large domestic appliances by retailers on delivery of a new item and increasing customers’ in-store take back options. Taken together, these proposals represent our intentions for WEEE reform, with the ultimate aim of delivering increased levels of separately collected WEEE for re-use and recycling.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 27194/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 29174/22-27 was answered on 25 June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of the iNEQE Safeguarding Group's roll-out of the Safer Schools Northern Ireland App; and (ii) to detail in what ways this has been successfully used to prevent drugs deaths amongst young people.
answer_text: (i)  My department is advised that some 922 schools have created a schools account on the Safer Schools Northern Ireland app with a total of over 55,000 downloads taking place by schools, parents and students up to April 2025.(ii)  The Safer Schools Northern Ireland app, provided by INEQE Safeguarding Group, is focussed primarily on the provision of safeguarding information. The information requested is not held.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will enter into urgent discussions with Ulster University to help secure the future of the Riverside Theatre, recognising that if funding is not found, Coleraine and the wider North Coast region stand to lose a vital arts hub.
answer_text: The Arts Council, an Arm’s-Length Body of my Department, is the funding and development body for the arts in Northern Ireland.  As such, it has already engaged with arts stakeholders and is well positioned to contribute to the ongoing discussions about the arts offering in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department's assessment of the potential impact that losing Scoil Spreagtha will have on language learning goals, recognising that the Languages for the Future report stated that widening access to language learning across the school age range would be of significant benefit to all.
answer_text: My Department fully recognises the educational value of second language learning and acknowledge the positive impact and popularity of the Scoil Spreagtha scheme in this regard. It is regrettable that the organisations which initially funded the scheme are no longer in a position to continue their support due to ongoing budgetary pressures.Nonetheless, my Department remains firmly committed to promoting and supporting language learning in our schools, including both Irish and other modern languages. The recent Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum has highlighted the importance of broadening language provision, specifically recommending the introduction of languages other than English at Key Stage 2.In response to this, I have approved the establishment of a Curriculum Taskforce to lead on the implementation of these recommendations. This taskforce will work collaboratively with educators and stakeholders across Northern Ireland to develop a practical and ambitious curriculum framework. This work will provide a refreshed and forward-looking context for language learning in primary schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 26846/22-27, to outline (i) what specific interventions will be introduced in the next twelve months to accelerate municipal recycling rates; and (ii) when will full data on non-household municipal waste be available to assess overall progress toward the 2035 target.
answer_text: As detailed in my response to AQW 26846/22-27, analysis of responses to the Rethinking Our Resources consultation has helped inform a draft Departmental Response which will provide policy direction. It is my intention to bring this to my Executive colleagues in the coming weeks to seek their support in moving forward together to not only meet our legal targets for recycling, but to also help drive green growth and develop our circular economy in Northern Ireland.Work is currently ongoing to address the data gap for municipal recycling rates in Northern Ireland. This work is due to complete in March 2027 and will provide DAERA with current data in respect of municipal waste, commercial and industrial wastes (C&I) and construction, demolition and excavation wastes (C, D&E). This is a highly technical and detailed piece of work that cannot be completed in a short timescale given the number of participants required to gather the data, and to allow for seasonal variation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department will approach Ulster University to provide financial support to maintain Riverside Theatre, recognising the vital significance of the Arts in maintaining and growing Northern Ireland's film and television industry.
answer_text: I recognise the cultural and historical significance of the Riverside Theatre as the oldest professional theatre outside of Belfast. 		The decision to close the theatre is deeply regrettable. 	However, Ulster University is an autonomous institution and decisions in relation to the theatre are a matter for them.  The Minister and officials remain open to discussing any issues affecting the institution and its campuses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what specific measures his Department is taking to reduce waiting times for respite care for parents, recognising that the current waiting times can lead to significant adverse impacts on those unable to access these services.
answer_text: My Department would acknowledge that the Residential Short Break waiting times can create stressful circumstances for parents of children with disability. My Department’s strategic response to the increasing level of Children with Disability need across Northern Ireland has been set out through a regional Framework for Children with Disability. This has been partly progressed through the Regional Children’s Services Strategic Reform Programme, which my Department is leading on, and which was established in response to Professor Ray Jones’ Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services. The regional needs of Children with Disability are being considered across specific workstreams focusing on Residential Care (Workstream 3), Fostering (Workstream 4), and Children with Disability (Workstream 5). Focused Work in respect of the projected residential care need for Children with Disability across the five HSC Trusts, in addition to work regarding enhancing the Short Break provision (both residential & fostering) for Children with Disability across the five HSC Trusts is being completed through the Regional Social Care Reform Workstreams. This work remains ongoing, and it will form the basis for future commissioning of services and investment plans in the coming years. Within the Reform Programme, we are looking carefully at the models of residential care, short breaks and fostering for children which are working well to see how they could be replicated and expanded across Northern Ireland. The Strategic Reform Programme aims to ensure that we have the right services to meet the holistic needs of young people (including those with disability), now and in the future.In October 2024, I announced recurrent funding, to improve services for Children with Disability. This is in acknowledgement of the pressures in this area which were also highlighted in the BBC Spotlight programme “I am not okay.”To date, my Department has worked with Trusts to assess what was deliverable on the ground within the 2024/25 financial year. The Health and Social Care Trusts have focussed on meeting initial short-term priorities. The Funding has been utilised to develop various initiatives across multiple Trusts to improve family and community support services for Children with Disability, enhance short break provision and expand targeted care programs. Collectively, the Trusts plans aim to meet the short-term priorities, such as increasing short break provision and delivering tailored family support services. In addition, the Trusts do have plans to increase contracts with a range of voluntary and community services to provide additional family support, or breaks from caring through youth club type provision, after school clubs and enhanced opportunities for socialisation and personal development. It is understood that it will be necessary to address the gap in residential care, otherwise the short breaks service will continue to be vulnerable to children entering the

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 27194/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 27194 was answered on 25 June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline whether his Department will take measures to ensure that all schools establish coordinated student health teams that adopt a systematic, multidisciplinary approach to support the emotional, mental, and physical well-being of pupils.
answer_text: My department has no plans to require schools to establish student health teams. Schools, however, can access support via the projects that are funded under the joint Department of Education and Health Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant of AQW 25263/22-27, to detail the steps his Department is taking to ensure that children experiencing prolonged Emotionally Based School Avoidance who are unable to return to regular attendance can still access their right to an education, as outlined in Article 28 of the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child.
answer_text: My officials, along with colleagues in the Education Authority are concerned about the growing number of pupils experiencing Emotionally Based School Non-Attendance and continue to provide support and advice to schools in the management of absence due to these circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant of AQW 27192/22-27, whether the Minister will ensure that CCEA reviews and revises its exam content to prevent questions which could be considered harmful and inappropriate from appearing in future papers.
answer_text: As previously noted, the setting of questions for public examinations is a matter for the relevant exam board in line with conditions set down by the regulator. In relation to drafting questions for examination papers, CCEA has provided my officials with assurances about its robust quality assurance processes, its compliance with the relevant regulatory conditions, and that it will continue to monitor examination content to prevent publication of questions that could be considered harmful and inappropriate.It is important for the integrity of CCEA’s qualifications that this process is allowed to operate without external interference.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department's assessment of the current arrangements for scenarios where separated parents disagree on a school move, specifically where the primary carer supports the move but it is prevented by the secondary carer, with special consideration of cases where this may involve coercive control by a former partner.
answer_text: Where parents cannot agree on a preferred school for a child, the Education Authority’s (EA’s) Admissions Service will contact both parents to seek a single application for the child.  Where this is not possible, the EA advises parents to obtain a Specific Issues Order from the Court.  Where separate applications have been submitted in respect of a child, they will both be progressed during the school admissions process, until agreement is reached or a Specific Issues Order is obtained, enabling one placement offer to be made.The Department is acutely aware of the serious impact coercive control can have on both parents and children. While schools must remain neutral in family disputes, they must be alert to any safeguarding concerns and to follow established child protection procedures, including making appropriate referrals where such behaviour is suspected.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he is aware of research on the psychological consequences for children forced to witness or participate in acts of animal cruelty.
answer_text: The protection of children is the primary responsibility of other statutory agencies, therefore my officials would not be familiar with this research. Any incidents involving children witnessing or participating in criminal acts, including animal cruelty, should be reported immediately to the PSNI.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what measures are in place to safeguard the welfare of racing greyhounds within animal welfare legislation.
answer_text: Under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, all dogs, including greyhounds, must be protected from pain, suffering, injury, and disease. This obligation applies in all settings, including races. Any concerns regarding the treatment and welfare of greyhounds should be reported promptly to the relevant council to facilitate a thorough investigation and enforcement action as necessary.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) whether racing greyhounds are more likely to sustain injuries than companion greyhounds; and (ii) whether on average racing greyhounds have poorer welfare than companion greyhounds.
answer_text: The welfare of any animal is determined by a wide range of factors and my Departmental officials are not aware of any independent research comparing the welfare standards of racing greyhounds and those which are kept solely as companion animals.Nevertheless, I am aware that animal welfare organisations have been collecting information regarding injuries to greyhounds which they attribute to racing. I will consider any evidence presented, and if required, engage with local councils who retain statutory responsibility for welfare concerns pertaining to greyhounds.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of drug overdoses recorded in the prison system for each of the last five years; and (ii) how many of these have resulted in a fatality.
answer_text: The number of drug overdoses recorded in the prison system for each of the last five years is as follows:202020212022202320242025 (Jan - May)Total344424242812166The number of these which resulted in a fatality is as follows:2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025 Total 1 0  0 0 1 0 2

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will consider introducing legislation to permit voluntary chemical castration for convicted child sex offenders.
answer_text: While this issue has received attention recently following the publication of the Independent Sentencing Review in England and Wales, it should be noted that that review did not recommend the widespread use of chemical suppression for sex offenders. Rather, it concluded that a comprehensive evidence base is needed and noted the importance of understanding how other international jurisdictions manage the ethical and practical implications of using chemical suppression in offender treatment. I am not, therefore, currently actively considering the introduction of such legislation in Northern Ireland but my officials are keeping developments in neighbouring jurisdictions under review to help inform future considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of occasions drugs have been found within each prison in Northern Ireland, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: Regrettably, many people entering our prisons have long-standing issues regarding addictions, with associated engrained drug seeking behaviours.  Prison officers work to disrupt the flow of, and reduce the harm caused by, illicit substances in prisons.  An Independent Review of Progress at Maghaberry and Magilligan Prisons undertaken by the Criminal Justice (NI) Inspectorate and His Majesty’s Chief Inspectorate of Prisons in late 2023 evidenced.“Medications were the most commonly found substances, indicating a level of success in preventing the ingress of illicit substances from the community.”The following table details the total number of drug or suspected drug finds within each Establishment in the calendar years 2020-2024.YearMaghaberryMagilliganHydebank Wood2020341967220214848833202267178482023557884920246767048

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of (i) attempted suicides; and (ii) suicides within the prison system in each of the last five years.
answer_text: NIPS does not hold information on “attempted suicides”. Data is held on individuals who have self-harmed in prison, the instances of self-harm and the nature of self-harm. Not all self-harm incidents are due to an individual attempting suicide. All deaths in prison custody are subject to a Coroner’s Inquest. A Coroner is responsible for determining the cause of death when there has been a death in prison custody. For the period 2020 to 2024 (inclusive) only 1 inquest has been completed. One death has been confirmed at inquest as by natural causes, with 14 potentially natural causes (58%); 9 potentially self-inflicted (35%); and 2 deaths potentially substance related (7%).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether CCTV is operational in (i) Bann House; and (ii) Foyle House committal units in Maghaberry Prison, including within corridors and staircases.
answer_text: Multiple layers of physical security measures are applied appropriately across the Northern Ireland Prison Service estate.  I cannot comment on specific physical, procedural or dynamic security measures used by the Prison Service.  To do so would potentially undermine their purpose and impact upon the safety and security of prisons, staff, prisoners and the wider public.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what steps are being taken, in conjunction with the Department of Health, to address the geographical disparity in access to autism assessments; particularly the disparity between the Belfast Trust,  where children must wait up to several years or pay privately, and other Trust areas where assessments and diagnoses are delivered through the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service, despite the Autism NI Act (Amendment) 2022 aiming to end such regional disparities.
answer_text: Waiting times for autism assessment is a matter for the Minister of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what steps his Department has taken to recruit Educational Psychologists, Speech and Language Therapists and Special Educational Needs professionals with the requisite language skills to support children and teachers in the Irish Medium sector.
answer_text: My department is committed to upholding its statutory duty to encourage and facilitate the development of Irish Medium Education (IME). As such, I have recently approved the development of an Irish-Medium Strategy. Officials are in the early stages of developing a Strategy and Action Plan, which will aim to address many of the long-standing challenges that face the Irish Medium sector, including special educational needs and disability.Speech and language therapists in schools are employed by Health and Social Care Trusts which is a matter for the Minister of Health.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister by what date they expect the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression to be in full operation.
answer_text: We are committed to making the relevant appointments and establishing the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression, along with the Irish Language Commissioner, and the Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition, as soon as possible.All three recruitment competitions are now at an advanced stage and work is ongoing to enable appointments to be made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant of AQW 27252/22-27, whether his Department can provide clear evidence that action short of strike in the industrial relations arena as carried out by unions in the last five years has compromised child safety.
answer_text: Action Short of Strike (ASoS) directives to union members in every instance have included refusing to engage with the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) on all inspection activity.  This has continued to be the case despite support for the new inspection framework which has been welcomed by all unions. ETI is the only organisation in Northern Ireland that inspects the arrangements for child protection and wider safeguarding in schools and educational establishments, including adult safeguarding.  When inspection is impeded by ASoS, there is no other organisation fulfilling this crucial role.Through inspection, ETI identifies organisations where child protection requires improvement. If ETI is prevented from inspecting schools, there is increased risk that organisations in need of support to strengthen their arrangements for child protection/safeguarding are not identified. ETI also disseminates good practice in the area of child protection and wider safeguarding, however, inspectors can only do so when they can access schools to see it.Whilst inspectors have continued to visit schools which, under legislation must be open to inspection at all times, inspectors undertaking their statutory duty have not been afforded access to firsthand observation of classroom ethos, to meet with the Designated Teacher or Safeguarding team, to have discussions with all staff to ascertain awareness and understanding of school policies and procedures.  Schools have also refused to distribute ETI surveys to staff, pupils and parents to learn of their views, experiences and concerns.  This has effectively obstructed ETI from identifying safeguarding issues that may have gone unnoticed or to highlight and address poor adherence with effective practice.  Over the last eight academic years, three quarters of inspections in statutory, publicly funded schools have been obstructed to the extent that the ETI were unable to evaluate fully the outworkings of the arrangements for safeguarding.  This is clearly stated in published reports from inspections impacted by ASoS.The Department can request an unannounced inspection when a safeguarding concern is raised and teacher unions advised they would make exceptions in the event of legitimate matters of safeguarding however this exemption did not extend to allowing inspectors to observe in the classroom and therefore even in these instances, ETI could not always provide meaningful assurances.In the last five full academic years, ETI has identified eight organisations where child protection has been unsatisfactory.  By way of comparison, in the previous five year period, 52 organisations were identified.Organisations across public life are inspected – care homes, restaurants, hospitals, prisons and the police service. To continue to leave our children, including our most vulnerable children, in schools as one of the only public services without external inspection for long periods of time, would represent a significant failu

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail: (i) his Department's assessment of the impact that reduced timetables for children has on their learning; and (ii) any measures his Department is taking to ensure that all learners are returned to full time timetables as soon as possible.
answer_text: Neither the Department of Education nor the Education Authority holds records on the number of children and young people on reduced days/timetables.  Reduced timetabling is not recorded on the Schools Information Management System (SIMS) and therefore there is no evidence to reflect in this response.  All children of compulsory school age are entitled to a full-time education in line with legislation. This decision to place a child on a reduced or part time timetable can occur at a school level however there are times that a multi-disciplinary team may agree that this action is in the best interests of the child at the time. If a decision is made to place a child on a reduced or part time timetable, the EA advises schools to ensure: A clear plan is put in place for a set length of time with clear measures/strategies to support the child needs in order to return to school/ full timetable. The plan must increase the hours and have a phased return to a full day.The plan must be kept under review.Include the voice of the child or young person and ask them what support they feel would help them. Parent/Carers to be supportive the time bound plans and actively involved in review meetings

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail the steps his Department is taking to ensure compliance with EU obligations under the Waste Framework Directive in relation to the registration, traceability, and reporting of hazardous waste; and (ii) how these are being enforced across all sectors.
answer_text: (i) Within my Department the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) is responsible for controlling the movement of hazardous waste both within Northern Ireland and between Northern Ireland & Great Britain under the Hazardous Waste Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005.(ii) NIEA controls the movement of hazardous waste via the consignment note tracking system, providing advice and guidance on the classification of waste, promoting awareness of the regulations and ensuring compliance through a monitoring and inspection programme. Where breaches are detected, NIEA implements timely and proportionate enforcement action in line with the DAERA Enforcement Policy. NIEA also works closely with other enforcement agencies throughout the UK.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant of AQW 26846/22-27, to detail what immediate actions his Department will take to accelerate progress, improve transparency, and ensure credible plans are in place to avoid breaching statutory recycling obligations., given that current municipal recycling rates do not meet the 2025 interim target of 55 per cent, and in light of the acknowledged data gaps in non-household municipal waste.
answer_text: As outlined in my response to AQW 26846/22-27, recycling data published by my department relates only to waste collected by local councils in Northern Ireland and does not include most of the recycling from the non-household municipal (NHM) sector. Therefore, at present it is difficult to determine if municipal recycling rates are meeting the interim target of 55% by 2025. While the lack of robust data available to determine recycling rates for NHM waste presents a challenge, my department is progressing work to address this data gap and obtain more current and reliable figures. Once complete, this work will provide valuable information to inform the level of improvement required. Proposals to improve the quality and quantity of our recycling and to help meet our legislative targets were included in a consultation document Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI, published in 2024. Analysis of responses to this consultation has helped inform a draft Departmental response. It is my intention to bring this to my Executive colleagues to seek their support in moving forward together to not only meet our legal targets for recycling, but to also help drive green growth and develop our circular economy in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant of AQW 26777/22-27,in light of the Department not holding the information to determine the percentage of textiles currently being recycled, (i) how he intends to establish a baseline for measuring progress; and (ii) whether he will commit to setting specific textile waste reduction and recycling targets, backed by data collection and reporting mechanisms, to ensure accountability and drive meaningful change.
answer_text: Officials in the Northern Ireland Environment Agency do not gather or hold the relevant data that would enable the establishment of a baseline for measuring progress on the percentage of textiles recycled. However,  NIEA can provide data on the tonnage of textiles recycled as reported to the Agency.I have no current plans to set specific textile waste reduction and recycling targets and instead my officials are concentrating on other interventions as I outlined in my previous answer to AQW 26777/22-27.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) what percentage of electrical goods are currently being recycled; and (ii) what steps his Department is taking to increase recycling for electrical waste.
answer_text: The UK WEEE recycling rate is currently 57%, with around 450,000t of WEEE still being disposed of through residual streams. Last year in NI, almost 10,000 tonnes of WEEE was treated by our licenced treatment facilities to the required standard, and in a way that does not cause harm to our environment.My department, working in conjunction with UK and the other devolved governments, will introduce the first stage of the WEEE reforms in June 2025 through amending legislation.It is imperative that NI householders have access to an effective waste collection infrastructure, one that prioritises electrical items for re-use rather than recycling, in line with Waste Hierarchy principles.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is exploring the introduction of dedicated mental health crisis response services, such as psychiatric ambulances or mobile crisis teams, that would enable individuals in mental health crisis to be taken directly to appropriate mental health facilities rather than emergency departments.
answer_text: Implementing the new regional mental health crisis service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, under actions 12 and 27. The Department of Health Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency are working in partnership to deliver on both of these actions and to develop a Regional Mental Health Crisis Service for all citizens of Northern Ireland. The components of the proposed regional mental health crisis service are: accessing mental health crisis services, primary care and interagency partnership, community crisis service, mental health liaison service and crisis resolution home treatment. Future state work has commenced across lifespan services including scoping what a good service would look like. The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service has been testing a ‘hear and treat’ mental health service as part of its 999 response. The service is currently operational during peak times, with mental health professionals available in ambulance control from 6pm to 2am on Friday to Sunday evenings.  This service offers more suitable alternatives than transporting patients with mental health issues to busy Emergency Departments.In order to develop and roll out the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service, additional funding will be required. My officials within the department are working in partnership to prioritise areas for next steps. This will include standardisation of services and levelling up and roll out of service areas across the region, to ensure a more equitable provision to those in need across the lifespan.In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission mental health crisis services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of his recent commitment to look again at funding options for the Scoil Spreagtha scheme, to provide a timeline by which Gael Linn will learn whether further funding will be forthcoming.
answer_text: My officials are currently engaging with Gael Linn on their funding request, submitted at our recent meeting on 14 May 2025 to discuss the Scoil Spreagtha scheme. Additional information has since been sought and received. This proposal will need time for evaluation, particularly when considered alongside the unavoidable cost pressures that education continues to face and the 2025-26 budget does not fully address. Whilst this evaluation is ongoing, it would be prudent for Gael Linn to pursue funding discussions with other organisations, particularly those who initially supported the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant of AQW 24882/22-27, for an update on (i) the operations of the Water Catchment Partnership, outlining the issues being dealt with and specific points regarding problems identified; and (ii) any solutions they are currently implementing, with an expected timeline for resolution.
answer_text: The Water Catchment Partnership (WCP) has helped farmers and land managers understand the core aspects of handling, applying and disposing of grassland sprays through community engagement at events across Northern Ireland. The commonly used herbicide, MCPA, represents a significant challenge to drinking water treatment in Northern Ireland currently. (ii) The WCP has delivered weed wiping projects to over 300 farmers to date, to control the rushes growing in their fields, as an alternative to using MCPA in a boom sprayer. The use of this form of precision application to administer an alternative pesticide has helped drive down MCPA levels within our water catchments.Engagement with pesticide users across the sectors continues on an ongoing basis through attendance at targeted events such as agricultural shows, delivery of knowledge transfer demonstration events and mail drops.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 26925/22-27, to detail an expected time frame for the full replacement of the Schools Information Management System under the Education Information Solutions programme, noting the importance of data on children on reduced timetables.
answer_text: A detailed implementation plan for the Education Authority’s Education Information Solutions programme, (EdIS) is currently being refined with the objective of having schools on the new Bromcom system for the 2026/27 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has plans to set clearly defined targets for (i) increasing the number of Integrated school places; (ii) increasing the number of schools transforming to Integrated status; and (iii) expanding the number of Integrated schools.
answer_text: In preparing the draft Strategy and Action Plan for Integrated Education, the key target proposed within the Integrated Education Strategy is to ensure that by 2030, we will aim to achieve a reduction in the post-primary gap between the percentage of first preferences for Integrated Education being met (currently 87%) and the Northern Ireland post-primary average (currently 90%).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that appropriate acoustic standards are incorporated into the planning and design of all future school buildings and ongoing renovations, particularly in recognition of the sensory and communication needs of pupils with special educational needs.
answer_text: All new school buildings are designed in accordance with relevant Building Control Guidance and Statutory Requirements, this incorporates guidance on buildings and grounds for special educational needs pupils and includes acoustic performance standards. Additionally, Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Method (BREEAM) accreditation is a requirement for any new school building.  Acoustic performance at both design and construction stage is monitored and reviewed to ensure standards are met to achieve strict requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what ringfenced money has been allocated for the construction of outdoor gyms in the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Neither my Department nor its arm’s length body, Sport NI, has allocated funding for outdoor gyms in the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail why the use of Expert Panels has not been considered for a review of Irish Medium Education, given that they have been utilised to carry out the Independent Review of Education, Review of the Curriculum and A Fair Start report.
answer_text: The Department currently has no plans to review Irish Medium Education. Reviews and research have already been carried out to ascertain the issues facing the Irish-Medium Education sector, most recently by Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta with their report “Reviewing the Review: An Audit of Progress on the Review of Irish-medium Education (2008)”.I have now approved the development of an Irish-Medium Education Strategy and Action Plan. Stakeholders with the relevant expertise in the sector sit on both the Steering and Working Groups and will take forward the development of this Strategy and implement the associated action plan.All stakeholders will have an opportunity to provide input into the development of the strategy and action plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 26923/22-27, to detail: (i) what percentage of children leave Key Stage 2 having achieved the required level of swimming proficiency, broken down by (a) gender, (b) age, and (c) any other relevant demographic factors; (ii) how his Department ensures consistent access to quality swimming instruction for all pupils; and (iii) what measures are in place to prevent disparities due to variations in resources or scheduling.
answer_text: (i)Neither my Department nor the Education Authority hold this information.(ii) and (iii)As previously advised, swimming is a statutory area of the curriculum within the Physical Education area of learning at Key Stage 2. It is a matter for schools and the relevant Board of Governors to decide how to implement swimming programmes in accordance with the Minimum Content Order.EA guidance advises schools should consult with the providers of swimming pool lessons on a frequent basis regarding their planning and implementation of the required curricular programme. Discussions should include appropriate teaching, learning and assessment strategies for both class groups and each individual child.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant of AQW 25573/22-27, to detail what specific steps he is taking to ensure that the review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum will lead to a consistent and comprehensive approach to teaching the historical causes of conflict, particularly the deep-rooted issues highlighted in the 2022 Ulster University report.
answer_text: The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum was published on 3 June.  The report makes highlights a range of subject specific issues including in regard to the history curriculum.  I have signalled my intention to establish a Curriculum Taskforce, to be chaired by Christine Counsell, to lead the development of a new Northern Ireland curriculum.  The Taskforce will consider the design of the history curriculum in detail taking account of the Review’s findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will commit to not introducing legislation that would criminalise teachers for taking part in action short of strike by refusing to cooperate with inspections, recognising that to do so would risk undermining industrial relations and punishing lawful union activity.
answer_text: I am fully committed to introducing legislation to ensure inspection is safeguarded during periods of industrial action, as it is in every other UK jurisdiction and the Republic of Ireland. It has not undermined lawful union activity in these jurisdictions.  The wider right to Action Short of Strike remains and this legislation simply removes inspection from the industrial relations arena. The Education and Training Inspectorate is the only organisation in Northern Ireland that inspects the arrangements for child protection and wider safeguarding in schools and educational establishments, including adult safeguarding.  When inspection is impeded by action short of strike, there is no other organisation fulfilling this absolutely critical role.Further, without inspection schools are not identified to receive the support and guidance they need to ensure children receive a high-quality education. A failure to safeguard inspection, is a failure to safeguard children.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 20623/22-27, to detail any plans the Department has to (i) reconsider or (ii) formally review its position on the three-year reapplication rule, given the over-subscribed nature of the funding schemes and recognising that other schemes may not be suitable for many artists.
answer_text: My Department has no such plans.  The Support for the Individual Artist Programme (SIAP) is delivered by the Arts Council of Northern Ireland which is responsible for all aspects of the programme including setting eligibility rules.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant of AQW 24983/22-27, whether the policy to introduce dual Irish and English signage and ticket machines at Belfast Grand Central Station will be extended to all other Translink train and bus depots.
answer_text: I am committed to the visibility and promotion of Irish Language across public services including the public transport network. The Irish language is a living and thriving language used daily by many people across the north.  It is fitting that the Irish Language will be visible at the station for Irish language commuters but also for the many visitors to the city. The provision of Irish, in additional to English and other languages, on ticketing machines will apply to all Translink ticket vending machines. This is a positive development to promote wider awareness and support for the Irish language. Given the on-going legal proceedings in relation to my decision, it would not be appropriate to comment further.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time from a patient's initial presentation at an Emergency Department to their admission to an acute mental health ward, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: BHSCTFollowing the implementation of Encompass, the BHSCT is unable to obtain this information at present.NHSCTUnfortunately, the Northern Trust is unable to provide a response to this question as the Trust is not able to access the information from the Encompass system. SEHSCTWithin the time available to the Trust to provide the data requested, the Trust has only been able to review data over a 3-month period.  Over that time, the mean average time from arrival at ED to admission to a mental health inpatient ward was 51 hours.  When in ED, every patient waiting for a bed in an inpatient mental health ward will be seen at least daily by mental health specialists to ensure patients are receiving appropriate care and treatment.  Where possible, our mental health teams care for people in the own home environment and provide and excellent Home Treatment team service, which is under significant pressure.  When required, Home Treatment team will seek to arrange admission to a mental health inpatient unit without a person having to attend ED.SHSCTAt present, this information is not routinely gathered in an easily accessible format.  From information available: Where clinically indicated, the mean average waiting time for an acute mental health admission is 6-8 hours from initial presentation*.  Within ED, there may a requirement for clinical assessment to rule out organic causation and/or treat a co-existing physical health issue, and when appropriate, assessment by the Mental Health Integrated Liaison Service will take place (target time of 2 hours from point of referral).  If clinically indicated the Mental Health Integrated Liaison Service will then coordinate admission to an inpatient mental health ward.  There may be times when this is delayed due to inpatient mental health bed availability.The Southern HSC Trust also operate a Mental Health Integrated Liaison direct referral from triage, where there is no indication for a clinical physical assessment/treatment within the ED department.  This can shorten the timescale for an individual within the ED.   *NB: it is noted that during times of extreme pressure in EDs, presentation to triage times can be lengthy. This will impact on the patient journey waiting time.WHSCTThe average waiting time from an initial presentation to a Western Trust Emergency Department to admission to a Trusts acute Mental Health Inpatient Hospital, for the period 1 April 2025 to 30 April 2025 was 12 hours & 35 minutes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will have discussions with the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment in relation to questions that have again appeared in the GCSE Learning for Life and Work exam papers that imply homosexuality is intrinsically negative.
answer_text: The setting of questions for public examinations is a matter for the relevant exam board, in this case CCEA. From the timing of this question it is assumed that it refers to the Summer 2025 Learning for Life and Work GCSE Unit 2 examination, held on 9th May.   CCEA  advised that the question on that paper relates to the general diversity of sexual orientation of individuals and the impact this may have on an individual’s well-being.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his Department's assessment of the impact that the 92 per cent shortfall in annual funding is having on the Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery strategy.
answer_text: Unfortunately, there continues to be severe budgetary pressures across my Department’s areas of responsibility, so not all that we would like, and need, to do can be delivered immediately.  My Department continues to seek opportunities to enhance funding in order to support work in this area.Full delivery of the Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery Strategy remains dependent upon additional resource being available. However, the related Substance Use Commissioning and Implementation Plan identifies a number of cost neutral actions enabling the progression of many pieces of work with a view to improving services and delivering the best outcomes for communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will consider introducing mandatory regulations for licensed venues to clearly display the alcohol content of drinks served.
answer_text: I have no plans to make any changes to liquor licensing law while my Department considers the outcome of an independent review of the licensing system and a review of provisions within the Licensing and Registration of Clubs (Amendment) Act (NI) 2021.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will (i) consider requiring the Arts Council to ensure that recipients of public funding through the Musical Instruments Scheme, commit to standards of conduct that explicitly prohibit sectarian behaviour, including the playing of sectarian tunes; and (ii) introduce mechanisms to withhold or withdraw funding where such standards are breached.
answer_text: The Arts Council is bound by its statutory duties under the Northern Ireland Act 1998 to have due regard to the need to promote equality of opportunity between the following: People of different religious belief, political opinion, racial group, age, marital status or sexual orientation.Men and women generally.Persons with a disability and persons without.Persons with dependants and persons without.It is a requirement of the funding conditions that the applicant organisation commits to an equality of opportunity and good relations duty.  Mechanisms to withhold or withdraw funding already exist.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what analysis his Department is undertaking to assess the environmental impact that potential mining in Fermanagh, Tyrone and Armagh may have, especially on water.
answer_text: I am aware of the granting of seven mineral prospecting licences, by the Department for the Economy (DfE), to four companies in Northern Ireland.Any potential future mining proposals will require a planning application to be submitted, by the applicant, and will likely be accompanied by an Environmental Statement, to report the findings of an environmental impact assessment.My Department is a statutory consultee to the NI Planning process and upon request by a planning authority, will review Environmental Statements and provide advice on all aspects of the environment within our remit, including water.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what action her Department is taking to address the 79 per cent of the current prison population who are classified as repeat offenders.
answer_text: Despite the Northern Ireland Prison Service facing acute challenges with a rising prison population with increasingly complex needs, it continues to prioritise rehabilitation to support and challenge prisoners to change and address their offending behaviour to reduce their risk of reoffending on release.  Working with partners from the statutory, voluntary/community, pastoral and education sectors they provide a range of programmes and interventions which are kept under review to ensure that they align with the needs and priorities of the prison population. It is also worth noting that there are significant challenges in supporting the rehabilitation of individuals held on remand and/or serving short sentences, particularly given the short time they may be in custody.  Reducing reoffending and supporting rehabilitation cannot be the sole responsibility of the Northern Ireland Prison Service, or indeed the Department of Justice, and relies upon cross-executive and multi-agency collaboration.  Evidence shows that a range of socio-economic factors impact on offending, including poverty and social deprivation, mental health issues, substance misuse, homelessness, and a lack of educational attainment and employment opportunities.  Delivering meaningful change, and improving outcomes for individuals, will involve the development and implementation of approaches to tackle those wider societal issues which sit outside my Department’s responsibility.  I welcome the commitment within the Programme for Government to ‘work collectively to develop a cross-governmental strategy to reduce offending and reoffending.’  My officials are engaging across government to progress this important cross-cutting work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how her Department is assessing the effectiveness of rehabilitation programmes, given that that 79% of the current prison population are repeat offenders.
answer_text: The Prison Service works with statutory and voluntary and community sector (VCS) partners to deliver a range of programmes and interventions to encourage desistance from reoffending on release.Programmes delivered by external partners are regularly monitored and evaluated by the Prison Service using outcomes based accountability performance management tools to ensure delivery is in line with Service Level Agreements.  This approach ensures that the Prison Service can identify and address potential issues, optimise resource allocation and improve the overall quality of the service to suit the needs of a changing prison population.However, the need for better management information and the ability to access data relating to individuals when they leave prison in order to assess the impact of specific interventions and subsequent outcomes in terms of desistance from reoffending is acknowledged.  Prison Service officials are currently working with NISRA colleagues on the development and implementation of a management information project to consider how the impact of interventions can be measured in the longer term and post release from prison.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any gender-specific interventions that are being applied, considering that 95 per cent of repeat offenders are male and only 5 per cent are female.
