answer_text: Working with partners from the statutory, voluntary/community, pastoral and education sectors the Prison Service provides a range of programmes and interventions which are kept under review to ensure that they align with the specific needs and priorities of the prison population.This includes programmes which meet the needs of both males and females.  Whilst programmes may be delivered to both genders, some service providers may tailor programme delivery to best suit the cohort which it is engaging.  Programmes which address gender specific issues, include:A Future Fatherhood programme for young men in Hydebank Wood College.  This programme seeks to help young men in custody develop positive, healthy relationships and prepare for future fatherhood; The Being a Dad/Mum Programme provides information and support to better understand the important role of a parent, focusing on helping to recognise that parental imprisonment results in negative effects for the family and emotional effects on children;The Safety for Relationships Programme is delivered specially to male perpetrators of domestic violence in Maghaberry Prison. The programme seeks to harness accountability and promote safety, focusing on healthy relationships and the impact domestic violence has on partners and families; andA Women’s Group in Hydebank Wood College provides support to females through group information sessions and programmes to develop parenting skills and raise awareness of gender specific health issues.Spanner in the Works facilitate sessions to raise awareness of the impact of domestic violence and violence against women and girls by accommodating prisoners in viewing the production “Runny Honey” live specifically in Maghaberry and Magilligan prisons which house males.Whilst some programmes don’t target gender specific issues, they are currently only delivered to male prisoners to meet the demand identified.  These include:Ulster GAA delivers the GAA Taster and Coaching programme for male prisoners in Maghaberry and Magilligan.Community Support Network (CSN) deliver sports related activities to enhance the confidence and self-esteem of participants by engaging them in activities that focus on health and well-being.  At present this is available to male prisoners within Maghaberry.Extern is running a Short Sentence programme which is tailored for those with short custodial sentences.  At present it is being piloted n Maghaberry therefore only available to male prisoners with a sentence of 12 months or less.  Programme includes the ‘Let’s Talk’ programme whilst in custody with individuals, where appropriate, being referred to the Community Support Team on release.  Specific examples of community support include accessing GP health care, addiction services and mental health counselling, and help with engaging with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to outline why repeat offender data is not aligned with standard reoffending statistics; (ii) whether her Department plans to reconcile the two in future reporting; and (ii) whether the Minister will consider publishing comparable one-year post-release reoffending rates, to allow for greater public scrutiny.
answer_text: Official Statistics for reoffending are calculated using one-year proven reoffending methodology. This looks at all individuals in receipt of a non-custodial disposal or released from custody within a given financial year, to determine the proportion of these individuals who go on to commit a proven reoffence within one year, with an additional six month period given to allow offences to be proven in court. Given the nature of reoffending analysis, there is a natural time lag in the publication of official reoffending rates to allow time for an individual to reoffend and for offences to be proven in court.In contrast, repeat offender data, such as that provided in response to AQW 25575, is based on a snapshot of individuals in custody at any given point in time. This uses real-time data from the PRISM Management Information system to provide more timely information on the number of individuals, within custody at that specific point in time, who have committed previous offences. Given that these are two different measures, that are not directly comparable, the Department has no plans to reconcile these into future reporting. However, published Official Statistics for Adult and Youth Reoffending in Northern Ireland also contain information on reoffending rates by number of previous offences and show that, for the most recent 2021/22 cohort, 65.3% (12,536) had committed previous offences; of these individuals, 22.6% (2,827) went on to reoffend. These published figures also include one-year proven reoffending rates for disposal type, broken down by custody releases, community supervision, community other and diversionary disposals. This information is published annually and available from 2010/11 to 2021/22.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether there is a strategy in place to reduce the rate of repeat offending among unsentenced prisoners, who currently account for 34 per cent of repeat offenders; and (ii) what measures are being taken to divert first-time unsentenced offenders away from becoming repeat offenders.
answer_text: Whilst a prisoner is on remand, Northern Ireland Prison Service will offer a range of programmes to support rehabilitation; however, remand prisoners cannot be compelled to engage with such activities. For sentenced prisoners, NI Prison Service delivers a range of programmes to support the rehabilitation of people in prison and to seek to ensure they desist from reoffending on their release from prison.  These were set out in my answer to your recent Assembly Question -AQW 27102/22-27.   In addition, a range of diversionary measures and community disposals are available to the courts and judiciary, as an alternative to custody.  These include cautions, informed warnings, probation and youth case conferencing.  When considering the appropriateness of diversion, each case is assessed on its own merits, taking into account the facts and circumstances of the individual case.   There are also a number of other options available that can support rehabilitation and desistance, for example, the substance misuse court, electronic monitoring and enhanced combination orders.   Reducing offending and reoffending is not something my Department, or the criminal justice system, can do alone.  In recognition of this, there is a commitment in Programme for Government to work collectively to develop a cross-governmental strategy to reduce offending and reoffending and implement initiatives to tackle some of the wider societal issues that contribute to offending behaviour.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy why seven prospecting licences have been granted to mining companies, despite these proposals being overwhelmingly rejected by public consultation.
answer_text: In November 2024, my Department received seven applications for Mineral Prospecting Licences for the exploration and development of minerals vested in the Department under the Mineral Development Act (Northern Ireland) 1969.  The provisions of a Mineral Prospecting Licence relate to prospecting for minerals only.  Those provisions are separate and distinct from those relating to mining of minerals. The legislation emphasises the fact that there is no automatic continuity between exploration and extraction.All seven applications met all of the necessary legislative requirements and consequently were issued for public consultation. My Department considered all the issues raised by the respondents to the consultation and concluded that there were no lawful barriers to prevent the granting of the seven Mineral Prospecting Licences.  Before any prospecting activities can be undertaken, a licensee is required to obtain other appropriate permissions including, but not limited to, from the Department and other regulatory authorities such as Councils, Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA), and Health & Safety Executive Northern Ireland (HSENI). This legislative framework aims to protect the natural heritage and minimise environmental impact of exploration activities.The Mineral Development Act (Northern Ireland) 1969 specifically excludes the exploration and development of gold and silver. The exploration and development of gold and silver remain under the remit of the Crown Estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his Department's assessment of why the amount of waste exported for incineration increased from 122,799 tonnes in 2019 to 281,660 tonnes in 2023.
answer_text: We are continuing to move our waste up the waste hierarchy towards more recovery and recycling and away from landfilling waste. The specific figures quoted are for refuse derived fuel (RDF). This is produced from residual waste after many recyclable materials have been removed, but unfortunately not everything can be recycled. It contains calorific value and can be used as fuel for incineration to produce energy from waste.Northern Ireland does not currently have sufficient infrastructure to use the volumes of RDF generated. It is therefore exported under EU regulations on shipments of waste to countries which have the facilities and capacity to use it to produce energy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what targeted support services are available or being developed to address the needs of women living with ADHD post-diagnosis; and (ii) what plans his Department has to improve training for healthcare providers to better recognise and diagnose ADHD in women.
answer_text: (i) what targeted support services are available or being developed to address the needs of women living with ADHD post-diagnosis;There is currently no commissioned service for Adult ADHD.All Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of adults with ADHD within Trust areas. The Department is commissioning a research exercise into the current and projected demand with a view to bringing forward recommendations to inform a commissioned ADHD service, subject to future budget availability. The exercise will (amongst other things) seek to establish the prevalence of ADHD (for both children and adults) in Northern Ireland, examine best practice from other jurisdictions and identify challenges and barriers in relation to ADHD diagnosis and treatment. Importantly, the research exercise will be expected to make recommendations on what any future commissioned service might look like in terms of, for example, costs, staffing profiles and recommended care pathways.A research lead has now been identified and work on the research has commenced, with an anticipated completion date of end June 2025.Any future commissioned service will be subject to both budget availability and evidenced demand, but the research, once completed, will help provide a firm evidence base to inform a future model.(ii) what plans his Department has to improve training for healthcare providers to better recognise and diagnose ADHD in women.As indicated above, any future commissioned service will be subject to both budget availability and evidenced demand, but the research, once completed, will help provide a firm evidence base to inform a future model which will include training provision required for healthcare provider to better recognise and diagnose ADHD in women.I hope this information is helpful and provides some clarity to you on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what data his Department holds on the prevalence of ADHD in women; and (ii) how this compares to men.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what specific measures are being taken to reduce diagnosis delays for women with ADHD; and (ii) how is his Department working to raise public and professional awareness about the unique presentation of ADHD in women and girls.
answer_text: (i) what specific measures are being taken to reduce diagnosis delays for women with ADHDADHD is a complex neurodevelopmental disorder requiring careful and comprehensive biopsychosocial assessment, care and treatment. NICE guidance (NG 87) advises that Trusts should develop multidisciplinary teams with specialist experience in diagnosis and treatment of ADHD in adults.Provision has grown across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within HSC teams. For all patients awaiting a diagnostic assessment, including women, the demand for ADHD assessment far outweighs capacity, which is resulting in long waiting times of several years.Whilst a level of provision is available, the Department is aware that this has also led to inconsistencies between Trusts including pathways and gaps between what can be provided by Trusts in terms of demand and the level of prevalence of child and adult ADHD, leading to individuals waiting longer than intended for assessment and support than described in NICE guidelines.There is currently no commissioned service for Adult ADHD.All Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of adults with ADHD within Trust areas. The Department is commissioning a research exercise into the current and projected demand with a view to bringing forward recommendations to inform a commissioned ADHD service, subject to future budget availability. The exercise will (amongst other things) seek to establish the prevalence of ADHD (for both children and adults) in Northern Ireland, examine best practice from other jurisdictions and identify challenges and barriers in relation to ADHD diagnosis and treatment. Importantly, the research exercise will be expected to make recommendations on what any future commissioned service might look like in terms of, for example, costs, staffing profiles and recommended care pathways.A research lead has now been identified and work on the research has commenced, with an anticipated completion date of end June 2025.(ii) how is his Department working to raise public and professional awareness about the unique presentation of ADHD in women and girls.Any future commissioned service will be subject to both budget availability and evidenced demand, but the research, once completed, will help provide a firm evidence base to inform a future model which will include raising public and professional awareness about the unique presentation of ADHD in women and girls.I hope this information is helpful and provides some clarity to you on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 26692/22-27, whether his Department will take measures to ensure that a specific Schools Information Management System code is introduced to allow for the accurate recording of children on reduced timetables, recognising that in the absence of such data children's right to full access to the curriculum may be at risk.
answer_text: Under the EdiS programme, a replacement for SIMS will be rolled out across all settings to improve the management and flow of data between schools, parents and EA support services and to support collaborative and multi-school working.Officials are working closely with EA colleagues to ensure that it will be possible to record absences at a more detailed level than at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to improve (i) cycle routes; (ii) greenways; and (ii) the active travel infrastructure in East Derry.
answer_text: My Department has developed a draft Active Travel Delivery Plan including network maps for our larger towns and cities including Limavady, Coleraine, Portrush and Portstewart in East Derry. Public consultation on the draft plan closed on 28 February 2025 and my officials are currently reviewing the responses received with a view to presenting a final plan to me for consideration in the autumn.My Department is also currently commissioning design consultants to commence work later this year on the development of six signature active travel schemes, including routes between Bellarena and Bishop’s Gate, and Coleraine to Portstewart.My officials regularly engage with all councils on their greenway priorities. Delivery of greenways are generally council led and I am aware that the Portrush to Giant’s Causeway Greenway is currently being progressed by Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council. My officials will continue to engage with the Council as they develop their Outline Business Case for the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will implement measures to ensure all children leaving primary school are able to swim.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland swimming is a statutory area of the curriculum within the Physical Education (P.E.) area of learning. The Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 states that Key Stage 2 pupils should be enabled to develop basic swimming and personal survival skills, understand the importance of personal hygiene in relation to pool use and progress from using a swimming aid to developing their confidence and competence in being able to swim without the use of any aids using recognised swimming strokes.However, it is a matter for schools and the relevant Board of Governors to determine the operational aspects of how they actually deliver swimming, including the frequency and timing of the lessons provided to children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has made any assessment of the emotional impact that failing the transfer test may have upon children in Primary 7.
answer_text: My Department has not recently commissioned any research on the transfer tests.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment on the roll-out of the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service, including (i) specific areas in which progression has been made; and (ii) specific areas in need of further development.
answer_text: (i) Implementing the new regional crisis service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, under actions 12 and 27. The Department of Health Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency are working in partnership to deliver on both of these actions and to develop a Regional Mental Health Crisis Service for all citizens of Northern Ireland. The components of the proposed regional mental health crisis service are: accessing mental health crisis services, primary care and interagency partnership, community crisis service, mental health liaison service and crisis resolution home treatment. (i) Progression has been made in the following areas in line with the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service Implementation Plan. A high-level working Crisis Definition has been agreed. A mapping report of current crisis services including community and voluntary sector is currently been finalised, this report informs the development of the future state model. A rapid review of national and international crisis models has been completed to ensure an evidence base of best practice moving into a future state model. Future state work has commenced across lifespan services including scoping what a good service would look like. A Regional Mental Health Crisis Service workshop took place on the 11th October 2024 with relevant stakeholders across lifespan services. A draft Regional Mental Health Crisis Service Lifespan Pathway was presented. Stakeholders provided feedback on the draft pathway, discussed gaps within services and how best to take forward the next phase of work of the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service model. My officials have reviewed the feedback in order to progress the future state work. Progression has also been made in the expansion of the Primary Care Multi-Disciplinary Team (MDT) Programme. The MDT Programme is a partnership between GP Federations and HSC Trusts. It introduces new physiotherapy, social work, pharmacy and mental health roles into general practice to work alongside the existing practice team. It aims to help shift the health service from treating illness to supporting good physical and mental health and social wellbeing. A Mental Health Liaison and Crisis Response Home Treatment Care Network has recently been formed and includes all key stakeholders. This Care Network’s primary aim is to ensure consistent evidence-based practice, improving the quality, safety and experience of care received by those in mental health crisis or experiencing thoughts of self-harm or suicide and to ensure equitable access to care across the region. (ii) In order to develop and roll out the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service, additional funding will be required. My officials within SPPG and PHA are working in partnership to prioritise areas for next steps. This will include standardisation of services and levelling up and roll out of service areas across the region, to ensure a more equitable provision to those i

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) recycling targets; and (ii) whether Northern Ireland is on track to meet the 65 per cent municipal waste prepared for re-use and recycled target by 2035.
answer_text: We currently have several ambitious recycling targets to meet, set in legislation.  The Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 contains a requirement for the department to ensure that at least 70% of waste is recycled by 2030. Meanwhile, the Waste & Contaminated Land (NI) Order 1997 (as amended) includes a 65% municipal recycling target by 2035, with interim targets of 55% municipal recycling by 2025 and 60% by 2030.The most recent annual figures for recycling come from the Local Authority Collected Municipal Waste Statistics Report 2023/24, published by my department on 28 November 2024. This shows an annual household recycling rate of 51.1% and annual municipal recycling rate of 50.5%, short of our current targets. However, this data only relates to waste collected by local councils here and therefore does not include recycling from the non-household municipal (NHM) sector.There is currently a lack of robust data available to determine recycling rates for NHM (business) waste, however, my department is progressing work to address this data gap and obtain more current and reliable figures. Proposals to improve the quality and quantity of our recycling and to help meet our legislative targets were included in a consultation document Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI, published in 2024. Analysis of responses to this consultation has helped inform a draft Departmental response, which my officials are currently finalising. It is my intention to bring this to my Executive colleagues to seek their support in moving forward together to not only meet our legal targets for recycling, but to also help drive green growth and develop our circular economy in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of The Provision of Access for Outdoor Recreation in Northern Ireland consultation; and (ii) what measures his Department will be bringing forward to advance this within the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: An initial consultation concluded in 2021 which focussed on attaining views of key stakeholders such as Council Access Officers, outdoor recreation interests, UFU, and eNGOs with large landholdings, such as the National Trust. Responses to the proposals in this limited consultation were generally supportive. The exercise recognised the need for existing access legislation to be reviewed and updated, providing better clarity for all stakeholders.  Policy development on this important issue will be progressed during this mandate, subject to other departmental priorities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the impact a rise in university tuition fees will have on the welfare of students in Northern Ireland, recognising that many students are already living in relative poverty.
answer_text: I recognise the financial pressures faced by university students given the challenges posed by the cost-of-living crisis, and it is for this reason that my Department recently uplifted maintenance loans by 20%. In addition, most students access tuition fee loans to pay their fees.  These are not repaid until after graduation, when a graduate starts earning above the repayment threshold, currently £26,065 a year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any measures she is taking to facilitate the roll-out full fibre broadband to all rural areas in Northern Ireland; and (ii) a timeline of when all areas of Northern Ireland can expect to avail of these services, recognising that current low speed broadband continues to adversely impact the quality of life and business opportunities for people unable to access high speed fibre broadband without paying exorbitant costs.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of high speed broadband for quality of life and business opportunities. My Department is working with the British Government to improve access to gigabit capable broadband services through Project Gigabit to ensure our rural areas have access to these benefits. The procurement for Project Gigabit commenced on 11 November 2024, with an invitation to interested suppliers to complete a selection questionnaire to demonstrate their capacity and capability to deliver such a contract. Those suppliers who met the published criteria of the selection questionnaire were invited to submit a response to the requirements of the Invitation to Tender.  Bids are expected to be received in late May. Evaluation of the bids will take place in June. Subject to any necessary approvals and value for money assessments being given, my Department expects to announce a decision in the summer. The procurement is seeking a supplier to address an initial scope of approximately 11,000 premises where it has been assessed that there are no viable commericial plans to provide gigabit services. Until bids are received and assessed it is not possible to give a precise timeline as to when premises can expect to avail of these services. Should a contract be awarded, I expect details of premises to benefit and timelines to be published shortly after contract award.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) what specific measures his Department is taking to measure outcomes for children with autism in the education system; and (ii) how the educational outcomes for children on the autism spectrum currently compare to those considered neurotypical.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) Autism Advisory and Intervention Service (AAIS) provides tailored support to autistic children and young people throughout their educational journey.  Outcomes are determined through multiple measures, including pupil voice, feedback from educational settings and parents (qualitative and quantitative), target completion rates, attendance levels and overall school engagement.AAIS also offers a broad range of support that can be accessed without a formal request for direct pupil involvement. While individual pupil outcomes are not collected following these forms of indirect support, the high levels of engagement with training modules and the significant volume of consultations via telephone advice lines indicate a positive impact for autistic children and young people.The Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and associated Delivery Plan seeks to transform educational experiences and outcomes for all learners with SEN which includes children and young people with autism and which will be reflected in an Outcomes Framework.  The Outcomes Framework is in the final stages of development and is expected to be completed and published in the coming weeks.(ii)  This level of data is not available for all pupils with ASD.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) what percentage of textiles are currently being recycled; and (ii) what measures his Department is taking to increase the recycling of textiles.
answer_text: The Department does not hold the information to determine the percentage of textiles currently being recycled. I am aware of the environmental and social impact of textile waste, and my Department is currently working on various initiatives to mitigate these impacts. In accordance with the waste hierarchy, DAERA aim to prevent waste generation and promote reuse and recycling to keep products in circulation for as long as possible. To support this goal, my Department has funded multiple projects focused on enhancing resource efficiency and minimising waste, including textile waste. When reduction, reuse or repair is no longer feasible, my Department encourage and support the recycling of unwanted clothing and textiles, through behaviour change campaigns, support to councils and by working with the third sector.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his Department's assessment of the environmental impact of nitrous oxide canisters; (ii) what percentage of nitrous oxide canisters are currently being safely disposed of; and (iii) what measures his Department is taking to encourage the safe disposal of nitrous oxide canisters.
answer_text: The Department has not carried out an assessment of the environmental impact of nitrous oxide canisters. All canisters must be disposed of at a suitably licenced facility which are regulated by NIEA. The Department does not hold this information.  The Department has not carried out any measures specific to nitrous oxide canisters however the Department funds wider communication and behaviour change campaigns to improve recycling behaviours and to deter littering.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 26386/22-27, whether she will commit to meeting with the Revenge Porn Hotline and UK Safer Internet Centre to best support victims of intimate image based abuse in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I have no current plans to meet with the Revenge Porn Helpline and UK Safer Internet Centre who provide support, on a UK-wide basis, to victims of intimate based abuse but am open to doing so in the future should an opportunity to do so arise.  I fully recognise the value that services of this kind can provide to help victims subjected to this abhorrent behaviour.  In addition to the services provided by this Helpline and Internet Centre, victims of this abuse in Northern Ireland can also avail of the local 24-hour Domestic and Sexual Abuse (DSA) Helpline, which is funded by my Department, the Department for Communities and the Department of Health. This DSA Helpline provides information, advice and support to those impacted by all forms of domestic and sexual abuse across Northern Ireland. The Helpline is available to people with lived experience; to individuals who concerned about someone else; to individuals who may have concerns as to whether abuse is occurring; and to professionals working with people who may be impacted by abuse. The Helpline will direct callers to the appropriate support in both emergency and non-emergency situations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the support available for women diagnosed with polycystic ovary syndrome.
answer_text: Women’s health, including services for those suffering from conditions such as polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis, is a priority for the Department.In 2023, the Department commissioned the Getting it Right First Time Team (GIRFT) to undertake an independent, external rapid review of gynaecology services in Northern Ireland.  The aim of the GIRFT review was to identify areas where improvements could be made in the extensive waiting lists for gynaecology services, and to ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes.A Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trust and PHA representation, has been established to oversee ongoing service improvement and implementation at a local and regional level.  Although work is at an early stage of development and will require additional funding, this will include mapping clinical pathways from presentation at GP services into secondary and tertiary care which will benefit both patients and clinicians. In addition, the Department recognises that there is a clear need for improved public awareness, information and education on women’s health matters from an early age and across the life course.  A large proportion of women diagnosed with polycystic ovary syndrome will have this diagnosed and treated in primary care. Those women diagnosed with polycystic ovary syndrome will have their diagnosis discussed in great detail with them by their diagnosing Consultant where they will be given advice, including leaflet and direction to useful websites, e.g., Royal College of Obstetrics and Gynaecology, and agree a management plan for their care.  This may be at a general clinic.  A cohort of patients may require fertility treatment and will be referred to Regional Fertility centre for specialist treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) his Department's assessment of why Civil Service Administrative Officer applicants have not yet received notification of the outcome of the recruitment process; and (ii) when applicants can expect to receive confirmation on the outcome of their applications, recognising that applicants were informed that they would hear by the end of April.
answer_text: I refer you to the response that issued for AQW 26577/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many women are currently diagnosed with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS); and (ii) to detail the average waiting time for a diagnosis of PCOS.
answer_text: I have been advised that polycystic ovary syndrome, is a condition that is diagnosed primarily in Primary Care and therefore this information would not be easily accessible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what measures his Department is taking to raise awareness of polycystic ovary syndrome; and (ii) what measures his Department is taking to support raising awareness of this condition within secondary education.
answer_text: (i) Women’s health, including services for those suffering from conditions such as polycystic ovary syndrome, is a priority for me and my Department.In 2023, my Department commissioned the Getting it Right First Time Team (GIRFT) to undertake an independent, external rapid review of gynaecology services in Northern Ireland.  The aim of the GIRFT review was to identify areas where improvements could be made in the extensive waiting lists for gynaecology services, and to ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes.The report, which was published in January 2024, set out recommendations designed to maximise capacity, reduce waiting times, improve patient care and outcomes. A Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trust and PHA representation, has been established to oversee ongoing service improvement and implementation at a local and regional level.  Although work is at an early stage of development and will require additional funding, this will include mapping clinical pathways from presentation at GP services into secondary and tertiary care which will benefit both patients and clinicians. (ii) In addition, my Department recognises the need for improved education and information on a wide range of women’s health issues and risk factors, to support women and healthcare professionals in the identification, management and treatment of conditions including polycystic ovary syndrome.While budget pressures continue to limit my Department's ability to improve public education and awareness on a range of important health matters, my officials have been engaging with the Public Health Agency to consider how best to do so within current resources and in a way that meets the specific needs of different groups of women and girls.  This will form part of the Women’s Health Action Plan which my Department is developing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for details of the data his Department currently holds regarding the number of children on reduced school timetables.
answer_text: The Department holds no data on the numbers of children who are on a reduced or flexible timetable. This is due to there currently being no specific code within the Schools Information Management System (SIMS) for children who are on a reduced or flexible timetable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration his Department is giving to lowering the price of vitamins necessary to support women with a polycystic ovary syndrome diagnosis on the NHS.
answer_text: My Department recognises the challenges faced by women living with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). However, the pricing of vitamins and supplements is not set locally.Northern Ireland is part of the wider UK market, and prices are determined by manufacturers and distributors at a national level. Most products used in this context are classified as food supplements rather than licensed medicines, and are therefore not routinely available on HSC prescription.Any changes to the accessibility or affordability of such products would require coordinated action at a UK-wide level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his Department's consideration for access to GLP-1 medication for women suffering from polycystic ovary syndrome.
answer_text: Like all medicines authorised in Northern Ireland, glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs) are authorised by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA). Marketing authorisations are granted by MHRA if they meet the MHRA’s high standards on safety, quality and efficacy.Depending on the preparation of the GLP-1 RA product concerned, they are currently only licensed by the MHRA for the treatment of type 2 diabetes and/ or for weight loss purposes. Therefore, there are no GLP-1 RA medicinal products specifically licensed to treat patients experiencing symptoms associated with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and so use for this purpose is considered to be ‘off-label’.Additionally, my Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals and Clinical Guidelines are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and were found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.There are currently no NICE recommendations for the use of GLP-1 RAs for management of PCOS. In the absence of a marketing authorisation for this indication or any NICE recommendations, GLP-1 RAs are not routinely commissioned for this purpose within the HSC.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rights of tenants in a housing estate when the developer refuses to bring the estate up to adopting standards.
answer_text: The responsibility for bringing a site to an adoptable standard remains with the developer until the adoption process is complete.A developer’s obligation to bring a site to an adoptable standard is set out in The Private Streets (Northern Ireland) Order 1980. The order requires the developer of a site which has a Private Streets Determination under the Planning act (conditioned as part of planning permission), to enter into a financial bond agreement with DfI Roads for completion of the road infrastructure.Similarly, the developer is obliged to enter into an agreement with NI Water for their infrastructure under the Water and Sewerage Services Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.Residents/House owners/tenants are not party to the agreement between the developer and the statutory authorities.My Department may undertake enforcement action against the developer under article 11 of the Private Streets Order, to bring a development up to an adoptable standard, however, there is no legislative requirement to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of housing estates currently awaiting confirmation of adoption.
answer_text: My department current holds details of 3,071 bonded phases within housing developments awaiting adoption.Many housing developments are taken forward in phases, and as such there may be multiple bonded extents within a single development.The 3,071 bonded phases range from those for which a bond agreement was recently sealed and have yet to be constructed, to phases that are largely complete and may have remained unadopted for a number of years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any support available for victims of financial abuse in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Financial abuse can be particularly challenging to address, as it is not always readily identified as a form of abuse; and financial matters are often perceived as private, which can lead to reluctance or embarrassment when it comes to reporting such behaviour.The Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 introduced a new domestic abuse offence that criminalises a range of harmful behaviours, including controlling, coercive, psychological, emotional, technological, and financial abuse. The offence targets patterns of abusive behaviour by a partner, ex-partner, or close family member and includes actions that are physically violent, threatening, or intimidating. We continue to monitor the effectiveness of the Act and engage with stakeholders to ensure it delivers the intended protections.To support the implementation of this legislation, my Department launched a public awareness campaign. A series of online videos—still available via the NI Direct website under the campaign title ‘Still Abuse’—highlight various forms of domestic abuse, including financial abuse, and to direct victims to sources of support and guidance on reporting abuse.Ensuring there are robust protections for victims of domestic abuse—including financial abuse—remains a key priority for me as Justice Minister. In addition, my Department contributes to the wider protection of individuals from financial harm through the ScamwiseNI Partnership, which we are proud to support alongside the PSNI. This collaborative initiative brings together over 45 organisations to raise awareness of scams and financial exploitation. Recent outreach included a six-month campaign featuring radio, newspaper, and public transport advertising, with a particular focus on supporting older people and those with limited access to online information.My Department also funds the delivery of a Northern Ireland-wide advocacy service through ASSIST NI. This service supports high risk victims of domestic and sexual abuse who are involved with the criminal justice system. ASSIST NI offers tailored guidance and advocacy, coordinates with other service providers, and ensures that service users can access the help they need. Advocates are trained to recognise the specific challenges faced by different groups, and work to ensure their support is inclusive and responsive. In addition, I understand that the Department of Health is currently developing a draft Adult Protection Bill, which is expected to include measures that will further help in addressing financial abuse where it relates to vulnerable adults.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any public awareness campaigns that have taken place in Northern Ireland to inform victims of intimate image-based abuse of the support that is available to them.
answer_text: As part of the communication and promotional strategy of the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 implementation programme, the Department ran a public awareness campaign from November 2023 to March 2024 to promote the commencement of three new intimate image related offences of up-skirting, down-blousing and cyberflashing contained in the Act. Promotion was delivered across a range of social media platforms as well as outdoor placement advertising. The campaign material directed viewers to information on a range of support services available, channelled through a dedicated advertising page on the NIDirect website to include the services of the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Helpline.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the process in relation to unadopted housing estates when the developer goes bankrupt.
answer_text: My Department applies the process set out in The Private Streets (Northern Ireland) Order 1980 when a developer responsible for providing roads infrastructure goes bankrupt.Should the bond held by the Department be insufficient to complete the works necessary to bring roads infrastructure to an adoptable standard, the Department has no funding stream or legal obligation to complete the works and the roads will remain unadopted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had during the 2022-2027 mandate with (i) the Revenge Porn Hotline; and (ii) the UK Safer Internet Centre in relation to intimate image-based abuse in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Revenge Porn Helpline is a UK-wide service which supports adult victims of intimate image abuse and is funded by the Home Office. The UK Safer Internet Centre (UKSIC), is a non-governmental partnership which is aimed at helping to make the internet a great and safe place for everyone. As UK led initiatives designed to support those affected by such crime, the Department of Justice has not had any direct engagement with the helpline or the UKSIC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department can better educate young people in relation to their rights under Article 42 of the UN Convention on Rights of a Child.
answer_text: Human Rights and Social Responsibility education is taught as part of the Local and Global Citizenship element of Learning for Life and Work Area of Learning which is compulsory at Key Stage 3.Further information on this Area of Learning is available on the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) website at: Local and Global Citizenship | CCEAUnited Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child Resource Hub | CCEA is also available on the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement she has had in relation to traffic concerns in the Riverside Retail Park, Coleraine.
answer_text: I can advise that the roads within the retail park are all unadopted private roads and as such do not fall within my Department’s area of responsibility. Traffic management within the retail park remains the responsibility of the developer and you should raise your concerns directly with them. There are processes whereby my Department can adopt a private road into the public road network; however, the road should be brought to an adoptable standard prior to the adoption, with costs borne by those wishing to have the road adopted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on tackling speeding in Drumsurn village, Derry.
answer_text: I can advise that 30mph road marking roundels have already been installed on the existing red surfacing adjacent to the speed limit signage. Unfortunately, supply issues have resulted in a delay to the installation of upgraded signage for Drumsurn village.  The upgraded signage will be installed as soon as the supply issue has been resolved and, in addition to this, my Department also intends to install rumble strips in advance of the gateway signs.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any additional measures being considered to support early detection of breast cancer in women with dense breast tissue.
answer_text: Women with dense breast tissue face particular challenges in breast cancer detection, as dense tissue can obscure tumours on standard mammograms. At present, the routine assessment or recording of breast density is not included in any population standard screening mammograms in the UK.Research is being conducted to assess appropriate imaging techniques across the range of risk factors and the NI and NHS National Breast Screening programmes await evidence for benefit and resource implications prior to the systematic collection of breast density on the entire eligible population.The UK National Screening Committee (UKNSC) collect and review evidence on a regular basis and consider any new evidence for changes to screening programmes in accordance with their criteria. They await the outcome of further UK NSC breast screening reviewsThe Very High Risk (VHR) Breast Surveillance Screening Programme in Northern Ireland follows UK national guidance and includes specific protocols to support early detection in women at elevated risk. Updated in April 2025 2023, the VHR guidance clarified the process for reviewing breast density and updated annual surveillance protocols.A key component of this protocol is Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), which provides detailed imaging capable of detecting cancers not visible on mammograms—particularly beneficial for women with dense breast tissue.Under the revised guidance Women aged 50 and older in the Very High Risk (VHR) breast screening program may get a mammogram alone or with an MRI, depending on their breast density.Around age 51, a woman’s breast density is checked: If her breasts are mostly fatty (called BI-RADS A), she will only need mammograms going forward—no MRI. This is recorded, and both she and her doctor are informed. If her breast density is higher (BI-RADS B to D), she will continue to get both MRI and mammograms each year.Breast density is reviewed every year until she switches to mammograms only, usually until age 71 or longer if she chooses to keep attending screening.Women who join the programme after age 50 will get both tests at first, and their breast density will guide future screening.These measures reflect a targeted, evidence-based approach to improving early detection in women with dense breast tissue, particularly those at very high risk. The Breast Screening Service in Northern Ireland will continue to monitor developments and align with NHS Breast Screening Programme England as further evidence becomes available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current pathways for redress for victims of Dr Michael Watt.
answer_text: Neurology compensation arrangements were announced by Ministerial Statement to the Assembly on 9 June 2022, by the former Minister of Health. These arrangements include a streamlined process, supported by a set of guiding principles and the potential to instruct a “joint expert” to provide a joint medical report, at no expense to eligible patients and / or families. The Written Assembly Statement was is available on the Departments website. All claims received in respect of the care provided by former consultant neurologist Michael Watt should be submitted under the standard arrangements for health service litigation claims. To assist patients or families wishing to consider a claim my Department’s Neurology Recall Compensation  webpage provides guidance to support a clear understanding of the claims process in the ‘Neurology Recall: Claims Process Flowchart’. The Department cannot provide legal advice to patients and families and would advise anyone seeking to pursue a compensation claim to seek appropriate independent legal advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of staff allocated to the review of patient cases of Dr Michael Watt.
answer_text: The recall of neurology patients of former consultant Michael Watt by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust commenced in May 2018 and concluded in April 2023. As this work has concluded, no staff are allocated to this work. Under my Department’s direction in August 2021, the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) commenced the Expert Review of Deceased Patients of Dr Watt (the DPR). RQIA commissioned the Royal College of Physicians (RCP) to undertake the expert review of the clinical records of the deceased patients. The RCP Expert Review panel consists of three independent consultant neurologists, none of whom work within the Health and Social Care Northern Ireland (HSCNI).Whilst both my Department and RQIA have staff that continue to support the progress of the DPR, none are directly involved in the review of patient cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of nursery facilities that saw a change in their Published Admission Number for pre-school places in the last five years, broken down by location.
answer_text: Improved accountability is an important aspect of the draft Fuel Poverty Strategy for Northern Ireland.  The public consultation closed on 6th March and in this I proposed introducing a basket of indicators to improve the understanding of fuel poverty and help measure progress towards outcomes, with an annual report outlining progress.  I also proposed establishing a Fuel Poverty Advisory Group with partners from statutory agencies and the Voluntary and Community Sector to guide, advise and challenge actions and progress over the lifetime of the strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of (i) full-time; and (ii) part-time pre-school places available in the last five years.
answer_text: The table below provides the number of children admitted to full-time and part-time funded pre-school education places in each of the last five years.24/2523/2422/2321/2220/21FTPTTotal PupilFTPTTotal PupilFTPTTotal PupilFTPTTotal PupilFTPTTotal Pupil9347126182196592101318622396907613462225389092136982279090561387622932Source ; NI School CensusData is based on pupils enrolled on Census day i.e. The Friday of the first full week in October (In 2024/25 this was the 11 October 2024).Figures for 'preschool settings' include funded children in voluntary and private preschools, nursery schools,  nursery units and pupils enrolled in preschool age SPiM settings (in nursery schools and nursery units).Data relating to 2023/24 was impacted by industrial action, due to Action Short of Strike (ASOS).  Although data was received from all schools, for 25% of schools (primary, post primary and special schools) pupil level information was not received in electronic format. It also meant it was not possible to fully undertake normal validation processes, some validations were impossible due to a lack of pupil information. Validation was undertaken on free school meals and a total was agreed per school, however due to the lack of pupil level information it was it not possible to break this total down by year group. Further information can be found in the notes section of the enrolments bulletin (https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/annual-enrolments-schools-and-funded-pre-school-education-northern-ireland-202324)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will consider ring-fenced funding to support after-school arts programmes, especially in areas of high social or economic deprivation.
answer_text: Performing Arts are already embedded as statutory elements of the Northern Ireland Curriculum across a number of areas of learning and through these, pupils are enabled to develop their creativity and encouraged to express their ideas, feelings and interpretations of the world.  A key design principle of the current curriculum is connected learning, which encourages pupils to make links across areas of learning and apply skills in new contexts.  For example, Drama and Music can be integrated within ‘Language and Literacy’, to support speech and language development, conceptual understanding, and emotional expression.To support schools in the delivery of lessons, the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) has published a progression framework for ‘The Arts’, which reiterates the key minimum requirements for Drama and Music.  This framework also provides examples of progression from pre-school to age 16 and highlights the qualifications that recognise and reward learning in Drama, Music and Performing Arts.Regarding the mental health and educational benefits of Performing Arts for primary school pupils, the curriculum recognises that the greater the encouragement for pupils to express themselves freely through ‘The Arts’ the greater likelihood there is that their individuality, imagination and creativity will blossom.  Furthermore, as part of CCEA’s Wellbeing Hub, Performing Arts are interwoven through many of the resources and guidance, recognising its value in supporting wellbeing and learning.  In terms of after-school programmes, particularly in areas of high social or economic deprivation, my Department continues to providing funding for the Extended Schools Programme.  This programme allows those schools serving the most disadvantaged areas to offer a wide range of services or activities outside of the normal school day to help meet the learning and development needs of pupils, their families and local communities.  Schools will be notified about the allocation for 2025/26 in due course.In the 2023/24 academic year, 69 schools offered arts, crafts and design activities as part of their Extended Schools provisions, with a further 39 offering drama and dance programmes.  Additionally, 12 Extended Schools clusters offered arts, crafts and design activities as part of their programme and a further eight clusters provided drama and dance programmes.  Funding used to support after-school art, dance and drama programmes, cannot be disaggregated from wider funding allocations made under the Extended Schools Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools that offer (i) drama therapy; and (ii) art therapy.
answer_text: My department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans in place to ensure equal access to dance and drama programmes in all primary schools, particularly in communities where parents cannot afford to fund after-school activities.
answer_text: Performing Arts are already embedded as statutory elements of the Northern Ireland Curriculum across a number of areas of learning and through these, pupils are enabled to develop their creativity and encouraged to express their ideas, feelings and interpretations of the world.
