question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will support the funding and adjustments required to allow playgroups to accept children under 2 years 10 months, to ensure the financial viability of this sector and ensure all children receive the benefit of early years education.
answer_text: Under the “Implementation Guidance for Sessional & Full Daycare” October 2017 devised by the then Health and Social Care Board with the endorsement of the Department of Health the minimum age a child can attend a playgroup is 2 years and 10 months and they can remain in this provision until they reach compulsory school age. This position will be considered as part of a wider review of the Minimum Standards and Implementation Guidance later this year”.The maximum number of places for which a playgroup can be registered is 26. If an application is made for more than this maximum number, the request must be made to the HSC Trust which in turn, will seek approval from the SPPG to raise the maximum number. Only in exceptional circumstances will the request be granted for an agreed period of time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the criteria for Pupil Allocation Numbers in pre-schools, specifically recognising that a child with special educational needs is often not diagnosed until they reach a school setting.
answer_text: The allocation of funded pre-school places is a matter for the Education Authority. I have written to the EA to request a review of the PAN allocation process, so that opportunities for improvement can be identified and explored. Circular 2024/17 sets out that within their admissions criteria, pre-schools can give some priority to the admission of children with special educational needs where they so wish. This is a matter for the management of individual pre-school settings.  This guidance does not relate to children with a statement of Special Educational Needs, as these children are not placed though the open enrolment process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the Pupil Allocation Number process for pre-schools in the 2025-26 academic year, in light of administrative errors and concerns from some playgroups and pre-schools in the East Derry constituency that have had to reject double the number of applications they received.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the allocation of funded pre-school places to non-statutory pre-school providers participating in the Pre-School Education Programme.The pre-school admissions process has two stages and the EA has advised that at this point, 97% of target aged children whose parents applied for a pre-school place have received an offer of a funded place in a setting of their preference.The EA has advised that there are currently 2,316 funded places available to accommodate the 620 children who have not yet received the offer of a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference.Within the East Londonderry parliamentary constituency area 1,030 funded pre-school places were available for 2025/26 with a total of 979 first preference applications received during Stage One of the admissions process.  The EA has advised that 935 target age children received an offer of a funded place on 3 April (not all first preference).  A further 40 children have yet to receive an offer of a place in a preferred setting.  However, sufficient pre-school provision remains within the East Londonderry parliamentary constituency area with 95 funded places still available.Parents whose children have not yet received the offer of a funded place in one of their preferred settings have already received advice on the next steps and have been invited to submit further preferences from Monday 7 April.  A list of providers with funded places still available has been posted on the EA website.I would encourage parents to engage with the admissions process to the end and provide further preferences when invited to do so in order to maximise the chances of their child being offered a place in one of their preferred settings.  Should additional funded places be required in local areas, the EA will adjust allocations to settings as necessary.I have written to the EA to request a review of the PAN allocation process, so that opportunities for improvement can be identified and explored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 24881/22-27, for her assessment of the Extern pilot scheme, detailing what benefits and limitations have been identified.
answer_text: Extern’s Prisoner Support Programme was introduced to HMP Maghaberry in January 2024 on a 2 year pilot basis as a direct response to the rise in the short sentence population.It offers pre and post release support (up to 6 months) for the rehabilitation of prisoners with a custodial sentence of under 12 months. Since January 2024, 94 individuals have engaged in programme work whilst in custody and 24 individuals have been directly referred to community support workers.  Specific examples of community support include accessing GP and addiction services, mental health counselling, and support to engage with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.The programme aims to deliver the following benefits for participants:improvement in self-esteem, confidence, self-awareness, physical and mental health; increased levels of engagement in treatment and reduction in harm from substance use;reduction in the risk of home/placement breakdowns by building positive relationships; and	an increase in pro-social behaviour, reducing the risk of re-offending.An external interim evaluation of the programme is due to commence shortly, and once completed will enable the Department to assess its effectiveness, and identify its benefits and limitations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration his Department has given to improving the consistency and depth of the teaching of the Northern Ireland troubles, recognising criticism from a 2022 report by Ulster University School of Education that current teaching often leaves deep-rooted causes of conflict and division untouched.
answer_text: There is significant content at both primary and post-primary level and through some qualifications where reconciliation, conflict, warfare and ethics are explored.  This supports the development of young people’s knowledge, understanding, skills and attitudes to topics such as the implications and consequences of war and conflict, and the meaning of citizenship and equality.  At key stage 3 level (years 8 – 10), it is a statutory requirement for pupils to investigate the long and short-term causes and consequences of the partition of Ireland and how it has influenced Northern Ireland today, including key events and turning points.  It is, however, currently a matter for teachers to decide how the curriculum should be delivered, which resources to use and which specific topics should be covered.  You will be aware that I have commissioned a strategic review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum, currently being carried out by Lucy Crehan, which will focus on the purpose, design, specification and implementation of the curriculum and how we can learn from high performing education systems to bring about necessary reform.  Ms Crehan will provide her report to my Department in May, at which point I will consider its recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on her Department's implementation of recommendations made in the High Street Task Force report; and (ii) to detail any specific measures her Department has considered for high streets in the East Derry constituency.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for recommendation 8 of the High Street Task Force Report: ‘Fair Work on Our High Streets’. Delivering on this recommendation aligns with my economic priority on Good Jobs. Ongoing work within my Department to progress this recommendation includes the development of a new Employment Rights Bill, promotion of greater adoption of the Real Living Wage, and collaboration with the Labour Relations Agency to create a Good Employment Charter. This work will have a positive impact across the north, including the East Derry constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the percentage of prisoners that are classified as repeat offenders.
answer_text: On 1st April 2025, there were 1,964 prisoners in custody and based on data available to the Department, 79% were deemed to be repeat offenders.  Of these prisoners who were repeat offenders, 95% were male and 5% were female.  Sentenced prisoners accounted for 66% of repeat offenders and unsentenced prisoners accounted for 34%.Notes: 1. Figures relate to prisoners in custody at midnight on 1st April 2025 as per PRISM – Management Information.2. Prisoner information from PRISM was matched to historical offending data based on offences dealt with in the judicial system in Northern Ireland.3. Some PRISM data could not be matched with the historical offending data, this could be due offending identifiers that did not match, prisoners who were tried in courts outside Northern Ireland, or those who were on remand without previous offences.4. Repeat offending is based on repeated instances of offending that resulted in a conviction or an out of court diversionary disposal such as a caution or informed warning.5. Of the 92% (1,803) of prisoners in custody who could be matched to the historical offences file, 86% (1,543) were deemed to be repeat offenders and 14% (260) were not.6. These figures are not comparable to published reoffending data which is calculated on the basis of offenders who were released from prison custody and were proven to have reoffended within one year of release.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline how much funding her Department will allocate to the PSNI in 2025 to address digital crime.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 8 - AQO 1922/22-27			ANSWER My Department issued the 2025-26 Final Budget allocation letter to the PSNI on 8 April 2025.The PSNI 2025-26 Final non-ringfenced Resource DEL Budget is £910.6 million. However, there are no specific amounts earmarked by my Department in the PSNI Budget to address digital crimes. Any decisions regarding internal budget allocations will therefore be a matter for the Chief Constable to determine in his role as Accounting Officer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, following the National Union of Students and Union of Students in Ireland report on Cost of Living 2024 report, that found one in three students reported having to skip meals because they could not afford the costs of living, to detail any steps her Department is taking to improve financial support for students.
answer_text: I understand the financial pressures that students face. In January of this year, my predecessor announced a 20% uplift in maintenance loan support for full-time undergraduate students with effect from the 2025/26 academic year.Minister Murphy also confirmed the allocation of an extra £1m of Support Funds in academic year 2023/24 to help students who are experiencing financial hardship. My officials are currently developing plans for a proposed review of higher education funding, including financial support to students.  Officials are due to brief me in coming weeks regarding the review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the routes he is exploring for post-16 learners, as part of his plan to ensure that all children remain in education or training until they are 18 years old.
answer_text: Work is on-going within the Department to bring forward legislation within this Assembly mandate to require all young people to participate in education or training until they are age 18.  The legislation to be developed, is likely to include a duty on the young person which can be fulfilled in a number of different ways including education in school or FE college, employment,  apprenticeship, training or volunteering. Irrespective of the route chosen, I want all our young people to progress, attain and to succeed in their chosen endeavour.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any measures her Department is taking to better understand the scale and extent of any form of spiking within households of intimate partners.
answer_text: It is challenging to provide verified data on spiking as there is currently no specific offence of spiking in law in Northern Ireland. However, according to a recent Home Office report using data from the National Police Chiefs’ Council, most spiking incidents happen in public places with a small number of incidents taking place in people’s homes, mostly during house parties.Included in the UK Government’s Crime and Policing Bill is a provision to update the legislation used to tackle spiking which, amongst other elements, aims to increase public awareness that spiking is illegal and encourage victims to report such incidents to the Police. Subject to Executive and Assembly approval, it is my intention to extend this provision to Northern Ireland. Consideration of how to capture the prevalence of spiking is an issue which can be considered in the lead up to, and following commencement of, the new offence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current learning and development infrastructures in place in Health and Social Care Trusts, detailing Whole Time Equivalent and job roles at AFC (i) Band 7; (ii) Band 8a; (iii) Band 8b; and (iv) Band 8c to support undergraduate and postgraduate learning and development for (a) registered nurses; (b) registered social workers; and (c) registered allied health professional staff.
answer_text: The information requested is set out in the tables below, broken down by HSC Trust and by AfC Band:Learning and development roles to support registered nurses TRUSTBand 7 WTEBand 8a WTEBand 8b WTEBand 8c WTEBelfast 10.5200Northern 5.4101Southern 7.24100South Eastern 4.8100Western5100Learning and development roles to support registered social workersTRUSTBand 7 WTEBand 8a WTEBand 8b WTEBand 8c WTEBelfast 13.62 10 Northern 11.52 11 Southern 12200South Eastern 9.8211Western9311Learning and development roles to support registered AHP professional staffTRUSTBand 7 WTEBand 8a WTEBand 8b WTEBand 8c WTEBelfast 7  00 0 Northern 3.5000Southern 3.5000South Eastern 4000Western3.5000SC Trusts have advised that the learning and development support for undergraduate and postgraduate employees across nursing, social work, and allied health professions is delivered through a range of teams and structures, the details of which differ across each Trust. While all Trusts have dedicated education teams for nursing, social work and allied health professions, learning and development roles are also embedded within wider service delivery and management functions and therefore are not identifiable as separate posts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to protect health workers in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, following reports that over 6000 members of staff were victim to attack in the last five years.
answer_text: On 12 December 2023 the Department launched a new Management of Violence and Aggression in the Workplace HSC Framework. The purpose of this framework is to outline the HSC commitment in partnership with staff representatives, to ensure the prevention, reduction and management of violence and aggression towards staff in the workplace, and to ensure associated structures, policies and support are in place to enable staff to work safely. This regional framework must form the basis of any policies and processes developed locally by the various HSC bodies in relation to dealing with such incidents in a diverse range of working environments. Each HSC employer will have differing arrangements in place, supported by appropriate risk assessments according to the setting. The Northern Trust established a Trust wide Management of Violence and Aggression (MOVA) Group (co-chaired by the HR Director and the Director of Nursing) in early 2024 to oversee the implementation of the Regional MOVA Framework and any actions arising from its implementation. To date the MOVA Group has overseen:support services that are available to Northern Trust staff who have been victims of violence in the workplace, these are set out in the Trust’s Management of Violence and Aggression (MOVA) Toolkit which was developed in 2019 and was recently updated following the issue of the Regional MOVA Framework.  The types of support services described in the Toolkit range from immediate emotional support to signposting staff to other services such as Occupational health or the Trust’s Employee Assistance Programme provider.  The toolkit also provides guidance to staff around the management of violence and aggression in the workplace.a formal launch and awareness campaign of the Framework and a revised Management of Violence and Aggression (MOVA) Toolkit, including distribution of the posters associated with the Framework.  carried out an initial training needs analysis across the entire Trust.  The Trust has invested in excess of £100,000 in the last 3 years as part of a new training package for staff working in its acute and community hospitals.  All staff in those settings have been offered online training whilst the Trust rolls out the face-to-face training to approximately 3500 staff).set up a dashboard on the Datix incident reporting system for relevant senior managers to monitor incidents and look at trends within their area of responsibilitythe development of Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) to help manage violence and aggressiona process to consider the possibility of using the NHS Violence Prevention and Reduction Standard Assessment as a means of managing performanceThe Trust has also begun a period of formal public consultation on a trial of the use of body worn cameras in the Emergency Department at Antrim Hospital and will consider wider use of that technology following the trial period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to promote the growth of the Irish language in English-medium schools.
answer_text: Under Article 89 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, my Department has a duty to encourage and facilitate the development of Irish-medium education. This Article also states “Irish-medium education” means education provided in an Irish speaking school.  There is no such statutory duty placed on my Department to encourage or facilitate the development of Irish language in English-medium schools. It is important to note that Irish, as a recognised language of the European Union is classed as one of the languages under the Area of Learning ‘Modern Languages’ which is within Key Stage 3 of the Northern Ireland Curriculum. As with all areas of curriculum content that is taught in schools across Northern Ireland, it is a decision for individual schools to make regarding which particular language(s) under this Area of Learning that they offer and deliver to their pupils.Given the unavoidable financial pressures and increasing demands on services in education across all of Northern Ireland, my priority remains with fulfilling statutory and policy obligations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 24374/22-27, to detail (i) the 14 Development Proposals (DPs) for transformation received by his office; (ii) the specific criteria used to approve the seven approved DPs; (iii) how this criteria was not met in the rejected cases; and (iv) the four DPs currently under consideration by his Department.
answer_text: Since the introduction of the Integrated Education Act on the 26 October 2022 my Department has received 15 Development Proposals (DPs) for Transformation as detailed below.DP NumberDate PublishedSchool NameDENI RefManagement TypeReasonPhaseDecision69008/12/2022Sion Mills PS2016442ControlledTransformationPrimaryApproved69308/12/2022Gillygooley PS2012648ControlledTransformationPrimaryNot Approved70407/09/2023Magherafelt NS3116177ControlledTransformationNurseryApproved with modification71814/09/2023Lurgan Model PS5010992ControlledTransformationPrimaryApproved71614/09/2023St Eugene's PS, Victoria Bridge2032682Catholic MaintainedTransformationPrimaryNot Approved71714/09/2023St Eugene's PS, Tircur2032688Catholic MaintainedTransformationPrimaryNot Approved71912/10/2023Cairnshill PS4013024ControlledTransformationPrimaryApproved72708/02/2024Bangor Academy and Sixth Form Coll4210296ControlledTransformationPost PrimaryNot Approved72808/02/2024Rathmore PS4016067ControlledTransformationPrimaryNot Approved73216/04/2024Downshire NS5116256ControlledTransformationNurseryApproved with modification73315/05/2024Ballymena Nursery School3110037ControlledTransformationNurseryApproved with modification73710/09/2024Culmore PS2012052ControlledTransformationPrimaryLive DP73511/09/2024Downshire PS4016649ControlledTransformationPrimaryLive DP73612/09/2024Ballyhenry PS3016170ControlledTransformationPrimaryLive DP74316/01/2025Duneane PS3010558ControlledTransformationPrimaryLive DPThe legal process for Transformation is defined and described in The Education Reform (Northern Ireland) Order 1989. The Department published guidance in relation to Transformation on 6 December 2017, ‘Integration Works, Transforming Your School’ to support schools that wish to consider Transformation.  The guidance also sets out the criteria the Department will consider in assessing proposals for Transformation. The proposals were assessed against the criteria outlined in the guidance and the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.  The Sustainable Schools Policy (SSP) is the framework, used by schools and school managing authorities, for assessing the viability and long-term sustainability of schools. At its core is the aim of ensuring that all pupils have access to a high quality, broad and balanced curriculum that meets their educational needs in schools that are educationally and financially viable.The assessment of any DP is a complex process, and each DP is assessed on its own merits and follows a comprehensive process that includes significant research, engagement with key stakeholders, consultation on a Case for Change, objections received within a two-month statutory objection period; other views including meetings with locally elected politicians, advice from policy teams within the Department; the professional advice of the Education and Training Inspectorate, and any other information considered pertinent to the proposal. The full detailed reports on which the decisions were ta

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps that will be taken to plan for Irish language signage where appropriate in all future projects, so as to support the growth of the Irish language and to ensure best management of public money.
answer_text: In line with its ECRML obligations the Department will continue to consider requests for Irish language signage on a case-by-case basis until such times as the outworkings of the Irish Language Strategy and subsequent action plans are developed, agreed and in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) who adopts private roads after companies who previously owned them go out of business; and (ii) the number of roads and streets awaiting adoption by her Department.
answer_text: My Department adopts private roads through the Private Streets (Northern Ireland) Order 1980.  Prior to starting work on a development site a developer is required by the Order to take out insurance via a bond.  If a company goes out of business the bond may be used by my Department to complete the works if necessary.  However, there are a number of development sites where the bond does not cover the shortfall between the value of the bond and the cost of bringing the roads to an adoptable standard.  In these cases the Department has no funding mechanism to bridge the shortfall and no legal requirement to do so.Our records indicate that there are currently 3,454 bonded stretches within development sites that contain unadopted roads.  This number includes long term unadopted developments and developments which have only commenced in the last few weeks.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any measures they have been taken to promote the North Coast in the United States of America, particularly with consideration to The Open at Royal Portrush Golf Club in 2025.
answer_text: We promoted the North Coast's role in hosting The Open during our visit to North Carolina as an example of NI’s attractiveness as a location for golf, for major events, for investment and for tourism.  The Deputy First Minister further highlighted The Open during her visit to Washington DC.  Hosting The Open featured in speeches at the NI Bureau Breakfast in Washington DC where the Claret Jug was on display for 250 guests and media outlets.  The NI Bureau takes every opportunity to promote the event in its engagements with key stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 24799/22-27, whether consultation was sought with teaching union representatives specifically to agree the demands as presented by the new Key Stage assessments.
answer_text: The proposed arrangements for Key Stage Assessment are specifically designed to have minimal workload implications for teachers and will be designed, administered and marked by CCEA.My officials engaged with the representatives of the teaching unions as well as school leaders prior to the announcement of the arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department is taking any measures to outlaw parental smacking of children.
answer_text: The current law in Northern Ireland on the physical punishment of children is based on the concept of “reasonable chastisement”.  Article 2 of the Law Reform (Miscellaneous Provisions) (NI) Order 2006 has the effect of restricting the grounds on which the defence of reasonable chastisement can be used.In essence, this means that if a parent or adult smacks a child and is prosecuted, they can defend themselves in terms of “reasonable chastisement” but only provided that the harm is minor.  Anything which causes more than transitory or minor discomfort is unlawful and can result in prosecution.  Consequently, a mild smack is, therefore, still legal in Northern Ireland.While I am fully supportive of changing the law here in Northern Ireland, the issues are broader than criminal justice alone, and any change to the law will require Executive agreement, particularly in areas such as health, parenting strategy and family law.Despite my efforts to do so, I have not been able to secure the cross-Executive agreement needed to deliver a change in the law in this area but I will continue to work with my Executive colleagues to seek their support to repeal the defence of reasonable chastisement here in Northern Ireland and move towards a position of equal protection for children.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any steps their Department is taking to cooperate with their counterparts in Dublin to support vulnerable migrants and combat people smuggling.
answer_text: Our officials liaise with counterparts in the Irish Department of Justice.  Tackling immigration crime is not a matter for The Executive Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 19302/22-27, to detail the number of incidents his Department has recorded of children being told not to return to school because the school has deemed it not safe for them to do so, recognising that there has been reports of at least one such incident in the last year.
answer_text: I can confirm that neither the Department of Education nor the Education Authority holds records of the number of such incidents. You may wish to note that the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, which came into effect in 2021, requires schools to record incidents or alleged incidents of bullying type behaviour, their motivation and their outcome. Therefore, records of this nature are held at individual school level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will commit to meet with CCEA to re-examine bereavement policy for exams.
answer_text: My officials raised the matter with CCEA on 14 February 2025 for them to consider through the appropriate channels.  CCEA’s examination processes operate within a framework that is set out by the Joint Council for Qualifications (JCQ), which ensures consistency of approach across awarding organisations in England, Wales and Northern Ireland.  Any changes to that framework must be agreed by JCQ members.  As such, it is not appropriate for my Department to intervene in these agreed processes beyond asking CCEA, as a member of JCQ, to raise the issue with the other JCQ member organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant of AQW 24373/22-27, to detail any new measures identified by his Department on the multi-agency Substance Use Strategy Programme Board that may be brought forward for implementation into the education system in the 2025-26 academic year.
answer_text: To date, there are no new actions for the department and my officials will continue to work with colleagues on a cross-departmental basis to ensure that, where possible, work in respect of this important topic is incorporated across relevant policy areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the measures his Department is taking to ensure all children engaged in Emotionally Based School Avoidance have full access to the curriculum.
answer_text: Where a pupil is absent due to any condition associated with Emotionally Based School Avoidance, schools in conjunction with parents and where appropriate the Education Welfare Service should work towards ensuring that full access to the curriculum is provided through a return to regular attendance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will extend free prescription of MiYOSMART lenses for children diagnosed with myopia for whom this would be the most effective treatment, as is available on the NHS in Britain.
answer_text: Unfortunately, at this time there is not sufficient robust evidence, from formal research, to demonstrate long-term effectiveness and value for money of these lenses. There is significant research ongoing, much of it industry-driven, around Miyosmart lenses, and other medical and pharmaceutical interventions that may help stop short sightedness progressing, and my Department keeps developments under review.		In order to be able to provide these types of lenses or interventions to everyone who may benefit from them, free or at a reduced cost via the Health Service, some more detailed and longer clinical studies are needed.  The evidence provided can then be used by the Department of Health to consider if these are not only cost effective but provide positive peer-reviewed results for management of short-sighted patients. Many of the studies have limitations and currently the Department of Health still has no valid scientific criteria to decide when to initiate treatment based on progression; further research is necessary. These results need to be confirmed in future as the current knowledge is limited by the length of study periods and number of populations studied before the interventions can considered properly as safe and effective evidence-based care. 				The provision under GOS for myopia control intervention is variable throughout the UK and not all areas fund the intervention. It is not currently available and funded by GOS in Northern Ireland although GOS sight tests are available to all children in line with recommended sight test intervals. All children are entitled to receive GOS funding for standard spectacles under the GOS voucher scheme, which will correct underlying myopia. Currently however, because of the lack of conclusive and robust evidence for interventional treatments, GOS do not fund any intervention or brand of specialist spectacle lens.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what measures his Department is taking to prevent deaths of inpatients in mental health facilities.
answer_text: I would like to begin by stating that any deaths of this nature are deeply regrettable and the loss of even one life, if it could have been prevented, is one too many. It is important to note that acute mental health services provide intensive treatment for those patients who are most acutely unwell, vulnerable and who cannot be appropriately cared for within their community. Patient safety remains my priority. A key focus across the region has been the investment programme for ligature resistant work on hospital sites. This investment has facilitated the transformation of service delivery through the creation of safer and more therapeutic environments for our patients.The region has also moved to adopting the Safe Wards Initiative which also gives a framework to maximise patient safety and ensure that inpatient mental health care is person centred which evidence shows enhances patient safety. For example, wards are creating more calming environments for patients and their families.Each Trust is also part of the Towards Zero Suicide Collaborative, whose aim is to improve the care and outcomes for people at risk of suicide who come in to contact with Mental Health Services. In addition, they have implemented the Suicide Prevention Care Pathway which co-produced in partnership with service users, staff and key stakeholders. As you have heard there are a number of ongoing measures in place to address preventable deaths within mental health facilities designed to reduce risk and deliver person centred care. I would continue to welcome your support in the coming months as I call for more sustainable and adequate funding for patient safety in mental health facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that people living with cancer in the Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council area continue to have access to the Move More programme, following concerns about reduced funding and service availability.
answer_text: The Macmillan Move More Programme was established by Macmillan Cancer Care and implemented in partnership with District Councils in 2016. HSC Trusts have referred cancer patients to Move More services since 2016. From 2016 – 2022 Macmillan provided 100% of all council salary costs for the Move More Programme. In 2022, my department committed investment to the voluntary sector to support the intention of the Cancer Strategy actions via the Cancer Charities Fund. Macmillan Cancer Support received funding to enable the testing of a prehabilitation model for Northern Ireland which included pathways to Move More physical activity programmes in all 11 councils. At this time, discussions around sustainability for Move More coordinator roles were held between Macmillan Cancer Care and the councils and salary costs were split equally between Macmillan Cancer Care and the District Councils for a two-year period (2022 to 2024).All Macmillan funding for the Move More Coordinators ended in March 2024. I understand that some district councils have been unable to sustain Move More Coordinator roles. My department continues to work very closely with our council partners, Public Health Agency and local Health and Social Care Trusts to maintain engagement and service continuity for all physical activity programmes for people living with cancer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the announcement that Belfast Grand Central Station will implement dual Irish and English language signage and ticket machines, whether she will roll-out this policy to include all other Translink train and bus depots.
answer_text: I am committed to equality, inclusivity and respect and in that context to increased visibility of the Irish Language across public services and in our society. My recent announcement is to provide physical signs in Irish which will be in addition to the existing signage at Belfast Grand Central Station. The precise details of the signs will be determined during the design process. Consideration was given as to whether this would benefit from conducting a full Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA). However, based on the anticipated positive impact, the Department has made the decision that a full EQIA is not required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any specific measures he is implementing to address outstanding concerns from teachers in relation to their workloads.
answer_text: Acknowledging the workload concerns that have been raised by teachers and school leaders constructive negotiations have taken place with trade unions and I have listened to their concerns about workload. I want to make meaningful progress on the issues to ensure that teachers have the time and space to focus on what matters most – teaching, learning and supporting pupils in the classroom.In order to seek to break the deadlock, I proposed an independent review of all aspects of teachers’ workload, that will be taken forward by a three-member panel, comprising an independent chair appointed by me, a member nominated by the unions and a member nominated by Management Side.The Panel will examine teachers’ workload and report to me by the end of November 2025. It will also monitor the implementation of the Heads of Agreement commitments agreed by the trade unions and Management Side and issued alongside the pay offer.I also recently launched a new education strategy, TransformED, which will reform and improve education delivery in Northern Ireland. It contains a range of commitments that are designed to drive improvement but also reduce workload.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, following the recommendations in the Committee on the Administration of Justice report Policing the Protectors: A Narrative Report of PSNI Policing of Environmental Protest in the Sperrins, whether she will be advancing the recommendations for the PSNI to ensure officers engaged in the policing of protests receive adequate training on the human rights and the rights to protest in such circumstances.
answer_text: The implementation of recommendations outlined in the Committee on the Administration of Justice report, Policing the Protectors: A Narrative Report of PSNI Policing of Environmental Protest in the Sperrins, falls within the operational remit of the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational responsibility of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.For further details on this matter, I would encourage you to direct your inquiry to the PSNI or the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 22720/22-27, to detail his Department's assessment of the administrative duties that should be performed by nursery school teaching principals.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) is not the employer of teachers and has no remit to intervene in the day-to-day running of schools, including administrative duties performed by nursery school teaching principals.  Teachers and school leaders of grant-aided schools are employed under the arrangements set out in TNC 1987/26, commonly referred to as the Jordanstown Agreement.  The Teachers’ (Terms and Conditions of Employment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1987, Schedule 1, Regulation 4 provides the following General Functions of a Principal: A Principal shall be required to carry out his/her professional duties in accordance with the school’s policy and objectives as determined by the employing authority and, in the case of a controlled school, the Board of Governors and shall obey the reasonable directions of the employing authority and, in the case of a controlled school, the Board of Governors. Subject to paragraph 1 the principal shall be responsible for the internal organisation, management and control of the school. In carrying out his/her duties the principal shall consult, where this is appropriate, with the employing authority, the staff of the school and, in the case of a controlled school, the Board of Governors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps his Department has taken to address water pollution in relation to farm run-off and pesticides.
answer_text: My Department is committed to protecting people and the aquatic environment from the risks associated with pesticides.DAERA delivers several programmes to support industry including knowledge and technology transfer projects, training, demonstration farms and guidance. These encourage better use, handling and storage of pesticides and support farmers in the uptake of Integrated Pest Management (IPM) techniques which have been shown to improve natural capital, including water quality. Further, the Water Catchment Partnership, aims to address the problem of pesticides in drinking water catchment areas.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on any new initiatives that are in place to improve rehabilitation in Northern Ireland's prison system and reduce recidivism.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) supports and challenges prisoners to change and address their offending behaviour whilst in custody to reduce their risk of reoffending on release.  Working with partners from the statutory, voluntary/community, pastoral and education sectors they provide a range of programmes and interventions which are kept under review to ensure that they align with the needs and priorities of the prison population, including the increased number of remand and short sentence prisoners.Over the past year, NIPS has partnered with a Voluntary and Community Sector organisation, Extern, to deliver a pilot, bespoke programme to manage the transition of short-term prisoners into the community by targeting the factors which positively impact on their desistance from reoffending.In addition to this programme, two other recent initiatives being delivered within Prisons are ‘Trauma Talks’ and ‘Snakes and Ladders’.  The ‘Trauma Talks’ programme aims to educate and inform participates in trauma awareness, and ‘Snakes and Ladders’ has been introduced to support the increasing ‘recall population’ to gain the insights they need to understand the link between trauma, addiction and crime.Our wide ranging curriculum of educational, vocational and employability skills training is kept under continuous review to ensure that it is relevant to the needs of individuals and cognisant of labour market trends and gaps in the labour market.  We have introduced a range of short accredited courses in areas such as Food Safety and Manual Handling that will enable individuals on short sentences or on remand to leave custody with employer recognised qualifications.Furthermore, NIPS has introduced a range of additional services to enhance support for prisoners in the weeks leading up to release. These include access to Northern Ireland Housing Executive Advisors to assist with accommodation planning, and engagement with Department for Communities Work Coaches to support employability and benefit readiness.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on any new measures that are being taken to meet environmental sustainability targets and to combat Climate Change.
answer_text: The headline requirement of the Climate Change Act is that it has placed a general duty on all Departments here to ensure that Net Zero is achieved by 2050. Under the Act TEO is responsible for making regulations to establish a Climate Commissioner. The NI Climate Commissioner Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2025 were approved by the Assembly on 8 April 2025.DAERA are the lead department on a number of other requirements of the Act, working in collaboration with all other Executive Departments. Examples include the setting of Carbon Budgets, and the drafting and publication of a Climate Action Plan.DfE lead on the Circular Economy Strategy and their work relating to product regulation provides a mechanism to place sustainability requirements on products being placed on the market; with DfI leading on planning including transport, and active travel.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the percentage of (i) primary schools; and (ii) post-primary schools, that have carried out a silent or intruder alarm drill in each of the last five academic years.
answer_text: The information requested is not held by the Department.  Officials have requested input from the Education Authority, who have also advised that they do not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on progress made in developing the concept of Jointly Managed Schools, as recommended by the Independent Review of Education, including any challenges or opportunities that have been identified.
answer_text: Clear guidance, developed in consultation with the Catholic Trustees and Transferors and circulated to all schools, is available on the mechanism through which jointly managed schools may be established (DE Circular 2015/15).The establishment of a new jointly managed school is required through the Development Proposal process and only where there is a clear desire to do so on the part of the prospective constituent schools and the communities they serve. Any proposed school – jointly managed or otherwise - must be viable in the longer term when assessed against the criteria set out in DE’s Sustainable Schools Policy.   My Department would welcome proposals for jointly managed schools, however no such proposal has been brought forward to date. My Department accepts the purpose of the Independent Panel’s recommendation to ‘Promote learners learning together’ and are continuing work to ensure that all learners have opportunities to learn alongside their peers from other communities and backgrounds, including through implementation of the long term Mainstreaming Shared Education Strategy.In line with the panel’s recommendation, my Department will seek to develop further guidance and support to facilitate schools from different sectors to become Jointly Managed Community Schools.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the timeline for bringing forward a technology-based strategy to help facilitate the end of online gender-based harm, as referenced in the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: Online abuse and violence against women and girls are an area of focus of the Executive’s Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework 2024-31. The Strategic Framework will be agile in its delivery to ensure it can respond to evolving and changing culture and practices.Action 2 of the Delivery Plan 2024-26 provides for support for technological and other innovations by the private sector to improve the safety of women and girls. To deliver this commitment, The Executive Office secured £475k ring-fenced funding for two Phase 1 projects from the DfE Small Business Research Initiative Challenge Fund. Challenge 1 - Addressing Violence Against Women and Girls Through Data and Tech Innovations and Challenge 2 - Making Spaces Safer For Women and Girls Through Design and Technology.Following an open Phase 1 (proof of concept) competition, six successful suppliers are working to develop their proof-of-concept solutions across a broad range of areas and across sectors. Subject to confirmation of future year funding, Phase 2 (development) would progress in 2025-26 for a proportion of the Phase 1 suppliers.As the online safety regulator, the Office of Communications (Ofcom) has responsibilities for addressing harms to women and girls online. In February, Ofcom launched a consultation on Guidance for A Safer Life Online for Women and Girls as part of the requirements of the Online Safety Act 2023. The draft Guidance focuses on four overlapping forms of harm covering content and activity set out in the Online Safety Act 2023 - online misogyny, pile-ons and online harassment, online domestic abuse, and image-based sexual abuse. Evidence suggests these disproportionately affect women and girls. The draft Guidance sets out nine Actions where technology firms should do more to improve women and girls’ online safety by taking responsibility, designing their services to prevent harm and supporting their users. Our officials are continuing to liaise with Ofcom officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the total amount spent to date on the consultation for the strategic review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum announced in October 2024.
answer_text: Consultation has been ongoing throughout the Review. This has included online meetings and focus groups, as well as an open call for evidence and teacher survey.  There have also been visits to schools and in-person meetings.  It is estimated that costs directly linked to consultation would be in the order of £3,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has agreed the proposed Key Stage Assessments with trade unions in relation to workload agreements.
answer_text: Key Stage assessments are an existing statutory duty and participation in the system level check is a legal requirement for selected schools specified in the Assessment Documentation produced by CCEA under The Education Levels of Progression Order (Northern Ireland) 2012. The proposed arrangements have been developed in consultation with the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) and key stakeholders, including school leaders. They are specifically designed to minimise workload being designed, administered and marked by CCEA. Outcomes will not be published at pupil or school level so no test preparation is required. My officials notified union representatives in relation to the assessment arrangements for the three-year period from 2025-26 and the workload implications were considered in advance. My officials and CCEA will work closely with schools, teachers and stakeholders to ensure the smooth implementation of the arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how much protected time he plans to allocate for teachers continued professional development in the 2025-26 academic year.
answer_text: Protected time for teacher professional learning (TPL) is a local management decision, with individual teachers, as professionals, identifying and undertaking learning that aligns with their pupils’ needs and their own development. Teachers’ directed working time excludes the time spent off school premises in undertaking planning, preparation and assessment (PPA), however school leaders may determine the need for protected time within a teacher’s Directed Time Budget for PPA and / or TPL.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department will take to ensure that the announced Key Stage Assessments will not adversely impact teaching, noting that a UCL study published in 2024 found that 32 per cent teachers said preparing for SATs had a negative effect on student engagement and 55 per cent reported teaching to the test.
answer_text: The recently announced Key Stage assessments in Northern Ireland are system level sample assessments and are, therefore, entirely different from SATs in England.  They have been specifically designed to avoid the phenomenon of teaching to the test.Outcomes will only be published at system level and will not be used to measure individual school performance nor published at pupil or school level. This means they will be very low stakes at pupil and school level meaning there is no need to engage in test preparation. System level evaluations are used widely internationally and the key purpose will be to provide a robust measure of national educational performance in the core areas of literacy and numeracy. The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment will design and mark the written assessments. Substitute cover will be provided to assist the invigilation of the assessments. This approach will further minimise workload for schools.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) for an update on the implementation of the recommendations made in the High Street Task Force report; and (ii) to detail any measures that have been considered for high streets in the East Derry constituency.
answer_text: A number of recommendations are being taken forward by Ministerial colleagues at the Departments for Communities, Economy, Finance, and Infrastructure. Further detail can be sought from those departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the stated aims of the Programme for Government 2024-2027 to improve childcare provision and support families facing disadvantage, to detail any steps being taken to extend the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme to include school-age children to ensure equitable support for families with children of all ages.
answer_text: I am currently reviewing the parameters of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS), including eligibility, to ensure that it remains effective in reducing childcare costs for working families in 2025/26.  I am also considering what changes would be affordable within the £50m draft allocation.  Once the budget is finalised, I will bring forward proposals to my Executive colleagues for approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that communities impacted by recently reported Santander branch closures continue to have access to cash services.
answer_text: ANSWERI am hugely disappointed in Santander’s recent announcement to close seven of its bank branches here. Although it is welcome that a banking hub will be set up in Larne, I am concerned about the impact these closures will have on those communities affected.I will continue my predecessors calls on the banks to protect their remaining branch network and to maintain access to cash and vital banking services especially for areas impacted by bank closures.I am due to visit the Warrenpoint Banking Hub next week, where I will meet with Cash Access UK and press on the need for appropriate in-person access to banking services for all those areas losing bank branches.My department recently published a report following a Banking Sector Roundtable event held by my predecessor, Dr Caoimhe Archibald, MLA. This sets out the key issues identified by stakeholders, including those in relation to ensuring access to cash and services. I have sent this report to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury, Darren Jones MP, and the Financial Conduct Authority Chief Executive, Nikhil Rathi, given their legislative authority over banking, seeking a response on how they will address the matters raised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether his Department has any plans to undertake a review of Area Planning, as outlined in the December 2023 Independent Review of Education; and (ii) whether an assessment has been made of how a review may impact integrated education.
answer_text: In its observations and recommendations on the Area Planning process, the Panel for the Independent Review of Education concluded that the concept of Area Planning is sound and essential for the management of the network of schools.  Noting a current lack of pace within area planning structures, it recommended a new approach through the establishment in the short term of an “Independent Planning Commission” and, longer-term, to establishment of a single strategic planning authority to be responsible for the ongoing planning of the network of schools and colleges.I have agreed “in principle” recommendation 20 (reconfigure network in schools) and in order to consider the step change that is required I have determined that an “Area Planning Taskforce” will be established.  Initial scoping work is underway through the existing area planning structures (at working and strategic group levels), examining how such a Taskforce could contribute to the delivery of the broader education programme (which would include the statutory duties to integrated education and how it operates within the area planning process) and I will be presented with findings and recommendations in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any measures his Department has taken to revise the Pupil Allocation Number for preschool placements in 2025-2026, to ensure it is sufficient to meet demand whilst noting that some schools are having to reject children, including those whose application has been a fifth choice.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the allocation of funded pre-school places to non-statutory pre-school providers participating in the Pre-School Education Programme; my Department does not play a role in the process.The EA has advised that at this stage in the pre-school admissions process, it has received 20,636 first preference applications for 22,099 funded places across Northern Ireland.The pre-school admissions process has two stages and stage one remains ongoing at this time.  Should additional funded places be required in local areas, the EA will adjust allocations to settings as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether his Department has made an assessment of the potential impact that any cut in benefits may have; and (ii) any assurances he has received from the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions that any new changes will avoid undue harm to those currently in receipt of disability benefits.
answer_text: I am extremely concerned about the proposed welfare changes announced on 18 March, which I raised during a meeting I had with Hilary Benn, Secretary of State for Northern Ireland on 20 March. I, along with Executive colleagues, have also written to the Prime Minister to record our concerns and disappointment at the changes announced.I understand the shock and uncertainty caused by the proposed changes, but my department does not yet know the full impact. My officials are continuing to work closely with the Department for Work and Pensions to understand each of the changes and their implications for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant of AQW 21296/22-27, to detail (i) actions that can be expected from the Outdoor Recreation Action Plan; and (ii) a timeline for when the review will be published.
answer_text: The actions that can be expected from a new Outdoor Recreation Action Plan will be identified during its development.It is expected that the review will be published in autumn 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the status of development proposals for Integrated schools seeking expansion, including a timeline of expected resolutions for the proposals outstanding.
answer_text: Development Proposal (DP) 738 – “A statutory nursery unit will be established at All Children’s Controlled Integrated Primary School to provide 26 part-time nursery places with effect from 1 September 2026, or as soon as possible thereafter” was published on 11 November 2024.I am informed that the report on which I will frame my decision is currently completing final internal review and is expected to be issued to me in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether a child with a statement of special educational need may be permitted access to the curriculum just one hour per day, per week, on the basis that the child's needs are too much for the school to manage.
answer_text: Where a child or young person’s statement of Special Educational Needs specifies her/his legal entitlement to the full Northern Ireland curriculum, there is a duty on a school to educate this pupil in line with that statement. In reducing the school day, schools could be considered to be discriminating against the pupil on the grounds of their disability and such action could result in legal action being taken against the school. In relation to the full school day, in a mainstream primary school the relevant legislation is Primary Schools’ General Regulation 1973 No 407. These regulations also apply to Post Primary Schools. The regulations state that daily attendance means 3 hours for a pupil under the age of 8 years and 4 ½ hours in the case of any other pupil. However, this does not include Religious Education which should be for ½ hour each day. This means that a pupil under the age of 8 should attend for 3½ hours per day and a child over the age of 8 for 5 hours.Any reductions to the timetable for a pupil should therefore only be for a short time, with the agreement of the parents and in the interest of the child e.g. when reintegrating a school refuser, following a significant emotional upset, as part of a multi-disciplinary approach to meeting the pupil’s needs or when a reduced day is supported by medical evidence. If a pupil’s timetable is temporarily reduced this should be kept under regular review with the intention of increasing the curriculum access as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 24026/22-27, to detail how the provision of the statistics requested is considered an operational matter.
answer_text: The incidence of crime in Northern Ireland is reported on by the PSNI and not the Department.  The statistics on the incidence of crime, as well as a range of others, such as, inter alia, statistics on stop and search, drug seizure statistics and domestic abuse incidents are collated and produced by statisticians seconded to the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) from the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA). Those statistics are produced in accordance with the pillars and principles set out in the Code of Practice for Statistics, with police recorded crime statistics being designated as an Accredited Official Statistic.  As these are produced outside of my Department and by the PSNI, they are for the Chief Constable to provide to individuals requesting the data.  A full list of the statistics produced by the core Department can be found at the Statistics and Research page of the Department of Justice website.  They include statistics on prosecutions and convictions at courts and out of courts disposals, first time entrants to the justice system, case processing times for criminal cases dealt with at courts, adult and youth reoffending, prison population statistics, Youth Justice Agency annual workload statistics, youth engagement statistics, statistics produced by the NI Courts and Tribunals Service, such as Judicial statistics, NICTS quarterly business statistics and Mortgages: action for possession statistics, as well as results from the Northern Ireland Safe Community Survey and from the Northern Ireland Victims and Witness Survey.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of prosecutions for the improper application of slurry in the last five years.
answer_text: There has been one prosecution relating to ‘the improper application of slurry’ in the last five years.Prosecution is not the only course of action in relation to improper application of slurry.
