question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action his Department is taking to ensure that all individuals entitled to compensation due to work relating to the A6 project receive timely compensation, recognising that some are now waiting in excess of five years.
answer_text: When answering AQW 18374 22-37 in November 2024, my department had 254 Full and Final Settlements that remained to be paid to landowners along the A6. Since that date a further 8 have been paid Full and Final Payments, reducing the total to 248.  191 interim claims have been paid, up to a value of 90% of Land and Property Services estimate of the value of land taken.My department continues to work closely with Land and Property Services (LPS) and Departmental Solicitors Office (DSO) to bring payments to closure.  The delay in the process is largely due to  time taken to settle offers made by LPS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the steps taken to measure the demand for integrated education, as required by the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: My Department set out a definition of demand for integrated education and its initial conclusions on demand in the statutory Section 10 report published in April 2024. Our analysis of existing admissions choices data suggested that while there is broadly sufficient provision of Integrated Education at the primary school level to meet parental preference, the same cannot be said for post-primary provision. In some areas, there is a higher demand for Integrated Education provision than that which is currently available. The report identified that there is demand for post-primary Integrated Education in excess of supply in six localities, namely Belfast, Lisburn and Castlereagh, Ards and North Down, Antrim and Newtownabbey, Mid and East Antrim, and Newry and Mourne. On conclusion of work to update guidance for schools considering transformation to Integrated status, my Department will commission the Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education (NICIE) to engage with these oversubscribed Integrated schools and the Education Authority (EA) in the six localities identified. The affected schools will be encouraged to review their enrolment numbers and school capacity and prepare and bring forward DPs to increase their enrolment numbers (if such changes are significant in nature). DE acknowledges that while this approach may partially address oversubscription issues, it is unlikely to resolve them in full. DE will therefore commission NICIE, working in partnership with EA and CSSC to issue a ‘Call for Transformation’ suggesting schools in the identified localities consider Transformation to Integrated status to meet demand. To further monitor demand across Northern Ireland, Departmental officials have included societal level questions on demand and the factors parents consider when makings school admission decisions in the annual Northern Ireland Life and Times survey.  Results will be available in late Spring with a report in the Summer.My officials are also working closely with colleagues in the Education Authority to develop and conduct a survey of parents who have recently made school preference decisions through the admissions portal. This survey will be carried out in April 2025 and will ask parents and guardians, in line with the Integrated Education Act, whether they have a preference for Integrated Education and how various factors have influenced their school preferences. Both surveys will allow my Department to consider existing and future demand and provide data on which existing Area Planning structures can take action to aim to meet that demand in the context the overall sustainability of the school estate. However, it is vital to note that according to statute, “Integrated Education” means the education together, in an integrated school, of (a) those of different cultures and religious beliefs and of none, including reasonable numbers of both Protestant and Roman Catholic children or young persons; (b) those who are experiencing socio

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 22720/22-27, to detail the difference in working hours in the contracts of a primary school principal and a teaching nursery school principal.
answer_text: There is no difference in the working hours in the contracts of a primary school principal and a teaching nursery school principal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any measures his Department is taking to address pollution; and (ii) the number of recorded unsatisfactory intermittent discharges of sewage in the Lower Bann in the last 12 months.
answer_text: (i) Addressing water pollution is a priority for my Department and all such reports are investigated. Members of the public can report water pollution at any time of the day via the NIEA Incident Hotline. Prompt reporting is critical to a timely response to enable sufficient evidence to be gathered to facilitate taking forward the most appropriate enforcement and/or remedial action.NIEA seeks to pursue formal enforcement action where the polluter is identified and where the pollution has had a High or Medium Severity environmental impact.My commitment to improving water quality is set out in the recently published Environmental Improvement Plan (EIP) which includes the requirement to publish a third cycle River Basin Management Plan (RBMP). The RBMP will contain a Programme of Measures, setting out the actions required to protect and improve all waterbodies in Northern Ireland.(ii) NIEA does not record the number of unsatisfactory intermittent discharges of sewage, but it does respond to reports of water pollution. In 2024 NIEA responded to eleven sewage related water pollution incidents in the Lower Bann from a range of sources including one Waste-Water Treatment Works, six from Combined Sewer Overflows or blockages of the sewerage network and four relating to Septic Tanks. Each incident was confirmed to have a Low severity environmental impact. In such cases NIEA ensures that the discharge is stopped and work with the polluter to prevent recurrence.NIW publish the data collected from Event and Duration Monitors installed at a range of discharge locations. The data can be accessed on the NIW website  www.niwater.com/storm/overflow. NI Water has also published the modelled data for the potential spill frequency for each CSO in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 19637/22-27, to detail any measures his Department is taking to address harm reduction, recognising that drugs deaths have increased nearly 50 per cent in the last decade.
answer_text: Officials are part of the cross -departmental, multi-agency Substance Use Strategy Programme Board which oversees the implementation of the Department of Health’s ‘Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery’ strategic framework to tackle the harm from substance use. My department’s primary role of harm reduction, which is informed by the strategy, is to ensure that through delivery of the statutory curriculum, children and young people are educated on the dangers of drugs and substance use.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures his Department is taking to protect and safeguard the access to harbours for the local fishing industry.
answer_text: My Department continues to provide the Northern Ireland Fisheries Harbour Authority with the financial and other resources required to protect and safeguard access to and from Northern Ireland’s three primary fishing ports of Ardglass, Kilkeel and Portavogie.The Department has no responsibility for other ports in Northern Ireland, which are either Trust Ports, private operations or owned by local authorities. Responsibility for the protection and safeguarding of all port users falls to the owners of such harbours.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on plans for the A2 Ballykelly Bypass.

To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 24173/22-27, to provide a comprehensive list of criteria used by the Education Authority for the selection of the schools, detailing (i) what made a school eligible; (ii) what ruled a school ineligible; and (iii) a detailed overview of how the process for selecting schools was undertaken.
answer_text: The aim of SEN Pilot (2020) was to develop, test and agree a new process that facilitates the establishment, closure or change of Specialist Provision at Mainstream Schools in an agile, efficient and expedient manner without the requirement for a Development Proposal (DP).The schools identified to test the process were already being considered for specialist provision and noted within the Education Authority’s (EA) Action Plan for Primary, Post-Primary and Special Schools 2019 – 2021 and were ready to proceed at the start of the pilot. The proposals aligned to the EA’s Draft Framework for Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools being consulted upon at that time. (Link to the Draft Framework and Pilot for Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools October 2020 consultation document). The EA identified nine projects, involving 10 schools, which had:existing temporary provisions established for more than four years with proven demand and delivery of high-quality specialist provision in a mainstream setting.major capital announcements, with existing provision, which provide the opportunity for enhanced provision in modern fit-for-purpose built accommodation.major capital announcements, without existing provision, where there is identified local need.Other schools named in the Action Plan 2019-21 but not included in the pilot were not deemed ineligible. The schools involved would be considered for the new process if it was adopted or continue on the DP process for significant changes in the character or size of a granted-aided school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 24173/22-27, to provide a full list of schools deemed to have met the requirements (i) existing temporary provisions established for more than four years with proven demand and delivery of high-quality specialist provision in a mainstream setting; (ii) major capital announcements, with existing provision, which provide the opportunity for enhanced provision in modern fit-for purpose built accommodation; and (iii) major capital announcements, without existing provision, where there is identified local need.
answer_text: The aim of SEN Pilot (2020) was to develop, test and agree a new process that facilitates the establishment, closure or change of Specialist Provision at Mainstream Schools in an agile, efficient and expedient manner without the requirement for a Development Proposal (DP).The pilot was undertaken by Education Authority (EA) on the Department’s behalf. The EA identified nine projects, involving 10 schools. A full list of the schools is provided in the table below.School Proposed ProvisionKey StagesFort Hill Integrated Primary School, Lisburn2 x Learning and 2 x AutismFoundation, Key Stages 1 and 2Holy Evangelists’ Primary School, Belfast1 x Learning and 1 x AutismFoundation, Key Stages 1 and 2Seaview Primary School, Belfast2 x Learning and 2 x AutismFoundation, Key Stages 1 and 2The Good Shepherd Primary School, Belfast2 x Learning and 2 x AutismFoundation, Key Stages 1 and 2Abbey Community College, Newtownabbey2 x AutismKey Stages 3 and 4Ballycastle Shared Campus:Ballycastle High SchoolCross & Passion College, Ballycastle1 x Learning and 1 x Autism1 x Learning and 1 x AutismKey Stages 3 and 4Key Stages 3 and 4Dunclug College, Ballymena1 x Learning and 1 x AutismKey Stages 3 and 4Fort Hill Integrated College, Lisburn2 x Learning and 2 x AutismKey Stages 3 and 4Lisneal College, Londonderry2 x AutismKey Stages 3 and 4Section two of the consultation document on the EA’s Draft Framework for Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (October 2020) provides additional information on each of the projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on progress made in the last three years on implementing the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 and fulfilling his Department’s duty to encourage, facilitate and support integrated education.
answer_text: I have met all of my obligations under the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.Since commencement I have:Published the initial Strategy for Integrated Education in Northern Ireland and associated Action Plan on 26 April 2023 in line with statutory requirements.Carried out a public consultation on the Action Plan as required by the legislation.Published the Section 10 report on 26 April 2024 in line with statutory requirements.  Officials are continuing to work closely with the Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education (NICIE) and the Education Authority (EA) to implement relevant actions within the S10 report.To further monitor demand across Northern Ireland, Departmental officials have included societal level questions on demand and the factors parents consider when making school admission decisions in the annual Northern Ireland Life and Times survey.  Results will be available in late Spring with a report in the Summer.Officials are also working closely with colleagues in the Education Authority to develop and conduct a survey of parents who have recently made school preference decisions through the admissions portal. This survey will be carried out in April 2025 and will ask parents and guardians, in line with the Integrated Education Act, whether they have a preference for Integrated Education and how various factors have influenced their school preferences. Both surveys will allow my Department to consider existing and future demand and provide data on which existing Area Planning structures can take action to aim to meet that demand in the context the overall sustainability of the school estate.A draft revised Strategy has been shared with NICIE, the Controlled Schools Support Council and the Integrated Education Fund as part of the consultation requirements under Section 3 of the Act. The revised Strategy and Action Plan will be updated and opened to public consultation following due consideration of their comments before final publication in spring 2025.I have considered 14 DPs for Transformation of which I have approved 7.  There are currently 4 DPs for Transformation with the Department for consideration.I have also considered 30 other DPs for Integrated schools, including increasing admission/enrolment numbers, opening nursery units and opening new schools in Mid Down and Causeway areas with associated school closures, of which I have approved 26.  There is currently 1 other such DP from an Integrated school currently with the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any research his Department has undertaken to measure trends in attainment between boys and girls in the education system since the Covid-19 pandemic, recognising that recent research from England shows a widening gap between boys and girls in Maths and Science.
answer_text: My Department commissions three international large-scale assessments (ILSAs) of education that measure trends in attainment between boys and girls and enable comparisons to be made pre and post Covid-19. These are the Progress in International Reading Literacy Study of Year 6 pupils (PIRLS), the Trends in International Mathematics and Science Study of Year 6 pupils (TIMSS) and the Programme for International Student Assessment (PISA), a study of the mathematics, science and reading attainment of 15-year-old pupils. Table 1 shows the subject specific gender gaps in pre and post Covid-19 cycles of PIRLS and PISA for Northern Ireland and England. Northern Ireland withdrew from TIMSS 2023 due to industrial action in schools. Therefore, no data are available to measure trends in primary pupils’ attainment in mathematics and science between TIMSS 2019 and 2023.    Table 1: Gender gaps in attainmentStudyNorthern Ireland gender gap (girls’ score-boys’ score)England gender gap (girls’ score-boys’ score)PIRLS 2016 reading18*15*PIRLS 2021 reading24*10*PISA 2018 mathematics7-13*PISA 2022 mathematics-12*-15*PISA 2018 reading36*20*PISA 2022 reading18*16*PISA 2018 science17*-3PISA 2022 science-6-8Sources: Progress in International Reading Literacy Study (PIRLS) | Department of EducationProgramme for International Student Assessment (PISA) | Department of EducationNote: PIRLS assesses Year 6 pupils and PISA assesses 15-year-old pupils  Gender gaps marked with an asterisk indicate score point differences that are statistically significant at the 95% level. The results from PIRLS 2016 show significant gender gaps in reading, favouring girls, in both Northern Ireland (18 score points) and England (15 score points) with the gap in Northern Ireland increasing to 24 score points by 2021 whereas the gap in England reduced to 10 score points.  The results from PISA 2018 show a non-significant gender gap, favouring girls, in mathematics (7 score points) and significant gender gaps, favouring girls, in reading (36 score points) and science (17 score points) in Northern Ireland. By PISA 2022 the gender gap in mathematics was significant and favouring boys (12 score points); the gap in reading reduced to 18 score points, favouring girls; and the gap in science of 6 score points, favouring boys, was not significant.    The results from PISA 2018 for England show a significant gender gap, favouring boys, in mathematics (13 score points) and a significant gender gap, favouring girls, in reading (20 score points). In science the gender gap, favouring girls, (3 score points) was not significant. By PISA 2022 significant gender gaps were found in mathematics, favouring boys, (15 score points) and in reading, favouring girls (16 score points). The 8 score point gap, favouring boys, in science was not significant.  The educational attainment gap in Northern Ireland since the Covid-19 pandemic has consistently favoured girls. In the 2018/19 academic year, 77.1% of female pupils ach

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any efforts his Department has been making in collaboration with the Department for Communities to ensure that Foras na Gaeilge may be in a position to continue the Scoil Spreagtha scheme into the 2025-26 academic year, recognising his Department's duty to support and facilitate the development of Irish language education.
answer_text: Under Article 89 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, my Department has a duty to encourage and facilitate the development of Irish-medium education.  This article also states “Irish-medium education” means education provided in an Irish speaking school. As the Scoil Spreagtha programme is an accredited scheme designed to recognise and celebrate efforts made by English-medium schools to encourage the use of Irish in the school environment, this would not fall within the Department of Education’s statutory duties.To date, my Department has not been involved in the Scoil Spreagtha project nor has it provided any funding.  Education continues to face unavoidable cost pressures and rising service demands associated with delivering statutory and policy obligations.  I am, therefore, not in a position to commit funding to this scheme.In addition, schools are empowered through local decision-making responsibilities.  It is a matter for individual schools to determine which resources or programmes they wish to use to support their delivery of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the March 2025 Northern Ireland Audit Office report Ambulance Handovers in Northern Ireland, to detail any assurances his Department can give that the proposed relocation of services away from Causeway Hospital will not further exacerbate delays and put patients at increased risk.
answer_text: The Northern HSC Trust held a public consultation on proposals to reconfigure general surgery services between 23 August and 29 November 2024.As part of this process the Northern HSC Trust have engaged with NI Ambulance Services (NIAS) and further engagement is planned to model ambulance capacity required for any potential change.The Trust is currently preparing a consultation outcomes report which will be submitted to the Department of Health in due course and will consider any recommendation made by the Northern HSC Trust at this time.As such, I will consider any recommendation made by the NHSCT on a permanent change or withdrawal of service at this time, in line with the DoH Circular on Change or Withdrawal of Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any dispensations that are available for pensioners who need connected to the electricity grid, so as to ensure that everyone can avail of an electricity connection without paying exorbitant connection fees.
answer_text: NIE Network’s statement of connection charges, which is approved by the Utility Regulator, does not differentiate between customers on the basis of age.My Department is currently consulting on proposals to socialise some elements of connection charges.  The intended consequence of the preferred option in this consultation is to make grid connection charges fairer and more affordable, particularly for those living in areas where the electricity network is weaker, usually in rural areas.  The consultation will close on 24 April and implementation of any changes is targeted for the end of the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 21574/22-27, and in response to research published by Ulster University entitled a Profile of Classroom Assistants in Northern Ireland, to detail any practical steps his Department is taking to evaluate current practices relating to classroom assistant support.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is the employing authority for the majority of classroom assistants in Northern Ireland. Currently, the EA is engaging with Department of Education officials on the operational delivery of one of the actions in the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda Delivery Plan 2025-2030: to design and implement a new classroom assistant employment model. As part of this workstream, there has been extensive engagement with school leaders, trade unions and classroom assistants in order to evaluate current practices relating to classroom assistant support. This evaluation will assist in the development of a new classroom assistant employment model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of school-aged children that have been de-registered from school for reasons other than leaving the state in the last five academic years.
answer_text: A pupil’s name may be removed from a school’s register only in the circumstances set out in Paragraph 10 of DE Circular 2024/13. The Circular advises schools of the process for de-registration, using the SA1 form.Figures for De-Registration through Elective Home Education (EHE):YEARDE-REGISTERED 2024-2025 (to date)258 (to date)2023-20243942022/20233042021/20222892020/20212482019/2020168Figures for De-Registration through the SA1 form: YEARDE-REGISTERED 2024-2025 (to date)31 (to date)2023-202430Data prior to 1 September 2023 is not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the criteria used for selecting the ten schools chosen for the Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools pilot scheme.
answer_text: The aim of SEN Pilot (2020) was to develop, test and agree a new process that facilitates the establishment, closure or change of Specialist Provision at Mainstream Schools in an agile, efficient and expedient manner without the requirement for a Development Proposal (DP).The pilot was undertaken by Education Authority (EA) on the Department’s behalf. The EA identified nine projects, involving 10 schools, which had:existing temporary provisions established for more than four years with proven demand and delivery of high-quality specialist provision in a mainstream setting.major capital announcements, with existing provision, which provide the opportunity for enhanced provision in modern fit-for-purpose built accommodation.major capital announcements, without existing provision, where there is identified local need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his Department is taking to better facilitate the recognition of privately diagnosed medical conditions as a precursor to receiving a statement of special educational need.
answer_text: The Statutory Assessment and Review Service will in conducting a Statutory Assessment give cognisance to any evidence or reports that a parent provides, as detailed in the current Code of Practice on the Identification and Assessment of Special Educational Needs. Parents may include as part of their advice or representations for the statutory assessment process privately commissioned reports. This is known as parental evidence. In the case of a private Educational Psychology report, this will be shared with the Education Authority’s Psychology Service (EPS) for consideration when submitting their statutory advice. Likewise, a private medical or therapy report can be shared with the statutory advice giver from the Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) for their consideration when producing their statutory advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the funding provided by Sport NI and any other funding bodies to support the building, maintenance, upgrading or renovations of council-run sports facilities in each local council area, including the details of the specific (i) sport; and (ii) facility.
answer_text: Since the Department for Communities was established in 2016, I am aware that in 2020/21 my Department provided capital funding of £135,000 directly to Belfast City Council in respect of its project to develop a new boxing facility for Tullycarnet Boxing Club at Tullycarnet Park, Dundonald, which is managed and maintained by Tullycarnet Action Group Initiative Trust. In addition, I am aware that Sport NI have also funding council-run facilities but owing to the volume of information I have arranged for this to be deposited into the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action her Department is taking to facilitate those on the autism spectrum into employment, recognising that research from Ulster University reveals that those with autism in Northern Ireland experience a greater gap in employment compared to any other group recorded as living with a disability.
answer_text: I fully appreciate that for many in our society, including those with autism, the barriers to employment are significant and varied.  My officials are working closely with both the Department for Communities and the Department of Health on the Disability and Work Strategy and the Autism Strategy. I recognise that many learners with special educational needs, including those who are autistic, face greater barriers at the point of leaving school and transitioning onto appropriate education, training and employment pathways. I am committed to taking action to address this issue and I expect to receive a report shortly from my officials who have been examining post-school provision for young people with special educational needs.  My department’s Careers Service is also developing an interactive technology which can help address the specific issues autistic people face when trying to access careers information and advice. This system will be integrated into the current guidance process to help individuals to articulate their interests and assess career opportunities. We also offer a range of skills provision which include measures to support those who face additional barriers, including autism, as well as financial support for those with a disability (physical, sensory or learning) including autism.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any measures he is taking to ensure that Foras na Gaeilge receives urgent funding so that Gael Linn may continue with the roll-out of the Scoil Spreagtha scheme, recognising his Department's obligation to support the Irish language and that organisers have stated that the scheme will come to an end in June 2025 without an urgent uplift in funding.
answer_text: My department provided around £3.6m in funding to Foras na Gaeilge in 2024 to allow them to meet their statutory responsibilities. I have no plans to reduce this funding in 2025. Decisions around funding priorities, including the level of funding provided to Gael Linn, is an operational matter for Foras na Gaeilge, and their Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the criteria for implementing rumble strips; (ii) the criteria for implementing a speed ramp; and (iii) the number of villages currently awaiting approval of either rumble strips or a speed ramp; and (iv) how far Drumsurn has progressed in this process.
answer_text: Road safety remains an extremely high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.  The policy governing the provision of traffic calming measures, such as rumble strips and speed ramps,  is set out in RSPPG E027 – Road Safety Engineering Procedures: Policy & Procedure Guidance RSPPC E027 - Road Safety Engineering ProceduresI can advise that 30mph road marking roundels have been installed at the existing red surfacing, upgraded signage for Drumsurn village will be installed within the next 6 weeks and, in addition to this, my officials also intend to install rumble strips in advance of the gateway signs.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of incidences recorded in Northern Ireland in relation to (i) revenge porn victims; (ii) childhood sexual abuse images; and (iii) voyeurism in the last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on reported incidences in relation to (i) revenge porn victims; (ii) childhood sexual abuse images; and (iii) voyeurism.  The recording of information in relation to offences reported to police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the investment from her Department for the Revenge Porn Helpline for Northern Ireland in the last five years.
answer_text: The Revenge Porn Helpline is a UK-wide service which supports adult victims of intimate image abuse who live in the UK. It is funded by the Home Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of parents currently being prosecuted due to child school non-attendance in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There are currently 27 parents being prosecuted due to child school non-attendance in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions his Department is taking to work with the Education Authority to ensure that no child who has applied to attend Our Lady of Fatima preschool, Tamnaherin, is left without a place in September, recognising that if placements are not facilitated children are at risk of having nowhere to attend.
answer_text: The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) is responsible for securing non-statutory pre-school education provision in all areas and for ensuring that sufficient pre-school provision is available to accommodate every target age child chose parents want it.  My Department is not involved in this process.The EA has advised that Tamnaherin Community Playgroup have been given a Pupil Allocation Number (PAN) of 10 for the 2025/26 academic year, based on their enrolment in October 2024, but have submitted a request to the EA for this to be increased.  The EA has confirmed that this request was declined as it did not meet any of the criteria for a PAN increase at this stage of the pre-school admissions process, as set out in their letter of 7th February 2025 to all pre-school settings.The pre-school admissions process is preference based and parents are encouraged to list a number of settings they would be content for their child to attend when submitting their pre-school application.  The EA has confirmed that sufficient pre-school provision is available within the area to accommodate every target age child whose parents want a funded pre-school place for the 2025/26 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she plans to review the speed limit on the Somerset Road, Coleraine, from 40mph to 30mph, following the increase of new build houses.
answer_text: I can advise that speed limits are set in line with policy document RSPPG E051 ‘Setting Local Speed Limits in Northern Ireland’ and should be rational, consistent, readily understood and appropriate for the circumstances.  In setting a speed limit, consideration is given to the nature and characteristics of the road, the speed and volume of existing traffic, road traffic collision data and the degree of frontage development.  Somerset Road is partially developed, with housing along the road serviced by spine roads, and limited frontage development. In addition to this, a review of personal injury collisions in the last three years has not highlighted a particular problem on this road. The speed limit for the road is still considered appropriate.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions she has had with the US Consulate on tourists' road safety ahead of The Open at Royal Portrush Golf Club in 2025.
answer_text: I have not had any specific discussions with US Consulate on tourists’ road safety ahead of The Open at Royal Portrush. However, road safety messaging to all international visitors is an important part of visitor servicing. Information about driving around the island of Ireland is available for US visitors on Tourism Ireland’s website. Tourism NI is working with a range or organisations, including the PSNI and DfI, in relation to planning ahead of the hosting The Open Championship. I have asked Tourism NI to ensure that there is engagement with car hire companies promoting road safety messages to all international visitors, as part of the wider visitor servicing plans for The Open.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the grants his Department offers looked after children to further their education after the age of 16.
answer_text: Neither the department nor the Education Authority provide grants to support ‘children looked after’ once they have attained the age of 16.The broader support for ‘children looked after’ would sit with the Department for Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider aligning its provisions with Section 17(6) of the Children Act 1989 to facilitate the provision of services, including accommodation, for children and families in need.
answer_text: Whilst the provisions of Section 17(6) of the Children Act 1989 do not apply to Northern Ireland, currently, under Article 18 of The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (the Order), a Health and Social Care Trust has a duty to provide social care for children in need, their families and others.When commenced, section 121 of the Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 will amend Articles 18(6) and 25 of the Children Order to enable Trusts to provide accommodation to a disabled child who resides normally at home, outside of the looked after children system.  Amendment of Article 18(6) of the Order will allow the Department to prescribe, by way of regulations, other categories of children to whom accommodation may be provided outside of the Looked After Children system, that is, in addition to disabled children.  The power to provide accommodation is in addition to the provision of assistance in kind, or in cash.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the grants her Department offers looked after children to further their education after the age of 16.
answer_text: My Department works closely with other government departments and agencies, including Department of Health and Department of Education, to deliver commitments in the ‘A Life Deserved Strategy’, to improve the education, training and employment outcomes for care leavers. In terms of grants offered, Further education students over the age of 16 and up to 19 have access to a number of student support measures which include Care to Learn childcare, Home to College transport and Free School Meals.  Educational Maintenance Allowance is also available for eligible students.  Post 18, eligible further education students can also access the Further Education Hardship Fund, and post 19 can access Further Education grant support. The Department does not directly allocate any specific grants to ‘looked after children’ students wishing to access Higher Education.  However, Higher Education providers do offer bursaries and non-financial support to widen participation in Higher Education for example both Queen’s University Belfast and Ulster University provide Care Experienced/Care Leavers Bursary of £1,000 per annum.  In addition, Queen’s and the Open University have offered bursary support through Step Up (2023-25) to care experienced learners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any consideration he has given to expanding the categories of children eligible for short-term breaks under Article 18 of the Children Act 1989; and (ii) whether his Department will introduce legislation or regulations to prescribe the inclusion of other categories of children who would benefit from such support.
answer_text: The Children Act 1989 does not extend to Northern Ireland. Northern Ireland’s equivalent legislation is the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (the Children Order). Article 18 of the Children Order places a general duty on an HSC Trust to provide social care for children in need, their families and others. Section 17 of the Children Act 1989 is the broad equivalent of Article 18 of the Children Order, which was brought more into line with Section 17 by way of the Adoption and Children (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 (the Act).The Act received Royal Assent in April 2022. When commenced, section 121 of the Act will amend Articles 18(6) and 25 of the Children Order to enable Trusts to provide accommodation to a disabled child who normally resides at home, outside of the looked after children system. Amendment of Article 18(6) of the Children Order will also allow the Department to prescribe, by way of regulations, categories of children for whom an HSC Trust may provide accommodation outside of the Looked After Children system, that is, in addition to disabled children. To determine which (if any) other categories of children this provision might be extended to in the future, my Department will consult publicly on any proposals.. Section 121 of the Act also amends Article 18 of the Children Order to remove the phrase “exceptional” in relation to cash payments, again aligning Children Order provision more closely with that of the Children Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what steps his Department is taking to support victims of sexual violence during a cervical smear test.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency has advised where an individual has particular needs relating to cervical screening that cannot be met within the GP practice, the GP has the ability to refer that patient to a Trust gynaecology service where additional support and alternative options may be considered. This is delivered on an individual patient basis. All women are offered cervical screening and colposcopy only after full informed consent has been obtained, and these women are treated with the utmost respect and sensitivity.In November 2023, the PHA delivered updated training to GPs and practice nurses who take cervical screening samples (‘smears’). This included a session provided by Jo’s Trust which incorporated dealing sensitively with patients who may find cervical screening distressing including those who are victims of sexual trauma.In addition, my Department, along with the Department of Justice, are joint leads on the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, which was published on 25 September 2024. The Strategy intends to deliver a range of outcomes that are specified against each of its five pillars – Partnership; Prevention; Children and Young People; Support and Provision; and Justice. Oversight of the Strategy is delivered by a Strategy Oversight Board.The supporting three-year action plan seeks to take immediate actions in the short term that are both challenging yet realistic across each of the five pillars.The Support and Provision Pillar, in the Strategy, focuses on ensuring that all victims of domestic and/or sexual abuse can access tailored, responsive and specialised support and provision to recover and rebuild their lives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the potential that psilocybin offers as highlighted by research from Johns Hopkins, whether his Department will consider trialling the administration of psilocybin to patients for depression and addiction.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the totality of evidence in relation to psilocybin as a treatment for depression and addiction, including research from the Johns Hopkins Center for Psychedelic and Consciousness Research.  We would consider support for any proposals from a suitable Principal Investigator to participate in relevant clinical trials, subject to appropriate governance and approvals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that the cost to the health service for medication for just one additional person diagnosed with HIV would core fund three years of prevention, awareness and support work, to detail the rationale for the decision taken in relation to funding for Positive Life NI from the Core Grant Funding Scheme 2025-26.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQW 23256/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to remove the mandatory registration payment of £44 for teachers in Northern Ireland paid to the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: GTCNI staff continue to carry out the important statutory function of registering new teachers entering the profession and maintaining the registration of the existing teaching workforce.This is an important safeguard, helping to protect the quality of the teaching workforce in all our schools; and this critical work will continue to be funded through the charging of the annual registration fee until final decisions have been taken on the future of GTCNI and the required legislative changes can be brought through the Assembly.Professional fees are typically unavoidable within regulated professions and teachers, as highly-skilled professionals, cannot realistically expect to be exempted from this requirement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the percentage of children in care, fostered or adopted from care that have been recorded as having a severe chronic absence rate, in each academic year since 2020.
answer_text: The percentage of children looked after who are recorded with severe chronic absence can be found in the table below.   Severe chronic absence is defined as being absent for 20% or more of the school year. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will consider intervening in the decision to prevent the reinstatement of a crossing patrol guard at DH Christie Memorial Primary School, Coleraine, recognising concerns of members of the public and the school community regarding the dangers of a serious accident.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the Home to School Transport Policy and the Education Authority (EA) for the delivery of school transport which includes the operation of the School Crossing Patrol Scheme. I understand that the EA has reassessed the site at DH Christie Memorial Primary School, Coleraine, and has determined that it meets the criteria for the operation of a school crossing patrol. The principal of the school has been informed and the EA has initiated the recruitment process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant of AQW 22723/22-27, to provide a breakdown of the 12 per cent of ASD and ADHD children recorded as chronically absent in academic year 2023-24, with absence rates by decile.
answer_text: The table below shows the percentage of pupils with ASD or ADHD with chronic absence by absence percentage decile for the 2023/24 academic year. Chronic absence (including severe chronic) is defined as being absent for 10% or more of the school year.% Absence band% of pupils10-19.995920-29.991830-39.99840-49.99450-59.99360-69.99270-79.99280-89.99290+2Source: Annual School Census

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered utilising ketamine therapy for treating depression.
answer_text: Ketamine is a general anaesthetic and short acting analgesic; it is used as a painkiller to reduce complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) and neuralgic pain.  Ketamine is not licensed as a treatment for depression in the UK, but its derivative, esketamine (Spravato®), has been approved for use in treatment-resistant depression.  However, NICE currently does not recommend the use of esketamine (Spravato®) for treatment-resistant depression and has published guidance to this effect (TA854).  On 10th January 2023 my Department endorsed TA854 as applicable in Northern Ireland, therefore esketamine (Spravato®) is not offered as a routinely commissioned treatment for treatment-resistant depression. I am aware of the growing interest in the “off-label” use of ketamine as a treatment for depression, particularly treatment-resistant depression.  The Royal College of Psychiatrists has acknowledged the growing use of ketamine as a treatment for depression, particularly treatment-resistant depression, but emphasises the importance of caution due to limited long-term data.  They state that extensive research is required to understand how to optimally use ketamine for treating depression due to concerns for patient safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when his Department will publish the independent research on intimidation points, referred to in his Ministerial statement on 28 January 2025.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 17325/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to reports that Invest NI has funded companies involved in manufacturing parts used by the Israeli Defence Forces, to detail any measures her Department is taking to ascertain whether public money is being used for this purpose.
answer_text: I have been assured by Invest NI that it does not provide support towards the manufacture of parts used by the Israeli Defence Forces.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the percentage of children classed as chronically absent are diagnosed with ASD or ADHD.
answer_text: For the 2023/24 academic year 12% of pupils with chronic absence have ASD or ADHD recorded by their school in their electronic medical register.  Chronic absence (including severe chronic) is defined as being absent for 10% or more of the school year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of types of systematic phonic systems taught in schools; and (ii) the percentage breakdown each of these systems represent.
answer_text: My Department does not hold data on the phonic systems or schemes taught in individual schools. Through its work in schools, the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) is aware that synthetic and analytical phonics are the two most common types of programmes used.  I have set out the need for a new Literacy Strategy. This new Strategy will be informed by best practice both locally and internationally and will aim to provide teachers with clear guidelines and appropriate resources and training to support  effective literacy teaching.  As part of this work there is a need to better understand current practice in schools and officials will be surveying schools to gather information on specific programmes and their use.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any measures her Department is taking in 2025 to ensure that vulnerable adults are not homeless when released from prison.
answer_text: The Department for Communities and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) have sole responsibility for assessing and meeting housing needs and working with communities to develop programmes to meet those needs. However, in recognition of the particular needs of people leaving prison custody, the Northern Ireland Prison Service is progressing a range of initiatives to mitigate against the risk of homelessness for those leaving prison.  For 2025 these include:To continue to undertake an assessment of prisoner needs upon committal, which includes their housing arrangements, and enables staff to put suitable interventions in place, such as support from the Housing Rights advocacy service;To carry out an evaluation of a pilot initiative which introduced NIHE Housing Advisors into Maghaberry Prison in April 2024.  This initiative provides early intervention on housing needs and allows decisions on accommodation to take cognisance of other resettlement factors including health and employability.  Subject to a decision by NIHE it is hoped to expand the initiative into Magilligan Prison and Hydebank Wood College;To continue to use ‘Approved Premises’ to enable appropriate support and supervision arrangements to be put in place for high-risk offenders who are subject to PPANI requirements; To engage with the Belfast City Council led Complex Lives multi-disciplinary team (including Extern, NIHE and Belfast Trust) to identify how better collaboration could support female prisoners who are known to the programme that come into contact with the prison system and most at risk of homelessness; andTo complete a review of the Prisoner Development Model for rehabilitation to ensure that processes and provision meet the rehabilitation and resettlement needs of those in our care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when his Department will allocate the funding required to allow two days a week release time for nursery school principals that teach in order to focus on planning, administrative and leadership duties, as is afforded to their colleagues in the primary school sector.
answer_text: I recognise the valuable work carried out by Principals in all education sectors, including the nursery sector, to support young people across Northern Ireland.  While nursery schools do not currently receive a specific allocation for principal release time (a Nursery Principal Release Factor), they receive additional funding which can be used to facilitate release time.  Nursery schools currently receive a higher level of ‘per pupil’ funding than primary schools as a result of the higher Age Weighted Pupil Unit[1] (AWPU) weighting factor.  The additional funding provides flexibility for nursery schools to use as required.  This can include engaging supply teacher cover to release time for the principal to carry out administrative duties if they wish.  The teaching day in a nursery school (whether full time or part time) is also shorter than in a primary school, which may also provide an element of ‘release time’ for principals in these schools that would not be available to principals in other schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 21649/22-27, given that an estimated 60 per cent of septic discharge is reaching Lough Neagh, whether he will commit to advancing measures to merge water discharge consent applications with the planning process.
answer_text: The issue of discharges into Lough Neagh is an environmental concern and integrating water discharge consent applications with the planning process may help address this. A more streamlined approach could ensure that environmental impacts are considered at the earliest stages of development, potentially reducing the risk of pollution. However, such a merger would need to be carefully designed to balance efficiency with robust environmental safeguards.While, as I advised in my response to AQW 21649/22-27, there have been no review or policy changes initiated or currently planned in this area, my Department remains committed to improving water quality and is open to exploring all viable options, including potential legislative or procedural changes, to enhance regulatory oversight. Any amendments would need to align with environmental protection legislation and ensure that planning legislation and the legislation governing water discharge consents work in harmony. Collaboration with key stakeholders with responsibility for various aspects of planning, including the Department for Infrastructure and local authorities, and with environmental bodies, would also be essential. I will continue to assess policy improvements that strengthen water quality protections while supporting sustainable development.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is exploring the use of GLP-1 medications to help address addiction to alcohol.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals and Clinical Guidelines are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and were found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. Under this formal link, in April 2023 my Department endorsed NICE Technology Appraisal TA875, which recommends semaglutide (Wegovy®), a Glucogen-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonist (GLP-1 RA), as an option for weight management, including weight loss and weight maintenance, alongside a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity in adults who meet the criteria outlined in the guidance, and my Department has endorsed NICE National Guidelines NG28, which recommends the use of semaglutide for the treatment of type-2 diabetes. There are currently no formal endorsements for the use of semaglutide products for the treatment of alcohol addiction.Advice issued by my Department to healthcare professionals is clear that these products should only be prescribed for their licensed indications and should not routinely be prescribed for obesity on an off-label basis, in order to support maintenance of supplies for patients with type 2 diabetes.My Department expects all providers of healthcare services, whether HSC or private, and all those with responsibility for prescribing to take appropriate account of national guidance such as Medicine Supply Notifications and National Patient Safety Alerts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the development of a targeted business support scheme for the childcare sector.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of homes, since the open market review of Project Stratum, that have contacted his Department with the inability to access broadband services.
answer_text: Since the Open Market Review of Project Stratum in 2018, my Department has been contacted on 5,150 occasions, regarding the inability of premises to access broadband services of at least 30 Megabits per second, the threshold which Project Stratum is designed to address.  This contact includes domestic and commercial premises, by both residents and their elected representatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of court cases in Northern Ireland that have resulted in the conviction of a British Army soldier for sexual assault in the last ten years.
answer_text: Convictions datasets held by the Department do not contain details on the occupation of those convicted of sexual assault, or of any offence.  Therefore it is not possible, from information held, to provide the information requested in the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any schemes available to support homes that are unable to access broadband as they were built after the open market review for Project Stratum.
answer_text: I am mindful of the importance people place on access to good broadband services. Project Gigabit is expected to benefit premises that are unable to access gigabit-capable broadband services, and are not part of suppliers’ commercial deployment plans.My Department expects to award a contract for Project Gigabit in the coming months, subject to compliant bids being made, bids meeting the published criteria, and all necessary approvals being agreed. A local area review process has been created to assess each Project Gigabit contract once it is in life, and is designed to ensure the intervention areas are as up to date as possible, and will include a review of data on new build premises. This will inform decisions on the best intervention options to address remaining gaps in coverage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of Primary 1 pupils that have been diagnosed with special educational needs and disabilities in each of the last five academic years; and (ii) the percentage this represents.
answer_text: Please see below a table showing the number of Primary 1 age children over the last five years with identified special educational needs (SEN) and who are at stage 3 of the SEN Framework, that is with a statement of SEN.Schools will hold information on their SEN registers of the number of children in primary 1 who have been identified as having SEN but their needs are being addressed at school based stages of the SEN Framework.Academic YearNumber of Primary 1 Statements Issued% of Overall Statements Issued2019-2047315.32020-2150314.32021-2251812.42022-2358112.62023-2454411.9The table below shows the number and percentage of pupils in year 1 enrolled in primary schools with SEN.Academic YearNumber of Year 1 pupils with SEN% of Year 1 pupils with SEN2019/202,0138.3%2020/211,9498.0%2021/221,9008.1%2022/232,0588.9%2023/242,1979.7%Source: Annual School Census (Includes SEN stages 1-3)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any new steps his Department will take in 2025 to ensure that all children in Irish-medium schools are able to access special educational needs and disabilities services and professionals, including speech and language therapists, educational psychologists, dyslexia support, through the Irish language.
answer_text: In recognition of the specific support needs of children with special educational needs (SEN) in the Irish-medium education (IME) sector, my SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan includes an action to develop, where appropriate, bespoke services to better support children in the IME sector.Delivery of this action has commenced with the establishment of a working group, involving the department, the Education Authority (EA) and Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) to work through potential solutions to any identified gaps in provision for children with SEN in the IME sector. The provision of Speech and Language Therapy (SLT) services is a matter for the Department of Health (DoH).  My officials continue to work closely with DoH and the Public Health Agency (PHA) to meet the needs of children and young people with SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider expanding the new International Ministerial Advisory Panel on curriculum review to include a member with expertise in Irish language education, recognising the unique challenges face by the Irish-medium sector and the need for specific expertise in this area.
answer_text: The International Ministerial Advisory Panel already includes a member with significant expertise in Irish medium education.  Dr Harold Hislop is currently Adjunct Professor in the School of Policy and Practice at Dublin City University and is the former Chief Inspector in the Republic of Ireland.  Dr Hislop, a fluent Irish speaker, has significant experience of Irish-medium education, including the specific challenges that it faces, having both taught Irish for a number of years as well as serving as an inspector of Irish-medium and English-medium schools. Dr Hislop’s most intensive engagement with the Irish-medium education sector occurred in the period 2015-2018, when  he had lead responsibility for Irish-language policy work in the Republic of Ireland. During that time, he led the development of the Republic of Ireland's first Gaeltacht Education Policy 2017-2022 as well as overseeing the establishment of the Gaeltacht Education Unit which drove implementation of the policy. The policy was informed by intensive consultations with teachers, parents, students in Gaeltacht schools, with Irish-language organisations and organisations supporting Irish-medium education. Dr Hislop led those consultations on behalf of the Department and subsequently chaired the Gaeltacht Education Advisory Group - the stakeholder forum that facilitated ongoing consultation to support the implementation of the Policy.  Dr Hislop will bring this considerable wealth of expertise and experience to the work of the International Ministerial Advisory Panel as it considers strategic issues relating to curriculum and assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what steps his Department has taken to ensure government departments are complying with the Managing Public Money Northern Ireland document.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for setting the ground rules for the administration of public money and we are accountable to this Assembly for doing so. Managing Public Money is our key guidance document for setting those ground rules, and it essentially sets out the framework for the use of public resources and is the cornerstone of our guidance for departments, agencies and Arm’s Length Bodies.It is however the responsibility for the heads of departments, i.e. the Permanent Secretaries who are designated as Accounting Officers, to ensure that they control and account for their own department’s business and use of resources allocated to them. This long-standing convention is the underpinning foundation to ensure compliance with the guidance. On appointment it is the Accounting Officers who are designated to take personal responsibility for following the guidance and being responsible for safeguarding public funds in their charge. My Department do of course work alongside the audit authorities and Accounting Officers through the public audit process in the scrutiny of public expenditure and compliance with the guidance contained in Managing Public Money.My officials recently completed a comprehensive review of Managing Public Money and the refreshed guidance was published in November 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why winter service drivers of gritting lorries have been excluded from an on-call allowance.
answer_text: Winter Service drivers are paid availability allowance for making themselves available for out of hours actions, rather than the NICS on-call allowance.  Recent changes to NICS on-call allowances did not include the availability allowance and did not therefore change the amount Winter Service drivers are paid.The Department of Finance has committed to considering an amendment to the availability allowance this year following on from the current pay negotiations.  This will include looking at the potential harmonisation of these rates going forward.The setting of these rates is not within the remit of my Department, but I will implement the outcome of these negotiations as and when required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the relevant training received by (i) judges; (ii) barristers; and (iii) social workers in relation to domestic abuse.
answer_text: I consider it essential that frontline professionals have an appropriate awareness and understanding of domestic abuse in all its forms, including coercive control, and how to recognise it.  This applies across the criminal justice system as a whole, as well as more widely, in terms of those who have a role to play in ensuring that offenders are brought to justice and that we can better support victims through the criminal justice system.  As you will be aware, the judiciary are independent, including with respect to training. I am bound to respect that independence. The Lady Chief Justice has statutory responsibility for the provision of training for the independent judiciary. Whilst delivery of such training is a matter for the Judicial Studies Board, which is independent of the Department of Justice, we are committed to sharing training and awareness-raising resources that have been developed by and for our justice partners, for information, with the Board, where that is appropriate and helpful.In terms of training for barristers on domestic abuse, my Department has no statutory role in the regulation of training of the legal profession.  My Department has, however, provided the Bar of NI with access to the Domestic Abuse eLearning “Context and Change” package which was added to their CPD portal, to coincide with the introduction of the new domestic abuse offence and associated aggravators. In addition, the Bar Library organises continuing professional development training events on the subject matter and regularly advertises training events organised by the Belfast Area Domestic and Sexual Violence Abuse Partnership.Social Workers employed in Health and Social Care Trusts in Northern Ireland are also encouraged to complete the Domestic Abuse e-learning package through the LearnHSCNI e-learning platform. In addition, each Trusts offer specific training on domestic abuse and coercive control. Domestic abuse and coercive control are also included in other training programmes including mandatory training programmes such as Safeguarding children and adults. My Department is required to publish an annual training statement, setting out information about the level of training undertaken by staff in criminal justice system organisations in relation to the domestic abuse offence, which captures patterns of coercive control, as well as physical or sexual violence and threats.  The first two annual training statements are available at: Training Statement for the Domestic Abuse Offence | Department of Justice (justice-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rights a pupil has to obtain a copy of their GCSE and A level exam papers post-exam.
answer_text: The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations & Assessment (CCEA) provide a Post Results Service after each GCSE and A level examination series, which allows candidates to request access to their examination script(s) via their centre i.e. school. CCEA provides access to these services in accordance with the Joint Council for Qualifications (JCQ) published guidance for Post Results Services – see https://www.jcq.org.uk/exams-office/post-results-services/ In line with the JCQ Post-Results Services, CCEA offers all candidates the option to request their examination script for the purpose of:reviews of marking; and/orteaching and learning.CCEA guidance to centres, parents and candidates on how to access scripts and any associated charges or deadlines are available on the CCEA website using the following link: https://ccea.org.uk/examiner-centre-support/post-results-support

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures his Department is taking to ensure that (i) fewer people lose power; and (ii) essential services are maintained for future storms, recognising that rural areas are disproportionately impacted by extreme weather events.
answer_text: Storm Éowyn was an unprecedented event, leaving almost one third of people (283,000 homes and businesses) without power. There were faults in over 5,000 locations, with each of these requiring engineers to physically attend and repair. Multi agency co-ordination was stood up before, during and after the storm via Civil Contingencies mechanisms and NIE Networks should be praised for its tireless work throughout the event.NIE Networks has a systematic process for preparing for major weather events, for managing them, and for reviewing them after the event occurs. My Department will liaise with NIE Networks after it has conducted its post major incident review for Storm Éowyn, and will respond to any learnings or requests arising from that review.Previous lessons learned from internal reviews of NIE Networks response to Storm Isha (January 2024) and Storm Darragh (December 2024) also helped to inform NIE Networks emergency response to Storm Éowyn.There is also recognition that climate change will bring more frequent intense weather events.  NIE Networks recognises this and has included plans for increased “weatherising” of the electricity network in the North in its RP7 business plan, which proposes an expenditure of £2.23B on operation, maintenance and development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to children who have been left without power as a result of Storm Éowyn, to detail any communication his Department has had with the Education Authority to discuss (i) providing hot food; (ii) help in providing shower facilities; and (iii) a relaxation of uniform regulations, given that uniforms may not be able to be washed.
answer_text: My Department and the Education Authority have focused on minimising any disruption on pupils’ ability to access learning and associated educational services such as school meals and home to school transport following Storm Éowyn and have not been involved in the provision of the wider governmental support mechanisms available to families impacted by the Storm. This includes the resumption of the EA school meals service when schools reopened on Monday 27 January.The school uniform policy operated in each school is a matter for each school's Principal to determine. Any relaxation of a school's uniform policy to take account of the impact of a loss of power on families' ability to wash their uniform would have been a decision taken at individual school level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will consider integrating simultaneously discharge consent applications alongside planning applications, to help streamline planning and water quality considerations.
answer_text: My Department acknowledges the potential benefits of integrating discharge consent applications with planning applications to improve efficiency and coordination. Discharge consents and planning applications are currently assessed separately due to their distinct legal and technical requirements. Whilst greater alignment could streamline processes, full integration would require careful consideration of statutory obligations, regulatory scrutiny, and environmental protections.At this time, there have been no review or policy changes initiated in this area. However, my Department remains open to exploring ways to improve coordination between planning authorities and environmental regulators. Any future changes would require consultation with stakeholders to ensure an effective balance between planning efficiency and water quality protections.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how removing the availability of intimidation points (i) helps the housing crisis; and (ii) helps those vulnerable to violence and paramilitary activity.
answer_text: With the removal of intimidation points, the Housing Selection Scheme will award points within three sections: insecurity of tenure (homelessness); housing conditions; and health/social wellbeing assessment. This will ensure that applicants in greatest need are prioritised for an allocation of housing; andthe points for violence or threat of violence in the scheme will not exclude any victim of abuse, violence or trauma. This will level the playing field for all, including those vulnerable to violence and paramilitary activity, regardless of the cause.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following the report of degrading hazing practices, to detail any new measures his Department will take in 2025 to tackle bullying in schools.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (“the Act”) states that the Board of Governors (BoG) must determine the measures to be taken at the school with a view to preventing bullying involving a registered pupil at the school. This includes while the pupil is in the lawful control or charge of a member of the staff of the school; or while the pupil is receiving educational provision arranged on behalf of the school and provided elsewhere than on the premises of the school. The Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) within the EA provides support for schools, educational settings, young people and parents to help them understand the requirements of the Act. ABSIT has a focus on preventative education to embed cultures that eradicate bullying. The team also provides children, young people and parents/carers, with support to deal with specific incidents of bullying. While the EA do not offer any specific service or support directly regarding hazing, and ABSIT do not refer to “hazing” specifically in guidance and advice, behaviours such as this would already be addressed through the wider definitions of bullying. Schools are required to ensure the implementation of child centred systems and processes to engender a supportive pastoral curriculum and to support this ABSIT advise schools that all incidents of bullying type behaviour must be shared with BoG for the purposes of identifying patterns and trends. A school’s BoG has the statutory remit to oversee the implementation of the Act in their setting, within the context of the pastoral suite of Positive Behaviour, Safeguarding and other associated policies. In this context, the Senior Leadership Team member with pastoral responsibilities determine which policies are appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department intends to review its current policies and procedures relating to public access to green space on its estate, outside of Forest Service land, to ensure that Northern Ireland improves its position as the region with the poorest public access to green space in Europe.
answer_text: The Outdoor Recreation Action Plan is currently being reviewed with a view to it being updated.  This will help to identify future delivery needs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department intends to carry out an assessment of the impact and effectiveness of its policies and practices relating to public access to designated nature conservation sites, compared to other jurisdictions in the UK, Ireland and Europe.
answer_text: My Department does not have any plans to undertake this assessment. Public assess to designated sites should continue to be considered in accordance with existing nature conservation legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will lead a cross-departmental review of all publicly-owned land with a view to creating a policy position on a statutory right of responsible access on public land, unless the relevant Department, Agency or Local Authority can prove it is in the public interest that the public be denied access.
answer_text: The Outdoor Recreation Action Plan is currently being reviewed with a view to it being updated.  This will help to identify future delivery needs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how his Department will ensure that its target to annually increase the population that is within a five minute, or 400 metre, walk of a quality green/blue space greater than two hectares will not disproportionately favour urban areas, when greatest need is in rural areas as outlined in his Department’s People in the Outdoors Monitor NI.
answer_text: My Department recognises the need for publicly accessible, quality natural space outdoors in both urban and rural areas. The Greenspace NI map can be used to assess access in urban and rural areas over time.The primary responsibility for the provision of outdoor recreation lies with local authorities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has an inventory of its estate, outside of Forest Service land, which can contribute to his Department’s target to annually increase the population that is within a five minute, or 400 metre, walk of a quality green/blue space greater than two hectares.
answer_text: The Department has an inventory of its estate, but this has not yet been analysed against this new target in the Environmental Improvement Plan.Much of the land owned by DAERA, which currently has accessible blue or green space, can be found on the Greenspace NI map: GreenspaceNI Public Dashboard.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any discussions he has had with the Minister for the Economy on uplifting the Education Maintenance Allowance payment for students in Northern Ireland; and (ii) the timeline for payments to rise.
