answer_text: The Minister for the Economy and I have not had any discussions on uplifting the Education Maintenance Allowance (EMA) payment for students in Northern Ireland. I recognise that the Education Maintenance Allowance (EMA) provides valuable support to enable young people to remain in education or training and my officials will continue to discuss support for these young people with DfE officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the recorded rate of chronic absence in (i) primary schools; and (ii) post-primary schools, in the 2023-24 academic year.
answer_text: The Chronic absence rate (including severe chronic) is defined as the percentage of individual pupils that are absent for 10% or more of the school year. The department receives monthly management information from the Education Authority to monitor such absence trends. This information can be found in the table below and shows the percentage of pupils with ten percent or more of half day sessions recorded as absent.2023/24Primary23%Post primary34%Source: EA SIMS

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether any measures to reduce the number of children living in poverty will be included in the final Programme for Government 2024-2027.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the date by which his Department plans to bring in the compulsory age of education to 18.
answer_text: I have no plans to raise the compulsory school age to 18.  To do so would require all young people to remain in school and prevent them from choosing other pathways at post-16 which may be more suitable to their age, ability and aptitude.In my statement to the Northern Ireland Assembly on 21 October 2024, I advised that I wanted to see young people continue in education or training (including apprenticeships).  Specifically, by mandating education or training until 18, I want to create a minimum offer for all young people, particularly our most vulnerable to ensure that they have the opportunity to explore a structured pathway of support and guidance which is suitable for their needs.I have therefore instructed officials to bring forward proposals for legislation to extend the age of participation in education or training which, with the support of the Executive and Assembly, I want enacted in the current Assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any communication his Department has had with counterparts in the Republic of Ireland following the reported cases of Avian Influenza in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My officials meet regularly with their counterparts in the Republic of Ireland regarding the risk of epizootic disease incursions.  In recent weeks, in response to the Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI) positive wild bird findings across Ireland, they have met specifically to discuss both this particular issue and the steps that could be taken to mitigate the risk of a HPAI incursion in poultry on the island of Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether funding from the Active Travel Scheme can be used to install electric vehicle charge points at schools engaged in the Active School Travel Programme; and (ii) whether consideration has been given to integrating electric vehicle infrastructure within active travel initiatives to support sustainable transport.
answer_text: Active travel can be defined as travelling by physically active means such as walking, wheeling, or cycling. As such, it is an umbrella term for cycling and all forms of pedestrian activity, including walking or the use of mobility aids such as wheelchairs. It does not extend to other forms of sustainable transport such as public transport, or funding for electric vehicle charging facilities.The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle (EV) charge points is a commercially led operation. Decisions regarding the installation maintenance, development and utilisation of those charge points rests solely with over 20 Charge Point Operators (CPOs). My Officials continue to engage with officials from the British Government’s Office for Zero Emission Vehicles on the availability of grants, including the Workplace Charging Scheme which includes state-funded education institutions.  This grant can provide 75% off the cost to buy and install charge points up to a maximum of £2,500 per socket with up to 40 sockets across all sites. This grant is available to 31 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether steps will be taken to end the practice of translating exam scripts from Irish into English for marking by English-medium examiners in CCEA; and (ii) whether increased monetary compensation will be considered to encourage Irish-medium practitioners to participate and to attract examiners from the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQW 6985/22-27. The response noted that the practice of translating scripts is necessary as CCEA continues to face significant challenges trying to recruit Irish speaking examiners due to the limited pool of Irish speaking teachers in each specialist subject. This situation has not changed.Marking and awarding qualifications is a matter for the relevant awarding organisation in line with the appropriate regulatory framework. As previously advised, CCEA continues to make every effort to recruit sufficient examiners including through digital marketing recruitment campaigns and broadening the recruitment criteria to try to expand the pool of Irish speakers who may apply to be examiners.  CCEA has also increased the fees paid to all examiners and moderators, so that the positions continue to remain attractive, and these fees are constantly kept under review.  This was funded through increasing the examination entry fees paid by schools.CCEA has explored recruitment opportunities in the Republic of Ireland, but remuneration rates offered for example by the State Examinations Commission, are much more competitive than those offered by CCEA.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any measures his Department has considered to prevent rent rises in light of reports that rent in Northern Ireland has risen 9 per cent in 2024, despite earnings increasing on average by just 1.2 per cent.
answer_text: As mandated by the Assembly, my Department considered the potential impacts of a rent cut and rent freeze. The research commissioned by my Department clearly outlined the negative impacts of these types of rent control.  In particular, the research report highlighted that a large proportion of landlords may exit the private rental market, and this would result in a greater shortage of available private rented properties and therefore a rapid increase in rents in an already stretched market.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children suffering from Anxiety Based School Avoidance in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on pupils who are suffering from Anxiety Based School Avoidance. My officials are working with the Education Authority to ensure that the new school administration system, “Education Information Solutions” (EdiS), is able to facilitate the recording of absences at a more granular level. That work is continuing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any discussions she has had with the British Government on Artificial Intelligence regulation.
answer_text: I have not had any discussions with the UK Government about Artificial Intelligence regulation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions he has had with the British Government on Artificial Intelligence regulation.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence regulation featured as part of my discussions around the AI Opportunities Action Plan with the British government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail why every school in Derry city was denied capital funding in the last ten years, despite the Department finding £475 million for major capital projects elsewhere between 2014-15 and 2023-24.
answer_text: The current Major Capital Programme comprises schools announced for funding following assessment against pre-published protocols.  Schools are nominated for funding by their managing authorities, or in the case of voluntary grammars by self-nomination.   Protocols since 2012 have placed an emphasis on establishing which schools are most in need of capital investment, primarily due to their existing accommodation condition and suitability, regardless of geographical area. Since 2012, there have been six projects in Derry/Londonderry City and immediate surrounding area, announced under my Department’s Major Capital Works Programme.Three have been completed, at a total capital cost of £46m million.  They are:- Eglinton Primary SchoolFoyle College/Ebrington Primary School and Our Lady of Fatima Primary School (in the Derry/Londonderry City Area) The construction of a new school for Ardnashee School and College is well advanced and is due to complete in February 2025. The total capital cost of the build is estimated at £42m. In April 2024 I also announced capital planning would begin for a second new build campus for Ardnashee. A further two schools, Lumen Christi College and St Brigid’s College, were announced under the 2022 Major Capital Works Programme.  Unfortunately, these, along with nineteen other projects, are currently on hold as a result of ongoing budget pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he has given to ensuring sufficient ambulance availability in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, as part of proposals to move emergency general surgery from Causeway Hospital to Antrim Area Hospital.
answer_text: The Northern HSC Trust held a public consultation on proposals to reconfigure general surgery services between 23 August and 29 November 2024.As part of this process the Northern HSC Trust have engaged with NIAS and further engagement is planned to model ambulance capacity required for any potential change. The Trust is currently preparing a consultation outcomes report which will be submitted to the Department of Health in due course. I will consider any recommendation made by the Northern HSC Trust at this time. Additionally, paramedicine in the UK has undergone a significant evolution, transitioning from vocational roles to highly professionalised, academically grounded professions requiring a BSc (Hons) in Paramedic Science in order to meet the increased educational requirements for Health and Care Professions Council (HCPC) registration?In support of this change my Department has, from 2021, commissioned 50 places per year in the BSc programme in Paramedic Science provided locally by the Ulster University. Prior to this, people from Northern Ireland who wanted to study for the degree would have had to travel outside Northern Ireland, so this is a very positive development for those people wanting to pursue a career as a paramedic and for NIAS.The first cohort of newly qualified Paramedics have now graduated from the Degree programme and my Department has provided funding for NIAS to employ the 41 newly qualified paramedics and I am pleased to say that they have recently taken up positions with the Trust.NIAS also plan to develop a structured Advanced Paramedic development programme to support paramedics in gaining the necessary skills for these expanded roles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is considering introducing a safe consumption facility for drug users, similar to what has recently been opened in Glasgow.
answer_text: My Department leads on the Executive’s substance use strategy Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery which recognises the need to target those most at risk, including those who are homeless, people who inject drugs and those who are in contact with the justice system. This strategy aims to directly reduce the harm for these groups through actions that place emphasis on harm reduction support measures for people who use alcohol and other drugs. There are no proposals in the strategy on Safer Drug Consumption Facilities. The issue of criminal sanctions for those found in possession of drugs is an issue that is frequently raised with this type of facility, hence any such intervention would also require a wider assessment of the legal position and be cross-cutting in nature. However, I am aware of the pilot sites that have just opened in Glasgow and Dublin, and my Department will closely watch the development and evaluation of these pilots to assess their potential effectiveness.  In the meantime, it is important to note that there is currently a full range of services available for those who require help and support with substance use issues, and for their families. A directory of services available in each Trust area across Northern Ireland is available online at: https://drugsandalcoholni.info/self-help-resources/.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that all Health and Social Care Trusts are able to endow Direct Payment carers with the authority to undertake percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy insertion procedures, recognising the current geographical inequality in this regard across the Health and Social Care Trust regions.
answer_text: All children on the PEG list are managed by the BHSCT so there is only one list across NI.This is a complex and challenging procedure that currently requires intensive pre and post-operative care.  All surgeries are currently carried out at the RBHSC.  However, the Trust is examining other options in order to try and mitigate the current waiting lists.BHSCT directly train parents in the care of these PEG devices.  Where Direct Payments are being used; as the parents are employing carers directly they would train the carers in the use of the devices.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to reduce the speed limit for Clooney Road, Ballykelly, from 50mph to 30mph, recognising that the current speed limit regularly leaves residents with a long wait before they can safely enter and exit the road during peak hours.
answer_text: I can advise that officials from my Department will visit the location and review the existing speed limits in Ballykelly following completion of the ongoing Active Travel scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any assessment his Department has made of civic, social and play needs of residents in Ballykelly; and (ii) whether there are any plans to build a playpark in Ballykelly in 2025.
answer_text: My Department has not made any assessment of civic, social and play needs of residents in Ballykelly. Playparks typically sit within the remit of local councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has any plans to scrap the stipulation requiring an artist to wait three years before they can reapply for the Support for the Individual Artist Programme if they are already in receipt of the award in the last three years.
answer_text: The Support for the Individual Artist Programme (SIAP) is delivered by the Arts Council of Northern Ireland.  SIAP covers a range of 8 schemes, each with their own eligibility criteria. Calls for applications for all of the schemes tend to be heavily over-subscribed in relation to the available budget.In the 2024/25 round of the SIAP programme, the Arts Council reduced the frequency with which successful awardees could re-apply to two of the eight schemes: Project Funding for Individuals, and the Artists International Development Fund. This was done with the aim of widening the opportunities for those artists who have not yet received support from the most applied to funding schemes.  It is important to note that this only relates to applying to the same scheme again: awardees of one scheme can still apply to any of the other SIAP schemes for which they meet the eligibility criteria. The Arts Council has no plans to change the application eligibility rules at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has considered changing laws in relation to parental access of children born as a result of rape.
answer_text: As the Department of Finance (DoF) has responsibility for the substantive law relating to private family law matters, including the power of a court to make a contact order with respect to a child, it would be for that Department to bring forward any amendments to the relevant legislation, the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995, in relation to this matter. I am advised that there are currently no plans to do so but DoF is keeping the matter under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the current rights of convicted rapists to access their child who was conceived through rape.
answer_text: The Department of Finance has responsibility for the substantive law relating to private family law matters, including the power of a court to make a contact order with respect to a child. However, I understand that where a child has been conceived through rape, the rapist would have no automatic right to contact with the child, rather would have to apply to a court for a contact order. While the determination of any application for contact is, of course, a judicial matter, the court is required by the legislation, the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995, to consider the child’s welfare as its paramount consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the support available for (i) children who were conceived through rape; and (ii) mothers of children who were conceived through rape.
answer_text: There is a range of support interventions available across Government in response to the impact of crime, including rape.  These include the provision of health, social care and economic supports, as appropriate, in response to the specific needs of the individual.With regards my own Department, it is responsible for ensuring that victims of crime are supported through their engagement with the criminal justice system. To this end, my Department makes provision for a range of supports which victims of rape within the criminal justice system are entitled to. This includes access to:free, independent legal advice through the Sexual Offences Legal Advisers (SOLAs). An equivalent scheme for children and young people who are victims of serious sexual offences will launch in February 2025; the ASSIST NI advocacy service, available to qualifying victims of domestic and sexual abuse engaging with the criminal justice system; a range of services provided by Victim Support NI and NSPCC Young Witness Service, funded by my Department, to help victims of crime, including victims of rape, by providing emotional support, information and practical help through their compensation, community, child advocacy and witness services. Victim Support NI can also provide services to those who do not wish to report the crime, and can refer victims to specialist support services, where appropriate and available.In addition to these support services, my Department has also introduced a range of measures intended to reduce any retraumatisation that victims of crime, including victims of rape and other serious sexual offences, may experience as a result of their engagement with criminal proceedings.  These include:the introduction of Remote Evidence Centres in Belfast and Craigavon to provide a safe and comfortable space away from the court building where vulnerable victims and witnesses can give their best evidence without the threat of encountering the defendant or their supporters; andnew legislative changes which collectively improve the experience of victims by: removing the requirement for them to give oral evidence at the committal hearing; excluding the public from court in serious sexual offence trials; and providing lifelong anonymity for victims of serious sexual offences until 25 years after death.in addition, a judge-led voluntary protocol is in place which expedites serious sexual offence cases involving child witnesses under 13 years of age. An evaluation found that for cases expedited under the protocol there was a 70% reduction in the time taken from report to disposal. Other available supports to children who were conceived through rape; or to the mothers of children who were conceived through rape are the responsibility of other Executive Departments to deliver.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any additional funding will be provided to GPs to assist with non-English speaking patients.
answer_text: Following the announcement of the 2025-26 Draft Budget allocation, my Department has assessed that health will be facing a significant funding gap in 2025-26 as a result of both inflationary pressures and increased demand. These funding pressures will need to be considered alongside other priorities.The Business Services Organisation (BSO) Interpreting Service is commissioned by my Department to provide foreign language interpreting support to Health and Social Care (HSC) providers in Northern Ireland (NI) to include GP Practices.  The main aim of the service is to improve access to HSC Services for Patients who do not speak English proficiently. This is demand-led and requests vary according to the needs of Patients/Service Users.In addition to the HSC Regional Interpreting Service, the Bigword (telephone foreign language interpreting) is also available. Commissioned by SPPG, the Bigword Telephone Interpreting enables GP Practices to effectively communicate with their Patients who do not speak English proficiently through a fully trained interpreter, over the phone.Despite the continuing challenging budgetary position, the Department remains committed to supporting General Practice so it can continue to provide vital services to our communities both now, and in the future.Significant funding has made available by my Department to enable to meet the growing demands for primary care services and enable practices to improve their telephony systems, improve accessibility and increase staffing levels to ensure telephony demand is managed effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has had discussions with Ulster University following the amending of GAA imagery from the Sports and Exercise Sciences BSc Hons course advertisements.
answer_text: The marketing of courses is entirely an issue for the University, which is an autonomous institution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the use of child development passports will be extended to (i) child carers; (ii) children who experience addiction in the home; and (iii) children who experience domestic violence in the home.
answer_text: The introduction of a child development passport is an action within my Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Delivery Plan.The focus will initially be on children with SEN; however, there may be scope to consider other groups of children in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has explored the use of Applied Behavioural Analysis for children with autism.
answer_text: Responsibility for legislation, strategy and policy in relation to Autism sits with the Department of Health (DoH).  The provision of supports for autistic children and young people, their families, teachers and support staff in our schools are operational matters for the Education Authority (EA) and Middletown Centre for Autism.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement he has had with the Vice-Chancellor of Ulster University in relation to the future of the Riverside Theatre, Coleraine.
answer_text: I have not had any engagement with the Vice-Chancellor of Ulster University regarding the Riverside Theatre.My Department maintains an ongoing dialogue with the University and is open to discussion of any issue affecting the institution and its campuses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why he has denied Transformation to Integrated status for Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College and Rathmore Primary School, despite the recommendation for this by his Department's officials.
answer_text: The recommendations presented to me by officials in respect of both of these Development Proposals (DP) noted the “on balance” nature of the decisions I was being invited to take.  Ultimately it is my responsibility to decide on proposals and in doing so there may be occasion where my decisions do not follow the recommendations made.  For both of these proposals I have set out in detail the rationale for my decisions, accessible here:  Development Proposal Decisions 2025 | Department of Education

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether traffic lights may be implemented at the Greenhall Highway and Carthall Road junction, Coleraine, under the guidelines of active travel.
answer_text: The provision of traffic signals at this location as part of the Local Transport and Safety Measures (LTSM) programme has been considered by my department however it would not meet the criteria to be prioritised for development of a scheme at this time.Future delivery of significant active travel improvements will be based on the Active Travel Delivery Plan. This Plan sets out proposals to prioritise and deliver high quality active travel infrastructure for shorter everyday journeys within our larger towns and cities, including Coleraine.The route along the Greenhall Highway/Wheatsheaf Road has not been identified as a priority or future route in the draft Active Travel Delivery Plan however the public consultation for the draft Plan runs until 28 February 2025 and I would encourage all those with an interest in active travel, or transport in general to engage with the consultation to help shape the plans for the Coleraine area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has plans to introduce medical retirement for those serving in the Fire and Rescue Service on grounds of mental ill-health.
answer_text: The Firefighters’ Pension Scheme Regulations (NI) 2015 and the Northern Ireland Fire & Rescue Service ill-health retirement process provide for employees to medically retire for a number of reasons which includes mental health related illnesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department plans to take to introduce the Pharmacy First scheme, similar to the scheme in place in England.
answer_text: I am happy to inform the member that the Pharmacy First service for Everyday Health Conditions has been operating in Northern Ireland since March 2022, replacing the previous Minor Ailments Service which had been in place since 2010.  Referral into the Pharmacy First service includes referral from GP practices, the community pharmacist or self-referral by the patient. Pharmacy First for everyday health conditions facilitates the assessment, and treatment if appropriate, of 13 common conditions such as acne, mouth ulcers and verrucae. All community pharmacies are contracted to provide this service and 166,382 consultations took place in 2023/24. In addition, the service has expanded in recent years to include a number of additional conditions as follows:The Pharmacy First Service for Emergency Hormonal Contraception (EHC) was introduced in July 2022. The service ensures that women and young people aged 13 years and over have timely access to sexual health service and free EHC (including the provision of bridging contraception) when clinically indicated. The service is currently provided by over 400 community pharmacies. In 2023/24 there were 22,055 consultations for this service. The Pharmacy First Service for Uncomplicated Urinary Tract Infections (UTIs) in women aged 16-64 years was rolled-out in November 2023. Currently, over 450 community pharmacies provide the UTI service and in 2023/24, 8,216 patients benefited from the service.The Pharmacy First Sore Throat Test and Treat Service for management of sore throat symptoms in people aged of 5 years and above, was piloted in 43 community pharmacies in NI between November 2023 and March 2024 as part of the winter preparedness plan. 6,768 people benefited from the service during this time. Following the successful evaluation of the service by MOIC, the service was   offered to all community pharmacists in December 2024, initially for 2 months but with a recent extension until 31st March 2025. The Pharmacy First Shingles Pilot was launched on 6th January 2025 in 50 pharmacies across Northern Ireland. This service for shingles will facilitate the assessment and treatment of patients aged 18 years and over in the community pharmacy, who present with symptoms of shingles. The Department of Health launched a Strategic Plan for Community Pharmacy in May of this year; it describes the vision and strategic priorities for community pharmacy up to 2030. One of these priorities is to expand the range of clinical services available through community pharmacies to improve access to patient care pathways for the diagnosis, treatment and prevention of illness. This will include the Pharmacy First Service, however, further development of this service in Northern Ireland is dependent on having an electronic platform and the necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline whether his Department takes into consideration the level of trauma in a particular community when awarding funding.
answer_text: Whilst my Department provides support to meet the needs of some of the most deprived people, families and communities in Northern Ireland, the level of trauma in a community is not part of the criteria in the award of funding. My Department is responsible for taking forward two projects that contribute to the Executive’s Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime, namely, Fresh Start in the Community Projects and the Developing Women in the Community Programme.While Fresh Start does not consider the levels of trauma when awarding funding, it is understood that communities where paramilitarism is prevalent are often associated with trauma and as such, the Executive’s Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime encourages all projects associated with the Programme to adopt a trauma informed approach.  It is also a requirement of the commissioning of the Developing Women in the Community Programme that delivery organisations involved in the Programme have either experience with, or are willing to sign up to, a trauma informed approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on his recent discussions with the Minister for Infrastructure on funding for rural transport.
answer_text: I met with the Minister for Infrastructure in September 2024 to discuss how we could work together to consider how to maximise the benefits of community transport for all, including rural provision.  I agreed for my Departmental officials to attend the Department for Infrastructure led Community Transport Cross-Departmental Working Group meetings in a rural champion capacity.  To date, two meetings have been held.  My officials will continue to join this Cross-Departmental Working Group to ensure the needs of rural dwellers are given due regard in the future, in line with the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any schools in Northern Ireland that have been found to have asbestos.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that there are 870 schools in Northern Ireland which have been found to contain asbestos.Grant maintained integrated and voluntary grammar schools retain responsibility for asbestos management and, as such, are not included in the above figure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has any plans to extend the training provided by Team Teach to include mainstream schools.
answer_text: Team Teach training is currently provided by the Education Authority (EA) for staff working in Special Schools and to both Primary and Post Primary Education Otherwise Than At School (EOTAS). There are no plans in place presently to expand Team Teach (TT) training to mainstream schools. The opportunity to offer an aspect of TT training to mainstream schools with specialist provisions is currently being considered by the EA. The Education Authority’s (EA) Primary and Post Primary Behaviour Support services have a full range of supports and available teacher professional learning on the Children and Young Peoples’ Services (CYPS) Training Programme which can be reviewed on the following link: EA Bookings (easds.org.uk). Schools can make direct requests to both services to request specific training relating to supporting pupils with social, behavioural, emotional and wellbeing needs, including responding to behaviours that require intervention, risk management and policy development training. Both services can also be contacted by telephone to receive advice and guidance in responding to particular presentations in the classroom.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he plans to monitor and publish data on (i) second home ownership; and (ii) the impact of second home ownership on housing stress.
answer_text: There is very limited data available on second homes which makes it difficult to establish their extent, location and what impacts they may have.For example, the latest Census for Northern Ireland showed that six percent of all houses had no usual residents. There are a range of possibilities for this including that they are truly vacant, being renovated, self-catering/short-term lets or second homes.My Department and the Housing Executive are beginning research on second home ownership to improve our understanding of the issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any measures his Department is taking to improve drugs education in schools; and (ii) any consultations his Department has conducted on the issue of harm prevention.
answer_text: (i) Schools have a statutory duty to deliver drugs education, covering both legal and illegal substances, as part of the statutory curriculum for Personal Development and Mutual Understanding in primary schools and Learning for Life and Work in post-primary schools.To support schools in the delivery of the curriculum, the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) develops and produces guidance documents and teaching support materials for schools, including resources in relation to drugs and alcohol abuse.  In 2015, CCEA published revised drugs guidance for schools and its support materials were updated in 2019.  The guidance and support materials provide schools with the context for why and how drugs education should be addressed.Additionally, the Department funds iNEQE Safeguarding Group for the provision of the Safer Schools Northern Ireland app which provides information to schools, parents/carers and pupils about a range of safeguarding issues, including alcohol and drugs.(ii) My Department has not carried out any consultations on the issue of harm prevention

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will grant Queen's University Belfast the £100,000 required for the acquisition of High Performance Liquid Chromatography equipment in order to establish a Rapid Action Drugs Alerts and Response system in conjunction with community services and the PSNI, given such a system is currently in place in Scotland and the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: In conjunction with the Public Health Agency, my Department is in the initial stages of considering the potential to expand drug testing services in Northern Ireland. I am aware of the outline proposal put forward by Queen’s University Belfast for a Rapid Drug Testing model, but I understand that proposals in this regard are still at an early stage. While the extremely challenging financial situation remains, funding for any enhanced testing service is unlikely to be available immediately. However, we will continue to monitor the position and seek to identify other potential funding sources.It is important to note that the Drug & Alcohol Monitoring & Information System (DAMIS), which is operated by PHA but overseen by a cross-departmental steering group, continues to provide up-to-date information on substances of concern to service users and those likely to come into contact with potentially harmful substances. Where appropriate, DAMIS pro-actively allows for rapid testing of substances of concern via the PSNI and Forensic Science Northern Ireland, and this information is used to inform related advice and alerts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department plans to extend pay parity to Foundation Classroom Assistants in line with SEND Assistants, recognising the workload of Foundation Assistants.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to extend pay parity to these roles. The Education Authority (EA) has a well-established job evaluation process against which all roles are assessed to determine their grade and associated pay points. All Classroom Assistant job descriptions have been evaluated under this process and have been graded accordingly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update of his Department's strategy to end drugs deaths.
answer_text: Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery was launched in September 2021 as the Northern Ireland Executive's new 10-year Substance Use Strategy. The strategy identifies 5 key outcomes including prevention, harm reduction, access to treatment services, and support for recovery. While being universal, the strategy has a focus on targeting the groups identified as being at most risk of harm and death – in particular, those who experience homelessness, injecting drug users, and those in contact with the justice system. This cross-sectoral strategy aims to directly reduce the harm for these groups through actions that place emphasis on harm reduction support measures for people who use alcohol and other drugs. For example, the recently agreed legislation to expand the provision of Naloxone will help prevent at-risk people who use drugs from dying due to an opioid overdose. Recent enhancements to needle exchange services are particularly valuable for engaging with people who inject drugs because it may be the only service they are willing to use and provides opportunities to signpost them to additional support and information. The First Update Report on the implementation of the Strategy was published earlier this year and is available on my Department’s website at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-update-prevent-harm-empower-recovery.pdf. This report outlines a range of early actions have been taken forward to pick up and address key priorities in the strategy, including the following:•	Rapid Review of Opioid Substitution Therapy in Prisons; •	Western Trust Needs Assessment; •	Review of our Inpatient Services; •	Consultation on Minimum Unit Pricing for Alcohol;•	Co-development of new strategic delivery plan for substance use services;•	Governance structures put in place; and•	Completion of work on indicators for monitoring the strategy.You may also wish to note the recent update provided to the Committee for Health at: https://data.niassembly.gov.uk/HansardXml/committee-34285.pdf.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered producing any guidance on phone addiction.
answer_text: Smartphone addiction can impact mental and physical health and cause anxiety, sleep disturbances, and reduced physical activity. It's important to be aware of how much time we spend on our devices and try to maintain a healthy balance. This Department is not currently considering producing specific guidance on phone addiction, however guidance was produced by the UK CMOs in 2019 on screen time generally and this is available at: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/uk-cmo-commentary-on-screen-time-and-social-media-map-of-reviews. The guidance provides advice for parents and carers on children and young people’s screen and social media use. The Department of Health has sponsorship of the Executive’s ‘Keeping children and young people safe: an Online Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland’ and accompanying three-year Action Plan which were published in February 2021.The Growing Up Online in Northern Ireland study was commissioned to inform the delivery of actions associated with the Online Safety Strategy. The study found, among other things, that children and young people are spending many hours online each day, on school days but especially at weekends and during holidays. While most internet use is within reasonable limits (2-4 hours per school day), there is evidence that many children and young people are spending much greater amounts of time online (frequently more than 7 hours per day). Alongside the Growing Up Online report, a series of spotlight reports have been published on key areas, including a report on the screen time debate for children and young people in January 2024.  The Department of Health is currently drafting an action plan for years 4 and 5 of the Online Safety Strategy. The Growing Up Online report and associated spotlight reports will inform the development and delivery of these new actions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any engagement they have had with the Roman Catholic Church on compensation for victims and survivors of Mother and Baby Homes and Magdalene Laundries.
answer_text: Victims and survivors have waited too long for truth, acknowledgement and accountability. The upcoming public inquiry will seek to answer three core questions of a) what happened b) why did it happen c) who was responsible? It is important that there is then meaningful redress provided from those found responsible.The inquiry will follow the principles of human rights investigation including procedural fairness and accountability, ensuring there is due and fair process.For this reason, formal negotiations on contributions cannot be entered into with the relevant non-statutory institutions at this stage. However, when appropriate, a suitable framework will be developed taking into account processes by other schemes, such as Historical Institutional Abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has considered producing any guidance on phone addiction.
answer_text: Many of the concerns associated with phone use occur outside school premises beyond the school day and are a wider societal issue which require cross-cutting consideration therefore, the Department does not have any plans at present to produce guidance solely on phone addiction.I did however issue DE Circular 2024/14 ‘Guidance for Schools on Pupils’ Personal Use of Mobile Phones and other Similar Devices During the School Day’ in September 2024.  The guidance recognises that a number of studies have raised concerns about the wider impact of phones on children and young people’s development and their mental health and wellbeing, including social deprivation, sleep deprivation, attention fragmentation and addiction.In addition, the guidance recognises the impact of the unauthorised use of mobile phones in class or during the school day in terms of issues related to bullying, healthy play, distraction and academic attainment.  Against this background, the guidance recommends that pupils do not use their mobile phones during the school day, with the aim of restricting the use of mobile phones to ensure that young people can fully engage in learning and participate in school life.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how the Sub-Regional Economic Plan will apply to the East Derry constituency.
answer_text: One of my core priorities is growing the economy across all areas of the North.  Local areas will be supported by enhanced support from Invest NI and the Department as detailed in my Sub-Regional Economic Plan.Local Economic Partnerships are a key part of my Plan.  Both Derry City and Strabane and Causeway Coast and Glens are on track to have their Local Economic Partnerships operational by 1st April 2025. It will be for each Partnership to identify and agree on actions that support delivery of their economic priorities within their areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans he has to bring forward legislation to address animal abuse during the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: Causing unnecessary suffering is already an offence under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, which also sets out legal provisions to protect animals from pain, suffering, injury and disease.  To bolster existing laws, I am actively considering priority measures for the remainder of this mandate and have met with members of the Northern Ireland Companion Animal Welfare Group to outline my initial proposals.  I will hold further meetings in the New Year with other stakeholders including local councils and veterinary associations.  Following this engagement, it is my intention to publish a programme of reforms which I wish to see delivered within the lifetime of this Assembly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures his Department is taking to realise pay parity for nurses in Northern Ireland, in order to avoid nurses feeling undervalued and leaving the profession to seek better salaries.
answer_text: Following the meeting with trades unions on 10 December 2024, my Department has improved on the offer made previously. This has been communicated to trade unions.I remain determined to implement, in full, the recommendations of the health pay review bodies, but it is clear that resolving all public sector pay issues remains an issue for the Executive as a whole. I will continue to seek a collaborative Executive response while efforts to secure additional savings within the health sector will also continue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health,  following recent drug-related deaths in Belfast, whether his Department is considering taking measures to make drug testing kits publicly available.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency currently offers single use Nitazene testing strips within all needle exchange sites across Northern Ireland which service users can avail of to test for the presence of Nitazenes. This has recently been expanded to include Fentanyl testing strips in areas of prevalence.NSP Direct was launched on 01 October 2024 – this is a free, confidential postal service for needle and syringe exchange equipment. Nitazene testing strips can be ordered on NSP Direct by anyone with a postcode in Northern Ireland. The PHA, in partnership with my Department, is currently in the early stages of scoping the options for other potential drug testing in Northern Ireland. Any recommendations to bring forward such a service is subject to legislative scrutiny and available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any specific measures his Department is taking to ensure that no child is told they cannot attend school out of fear for their safety.
answer_text: The safety of all children in our schools is of the utmost importance. The Department works closely alongside the Education Authority’s (EA) and other relevant organisations to support the needs of all children within our education system irrespective of their ethnicity or religious backgrounds.Bullying in our schools, for whatever reason and in whatever form, is always unacceptable. The Department of Education (DE) is committed to tackling bullying in schools and supporting all our pupils. The Boards of Governors of grant-aided schools also have statutory duties under Articles 17 and 18 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 to safeguard and promote the welfare of all their pupils. In addition, schools are expected to do whatever is reasonable to safeguard and promote their pupils’ safety and wellbeing.The Department publishes Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools - A Guide for Schools, which sets out the statutory duties of schools to safeguarding and promote the welfare of their pupils. The guidance also outlines the established processes and procedures to be followed in relation to child protection.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has made any assessment of the drop in business in Portstewart, following Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council's findings that footfall in Portstewart has dropped 34 per cent this year.
answer_text: I recognise the essential role of independent businesses in enhancing the vibrancy of our local economies and communities; responsibility for town centre regeneration however, rests with the Department for Communities, and development of local enterprise interventions is primarily the responsibility of local councils.  Regional balance however, is one of my four economic priorities, and the recently published Sub-Regional Economic Plan sets out my ambition to deliver improved regional balance across the North.  		I and my Officials will continue to engage with local business and policy stakeholders on trading conditions, and other relevant issues, in the context of my economic plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of recent reports of Islamophobia and racism in schools, whether he has any plans to initiate engagement with Northern Ireland's Islamic community.
answer_text: It is regretful and disappointing to hear of any individuals that experience Islamophobia and racism in Northern Ireland. Racism in schools would be the responsibility of the Department of Education and the Police Service of Northern Ireland.I regularly meet and engage with members from all communities and whilst Racial Equality is the responsibility of the Executive Office, my Department continues to have a strong focus on equality, diversity and inclusive communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether ketamine therapy has been used to treat depression in the last five years.
answer_text: The five Health & Social Care Trusts have not utilised ketamine therapy for the treatment of depression in the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the budget allocation for transport in the 2024-25 financial year, including a breakdown of funding for the Disability Action Transport Scheme and their rural partners, including Bridge in Derry.
answer_text: To provide the public transport network, my Department has allocated Translink Resource funding of £152.9m and Capital funding of £244.4m in this financial year.A breakdown of funding to support community transport and frontline Shopmobility services is provided in the tables below.  For the Disability Action Transport Scheme (DATS), my Department provides direct grant to Disability Action to manage the delivery of DATS services.  Disability Action directly delivers transport services in the Belfast and Greater Belfast area and allocates funding to 10 Delivery Partners for services across the rest of the urban areas across the North from the overall grant award.
