question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether an assessment has been made of whether Antrim Area Hospital will have sufficient capacity to provide health care for patients who currently present to Causeway Hospital, in the event that emergency care and surgery services are withdrawn from the Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: Northern HSC Trust have advised that they are fully aware of the pre-existing pressures on the Antrim site and have developed a plan to mitigate the impact of the additional emergency surgical patients who may be managed there in the Trust’s proposed model. This includes creating more surgical bed capacity and investing in ambulatory services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of reports that children in England and Wales with special educational needs are thought to be particularly vulnerable to exploitation, to detail (i) whether it is the same in Northern Ireland; and (ii) any strategies her Department has considered to address this.
answer_text: The Department of Education is the lead Department for children with special educational needs and therefore you should address your query to it. I have responded below:I am not aware of any similar research specific to Northern Ireland, therefore cannot comment if the findings for England and Wales would be the same for Northern Ireland. While not specifically for children with special educational needs, I, together with the Ministers of Health and Education recently launched the NI definition of Child Criminal Exploitation (CCE) and the multi-agency, cross-departmental CCE action plan.The development of a system response to child criminal exploitation seeks to ensure that safeguarding takes account of a holistic assessment of the risk factors and protective factors on an individual basis.The implementation of the action plan is led by the Child Exploitation Committee of the Safeguarding Board for NI of which the Education Authority is a member.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether an assessment has been made of health outcomes for those requiring ambulance transfer to Antrim Area Hospital or Altnagelvin Hospital, in the event that emergency care and surgery services are withdrawn from the Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: Emergency care is not being removed from Causeway Hospital.  There are no plans to change transfer patterns from Causeway to Altnagelvin.  The Northern HSC Trust are continuing to work with Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) to assess demand and capacity availability for the transfer of patients requiring Emergency general surgery from Causeway to Antrim hospital if the proposed change is approved by Trust Board and is sent to me for Ministerial approval of the decision.  The Northern HSC Trust has a clinical reference group consisting of consultant surgical, medical and critical care considering the safest pathways and support needed to meet the clinical needs of patients transferring from Causeway to Antrim for admission under a surgeon or requiring an emergency procedure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has made an assessment of how long Causeway Hospital Emergency Department can sustain its current status.
answer_text: The Northern HSC Trust has reiterated its commitment to maintaining a 24/7 Emergency Department in Causeway Hospital in its Strategic Vision for Causeway Hospital (March 2024) and more recently in its public consultation on the future configuration of general surgery. This is in line with Causeway’s status as a General Hospital, as set out in the Department’s ‘Hospitals – Creating a Network for Better Outcomes’ (Oct 2024).Causeway Hospital is and will remain an important part of our acute hospital network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment his Department has made in relation to (i) the health outcomes of patients on the Causeway coast who require an anaesthetist to be present in an ambulance transfer to either Antrim Area Hosptial or Altnagelvin Hospital; and (ii) whether an anaesthetist could reliably be expected to be present for such a journey.
answer_text: Northern HSC Trust have advised that should the proposed changes to general surgery be implemented there will be no transfers from Causeway to Altnagelvin hospital as a result.  Northern HSC Trust anaesthetic and surgical teams are working collaboratively to agree and develop the safest pathways and support needed to meet the clinical needs of patients transferring from Causeway to Antrim for admission under a surgeon or requiring an emergency procedure.  They are identifying the types of conditions and likelihood of patients requiring an anaesthetist to accompany them during transfer.  These will be low numbers and will be facilitated by the team.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the main roads in the East Derry constituency that are guaranteed to be gritted during periods of heavy snow.
answer_text: My Department has an emergency snow plan which may be initiated during periods of severe, widespread snow fall.  The routes are categorised and prioritised with an emphasis on trunk roads and motorways, with further provision of links to hospitals, ambulance, fire, and police stations.  The next category places emphasis on the remaining A class road network and linkage to isolated settlements, then the remainder of the salting schedule.  However, it should be noted that severe snow events rarely fall uniformly over the entire area, so the enactment of the emergency snow plan is not deemed necessary during localised snow events.Therefore, normal treatment of the salted network can generally be relied upon for most winter salting actions.  My Department’s Winter Service leaflet, which includes details of the salting schedule is available at:  https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/dfiroads-winter-service-information-leaflet

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools that have reported black mould on their campus since the start of the 2023-24 academic year.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that eighty-four controlled and maintained schools have reported issues with black mould since the start of the 2023-24 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the number of people who had their land vested as part of the A6 project and have not yet been compensated.
answer_text: To date, my Department has 254 Full and Final Settlements that remain to be paid to landowners along the A6.191 interim claims have been paid, up to a value of 90% of Land and Property Services estimate of the value of land taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the combination of dabrafenib and trametinib, as available in Wales, has been approved to treat childhood gliomas in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.Dabrafenib (Finlee®) with trametinib (Spexotras®) for the treatment of BRAF V600E mutation-positive glioma in children and young people aged 1 year and over, was endorsed by my Department in June 2024 and is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland in line with the current NICE guidelines (TA977), where infrastructure is in place and the service has capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) who made the decision to end the deal between the Education Authority and Fujitsu; and (ii) the rationale for this decision.
answer_text: The Education Authority and Fujitsu mutually agreed not to continue with the Strategic Partner / Schools Management System (SPSMS) Contract. The Education Authority, and more specifically, the SPSMS Project Board supported by the EdIS Programme Board, made this decision to ensure continuity of services to schools in Northern Ireland. The Education Department has not lost any money as a result of the Fujitsu IT systems no longer being implemented.   The rollout of the new Schools Management System will begin this academic year.  Continuity of services to schools will continue to be provided by the incumbent provider Capita, until a new Strategic Partner is procured.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how long schools will have to wait for a new IT system to be put in place, following the collapse of the Fujitsu IT contract.
answer_text: The Education Authority and Fujitsu mutually agreed not to continue with the Strategic Partner / Schools Management System (SPSMS) Contract. The Education Authority, and more specifically, the SPSMS Project Board supported by the EdIS Programme Board, made this decision to ensure continuity of services to schools in Northern Ireland. The Education Department has not lost any money as a result of the Fujitsu IT systems no longer being implemented.   The rollout of the new Schools Management System will begin this academic year.  Continuity of services to schools will continue to be provided by the incumbent provider Capita, until a new Strategic Partner is procured.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of money his Department estimates to have been lost given the Fujitsu IT systems will no longer be implemented.
answer_text: The Education Authority and Fujitsu mutually agreed not to continue with the Strategic Partner / Schools Management System (SPSMS) Contract. The Education Authority, and more specifically, the SPSMS Project Board supported by the EdIS Programme Board, made this decision to ensure continuity of services to schools in Northern Ireland. The Education Department has not lost any money as a result of the Fujitsu IT systems no longer being implemented.   The rollout of the new Schools Management System will begin this academic year.  Continuity of services to schools will continue to be provided by the incumbent provider Capita, until a new Strategic Partner is procured.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether considerations will be made to extend the offense of domestic abuse to those under 18 years of age who perpetrate violent crimes towards an intimate partner.
answer_text: The domestic abuse offence in Northern Ireland already applies to those who are under 18 years of age but who are over the age of criminal responsibility.  This ensures that abuse between teenagers in an intimate partner relationship is captured, recognising that such abuse can be just as severe as that within an adult relationship.In addition, the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 also provided for a statutory domestic abuse aggravator which can be attached to any offence (including murder), committed by someone over the age of criminal responsibility.  This means that the court can increase the sentence imposed and that the aggravator, if proven, will be included on the perpetrator’s

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work his Department is undertaking to prevent further strike action by teachers, given that the four main teacher unions suggest that strike action may be imminent if further pay is not agreed.
answer_text: Through the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) a joint pay group is operational and meetings have taken place with employers, sectoral support bodies and the recognised teaching trade unions (NITC) to explain and update them on the current financial pressures in Education and the current pay claim from NITC. The NITC pay claim is substantially higher than what has been recommended by the UK Government and accepted by teachers in England.  In line with the requirements of Public Sector Pay Policy, any proposals for a teachers’ pay award must be affordable and this is proving difficult to resolve given the pressures that already face the education budget. The Chair of TNC Management Side has also written to NITC formally requesting for a de-escalation and for the formal ballots to be stood down.I will continue to do all I can to secure funding for an appropriate pay settlement for teachers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps his Department is taking to ensure that students studying in the Republic of Ireland receive instalments of their student loan on time, as many students are reporting late receipt of their loan instalments.
answer_text: Processing of student loans is carried out on behalf of the Department by the Education Authority and the Student Loans Company under Service Level Agreements. No service level issues have been identified after consultation with them. If an individual has any queries about their application they should contact their local Student Finance NI office which will be able to provide any updates and assistance on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department is exploring rates relief for local businesses, given that whilst businesses in Britain received up to 75 per cent relief during Covid and 40 per cent now, business owners here have never received this support.
answer_text: Currently, around 70% of non-domestic ratepayers here already get some form of rate support. The proportion rises to 75% when Non-Domestic Vacant Rating is included. For years we have also provided support here that does not exist in England, Scotland and Wales, such as Industrial Derating at a cost of around £75M per annum in 2024/25.While calls for ever increasing rates support are prominent, every pound we spend on rate relief as an Executive means either reduced revenue for vital public services or higher rates bills for other homes and businesses.The business rate relief of 75% you refer to only applies in England. The support was never available in Scotland, and Wales had to provide a lower rate of support for 2024/25. As the question alludes to this was a legacy support scheme from the COVID period. The Executive here provided 100% rate relief for 27 months for those businesses most affected by Covid, including for periods when the support in England had reduced to 75%. The Chancellor’s actions to reduce support in this area serves to confirm the affordability issues that I have consistently referred to with this particular business rates scheme.  Those affordability issues are exacerbated if implanted into our local taxbase and economy which is heavily comprised of small and medium-sized enterprises.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the increase in financial pressures, to detail any action his Department is taking to support our GPs with running costs.
answer_text: My Department provides GP Practices with funding on a monthly basis, via global sum payments, in order that the practices can deliver General Medical Services (GMS), and this funding is also used by the Practices to support the running costs of the Practice, for example, staff costs, heat, light and power, telephone etc.Financial support is also provided through the full reimbursement (for GMS areas only) of rent & rates for their premises. The rent for GPs in their own premises and Trust premises is reviewed every 3 years by Land & Property Services (LPS) and a review and potential uplifted is granted if required. For Practices in leased premises the rent is also reviewed by LPS if there is an increase by the landlord and, if in line with the LPS valuation, the rent will be covered by my Department.  Yearly increases to rates bills are also covered in full for areas occupied for GMS purposes.All of the IT costs associated with managing the GP Clinical systems operating in the Practice are funded by my Department.Furthermore, Practices receive a budget to go towards the cost of clinical waste collection, and this is reviewed on an annual basis.  Similarly, there is funding made available to Practices towards the cost of GP Consumables and which is also reviewed annually

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to ensure wheelchair users are able to access smear tests at their GP surgery, especially when the patient is known to have a family history of cervical cancer.
answer_text: With regards to accessibility to GP premises, the GMS Contract states that for GP services, ‘a contract must contain a term which requires the contractor in core hours- to provide – essential services, and additional services funded under the global sum, at such times, within core hours, as are appropriate to meet the reasonable needs of his patients’.  It also stipulates that the practice leaflet must detail, ‘Whether the practice premises have suitable access for disabled patients and, if not, the alternative arrangements for providing services to such patients’.Many service users may need extra support or adjustments made to enable them to undergo cervical screening, for example those who have experienced sexual violence, those who have a physical disability or a learning disability, or a sensory impairment, or those who need an interpreting service. Patients who require additional support or adjustments, are encouraged to contact their GP practice before their cervical screening appointment to outline their additional support needs, so that this can be accommodated.The Public Health Agency confirm there are no special arrangements in place for cervical screening for anyone with a family history of cervical cancer – all eligible women aged 25-49 get invited for screening every 3 years and those aged 50-64 are invited to attend every 5 years. The majority of cervical cancers are not hereditary/genetic, and while there may be a family history in some cases, this is more likely linked to environment factors and the way the body responds to HPV infection. If a GP identifies a clinical need, they have the option to refer the patient to their local Trust gynaecology department to carry out their cervical screening test.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures his Department is taking to ensure that all taxi firms are providing consistent services for those with disabilities, noting that many firms only provide irregular and intermittent services for those with disabilities.
answer_text: Taxi businesses are directly responsible for complying with equality legislation.  It is against the law for transport service providers such as taxi operators to discriminate against disabled people in the way in which they provide or do not provide their services. The legislation makes it unlawful for taxi operators to refuse or deliberately fail to provide a service to a disabled person. They must also make "reasonable adjustments" to overcome elements in their services which present barriers to disabled people.Under the current taxi legislative framework, there is no requirement for taxi operators to have a minimum number of wheelchair accessible vehicles in their fleet, and the option to include these types of vehicles in their fleet is at the discretion of taxi operators and drivers. I currently have no plans to make any changes to this policy. However, my Officials have recently met with the Inclusive Mobility and Transport Advisory Committee (IMTAC) and it was agreed that a workshop would be arranged in the near future with Class B Taxi drivers and IMTAC representatives/members to better understand the accessibility issues from both the perspective of taxi drivers and users of wheelchair accessible taxis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in recognition that recent increases in National Insurance contributions and the minimum wage will increase the wage bill of dental practices, to detail any measures his Department is taking to ensure that (i) costs for dental appointments do not rise; and (ii) dental practices do not leave the NHS.
answer_text: It is not possible to provide a definitive assessment on our future funding position until a Budget settlement for future years has been reached. Despite the positive news about the budget it is still likely we still face a significant funding gap in Northern Ireland as a result of both inflationary pressures and increased demand. I acknowledge there are a number of healthcare providers that will be affected by the rise in employer National Insurance contributions announced in the Budget and I understand that as well as General Dental Practitioners, GPs, care homes and hospices have voiced concerns about the impact this will have on them. Recognising this and the valuable role played by those providers, these funding pressures will nevertheless need to be considered and prioritised alongside other priorities before I am in a position to make any commitments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any discussions his Department has had with Northern Health and Social Care Trust to ensure that female patients in need of home care are provided with female carers, especially recognising that intimate care, including showering, can cause female patients considerable stress when performed by male carers.
answer_text: My department has contacted the Northern Trust in relation to your query and they have provided this response: ‘All requests for Homecare are commissioned via our brokerage system, with packages of care being offered based on service user assessed needs. If male staff are employed within the care rota that a package has been offered from, the provider will communicate this to the Trust Named Worker who will discuss if this is acceptable to the service user and/or family. If support from male care staff is considered not acceptable by the service user and/or family, the Trust will continue to try to source an alternative provider. The Trust is committed to ensuring that a high standard of care is provided to all service users, and that individual preferences with regards to care provided are supported where possible. All providers endeavour to provide female care staff to support female service users with personal care tasks while ensuring compliance with equality legislation when employing staff of differing genders.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department is undertaking any consideration of extending funding to the Buddy Bear Trust Conductive Education School.
answer_text: Buddy Bear Trust Conductive Education School is an independent school approved by the department, under Article 26 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996, as an institution suitable for the admission of children with special educational needs (SEN).If following the conclusion of a statutory assessment process the decision to issue a statement of SEN is taken by the Education Authority (EA) to name an independent school, the EA must pay any fees payable in respect of the education of the child at the named school. The EA has allocated £124,000 to the Buddy Bear Trust in 2024/2025 for the children it has placed there.The Buddy Bear Trust has also received a total of c£50,000 (2020/21 to 2024/25) from the department’s Pathway Fund, which contributes to the overall cost of the Buddy Bear Pathway project.  This includes meeting the costs of the principal and two teachers.  The Pathway Fund will commence a new two-year cycle in 2025/26 and will be open for applications in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to cut the VAT rate on donor sperm for couples where the man is infertile.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, publicly funded fertility treatment is available to those who meet the access criteria - those patients do not have to pay for the treatment costs, including for donor sperm, if that is required. 		Any query regarding the VAT arrangements for patients who choose to undergo private fertility treatment would need to be raised directly with the private provider.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on plans for the A2 Ballykelly Bypass.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.My Department is in the process of developing a new Transport Strategy that will help to shape the vision and strategic priorities for transport through to 2035.  Once this is published, it will help to inform the Transport Plan for the Regional Strategic Transport Network, which includes the A2 at Ballykelly.A preferred route for Ballykelly Bypass was announced in 2010, however, in advance of any decision to prioritise this scheme, officials would need to undertake a reassessment of the route due to the passage of timeThe estimate for the bypass, compiled back in 2008, was in the range of £35m-£40m. Again, this would need to be brought up to date and would be subject to the high levels of inflation and cost increases experienced by the construction sector in recent years.Any decision to proceed with this project can only be made when there is budget certainty for the scheme and its delivery is in line with my Department’s emerging Transport Plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on waiting lists for specialist dental care for children in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust and the Western Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether his Department has been in contact with Northern Ireland Electricity Networks to discuss recurring incidences of power outages in the Greysteel and Ballykelly areas; and (ii) to detail the steps being taken to prevent future occurrences.
answer_text: Officials in my department have confirmed that they are not aware of any incidences of power outages affecting the streetlights in the Greysteel and Ballykelly area, and as such have not been in contact with NIE.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, following a spike in the reported incidences of dog fouling in the Causeway Coast and Glens district, to detail the number of people in Northern Ireland that have been prosecuted for the fouling of lands by dogs in 2024.
answer_text: Offences under The Fouling of Lands by Dogs (Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council) Order 2018 may result in a fixed penalty fine being issued by a District Council, and not all may result is a resolution at courts, so you may wish to approach the Causeway Coast and Glens Borough council directly for information on the number of fixed penalties issued by them. The Department holds information on cases dealt with at courts in Northern Ireland.  In the period to the end of September 2024, there were no cases dealt with at courts which were prosecuted on behalf of Causeway Coast & Glens Borough Council and where there was an offence in relation to dog fouling offences.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline how resources will be deployed to address the increase in violent and sexual attacks against women and girls over the last year.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the processes in place to ensure that next of kin are informed of a patient's decision to request a do-not-resuscitate order.
answer_text: The following information has been provided by NHS UK and the HSC Trusts in response to this question on Do Not Resuscitate orders.NHS UK If you are not the patient and the patient has capacity to make their own decisions, they will need to agree to it being discussed with you. If you do not have capacity, your family, friends or those with the legal power to represent you (such as a Lasting Power of Attorney) can raise concerns on your behalf.Belfast HSC TrustThe Belfast HSC Trust has a “Do Not Attempt Cardio Pulmonary Resuscitation (DNACPR) policy: Decisions relating to cardiopulmonary resuscitation in adults”. It states at:4.12.1 The family/carers of a patient who has capacity should not be involved in resuscitation discussions without that patient’s consent.4.12.2 If you conclude that the patient does not wish to know about or discuss a DNACPR decision or if they decline a discussion, then it is appropriate to make the decision without consulting the patient further. It would be appropriate to ask the patient who they would wish to be consulted. 4.12.3 If a patient lacks capacity, you should inform those close to the patient about the DNACPR decision and the reasons for it.Northern HSC TrustThe Northern Trust DNACPR policy, reviewed by Directors of Medicine and Nursing prior to November 2024 Encompass go live has the following extract: "3.21	A DNACPR decision will usually only be made after discussion with the patient and their relevant others (unless the patient has requested confidentiality). Any discussion with the patient or their relevant others regarding resuscitation must be recorded on the EPIC system of Encompass. If the relevant others are not present for the discussion they should be informed of the outcome within 24hrs (unless the patient has requested confidentiality)"Western HSC TrustThe Western HSC Trust DNACPR policy highlights communication with patient and/or next of kin depending on patients’ capacity and wishes.Sections 5.0, 6.3 and 6.7 of the policy states:Section 5.0“As part of the admission process the Framework for Decisions Relating to CPR (Appendix II) must be reviewed and decision made documented in the patient’s medical notes/records. If a DNACPR decision is recommended this should be clearly documented in the patient’s medical notes/records and nursing notes. In these instances, the DNACPR Communication sheet (Appendix I) should also be completed and then be placed inside the front cover of the patient’s medical notes/records. The DNACPR order must be tailored to the individual and not based on factors such as age or care setting. The opinion of other members of the multidisciplinary team, the patient if appropriate, and, with due regard for patient confidentiality, the patient’s relatives or close friends in absence of family, may all be valuable in informing the decision of the Consultant/General Practitioner/Health Care Professional in charge of the patients care. If there is no discussion with the patient or t

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to new rulings on inheritance tax, whether his Department plans to bring in any new measures to help ease the financial burden on farmers.
answer_text: I recognise that the recent Budgetary announcements regarding inheritance tax and the limitation of agricultural property relief and business property relief at a rate of 100% to a combined value of £1m has caused great concern in the agricultural community. I met with the NI Secretary of State on 4th November 2024 and expressed my concerns and urged that the matter be reconsidered.  I also raised the matter with the Defra Minister of State, Daniel Zeichner MP in a meeting with other Devolved Agriculture Ministers on 18th November 2024 and will continue to do the same in meetings with other UK Government Ministers. I can also state that a letter signed by the First Minister, deputy First Minister, the Finance Minister and myself was sent to the Chancellor of the Exchequer, Rt. Hon Rachel Reeves MP on 18th November 2024 urging the UK Government to reconsider their approach. It would not be feasible for my Department to introduce measures to offset the potential impacts of UK Government taxation policy which are not devolved matters.  I will however continue to do all that I can to support the farming industry in Northern Ireland on this issue within the remit of DAERA.My Department’s new Farm Support and Development Programme contains a Farming for the Generations Scheme which is currently being rolled out as a pilot. The pilot scheme aims to raise awareness of the need for succession planning on farms. The scheme aims to support farm families through a three phased approach: planning for succession, developing the successor, and supporting the lead generation within the farm family. It will also link farmers without a family successor to new entrants to provide access to land and other resources.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the cost to patients and the potential risk to those unable to organise their own medicines, whether his Department plans to reconsider implementing the Nomads scheme for new elderly patients.
answer_text: “Nomad” refers to a pre-packed medication system (blister pack or Monitored Dosage Systems (MDS) for patients and can be used to assist patients to take their medicines as prescribed by the healthcare professional.My Department provides substantial support to community pharmacies to support their patients, including elderly patients, to take their medicines.In 2024/25 funding of £18 Million has been made available to support community pharmacy providers in meeting their obligations under the Disability Discrimination Act 1995 (as amended) (‘DDA’) to assess the needs of patients in relation to medicines adherence support and provide reasonable adjustments where required. Reasonable adjustments include a range of options, not exclusively Monitored Dosage Systems (MDS) e.g. medication reminder charts/prompts, larger print labels etc. Interventions to support adherence should be considered on a case-by-case basis and should address the needs of individual patients. No specific intervention can be recommended for all patients. It should be noted that this funding does not cover a patient who does not require reasonable adjustment under DDA, but prefers to have their medications managed through MDS.  Some pharmacies may provide a private service to patients in this cohort; charging for this service would be a commercial decision for the pharmacy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline why medical students, who receive funding from his Department to cover tuition fees, receive a reduced maintenance loan.
answer_text: The maintenance support arrangements for NI-domiciled medical students whose tuition fees are met by my Department during their fifth year of study on a first degree, are determined by the Education (Student Support) (No.2) Regulations (NI) 2009, which are the responsibility of the Department for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether there will be free provision of rape alarms for those in need, given the recent rise of attacks in the North West.
answer_text: I deplore the recent rise of attacks in the North West. There is no place in our society for this type of crime. We have a duty to tackle this epidemic of violence in our society targeted at women and girls and to start tackling those attitudes and that culture that treats women’s lives as though they are expendable.My Department, along with the Department of Health, jointly launched the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy in September and is committed to creating a society in Northern Ireland where domestic and sexual abuse is not tolerated. As women and girls are known to be disproportionately affected by these offences, the above Strategy is closely aligned to the Executive’s Ending Violence against Women and Girls’ strategy. Funding is currently available from my Department for projects which aim to tackle domestic and sexual abuse through Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) and the Asset Recovery Community Scheme (ARCS). These projects provide a selection of crime prevention equipment, which includes personal alarms. Personal alarms are available from PCSPs across Northern Ireland, primarily at crime prevention and engagement events but may also be requested directly from the PCSP by individuals and groups. ARCS funding has been provided to PSNI projects in Derry City and Strabane, Lurgan and Cookstown, supplying crime prevention equipment to vulnerable people, which includes personal alarms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is aware that students from Northern Ireland with chronic or lifelong health conditions, under the care of a consultant and studying in Great Britain, must be referred separately in each jurisdiction.
answer_text: Northern Ireland students living in Great Britain are considered to be ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland during their course of study, and are therefore able to access healthcare in Northern Ireland as if they were resident here. Accordingly, students who remain under the care of a consultant in Northern Ireland for a long-term health condition can discuss their ongoing care requirements with their consultant. Students can either travel back to NI if required during the semester, or the NI consultant/Trust, or the new GP, can decide on the appropriate care plan, directly with relevant NHS providers, to ensure individual care needs can be met during the semester.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how many learners have (i) applied for Education Maintenance Allowance payments in the last six months; and (ii) been waiting more than six weeks to receive this payment.
answer_text: Responsibility for the administration of the Education Maintenance Allowance (EMA), including the assessment of eligibility for support, has been delegated by my Department to the Student Loans Company (SLC), and performance is monitored in line with Service Level Agreement targets. The applications portal for academic year 2024/25 opened on 8th April 2024, and data provided by the SLC indicates that 4,926 applications have been submitted for processing since then. 100% of applications received were processed by the SLC within 14 working days during the peak period of August to November; and 100% were processed within 7 working days during non-peak periods. Although students are able to submit applications to the SLC for processing in advance of the new academic year, no support is payable until the beginning of term. Those submitting applications during April, May, June and July would not have received support within 6 weeks as no support would have been payable at that point. Payment of the EMA weekly allowance is also contingent upon students signing an EMA Learning Agreement with their school or college, and having their attendance confirmed on the online EMA portal each week. Students may in some instances experience delays in receiving EMA support due to insufficient evidence having been provided in support of their application. Students who submit their applications to the SLC before 30th September will have any support payable backdated to the beginning of term. Those who are experiencing difficulties are advised to contact the Education Maintenance Allowance student helpline on 0300 200 7089.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is taking measures to end the requirement for students from Northern Ireland to have to deregister and re-register with a GP every time they move between Northern Ireland and Great Britain.
answer_text: Patients can only be registered with one GP in the United Kingdom (UK) and this should be a GP practice that has a practice area which covers the address where they spend the majority of their time.  This is to ensure a single GP acts as a central point for continuity of care.Students are therefore required to register with a GP in the area where they reside for the majority of their time. When a student from Northern Ireland is studying in, and registers with, a GP in another part of the UK, their previous registration with a GP in NI is cancelled and their medical record is sent to the new GP.  This is required because the GP with whom the patient is registered is responsible for the provision of care to that patient. As students from NI are considered to be Ordinarily Resident in NI during their course of study they are therefore able to access all healthcare in NI, including access to GPs, as if they were resident here, during a visit.  There is no requirement for them to re-register and de-register with a GP in NI on a visit during their course of study and they are not subject to entitlement to healthcare checks.    If the student returns to live in NI at the end of their course, they are then subject to the normal registration rules. During their course of study students are not removed from Health and Social Care (HSC) hospital lists in NI and may attend outpatient and inpatient appointments.  They may also request their GP in another part of the UK to refer them to NI for secondary healthcare.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to the findings of the Sub-Regional Economic Plan Technical Analysis which found that Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council received zero per cent of new jobs from Foreign Direct Investment from 2015 to 2022,  what measures he is taking to bring investment to the Causeway Coast and Glens council area.
answer_text: My department and Invest NI are firmly committed to delivering greater levels of investment to all parts of the North. In line with this commitment, my Sub-Regional Economic Plan has been created as a framework to improve sub regional economic performance.To support this, all 11 Councils have been asked to establish Local Economic Partnerships, bringing together central government, the business community, universities and colleges, local enterprise agencies, and civil society. These Partnerships will identify the main barriers to economic development and the priority interventions that will build the region’s value proposition to help deliver improved sub regional delivery. I have committed to provide funding of £45 million over the next three years to support the activities identified through these Partnerships. Invest NI has also agreed to deliver 65% of its investments outside of the Belfast Metropolitan area by the end of it’s 2024-2027 Business Strategy. This will also include targets for Foreign Direct Investment in each of the regional areas. Invest NI has also committed to strengthen its regionally based structure and staffing.The approach has been welcomed by Council Officers and Economic Development Partners in the Causeway Coast & Glens Council area who have already been proactively working closely with my department and Invest NI, recently providing with a high-level outline of their priority focus areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department is taking to ensure the sewage systems in Ballintoy, Ballymoney, Coleraine, Dungiven, Limavady, Kilrea and Stranocum are urgently updated to cope with demands for new developments.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:There is available wastewater capacity at wastewater treatment works (WwTW) in Ballintoy, Ballymoney, Coleraine (North Coast WwTW), Dungiven and Limavady. An upgrade to Kilrea WwTW, ca. £9.3m, is currently under construction which will create wastewater capacity. There is currently no wastewater capacity at Stranocum WwTW. However, all of these towns have capacity issues within their wastewater networks. An investment of ca. £4.1m within the Limavady catchment is included within Price Control 21 (2021-27) which includes the upgrade of Whitehill wastewater pumping station (WwPS) (already completed) and the upgrade of Bovally WwPS, which will help to address capacity issues in this area.In addition, NI Water has established a Development Constraints Project Team, to help alleviate capacity issues and is also encouraging developers and wastewater management professionals to engage with it early, through the Pre- Development Enquiry process, to try and find a resolution to any issues at an early stage, to help facilitate development, where possible.I am also striving to help facilitate development by taking a three-pronged approach to this matter:  (i) my officials are currently examining the legislative and regulatory frameworks to assess the feasibility of developers being able to make financial contributions to NI Water in order to improve waste and sewerage infrastructure and facilitate development; (ii) I am also bringing forward legislation to encourage increased use of nature-based solutions such as Sustainable Drainage Systems in developments to separate and release stormwater more slowly to the system so that it does not overrun wastewater infrastructure. This can also reduce flooding and pollution risks ; and (iii) I continue to work with Executive colleagues to try and increase wastewater investment. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has plans to review the amount a Northern Ireland student is offered for studying in Great Britain, to ensure they are able to meet living costs.
answer_text: The level of maintenance support for the 2025/26 academic year for students studying here and in Britain is still under consideration.	I am committed to a review of Higher Education Funding, including financial support provided to students.  I have instructed my officials to bring forward detailed proposals for this review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department is taking to address reported concerns of serious adverse consequences as a result of the proposed reconfiguration of Causeway Hospital as a General Hospital.
answer_text: The Northern Trust is holding a public consultation on a proposed reconfiguration of general surgery between Antrim Area Hospital and Causeway Hospital. The consultation opened on 23 August and will close on 29 November 2024. Once the consultation has closed the Trust will produce an outcomes report addressing the issues raised during the consultation including any concerns about patient safety. The Department will consider this report and I shall take the final decision on any proposed reconfiguration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, given the recent rise of sexual assaults, whether she will consider reviewing legislation in relation to the right to carry pepper spray.
answer_text: Personal safety is very important to me as Justice Minister and the risk of individuals being subject to an attack.  However, as in the rest of the UK and Ireland, ownership, possession and use of pepper spray by citizens in Northern Ireland is illegal.  This is prohibited under Article 45(1)(f) of the Firearms (Northern Ireland) Order 2004.  There are currently no plans to review this position.I would encourage anyone to contact the PSNI immediately if they are concerned about someone’s behaviour.Further advice on personal protection can be found on the PSNI website at https://www.psni.police.uk/safety-and-support/keeping-safe/protecting-yourself

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what engagement he has had with his counterparts in the Irish Government in relation to reviewing financial incentives for students to study at local universities.
answer_text: Question 11 – AQO 1119/22-27I am committed to removing barriers to people from the south who want to study here and vice versa.Earlier this year I engaged on this issue with my Ministerial counterpart in the South at the time, Simon Harris, and I have sought a meeting with his successor, Minister O’Donovan.My Department has already taken positive steps already to address some of the financial barriers.  For instance, tuition fee loans for postgraduates from here studying in the South have been introduced this year.  I also intend to introduce similar loans for part-time study in the South. These discussions will continue once a new government is formed in Dublin.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to extend the prescription of weight loss injections to patients suffering with hypothalamic obesity, a known sequala of brain tumour complications, as it is already available for patients in Great Britain.
answer_text: The Department of Health has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals (TAs) are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.NICE has published TA664 (liraglutide) and TA875 (semaglutide) which advises that these treatments are recommended as an option for weight management alongside a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity in adults, however it can only be prescribed in secondary care by a specialist multidisciplinary weight management service.Northern Ireland does not currently have a specialist weight management service.In November last year, the Department launched a public consultation on the proposed introduction of a Regional Obesity Management Service (ROMS), which would provide a means for people living with obesity in Northern Ireland to access surgery or access GLP-1 medications.The consultation closed on 1 March 2024, and my Department is currently considering the analysis of the responses received.  At this time, I am unable to give any commitments as to the potential future establishment of this service.  Any decisions regarding future development of this service will be based on consideration of the evidence presented, availability of resources and competing priorities.In addition, whilst tirzepatide (Mounjaro®) is currently accepted for use in Northern Ireland for treating type 2 diabetes alongside diet and exercise in line with NICE TA924, guidance for the use of tirzepatide for managing obesity or overweight with risk factors is currently in development with NICE and the guidance is expected to be published in December 2024. Once published, my Department will consider this in line with Circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22.It is also important to note that certain liraglutide and semaglutide products, such as Victoza® and Ozempic®, are only authorised for the treatment of type 2 diabetes and are not licensed for the management of obesity, therefore their use for this purpose would be considered ‘off-label’.There have been ongoing UK wide and global supply disruptions to glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist (GLP-1 RA) products authorised for treatment of type 2 diabetes since mid-2023, including Ozempic® and Victoza® preparations. These supply disruptions have arisen due to an increase in global demand for these products for licensed indications as well as off-label use, including a significant increase in private supplies for weight loss purposes. Advice issued by my Department to healthcare professionals is clear that these products should only be prescribed for their licensed indications and should not routinely be prescribed for obesity on an off-label bas

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any action being taken to rectify the delays in issuing occupational pensions affected by the McCloud judgement; and (ii) the steps being taken to provide information to recipients on their McCloud decision.
answer_text: My Department is in communication with pension scheme members who are awaiting implementation of their retirement pension in order to keep them informed of progress and to help them understand the options open to them in order to assist them in making option choices.A number of payments were delayed due to a software issue, which has now been resolved by the pension system provider.This has been the first year of an updated pension process involving member choice of options. I have asked relevant officials to carry out a review of all processes and timescales involved in the retirement process to ensure lessons learned this year are acted on in future years to ensure timely payments to retiring teachers.The Department of Education website contains a range of information relating to the McCloud ruling.  Those who are directly affected by the ruling will receive individual correspondence outlining how the decision affects them and the choices they can make.  The affected individuals who retired after 1 April 2015 will receive details of their choices by 31 March 2025 in line with the requirements of the Teachers’ Pension Scheme (Remediable Service) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2023.Information on the choices available for affected teachers who are still in service will be communicated at the point of retirement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a full list of (i) organisations he has met; and (ii) the organisations he has declined to meet, since he took office.
answer_text: A list of (i) the organisations I have met and (ii) the organisations I have declined to meet are below:the organisations I have met7th Ballymena Scout GroupEastside LearningNorthern Ireland Schools and Colleges Careers Association Abbey Sure StartEducation World ForumNorthern Ireland Schools' Football AssociationAdapt my PlayEmployers for ChildcareNorthern Ireland Youth AssemblyAll Ireland HockeyEmotionalWelbeingGroupParent FocusAll Party GroupExpert Panel for Educational UnderachievementParentKindAmira LearningFóram na nÓgPhoenix Education CentreAPC on Voluntary and Community workGMB UnionPivotalAssociation of School and College LeadersGoverning Bodies AssociationPlayboardAsthma + Lung UK Northern IrelandGreater Shankill Principals' GroupPolicy Forum for Northern IrelandAt the Heart of the GameHarland & WolffPSNIBallymoney Community PlaygroupHeath and Social Care NIRegional Integrated Support for Education Northern IrelandBanana Bunch Playgroup WhiteheadHelping Hands DaycareRoad Safe NIBank of IrelandHistoric Royal Palaces - question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has had any contact with the PSNI to discuss the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework, following the death of Mary Ward.
answer_text: I would like to start by offering my sincere condolences to the family and friends of each of the women who have recently lost their lives as a result of violence perpetrated by men in recent weeks.  These are appalling and unacceptable crimes and it is right that we are alarmed and that we need to work collaboratively across government and society to see what more we can do to make women safer.I welcome the recent launch of the next phase of the PSNI’s Action Plan on violence against women and girls, as well as the Executive’s Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls and its first delivery plan.  In partnership with the Minister of Health I have also launched a new seven year Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, which  will directly contribute to the broader goals of ending violence against women and girls.  My officials are working closely with PSNI and with the Executive office to deliver the outcomes set out in both strategies that will make Northern Ireland safer for women and girls and for all victims of domestic and sexual abuse.However, as both these strategies make clear, ending violence against women and girls in all its forms is a whole societal issue; everyone has a role to play and, in light of these horrific murders, I am clear that the need for a collaborative response has never been greater.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide an update on the review into support for young people, post 19 years old, with special educational needs.
answer_text: Question 13 – AQO 941/22-27In recognition of the importance of this issue, I commissioned my officials to carry out a review of the the current skills and training provision for school leavers with special educational needs (SEN), and on the legislative protections. These are complex and cross cutting issues.Officials are currently working through the planning and scoping activity, engaging with partners and those with lived experience, including through participating in DE-led parent/carer stakeholder sessions, meeting representative organisations, and briefing the All-Party Group on Learning Disability.  The work will contribute to the Executive’s commitment to support young people with SEN, as set out in the draft Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the steps his Department is taking to address hesitancy among women to come forward for cervical screening.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline when they intend to publish the summary report of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Living With Water Plan in Eglinton village.
answer_text: My Department is leading on the development of a Living With Water in Derry Plan, similar to the Living with Water in Belfast Plan. The geographical scope of the draft Derry Plan extends to include Eglinton Village. Engagement with key stakeholders is continuing during the development of the draft Plan, including Derry City & Strabane District Council, Northern Ireland Housing Executive, and the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs.  ‘A Vision for Living With Water in Derry’, a pre curser to the Plan, was published in May 2023. The draft Plan is due to be published for consultation in late Summer 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department's work on preventing flooding in Eglinton in the last 12 months.
answer_text: Development of flood alleviation proposals for Eglinton are currently being progressed to determine if an economically viable solution can be found. The flooding mechanisms in Eglinton are complex with many compounding factors. A potential solution that would mitigate the risk of flooding from the river has been identified, however, surface water flooding would still affect properties.Work is progressing to inform a wider-ranging review, which will look beyond traditional hard engineered solutions. My officials have recently met with representatives from the local community to understand first hand their experiences of the flooding in 2017 and 2022 and to introduce the team leading this study and to the approach now being taken. It is hoped the review will be completed by Spring 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has considered a moratorium on planning in Eglinton village until the Living With Water Plan is designed and delivered.
answer_text: I do not consider it necessary to implement a moratorium on planning in Eglinton Village. Planning authorities, through their locally elected representatives, in dealing with applications for planning permission, must have regard to the local development plan, so far as material to the application, and to any other material considerations and may refuse planning permission. Although there is no defined list of material considerations, Planning Policy Statement 15 ‘Planning and Flood Risk’ and the Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) are both material considerations. PPS 15  and the SPPS set out the Department’s planning policies to minimise and manage flood risk to people, property and the environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will meet with her counterpart in the Republic of Ireland to address the all-island issue of violence against women and girls.
answer_text: The Executive Office has lead responsibility for developing a strategic response to end violence against women and girls. However, I recognise that many of the interventions delivered by the Department of Justice contribute directly to tackling gender-based violence, including violence against women and girls, particularly in relation to tackling domestic and sexual abuse, which disproportionately affects women and girls.  Since reprising the role of Justice Minister, I have already had a number of meetings with Minister McEntee where we have discussed our mutual responses to domestic and sexual abuse across the island of Ireland and where there is scope to work collaboratively and to share information and best practice, and I look forward to this positive engagement continuing.In addition, my officials have ongoing engagement with their counterparts in the Republic of Ireland, including through structures underpinning the Inter-Governmental Agreement on Co-operation on Criminal Justice Matters to ensure that there is a strong and effective response for all victims of domestic and sexual violence across both jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will meet the Secondary Students' Union of Northern Ireland to discuss changes to the Learning for Life and Work module.
answer_text: I met with representatives from the Secondary Students' Union of Northern Ireland on 27 March 2024.  At that meeting we discussed a range of topics including Learning for Life and Work and wider curriculum issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether cloud seeding technologies have been used in Northern Ireland in the last ten years.
answer_text: Cloud seeding, as a technology which aims to generate more rainfall through manipulating clouds, is not a technology which has been used in Northern Ireland in the last ten years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the average air quality levels in (i) Dungiven; (ii) Belfast; (iii) Portrush; and (iiii) Derry in (a) 2022; and (b) 2023.
answer_text: Information on air quality and air pollution levels is available on the DAERA website, www.airqualityni.co.uk . This data is available on a real time basis, and the website contains all historical data for every monitoring station in Northern Ireland.  Annual reports on air quality are also available on this website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the suicide prevention education he plans to introduce into the school curriculum during this mandate.
answer_text: The Council for Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment’s (CCEA) Wellbeing Hub already provides a wide range of curriculum linked resources and support for teachers and parents. It also supports the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework which was developed collaboratively by my Department and the Department of Health (DoH).The main emphasis of the framework is to support educational settings in promoting emotional health and wellbeing to enable children and young people to cope with challenges they may face in life and to provide support for those who may be at risk or show signs of needing further help.My officials are also engaging with the DoH on the Protect Life 2 Strategy and I will consider any actions for education when an action Plan is developed.Further there are a number of projects within the Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework, currently available to schools and pupils, which aim to promote positive emotional health and prevent escalation of more serious mental health issues.  These include the Being Well Doing Well (BWDW) project, which helps schools to embed a whole school approach to emotional health and wellbeing and includes suicide prevention training for teachers and staff, the Text a Nurse confidential text messaging service and the post-primary counselling service.  Implementation of the framework also includes the roll out of the Public Health Agency’s (PHA) Mental Health First Aider training to school and Education Authority staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider opening a Community Crisis Intervention Service in East Derry to support those experiencing suicidal ideation and mental health crisis.
answer_text: Implementing the new regional crisis service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, under actions 12 and 27. The Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency are working in partnership to deliver on both of these actions, to develop a Regional Mental Health Crisis Service for Northern Ireland.The components of the proposed regional mental health crisis service are:  accessing mental health crisis services, primary care and interagency partnership, community crisis service, mental health liaison service and crisis resolution home treatment. A mapping report of current crisis services including community and voluntary sector is currently been finalised. Phase 2 future state work has commenced across lifespan services including scoping what a good service would look like. A Regional Mental Health Crisis Service workshop took place on the 8th March 2024 with relevant stakeholders across lifespan services. The Workshop outcomes will help progress the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service, including pathways and access to crisis emergency mental health.My Department officials continue to engage with stakeholders to ascertain their current pathways and a subsequent workshop is planned for October 2024. Furthermore, a Cross Departmental Coordinated Action Group was established in October 2023 by the Mental Health Champion to consider crisis services and suicide prevention in Derry/Londonderry. The purpose of the Group is to develop actions to address suicide prevention, along with other relevant issues, at the river and bridges in Derry/Londonderry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will work with the Education Authority to allow Destined, a group based in Feeny, to use the Education Authority Claudy Youth Club facilities.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that it does not own the building in which Claudy Youth Club operates, rather the premises is rented from an external provider. On this basis the Education Authority would not be in a position to grant permission for external groups to use the premises.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will consider funding the Destined Five Circles Project, which aims to reduce levels of isolation and exclusion experienced by children with learning disabilities and their families.
answer_text: My Department provides funding to local Councils through the Community Support Programme for community development activity aligned to local need. Destined Ltd may wish to engage with the Community Development Team in Derry City & Strabane District Council to identify any potential funding source for this project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider the utilisation of at home cervical smear tests in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), which advises Ministers and health services in the four nations on screening matters, has not made a recommendation on the use of HPV self-sampling for cervical screening.A number of research studies including the “Youscreen,” trial and “HPValidate,” study have been completed and are due to be published. These publications will help inform the evidence that the UK NSC will consider in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current (i) medical support; and (ii) guidance available for people diagnosed with body dysmorphia.
answer_text: Body Dysmorphic Disorder (BDD) is considered an anxiety spectrum disorder.  It is often co-morbid with other mental health conditions such as depression or more severe anxiety.A person presenting with symptoms of BDD should initially be assessed by their GP and mild cases should be managed in Primary Care with only more severe cases or those with co-morbid mental illness being referred to secondary care.Under the General Medical Services contract GP practices must provide services for management of their patients who are, or believe themselves to be ill and this would include mental health conditions such as Body Dysmorphic Disorder.‘Management’ includes:offering consultation and, where appropriate, physical examination for the purpose of identifying the need, if any, for treatment or further investigation; andthe making available of such treatment or further investigation as is necessary and appropriate, including the referral of the patient for other services and liaison with other health care professionals involved in the patient’s treatment and care.NICE recommend 10 hours of individual or group therapy, followed by a further 10 hours if needed. The first line of treatment is Cognitive Behavioural Therapy.  This aims to help identify triggers for symptoms and teach ways to manage symptoms and avoid the compulsive checking behaviours. Therapy includes Exposure and Response Prevention which involves gradually facing situations that trigger the obsessional thoughts regarding appearance.If symptoms are more severe, antidepressant medication can be helpful.  Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs) such as Fluoxetine are the first line of medication treatment. As well as CBT and medication, patients will be provided with information about their diagnosis and signposted towards online support and information.  Services will liaise with psychiatry when required for further assessment and medication management.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the current education provision in schools on body dysmorphia.
answer_text: Within the curriculum, schools provide learning opportunities for pupils to develop and support their physical and emotional health and wellbeing.  The Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework is provided to enable our education settings to support their pupils to be empowered and assisted to understand and take care or manage their emotional health and wellbeing.  Supports are also available for pupils to access directly as part of Framework, such as the Text-a-Nurse service. Whilst schools ensure the safety of pupils at school and raise concerns as necessary, information and support on body dysmorphia and any other health conditions are informed by the Department of Health (DoH) and Public Health Agency.  My officials are working with DoH as it undertakes a review of the Healthy Child, Healthy Future Policy.  A key element of the review is to take account of the social and emotional wellbeing of children including those vulnerable to a range of associated disorders.  The review document will provide guidance to School/Public Health Nurses as well as being a valuable resource available to schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the meetings his Department has had (i) internally; and (ii) with Health and Social Care Trusts since February 2024 on improving support for (a) those with an ADHD diagnosis; and (b) access to assessments for those seeking an ADHD diagnosis.
answer_text: internally;There have been a number of internal Department of Health discussions in recent months to discuss the current position and next steps in relation to the provision of ADHD services, including between representatives from DoH Mental Health policy and the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG).  Those discussions have helped shape work that is underway to commission a research exercise in order to ensure a robust evidence base for future options for an ADHD service, subject to future budget availability.with Health and Social Care TrustsIn relation to adults, no formal meetings have taken place with Trusts regarding ADHD. However, due to ongoing requests for information about adult ADHD, the issue of provision for those with ADHD has been raised with Trusts. Adult ADHD has been included on the agenda for the August 2024 meeting of the Adult Mental Health Group, which consists of assistant directors from all Trusts.In relation to Children’s Services, DoH Children’s Commissioning lead for CAMHS and Emotional Health chairs a monthly meeting with each of the Trusts’ ADHD and Autism Heads of Service. There were meetings on April 10th, May 8th and June 12th. The March meeting was cancelled due to end of year planning and leave. These meetings cover both improving access to assessment pathways for ADHD and providing ongoing support for those with a diagnosis. The Children’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework (EHWB) Coordinator attended an online meeting on the 8th May by BHSCT Paediatric ADHD Head of Service and staff with a presentation on their Quality Improvement project relating to Nurse managed medication review clinics. A follow up in person meeting was attended by the CAMHS and Emotional Health Commissioner and the EHWB Coordinator and senior management and clinical staff from the paediatric ADHD service. Both of these meetings were directly related to improving post diagnosis support and improving the pathway for assessment. There have also been two regional meetings of the EHWB Steering Group, on the 8th April and 3rd June, co-chaired by the Department of Health and the Public Health Agency. These meetings included representatives from all Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline how Members can be better informed of the legal support available to them in relation to their role as an employer.
answer_text: As part of its HR Service to Members, the Assembly Commission has procured a Legal Expenses policy to cover legal costs and offer support to Members in the event of employment disputes. This policy provides protection against the risk of paying legal costs to defend against a range of legal disputes, including employment disputes. Access to a legal advice helpline and an online employment law guide and template documents are also available.  This policy is administered by the Finance Office.In terms of communication, the Assembly Commission has established a ‘‘Members Portal’’ via AssISt, which is an easy reference point for Members to understand the wide range of services, including HR services, that are provided by the Assembly Commission.  From time to time, Briefing Notes are issued by the Assembly Commission to provide further guidance and assistance on a range of matters.  Briefing notes 26, 28 and 34 provide Members with information on the provision of HR Services to Members, along with details of the Legal Expenses policy and how to utilise it.   These are published on the Members Portal. The Finance Office is currently working with the providers of the Legal Expenses policy to see what other options are available to improve communication about the level of support available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will work with Translink on developing an hourly train service on a Sunday from Derry to Coleraine.
answer_text: I fully recognise the need to address regional imbalance and better connect our communities including those within the Northwest and this year I have therefore ring-fenced funding to ensure the Coleraine to Derry Phase 3 project proceeds.You will also be aware that my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government, and currently there is no scope to increase services of this scale without securing additional funding. I can assure you however that the value of our public transport services cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on any discussions he has had on reducing speeding in (i) Feeny; and (ii) Drumsurn, Londonderry.
answer_text: My department has developed a priority traffic scheme for Feeny which is currently going through the legislation process and is programmed to be delivered later this financial year.Departmental officials are currently considering what further engineering enhancements would help address the traffic issues in Drumsurn. Any proposals will be agreed with the PSNI Roads Policing Unit prior to implementation.Meanwhile, concerns about driver behaviour such as speeding or other inconsiderate behaviour should be directed to the PSNI on their non-emergency 101 telephone number.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the current frequency of buses available on the Limavady to Derry route.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:“Translink operate Goldliner 234 and Ulsterbus 143, 144 and 152 services between Limavady and Derry~Londonderry. Monday to Friday this provides a combined service on or around an hourly timetable, with services commencing between 0600-0700 and last services departing between 2000-2100. Saturday and Sunday service provision is reduced proportionately.Currently there is sufficient capacity to cater for passenger demand on the route and we have no immediate plans to alter the services.  We regularly review passenger feedback, in conjunction with wider stakeholder engagement and regular analysis of passenger numbers, meaning the current timetable is subject to periodic review.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department is assessing the impact of the level of investment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 on people in the (i) Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Department is committed to ensuring that investment in the Mental Health Strategy is delivering meaningful improvements for people across all Health and Social Care Trusts, including the Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT) and the Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT).A key mechanism for assessing this impact, and the impact of mental health services generally, is the Regional Mental Health Outcomes Framework (MHOF), which is being developed and implemented on a Northern Ireland wide basis.
