The first phase of implementing the MHOF is progressing well, with work centred on embedding the framework within Encompass, the new regional digital patient record system, and developing the capacity to report robust regional mental health service activity data. All Trusts have completed mapping their services to a common regional template, and the Encompass Team is now developing mental health activity and performance reports, with October 2026 targeted for completion. The MHOF Lead has now commenced post and is overseeing delivery of this first implementation phase.Further, as part of the Regional Mental Health Service, a business unit will be responsible for the development and provision of a regional system for data capture, analysis and presentation on mental health service, performance, quality management, patient safety and service outcomes.These tools and approaches will provide data on mental health service activity, performance, and outcomes which can then be used to assess the impact of mental health services and the service improvements delivered as a result of the Mental Health Strategy at both a Trust and regional level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the actions his Department is taking to stop the decline of seabird populations in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is finalising the draft Seabird Conservation Strategy for Northern Ireland which outlines proposed objectives and actions required to improve the conservation status of our seabird species that are currently in decline.  I intend to consult on this draft Strategy during autumn 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the engagement his Department has had; and (ii) the actions his Department has taken in relation to protecting marine ecosystems since February 2024.
answer_text: During this period, my Department has engaged with stakeholders with an interest in Strangford Lough and Rathlin Island Marine Protected Areas and met with the Northern Ireland Marine Task Force, a coalition of non-government environmental organisations, to discuss a range of marine environment issues. My Department has also engaged with stakeholders to develop three Fisheries Managements Plans for Irish Sea pelagic, Irish Sea demersal and Northern Ireland inshore stocks.  These plans will set out measures to ensure the long-term sustainability of our fish stocks for current and future generations, whilst protecting the marine environment.In April 2024 I launched public consultations on Northern Ireland’s first Blue Carbon Action Plan and a reviewed Marine Protected Areas Strategy for the Northern Ireland Inshore Region. Both measures were developed following extensive engagement with key stakeholders as part of a facilitated co-design process. Stakeholders included fishing industry representatives, environmental NGOs, local councils, Ulster Farmer’s Union, public bodies, Republic of Ireland officials and other Devolved Administrations. The objectives and proposed actions outlined in the Blue Carbon Action Plan and reviewed Marine Protected Areas Strategy have been designed to preserve and enhance marine features and habitats, providing protections for their dependent ecosystems. My officials have been finalising the draft Seabird and Elasmobranch Conservation Strategies for Northern Ireland. The Strategies will outline the proposed objectives and actions required to improve the conservation status of our seabird and elasmobranch species. I intend to consult on both strategies in the Autumn of this year. My Department’s marine scientists and fisheries officers continue to maintain their programme of monitoring, assessment, regulation, inspection and enforcement, ensuring that current management measures are providing effective protection for marine ecosystems.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for the average time taken in Crown Court cases, from offence reported to case dealt with at court, for sexual offences against children in (i) 2022-23; and (ii) 2023-24.
answer_text: Provisional figures indicate that the median time taken from when an offence is reported until the case is dealt with at Crown Court was 919 days for cases dealt with in 2023/24, where the main offence at disposal was a sexual offence involving a child. The equivalent figure for cases dealt with in 2022/23 was 896 days.  Note:1. Figures relate to the number of calendar days between the dates listed.  Court recess dates are not taken into account.2. Figures relate to cases disposed at Crown Court during the time period specified. The period reported is a financial year, running from 1st April to the following 31st March.3. Figures relate to cases brought on behalf of the PSNI, Harbour and Airport police in Northern Ireland that resulted in a court disposal.  Appeals are not included.4. Figures relate to case where the principal offence was a sexual offence that specified that the victim was a child in the offence description.5. Figures for 2023/24 are provisional.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will bring forward legislation to enable councils to hold remote or hybrid meetings.
answer_text: The Local Government (Remote Meetings) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2024 were laid in draft in the Assembly on 7 June 2024.The Regulations are subject to the draft affirmative procedure in the Assembly.   I will shortly table a motion to have the Regulations approved by the Assembly.Subject to Assembly approval, it is my intention to have these Regulations in place by Assembly summer recess.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to reverse his Department’s decision to introduce a five hectare requirement for farms to access payments through the new Farm Sustainability Transition Payment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what further reforms are being considered to support rape victims during a trial.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of how the current reporting methods for animal cruelty in Northern Ireland can be improved.
answer_text: Three separate bodies are responsible for the enforcement of animal welfare legislation: my Department, as it applies to farmed animals, councils for non-farmed animals, and the PSNI in respect of wild animals, animal fighting, or where other criminal activity is involved. Information on how to report concerns about an animal’s welfare, including the contact details of the appropriate enforcement body, can be found on NI Direct, as well as the respective websites of my Department, councils, and the PSNI. Enforcement statistics are set out in the yearly animal welfare reports. If you have any information which would support the need for amending the current reporting mechanisms, I am happy to consider how this could be taken forward.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the Strategic Framework Ending Violence Against Women and Girls will include a strategy on tackling domestic violence in Traveller communities.
answer_text: Violence against women and girls occurs across all parts of our society. We recognise that some women and girls may face particular risks to their safety, and additional barriers to justice, services and support. The draft Strategic Framework takes an inclusive and intersectional approach to ending violence against women and girls. This includes those from minority ethnic groups such as Traveller communities. The Framework will be evidence-based, ensuring that lived experience is a central thread.Development of a new Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, which is led by the Department of Health and the Department of Justice, is in its final stages with a view to publication in coming months. The draft strategy recognises that anyone can be a victim of domestic and/or sexual abuse regardless of their age, ethnicity, gender, gender identity, sexual orientation or background. Further work will progress under this strategy to better understand the intersectional needs of victims, including victims in Traveller communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department's actions to (i) support Traveller children who experience prejudice in school; and (ii) support Traveller children in overcoming barriers to educational attainment.
answer_text: All schools are required to have a policy in place which promotes positive behaviour among pupils in order to safeguard their welfare and facilitate their educational progress at school. The Education Authority’s  Behaviour Support Service supports schools in relation to behaviour and provides capacity building training to help staff address the needs of all young people in their school.		The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (the Act) requires schools to record the motivation and the outcome of incidents of bullying, including racist bullying. The legislation provides an inclusive list of the types of motivations a school would be required to record. This reflects relevant Section 75 groups as well as some additional common motivating factors.  Capturing the motivation for a bullying incident is important. It will identify any specific problems, such as racist bullying, where the school could benefit from specialist expertise. The guidance to support the Act, which provides case studies, assists schools, governors, parents and pupils in this process. The Department funds the Education Authority’s (EA’s) Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) to support schools to successfully implement the Act and to provide parents/carers and young people with support to deal with incidents of bullying. The Education Authority’s Intercultural Education Service (IES) also provides a range of supports and services for Traveller and Roma children and their families (further details provided under ii)).ii) The Department’s Traveller Child In Education Action Framework was published in 2013, in response to a 2011 report by the Taskforce on Traveller Education, which had been established to assist the Department to develop an action plan on Traveller education.  The framework outlines commitments to Traveller children and the steps to be taken to ensure that every traveller child has full access to education. It details what actions are in place:	To ensure information is provided to Traveller families detailing how to avail of all educational provisions and opportunities;	To help improve and maintain school attendance for Traveller children;	To ensure Traveller parents are aware of the role they play in their children getting the required level of education they deserve;	To help schools develop an inclusive and welcoming environment for Traveller children; and	To provide education on Traveller culture in schools to eradicate the potential for prejudice and hate crimes. The Department is now commencing a review of the Traveller in Education Framework.  The aim of this review is to: consider the effectiveness of the current framework and associated educational services for Traveller children; explore existing funding arrangements for Travellers in education; and develop and agree a revised forward-looking policy framework, which delivers the best outcomes for Traveller children and young people and contributes to achieving t

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will commit to undertaking research with the Department of Education on links between suicide and bullying while in school.
answer_text: While new research is not planned in this area, it is recognised that adverse childhood experiences which includes bullying is a risk factor which can contribute to suicidal ideation and self-harm for young people. School is a key environment that shapes young people’s mental health in which early intervention and prevention needs to happen. The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (NI) 2016 came into operation on 1 September 2021. The Act provides a common definition of bullying; established a new duty for schools to record all incidents of bullying, their motivation and their outcome; and requires the school Board of Governors to take responsibility for the development, implementation, monitoring and periodic review of the school’s anti-bullying policies. We will continue to work with other Departments to reduce all risk factors for suicide including bullying.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration is being given to the needs of deaf children as part of the development of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: My Written Ministerial Statement on 23 May 2024 sets out my plans for early learning and childcare measures during 2024/25. These include support for children with special educational needs and disabilities, the detail of which will be informed by further stakeholder engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) what oversight is in place to monitor the work of Boards of Governors; and (ii) any issues his Department is currently aware of in relation to the monitoring of Boards of Governors.
answer_text: Governors are expected to work together to fulfil the board of governors statutory functions in relation to the school. The board of governors is accountable for ensuring that its decisions support the best interests of the school and its pupils. The Department does not have an official role in monitoring the work of Boards of Governors. The Education and Training Inspectorate may evaluate the quality of the strategic and corporate work of the Board of Governors during inspections.  If the inspection identifies that the school is failing to give its pupils an acceptable standard of education and that failure is due (in whole or in part) to the management of the school by the Board of Governors, ETI can recommend that the Department exercises its powers under Article 14 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 and appoint additional governors to the Board of Governors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients with eating disorders that have had to receive medical support outside Northern Ireland in the last five years.
answer_text: A total of 14 patients with a primary diagnosis of eating disorder were funded by HSC to access inpatient treatment outside of Northern Ireland over the five year period 2019/20 to 2023/24.  The DOH must be mindful of its duty to protect the privacy of its patients. Given the very small number of patients travelling each year to access treatment from highly specialist eating disorder facilities, to provide further breakdown has the potential to identify an individual and impact on their privacy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the work his Department has undertaken with the PSNI in relation to car cruises in the East Derry constituency; and (ii) the impact car cruises have on (a) the general public; and (b) roads.
answer_text: Since April 2022, the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) assisted the PSNI to carry out six enforcement operations in the East Derry constituency aimed at addressing road safety concerns associated with car cruise events.These operations were focussed on targeting unroadworthy vehicles used in connection with car cruise events in the Portrush, Portstewart and Coleraine areas during which a total of 145 modified cars were inspected by DVA examiners. Of these, 134 vehicles (92%) were subjected to prohibition action due to breaches of the Construction & Use Regulations. Unroadworthy and illegally modified vehicles not only compromise road safety but can often create a public nuisance as a result of excessive noise and smoke pollution.  DVA will continue to provide technical support to PSNI to help address the problem of unroadworthy and nuisance modified cars used on our roads.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work his Department has carried out on improving (i) diagnosis times; and (ii) timely medical intervention, for women diagnosed with polycystic ovary syndrome.
answer_text: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a common condition that affects how a woman's ovaries work, and the journey to diagnose usually starts with the GP, who will perform a number of checks and tests, then if required the woman will be referred for a transvaginal ultrasound scan.  At this point the treatment options would be correction of abnormal thyroid function with thyroxine, a reduction in BMI (sometimes using metformin) or treatment of diabetes and they would consider hormone therapy ie. mini pill or some form of contraception, if appropriate. This journey for diagnosis is necessary to ensure that the woman is treated appropriately for her condition and symptoms.Covid, and general overarching pressures within the Gynaecology Service in Northern Ireland have delayed the development of many Gynaecology Services. As you may be aware, my Department recently launched the Women’s Health Strategy for Northern Ireland as well as commissioning the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) team to undertake an independent, external rapid review of gynaecology services in NI. The GIRFT report, Gynaecology across Northern Ireland, was published on 31 January 2024. It identifies 20 regional recommendations, designed to improve patient care and tackle waiting times for Gynaecology conditions. The Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trust, PHA and GP representation, to oversee the ongoing service improvement and the implementation at a local and regional level. This will include considering the clinical pathways of women for assessment, diagnosis, treatment, and ongoing management for conditions such as PCOS and well as overseeing the wider implementation of the GIRFT gynae report. It should be noted that the current financial position remains very challenging and as such not all of the service improvement officials identify may be able to proceed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the cost of train travel from Derry City to Coleraine University.
answer_text: Translink offers a variety of ticketing options depending on the type and time of travel that aim to provide value for money. Further information on the range of fares can be obtained from their website: www.translink.co.uk/usingtranslink/ticketsandtravelcards

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has considered extending the 24+ Student Railcard to part-time students.
answer_text: The 24+ Student Railcard is one element of Translink fare’s policy that sets fares at a level so that public transport remains a reasonable option for all members of society while generating sufficient income to facilitate the delivery of a range of bus and rail services. Translink offer a range of discounted products to assist passengers to find the best value for their journey. Any plans to extend the 24+ Student Railcard would be an operational decision for Translink.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why the 24+ Student Railcard does not apply to part-time students.
answer_text: The 24+ Student Railcard is one element of Translink fare’s policy that sets fares at a level so that public transport remains a reasonable option for all members of society while generating sufficient income to facilitate the delivery a range of bus and rail services. Translink offer a range of discounted products to assist passengers, including part-time students, to find the best value for their journey.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail whether her Department plans to replicate and implement any policies from the Republic of Ireland to improve support for rape victims.
answer_text: The 2019 Gillen Review and the 2020 O’Malley Review set out many similarly themed recommendations which aim to improve support for rape victims in each jurisdiction respectively. I engage regularly with my counterpart in the Irish Government, Minister McEntee TD, including on these issues, under the auspices of the Inter-Governmental Agreement on Co-operation on Criminal Justice Matters (IGA). My officials are also engaged positively through quarterly meetings with Irish Government officials via the IGA “Support for Victims” Project Advisory Group (PAG) in order to share information and best practice and inform the development of policy to improve support for victims, including victims of serious sexual offences. Under the current IGA Work Programme officials from both jurisdictions plan to co-host a joint seminar later this year with the aim of sharing experiences and emerging learning from the pilots, projects and initiatives that have been progressed under both the Gillen and O’Malley Reviews in order to improve the experiences of and support for victims of serious sexual offences in our respective criminal justice systems.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the infrastructure projects his Department plans to progress in the East Derry constituency during the current mandate.
answer_text: Having decided on indicative budget allocations (subject to a consultation on the equality impacts) my officials can now consider the detailed programmes of work across the Department’s areas of responsibility, including plans for infrastructure projects in the East Derry constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on any traffic calming measures his Department plans to implement in Drumsurn, Derry.
answer_text: Departmental officials have agreed to upgrade current signage to provide more prominent gateway signage which will include red high friction surfacing with the speed limit (30MPH) painted on it to make drivers more visually aware they are entering a 30mph speed limit.  Concerns about driver behaviour such as speeding or other inconsiderate behaviour should be directed to the PSNI on their non-emergency 101 telephone number.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount recouped from primary carers of children and young people with complex needs as a result of unspent direct payments in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Department does not hold this information centrally, and each HSC Trust provided the following figures on request. Northern HSC Trust reported that their format of analysis changed for 2023/24. As such, they do not hold the figures for previous years centrally and a substantial manual exercise would be required to provide them.Total unspent Direct Payments recouped (£)HSC Trust2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24Belfast        2,622       6,195       2,510       2,222     40,011 NorthernN/AN/AN/AN/A    61,950 South Eastern        3,000     12,965     29,892     48,961   128,637 Southern      10,462     27,913       4,029     11,310     28,591 Western        4,530     31,226     61,698   119,174     90,667

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether an independent evaluation of the effectiveness of direct payments to families with a child with complex needs has been conducted since their introduction; and (ii) whether he will consider commissioning such an evaluation.
answer_text: (i) The Department of Health is currently in the process of finalising The Children With Disability Framework in response to increasing challenges faced by families of Children With Disability, and the corresponding difficulties experienced by Trusts in meeting rising demands and increased complexity of need. There has not yet been an independent evaluation of Direct Payments. However, one of the recommendations that is included within The Children’s Disability Framework is that a Task and Finish group should be established to explore the issues in the use of Direct Payments, and to make recommendations for any necessary change. It is acknowledged within the draft Children’s Disability Framework that there is a need to consider how families can be supported to make the most effective use of direct payments. (ii) As outlined above, the Department of Health recognises the need to review Direct Payments, and it is anticipated that a review will occur in the near future following finalisation of The Children’s Disability Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department is taking to address the current shortage in ADHD medication.
answer_text: While supply disruptions of some attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) treatments have now been resolved, there continues to be ongoing disruptions across the UK affecting various preparations and strengths of these medicines due to global manufacturing issues and an increase in demand for these products at national and global level.The Department of Health and Social Care leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK, working closely with the devolved administrations, including my Department, and other operators in the supply chain to put in place mitigations should any disruptions arise. My Department has taken several steps to help mitigate the impact the ongoing supply disruptions of ADHD medicines is having on patients in Northern Ireland (NI).Guidance has been issued to healthcare professionals, initially via a National Patient Safety Alert issued by my Department on 28 September 2023. As the supply situation has subsequently resolved for some strengths and preparations of products, updated advice has been provided via several Medicines Supply Notifications. Both the National Patient Safety Alert and the Medicines Supply Notifications are reflective of messaging agreed by the UK administrations.A list of currently available and unavailable ADHD products has been published to assist healthcare professionals prescribing these medications on the NHS Specialist Pharmacy Service’s (SPS) website-https://www.sps.nhs.uk/articles/prescribing-available-medicines-to-treat-adhd/. Healthcare professionals can also register with SPS to access SPS medicine supply tool for information on shortages and resupply dates of affected products. These resources are updated regularly by DHSC, and my Department has encouraged healthcare professionals to use these resources.In terms of an additional local measure, Intuniv® (guanfacine) 4mg modified release tablets were added to the NI MHRA Authorised Route (NIMAR) list on 28 March 2024, which permitted the movement of Great Britain (GB) authorised stock into NI to meet patients’ clinical needs. This product was subsequently removed from the NIMAR list on 10 May 2024 following improvements in the availability of NI compliant stock. It is important to note that until Windsor Framework arrangements are implemented from 1 January 2025, NIMAR can be used when authorised stock is available in GB and there is no compliant authorised stock available in NI to meet patients’ clinical needs. If required, my Department will help to identify and assess any products that may require addition to the NIMAR list and work with DHSC to ensure patients’ clinical needs in NI are met.These are the steps that have been taken by my Department since my officials became aware of supply disruptions to ADHD medicines in September 2023. Healthcare professionals should follow the guidance that has been issued as they provide them with actions to take to ensure patients can have access to ADHD treatments. Additional measur

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when conversion therapy will be banned.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the steps his Department has taken to provide anaesthetic for victims of sexual trauma who require a cervical smear test; and (ii) the stakeholders his Department has engaged with on this matter in the last five years.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency has advised where an individual has particular needs relating to cervical screening that cannot be met within the GP practice, the GP has the ability to refer that patient to a Trust gynaecology service where additional support and alternative options may be considered. This is delivered on an individual patient basis. All women are offered cervical screening and colposcopy after full informed consent has been obtained and are treated with the utmost respect and sensitivity.In November 2023, the PHA delivered updated training to GPs and practice nurses who take cervical screening samples (‘smears’). This included a session provided by Jo’s Trust which incorporated dealing sensitively with patients who may find cervical screening distressing including those who are victims of sexual trauma.Anaesthetic for victims of sexual trauma at smear tests was not raised at either a Call for Views exercise, or as part of the consultation process, of the new joint Department of Health and Department of Justice’s draft “Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department has taken to provide home smear test kits, in the last five years.
answer_text: The UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), which advises the four nations on screening matters, made a recommendation in June 2025, that self-sampling for HPV testing should be offered to eligible under-screened people, as part of the Cervical Screening Programme.The Northern Ireland Screening Committee is due to consider the UK NSC recommendation before putting policy advice to me for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work carried out by his Department in identifying cross-border work on children’s hospice care.
answer_text: Departmental Officials have not had any discussion around cross border working in relation to children’s hospice care but are open to exploring opportunities. The NICH advised that they have had initial conversations through the All Ireland Institute of Hospice and Palliative Care about cross border working as part of the Children Palliative Care Strategy. The NICH have advised they are considering how best to meet the needs of children and families and utilise resources across the Island of Ireland to provide the best quality of care possible for children who require Hospice Care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current number of Community Children’s Nurses.
answer_text: Information provided by Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts on the number of community children’s registered nurses employed (at April 2024) is detailed in the table below.HSC TrustCommunity Children's Registered Nurses employed (whole-time equivalent)Belfast21.55Northern23.99South Eastern16.80Southern22.77Western17.10Total102.14The Department will continue to engage with HSC Trusts to identify pressures and explore mechanisms which can support service delivery more effectively including that of appropriate staffing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of nightclubs that have closed in the last ten years; and (ii) any measures he is putting in place to create a vibrant and thriving night-time economy.
answer_text: My department does not hold specific information regarding the number of nightclub closures in the past ten years. I do understand the importance of a thriving night-time economy to our hospitality sector, our tourism industry and our wider high street economy with approximately 57,000 jobs in the accommodation and food service sectors.  Whilst we are constrained by the challenging budget we have available there is still much we can do to assist these vital sectors.Tourism NI’s destination marketing campaigns support a vibrant night- time economy by bringing in visitors who are likely to visit bars, restaurants etc and provide additional support to this sector throughout the year. This includes a suite of advisory supports to help tourism and hospitality businesses reduce costs, improve productivity and be more sustainable, including Leaner and Greener, and Innovate Tourism.The level of VAT is also a specific issue for the hospitality sector, and I know that the Finance Minister has repeatedly raised with the Treasury the negative impacts on hospitality of high levels of VAT.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to reduce services at Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: Causeway Hospital is and will remain an important part of our acute hospital network. The Northern Health and Social Care Trust remains committed to maintaining acute inpatient services and a 24/7 Emergency Department at  the Causeway Hospital. The Trust are eager to explore options to further develop the Causeway site. I am assured that Northern Trust have been engaging with staff and other stakeholders as they develop a vision for the future of the hospital.  The Trust have recently launched their vision for Causeway Hospital.Transformation is needed in how we deliver services across the health and social care system as a whole, and work is currently underway to develop a regional blueprint in relation to Northern Ireland’s hospital network. I believe there is an opportunity for Causeway Hospital to be developed to better serve the changing needs of the local population, and enhance the site, growing same day emergency care, elective and cancer services.  Causeway can be an exemplar model of how to do joined-up, integrated care, and is perfectly positioned to become an elective and diagnostic hub for the North West.   I understand Northern Trust is also taking the opportunity to reshape mental health provision to better meet the needs of their population.Causeway Hospital has a bright future ahead, demonstrated by the continued investment in services there. In the past year alone, there has been investment of £1.8m into urgent and ambulatory care, £1.2m into solar energy, and £1m in the form of a new state-of-the-art CT scanner.  The Trust also expect to add an MRI scanner to this list of available diagnostic services. By focusing on the right services, attracting investment and building workforce, the Trust is confident that Causeway Hospital can continue to thrive and serve the local population for many years to come.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how much additional funding her Department has allocated to the PSNI Cyber Crime Centre in the last three years.
answer_text: The PSNI Cyber Crime Centre maintains a specialist capability which is focussed on tackling the increasing threat posed by cyber criminals.  A team of dedicated and experienced investigators is co-located with and supported by digital forensic experts operating from the purpose built Centre which opened in June 2018. In 2017, as a result of a successful bid made by PSNI and my department, PSNI received £0.94m funding under the Cabinet Office’s National Cyber Security Programme fund.   The funding was used to enhance PSNI’s overall capacity and capability in responding to cybercrime and while work on the new Cyber Crime Centre was at an advanced stage, some of the funding was used to improve the accessibility of the centre’s resources to both frontline officers and national partners.  Further funding was provided to PSNI, through the National Cyber Security Programme fund, of £54,000 in 2021-22 and £173,000 in 2022-23.The Cyber Crime Centre works with both internal and external partners, on a local, national and international basis. As part of the Team Cyber UK network, liaison and partnership working helps police gain maximum benefit in progressing complex and challenging investigations.More generally, decisions relating to the deployment of resources to the PSNI’s Cyber Crime Centre are an operational matter for the Chief Constable.  My Department has not provided any specific funding to the PSNI Cyber Crime Centre in the last three years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on an MRI scanner for Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: The Northern Health and Social Care Trust has confirmed that preparation work for the installation of an MRI scanner at Causeway Hospital is ongoing and it is anticipated the scanner will be operational by February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work his Department has carried out on improving ADHD support services since taking up office in February 2024.
answer_text: Department of Health officials recognise that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years. My officials are currently considering the commissioning of ADHD services, in discussion with relevant stakeholders. Subject to future budget availability, potential next steps include the commissioning of research/analysis of adult ADHD demand to inform options for future commissioning strategies, as well as a regional workshop to explore potential ways forward. My officials have recently completed an assessment of paediatric ASD and ADHD pathways. This work has identified a number of inconsistencies and gaps across the region. The development of consistent pathways will be taken forward through the Community Children’s Social Care Group (Children’s Disability MH/ASD/ND) jointly chaired by SPPG and PHA.  Pathways will take a broader neurodevelopmental approach, incorporating a regionally consistent Children and Young Peoples Referral Service. This will allow earlier intervention needs to be assessed and define capacity requirements. ASD and ADHD diagnostic pathways and interventions will be addressed through the implementation of the HSC Children’s EHWB Framework. Any decision to commission ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what changes he plans to make to animal welfare legislation by the end of this Assembly mandate.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the calls for reform of animal welfare in Northern Ireland and I will be considering options in the time ahead, to determine what is needed, and what is deliverable, within the remainder of this mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what steps his Department will take to revise the SmartPass application process to make it more accessible for individuals with epilepsy; and (ii) whether his Department will implement an alternative verification system that bypasses the need for a driving licence application, including the use of digital tools or medical certifications to streamline the process.
answer_text: My Department recently consulted on options to review the eligibility for the NI Concessionary Fares Scheme. As part of that consultation, some stakeholders took the opportunity to raise wider issues with the Department, including concerns regarding the SmartPass application process for people with epilepsy. I am currently considering the outcome of the public consultation and will publish my response shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the (i) number of prosecutions; and (ii) length of prison sentence for those who are perpetrators of animal cruelty in the last five years.
answer_text: Information on the number of defendants dealt with at courts, where there was a prosecution or conviction for at least one offence under animal welfare legislation, has been provided for the period 2018 – 2022, the most recent five year period for which information is available.Custodial sentences were imposed in relation to 33 of the cases where there was a conviction.  The lengths of sentences imposed ranged from 1 month custody to 15 months in custody with 15 months on licence.Prosecutions and convictions at court for animal welfare offences, 2018 – 2022YearProsecutionsConvictionsConvictions resulting in a custodial sentence2018                   97                    68                              5 2019                   72                    51                              5 2020                   62                    44                              7 2021                   75                    62                              7 2022                   72                    49                              9 Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposal at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases dealt with at Crown Court and magistrates' courts.3. Figures relate to cases where at least one offence was prosecuted under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985 and other relevant animal welfare legislation.4. Figures relate to cases prosecuted by PPS on behalf of police and other public bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the funding available from his Department to (i) assist people with a disability to access swimming pools; and (ii) upgrade swimming pools to improve access for people with a disability.
answer_text: At this time, neither the Department for Communities nor Sport NI, have any funding opportunities available to assist people with a disability to access swimming pools.My Department works in partnership annually with the disability sector, DAERA, local Councils and the Northern Ireland Museums Council (NIMC) to deliver the Access and Inclusion programme – which aims to improve accessibility at arts, cultural and active recreation venues for people with disabilities.  Local Councils can put forward proposals to enhance their own venues, including swimming pools.To date, the Department has invested more than £6 million in over 300 projects for upgrades including sensory rooms and gardens, accessible doors, accessible play park equipment, changing place facilities and adaption to swimming pools.  The 2023-24 programme for example included an award of up to £30,000 for a Poolpod at Dungannon Leisure Centre.In relation to future funding of the Access and Inclusion programme I am currently working with my officials to understand the implications of the recent Budget announcement. However, it is clear that the capital funding position for my Department in 2024-25 will be a significant challenge.  I can also advise that previously my Department and Sport NI, an arm’s length body of the Department, provided £400,000 under the Active Living No Limits Action Plan (2016-2021) for the installation of thirteen Poolpod platform lifts at leisure centres in each of the Council areas across Northern Ireland. These Poolpods have transformed access to swimming pools for people with a disability or restricted mobility.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the funding his Department can provide swimming clubs that are located on a school campus.
answer_text: Swimming pools located within the school estate are a responsibility for the Department of Education, as physical education is a compulsory part of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.Any school that would wish to make its swimming pool available to its local community may be eligible to apply for funding through the Your School Your Club initiative which aims to open up access to school sports facilities for community use.Your School Your Club is a collaborative initiative between my Department, Sport NI (my Department’s arm’s length body), Department of Education, Education Authority, Council for Catholic Maintained Schools alongside the Department for Agriculture, the Environment and Rural Affair and local councils.   This initiative is dependent on availability of budget this year – which is yet to be confirmed, however, any schools interested should engage with their local Council and Sport NI in the first instance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the funding his Department has provided to assist children with autism in learning to swim in the last five years.
answer_text: Schools receive delegated budgets through the Common Funding Scheme to enable Boards of Governors and Principals to use resources in accordance with individual schools’ needs and priorities, including effective delivery of the curriculum.Given that swimming is a statutory element of the Physical Education curriculum at Key Stage 2 (years 5-7) it is for schools to determine how much of their funding is attributed to swimming lessons in terms of curriculum delivery. Therefore neither my Department nor the Education Authority would have detail on the amount of funding allocated by schools to assist children with autism in learning to swim in the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the plans his Department has to support arable farmers, specifically in adopting sustainable practices such as agroforestry and advanced field management; and (ii) the (a) incentives; and (b) support available to encourage this shift.
answer_text: Within the new DAERA Farm Support and Development Programme, the Farming for Carbon measure seeks to identify and support the adoption of sustainable practices to reduce greenhouse gas emissions on farm and improve carbon sequestration. Agro-forestry, on both arable and livestock farms, can play a role and opportunities to support and incentivise farmers to adopt such practices are being explored within the Farming with Nature Package.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given that the arable sector can significantly influence carbon sequestration and emission reduction, what strategies his Department is considering to enhance productivity and environmental health in arable farming, particularly through the use of improved seed varieties and innovative farming techniques.
answer_text: The new Farm Support and Development Programme is designed to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and nutrient loss through farm productivity improvements, which will deliver better environmental sustainability.  Schemes to facilitate ecosystem restoration, and to address biodiversity loss, and water and air quality (especially reducing emissions) are under development and will support all farm businesses, including the arable sector, to continue to produce high quality, nutritious food whilst importantly, also reducing their environmental impact. Innovation, such as the use of improved seed varieties and innovative farming techniques, is vital.  The new Knowledge Transfer and Innovation measure will support changes in on-farm management practices and the Capital Investment measure will assist the adoption of precision technology and equipment to reduce emissions, nutrient loss and improve business efficiency.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how his Department will ensure that arable farmers receive a fair and sufficient portion of the Farming for Nature Package, to effectively contribute to Northern Ireland’s climate goals.
answer_text: The Farm Support and Development Programme is a broad and balanced Programme designed to encourage and support changes in behaviour to deliver better outcomes across a range of defined metrics. In particular, the schemes and measures being introduced will provide levers to contribute to statutory obligations under the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022, with a firm focus on just transition. The Farming with Nature (FwN) Package will be open to all land managers with three hectares or more of eligible land who meet the scheme requirements, including arable farmers.This Package of Schemes will, as far as possible, seek to recognise and reward the public goods provided by arable farmers and other farm businesses through the delivery of improved environmental outcomes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given the importance of timing in the application of slurry and fertilisers for optimising crop yield and minimising environmental impact, (i) to provide further details on the project evaluating the optimal timing for these applications; and (ii) how the findings will be (a) communicated to; and (b) implemented by arable farmers.
answer_text: (i) A research project entitled “Evaluating the impact of a range of organic manures applied to arable land on soil, crop and Northern Ireland agriculture” has been undertaken by the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI). The overall objective is to improve the sustainability of NI agriculture by optimising the value of arable land through the application of organic manures.(ii) (a) The emerging findings of the project have been widely disseminated through a range of knowledge transfer meetings, conferences and reports. The final findings, including evaluations, will be presented at forthcoming AFBI Open Days and also published in a booklet and in peer-reviewed scientific publications.(ii) (b) The regular dissemination of emerging findings as the project progresses has provided farmers with information to assist them in making decisions on the use and management of organic manures on arable crops.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in relation to answering Assembly Questions, to detail (i) matters that relate to her official responsibility; and (ii) justice-related matters that do not come under her official responsibility.
answer_text: I am responsible for responding to Assembly Questions that relate to policing and justice functions devolved to the Department of Justice as set out in the Northern Ireland Act 1998 (Devolution of Policing and Justice Functions) Order 2010. However, the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), which is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board, and the Office of the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland are operationally independent of the Department of Justice.  As I respect the operational independence of these bodies, where an Assembly Question relates in its entirety to operational issues for the PSNI or the OPONI, Members are advised to contact the relevant organisation directly.   Furthermore, although the Public Prosecution Service (PPS) is an important part of the wider justice system, it is independent of my Department.  Members are therefore advised to contact the PPS directly if an Assembly Question relates to the responsibilities of the PPS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services is audited by an independent organisation; and (ii) which organisation carries this out.
answer_text: An audit of CAMHS was completed by the Northern Ireland Audit Office, within the wider Mental Health Services in Northern Ireland audit published on the 23rd May 2023. The Regional General Adolescent Inpatient Unit, Beechcroft is inspected by the Regulation Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting time for diagnosis of ADHD for those under the age of 18.
answer_text: Please find children’s ADHD waiting time information detailed below. Table 1. Number of children waiting for an ADHD assessment as at 22.04.2024*, by waiting time bandsWaiting time Bands 0-4 Weeks (0-28 days)           171 >4 to 8 Weeks (29-56 days)           239 >8 to 13 weeks (57-91 days)           246 > 13 to 18 weeks (92-126 days)           256 > 18 to 26 weeks (127-182 days)           517 > 26 to 39 weeks (183-273 days)           528 > 39 to 52 weeks (274-364 days)           725 > 52 weeks (365+ days)       1,938 Total Waiting       4,601 Source: HSC Trusts*Please note that the South Eastern Trust could only provide information as at 08.11.2023 in addition to a manual analysis of data as of 23.04 2024, as their latest position, and the Western HSC Trust provided information as at 19.04.2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the ADHD support services available in the (i) Northern Health and Social Trust; and (ii) Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Northern Health and Social Trust;The NHSCT has commenced a small, unfunded pilot of those who have been referred to the Trust for Adult ADHD diagnosis and treatment.  Referrals are gradually going onto a waiting lists for an appointment with the Trust following clinical validation.For children diagnosed with ADHD, the Northern Trust’s Children’s Early Intervention Service offers intervention via the Parents’ Plus ADHD programme alongside 1-1 psychotherapeutic intervention to Children and Young People who present with behavioural challenges, if this is deemed clinically appropriate.Post diagnostic monitoring of medication is completed by Acute Paediatricians. Western Health and Social Care Trust.The WHSCT Adult Mental Health (AMH) services have an approved ADHD pathway for patients with an existing diagnosis of ADHD.  If a person is diagnosed with ADHD in children’s services and requires ongoing medication management by a Consultant Psychiatrist, they will continue to receive regular reviews in adult mental health services.AMH services provide ADHD nurse led clinics to monitor effects of medication in six monthly and yearly medical review clinics.  For children, when an ADHD diagnosis is confirmed, treatment and support options are considered and discussed with young person and family. This may involve a trial of medication and or targeted therapeutic support.WHSCT has developed an educational online platform via an email service supporting families following ADHD diagnosis. Information pertaining to a variety of topics including ADHD resources and emotional wellbeing strategies. Verbal feedback from families has been positive. The service is keen to further progress this initiative and implement more online courses using feedback received from families in receipt of the service.(iii) for his assessment of the provision of diagnostic services for ADHD patients.Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals there is currently no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The limited capacity available in Trusts in terms of meeting demand and the level of prevalence of child and adult ADHD is leading to individuals waiting longer for assessment and support than described in NICE guidelines.Department of Health officials are regularly reviewing the situation regarding the commissioning of ADHD services, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years. Any decision to commission ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of budget availability. Department of Health officials are now considering

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of prosecutions that have taken place for human trafficking since 2022, broken down by (i) adult victims; and (ii) victims under the age of 18.
answer_text: Information has been provided in relation to the number of prosecutions and convictions at courts, for offences under the Human Trafficking and Exploitation (Criminal Justice and Support for Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 and other offences which specified human trafficking in the description.  Departmental prosecutions and convictions datasets do not contain information in relation to the age of a victim, apart from where it is specified in the offence description.  Therefore, from information held, it is not possible to provide a breakdown, in relation to whether associated victims were adults or under the age of 18.Prosecutions and convictions at courts for offences under human trafficking legislation, 2022 – 2023YearProsecutionsConvictions2022                     3                    3 2023                     *                     * Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposals at court.  Appeals are not included.  2. Figures relate to cases where there was a prosecution or conviction for at least one human trafficking offence.3. Figures relate to offences under the Human Trafficking and Exploitation (Criminal Justice and Support for Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 and other offences which specified human trafficking in the offence description.4. Figures relate to cases completed in the years specified. Cases which are currently in process have not been included.5. Figures for 2023 are provisional. * relates to fewer than three cases.  Small numbers have been suppressed under rules of disclosure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what funding support is available for households wishing to install solar panels.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 set out a target of 80% of electricity consumption to be from renewable sources in the north of Ireland by 2030. Essential to meeting this target will be the establishment of a support scheme for renewable electricity.Departmental officials are currently working on a scheme design that will incentivise investment in larger scale renewable electricity projects ensuring consumers pay a fair price for electricity produced locally.Options for microgeneration support, including domestic renewable generation such as solar panels, will continue to be evaluated as part of our net zero ambitions.The Windsor framework enables the extension to the North of the VAT relief for energy-saving materials that was previously available only in Britain. That means that people here will also be able to apply zero rates of VAT to the installation of energy-saving materials, such as solar panels.Independent advice on solar panels, heat pumps and other energy efficiency matters is available from organisations such as the Energy Saving Trust. These technologies have the potential to provide an attractive payback to homeowners without grant support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of reports of human trafficking that have been made to the PSNI since 2022, broken down by (i) adult victims; and (ii) victims under the age of 18.
answer_text: The need to raise public awareness of this often hidden crime and increase reporting of Human Trafficking offences is a key objective for my Department. Victims are among the most vulnerable and traumatised in the criminal justice system. The draft Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Strategy 2024-27 sets out a comprehensive plan to improve awareness and encourage reporting over the next three years.  The Department of Justice however does not hold information on the numbers of reports to police in relation to human trafficking.  Information on offences reported to the police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many transfer tests were issued in (i) English; (ii) Irish; and (iii) Ulster Scots in the last five years.
answer_text: Post-primary transfer tests have not been regulated by my Department since 2009.  Separate tests were organised and operated by the Association for Quality Education Ltd and the Post Primary Test Consortium until 2023 when they were replaced with a single post-primary transfer test by the Schools’ Entrance Assessment Group; however, my Department has no role in the operation or administration of these tests.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of catering staff that have left roles in (i) primary school canteens; and (ii) post-primary school canteens, in the last five years.
answer_text: The Education Authority advise that in the last five years the number of catering staff leaving their roles is as follows:  		Primary school canteens		1,630		Post-primary school canteens 	678Notes:The figures provided by the Education Authority are for controlled and maintained schools only.Figures are based on assignment level (i.e. a count of posts not headcount).Figures are the totals in each school type based on the timeframe of 1 April 2019 to 31 March 2024.Information for Voluntary Grammar and Grant Maintained Integrated schools is not held centrally and is therefore not available for inclusion in this answer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the process by which translation for transfer tests to the Irish language are (i) made; and (ii) monitored for accuracy.
answer_text: Post-primary transfer tests have not been regulated by my Department since 2009.  Separate tests were organised and operated by the Association for Quality Education Ltd and the Post Primary Test Consortium until 2023 when they were replaced with a single post-primary transfer test by the Schools’ Entrance Assessment Group; however, my Department has no role in the operation or administration of these tests.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department records statistics on the number of second homes in the North Coast area.
answer_text: My Department does not record statistics on the number of second homes in Northern Ireland including the North Coast area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many mental health beds are available for (i) adult patients; and (ii) patients under 18, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The most recent annual statistics for the average number of available beds are in the ‘Mental Health Programme of Care’ section (under ‘Bed Availability and Occupancy’) of the ‘Northern Ireland Inpatient Activity Statistics 2022/23’ publication. This is available at:Northern Ireland Inpatient Activity Statistics 2022/23Table 38 of the publication includes data from 2018/19 to 2022/23. Please note that while the data held by the Department may be broken down by Health and Social Care Trust (as shown in the table), they do not include information about patient age.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has considered introducing legislation in relation to the release of relevant information to protect the public from sexually violent offenders, similar to Megan’s Law in the United States of America.
answer_text: Child Protection Disclosure Arrangements were contained in the Justice (NI) Act 2015 and introduced in Northern Ireland in March 2016. The scheme makes it easier for anyone who has concerns about someone who might pose a risk to children to find out whether that person has a criminal record for sexual or violent offences which could indicate a risk to children. A member of the public can apply, using a form available on the PSNI website, for disclosure of relevant conviction information regarding a person with access to a particular child or children.Information about relevant criminal convictions will only be provided to the person with primary care responsibility for the specific child(ren) and only if considered

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what discussions have taken place in his Department to increase footfall in (i) Coleraine; (ii) Dungiven; (iii) Kilrea; and (iv) Limavady towns' high street shopping areas.
answer_text: Addressing regional economic imbalances is one of my core priorities, and I recognise the essential role of high streets in enhancing the vibrancy of local economies. While responsibility for town centre regeneration rests with the Department for Communities and development of local economic policies primarily falls to the relevant district council, my Department and its agencies will work with local partners to ensure that economic development meets local needs and priorities. This includes working with all councils, alongside Invest NI, to co-design sub-regional priorities which may include place building to strengthen local high streets.Furthermore, support for high streets will require collaboration across several Departments. I intend to work with Executive colleagues to consider the High Street Task Force recommendations to take this agenda forward, a process that will necessitate consent from other Ministers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any incentives that are available for young entrepreneurs to open a business.
answer_text: Encouraging young entrepreneurs to open a business is vitally important and aligns with my key priorities, particularly the promotion of regional balance. 		Across the north, there is a rich network of organisations providing a range of incentives to encourage and support young entrepreneurs at all stages of their journey.  These include Young Enterprise and Catalyst, who deliver a variety of programmes to foster entrepreneurship amongst young people, from school age through to university and beyond.		The Further Education Colleges’ Entrepreneurship Hubs provide advice and guidance, mentoring, signposting to wider assistance, and support for business plan development to their students. Within Higher Education, Queens’ University InQUBate network and the University of Ulster’s Enterprise Hub support students to develop their entrepreneurial skills and build their ambition.		Also available for all young people over the age of sixteen is ‘Go Succeed’, the council-led business start-up service providing advice, support, and financial incentives aimed at helping potential entrepreneurs turn their business idea into reality. 	Finally, Invest NI, provide advice and information through www.nibusinessinfo.co.uk. Two parts of the website are particularly helpful for young people considering starting their own businesses – My New Business and Starting a Business.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what quantifiable steps his Department has taken to achieve its target of planting 18 million trees by 2030.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the funding available from her Department for road safety projects.
answer_text: My Department has no specific funding provision for road safety projects and given the ongoing priorities within the remit of the Department of Justice, and the extraordinary constraints on our budget, that position is unlikely to change. My Department fully recognises the devastating impact that fatalities and serious injuries from road traffic collisions have on families and communities across Northern Ireland. In partnership with the Northern Ireland Policing Board, my Department provides annual funding to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) who lead on the Department’s response to community safety issues at a local level.  Currently all PCSPs are engaged with initiatives to help make our roads safer for everyone. The Department engages with the Department for Infrastructure, who lead on road safety, and other partners, including via membership of the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership, and the NI Road Safety Strategic Forum, and is committed to working collaboratively on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the funding available from his Department for road safety projects.
answer_text: Budgets have not yet been agreed for 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of vacant posts in the Transition Service 14+ service; and (ii) how long each post has been vacant.
answer_text: The information requested has been provided by the Education Authority as follows:At present there are two vacancies in the Transition Service 14+ service.  One post has been vacant from June 2022 and the other from November 2022.  These posts are presently going through the recruitment process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what changes have been made to endometriosis care since 2020.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail all support available for deaf children who need to learn sign language.
answer_text: The Department for Communities (DfC) has responsibility for sign language; however, the Education Authority’s (EA) Sensory Service does provide support to deaf children and their families.The service signposts families to the National Deaf Children’s Society who provide free family sign language classes for all families of deaf children.  The Service also endeavours to allocate those who require sign support to Teachers of Deaf who are proficient in sign. Where a deaf child who requires sign support attends a mainstream school, the school may employ a classroom assistant to support this child, through the Special Educational Needs (SEN) statutory assessment process.  The service advises schools to recruit classroom assistants who have at least BSL Level 3 and funds training for those assistants to attend accredited sign language courses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department will consider introducing legislation which would restrict people (i) arrested for sexual offences; and (ii) charged with sexual offences from being near children.
answer_text: I have no plans to introduce legislation on this issue. Currently, where persons charged with a sexual offence are considered to pose a risk to children, and who are released on bail by the court pending trial, the court can impose, as part of bail conditions, a requirement that the defendant has no contact with any person under 18. Any breach of this bail condition will result in the arrest of the defendant who will be brought back before the court.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department will introduce legislation to close the loophole which allows people who have been convicted of an offence to change their names.
answer_text: I am conscious of proposals being progressed by Home Office to make legislative change to the sex offender notification arrangements applicable to relevant sex offenders.  I understand this is to include a specific requirement around name change.I have asked my officials to explore options available for Northern Ireland.I am not aware of any other planned reforms in respect of when an offender can change their name.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how her Department will ensure it provides victims of sexual crimes with the appropriate communication, support and advice in a timely manner.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of providing victims of crime with appropriate and timely support, information, advice and assistance and this is particularly true in cases of sexual crime. I and my Department acknowledge this as a responsibility of the justice system. The Victim Charter sets out how victims of crime should be treated within the justice system and includes automatic referral, unless the victim objects, to free confidential support services, based on an individual’s needs. My Department provides funding to Victim Support Northern Ireland (VSNI) who will seek to contact all victims within five business days of a referral and to NSPCC’s Young Witness Service to provide support to young prosecution witnesses, including in sexual crime cases, who are called to give evidence at court. My Department has also established a Sexual Offences Legal Adviser (SOLA) Service, again delivered by VSNI and which provides free, independent legal advice to adult complainants in serious sexual offence cases, up to the point of trial, even prior to a report being made to the police. ASSIST NI is contracted by my Department to provide advocacy services for victims of domestic and sexual abuse across Northern Ireland, primarily those engaging with the criminal justice system. In addition to the services that are funded by my Department, the Victim and Witness Care Unit (VWCU) within Public Prosecution Service provides victims of crime, including victims of serious sexual offences, with a single point of contact from the point that an investigation or charge file is submitted to the PPS, through to and including the outcome of any court proceedings. VWCU Case Officers keep victims updated and can also refer victims for specific supports provided by other organisations.I acknowledge that there are opportunities to improve how victims are informed and supported through the criminal justice system. My Department is leading multi-agency work to develop and deliver a new structured digital information hub, which will provide information about the criminal justice system in an easy to navigate format. Work is well advanced to develop a new seven year Domestic and Sexual Abuse (DSA) Strategy. It is jointly led by the Department of Justice and the Department of Health, with inputs from other Departments.  Publication of the Strategy, along with an accompanying Performance Framework and Action Plan, is planned to take place before the summer. The Strategy includes a specific Support and Provision pillar which will ensure effective communication with victims of domestic and/or sexual abuse, and that they can access tailored, responsive and specialised support, including advice and advocacy, to help recover and rebuild their lives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many people who have been convicted of a sexual crime live in the District Electoral Areas of (i) Benbradagh; (ii) Causeway Central; (iii) Coleraine; (iv) Limavady; (v) Ballymoney; (vi) The Glens; and (vii) Bann.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on the current address of those convicted of a sexual offence.  However, such information may be recorded in relation to persons who have been convicted of a sexual offence and who are subject to statutory notification requirements, commonly known as ‘the Sex Offenders Register’.  The operational management of persons required to notify in this regard is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what percentage of convicted sex offenders have been given a sentence of five years or more, in the last 10 years.
answer_text: Information on the number of convictions for a sexual offence that resulted in a custodial sentence of five years or more has been provided for cases that were dealt with in the period 2013 to 2022, the most recent ten years for which information is available.Convictions for sexual offences resulting in a custodial sentence of 5 years or more, 2013 – 2022YearTotal convictions for sexual offences Number of convictions resulting in custodial sentence of 5 years or morePercentage convictions resulting in custodial sentence of 5 years or more2013                           205                              32 15.6%2014                           192                              38 19.8%2015                           170                              25 14.7%2016                           185                              21 11.4%2017                           172                              16 9.3%2018                           194                              25 12.9%2019                           234                              21 9.0%2020                           148                              15 10.1%2021                           290                              25 8.6%2022                           309                              24 7.8%Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposals at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases where there was a conviction for at least one sexual offence.3. Figures in relation to length of sentence imposed include cases where the total to be spent in custody and on licence was 5 years or more.4. Figures for total convictions relate to all cases where there was a conviction for a sexual offence, whether or not the main disposal was a custodial sentence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an updated timeline on the previously postponed improvements to be carried out on Drumagarner Road, Kilrea.
answer_text: My Department has a number of resurfacing schemes planned along the Drumagarner Road over the coming years, subject to funding and competing priorities.  The first of these is planned for this summer, beginning in Kilrea at New Row and continuing out past the bus station. The delivery of this scheme is subject to future budget allocations.  A scheme to improve the vertical alignment in the region of the Old Point Inn has also been assessed, however it is a low priority when compared to other schemes and is unlikely to be included in a works programme for the foreseeable future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department will bring student loans for post-graduate students from Northern Ireland studying in England, Scotland or Wales in line with the amount offered to post-graduate students in the rest of the UK.
answer_text: My Department undertook a public consultation on postgraduate tuition fee loans from 26 April 2021 to 25 June 2021. The consultation document, along with the Departmental response to the consultation, can be found online at the following web address: https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/consultations/review-northern-ireland-postgraduate-tuition-fee-loan.Officials are currently implementing the outcomes of that consultation in partnership with the Student Loans Company, which administers the loans. The consultation response envisaged that the level of the postgraduate tuition fee loan would be subject to further review and my Department will undertake that work in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on the A2 Ballykelly Bypass scheme.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.My Department is in the process of developing a new Transport Strategy that will help to shape the vision and strategic priorities for transport through to 2035.  Once this is published, it will help to inform the Transport Plan for the Regional Strategic Transport Network, which includes the A2 at Ballykelly.
