question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the pre-release employment opportunities her Department has with Magilligan prisoners’ rehabilitation programmes in the East Derry constituency.
answer_text: Magilligan currently facilitates three pre-release employment opportunities within the East Derry constituency. One is within a service industry, providing prisoners with experience of a busy working environment and enhancing their skills and understanding of working in a customer-facing role.  This builds on the qualifications attained through the North West Regional College whilst in Magilligan.The second is within the light engineering sector, giving those with a mechanical background the opportunity to gain experience and refresh their skill base as they prepare for release.  The final opportunity is within the manufacturing sector, affording prisoners the expertise and competencies associated with a production environment.  This provides a natural progression for those who have acquired relevant qualifications within Magilligan. You are welcome to visit Magilligan Prison to discuss these opportunities and other rehabilitation work with the Governor and his team.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what steps can be taken to reduce the scope of medical records of rape victims throughout the course of a trial.
answer_text: I acknowledge the significant impact there can be for victims of rape who face the prospect of their medical records being disclosed in the course of a trial. However,  I also recognise that the disclosure regime is an essential aspect of the court process and in considering any steps to reduce the scope to disclose victims’ personal information, including their medical records, it is essential that the defendant’s Article 6 rights to a fair trial are not compromised in any way.I am aware that, operationally, there are measures in place and under review to try to narrow the scope of material disclosed during a trial, to ensure that it is necessary and proportionate. The Public Prosecution Service, which is independent of government, has advised that it is working with PSNI, to ensure that only reasonable lines of enquiry are followed when accessing relevant material and applying the Disclosure Test, limiting the scope of a complainant’s records that may be requested to be disclosed to those required by law.  Both the police and the prosecution are bound by the law in this area as set out in the Criminal Procedure and Investigation Act 1996. I am also informed that police and PPS employ disclosure management documents in relevant cases, which record what has and has not been requested, in order to reduce the possibility of vexatious information requests. Practice Direction 2/2019, which was issued by the former Lord Chief Justice, sets out the process for the disclosure of third party material (which includes medical records), including the roles of the Investigating Officer, the Prosecution, the Defence and the Judiciary. The Practice Direction sets out that the disclosure process includes pre-trial hearings of applications made by the defence team for medical records and other personal information. It is a matter for the judge to determine what information is relevant and proportionate and can be shared with the defence, after balancing the defendant’s right to a fair trial, the law and practice in relation to disclosure and the victim’s privacy rights. My Department also introduced a Sexual Offences Legal Adviser (SOLA) service in April 2021, which is delivered through Victim Support NI.  SOLAs provide free, independent legal advice to complainants in serious sexual offence including in relation to the disclosure of their private material (including medical records) to the judge making disclosure decisions in the case. I am also grateful to the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime for the work that she is taking forward in this area and I look forward to the outcome of that work and any recommendations arising from it in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has considered research from other countries in relation to their methodologies of limiting victim harm in instances where rape victims' counselling notes are used in court.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the harm and distress that victims of serious sexual offences may experience when faced with the prospect of having their personal counselling notes shared in court as part of the disclosure process. I recognise that concerns about the disclosure of personal information may contribute to some victims not reporting crimes, withdrawing from the criminal justice process or delaying availing of therapy until the case has concluded. However I also recognise that the disclosure regime is an essential aspect of the court process and it is important that, in considering how to enhance victims’ control over their personal information, the defendant’s Article 6 rights to a fair trial are not compromised in any way.I am therefore keen to ensure that any changes to the disclosure regime to improve protections for victims of serious sexual offences should be informed by the experience and learning of what has proved effective in other jurisdictions. As such, my officials are in the process of scoping models used in other common law jurisdictions such as Canada, New South Wales, Scotland and Ireland, in order to identify areas of good practice to help inform potential changes in Northern Ireland. My officials are also monitoring ongoing developments in England and Wales. The Victims and Prisoners Bill, as currently drafted, includes statutory duties on policing to enhance the protections for victims around disclosure of third party material and again I will wish to consider whether there is learning from this approach which would be appropriate within Northern Ireland.I am also grateful to the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime for the work that she is taking forward in this area and I look forward to the outcome of that work and any recommendations arising from it in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what surveys have been carried on the road from Limavady to Greysteel in the last two years in relation to (i) the condition of the road; and (ii) the volume of traffic.
answer_text: The A2 Clooney Road between Limavady and Greysteel is inspected monthly and any defects repaired in accordance with my Department’s limited service policy.  As a trunk road it is also assessed annually for skid resistance and the structural strength of the road is tested on approximately a 3-year cycle.  There has been no specific traffic volume surveys carried out within the last two years, however, traffic count information for this area can be viewed at Traffic and travel information (incorporating annual traffic census and variations in traffic flow) | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps his Department has taken since January 2024 to address the volume of traffic in the Ballykelly area.
answer_text: The A2 Clooney Road, which runs through Ballykelly village, forms part of the strategic transport network. The Department is in the process of developing a new Transport Strategy that will help to shape the vision and strategic priorities for transport through to 2035. Once this is published, it will help to inform the Regional Strategic Transport Network Transport Plan which will consider improvements to the strategic transport network.In the interim, the Department will continue to monitor the existing arrangements within the village.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to create a bypass in Ballykelly.
answer_text: My Department is in the process of developing a new Transport Strategy that will help to shape the vision and strategic priorities for transport through to 2035.  Once this is published, it will help to inform the Transport Plan for the Regional Strategic Transport Network, which includes the A2 at Ballykelly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will commit to (i) adding more signage; and (ii) implementing traffic calming measures in Drumsurn.
answer_text: I understand an official from my Department recently met on site with yourself and members of the local community to discuss the issue of speeding at Drumsurn Village. Officials are currently considering what, if any, further engineering enhancements would help address the issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered running a pilot programme to create a specific area in a hospital for women who are experiencing a miscarriage.
answer_text: Early Pregnancy Clinics have now been established in most Maternity/Gynaecology units across Northern Ireland.  These clinics will have telephone triage and self-referral triage in place that successfully help to divert those experiencing pregnancy or loss away from Emergency Department units, however it is not possible at this time to provide 24/7 Early Pregnancy Clinics and this can mean that not every patient avails of this service in their time of need.Some patients with symptoms of miscarriage present to the Emergency Department. Many patients present to Early Pregnancy Clinics successfully diverting those experiencing pregnancy loss away from the Emergency Department. This clinic can be accessed via a patient’s GP or self-referral. Not all referrals are a pregnancy loss.  Women are assessed by the Early pregnancy team at this clinic, admitted if required, or managed as an ambulatory outpatient if stable, and reassessed the following day by the early pregnancy team.Most Emergency Departments already have a private area for families following bereavement, but not specifically used for miscarriage patients only.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many Irish speaking speech and language therapists currently work in schools.
answer_text: My department does not hold this information.Speech and language therapists in schools are usually employed by Health and Social Care Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of whether the size of the building at Rossmar School, Limavady, meets the needs of the school.
answer_text: The new build Rossmar Special School was completed in February 2022. Every new Special School capital build project is sized based primarily on the nature of the needs and number of the pupils presenting. The accommodation provided is therefore based on a bespoke Schedule of Accommodation that is prepared and agreed in advance of project commencement. Substantial changes to the scope of the Rossmar project, due to increased enrolments and an increase in the complexity of pupil needs resulted in five additional classrooms and associated ancillary space being added to the project during construction to meet the increased enrolment of 140 pupils.  Current enrolment in the 2023/24 school year has increased to 185 pupils. In September 2023 Rossmar School accepted and admitted an additional dual day nursery class for pupils from the Coleraine area because of increased demand for places in the area at that time. This created accommodation pressures within Rossmar and the need for additional classrooms for September 2024.The Education Authority is currently examining options to facilitate the increased numbers and maximise the space available to the school.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department can take to address the high price of insurance for single operator taxi businesses.
answer_text: I am aware of the of the rising cost of vehicle insurance and the challenges this is causing for all of our road users and in particular for those who have a business requirement.The rise in insurance premiums is commercial and so is outside of the remit of my Department’s role in regulating the taxi industry

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps his Department is taking to reduce the costs for single operator taxi businesses.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry, in relation to taxi driver, taxi operator and vehicle licencing.  In that regard I would advise that the current fees for these licences, including those relating to single taxi operator businesses, will remain unchanged.For your reference please see the link below which provides details of operator’s licence fees Taxi - Licence Fee Guide TOL7 (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of current support for Irish Travellers.
answer_text: My Department is committed to making it easy for all people, including those from the Irish Traveller community, to access our services.  We have a network of 35 Jobs & Benefit Offices located across Northern Ireland where staff will provide individually tailored face to face support to help people access social security benefits.  Universal Credit claims are primarily made and maintained using our online service.  A telephony claim service is available to people who do not have access to a digital platform or who may experience difficulty in maintaining a digital claim.   Further assistance can be obtained by telephoning our Service Centre on 0800 012 1331. The Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE), through the Supporting People (SP) Programme, currently funds two Floating Support Services where Irish Travellers are either the only client group or are included within the Primary Client Group.  These are Bryson Care Floating Support and South Tyrone Empowerment Programme (STEP) Floating Support; both are SP accredited provider organisations.  My Department also provides funding to the NIHE for Traveller sites, including maintenance and running costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of businesses in the East Derry constituency that were turned down for Hardship Rates Relief in (i) 2022; (ii) 2023; and (iii) 2024.
answer_text: The numbers of businesses in the East Derry constituency that made an unsuccessful Hardship Rate Relief application in the years requested are as follows:2022:	12023:	22024:	0 (at 19th March 2024)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the challenges with retention of non-teaching support staff due to uncompetitive pay scales.
answer_text: During my frequent visits to schools, I hear first-hand about the challenges associated with the recruitment and retention of school support staff.  This issue is also at the forefront of the Pay & Grading review business case, which is seeking to address the recruitment and retention problems caused by the current grading structures for support staff.  I am committed to ensuring that all staff in the education sector are paid at a fair level for the important work they do. l therefore intend to bid for additional funding as part of the Executive’s Budget 2024-25 process so that the recommendations from the Pay & Grading review can be implemented.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the business contracts her Department has with Magilligan prisoners rehabilitation programmes, in the East Derry constituency.
answer_text: Magilligan Prison works in collaboration with several businesses and voluntary and community sector organisations in the East Londonderry constituency to provide pre-release employment opportunities to prisoners.  All placements are subject to a risk assessment and appropriate to the prisoner’s skill base and resettlement needs.  None of these, regardless of sector are on a contract basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail her consideration for the use of Access NI checks for (i) staff; and (ii) volunteers in caravan parks.
answer_text: Criminal history information contained on AccessNI checks is used as part of a wider toolkit to help employers / voluntary groups to make more informed recruitment decisions.  It is for the individual sector / operation to determine the level of check they require, and they should do so in reference to published guidance.  The requirement for AccessNI checks is established by management / oversight authorities in number of ways, including:-A range of ‘fitness to practice’ regulations in the Health sector, for example Nursing Homes Regulations (NI) 2005, which require Enhanced checks.Security Industry Authority licensing arrangements, which require Standard checks for SIA operatives.Department for Transport operating procedures, which require Basic checks for staff working ‘airside’ in airports.Central sports bodies, whose safeguarding guidelines require Enhanced checks for (for example) children’s coaches. It might be that local councils could consider the need for AccessNI checks as part of their licensing conditions for caravan and theme parks.  Whilst setting such guidelines is not the responsibility of the Department of Justice, I would encourage those considering such options to liaise directly with AccessNI, where details on the range of checks, eligibility criterion, volumes and costs can be discussed.  In the meantime please do not hesitate to contact the General Manager of AccessNI, Brian Thomson, at 02890 523413 or brian.thomson@accessni.gov.uk,  if you have any queries about the specific role of AccessNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will consider adding to the curriculum in the Learning for Life and Work module to include education on housing rights.
answer_text: Learning for Life and Work is a statutory Area of Learning within the Northern Ireland Curriculum at key stages 3 and 4.  Through the Home Economics strand, students have the opportunity to explore a range of topics including independent living, consumer rights, responsibilities and support available in a range of scenarios.  Schools are, however, provided with sufficient flexibility to decide which specific topics should be covered to best suit the needs of their pupils.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline what plans it has to introduce maternity and parental leave arrangements for MLAs, in line with provisions in other legislatures.
To ask the Minister of Education what he is doing to increase the levels of investment for early years providers outside of Education Authority support staff.
answer_text: Since my appointment as Education Minister, I have made the development and implementation of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy a priority.  A range of stabilisation and expansion options are currently being scoped.  I have bid for funding to enable me to increase the levels of investment for early years provides in the 2024/25 financial year.  I will work with my Executive colleagues on this but progress will ultimately depend on the funds being made available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department seeks to address poverty impacting on people under the age of 18.
answer_text: Addressing poverty in all its forms is a key priority for me and I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for our communities right across Northern Ireland.I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to the Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy and addressing poverty impacting on people under the age of 18 remains a key focus of this ongoing work. Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of how the removal of hybrid meetings will impact local government councillors on (i) maternity leave; and (ii) paternity leave.
answer_text: I appreciate there may be a short term impact on some district councillors but I do not believe there is justification in prolonging provisions of the Coronavirus Act which was intended as emergency legislation.  I believe it is more important to ensure that new permanent regulations, enabling councils to hold meetings by remote or hybrid means, are brought forward using the powers in the Local Government (Meetings and Performance) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.   I will be bringing forward these regulations in as quick a timeframe as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his engagement with junior doctors following their strike action.
answer_text: I have restated, to the Chair of the BMA Junior Doctor Committee, my commitment to further negotiations when I have greater clarity on any final settlement in the ongoing disputes in England and Wales. I believe that there are grounds for productive negotiations on several fronts which include 2024/25 pay, and potentially reforming the Junior Doctor contract, as well as addressing concerns on such as working conditions.I have also advised that the Labour Relations Agency is available and willing to facilitate discussions and have indicated that my Department would enter such discussions in a positive spirit, should this be acceptable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps his Department is taking to lower the gap in wages between Nursery teachers and Early Years teachers.
answer_text: All teachers, including nursery and early years teachers, in grant-aided schools are paid on the same salary scale; their placement on the scale is based on their length of teaching experience.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department collects data on women who do not attend their smear test appointments due to sexual trauma.
answer_text: It is recognised that sexual trauma is one of the reasons why women may not attend a cervical screening test, however specific data is not collected by my Department or the Public Health Agency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline who makes a decision on whether the subject of an Assembly Written Question is an operational matter.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider implementing a healthy relationships strategy.
answer_text: There are no current plans to develop a healthy relationships strategy. A new Sexual Health Action Plan was published in November 2023. This plan can be accessed on the Department’s website: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-sexual-health-action-plan_0.pdf As part of strategic objective five within the plan, there is an action to 'ensure evidence-based sexual health, consent and relationship information is available to school-aged children, adolescents and young adults'. Delivery Groups are currently being established to take the Action Plan forward. Providing people with the information and support they need to better understand healthy sexual and personal relationships will be explored as part of this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work his Department has undertaken in supporting children of alcoholics between 2020 to 2023.
answer_text: The impact of substance use by parents or carers on the children or young people who live with them is often known as Hidden Harm. Under the auspices of Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery – NI’s Substance Use Strategy – substance use and Hidden Harm will continue to be addressed as appropriate. The Public Health Agency and DoH Strategy Planning & Performance Group are currently finalising the Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan (‘The Plan’). With the voice of the child, young person and family central to the process, the Plan commits to establish a regional working group to update the Hidden Harm Action Plan and support its release with a comprehensive communication plan and workforce training package to inform everyone involved in supporting people and families dealing with substance use. In particular, the Joint Working Protocol on Hidden Harm will be promoted and used across all services.Funded by the Public Health Agency, there is currently a range of services across Northern Ireland offering therapeutic treatment and support for children, young people and families affected by parental alcohol and/or drug use. Examples of these include Barnardo’s Pharos programme, the Drug & Alcohol Intervention Service for Youth (DAISY) provided jointly by Start360/ASCERT, and the Voices project managed by Start360. Family Support Services will also be reviewed by the PHA to ensure that evidence-based supports are available for all those who wish to avail of them, whether or not their family members are in treatment. Service models will be updated accordingly to ensure the involvement of family members in treatment as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will consider introducing Access NI checks for staff who work in (i) caravan parks; and (ii) theme parks.
answer_text: This answer should be read in conjunction with the answer for AQW 6982/22-27.It is not the role of the Department of Justice (or AccessNI) to place requirements on which roles or services should obtain AccessNI checks.  It is generally the responsibility for Departments, Regulators, Local Councils, etc, with policy responsibility for that area, to consider what checks should be obtained in reference to publish guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the process by which women can access the morning after pill; and (ii) to detail any existing barriers to accessing this form of contraceptive.
answer_text: Emergency hormonal contraception (‘the morning after pill/plan B’) can be accessed in a number of ways.  A prescription is required if accessing the morning after pill from a GP, GP Out of Hours or the Family Planning Service. Progesterone-only oral contraception (Hana and Lovima) are available to buy in pharmacies and online without a prescription, following a clinical assessment. There have been several service developments in recent years which have improved access to the morning after pill.  It should be noted the most effective form of contraception is the intra-uterine coil.  This can be accessed via sexual and reproductive health services in Health and Social Care Trusts.  There has been significant investment in sexual and reproductive health services over the last two years which has improved access across the region.The Pharmacy First service in Northern Ireland offer emergency hormonal contraception for individuals who are aged 13 or older.  As part of this service, clients are also assessed for bridging oral contraception, i.e. a three month supply of contraception to ‘bridge’ the time until they can see a care provider to access more regular contraception.  This service is free of charge.In addition, from February 2022, Northern Ireland residents aged 16 and over have been able to access the combined oral contraceptive pill (COC), the progesterone-only pill (POP), and emergency hormonal contraception by placing an order with an online contraception provider.  Users can request contraception any time of the day, seven days per week. Orders are reviewed by a clinician and, if approved, contraception is prescribed and posted out on the same day (if ordered before 2pm Monday to Friday) or the next working day (if ordered after 2pm or on the weekend).  This service is free of charge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the criteria for businesses to be eligible for Hardship Rate Relief; and (ii) the number of businesses granted Hardship Rate Relief in the last 12 months.
answer_text: Hardship Rate Relief for a non-domestic property is intended to assist a business recover from a temporary crisis, as a result of exceptional circumstances.  Each case is considered on its own merits and applicants should demonstrate how payment of Rates would cause hardship to the business’ recovery plan. Detailed information and the application form is available online at:Hardship Rate Relief | nibusinessinfo.co.uk10 applications for Hardship Rate Relief are currently at assessment stage.  My Department provided 100% rate relief for the period 1st November 2023 to 31st March 2024, to 255 businesses impacted by localised flooding, reducing the need to apply for Hardship Rate Relief.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps he will take to address speeding in Eglinton village.
answer_text: I can confirm that a request for traffic calming to be introduced on the Woodvale Road is being progressed by the Department. Preliminary public consultation has taken place and responses to this consultation are now under consideration.My Department is also considering the further requests received for traffic calming in Eglinton in line with the relevant policy. However, there is a legal responsibility on all drivers to drive in accordance with the set speed limit of a road and have due care and consideration for all other road users. Concerns about speeding or careless driving should be reported to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his consideration on creating baby loss certificates before 24 weeks gestation.
answer_text: I recognise the impact that pregnancy loss at any stage can have on parents and welcome the recent introduction of pre-24-week pregnancy loss certificates in England, as recommended by the Independent Pregnancy Loss Review commissioned by the Westminster Government. My Department will continue to engage with their counterparts in the Department of Health and Social Care in England as the new system is embedded there, with a view to understanding whether it might be possible to implement such a scheme in Northern Ireland, including any associated costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what action her Department is taking to promote and encourage individuals identifying as Roman Catholic to join the Police Service of Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The current legislative framework provides for appropriate measures to ensure accountability in the recruitment process in addition to the normal oversight arrangements operated by the Policing Board, and there is provision for targeting advertising to reach under-represented groups. The recruitment process is open to all those who meet the minimum criteria.  It is incumbent upon all of us as elected representatives to show our support, and I would press upon my fellow elected members and wider community leaders the value of encouraging people from all walks of life, not only religious background, to consider a career in policing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what (i) decisions her Department has made; and (ii) conversations she has had with the Department of Health, to ensure the reinstatement of funding for counselling services for children who are victims of sexual abuse.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of counselling and support for children who are victims of sexual abuse. My Department works closely with the Department of Health to ensure that victims of serious sexual offences receive the support and services they need to help with the criminal justice process.My Department and the Department of Health jointly lead on the development of the draft Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, which is nearing finalisation. The Strategy includes a Children and Young People Pillar which seeks to ensure that measures are in place to prevent children and young people from being abused and, where abuse occurs, that their specific service needs are identified and addressed. The Minister of Health and I hope to publish that Strategy, along with an action plan, early in the next financial year.My Department is also working with the Department of Health to scope options for consideration for a Barnahus model in Northern Ireland to meet the therapeutic, health and justice needs of child victims within the justice system, in order to minimise trauma and assist recovery. Separately, my Department has prioritised funding for front line services to victims including the NSPCC’s Young Witness Service, which provides vital support to the most vulnerable victims involved in the criminal justice system. Advocacy support to victims of sexual and domestic abuse has also been maintained and funded. I hope to meet with the Minister of Health in the coming months on a range of issues in which our Departments have a joint interest.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding from his Department to assist (i) under-18s; and (ii) adults, with ADHD.
answer_text: Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals there are currently no specifically commissioned services. It would be challenging to accurately determine the amount of funding allocated for individuals with ADHD, as they have access to various health services under different provisions.The Department of Health are committed to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet NICE compliant pathways for ADHD diagnosis and support.Department of Health officials are considering how a future ADHD service might be delivered to meet needs, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years.  In line with the usual process for commissioning services, any decision to commission ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the shortage of ADHD medication; and (ii) to detail his Department’s strategy to address this issue.
answer_text: There are ongoing issues with the supply of ADHD medications.  The supply disruption of these products is caused by a combination of manufacturing issues and an increased global demand. This was subject to a National Patient Safety Alert in September 2023 which was notified to the health service via the Chief Medical Officer on 28 September 2023.  The supply disruption issues are still taking some time to resolve however there are stocks of some medicines available presently in the pharmaceutical wholesale chain.  The Chief Medical Officers letter details guidance on alternative options for supply and where a clinician is considering switching to an alternate medication. Current medicines availability is listed on the UK Specialist Pharmacy Services website which pharmacy services have been signposted to.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department’s policy for the removal of flags flying from lamp-posts, including those with inflammatory political messaging, with specific regard to the East Londonderry constituency.
answer_text: Article 87 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993 makes it an offence to attach unauthorised signs or advertisements to structures such as lamp posts. One of my Department’s primary considerations is the safety of the public and where unauthorised flags or attachments pose a risk to road users, my Department will seek to remove that hazard. Where there is no such danger, my Department will liaise closely with other key stakeholders and seek to provide a solution.The display of a flag of a proscribed terrorist organisation is a criminal offence that may be capable of investigation by the PSNI, and I would encourage anyone with information in regards the display of such items to contact the PSNI.I note the commitments to respect for different cultures and identities contained in the New Decade New Approach. Finding a sustainable solution to the ongoing problem of illegally erected flags and emblems requires leadership and a united approach. I can assure you that I am committed to working with Executive colleagues and all stakeholders to deal with this issue comprehensively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of Irish Medium Education students having their exam papers marked by English speakers.
answer_text: Ideally CCEA would like to have a sufficient supply of suitably qualified specialists capable of marking papers in Irish across the full range of qualifications but has faced significant challenges in trying to recruit Irish speaking examiners due to the limited pool of Irish speaking teachers in each specialist subject. To ensure students in Irish Medium settings are able to complete their qualifications in Irish CCEA operates a translation model enabling the wider pool of non-Irish speaking examiners to mark the translated work of Irish Medium pupils.CCEA continues to make every effort to recruit sufficient examiners including through digital marketing recruitment campaigns and broadening the recruitment criteria to try to expand the pool of Irish speakers who may apply to be examiners.  However, to date, despite their best efforts, they have not been able to recruit the examiners necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the waiting list for Access NI checks.
answer_text: The number of applications received in AccessNI and waiting to be processed at 21 February 2024 is as follows:Type of checkNumber of outstanding applicationsWhy outstanding?Basic(Direct application by member of the public)277Applications cannot be processed until applicants send identification documents to AccessNIBasic (Application made through Responsible Body)3AccessNI working on Basic check applications received on 21/02/24Standard 0AccessNI has no outstanding applicationsEnhanced88AccessNI working on Enhanced check applications received on 21/02/24Total 368The number of Enhanced applications processed by AccessNI and waiting for a response from a police force before issue is 1,132 (which includes 1,051 outstanding with PSNI and 81 with other GB Forces and ACRO).  AccessNI cannot issue these certificates without police confirming if they have information to be included in the certificate.In addition, AccessNI is aware from its IT system that organisations are holding significant numbers of applications submitted to them by members of the public.  These applications cannot be processed by AccessNI until the relevant organisation countersigns an application and sends it electronically to AccessNI. The number of applications held by organisations is 11,081 and is as follows:Type of checkNumber of applications not yet countersignedBasic (Application through Responsible body)596Standard464Enhanced10,021Total11,081The following table provides details of AccessNI performance in issuing disclosure certificates against published targets for the period April to December 2023:Type of checkAchievedAverage number of days99% of RB Basic in 7 days100%195% of citizen Basic in 14 days99.8%299% of Standard in 7 days99.8%170% of Enhanced in 10 days86.9%495% of Enhanced in 28 days96.9%98% of Enhanced in 28 days97.8%

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the absence of legislation requiring caravan park staff to be checked by Access NI.
answer_text: Part V of the Police Act 1997, along with the supporting Criminal Record Disclosure Regulations, provides the statutory framework under which AccessNI operates.  This sets out the nature of disclosure checks available, the type of information to be included on disclosure certificates and (for Enhanced checks) the eligibility criterion.  This is enabling legislation to facilitate the disclosure of criminal history information.  It is not the purpose of this legislation to place requirements on what roles should obtain what level of AccessNI check; that is a matter for other Departments and Regulatory Authorities to consider in the sectors for which they have responsibility.  In doing so, they should have regard for the specific functions / responsibilities of the particular role and how that sits in reference to the published guidance, including if the role is undertaking Regulated Activity as defined by the Safeguarding Vulnerable Groups (NI) Order 2007.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what actions he has taken to address MOT backlogs in the last three weeks.
answer_text: I have received briefing from officials, and I am aware that the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has adopted a range of measures to increase its vehicle testing capacity, including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners and offering vehicle testing appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  A further vehicle examiner recruitment competition launched on 19 February 2024 to fill staff vacancies across the DVA’s network of test centres.In 2022-2023, the DVA conducted over 1.15 million vehicle tests which is the highest number ever recorded in a year.  Disappointingly, the number of vehicles that failed to attend a booked appointment in 2022-2023 was also the highest on record at 50,772.The DVA appreciates that, in some cases, customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires and has taken steps to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires.  The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/guide-booking-your-mot-vehicle-test and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers.In addition, vehicle test appointments are released on a daily basis, when resources become available, so customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available.  Information on how a customer can change, cancel or view their MOT appointment online is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/services/change-cancel-or-view-your-mot-test-appointment-online.To further increase vehicle testing capacity, the DVA is investing in new test centres.  The construction of a new test centre at Hydebank, in Belfast, is nearing completion and will open later this year.  The construction contract for a second new test centre at Mallusk has also been awarded and this new test centre is expected to open in 2025.  Once fully operational, these two new centres will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per annum, which will help the DVA meet the future demand for this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department has taken to address delays in accessing Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT) operate a Children’s Referral Service which serves as a central point for all referrals received. When a referral is received and if required direct contact is made with the family. This occurs on the day the referral is received. The NHSCT Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS) is experiencing high demand. Official statistics published by my Department on the 14th February 2024 highlighted that as of 31st December 2023 the total number of young people in the NHSCT waiting to access CAMHS was 697. This was a small increase from the previous quarter but significantly below the number as of 31st December 2022 which stood at 1011.The NHSCT is conscious of the demand on its services and has implemented Waiting List Initiatives across services to address delays which includes:Access for children and young people to enhanced triaging and mental health consultation and advice whilst awaiting services. Access to psycho-educative tools whilst awaiting full mental state assessment.Monitoring of children’s needs whilst awaiting services to allow for appropriate movement across provision.Access to specialist group-based intervention for children (and parents) who may or may not require assessment.  This specialist intervention can be allocated at the point of referral in an attempt to prevent escalation of need and risk.Effective management of internal waiting lists by implementing central allocation of appointments.  This ensures that our rural families in those lesser populated communities are accessing the same support and clinical expertise as families in towns.Greater emphasis on collaborative practice with our third sector colleagues. Support for parents and carers is also provided as an additional measure to support therapy for children who require support in relation to their mental health. Targeted collaborative practice with the Education Authority has served to improve outcomes for specific groups of children.  The Emotional Wellbeing Team for Schools are fully operational in a number of post primary school settings across the Trust.Emphasis placed on tackling Do Not Attend’s, which includes reminder phone calls and communication with families, and awareness posters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of people at each grade for Special Educational Needs Classroom Assistants, broken down by gender.
answer_text: Special Educational Needs Classroom Assistants working in schools in Northern Ireland are employed by the Education Authority (EA) or directly by schools in the Voluntary Grammar and Grant Maintained Integrated sectors.  The number of individuals by gender, is contained in the table below.Pay Grade DescriptionFemaleMaleSpecial Educational Needs Classroom Assistant (JE2)11,905852Additional Special Educational Needs Classroom Assistant (JE3)3,418311Notes:The data includes all active staff head count within the EA, VG and GMI schools, including permanent and temporary staff.The EA information is based on 23 February 2024 and does not include 13 individuals where gender information was not available.VG/GMI figures are based on reference week in the autumn term of 2022/23. Source Annual VG and GMI Support Staff Survey. In 2022/23, two VG schools and six GMI schools did not return the survey.Headcount figures may include double counting if, for example, someone works in more than one school or in more than one post.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration his Department has given to improving the average travel time on the A2 from Coleraine town to Derry City.
answer_text: The A37 between Coleraine and Limavady and the A2 between Limavady and Derry forms part of the strategic transport network.  The Department is in the process of developing a new Transport Strategy that will help to shape the vision and strategic priorities for transport through to 2035.  Once this is published, it will help to inform the Regional Strategic Transport Network Transport Plan which will consider improvements to the strategic transport network.  .

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has considered bringing forward a bill to help students struggling with (i) dyslexia; and (ii) dyscalculia.
answer_text: I am not considering legislation in these areas, at this time.Literacy and numeracy are both fundamental pillars of education and supporting children and young people who struggle in these areas is vital to ensure that EVERY CHILD is happy, learning and succeeding.  The End-to-End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) and associated workstreams are considering all options for reform across (SEN) policy areas to enable children and young people to access the right support to meet their presenting needs at the right time and that our educational workforce is appropriately resourced, confident and skilled to meet that need in the right place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has considered changing the legislation that requires restricted drivers to drive at a reduced speed limit.
answer_text: The Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 provides the statutory framework for the introduction of graduated driver licensing (GDL). This will establish a revised training, testing and post-test regime for new drivers (and riders). It will include the removal of the 45 miles per hour speed restriction on learner and new drivers.I have asked my officials for an update on the GDL programme and the potential timeline for implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what laws apply to a person driving significantly below a speed limit.
answer_text: There are no specific laws which apply to someone driving significantly below a speed limit, however, under Article 12 of the Road Traffic (NI) Order 1995 it is an offence to drive without due care or attention, or without consideration for other road users. The enforcement of this would be a matter for the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what discussions he has had with (i) the Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the Minister of Education on the provision of ADHD support services and the impact on pupils.
answer_text: Department officials recently completed a review of paediatric ADHD pathways across the region. The review involved engagement with all Trusts, including the Northern Health and Social Care Trust. Access to integrated assessment and support pathways for people with neuro-developmental presentations, such as ADHD, is currently being considered as part of the development of the HSC Children’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework. It is anticipated the Framework will standardise access to services across the region and deliver a proactive, integrated and streamlined pathway of care for all children who present to children’s services within Trusts.There are a number of current initiatives and projects where the Department of Health (DoH) and Department of Education (DE) are effectively working together to target the holistic emotional health and wellbeing needs of children and young people, including those with ADHD.This work includes the collaboration between both Departments in the development of Emotional Health and Wellbeing Frameworks which address needs in both health and education settings. These acknowledge that linkages, integration and collaboration across systems involved in each Framework are critical in their implementation.In preparation for implementation of the HSC Children’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework, Department officials facilitated a number of regional webinars and workshops bringing together all stakeholders providing support, assessment, intervention and diagnosis across neurodevelopmental and emotional health and wellbeing support pathways. This included a specific webinar for health and education staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the rationale for the decision to replace existing special schools with new builds that will accommodate fewer pupils.
answer_text: Every new Special School capital build project is sized based primarily on the needs and number of the pupils presenting.  The accommodation provided is based on a bespoke Schedule of Accommodation that is prepared and agreed in advance of project commencement. Since 2019/20, there has been a 26% increase in the total number of children with statements and an 18% rise in those attending a Special School setting.This is a significant increase in the number of pupils requiring bespoke and highly specialised accommodation, which creates a very real and immediate pressure on not only existing Special School provision but also on recently completed Special School builds.  The Department is working to plan and address the need for Special Educational Needs provision across all settings.  Therefore, in addition to new build projects, the increase in specialist accommodation required in special schools and mainstream settings is being actively addressed through the delivery of School Enhancement Programme and Minor Works projects. It is clear however that to meet the increasing specialist needs of our children across all settings in the future, will require a significant increase in current levels of capital that are available to me.  It is therefore my intention to bid for significant and sustained additional Capital Budget in 2024/25 and subsequent years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether his Department has assessed the impact blue-green algae in Lough Neagh has had on businesses in the East Derry constituency; and (ii) how his Department will support businesses negatively impacted by the algae.
answer_text: My Department is leading in the production of an Inter-Agency Monitoring Protocol to address blue-green algae issues.  This will provide guidance for land owners and businesses to consider blue-green algae risks where their business may be impacted. My Department will also continue to share water quality data to assist business owners with their considerations.There are currently no schemes within the Department that target support for businesses that have been impacted by blue green algae.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on pressures on sewerage capacity and infrastructure in East Derry.
answer_text: The issue you raised is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the social housing waiting list in East Derry, broken down by age.
answer_text: Details of the social housing waiting list in East Londonderry is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many PIP cases for applicants diagnosed with anxiety and depression were appointed zero points between January 2023 and January 2024.
answer_text: The Department does not hold the information as requested. The latest published NI Personal Independence Payment (PIP) data (up to August 2023) can be accessed via the link below which provides information on the number of people receiving PIP for psychiatric disorders as the main disabling condition:   Personal Independence Payment Statistics - August 2023 | Department for Communities (communities-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the lack of overtaking lanes between Limavady and Greysteel.
answer_text: My Department follows the standards and advice notes set out in the Design Manual for Roads and Bridges (DMRB).The DMRB outlines that inclusion of an overtaking lane requires a minimum length of 800m This requirement, when combined with the proximity of road junctions, private accesses, and general layout of the A2 Ballykelly Rd/Clooney Road between Limavady and Greysteel would not permit the introduction of overtaking lanes to current standards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of road safety on Drumagarner Road, Kilrea.
answer_text: Road safety is monitored by the Department on an ongoing basis, through the receipt of information provided by the PSNI.  This information is used to consider schemes which could improve the road network, such as resurfacing.On review of this data, the road safety record for Drumagarner Road is considered to be good, however, the department is aware of some speeding issues along the road, and is considering options to address this. Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered. As such, I am taking action to begin to address this issue by announcing the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs.This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective.However, this is only the beginning of much needed investment in our road infrastructure. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the roads in (i) Claudy village; and (ii) Park village in relation to (a) potholes; and (b) road safety.
answer_text: Due to the budgetary constraints faced by my Department as a result of a decade of Tory cuts and austerity it has been necessary to operate a limited road maintenance service for multiple years. This means that only the highest priority defects are repaired so that the budget can cover the entire year and ensures that my Department can respond to emergencies or serious defects that arise in later months. This is not a position that I want to be in, and I will continue to seek ways to restore a standard service capable of maintaining our roads in better condition.While there are currently no safety interventions planned in Claudy or Park Villages, my officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections to repair defects or identify safety improvements in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact the provisions in the Economic Activity of Public Bodies (Overseas Matters) Bill will have on local government.
answer_text: The purpose of the Bill, which will extend to England, Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland, is to prevent public bodies, when making decisions about procurement and investment, from considering a country or territory of origin or other territorial considerations in a way that indicates political or moral disapproval of a foreign state.District councils in Northern Ireland are already prevented from considering country or territory of origin in their procurement contracts under Article 19 of the Local Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Order (Northern Ireland) 1992.The ban imposed by the Bill differs from that in the 1992 Order in that it covers investment as well as procurement.I have asked my officials to keep me inform of any changes to the Bill, as it progresses through its parliamentary stages, which may have an impact on councils here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action he will take to address the challenges facing Coleraine town centre.
answer_text: Sub-regional economic development, including addressing regional imbalances, is one of my core priorities.  While development of policies to improve town centres are primarily matters for the relevant district council to take forward, my Department will work in partnership with local partners to ensure that economic development reflects local needs and priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what funds are available to provide additional classroom assistants for Special Educational Needs pupils.
answer_text: The provision of classroom assistants is demand-led following the conclusion of the statutory assessment made under Article 15 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996 in relation to the assessment of special educational needs (SEN) and then the subsequent issuing of a formal statement of SEN under Article 16 of the 1996 Order.  The statement will set out the special education provision considered necessary to meet the pupil’s SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of sexual assault counselling waiting times.
answer_text: It is assumed that this question relates to a service provided under contract with the Department. I have been advised that a waiting list of around 400 exists and that work is ongoing with the service contract holder to try to address this. A number of meetings with the service provider have taken place and a further meeting is scheduled for 26 February 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his Department is doing to reduce cervical smear result waiting times.
answer_text: Since primary HPV (human papillomavirus) testing was fully introduced into the cervical screening pathway on 11th December 2023 the turnaround time for the issuing of results has improved. The results of cervical screening samples which test negative for HPV are being issued to the woman’s GP within 2 weeks. Where screening samples test positive for HPV, cytology assessment is also carried out, so the issuing of results will take a few weeks longer.For those women whose tests were taken prior to 11th December 2023, plans are now in place to clear the backlog and release results over the following weeks.  It should be noted that the slides in the backlog had already been tested for HPV and those slides that tested positive were prioritised for cytology assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider permission for the use of anesthesia for IUD insertion in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: IUD insertion is an Enhanced Service and as such, all GP Practices are not required to provide this service in Northern Ireland.  Many insertions are carried out in Trust family planning / gynae departments.Where IUD insertion is carried out in General Practice, for the majority of individuals anaesthesia would not be required.  However, if an individual required sedated/ anaesthetised in order to carry out this procedure, the patient would need to have the IUD insertion carried out in a hospital environment where there are the appropriate expertise and facilities available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact of rape victims' counselling notes being read out in court.
answer_text: I recognise that counselling records can contain deeply personal material that we would not want others to hear and I acknowledge how difficult it can be for victims in serious sexual offence cases to have such information shared in court, which can happen as part of the disclosure process.  Research shows that victims may be so concerned about this that they may not report a crime or that they may withdraw from the criminal justice process. In some cases, victims have delayed availing of important therapy to help recover from the trauma of the crime until the case has concluded. More needs to be done to ensure that disclosure, which is an essential aspect of the court process, is managed in a way that ensure that such concerns are minimised, and that allows victims to have more control over their personal information where that is appropriate.  I am grateful to the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime for setting out her recommendations to help address these issues in her report; “A Second Assault”, and to the Lady Chief Justice for her efforts to ensure compliance with existing guidelines. I look forward to working with partners to deliver effective safeguards for victims in such cases.I am also fully committed to implementing the recommendations in Sir John Gillen’s Review of the Law and Procedures in Serious Sexual Offences. My Department, working with Victim Support NI, launched the Sexual Offences Legal Adviser (SOLA) service in April 2021. SOLAs provide free, independent legal advice to complainants in serious sexual offence cases who wish to exercise their right to appear in court to object to the disclosure of their private material to the accused’s defence team or to ensure it is restricted to the minimum necessary.   I am pleased to confirm that the SOLA service has been extended to run until 31 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the strategy to provide support for patients who have been diagnosed with ADHD.
answer_text: Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals there is currently no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The limited capacity available in Trusts in terms of meeting demand and the level of prevalence of adult ADHD is leading to individuals waiting longer than assessment and support than described in NICE guidelines.The Department of Health Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) are committed to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet NICE compliant pathways for ADHD diagnosis and support.DoH SPPG, alongside DoH policy colleagues, are considering how a future ADHD service might be delivered to meet needs, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years.  In line with the usual process for commissioning services, any decision to commission ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how his Department is assisting students with dyscalculia in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Schools are the first line in addressing any difficulties a pupil may be facing in relation to numeracy and it is important that any concerns regarding difficulties in the area of mathematics should be brought to the attention of the class teacher or the school Special Educational Needs Co-ordinator (SENCo), in the first instance. SENCos, classroom teachers and classroom assistants have had training on meeting the needs of children with a wide range of SEN, including literacy and numeracy.  They will be able to advise on how best they can support a child with this presenting need.The challenges the pupil is experiencing in relation to maths should be clearly identified within their Individual Education Plan (IEP), which should set out agreed actions for development and support, reflecting the needs of the individual pupil.  Progress should then be monitored and reviewed in consultation with the child/young person and their parent/carer in line with the Code of Practice of the Identification and Assessment of Special Educational Needs (1998).  It is important at these early stages that schools can demonstrate relevant and purposeful measures, as well as reasonable adjustments having been put in place to help support a pupil.Further support is available via the Education Authority (EA) Pupil Support Services (PSS) which can be accessed at https://send.eani.org.uk/support-servicesMy Department has also made some additional resources to schools to support pupils with Dyscalculia, eg through the online SEN Resource which provides specific advice in relation to Maths for Children with SEN including Developmental Dyscalculia, as well as other Numeracy and Mathematics applications.As part of the End-to-End (E2E) Review of SEN, a key focus will be on the supports provided to children and young people as early intervention, particularly in relation to numeracy and literacy, is fundamental to a child’s engagement with learning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's work to provide further funding for the Northlands Addiction Treatment Centre in Foyle.
answer_text: I launched Northern Ireland's new 10-year Substance Use Strategy “Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery” last September.A key action within the new Strategy is that the Public Health Agency and the Strategic Planning and Performance Group will produce, across all tiers of service, a new outcomes-focused strategic service delivery plan, to replace the Alcohol and Drug Services Commissioning Framework. This new plan will: ensure that the population of NI have access to a continuum of service with clear pathways and step up/step down provision;ensure equal access to community detox for alcohol and other drugs across NI;ensure that all services are delivered in line with the UK-wide “Drug Misuse and Dependence: Guidelines on Clinical Management” and NICE Guidelines; provide support to address the wider physical, mental health, and wellbeing needs of those in treatment, including housing, education, employment, personal finance, healthcare e.g. should be supported to stop smoking and address other physical health conditions; andrecognise the importance of co-production and strengthen joint working between the community/voluntary sector, service users and peers, and the Health & Social Care Sector.In addition, two early pieces of work are commencing – a population needs-based assessment of substance use and related services in the Western Health & Social Care Trust area and a review of inpatient Tier 4 services across Northern Ireland. These will inform the strategic delivery plan and it is the intention that all future funding decisions around substance use related services will be based on the new commissioning framework. The full delivery of the strategy will be dependent on additional funding being available into the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an estimated timeline for completion of the road works in the Mountsandel area of Coleraine.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the percentage of Special Educational Needs and Disability Tribunal appeals won by parents or carers in 2020-21.
answer_text: The Special Educational Needs and Disability Tribunal (SENDIST) is an independent Tribunal and, therefore, the Department of Education (DE) does not hold the information you have requested.  You may wish to write directly to: The Special Educational Needs and Disability Tribunal (SENDIST) 2nd FloorRoyal Courts of JusticeChichester StreetBelfast BT1 3JFor via e-mail to  SENDIST@courtsni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how much the Education Authority spent on Special Educational Needs and Disability Tribunal appeals in 2020-21.
answer_text: On the basis of information held, Education Authority (EA) Solicitors have advised:In respect of Special Educational Needs and Disability Tribunals (SENDIST) appeals in which the EA is involved, these are handled internally by professional and administrative staff within EA Solicitors.  On occasion, Counsel is instructed by EA Solicitors.  However, during the financial year 2020/21, the EA did not make any payment in respect of Counsels' fees arising from SENDIST appeals. The EA, as part of its statutory maintenance duties, is required to meet legal costs (including solicitors' and Counsels' fees) incurred by Catholic maintained schools.  During the financial year 2020/21, the sum of £17,788.52 inclusive of VAT was spent by the EA in respect of legal costs arising from SENDIST appeals initiated against Catholic maintained schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what action he has taken to deliver the New Decade, New Approach commitment for additional funding to support an addiction centre in the North West.
answer_text: In the context of the agreement to restore the NI Executive, the UK Government outlined Financial and Economic Commitments to Northern Ireland in Annex A to the “New Decade, New Approach” (NDNA) document. Under the section Addressing Northern Ireland’s Unique Circumstances, the UK Government stated that it would make funding available for a range of projects aimed at supporting community and reconciliation initiatives to remove barriers, bring the people together and build a safer, more secure society, which could include areas such as additional funding to support the Derry/Londonderry addiction centre.Following discussions with the Northern Ireland Office (NIO), my officials, along with the Public Health Agency (PHA) and the Strategic Planning & Performance Group (SPPG) as the commissioners of substance use services, have been looking at how best to develop potential proposals for the allocation of funding under this commitment.As an initial step, a population needs-based assessment of substance use and related services in the Western Health & Social Care Trust area is now underway and a review of inpatient Tier 4 services across Northern Ireland is also being commissioned.In addition, my Department is leading on the implementation of Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery, NI’s new substance use strategy. As part of this, SPPG in association with PHA are in the process of developing an approach to Multi-Agency Strategic Planning and Commissioning. It is the intention that a comprehensive, whole-system, planning approach to substance use will ensure that demographic trends of increasing demand and complexity, current and future resource, gaps, service pressures and workforce issues will be addressed.All of these pieces of work will be utilised so that my Department can make informed proposals to the NIO around this NDNA commitment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department is ensuring that GP services are fully accessible for the elderly via (i) telephone; and (ii) internet services.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of ensuring that our HSC facilities and services, including primary care services, are accessible to everyone who needs to use them.  There are a variety of contact methods available to patients who wish to access GP services which offer increased accessibility.  These include telephone contact, using online options available on their GP practice website, the use of video consultations or through a face to face appointment, if considered clinically appropriate.  Clinicians will determine the most appropriate approach to safely addressing a patient’s needs and, on occasion, may consider that one mode of appointment is more appropriate than another given a particular patient's circumstances.GP practices in Northern Ireland are expected to address need within the requirements of equality, disability and human rights legislation.  Each practice can determine how best to do this within their local context.  Telephone consultations can allow for the freeing up of GP time to address the more complex needs of patients, including the older cohort of patients.  This approach also ensures that those older patients who do not need to be seen face to face can avoid the inconvenience of an avoidable journey to their local surgery.  For the majority of patient contacts, telephone consultations do not require access to a smartphone.  Only a small number of practices use software algorithms to help manage practice triage.  It should also be noted that practices will be familiar with the particular circumstances of their older patients, their level of family support and their capabilities with respect to technology and can respond accordingly to reflect this.  My Department has made significant investment to enable GPs to improve their telephony infrastructure and improve accessibility, such as the use of on-line systems for ordering repeat prescriptions, helping to free up telephone lines and staff time.  This investment has also allowed GP practices to employ additional staff to manage their systems efficiently.Funding for equipment has been provided for surgery Jayex boards and T loops (audio induction) which will improve the quality of access for older people with hearing difficulties. The Quality and Outcomes Framework (QOF) rewards contractors for the provision of quality care and helps to standardise improvements in the delivery of primary medical services.  The 2022/23 Framework includes an indicator and guidance to support improved access for patients with hearing loss.  The Guidance asks practices to review their access arrangements for patients who are deaf or who have hearing loss and provides advice for good practice.  It also asks practices to consider offering a range of different methods to contact the surgery e.g. text messaging, email and Video Relay Service (VRS).Interpreter services such as The Big Word are available for older patients when English is not their first language.  GP practices have also received informat

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what discussions her Department has had with the Department of Health in relation to health-related study bursaries negatively impacting student’s Universal Credit allowance.
answer_text: My Department has not had any recent discussions with the Department of Health in relation to health-related study bursaries and their impact on students’ Universal Credit awards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action is being taken to address nursing students losing a large portion of their Universal Credit allowance, due to being awarded a Department of Health funded bursary.
answer_text: When a nursing student claims Universal Credit they will need to provide details of any income including bursaries they receive from the Department of Health. Relevant disregards are applied to their bursary based on the breakdown of their student finance and their individual circumstances.Changes to the current method of calculating a nursing student’s Universal Credit payment would not be able to be progressed without the necessary legislative and funding arrangements being put in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what percentage of schools utilise Love for Life programmes to deliver Relationship & Sexuality Education in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: It is a matter for individual schools to determine which resources or programmes they wish to use to support their delivery of the curriculum.  I am aware Love for Life is one of a number of external providers which some schools choose to use to support their delivery of aspects of Relationships and Sexuality Education.owever

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what training GP’s receive on how to (i) support rape victims; and (ii) signpost victims to the correct support services.
answer_text: GPs are expected to identify and undertake educational activity based on their own assessment of their professional development needs and this is facilitated through compulsory annual appraisal. GPs are also expected to be familiar with relevant local and regional services and refer to these as required.The Rowan is the regional Sexual Assault Referral Centre (SARC) in Northern Ireland.
