It provides support and services 24 hours a day, all year to children, young people, women and men who have been sexually abused, assaulted or raped in the past.The Rowan or SARC is likely to be the source of information and support that GPs use to support patients who present to them following sexual assault or rape. The management of victims of rape is appropriately centralised and GPs act as a support and to signpost as necessary.It has a website http://therowan.net/women which contains centralised resources for further support, guidance and information. In relation to GP trainees, the topic is covered in the locality training and in GP practice when such patients present to primary care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to identify what funding from his Department went to support victims of rape in 2021/2022.
answer_text: The Rowan is the regional Sexual Assault Referral Centre in Northern Ireland. It is managed by the Northern Health and Social Care Trust and offers support 24 hours a day to women, men, children and young people who have been sexually abused, assaulted or raped in the past or recently. Services include forensic medical examination, emergency contraception, risk assessment of HIV, assessment and treatment for sexually transmitted infections, emotional support, and referral to counselling. In 2021/2022, Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) allocated funding to the Northern Health and Social Care Trust to the value of £100,000.SPPG also commission Nexus NI to deliver counselling, education and research in surviving sexual violence and abuse.  The funding allocation for 2021/2022 was £854,000.The regional Domestic and Sexual Abuse Helpline is also available to support victims of sexual violence and abuse. The Helpline is a confidential, Freephone service, funded jointly by my Department, the Department of Justice and the Department for Communities. In 2021/2022 my Department provided funding of approximately £127,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether training to support victims of rape is mandatory for the PSNI.
answer_text: Specific training received by PSNI officers is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. For details of PSNI training the member would need to direct the question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what training does the PSNI receive to supporting victims of rape.
answer_text: The Gillen Strategic Training Group was established to implement the recommendations relating to training as set out in the Gillen Review. Both my Department and the PSNI are represented on the Group.Specific training received by PSNI officers is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. For details of PSNI training the member would need to direct the question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures have been taken to ensure that students receiving bursaries for nursing are not negatively impacted when seeking means-tested benefits.
answer_text: Nursing and midwifery students already in receipt of other means-tested benefits need to liaise with their benefits adviser to determine the implications of the bursary support provided by my Department.  While these will vary according to individual’s circumstances my understanding is that any adverse impact would be minimal and would relate to a very small number of the over 3000 nursing and midwifery students currently supported by my Department in their studies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of how difficulties in accessing primary care impacts Personal Independence Payments outcomes in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Personal Independence Payment (PIP) application process does not place any onus on the applicant to provide medical evidence to support their claim. The need for further medical evidence is considered as part of the Initial Review completed by Capita on every case. In most cases the functional assessment completed by Capita, coupled with the information provided by the customer in the PIP2 form, is likely to be sufficient to accurately assess the impact of a person’s condition(s) or disability on their ability to complete everyday tasks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when AQW 3693/22-27 will be answered.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline what support services are available to young people who are struggling with gambling.
answer_text: At present, there are no specific mainstream support services for gambling related harm in Northern Ireland for adults or young people. Whilst addiction services at HSC Trust level are not commissioned to cover gambling issues, individuals aged 18 and over with a gambling addiction may receive support through HSC Trust addiction services if the primary presenting issue is substance use.  There are also Children and Adolescent Mental Health support services for young people who present with mental health issues, and alongside this struggle with gambling, where appropriate support measures can be put in place. Cross departmental work is ongoing in this area and as part of this my officials are considering the recommendations within the All-Party Group on Reducing Harm Related to Gambling Inquiry Report into the Future Regulation of Gambling in Northern Ireland, which includes the establishment of a specialised young people’s gambling service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans are in place to address social housing need in Ballykelly.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“As part of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) 2024/25 – 2026/27, a 17-unit proposal at Oakdene Crescent (former KFC site) is being investigated by Ark Housing for potential to start on-site in 2024/25. There are no further schemes programmed to start in Ballykelly as part of the SHDP at this stage”. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly at  Public.Affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what staff resources are available in his Department to deliver the Protect Life 2 Strategy.
answer_text: The Suicide Prevention team within the Department of Health is fully staffed. There are also a very wide range of staff implementing and delivering the Strategy across the Health and Social Care sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact of the proposed Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill on (i) Habitats; (ii) Water Quality; (iii) Wild Birds; and (iv) Bathing Waters regulations, in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The UK Government has recently introduced the Retained EU Law (REUL) (Revocation and Reform) Bill which proposes that retained EU Law is repealed, amended or extended from 31 December 2023. My officials are considering the potential impact of the REUL Bill along with colleagues across the NICS and in other UK jurisdictions.  I can confirm that Habitats, Water Quality, Wild Birds and Bathing Waters Regulations fall within the scope and my Department will be assessing the specific impact on each regulatory area, as the specific requirements of the Bill become clearer.It is my intention going forward that Northern Ireland continues to have a clear statutory basis for protecting the environment and human health, as has been the case throughout the changes resulting from the UK’s withdrawal from the EU.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update from his Department on what initiatives are available in Northern Ireland to help address loneliness.
answer_text: My Department has completed a scoping exercise and a literature review across Northern Ireland to identify existing policy initiatives and projects which address loneliness. Addressing loneliness is a vital theme within a number of existing and relevant Department of Health policies with the overall objective of improving the health and wellbeing of the population.  Examples include: The Reform of Adult Social Care; the Social Work Strategy; the Mental Health Strategy; the Autism Strategy and the Strategy for Looked After Children, ‘A Life Deserved: “Caring” for Children and Young People in Northern Ireland. My Department has provided funding to Health and Social Care Trusts as well as the community and voluntary sector across Northern Ireland to support initiatives in addressing loneliness. Multi-Disciplinary Primary Care Teams in collaboration with the community and voluntary sector across Northern Ireland continue to play a significant role in developing and enhancing service initiatives which address the issue of loneliness.  There are loneliness networks across Northern Ireland, Antrim and Newtownabbey Loneliness Network, Mid and East Antrim Loneliness Network, Causeway Loneliness Network and the Mid Ulster Loneliness Network. Community, voluntary and statutory partners have come together from all council areas in Northern Ireland to raise greater awareness around loneliness, share good practice learning and initiate actions which address loneliness and its causes. My department has continued to support and fund some of their loneliness prevention initiatives on Chatty Benches, Chatty Cafes, and the kindness post initiative.  By breaking down social barriers and encouraging people to connect and chat, the chatty benches and cafes initiative is a small but very effective way of reducing loneliness and Isolation.The Kind Postbox was started in Northern Ireland with the intent to bring a gift of kindness to residents of local care homes that had been affected by Covid by matching school children with home residents and getting them to write letters to someone they had never met. This continues to be a very successful initiative and promoted throughout Northern Ireland by its founder Nuala O Toole. My department continues to chair a regional interdepartmental and HSC Trust leads loneliness forum which continues to identify and promote policy and service initiatives in addressing the issue of loneliness across Northern Ireland such as those noted above.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration is being given to assist private renters, given the recently published survey by Renter’s Voice which indicates that two out of three respondents have said that they currently cannot afford to cover the cost of all their household expenses.
answer_text: I note the Renter’s Voice survey referred to and the follow up survey, conducted in February 2023, had relatively small sample sizes. That said, the findings chime with wider evidence about the difficulties that lower income households are facing in meeting household expenses. Rents are generally a household’s largest single expense. It is these households that are then most likely to have been most impacted by the freeze in Local Housing Allowance. The increase in Local Housing Allowance rates from last month, some of which have increased by over 40 percent, will go some way in helping renters pay their rent and relieve pressure on their household budgets.  Other forms of assistance that may be available to these households, includes: Discretionary Support – this scheme provides financial support to people on a low income who find themselves in an extreme, exceptional or crisis situation. Awards can be made as either a non-repayable grant or an interest free loan to assist with the purchase of household items (e.g. a cooker) or to assist with short-term living expenses; Discretionary Housing Payments - provide additional financial assistance with housing costs for tenants in the Private Rented Sector who may face a shortfall in meeting the cost of their contractual rent because their Universal Credit or Housing Benefit does not fully cover their rent charge; andWinter Fuel Payments and Cold Weather Payments – to help people pay their heating bills. Over the past two years these households may also have been eligible to receive Cost of Living Payments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for (i) an update on the rollout of Project Stratum in the East Derry constituency; (ii) a list of areas within the constituency where work has commenced; and (iii) what action is being taken to address anomalies from the Pointer database of addresses.
answer_text: As you are aware, Project Stratum was developed to improve connectivity for premises unable to access broadband services of 30 Mbps or greater, primarily across rural areas of Northern Ireland. The project has been allocated £197m of public funding, £150m of which was secured under the Confidence and Supply Agreement between the DUP and the Conservatives. Fibrus Networks was appointed as the contractor in November 2020 and is currently delivering gigabit-capable broadband to 85,000 premises in rural areas across Northern Ireland.The number of premises in the East Londonderry Constituency to benefit from Project Stratum is 3,296. Deployment in East Londonderry commenced in autumn 2021 and will continue until winter 2024. So far, 2,646 premises have been passed by the project and are able to order a gigabit service.The table below provides a breakdown of the areas within the East Londonderry Constituency where deployment is taking place, along with an update on deployment. While not all the towns listed in the table are in the East Londonderry Constituency, the deployment area associated with each town includes premises which are located within the constituency. This table only refers to premises which are located within the East Londonderry Constituency. AreaNumber includedPassedremainingBallymoney2922920Castlerock3433430Claudy98098Garvagh1,4251,39134Limavady2340234Portstewart5975961Premises added in January 202230724283		In January 2022, my Department secured additional capital funding of £32 million to bring a further 8,500 premises into Project Stratum, of which approximately 6,000 were premises not considered for inclusion during the Open Market Review due to anomalies in Land & Property Services’ Pointer address database.My Department recently conducted a Review to identify further premises which may now be eligible for intervention but were not previously categorised as eligible because of the limitations in the datasets used, or because of coverage claims by infrastructure providers, which have now changed or cannot be sufficiently substantiated. Inclusion in Project Stratum will depend on the outcome of this Review, and the availability of funding and/or capacity. However, my Department remains hopeful that following this Review, additional premises will be brought into scope of the Project Stratum contract. My Department expects to be in a position to provide further information regarding the outcome of this Review in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a timeline for completion of the A6 road.
answer_text: The pandemic has had a major impact on delivery of the A6 scheme with various activities having been disrupted due to social distancing requirements, staff absences and difficulties with the supply chain. Current market volatility is also impacting on delivery timescales.Despite these exceptional challenges, my Department and our contractors have sought to maintain progress on this strategically important road scheme. Although the scheme is now well advanced, a significant amount of work has still to be completed before it can be fully opened, which includes the provision of safety barrier, road marking, signage, street lighting and the completion of a Road Safety Audit.My officials continue to work closely with the contractor to have this work completed as quickly as possible in order that the road can be opened in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many children were diagnosed with dyscalculia in 2020-21.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on when a pupil received their diagnosis. The total number of pupils recorded by schools with dyscalculia in the 2020-21 academic year was 757.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what discussions she has had with the Education Authority on the need for a youth club and other youth service provision in Park village, East Derry.
answer_text: All youth service provision is based on regional and local assessment of need with work to agree the assessment of need for the 2023 – 2026 period nearing completion. The assessment of need for the Derry City and Strabane Council Area will inform youth service provision for children and young people from the age 4 to 25 across the Sperrin District Electoral Area. With specific regard to the Park Village area, the EA has advised that the Area Youth Work team have engaged with staff at Learmount Community Development Group with a view to supporting the Park Youth Drop-in at Park Village. EA are currently in the process of recruiting Area Youth Work staff who will be periodically deployed across multiple locations, one of which will be Park Village. In the interim the EA Youth Worker for FLARE (Facilitating Life and Resilience Education) will link with Learmount Community Development Group to support the Emotional, Health and Wellbeing of young people in this locality, through the Park Youth Drop-in. In addition, EA Youth Service are currently operating a mobile youth unit which enables youth work staff to connect with those young people unattached to youth/other services in the Park Village area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Task Force action plan; and (ii) what conversations his Department has had with councils in relation to an EV charging network.
answer_text: As you are aware, my Department has set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force which brings together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider our EV Infrastructure requirements and to help set out a clear action plan by autumn 2022 to deliver a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network. Derry City and Strabane District Council which leads the On-street Residential Charge Point Scheme (ORCS) council consortium is represented on this Task-Force. The Action Plan will be submitted to me shortly.Funding opportunities for the installation of charge point infrastructure in GB-NI are provided by the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV). A local council consortium, led by Derry City and Strabane District Council, submitted a joint application to the Energy Saving Trust which manages the fund on behalf of OZEV, on 30 March 2022. It received a Grant Offer Letter from OZEV in July of funding to install 124 charge points across 9 council areas. My Department is represented, in an advisory capacity, on the ORCS council consortium.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what support is available for children diagnosed with dyscalculia in (i) primary; and (ii) secondary education.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the reply I gave to AQW 23515/17-22 on 15 October 2021.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) what steps his Department is taking to replace the funding lost due to the cessation of European Social Fund; and (ii) for his assessment of the impact that the loss of this funding will have on communities.
answer_text: My Department lead on the loss of EU Funds for the Executive. The loss of the European Social Fund (ESF) and the need for replacement has been an area of significant concern for me. I have met with a number of groups who are in receipt of ESF funding and have listened to their concerns. I have discussed the loss of ESF at length with British Government Ministers and continue to press this issue with them and I have kept my Executive colleagues updated on these issues. The Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) has been introduced by the British Government as a replacement for the European Social Fund (ESF). This fund raises significant concerns; it has not been subjected to screening under our equalities legislation, it will operate directly from Whitehall bypassing Executive Departments, it will deliver too little money, risks duplication of provision and lacks alignment to our local priorities.The impact of this approach on communities is not yet fully measurable but the British Governments centralised approach to this fund has crystallised a significant loss of spending power for the Executive. This loss and Whitehall’s refusal to afford us an appropriate design and decision making role in this fund will prevent us from providing much needed assurance to communities.I remain committed to securing as much funding for here as possible, and will not stand in the way of any funds flowing to the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the impact that planned roadworks will have on (i) businesses; (ii) traffic; and (iii) health and safety, in Coleraine.
answer_text: My Department is not aware of any specific problems likely to arise from planned roadworks in Coleraine.My Department carefully plans road works and associated traffic management arrangements in order to minimise inconvenience to businesses and the travelling public.  Contractors are encouraged to ensure businesses and residents are made aware of any planned road works, particularly road closures.  There is a requirement for signs for roadworks to be in accordance with legislation so that road users are clear as to how to negotiate the roadworks or associated diversionsInformation on roadworks is uploaded onto the Trafficwatch NI website.  Depending on the scale of the works, residents and business may also be contacted by letter drop, social media or other means to inform them of upcoming roadworks and how traffic will be managed at those sites.Health and safety during roadworks is the responsibility of the Contractor with obligations set out in legislation including the Health and Safety at Work (NI) Order 1978, the Management of Health and Safety at Work (NI) Regulations 2000 and the Construction (Design and Management) Regulations (NI) 2016.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has considered harsher sentences for child sex offenders in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Sexual Offences (Northern Ireland) Order 2008 (the 2008 Order) provides a robust sentencing framework with maximum penalties available to enable the court to deal appropriately with a range of sexual offending, including against children.The decision on the appropriate sentence within the maximum set is a matter entirely for the independent judiciary, taking into account the particular circumstances of the individual case. Where a sentence is considered unduly lenient, the Director of the Public Prosecutions has the power to refer certain cases to the Court of Appeal for reconsideration.  While I have no current plans to review the maximum penalties available in the 2008 Order, I have taken steps to ensure that, when presented with evidence of new types of sexual offending, the legislative framework is amended accordingly and to ensure that a wider range of sentences can be appealed by the Director of Public Prosecutions on grounds of undue leniency. I have also introduced a number of new offences in the Justice (Sexual Offences & Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022. This Act amends the 2008 Order to create new offences of up-skirting, down-blousing and cyber-flashing. It also criminalises adults who pretend to be children in order to communicate with a child under 16 with a view to grooming a child; a provision which is unique to Northern Ireland. In addition, the scope of the existing offences, in the 2008 Order, of the abuse of a position of trust of a child has been extended beyond the current statutory sector settings to sport and religious settings: further extension of this provision remains under review and can be done via regulations should evidence of need emerge.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of current student loans in Northern Ireland compared to (i) England; (ii) Scotland; and (iii) Wales.
answer_text: For the current 2022/23 academic year my Department continues to provide student support to eligible higher education students in the form of a means tested maintenance loan of up to £3,750 for students living at home, £4,840 for those living away from home and studying outside of London, and up to £6,780 for students living in London.  In addition, my Department provides a means tested maintenance grant of up to £3,475. In relation to tuition fees, my Department provides a tuition fee loan of up to £4,630 for students studying at an eligible Higher Education Institution (HEI) in Northern Ireland and up to £9,250 for NI students studying at an eligible HEI throughout the rest of the UK. For students currently studying or starting a higher education course in the Republic of Ireland, they may be eligible for a loan to cover the full cost of the student contribution charge, which is €3,000.As higher education is a devolved matter, each of the four jurisdictions across the UK has responsibility for developing its own policies. A meaningful comparison that focuses solely on student loans offered across the UK administrations cannot be drawn due to the divergences in how higher education is funded as a whole.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the current protocol for housing child sex offenders in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“For applicants who are subject to Public Protection Arrangements Northern Ireland (PPANI), which covers certain sexual and violent offenders, the Housing Executive will assess the suitability of any offer of a temporary placement or permanent accommodation by means of a check with the relevant Designated Risk Manager (DRM) where required.Engagement with the DRM ensures that any offer of accommodation is suitable.  Article 50 of the Criminal Justice (N.I.) Order 2008, specifies the agencies (including the Housing Executive) which have a duty to co-operate to ensure effective assessment and management of the risks posed by certain sexual and violent offenders.  Overall responsibility for PPANI lies with the Department for Justice.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how her Department will include the views of rural community groups in the co-design process of the People and Place Strategy.
answer_text: The review is underway to develop a new ‘People and Place Strategy’ with an inclusive co-design approach with structures established to facilitate inputs and views from a wide range of stakeholders involved in tackling poverty and deprivation including local rural groups, and rural networks. Locality Co-Design Groups have been established within each council area and includes representatives from community and voluntary sector, including rural groups, local council, and statutory agencies. In addition, the rural perspective is provided by rural community network to a regional advisory group which was established to provide input from both an academic and lived experience. Updates on the People and Place co-design process and structures can be accessed on my Departments website https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/articles/people-and-place-review

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the creation of an anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: My Department continues to lead a significant programme of work in the co-design of the Executive’s social inclusion strategies. 	The drafting of the Anti-Poverty Strategy is ongoing in readiness for presentation to an incoming Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the sports funding from her Department in East Derry, in the last five years, broken down by District Electoral Areas.
answer_text: Sports funding awards made by my Department and its arm’s length body, Sport NI, are recorded on the Government Funding Database (https://govfundingpublic.nics.gov.uk/Home.aspx) .

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the need for youth facilities in Park Village in East Derry.
answer_text: Neither my Department nor its Arm’s Length Body, Sport NI, have been made aware of any sport facility need in Park Village.	The Community Planning Partnership for Derry City and Strabane District Council is the appropriate forum for such needs to be highlighted by the local community. Sport NI is one of a number of statutory bodies engaged in this partnership.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has considered increasing student grants for university students pursing a (i) Bachelor’s Degree; and (ii) Master’s Degree.
answer_text: In terms of undergraduate student support, any increase in the level of support provided through the higher education student support package is assessed on an annual basis and subject to the financial position of my Department at that time. With regard to postgraduate support, my Department is currently considering the responses to the public consultation on postgraduate support along with policy options, and I hope to publish a formal consultation response in the coming weeks. At that point I will be able to advise of any possible changes to postgraduate policy and funding, and indicate when these changes will be implemented. However, any outcomes, particularly in relation to the levels of financial support available, will be dependent on the funding that is available to the Department over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department has considered providing additional funding to breakfast clubs in (i) primary; and (ii) secondary schools.
answer_text: In 2022/23, funding of £9.1m was made available to schools for the Extended Schools (ES) programme, which has been in place since 2006. The programme currently enables around 480 schools serving the most disadvantaged communities to provide a range of interventions and additional learning opportunities outside of the traditional school day. Provision is determined by individual schools, in response to the particular needs of their pupils, parents, families and the wider community. Many schools choose to offer breakfast and after-school clubs to pupils.In view of the on-going financial crisis faced by many families, I would welcome an increase in funding to DE's baseline for programmes like the Extended Schools Programme but appreciate that there are many other issues facing the NI Block and indeed, in light of the advised 2022/23 indicative baseline, the Department of Education alone is currently facing inescapable or pre-committed pressures in excess of £500m.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how much her Department funded debt and advice services in the East Derry constituency, in the year of 2020/21.
answer_text: My Department invested in the region of £230,000 in the year 2020/21 in the delivery of independent, community based advice services within the Causeway Coast and Glen Borough Council Area, which services the majority of the constituency.  This included dedicated support for social security appeals and help with problem debt. In addition to this, my Department provided Causeway Coast and Glen Borough Council with approximately £58,000 of additional monies from the Covid-19 Financial Inclusion Fund to deliver advice services throughout the Covid 19 pandemic.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a timeline on the planned social housing build of 14 units in Claudy, East Derry constituency.
answer_text: The Housing Executive is not currently aware of a proposed 14-unit social housing New Build scheme planned for Claudy. There is no scheme of this nature currently included in the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of people under 18 years old legally receiving Botulinum toxin Type A in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information on under 18 years olds legally receiving Botulinum toxin Type A.  However, I can advise that my Department’s Medicines Regulatory Group (MRG) carried out an enforcement and compliance programme into the provision of Botox over a 6 month period. Initial investigations and assessments have been conducted into over 200 individual providers of Botulinum Toxin Type A.  Shortcomings in prescribing processes and breaches of advertising legislation have been identified.  Those in breach of legislation have been issued formal advice and warning notices where appropriate and have moved into legal compliance in the vast majority of cases investigated.  In addition, formal referrals have been made to professional bodies, e.g. GMC where appropriate.  The MRG continues to monitor the situation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department has plans to provide for classroom assistants to be paid outside term time.
answer_text: The majority of Classroom Assistants working in schools in Northern Ireland are employed by the Education Authority (EA) on Conditions of Service determined by the National Joint Committee (NJC) for Local Government Services. Within these Conditions there are various arrangements in place that impact on how Classroom Assistants are paid, including a range of flexible working options. The three main types of Classroom Assistant contracts are as follows:  Term time working, plus up to an additional two weeks annually. In these cases, staff are paid for hours worked, with a further payment in lieu of annual leave. 52 week contracts, which require classroom assistants to be on duty for contracted weekly hours during periods of school closure except when on annual leave or other authorised absence. 52 weeks contracts, with an option to request periods of unpaid leave during extended school holidays or closures.In line with established negotiating arrangements, Trade Unions have recently raised with EA Management the issue of existing term time staff moving to a 52 week contract, if they wish to do so.  The EA are currently considering this request and will be liaising further with Trade Unions on this issue.  They note that it may be appropriate to consider this request within the wider context of a non-teaching pay & grading review and the SEND Transformation Programme, a strand of which includes a project to review the Special Classroom Assistant Employment model.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what training has been provided to assessors of Disability Living Allowance in relation to applicants who are neurodivergent.
answer_text: Decisions on entitlement to Disability Living Allowance are made by Decision Makers appointed by the Department, who are civil servants. All Decision Makers within Disability and Carers Service are extensively trained on the eligibility criteria for Disability Living Allowance and how to apply it when deciding claims. They also have access to online guidance, developed by leading medical experts, which provides the most up to date information on medical conditions. Health Professionals are also available to provide a daily advice service to Disability Living Allowance Decision Makers.		Officials are consistently striving to improve their knowledge and understanding of all aspects of their work and opportunities for improvement are always kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what mitigations his Department can put in place in Mark Street Lane, Portrush, to ensure ease of access for emergency service vehicles.
answer_text: As you are aware, Mark Street Lane is a private lane which has not been adopted by my Department. Measures to maintain access would therefore be a matter for the owners and/or residents of the laneway.  Parking restrictions, such as double yellow lines, cannot be installed or legislated for by my Department on a private road so the onus is on those parking there to do so considerately so it remains accessible for other vehicles, including the emergency services.    Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many times in the last six months have there been disruptions to patients receiving their oxygen tank orders; and (ii) what assurances can be given to those patients that this will be mitigated for in future.
answer_text: Data is only available for the months of June, July & August 2022, when approximately 27% of cylinder deliveries were disrupted. This is where, for example, a patient ordered 6 x cylinders but may have received 4 or 5 instead. It is important to note that no patient is being denied oxygen. They are instead being asked to, where possible, use their oxygen as circumspectly as possible.  Supplies are being managed as described above to ensure that no patient is left without their oxygen. BOC and the SPPG are planning a communication to all patients by mid-October to update them on the efforts being made and to provide assurances. BOC is continuing to monitor the situation closely as it is clear that the impact of COVID is still playing out.  The demand for oxygen continues to rise monthly, with projections now that total demand in 2022 will end at +8 or +9% above that of 2021. Longer-term solutions are described below, along with current efforts to manage the situation, but are not expected to impact fully until Q1 2023. With winter pressures still to come, it is clear the situation requires the constant vigilance and planning that is currently being undertaken. The supply of oxygen to patients in Northern Ireland is under constant review by all stakeholders involved, including BOC as the oxygen supplier, lead respiratory clinicians from across Northern Ireland and the SPPG (formerly Health & Social Care Board) via the home oxygen contract. Indeed, these parties have never worked more closely than in recent years to manage the pandemic and its impact on demand for oxygen. Many inputs are required to deliver a home oxygen service efficiently. These include how oxygen is prescribed; which oxygen modalities are prescribed; the availability of these modalities; and, crucially, that the use of oxygen in the community is reviewed by assessment-and-review services to ensure appropriate usage by all patients. All factors interplay with the supply of oxygen so the situation is not as straightforward as simply supplying ever-increasing amounts of oxygen indefinitely for example. Oxygen cylinders need to be returned from the community; patients need to use oxygen safely and appropriately; clinicians need to prescribe to best-practice guidelines; patients require regular review; and all ambulatory oxygen modalities (cylinders and battery-powered devices) need to be available in good supply. All the key stakeholders referenced above continue to work together to ensure that all these components are optimised to minimise disruption around oxygen supply. Further steps in place to deal with the ongoing demand include:BOC has increased its workforce and further recruitment is ongoing, with new fillers to be in place and fully trained by Jan ‘23BOC has purchased thousands of additional cylinders throughout 2021 and 2022 BOC installed a new silencing system at its Belfast plant in September 2022 to allow us to, by January 2023, fill cylinders 24/7 @ Belfas

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to improve pay and mileage allowances for domiciliary care workers.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the continuing impact that the increase in living costs and fuel prices is having across Northern Ireland, particularly in relation to those who use their vehicles to deliver services as part of their day to day roles.In regards to the statutory sector I was pleased to recently be able to ensure a mileage rate increase for Agenda for Change (AfC) staff, including Trust employed domiciliary care workers and district nurses. I have approved a 50% increase to the secondary rate on annual mileage over 3,500 miles to bring it up to 30p per mile. This measure, which will be for an initial six month period, to be reviewed, will benefit those who are required to use their vehicles most frequently. However, as you are aware this particular increase does not apply to the independent health and social care sector.Work has been ongoing to deliver additional funding to the independent sector, both in-year and on a recurrent basis, to try to alleviate the ongoing pressures on the system. The £23m funding package which I approved in November 2021, was intended to stabilise and enhance capacity within the independent domiciliary care and wider social care sector. This investment has allowed providers to deliver enhanced rates of pay and improve other terms and conditions, which may also include increased mileage rates, at individual employers’ discretion. This additional funding secured an increase in the hours of domiciliary care delivered by the independent sector and helped to stabilise the care sector during a very difficult period. Despite a very challenging financial context I have ensured that funding was secured to ensure this uplift was carried through to the current year and has been made recurrent. I recognise however that the independent sector continues to face significant difficulties. I have therefore tasked officials to continue engaging with the sector on ongoing challenges, including those arising from travel and the cost of living. However, given the financial pressures and budgetary uncertainty, there remains limited room for manoeuvre in terms of additional funding.   You may be aware that a public consultation on the Reform of Adult Social Care recently closed. Proposals contained within this consultation will see wider ranging changes to the delivery of social care, including the consideration of recognition and reward of staff. However, this reform package will require sustained additional investment and budgetary certainty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what financial support is available to rural sporting clubs who provide floodlit walking nights in their grounds, to allow rural residents to exercise safely.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.	My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.	Details of current funding opportunities available from Sport NI can be found on its website Sport NI – Funding (www.sportni.net/funding/). Additionally, details of Government Grants paid to the Voluntary and Community Sector are accessible via the Government Funding Database (https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consultation was carried out with domiciliary care staff and unions ahead of the changes made to the delivery of domiciliary care in the Western Trust.
answer_text: Colleagues in Department of Health have consulted with the Western Health and Social Care Trust regarding this.  Western Trust have issued this response:‘The Homecare staff members in Limavady were first advised of plans to change in early 2021 as the initial implementation was scheduled for 1st November 2021.  In October 2021 the full detail of the service change process was shared with all affected staff.  This planned implementation was temporarily stood down, initially due to operational challenges and then further delayed due to Covid service pressures.  Staff were routinely informed by their line management in respect of progress.  The implementation process was re-established in spring 2022 with a planned implementation of September 2022.  Homecare Managers ensured that all affected staff were fully aware of the plans and progress.Homecare managers met with UNISON trade union representatives [Regional Organiser and Local Representatives] on 5 occasions from November 2021 to June 2022.  Homecare Optimisation was discussed in detail at each of these meetings.  The Trust has had weekly meetings with UNISON since the changes have been implemented.’Western Trust have reported that as a result of optimising rotas they have been able to provide additional package of care to enable people to leave care homes and hospital and return home with a package of care.You will be aware that on 24 November 2021 I announced a support package of up to £23m for the independent domiciliary and wider social care sector. This additional funding has been provided with the expectation that providers will not only maintain current service levels, but increase capacity and be in a position to reach out particularly into rural areas.  £25 million has been released for 2022/2023 period.Care hours delivered up to year ending March 2022 have increased regionally compared to figures at year end 2017.There remain ongoing challenges with providing domiciliary care packages due to difficulties with recruitment and retention of domiciliary care staff with particular challenges in rural areas due to high fuel costs. Providers operate rolling recruitment exercises in an attempt to fill workforce vacancies. I will continue to support the domiciliary care sector in their attempts to meet the current demand for packages of care in the community.  The local HSC Trusts are promoting Direct Payments as an alternative to traditional packages of care and we would encourage as many people as possible in the community to avail of this option.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures have been taken to address disruption in the supply chain of oxygen tanks.
answer_text: Data is only available for the months of June, July & August 2022, when approximately 27% of cylinder deliveries were disrupted. This is where, for example, a patient ordered 6 x cylinders but may have received 4 or 5 instead. It is important to note that no patient is being denied oxygen. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what policies on transport ratios are in place for transporting students to and from school; and (ii) what safeguarding mechanisms are in place for these journeys.
answer_text: There are no specific policies in place for transport ratios on home to school transport.Escorts travel as standard on services to special schools and are provided on other services where it has been identified as a special transport need on the pupil’s statement of educational needs.In the majority of cases vehicles will be used to capacity with the assistance of a driver and with the addition of a bus escort to special schools. While pupils may receive transport assistance as per their Statement of Educational Need, there is not always a special transport need that requires additional assistance other than with boarding and disembarking the vehicle. Where a specific behavioural or medical need is identified, 1 to 1 escorts may be put in place or pupils may be required to travel alone or in smaller groups of pupils or vehicles.All transport frontline staff must complete an Access NI check prior to working on home to school transport services. All bus drivers and escorts are expected to receive staff inductions and regular training relating to their role. This includes child protection and safeguarding and the Passenger Assistant Training Scheme which is a nationally recognised standard for training staff whose role is to provide care and assistance to passengers travelling by road. A range of other training, including child protection training and training specific to the needs of individual pupils as identified in their statement, is arranged as required and appropriate. Detailed driver and escort manuals outlining key procedures are also in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what measures she is taking to support those asylum seekers that are based in hotels in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Issues around immigration, including accommodation and support for asylum seekers, are excepted matters here.  The Executive Office holds strategic responsibility both for refugees and asylum seekers, and for coordinating the regional response with the Home Office.  I have written to the Executive Office to reiterate the importance of resolving these issues with the Home Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline her awareness of the difficulties that asylum seekers currently face in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Issues around immigration, including accommodation and support for asylum seekers, are excepted matters here.  The Executive Office holds strategic responsibility both for refugees and asylum seekers, and for coordinating the regional response with the Home Office.  I have written to the Executive Office to reiterate the importance of resolving these issues with the Home Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how many hip replacement surgeries have taken place this year as day procedures; (ii) how many of these patients were aged (a) over 60 years old; (b) over 70 years old; and (c) over 80 years old.
answer_text: In 2021/22, there were 1,242 hip replacement operations carried out in Northern Ireland. Of these, 259 were for patients between ages 60-69; 361 between the ages of 70-79 years; and 426 patients were aged 80 or above. However, none of these operations were performed as day cases where the patient was discharged from hospital on the same day of admission. Please note that data for 2021-22 is provisional until publication of official statistics later in the month.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what progress has been made to widen the availability and use of smart electricity and gas meters in Northern Ireland; and (ii) what learning has been taken from the Girona and RULET projects.
answer_text: The Energy Strategy Action Plan 2022 contains an action to “Carry out a cost benefit analysis of electricity and gas smart meters and other technologies in order to access half-hourly and daily consumption information”. This action is on track to be completed before the end of this year. The Girona and RULET projects have demonstrated how renewable energy systems and smart data can be beneficial for consumers. My officials have visited these projects and gathered information which is being considered as part of the above work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what level of oversight is in place in relation to the spending of animal welfare funds at council level.
answer_text: My Department has a robust system in place to ensure that the delivery of animal welfare services by councils is monitored and reviewed.  This includes budgetary controls. Councils are required to submit information on their planned animal welfare spend to my Department at the start of each financial year and, in line with government monitoring rounds, they subsequently provide details of actual expenditure.  This information is analysed by my Department and, where there is a material variance from the planned spend, an explanation is sought from the relevant council and plans are amended, were necessary.  In addition, details of all spend incurred by each council is supplied to my Department quarterly.  My officials conduct sample checks on this information to assure that any costs incurred, as well as any goods and services procured, are in pursuit of animal welfare objectives. The Animal Welfare Strategic Partnership Board, which comprises of representatives from my Department and councils, also reviews whether the councils have taken sufficient steps to deliver the animal welfare service within the resource budget allocated to them.  It meets biannually.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on how his Department is liaising with individual councils on eradicating illegal dog breeders and puppy farms.
answer_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on how his Department is liaising with individual councils on eradicating illegal dog breeders and puppy farms.My Department continues to engage regularly with councils to address the illegal breeding of dogs.  The ‘Paws For Thought’ campaign, which aims disrupt this abhorrent activity, is overseen by a multi-agency group.  This group remains the key forum for engagement with councils and other key stakeholders on the matter of illegal breeding. Information continues to be shared between my Department and councils, in accordance with an established protocol, to ensure that co-ordinated action can be taken to tackle illegal dog breeding activity where appropriate.  In addition, my Department continues to work with councils and the other relevant statutory bodies to promote messages and communications that urge the public to secure dogs from reputable and licenced breeders.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the provision of treatment offered at Long Covid clinics; and (ii) whether his Department will extend the treatments on offer to include oxygen therapy and lung function tests.
answer_text: (i) Knowledge of Long Covid is still evolving.  The condition is complex with more than one body system affected.  Individuals can have unique combinations of fluctuating symptoms.   There is no specific diagnostic test for PCS and the published evidence base for interventions specifically aimed at a curative treatment is sparse at present.  High quality research is needed to inform future treatments and models of care.  Until then, the current service is focussed on holistic assessment, exclusion of other significant conditions, identifying patients with breathlessness who may benefit from pulmonary rehabilitation and those who need onward referral to secondary or community services.  This is complemented by signposting to self-management resources.  (ii) The Long Covid clinics include the provision of lung function tests and clinical staff will decide on the basis of individual assessment which patients need them.     Patients who need ongoing oxygen prescription as a result of COVID-19 would usually receive this under the care of a Trust respiratory team and/or their GP.  Long Covid clinics would not be the route for patients to receive oxygen therapy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to outline the reasons for the delay in the transition to the new NI Planning Portal; and (ii) what practical solutions his Department have devised to mitigate councils being without any system to process planning applications for at least three weeks in October 2022.
answer_text: The new Planning Portal was originally due to go fully live during the first quarter in 2022. Unfortunately, the size of the data in the current NI Planning Portal (NIPP) was larger and more complex than was originally identified which has contributed to the project being delayed. The new Portal is due to go-live in Autumn.      A transition plan has been agreed between relevant planning authorities and the IT supplier Terraquest Solutions that sets out the key activities in the run-up to and during the transfer to the new Planning IT System. While the transition period is estimated to be three weeks, the Northern Ireland Planning Portal will only be closed for one weekend and will then be available to provide read only access until the new System goes live. Therefore, the public and agents will be able to view planning applications but will be unable to submit representations online until the new Portal is operational approximately three weeks later. During this transitional period customers can submit their representations to the Councils or Department via e-mail or by post.The Councils and Department will continue to accept new planning applications during this transitional period but copies of the submitted forms and plans will not be made available online until the new Portal is launched.  These applications will continue to be checked and validated by staff offline in preparation for being uploaded. However, they will not be advertised, neighbour notified or consultations issued until the new Portal is up and running.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what oversight measures are in place to ensure consistency and quality of service from driving examiners; (ii) what measures are in place to manage complaints and appeals following the decision by an examiner to fail an examinee; and (iii) what measures are in place to support examinees with mental health issues and/or disabilities.
answer_text: In order to ensure consistency and quality of service, Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) driving examiners undergo rigorous training courses for each category of vehicle they conduct driving tests in.  Failure to meet the required standard for any training would preclude them from conducting driving tests for that category of vehicle.  In addition, in order to ensure that the correct standard is applied when testing, driving examiners are regularly accompanied on test by a supervising examiner. This process was temporarily suspended due to Covid restrictions but was reinstated in May 2022.EC Directive 2006/126, “the Driving Licence Directive”, clearly sets out the requirements regarding ongoing training of driving examiners.  The DVA adheres fully to these, and in some instances exceeds them.  Driving examiners must complete four days training in examining skills every two years, as well as five days training in practical driving skills every five years.  If these requirements are not complied with, a driving examiner cannot conduct driving tests in any category until the mandatory training has been successfully completed.Fault analysis of driving examiners is scheduled to be carried out once a year on all centres or when dictated by other circumstances, such as supervised tests, complaints, pass rates etc.  This analysis is used to identify the dangerous and serious fault markings recorded by examiners, the number of driving faults recorded, the average number of faults per test by male/female and whether or not a variety of test routes and manoeuvres are being used.  This information is used to help identify any differences between examiners at centres and between centres, and to help identify and prioritise training requirements.  The DVA’s formal complaints process is available on NI Direct and can be accessed at the following link https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/complain-or-claim-compensation-dva.   In addition, if a candidate thinks their driving examiner did not follow the law about how they must carry out driving tests, they can make an appeal to a court under Article 7 of the Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1981.  The court cannot change the test result: if the candidate wins their appeal, the court can decide the candidate should either be allowed to take a free retest or get a refund of their test fee.  Under the Magistrates Court Rules (Northern Ireland) 1984, as amended, an appeal should be lodged with the court with 3 months of the driving test date.Finally, candidates with a special need or disability take the same driving test as every other candidate, regardless of their impairment or condition.  The DVA offers facilities for candidates with special needs or physical disabilities, which can be availed of by calling the driving test booking line on 0300 200 7862.  The DVA will endeavour to support all candidates to take their driving test; candidates are asked to provide advance notification to allow the DVA time to make

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department will consider introducing a basic income for care leavers, similar to that in Wales.
answer_text: I appointed an independent panel to complete a comprehensive review of existing welfare mitigation measures and to consider a future mitigation package. As part of this work the independent panel considered the needs of children and young people. The panel’s recommendations will inform future decisions by my Department on how best to deliver financial support to the most vulnerable in our society. The implementation of any new payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she is engaging with the Minister for the Economy on initiatives to provide tenants in social housing with access to renewable energy to assist with the rising costs of living.
answer_text: My Department continues to engage with Department for Economy on matters relating to the Climate Change Act, Energy Strategy and Green Growth Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail her Department's engagement with sports clubs on creating educational campaigns on the importance of sexual consent.
answer_text: My Department, through Sport NI and in partnership with the NSPCC Child Protection in Sport Unit, works with Sports Governing Bodies through the Safeguarding Children in Sport Strategic Group to highlight the importance of the sector’s safeguarding obligations and to encourage the best practice outlined in the following link: http://www.sportni.net/about-us/safeguarding/safeguarding-children-in-sport-strategic-group-northern-ireland-strategic-safeguarding-plan.This aims to protect children and young people in sport through the provision of a safe environment which protects them from all forms of abuse.This work complements that of other Departments, which have the lead on adult safeguarding policies, who have also engaged positively with the sports sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people in the East Derry constituency are currently experiencing homelessness.
answer_text: The Housing Executive, has provided the following information.The waiting list figures as at 31st March 2022 30th for the East Derry constituency including those registered as homeless are as follows:Parliamentary ConstituencyAll ApplicantsApplicants in Housing StressFDA Applicants (Homeless)Allocations to ApplicantsAllocations to FDA ApplicantsEast Derry25281397989307275Applicant Information (all applicants and applicants in housing stress) is as at 31st March 2022. Allocation information (all allocations and allocations to FDA applicants) refers to the number of allocations in the preceding 12 month period. FDA refers to Full Duty Applicants – these are applicants accepted as being homeless, to whom the Housing Executive owes a duty under the Homelessness legislation, Article 10(2) of the Housing Order, 1988 to “secure that accommodation becomes available for his/her occupation”.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what engagement she has had with disability groups on ensuring housing is available and fit for purpose for people living with a disability.
answer_text: Work to develop my new Housing Supply Strategy has encompassed engagement with a wide range of stakeholders. This has included groups representing those living with physical, sensory and developmental disabilities, learning difficulties and mental health issues. The Supporting People Programme, which is administered by the Housing Executive on behalf of my Department, grant funds the provision of housing related support to vulnerable people, including people living with a disability, to enable them to live as independently as possible in the community. The Housing Executive actively promotes service user involvement to ensure housing is fit for purpose for people living with a disability, and have service user representation on the Interdepartmental Housing and Health Adaptions Programme Board co-chaired by my Department and the Department of Health through both Disability Action and a disabled service user.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what work is currently progressing on the recommendations she produced in March 2022 for Harry’s Law; and (ii) when does she expect the statutory guidance will be finalised.
answer_text: The report published on 25 March 2022 includes a commitment that new statutory guidance for educational settings will be published in the 2022/23 school year.  Officials are progressing the development of this through engagement with key stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps her Department has taken to create housing pathways for people living with addiction issues.
answer_text: The Inter Departmental Homelessness Action Plan which is led by my Department addresses non-accommodation based issues relevant to homelessness with the Housing Executive leading on the delivery of actions relevant to accommodation based issues as outlined in the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 which was published in March 2022.A key action of the Inter Departmental Homelessness Action Plan includes the development of a new strategy to prevent and address the harm related to substance use.In the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27, a key action in supporting those people living with addiction issues is the delivery of the Complex Lives model. The delivery of this model is led by a Multi-Disciplinary Team (MDT) with membership from the Housing Executive, Belfast City Council, Probation Board, Police Service, Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, Extern, Welcome Organisation, Simon Community and DePaul. Objectives of the Complex Lives model in Belfast include:•	Provide service users and frontline workers with a clear pathway into Complex Lives wrap around support.•	Quickly establish and collate a picture of the service and support connections with the individual.•	Establish what is known about individual motivation and wants (user voice).•	Establish initial objectives, providing a foundation for more detailed work by empowered front line staff. •	Convert this picture of activity into a single coordinated plan of activities, filling gaps where necessary.•	Place a key worker alongside the individual. The Year 1 Action Plan of the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 includes a commitment to evaluate and extend the Complex Lives project. It should be noted that any extension will be subject to a successful evaluation and the provision of appropriate funding.The Supporting People Programme, which is administered by the Housing Executive on behalf of the Department for Communities, grant funds the provision of housing related support to vulnerable people, including people with alcohol and drug problems, to enable them to live as independently as possible in the community.  Support for people living with addiction issues is provided through short term temporary accommodation, including hostels and supported housing, for those in housing need; to attain the skills necessary to live independently and move into permanent housing. Floating Support is also grant funded to provide support to those affected by addiction, in order to maintain their independence when living in the community.  The Housing Executive has developed a Supporting People Strategic Plan and Covid Recovery Plan 2022-2025, which was recently subject to public consultation, and considers the existing needs and emerging demands on Housing Support Services. It has been informed in part by a Strategic Needs Assessment which highlights an undersupply of services for single people with alcohol and/or drug problems experiencing homelessness and identifies this an area of increasing future need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what the protocol is regarding registered sex offenders who live near schools.
answer_text: Registered sex offenders are required to notify the PSNI annually of their address and to advise the PSNI if they change address or stay at another address for 7 days or more. Where a registered sex offender is on licence in the community, the Probation Board for Northern Ireland (PBNI) is required to approve their address.  In approving an address, the PBNI works closely with other agencies which form part of the Public Protection Arrangements for Northern Ireland (PPANI), including the PSNI and Social Services. The consideration of victim’s issues and potential opportunities to access current and prospective victims are central to this process. If the registered sex offender has been convicted of sexual offences against children, this will include whether the proposed address is located near to a child centred location, for example, a school or a play park. Approval for an address can be removed if further information of concern comes to light at any time.Where an individual fails to comply with a licence condition, the Department of Justice has the authority to revoke the licence and recall the individual to custody where it is considered the risk posed by the individual cannot be managed safely in the community.In circumstances where a registered sex offender is not subject to supervision by the PBNI, but is subject to a Sexual Offences Prevention Order (SOPO) with a condition requiring their address to be approved, it is the responsibility of the PSNI to approve the address. Breach of a SOPO is an arrestable offence. Where an individual subject to a SOPO breaches the terms of the Order, the PSNI will arrest the individual and initiate criminal proceedings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many prosecutions were made by the Public Prosecution Service on cases of human trafficking in Northern Ireland, over the last 10 years.
answer_text: Information has been provided in relation to the number of prosecutions and convictions at courts, for offences under the Human Trafficking and Exploitation (Criminal Justice and Support for Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 and other offences which specified human trafficking in the description.  The most recent ten year period for which information is available is 2012 – 2021.Prosecutions and convictions at courts for offences under human trafficking legislation, 2012 – 2021YearProsecutionsConvictions2012                     4                    * 2013                     *                    * 2014                     4                    * 2015                      -                     - 2016                     6                    4 2017                     *                     - 2018                     7                    * 2019                     4                    * 2020                     *                    * 2021                     *                     - Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposals at court.  Appeals are not included.  2. Figures relate to cases where there was a prosecution or conviction for at least one human trafficking offence.3. Figures relate to offences under the Human Trafficking and Exploitation (Criminal Justice and Support for Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 and other offences which specified human trafficking in the offence description.4. Figures relate to cases completed in the years specified. Cases which are currently in process have not been included. * relates to fewer than three cases.  Small numbers have been suppressed under rules of disclosure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many cases of human trafficking were reported in the last year.
answer_text: The National Referral Mechanism (NRM) is the UK wide mechanism for identifying and supporting victims and potential victims of modern slavery and human trafficking.   The most recent year for which statistics are available is the 2021 calendar year. The figure below is drawn from Home Office published data, and as such does not represent official statistics on behalf of my Department:2021 NRM Referrals363Statistics for Quarter 1, 2022 are showing 160 referrals in the year to date. The increase in the number of referrals in the last three years is not necessarily indicative of an increase in the prevalence of the crime.  It may be influenced by factors such as increased awareness of modern slavery and human trafficking, and improved identification by statutory bodies of potential victims, which is to be welcomed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities why Northern Ireland Housing Executive tenants who were allocated a one or two bed bungalow on or after 1 September 2002 may not have the opportunity to buy their property.
answer_text: There exists within the House Sales Scheme a property exemption. In summary, nobody is allowed to buy a one or two bedroom bungalow who (i) was allocated a tenancy after 1 September 2002, or (ii) was allocated a bungalow before 1 November 2000, where someone aged at least 60 was in the household.
