question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, further to paragraph 9.15 in the Gillen Review Report, what is the average time taken in Crown Court cases, from offence reported to case dealt with at court, for sexual offences against children for (i) 2020/21; and (ii) 2021/22.
answer_text: Provisional figures indicate that the median time taken from when an offence is reported until the case is dealt with at Crown Court was 876 days for cases dealt with in 2021/22, where the main offence at disposal was a sexual offence involving a child. The equivalent figure for cases dealt with in 2020/21 was 867 days.  Note:1. Figures relate to the number of calendar days between the dates listed.  Court recess dates are not taken into account.2. Figures relate to cases disposed at Crown Court during the time period specified. Each period is a financial year, running from 1st April to the following 31st March.3. Figures relate to cases brought on behalf of the PSNI, Harbour and Airport police in Northern Ireland that resulted in a court disposal.  Appeals are not included.4. Figures relate to case where the principal offence was a sexual offence that specified that the victim was a child in the offence description.5. Figures for 2021/22 are provisional.	In September 2019, in order to reduce the adverse impact of delay on child victims of sexual offences, a judge-led voluntary protocol was introduced to expedite serious sexual offence cases involving children under 13 in the Belfast Crown Court.  This has made a positive difference to the children and their families, and has recently been extended to September 2022.  		My Department, in co-operation with justice partners and the Lady Chief Justice’s Office, are building on the lessons being learned from the judge-led voluntary protocol and are developing a permanent protocol to ensure that these benefits can be extended to all child witnesses of serious sexual offences in Northern Ireland.  Implementation of the permanent protocol and wider roll out will however be subject to additional funding being made available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Tourism NI Experience Development Programme grants.
answer_text: Tourism Northern Ireland’s Experience Development Programme for 2021/23 has generated significant interest from a range of applicants across Northern Ireland.   I am delighted to advise that Tourism Northern Ireland has now received confirmation of its capital budget for the year ahead. Case officers will now seek to align successful applicants with the available budget, and it is hoped that Letters of Offer will be issued to successful applicants in late June.  My Department, Tourism NI and I remain fully committed to supporting the tourism industry recover and rebuild from the devastating impact of Covid-19.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to hardship as a result of the cost of living crisis, what consideration he has given to supporting a reduction in public transport fares.
answer_text: I fully recognise the difficulties facing our citizens in light of the current cost of living crisis and, in light of that, I can confirm that fares are currently frozen on our public transport network. Whilst I can see the benefits that a reduction in public transport fares would bring to our citizens, My Department is facing significant resource pressures in 2022-23, impacted by a decade of austerity budgets from Westminster, the unprecedented increases in energy costs and rising inflation. The refusal of the DUP to form an Executive means over £420m of funds being held at the centre cannot be released and I therefore cannot bid for additional in year funding. The blocking of the Executive also means a three year budget cannot be agreed which makes it extremely difficult for my Department to plan interventions which would support hard pressed citizens at this time.However, I would note that the Concessionary Fares Scheme remains available to provide free and half fare travel to some of the most vulnerable in our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people accessed food banks in East Derry over the last five years.
answer_text: The Department does not hold the requested data.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how much money received from the Treasury is going unspent per month as a result of the absence of an Executive.
answer_text: In the absence of an Executive a Budget has not been agreed for 2022-23 meaning that departments have no certainty upon which to plan. To assist with this DOF has informed departments of Contingency Planning envelopes which provide an understanding of funding for which there may be a reasonable degree of certainty that it will be contained in any Budget position brought to the Executive for consideration. However, Ministers have also been advised that they should not cease expenditure where there is a reasonable expectation that the department will receive further funding later in the financial year once a Budget is put in place. This is particularly important where other organisations are dependent upon funding from departments.In terms of funding available, I can confirm a further £435 million has become available since the Draft Budget was published. This funding cannot be allocated in the absence of an Executive. It is not possible to quantify the level of funding which is currently going unspent as this depends on decisions taken by individual Ministers. Furthermore, the available funding can be allocated later in the year once an Executive is formed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has carried out any impact assessments on the Northern Ireland economy as a result of the absence of an Executive.
answer_text: My Department has not carried out an impact assessment concerning the current absence of the NI Executive. As you are aware however, Ministers remain in post and I, as Economy Minister, continue to meet with local businesses and potential investors to promote Northern Ireland as a prime location to work and do business.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has had any discussions with EasyJet to prevent flights, which are leaving and entering Northern Ireland, being cancelled with less than 24 hours' notice.
answer_text: As Minister for the Economy my responsibility is with maintaining and enhancing Northern Ireland’s air connectivity, both domestically and internationally. In doing so, I fully recognise that civil aviation is a reserved matter for the UK Department for Transport (DfT). The Secretary of State for Transport has pointed out that this is a private sector industry and responsibility for cancelled flights and lengthy queues lies with the airlines and airports.  They are commercially responsible for manging their businesses and providing their customers with an appropriate and reasonable service.The scenes we have witnessed at airports are distressing, with holidaymakers missing out on their first trips abroad after the pandemic. EasyJet has quite rightly apologised to its customers for the disruption that flight cancellations have caused. I welcome the fact that the UK Government is working closely with the sector to ensure that there will be no repeat of those scenes this summer.Passengers who have been affected by cancelled flights should be offered a full refund within seven days, or re-routing via alternative transportation at the earliest opportunity by the airline.  If the flight is delayed or cancelled the consumer may also be entitled to compensation.  To claim compensation, passengers can complete an online form on the easyJet website. Should passengers remain dissatisfied with the airline’s response, they can get in touch with the Consumer Council, which is the designated complaints handling body for flights in and out of Northern Ireland, and it will take up the complaint on their behalf.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update of measures the Driver and Vehicle Agency is taking to address the MOT backlog.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.Official vehicle testing statistics are published on a monthly basis and can be accessed at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/driver-and-vehicle-agency-monthly-tests-conducted-statistics.  The DVA continues to experience significant demand for vehicle testing services and in some cases customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires. In such cases, customers must book the earliest available test appointment for their vehicle, which may mean travelling to another test centre instead of their preferred location. The DVA releases vehicle test appointments on a daily basis and customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available. The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/book-view-change-or-cancel-mot-vehicle-test and is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to their customers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what work her Department is undertaking to support litigants in person, in both tribunal and court settings.
answer_text: Improving the accessibility of the court system is a key component of the Civil Justice Modernisation Programme I initiated in the last mandate. Together with the Ulster University and Northern Ireland Human Rights Commission, my Department has established a Litigant in Person Reference Group to help identify barriers to LiP participation and means of addressing them. The Group, which also includes Litigants in Person, members of the judiciary, and representatives of the legal profession, has decided to focus initially on improving the accessibility of family proceedings and good progress is being made including the delivery of training on dealing with distressed litigants and development of a website to host the work of the Group. The Department has also been developing tools to better aid parents involved in disputes relating to children as part the Private Law Early Resolution Action Plan I launched in August 2021. The Litigant in Person Reference Group has been critical in informing that work which I hope the Department can continue to build on to further improve the experience of personal litigants in the current mandate. Next steps will seek to build on existing good practice and tools including leaflets and guidance on processes and procedures which have been effectively used to help litigants navigate the tribunals system and the links that have been forged to help signpost litigants to third sector/ voluntary organisations who can provide support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department will fund English classes for Ukrainian refugees settled in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: English for Speakers of Other Languages (ESOL) classes are run through Further Education colleges and a number of voluntary and community organisations.The Department for the Economy is responsible for the policy, strategic development and financing of the further education sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when her Department plans to implement the recommendations of the Discretionary Support Review and publish the findings of the Mitigations Review.
answer_text: The report of the independent review of Discretionary Support has been published on my Department’s website.  I hope to make an announcement soon on how I propose to improve Discretionary Support by making it more accessible and ensuring that emergency financial support is there for people when they need it most.The independent review of welfare mitigations has been completed and a final report has been submitted to the Department. This will be published at the earliest opportunity.The implementation of any new payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on steps being taken to ensure that core messages and a bottom to top approach are reflected in the composition of the Rural Stakeholder Oversight Committee and its sub-groups, as part of the Rural Policy Framework for NI.
answer_text: In establishing the Rural Stakeholder Oversight Committee my officials are engaging with key rural stakeholders to ensure that membership of the Oversight Committee and associated sub-groups is representative. This process will also establish arrangements that ensure core messages, as outlined in the Priority Interventions of the Rural Policy Framework, and a bottom up approach will inform the future programme.  It is intended that the approach to membership, format and operation of the sub-groups will also be agreed through the Oversight Committee.		The work of the Oversight Committee, associated sub-groups and the development of the new programme will also be informed by the lessons learned from a range of evaluations on key programmes such as LEADER where there is strong recognition of the importance of a bottom up approach in addressing the needs of rural communities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many proposed projects does his Department have that cannot be started as a result of the absence of a functioning Executive.
answer_text: From the Office of the Minister 	Gordon Lyons MLA								My Department does not have any proposed projects which cannot be started as the result of the absence of a functioning Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has had any discussions with his officials to rejuvenate Coleraine Town Centre, given the recent numbers of businesses closing.
answer_text: Town centre rejuvenation is not within the remit of my department. The Executive Office has established a High Street Task Force, whilst other departments have lead responsibility for town regeneration, planning and licensing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the process and progress of recruiting social partners to the Rural Stakeholder Oversight Committee and its sub-groups, as part of the Rural Policy Framework for NI.
answer_text: I approved the new Rural Policy Framework at the end of March and my officials have now started to draft the new programme and progress the work necessary to form the Rural Stakeholder Oversight Committee and associated sub-groups.In establishing the Rural Stakeholder Oversight Committee, my officials are engaging with key rural stakeholders to ensure that membership of the Oversight Committee is representative.  This process will also establish arrangements that ensure core messages, as outlined in the Priority Interventions of the Rural Policy Framework, and a bottom up approach will inform the future programme.  It is intended that the approach to membership, format and operation of the sub-groups will also be agreed through the Oversight Committee.The Oversight Committee will be made up of a wide range of rural stakeholders and it is anticipated that a terms of reference and invitations to members will issue before the end of June 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on what steps are being taken to ensure community groups and rural networks are supported in terms of long-term funding, specifically for resource, under the Rural Policy Framework for NI.
answer_text: Following approval of the Rural Policy Framework in March of this year, my officials are in the process of developing the new Rural Business and Community Investment Programme which will help to provide more certainty on the longer term funding that will be available for community groups and rural networks. In addition, my Department continues to deliver a wide range of initiatives through the Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation Programme and pilot initiatives that have been designed to test the assumptions in the Rural Policy Framework. This ongoing support includes: a wide range of TRPSI initiatives; the Village Catalyst Programme; the Rural Halls Enhancement Scheme; the Rural Community Garden Pollinator Scheme; and the School Garden Pollinator Scheme. In addition, the Department continues to provide vital support to the Rural Support Networks, Northern Ireland Rural Women’s Network and the Rural Community Network. The Department is taking forward a wide range of evaluations into its pilot schemes, the Rural Tourism and LEADER programmes to ensure that lessons learned can be reflected in the new programme. The development of the new programme will help to inform the longer term support, including resource funding, which will be available for community groups and rural networks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department has considered adopting the approach of the Netherlands in relation to Relationships and Sexuality Education.
answer_text: The approach to the teaching of Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) in Northern Ireland is in line with the general principles of the Northern Ireland curriculum and a topic based approach to RSE would be out of step with the approach to other areas of the curriculum and the general approach to education delivery here. The school curricula across all Areas of Learning, including RSE, can be very different across jurisdictions and direct comparisons are not always appropriate.   In addition, what is perceived as working well in one jurisdiction may not necessarily work as well in the Northern Ireland context.  I understand that, in the Netherlands, there is a core curriculum or RSE from age four but teachers have some degree of autonomy in how they deliver the material.  This is broadly similar to the approach in Northern Ireland where greater flexibility and less prescription are key features allowing schools autonomy to offer and deliver suitable curricular approaches matched to needs of their pupils and develop the skills that young people need for the modern world. Such flexibility in curriculum delivery and pedagogical autonomy is a consistent feature of high performing school systems, where school-level flexibility and teacher collaboration lead to innovations in teaching and learning at a time when issues are important to young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on plans to deal with the housing stress in the Portrush and Portstewart areas.
answer_text: The Housing Supply Strategy sets out a framework and direction of travel for the collaborative action required across the public, private and voluntary & community. Options are being considered for finalising the Strategy in the continuing absence of the Executive and an agreed budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many second homes exist within the East Derry constituency, broken down by town.
answer_text: My Department does not collect or hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) how many individuals own more than one property within the East Derry constituency; (ii) how many of those properties are currently vacant.
answer_text: My Department does not collect or hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment on the impact of second home purchases on housing stress and those who are homeless in Portrush and Portstewart.
answer_text: The need for improved housing related data collection and analysis forms part of ongoing work on my Housing Supply Strategy. The Housing Supply Strategy sets out a framework and direction of travel for the collaborative action required across the public, private and voluntary & community sectors. Options are being considered for finalising the Strategy in the continuing absence of the Executive and an agreed budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Personal Independence Payment applicants in each year since its introduction; and of that number, how many were awarded: (i) a standard rate daily living award only; (ii) a standard rate mobility award only; (iii) a standard rate daily living and standard rate mobility; (iv) an enhanced rate daily living only; (v) an enhanced rate mobility only; (vi) an enhanced rate daily living and standard rate mobility; (vii) an enhanced rate daily living and standard rate mobility; (viii) a standard rate daily living and enhanced rate mobility; a
answer_text: The information requested is included in the supplementary tables of the latest Personal Independent Payment statistics and can be found here: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/articles/personal-independence-payment-statistics

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department will consider working with the Post Office to deliver a mobile service for users in rural areas.
answer_text: The Post Office service is a reserved matter, falling under the responsibility of the Department for Business, Energy & Industrial Strategy (BEIS).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department has mitigations or plans in place to reduce the waiting times of social security appeals for claimants.
answer_text: My Department continues to list benefit appeals for hearing despite the challenges faced because of the pandemic. To maximise listing capacity, The Appeals Service (TAS) introduced additional options for case listing. Appellants can now choose to have their appeal listed on the papers, by telephone, video link or face to face. Paper hearings and remote hearings are listed consistently in response to demand.A number of venues have been identified and adapted to ensure they are COVID secure for all parties to attend a physical hearing including new bespoke premises in Omagh Library where hearings commenced on 25 April 2022. TAS has also secured an additional Belfast venue that will be operational from 4th July 2022 and two further hearing rooms in Cleaver House have now been adapted to facilitate hearings. Work is continuing to find additional venues to list benefit appeal hearings.These mitigations have contributed to a 15.5% reduction in outstanding caseload to 6686 appeals, compared with the same month in 2021 (8128) and a 20% reduction compared with May 2019 (8610), the last comparable pre-pandemic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department plans to introduce traffic calming measures in Feeny, County Derry.
answer_text: I am advised that traffic calming measures were implemented in Feeny back in 2016.  In light of community concerns about traffic speeds, further assessments have been recently undertaken and local officials are currently considering the appropriateness of providing additional traffic calming features such as traffic islands, build outs and enhanced road markings.  In the intervening period I can confirm that work to renew and enhance the existing traffic calming gateway features is being progressed.
